### [1] [Governing the Agentic Enterprise: A Governance Maturity Model for Managing AI Agent Sprawl in Business Operations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16338)
*Vivek Acharya*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Agentic AI Governance Maturity Model (AAGMM), a five-level framework addressing governance challenges in enterprise agentic AI adoption, validated through simulations showing significant improvements in business outcomes at higher maturity levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rapid adoption of agentic AI in enterprise operations has created a governance crisis characterized by uncontrolled agent sprawl—proliferation of redundant, ungoverned, and conflicting AI agents. Industry data shows poor governance maturity and high projected failure rates for agentic AI projects, yet academic literature lacks formal, empirically validated governance models connecting governance capability to measurable business outcomes.

Method: The paper introduces the Agentic AI Governance Maturity Model (AAGMM), a five-level framework spanning 12 governance domains, grounded in NIST AI RMF and ISO/IEC 42001 standards. It also proposes a taxonomy of agent sprawl patterns with quantifiable business cost models. The framework is validated through 750 simulation runs across five enterprise scenarios and five governance maturity levels, measuring outcomes including cost containment, risk incident rates, operational efficiency, and decision quality.

Result: Results show statistically significant differences (p < 0.001, large effect sizes d > 2.0) between all governance maturity levels. Level 4-5 organizations achieved 94.3% lower sprawl indices, 96.4% fewer risk incidents, and 32.6% higher effective task completion rates compared to Level 1 organizations.

Conclusion: The AAGMM provides practitioners with an actionable roadmap for governing autonomous AI agents while maximizing business returns. The model addresses the critical governance gap in enterprise adoption of agentic AI, offering a formal framework that connects governance maturity to measurable business outcomes.

Abstract: The rapid adoption of agentic AI in enterprise business operations--autonomous systems capable of planning, reasoning, and executing multi-step workflows--has created an urgent governance crisis. Organizations face uncontrolled agent sprawl: the proliferation of redundant, ungoverned, and conflicting AI agents across business functions. Industry surveys report that only 21% of enterprises have mature governance models for autonomous agents, while 40% of agentic AI projects are projected to fail by 2027 due to inadequate governance and risk controls. Despite growing acknowledgment of this challenge, academic literature lacks a formal, empirically validated governance maturity model connecting governance capability to measurable business outcomes. This paper introduces the Agentic AI Governance Maturity Model (AAGMM), a five-level framework spanning 12 governance domains, grounded in NIST AI RMF and ISO/IEC 42001 standards. We additionally propose a novel taxonomy of agent sprawl patterns--functional duplication, shadow agents, orphaned agents, permission creep, and unmonitored delegation chains--each linked to quantifiable business cost models. The framework is validated through 750 simulation runs across five enterprise scenarios and five governance maturity levels, measuring business outcomes including cost containment, risk incident rates, operational efficiency, and decision quality. Results demonstrate statistically significant differences (p < 0.001, large effect sizes d > 2.0) between all governance maturity levels, with Level 4-5 organizations achieving 94.3% lower sprawl indices, 96.4% fewer risk incidents, and 32.6% higher effective task completion rates compared to Level 1. The AAGMM provides practitioners with an actionable roadmap for governing autonomous AI agents while maximizing business returns.

</details>


### [2] [Semantic Consensus: Process-Aware Conflict Detection and Resolution for Enterprise Multi-Agent LLM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16339)
*Vivek Acharya*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The Semantic Consensus Framework (SCF) is a process-aware middleware that addresses Semantic Intent Divergence in multi-agent LLM systems, achieving 100% workflow completion by providing shared context, formal intent representation, conflict detection, and resolution protocols.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent LLM systems in enterprise settings have high failure rates (41%-86.7%), with nearly 79% of failures stemming from specification and coordination issues rather than model limitations. The paper identifies Semantic Intent Divergence—where agents develop inconsistent interpretations of shared objectives due to siloed context and absent process models—as a key unaddressed root cause of multi-agent failure.

Method: The proposed Semantic Consensus Framework (SCF) is a process-aware middleware with six components: 1) Process Context Layer for shared operational semantics, 2) Semantic Intent Graph for formal intent representation, 3) Conflict Detection Engine for real-time identification of contradictory, contention-based, and causally invalid intent combinations, 4) Consensus Resolution Protocol using policy-authority-temporal hierarchy, 5) Drift Monitor for detecting gradual semantic divergence, and 6) Process-Aware Governance Integration for organizational policy enforcement.

Result: Evaluation across 600 runs spanning three multi-agent frameworks (AutoGen, CrewAI, LangGraph) and four enterprise scenarios shows SCF achieved 100% workflow completion compared to 25.1% for the next-best baseline. It detected 65.2% of semantic conflicts with 27.9% precision and provided complete governance audit trails.

Conclusion: SCF effectively addresses Semantic Intent Divergence in enterprise multi-agent LLM systems, making it protocol-agnostic and compatible with MCP and A2A communication standards. It demonstrates significant improvements in workflow completion and conflict detection while enabling governance compliance.

Abstract: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) systems are rapidly emerging as the dominant architecture for enterprise AI automation, yet production deployments exhibit failure rates between 41% and 86.7%, with nearly 79% of failures originating from specification and coordination issues rather than model capability limitations. This paper identifies Semantic Intent Divergence--the phenomenon whereby cooperating LLM agents develop inconsistent interpretations of shared objectives due to siloed context and absent process models--as a primary yet formally unaddressed root cause of multi-agent failure in enterprise settings. We propose the Semantic Consensus Framework (SCF), a process-aware middleware comprising six components: a Process Context Layer for shared operational semantics, a Semantic Intent Graph for formal intent representation, a Conflict Detection Engine for real-time identification of contradictory, contention-based, and causally invalid intent combinations, a Consensus Resolution Protocol using a policy--authority--temporal hierarchy, a Drift Monitor for detecting gradual semantic divergence, and a Process-Aware Governance Integration layer for organizational policy enforcement. Evaluation across 600 runs spanning three multi-agent frameworks (AutoGen, CrewAI, LangGraph) and four enterprise scenarios demonstrates that SCF is the only approach to achieve 100% workflow completion--compared to 25.1% for the next-best baseline--while detecting 65.2% of semantic conflicts with 27.9% precision and providing complete governance audit trails. The framework is protocol-agnostic and compatible with MCP and A2A communication standards.

</details>


### [3] [Computational Hermeneutics: Evaluating generative AI as a cultural technology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16403)
*Cody Kommers,Ruth Ahnert,Maria Antoniak,Emmanouil Benetos,Steve Benford,Mercedes Bunz,Baptiste Caramiaux,Shauna Concannon,Martin Disley,James Dobson,Yali Du,Edgar Duéñez-Guzmán,Kerry Francksen,Evelyn Gius,Jonathan W. Y. Gray,Ryan Heuser,Sarah Immel,Richard Jean So,Sang Leigh,Dalaki Livingston,Hoyt Long,Meredith Martin,Georgia Meyer,Daniela Mihai,Ashley Noel-Hirst,Kirsten Ostherr,Deven Parker,Yipeng Qin,Jessica Ratcliff,Emily Robinson,Karina Rodriguez,Adam Sobey,Ted Underwood,Aditya Vashistha,Matthew Wilkens,Youyou Wu,Yuan Zheng,Drew Hemment*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that generative AI systems should be evaluated as cultural technologies using hermeneutic principles that consider context, multiple interpretations, and ambiguity, rather than just measuring accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI evaluation frameworks treat culture as a measurable variable rather than fundamental to system operation, failing to address how GenAI systems function as cultural technologies that inherently interpret context and meaning.

Method: The paper draws on hermeneutic theory from humanities to develop "computational hermeneutics" - a framework that analyzes GenAI as "context machines" addressing three interpretive challenges: situatedness, plurality, and ambiguity.

Result: The paper presents three principles for hermeneutic evaluation: benchmarks should be iterative (not one-off), include people (not just machines), and measure cultural context (not just model output).

Conclusion: This hermeneutic perspective offers a nascent paradigm shift for AI evaluation - moving from standardized accuracy questions to contextual questions about meaning, providing a better framework for designing and evaluating contemporary AI systems as cultural technologies.

Abstract: Generative AI systems are increasingly recognized as cultural technologies, yet current evaluation frameworks often treat culture as a variable to be measured rather than fundamental to the system's operation. Drawing on hermeneutic theory from the humanities, we argue that GenAI systems function as "context machines" that must inherently address three interpretive challenges: situatedness (meaning only emerges in context), plurality (multiple valid interpretations coexist), and ambiguity (interpretations naturally conflict). We present computational hermeneutics as an emerging framework offering an interpretive account of what GenAI systems do, and how they might do it better. We offer three principles for hermeneutic evaluation -- that benchmarks should be iterative, not one-off; include people, not just machines; and measure cultural context, not just model output. This perspective offers a nascent paradigm for designing and evaluating contemporary AI systems: shifting from standardized questions about accuracy to contextual ones about meaning.

</details>


### [4] [Heterogeneous Self-Play for Realistic Highway Traffic Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16406)
*Jinkai Qiu,Alessandro Saviolo,Chaojie Wang,Mingke Wang,Xiaoyu Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PHASE is a self-play framework for realistic highway traffic simulation that generates diverse, controllable scenarios with credible multi-agent interactions, achieving strong zero-shot transfer to real-world data without direct imitation learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Highway simulation is crucial for autonomous vehicle safety evaluation, but current methods struggle with three key requirements: broad coverage across speeds and maneuvers, controllable generation of rare safety-critical scenarios, and behavioral credibility in multi-agent interactions.

Method: PHASE uses a context-aware self-play framework with explicit per-agent conditioning for controllability, synthetic scenario generation for broad coverage, and closed-loop multi-agent training for realistic interactions. It supports different vehicle profiles (cars, trucks) via vehicle-aware dynamics and context-conditioned actions, with stabilization techniques like early termination, at-fault collision attribution, highway-aware reward shaping, coupled curricula, and robust policy optimization.

Result: Zero-shot transfer to 512 unseen real scenarios in exiD dataset achieved 96.3% success rate, reducing ADE/FDE from 6.57/12.07 m to 2.44/5.25 m compared to prior self-play baseline. Improved behavioral realism over IDM with 13.1% reduction in Frechet trajectory distance and 20.2% reduction in energy distance in learned trajectory embedding space.

Conclusion: Synthetic self-play can provide a scalable route to controllable and realistic highway scenario generation without requiring direct imitation of expert logs, demonstrating strong transfer capabilities to real-world driving scenarios.

Abstract: Realistic highway simulation is critical for scalable safety evaluation of autonomous vehicles, particularly for interactions that are too rare to study from logged data alone. Yet highway traffic generation remains challenging because it requires broad coverage across speeds and maneuvers, controllable generation of rare safety-critical scenarios, and behavioral credibility in multi-agent interactions. We present PHASE, Policy for Heterogeneous Agent Self-play on Expressway, a context-aware self-play framework that addresses these three requirements through explicit per-agent conditioning for controllability, synthetic scenario generation for broad highway coverage, and closed-loop multi-agent training for realistic interaction dynamics. PHASE further supports different vehicle profiles, for example, passenger cars and articulated trailer trucks, within a single policy via vehicle-aware dynamics and context-conditioned actions, and stabilizes self-play with early termination of unrecoverable states, at-fault collision attribution, highway-aware reward shaping, coupled curricula, and robust policy optimization. Despite being trained only on synthetic data, PHASE transfers zero-shot to 512 unseen high-interaction real scenarios in exiD, achieving a 96.3% success rate and reducing ADE/FDE from 6.57/12.07 m to 2.44/5.25 m relative to a prior self-play baseline. In a learned trajectory embedding space, it also improves behavioral realism over IDM, reducing Frechet trajectory distance by 13.1% and energy distance by 20.2%. These results show that synthetic self-play can provide a scalable route to controllable and realistic highway scenario generation without direct imitation of expert logs.

</details>


### [5] [Support Sufficiency as Consequence-Sensitive Compression in Belief Arbitration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16434)
*Mark Walsh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that systems need to preserve more than just selected content and scalar confidence during compression - they must maintain sufficient support structure for policy decisions, which should be dynamically regulated based on consequences and resource constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard accounts assume that selected content and scalar confidence are sufficient for downstream control, but this paper argues they are not. When a system commits to a hypothesis, evidential structure is lost to compression, and determining what must survive compression is itself a consequence-sensitive problem that affects verification, abstention, and recovery.

Method: Developed a recurrent arbitration architecture where active constraint fields jointly determine a hypothesis geometry over candidates. The system compresses this geometry into a support-aware control state regulated by current consequence geometry, arbitration memory, and resource constraints. Used a bounded objective formalizing the tradeoff and confirmed predictions through minimal repeated-interaction simulation.

Result: Adaptive controllers that regulate support resolution outperform all fixed-resolution controllers in cumulative utility. Agile adaptive control outperforms sluggish adaptive control. Fixed high-resolution control achieves best commitment accuracy but still trails adaptive controllers due to resource costs and learning fragmentation offsetting gains from richer retention.

Conclusion: Support sufficiency should be understood as a dynamic compression criterion rather than a static representational threshold. Robust arbitration depends on preserving the smallest support structure adequate for policy under current consequences and regulating that structure as conditions change across inference-action cycles.

Abstract: When a system commits to a hypothesis, much of the evidential structure behind that commitment is lost to compression. Standard accounts assume that selected content and scalar confidence suffice for downstream control. This paper argues that they do not, and that determining what must survive compression is itself a consequence-sensitive problem. We develop a recurrent arbitration architecture in which active constraint fields jointly determine a hypothesis geometry over candidates. Rather than carrying that geometry forward in full, the system compresses it into a support-aware control state whose resolution is regulated by current consequence geometry, arbitration memory, and resource constraints.
  A bounded objective formalizes the tradeoff. Too little retained support collapses policy-relevant distinctions, producing controllers that select content adequately while misrouting verification, abstention, and recovery. Too much retained support fragments learning across overly fine contexts, degrading adaptation even as discrimination improves. These failure modes yield ordered controller predictions confirmed by a minimal repeated-interaction simulation. Adaptive controllers that regulate support resolution outperform all fixed-resolution controllers in cumulative utility. Agile adaptive control outperforms sluggish adaptive control. Fixed high-resolution control achieves the best commitment accuracy but still trails adaptive controllers because resource cost and learning fragmentation offset the gains from richer retention.
  Support sufficiency should be understood not as a static representational threshold, but as a dynamic compression criterion. Robust arbitration depends on preserving the smallest support structure adequate for policy under the current consequence landscape, and on regulating that structure as conditions change across repeated cycles of inference and action.

</details>


### [6] [Healthcare AI for Automation or Allocation? A Transaction Cost Economics Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16465)
*Ari Ercole*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Healthcare coordination costs vary systematically across occupations, with clinicians facing higher transaction costs due to information search and decision complexity, suggesting AI intervention opportunities depend more on coordination structure than technical complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While healthcare productivity is influenced by both clinical complexity and coordination costs under uncertainty, transaction-cost economics has rarely been applied at task level across health occupations to understand coordination frictions systematically.

Method: Used O*NET occupational database task statements and frequency weights to characterize healthcare work at task granularity; coded each unique task using constrained large language model into transaction-cost categories (information search, decision/bargaining, monitoring/enforcement, adaptation/coordination) and overall transaction-cost intensity score.

Result: Clinician roles showed substantially higher transaction-cost intensity than non-clinician roles, primarily driven by greater information search and decision-related coordination burdens; dispersion of transaction costs within occupations did not differ significantly.

Conclusion: Systematic heterogeneity exists in coordination work across healthcare roles, and opportunities for digital/AI interventions are unevenly distributed, shaped more by underlying coordination structure than by technical task complexity.

Abstract: Healthcare productivity is shaped not only by clinical complexity but by the costs of coordinating work under uncertainty. Transaction-cost economics offers a theory of these coordination frictions, yet has rarely been operationalised at task level across health occupations. Using task statements and frequency weights from the O*NET occupational database, we characterised healthcare work at task granularity and coded each unique task using a constrained large language model into one dominant transaction-cost category (information search, decision and bargaining, monitoring and enforcement, or adaptation and coordination) together with an overall transaction-cost intensity score. Aggregating to the occupation level, clinician roles exhibited substantially higher transaction-cost intensity than non-clinician roles, driven primarily by greater burdens of information search and decision-related coordination, while dispersion of transaction costs within occupations did not differ. These findings demonstrate systematic heterogeneity in the nature of coordination work across healthcare roles and suggest that the opportunities for digital and AI interventions are unevenly distributed, shaped less by technical task complexity than by underlying coordination structure.

</details>


### [7] [Agentic Frameworks for Reasoning Tasks: An Empirical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16646)
*Zeeshan Rasheed,Abdul Malik Sami,Muhammad Waseem,Kai-Kristian Kemell,Mika Saari,Pekka Abrahamsson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The first large-scale empirical evaluation of 22 agentic AI frameworks shows that framework orchestration quality (memory control, failure handling, cost management) is more critical for reasoning performance than pure reasoning capabilities, with most frameworks showing stable performance but significant drops in mathematical reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite recent advances in agentic frameworks enabling complex AI reasoning, there's limited evidence comparing their actual performance, efficiency, and practical suitability for reasoning-intensive tasks.

Method: Empirical evaluation of 22 widely used agentic frameworks across three reasoning benchmarks (BBH, GSM8K, ARC) using 1,200 GitHub repositories collected between Jan 2023-July 2025, organized into a taxonomy based on architectural design, measured under unified settings for accuracy, execution time, computational cost, and cross-benchmark consistency.

Result: 19 of 22 frameworks completed all benchmarks, with 12 showing stable performance (74.6-75.9% accuracy, 4-6 seconds per task, $0.14-0.18 per task). Poor performance was due to orchestration problems like uncontrolled context growth (Camel), expensive retries (Upsonic), and inefficient API usage (AutoGen, Mastra). Mathematical reasoning performance dropped sharply (GSM8K: 44.35% vs BBH: 89.80%, ARC: 89.56%).

Conclusion: Framework selection should prioritize orchestration quality - particularly memory control, failure handling, and cost management - over pure reasoning capabilities, as orchestration problems were the main cause of poor performance rather than reasoning limitations.

Abstract: Recent advances in agentic frameworks have enabled AI agents to perform complex reasoning and decision-making. However, evidence comparing their reasoning performance, efficiency, and practical suitability remains limited. To address this gap, we empirically evaluate 22 widely used agentic frameworks across three reasoning benchmarks: BBH, GSM8K, and ARC. The frameworks were selected from 1,200 GitHub repositories collected between January 2023 and July 2025 and organized into a taxonomy based on architectural design. We evaluated them under a unified setting, measuring reasoning accuracy, execution time, computational cost, and cross-benchmark consistency.
  Our results show that 19 of the 22 frameworks completed all three benchmarks. Among these, 12 showed stable performance, with mean accuracy of 74.6-75.9%, execution time of 4-6 seconds per task, and cost of 0.14-0.18 cents per task. Poorer results were mainly caused by orchestration problems rather than reasoning limits. For example, Camel failed to complete BBH after 11 days because of uncontrolled context growth, while Upsonic consumed USD 1,434 in one day because repeated extraction failures triggered costly retries. AutoGen and Mastra also exhausted API quotas through iterative interactions that increased prompt length without improving results.
  We also found a sharp drop in mathematical reasoning. Mean accuracy on GSM8K was 44.35%, compared with 89.80% on BBH and 89.56% on ARC. Overall, this study provides the first large-scale empirical comparison of agentic frameworks for reasoning-intensive software engineering tasks and shows that framework selection should prioritize orchestration quality, especially memory control, failure handling, and cost management.

</details>


### [8] [From Subsumption to Satisfiability: LLM-Assisted Active Learning for OWL Ontologies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16672)
*Haoruo Zhao,Wenshuo Tang,Duncan Guthrie,Michele Sevegnani,David Flynn,Paul Harvey*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs are used in active learning to provide real-world examples for membership queries, reducing ontology modeling errors to only Type II errors (false negatives) that delay but don't corrupt the learning process.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve active learning for ontology construction by reducing the risk of introducing inconsistencies through membership queries, and to leverage Large Language Models as sources of real-world knowledge while minimizing errors.

Method: The approach reformulates candidate axioms into counter-concepts, verbalizes them in controlled natural language, and presents them to LLMs to obtain real-world examples approximating instances of the counter-concepts. This leverages the standard reduction of subsumption to satisfiability in description logics.

Result: Experimental results on 13 commercial LLMs show that recall (corresponding to Type II errors) remains stable across several well-established ontologies, demonstrating the robustness of the approach.

Conclusion: Using LLMs as a component in active learning for ontology construction successfully limits errors to only Type II errors (false negatives), which merely delay the construction process without introducing inconsistencies, making the approach safe and practical for ontology modeling.

Abstract: In active learning, membership queries (MQs) allow a learner to pose questions to a teacher, such as ''Is every apple a fruit?'', to which the teacher responds correctly with yes or no. These MQs can be viewed as subsumption tests with respect to the target ontology. Inspired by the standard reduction of subsumption to satisfiability in description logics, we reformulate each candidate axiom into its corresponding counter-concept and verbalise it in controlled natural language before presenting it to Large Language Models (LLMs). We introduce LLMs as a third component that provides real-world examples approximating an instance of the counter-concept. This design property ensures that only Type II errors may occur in ontology modelling; in the worst case, these errors merely delay the construction process without introducing inconsistencies. Experimental results on 13 commercial LLMs show that recall, corresponding to Type II errors in our framework, remains stable across several well-established ontologies.

</details>


### [9] [Agentic Risk-Aware Set-Based Engineering Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16687)
*Varun Kumar,George Em Karniadakis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multi-agent LLM framework assists early engineering design by automating exploration and risk assessment, demonstrated on aerodynamic airfoil design with human oversight.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early-stage engineering design faces challenges with vast parameter spaces and uncertainty, requiring extensive exploration that's labor-intensive and time-consuming for human engineers.

Method: Multi-agent LLM framework with specialized agents (Coding Assistant, Design Agent, Systems Engineering Agent, Analyst Agent) coordinated by human Manager, operating under set-based design philosophy with CVaR-based risk management and global sensitivity analysis.

Result: Framework systematically explores and prunes large design sets, generates actionable heuristics, and presents curated final designs with high-fidelity CFD simulations, automating high-volume analytical tasks.

Conclusion: The AI-assisted framework effectively handles computational complexity, enhances human decision-making with quantitative risk assessment, and provides a scalable approach for early engineering design under uncertainty.

Abstract: This paper introduces a multi-agent framework guided by Large Language Models (LLMs) to assist in the early stages of engineering design, a phase often characterized by vast parameter spaces and inherent uncertainty. Operating under a human-in-the-loop paradigm and demonstrated on the canonical problem of aerodynamic airfoil design, the framework employs a team of specialized agents: a Coding Assistant, a Design Agent, a Systems Engineering Agent, and an Analyst Agent - all coordinated by a human Manager. Integrated within a set-based design philosophy, the process begins with a collaborative phase where the Manager and Coding Assistant develop a suite of validated tools, after which the agents execute a structured workflow to systematically explore and prune a large set of initial design candidates. A key contribution of this work is the explicit integration of formal risk management, employing the Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR) as a quantitative metric to filter designs that exhibit a high probability of failing to meet performance requirements, specifically the target coefficient of lift. The framework automates labor-intensive initial exploration through a global sensitivity analysis conducted by the Analyst agent, which generates actionable heuristics to guide the other agents. The process culminates by presenting the human Manager with a curated final set of promising design candidates, augmented with high-fidelity Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations. This approach effectively leverages AI to handle high-volume analytical tasks, thereby enhancing the decision-making capability of the human expert in selecting the final, risk-assessed design.

</details>


### [10] [The Query Channel: Information-Theoretic Limits of Masking-Based Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16689)
*Erciyes Karakaya,Ozgur Ercetin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper frames post-hoc explanation methods like SHAP/LIME as a communication problem where explanations are messages sent through query channels, derives information-theoretic limits on explanation recovery, and shows current methods fail within achievable bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing post-hoc explanation methods (KernelSHAP, LIME) estimate feature importance via randomized queries, but there's no theoretical understanding of their fundamental limits or conditions for reliable explanation recovery.

Method: Formulates explanation as communication over query channels with latent explanations as messages. Uses information theory to derive capacity limits, strong converse theorem for exact recovery, and achievability via sparse maximum-likelihood decoder. Benchmarks using Monte Carlo mutual information estimation.

Result: Derived information-theoretic capacity for explanation recovery, showing standard methods (Lasso/OLS-based surrogates) fail within achievable bounds. Identified that Gaussian noise and nonlinear curvature degrade query channels, creating waterfall/error-floor behaviors that make high-resolution explanations unattainable.

Conclusion: Information theory provides fundamental limits on explanation reliability, revealing current post-hoc methods are suboptimal. The communication framework explains why high-resolution explanations fail and suggests redesigning query channels for better performance.

Abstract: Masking-based post-hoc explanation methods, such as KernelSHAP and LIME, estimate local feature importance by querying a black-box model under randomized perturbations. This paper formulates this procedure as communication over a query channel, where the latent explanation acts as a message and each masked evaluation is a channel use. Within this framework, the complexity of the explanation is captured by the entropy of the hypothesis class, while the query interface supplies information at a rate determined by an identification capacity per query. We derive a strong converse showing that, if the explanation rate exceeds this capacity, the probability of exact recovery necessarily converges to one in error for any sequence of explainers and decoders. We also prove an achievability result establishing that a sparse maximum-likelihood decoder attains reliable recovery when the rate lies below capacity. A Monte Carlo estimator of mutual information yields a non-asymptotic query benchmark that we use to compare optimal decoding with Lasso- and OLS-based procedures that mirror LIME and KernelSHAP. Experiments reveal a range of query budgets where information theory permits reliable explanations but standard convex surrogates still fail. Finally, we interpret super-pixel resolution and tokenization for neural language models as a source-coding choice that sets the entropy of the explanation and show how Gaussian noise and nonlinear curvature degrade the query channel, induce waterfall and error-floor behavior, and render high-resolution explanations unattainable.

</details>


### [11] [RankGuide: Tensor-Rank-Guided Routing and Steering for Efficient Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16694)
*Jiayi Tian,Yupeng Su,Ryan Solgi,Souvik Kundu,Zheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RankGuide improves small-large reasoning model collaboration efficiency using tensor-rank signals for routing and steering, achieving 1.75x latency reduction while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large reasoning models (LRMs) generate explicit chain-of-thought reasoning but have high inference latency. Collaborative paradigms with small reasoning models (SRMs) offer better accuracy-latency trade-offs, but effectively detecting and mitigating SRM failures remains challenging.

Method: Analyzes SRM inference in text and hidden-state spaces, identifies three failure modes (overconfidence, uncertainty, heavy revalidation). Proposes RankGuide framework with tensor-rank-guided routing and steering: uses tensor-rank signals from consecutive hidden states to detect SRM failures and selectively invoke LRMs; introduces tensor-rank-filtered steering vector extraction to modulate SRM reasoning trajectories.

Result: Experiments on multiple reasoning benchmarks show RankGuide reduces latency by up to 1.75× compared to LRMs while maintaining competitive accuracy relative to prior methods.

Conclusion: RankGuide demonstrates that tensor-rank signals can effectively improve SRM-LRM collaboration efficiency through better failure detection and reasoning trajectory modulation, achieving significant latency reductions without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) enhance problem-solving capabilities by generating explicit multi-step chains of thought (CoT) reasoning; however, they incur substantial inference latency and computational overhead. To mitigate this issue, recent works have explored model collaboration paradigms, where small reasoning models (SRMs) generate intermediate reasoning steps to achieve a better accuracy--latency trade-off. Despite recent progress, effectively and efficiently detecting and mitigating SRM failures in collaborative systems remains a key challenge. To address this issue, we analyze SRM inference in both the generated text and hidden-state spaces, and identify three types of failure modes: \textit{overconfidence}, \textit{uncertainty}, and \textit{heavy revalidation}. Building on these insights, we propose \textbf{RankGuide}, a framework that improves the efficiency and effectiveness of SRM--LRM collaboration through tensor-rank-guided routing and steering. Specifically, RankGuide leverages a routing signal that incorporates tensor-rank signals derived from consecutive hidden states to detect when SRMs are likely to fail and selectively invoke LRMs. In addition, we introduce a tensor-rank-filtered steering vector extraction method to modulate the reasoning trajectory of SRMs, thereby improving their generation quality. By improving both routing and steering through tensor-rank signals, RankGuide enables SRM--LRM collaborative systems to achieve more efficient reasoning with fewer steps and improved accuracy. Experiments on multiple reasoning benchmarks demonstrate the efficacy of RankGuide in reducing latency by up to $1.75\times$ compared to LRM, while maintaining competitive accuracy relative to prior methods.

</details>


### [12] [Evaluating Tool-Using Language Agents: Judge Reliability, Propagation Cascades, and Runtime Mitigation in AgentProp-Bench](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16706)
*Bhaskar Gurram*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentProp-Bench is a comprehensive 2,000-task benchmark for evaluating tool-using LLM agents, revealing that automated evaluation methods perform poorly compared to human annotation and showing how parameter-level injections propagate to wrong answers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is widespread but unvalidated assumption that automated evaluation of tool-using LLM agents is reliable, with little validation against human annotation. The researchers aim to systematically test this assumption and understand error propagation in agent systems.

Method: The researchers created AgentProp-Bench with 2,000 tasks, 2,300 traces across four domains, using nine production LLMs and a 100-label human-validated subset. They quantified judge reliability, characterized error propagation, evaluated runtime mitigation strategies, and tested parameter-level injections in agent systems.

Result: Substring-based judging showed near-chance-level agreement with humans (kappa=0.049), while a three-LLM ensemble achieved moderate agreement (kappa=0.432). Parameter-level injections propagate to wrong answers with ~62% probability. Rejection and recovery capabilities were independent. A runtime interceptor reduced hallucinations by 23% on GPT-4o-mini but had no significant effect on Gemini-2.0-Flash due to its aggressive parameter rejection.

Conclusion: Automated evaluation of tool-using LLM agents is unreliable compared to human validation, parameter-level errors propagate significantly through agent systems, and different LLMs have distinct error mitigation capabilities. The benchmark and findings highlight the need for human-validated evaluation in agent research.

Abstract: Automated evaluation of tool-using large language model (LLM) agents is widely assumed to be reliable, but this assumption has rarely been validated against human annotation. We introduce AgentProp-Bench, a 2,000-task benchmark with 2,300 traces across four domains, nine production LLMs, and a 100-label human-validated subset. We quantify judge reliability, characterize error propagation, and evaluate a runtime mitigation. Substring-based judging agrees with human annotation at kappa=0.049 (chance-level); a three-LLM ensemble reaches kappa=0.432 (moderate) with a conservative bias. Under validated evaluation, a parameter-level injection propagates to a wrong final answer with human-calibrated probability approximately 0.62 (range 0.46-0.73 across models). Rejection (catching bad parameters) and recovery (correcting after acceptance) are independent model capabilities (Spearman rho=0.126, p=0.747). A tuned runtime interceptor reduces hallucination on GPT-4o-mini by 23.0 percentage points under a concurrent n=600 control, but shows no significant effect on Gemini-2.0-Flash, whose aggressive parameter rejection eliminates the target failure mode. All code, data, traces, and human labels are released at https://github.com/bhaskargurram-ai/agenthallu-bench.

</details>


### [13] [Debate as Reward: A Multi-Agent Reward System for Scientific Ideation via RL Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16723)
*Moein Salimi,Babak Hosseini Mohtasham,Amin Aghakasiri,Mahdi Naieni,Amir Hossein Qeysarbeigi,Mohammad Masih Shalchian Nazer,Zahra Azar,Mahdi Jafari Siavoshani,Mohammad Hossein Rohban*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RL framework with multi-agent reward function for scientific idea generation reduces reward hacking and outperforms SOTA baselines on novelty, feasibility, and effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM approaches for scientific ideation suffer from hallucination and computational inefficiency. RL faces reward hacking problems where models exploit imperfect evaluation proxies without producing genuine innovation.

Method: Propose RL framework with first multi-agent reward function serving as a judge, decoupling methodological validation from implementation details with strict binary rewards robust to reward hacking. Use unbiased variant of Group Relative Policy Optimization to mitigate length bias, trained on ICLR-320 dataset.

Result: Framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across expert-evaluated metrics of novelty, feasibility, and effectiveness.

Conclusion: The RL framework with specialized multi-agent reward function effectively addresses reward hacking in scientific ideation and improves quality of generated scientific ideas across key dimensions.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated potential in automating scientific ideation, yet current approaches relying on iterative prompting or complex multi-agent architectures often suffer from hallucination or computational inefficiency. A critical bottleneck in applying Reinforcement Learning (RL) to this open-ended domain is reward hacking -- where models exploit imperfect evaluation proxies to maximize scores without producing genuine scientific innovation. To address these limitations, we propose an RL framework explicitly tailored for high-quality scientific idea generation. We propose the first multi-agent reward function designed to serve as a judge, decoupling methodological validation from implementation details while providing strict binary rewards that are robust to reward hacking. To effectively optimize against this sparse signal, we utilize an unbiased variant of Group Relative Policy Optimization to mitigate artificial length bias. We grounded our training in ICLR-320, a curated dataset of problem-solution pairs extracted from ICLR 2024 proceedings. Experiments demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across expert-evaluated metrics of novelty, feasibility, and effectiveness.

</details>


### [14] [LLM-Guided Strategy Synthesis for Scalable Equality Saturation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17364)
*Chenyun Yin,Youwei Xiao,Yuze Luo,Yuyang Zou,Yun Liang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EggMind is an LLM-guided framework for synthesizing reusable equality saturation strategies, improving resource-quality trade-offs by 45.1% cost reduction and 69.1% RAM reduction compared to full EqSat.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual strategy design for equality saturation (EqSat) is a major bottleneck, and while rule-synthesis frameworks can generate large rewrite vocabularies, they exacerbate e-graph explosion. LLMs show promise for automated strategy synthesis but lack reusable abstractions and actionable feedback.

Method: Introduces EggMind with EqSatL domain-specific language for representing strategies as explicit artifacts, and an LLM-guided agentic workflow with novel techniques including proof-derived rewrite motif caching and tractability guidance to search efficiently while maintaining stability.

Result: EggMind substantially improves resource-quality trade-off on vectorization benchmarks (45.1% final cost reduction, 69.1% peak RAM reduction vs full EqSat), transfers effectively to XLA-based tensor compiler, and shows practical potential in logic-synthesis case studies.

Conclusion: EggMind successfully addresses the automated strategy synthesis problem for equality saturation by combining domain-specific language representation with LLM-guided workflow, enabling practical automation of e-graph-based compilers with significant performance improvements.

Abstract: Equality saturation (EqSat) is a powerful optimization paradigm that compactly represents many equivalent programs in an e-graph and delays commitment until extraction selects a lowest-cost program. Making EqSat effective, therefore, requires not only domain-specific rewrite rules but also domain-specific strategies. Today, much of this strategy design is still manual, making it a major obstacle to automating e-graph-based compilers. Recent rule-synthesis frameworks can automatically infer large rewrite vocabularies from semantic specifications, but they also enlarge the rewrite space and further exacerbate e-graph explosion. Although large language models (LLMs) make automated strategy synthesis plausible, directly evolving backend code remains ineffective in practice. The search lacks reusable strategy abstractions and actionable feedback, and can easily trigger e-graph explosion or converge to poor designs.
  We present EggMind, an LLM-guided, end-to-end framework for synthesizing reusable EqSat strategies. At its core, EggMind introduces a domain-specific language, EqSatL, to represent EqSat strategies as explicit and inspectable artifacts. It then proposes an LLM-guided agentic workflow, equipped with novel techniques including proof-derived rewrite motif caching and tractability guidance, to search efficiently for high-quality strategies while keeping synthesis stable under e-graph growth. Evaluation shows that EggMind substantially improves the resource-quality trade-off on vectorization benchmarks, reducing final cost by 45.1% and peak RAM by 69.1% relative to full EqSat. We further show that the same methodology transfers effectively to an XLA-based tensor compiler, and demonstrate its practical potential in a logic-synthesis case study with augmented rewrite spaces.

</details>


### [15] [When Agents Go Quiet: Output Generation Capacity and Format-Cost Separation for LLM Document Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16736)
*Justice Owusu Agyemang,Michael Agyare,Miriam Kobbinah,Nathaniel Agbugblah,Prosper Addo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a theoretical framework to explain and prevent "output stalling" in LLM-powered coding agents, where agents silently produce empty responses when generating large, format-heavy documents. The framework includes Output Generation Capacity (OGC), Format-Cost Separation Theorem, and Adaptive Strategy Selection, validated through experiments showing 48-72% token reduction and elimination of output stalling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-powered coding agents suffer from a poorly understood failure mode called "output stalling" - they silently produce empty responses when attempting to generate large, format-heavy documents. This failure undermines the reliability and effectiveness of coding agents in real-world applications where complex document generation is required.

Method: Three key contributions: (1) Output Generation Capacity (OGC) - a formal measure of an agent's effective ability to produce output given its current context state, distinct from raw context window. (2) Format-Cost Separation Theorem - proves deferred template rendering is always at least as token-efficient as direct generation for formats with overhead multiplier μ_f > 1, with tight savings bounds. (3) Adaptive Strategy Selection - a decision framework that maps output cost/OGC ratio to optimal generation strategy (direct, chunked, or deferred). Validated through experiments across three models, four document types, and ablation studies.

Result: Deferred rendering reduces LLM generation tokens by 48-72% across all conditions and eliminates output stalling entirely. The framework was instantiated as GEN-PILOT, an open-source MCP server, demonstrating direct practical applicability.

Conclusion: The theoretical framework successfully explains and prevents output stalling in LLM coding agents. The combination of OGC measurement, format-cost separation theorem, and adaptive strategy selection provides a comprehensive solution that significantly reduces token usage while eliminating the silent failure mode, with practical implementation as a working tool.

Abstract: LLM-powered coding agents suffer from a poorly understood failure mode we term output stalling: the agent silently produces empty responses when attempting to generate large, format-heavy documents. We present a theoretical framework that explains and prevents this failure through three contributions. (1) We introduce Output Generation Capacity (OGC), a formal measure of an agent's effective ability to produce output given its current context state - distinct from and empirically smaller than the raw context window. (2) We prove a Format-Cost Separation Theorem showing that deferred template rendering is always at least as token-efficient as direct generation for any format with overhead multiplier $μ_f > 1$, and derive tight bounds on the savings. (3) We formalize Adaptive Strategy Selection, a decision framework that maps the ratio of estimated output cost to available OGC into an optimal generation strategy (direct, chunked, or deferred). We validate the theory through controlled experiments across three models (Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GPT-4o, Llama 3.1 70B), four document types, and an ablation study isolating each component's contribution. Deferred rendering reduces LLM generation tokens by 48-72% across all conditions and eliminates output stalling entirely. We instantiate the framework as GEN-PILOT, an open-source MCP server, demonstrating that the theory translates directly into a practical tool.

</details>


### [16] [SkillGraph: Self-Evolving Multi-Agent Collaboration with Multimodal Graph Topology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17503)
*Zheng Nie,Ruolin Shen,Xinlei Yu,Bo Yin,Jiangning Zhang,Xiaobin Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillGraph is a framework that dynamically evolves both agent expertise and communication topology in Visual Multiagent Systems, addressing limitations of fixed topologies and static reasoning abilities through a Multimodal Graph Transformer and self-evolving Skill Bank.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current visual multiagent systems have two coupled problems: 1) fixed communication topologies that don't adapt to visual content or query context, and 2) static agent reasoning abilities during deployment. These issues reinforce each other - rigid topologies can't leverage agent expertise while static agents lack specialization incentives.

Method: SkillGraph introduces a joint framework with two components: 1) Multimodal Graph Transformer (MMGT) that encodes visual tokens, instruction semantics, and skill embeddings to predict query-conditioned collaboration graphs, enabling dynamic, content-aware information flow; 2) Skill Designer that distills and refines reasoning heuristics from failure cases to build a self-evolving multimodal Skill Bank. Updated skill embeddings feed back into MMGT, allowing topology to adapt with capability growth.

Result: SkillGraph achieves consistent improvements across four benchmarks, five common MAS structures, and four base models, demonstrating effectiveness in evolving both agent expertise and communication topology.

Conclusion: SkillGraph successfully addresses the coupled problems of rigid communication topologies and static agent reasoning in visual multiagent systems through a framework that jointly evolves agent expertise and communication topology, enabling dynamic adaptation to visual content and query contexts.

Abstract: Scaling vision-language models into Visual Multiagent Systems (VMAS) is hindered by two coupled issues. First, communication topologies are fixed before inference, leaving them blind to visual content and query context; second, agent reasoning abilities remain static during deployment. These issues reinforce each other: a rigid topology fails to leverage richer agent expertise, while static agents lack incentives to specialize for a given query. We address this with SkillGraph, a joint framework that evolves both agent expertise and communication topology. Within this framework, a Multimodal Graph Transformer (MMGT) encodes visual tokens, instruction semantics and active skill embeddings to predict a query-conditioned collaboration graph, replacing hand-crafted routing with dynamic, content-aware information flow. Complementing this, a Skill Designer distills and refines reasoning heuristics from failure cases, constructing a self-evolving multimodal Skill Bank. Crucially, updated skill embeddings are fed back into the MMGT, enabling the topology to adapt alongside capability growth. Experiments show that SkillGraph achieves consistent improvements across four benchmarks, five common MAS structures and four base models. Code is available at https://github.com/niez233/skillgraph.

</details>


### [17] [CT Open: An Open-Access, Uncontaminated, Live Platform for the Open Challenge of Clinical Trial Outcome Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16742)
*Jianyou Wang,Youze Zheng,Longtian Bao,Hanyuan Zhang,Qirui Zheng,Yuhan Chen,Yang Zhang,Matthew Feng,Maxim Khan,Aditya K. Sehgal,Christopher D. Rosin,Ramamohan Paturi,Umber Dube,Leon Bergen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CT Open is an open-access platform for clinical trial outcome prediction challenges that uses an automated LLM-powered pipeline to ensure predictions are evaluated only on trials whose outcomes weren't publicly known at submission time, creating a reliable benchmark for forecasting real-world events.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the fundamental challenge of predicting real-world events before they happen, focusing on clinical trial outcomes as a high-stakes domain where even experts struggle. There's a need for reliable AI forecasting systems and standardized evaluation platforms that ensure fair assessment by preventing contamination from publicly available outcomes.

Method: The authors introduce CT Open, a platform that runs four prediction challenges annually. Their key innovation is a fully automated decontamination pipeline that uses iterative LLM-powered web search to identify the earliest mention of trial outcomes, ensuring submissions are only evaluated on trials whose outcomes weren't publicly reported at submission time. The pipeline is validated by human expert annotations. They release training data and time-stamped test benchmarks (Winter 2025, Summer 2025).

Result: CT Open provides an operational platform (https://ct-open.net/) with validated decontamination pipeline, training dataset, and two test benchmarks. The platform enables participants to use any methodology and data source while ensuring fair evaluation, establishing a central hub for forecasting research.

Conclusion: CT Open serves as a pioneering platform that not only advances AI research on real-world event forecasting but also has practical implications for biomedical research and clinical trial design. By solving the decontamination problem through automated LLM-powered web search, it enables reliable evaluation of prediction systems while maintaining flexibility for participants.

Abstract: Scientists have long sought to accurately predict outcomes of real-world events before they happen. Can AI systems do so more reliably? We study this question through clinical trial outcome prediction, a high-stakes open challenge even for domain experts. We introduce CT Open, an open-access, live platform that will run four challenge every year. Anyone can submit predictions for each challenge. CT Open evaluates those submissions on trials whose outcomes were not yet public at the time of submission but were made public afterwards. Determining if a trial's outcome is public on the internet before a certain date is surprisingly difficult. Outcomes posted on official registries may lag behind by years, while the first mention may appear in obscure articles. To address this, we propose a novel, fully automated decontamination pipeline that uses iterative LLM-powered web search to identify the earliest mention of trial outcomes. We validate the pipeline's quality and accuracy by human expert's annotations. Since CT Open's pipeline ensures that every evaluated trial had no publicly reported outcome when the prediction was made, it allows participants to use any methodology and any data source. In this paper, we release a training set and two time-stamped test benchmarks, Winter 2025 and Summer 2025. We believe CT Open can serve as a central hub for advancing AI research on forecasting real-world outcomes before they occur, while also informing biomedical research and improving clinical trial design. CT Open Platform is hosted at $\href{https://ct-open.net/}{https://ct-open.net/}$

</details>


### [18] [SafeAgent: A Runtime Protection Architecture for Agentic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17562)
*Hailin Liu,Eugene Ilyushin,Jie Ni,Min Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SafeAgent is a runtime security architecture for LLM agents that separates execution governance from semantic risk reasoning to protect against multi-step prompt-injection attacks across agent workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents are vulnerable to prompt-injection attacks that propagate through multi-step workflows, tool interactions, and persistent context. Traditional input-output filtering methods are insufficient for reliable protection against these complex, stateful attacks.

Method: SafeAgent treats agent safety as a stateful decision problem with two coordinated components: (1) a runtime controller that mediates actions around the agent loop, and (2) a context-aware decision core formalized as a context-aware advanced machine intelligence. The core includes operators for risk encoding, utility-cost evaluation, consequence modeling, policy arbitration, and state synchronization.

Result: Experiments on Agent Security Bench (ASB) and InjecAgent show SafeAgent consistently improves robustness over baseline and text-level guardrail methods while maintaining competitive benign-task performance. Ablation studies reveal that recovery confidence and policy weighting determine distinct safety-utility operating points.

Conclusion: SafeAgent demonstrates that treating agent safety as a stateful decision problem with separated execution governance and semantic risk reasoning provides effective protection against complex prompt-injection attacks while maintaining utility, offering a practical architecture for runtime security in LLM agent systems.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are vulnerable to prompt-injection attacks that propagate through multi-step workflows, tool interactions, and persistent context, making input-output filtering alone insufficient for reliable protection. This paper presents SafeAgent, a runtime security architecture that treats agent safety as a stateful decision problem over evolving interaction trajectories. The proposed design separates execution governance from semantic risk reasoning through two coordinated components: a runtime controller that mediates actions around the agent loop and a context-aware decision core that operates over persistent session state. The core is formalized as a context-aware advanced machine intelligence and instantiated through operators for risk encoding, utility-cost evaluation, consequence modeling, policy arbitration, and state synchronization. Experiments on Agent Security Bench (ASB) and InjecAgent show that SafeAgent consistently improves robustness over baseline and text-level guardrail methods while maintaining competitive benign-task performance. Ablation studies further show that recovery confidence and policy weighting determine distinct safety-utility operating points.

</details>


### [19] [Why Training-Free Token Reduction Collapses: The Inherent Instability of Pairwise Scoring Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16745)
*Yang Shanglin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper explains why training-free token reduction methods for Vision Transformers collapse at high compression by developing a diagnostic framework that identifies two key issues: inherent error amplification in layer-wise reduction and unstable pairwise similarity signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand why various training-free token reduction methods (ToMe, ToFu, PiToMe, MCTF) for Vision Transformers all exhibit similar cliff-like collapse at high compression ratios, despite using different scoring mechanisms. The authors want to identify the fundamental reasons behind this shared failure pattern.

Method: The authors developed a diagnostic framework with two analytical tools: ranking consistency (ρₛ) and off-diagonal correlation (ρₒff). They use these to decompose the collapse into two components: (1) a signal-agnostic error amplifier inherent to layer-wise reduction, and (2) the shared reliance on pairwise similarity signals whose rankings become unstable. They derive three design principles from this diagnosis and construct CATIS (constructive validation) as a solution based on these principles.

Result: CATIS achieves superior performance: on ViT-Large at 63% FLOPs reduction, it retains 96.9% of vanilla accuracy (81.0%) on ImageNet-1K, while all baseline methods collapse to 43-65% accuracy. The diagnostic analysis reveals that pairwise similarity signal rankings degrade from ρₛ=0.88 to 0.27 in deep layers, explaining the instability of existing methods.

Conclusion: The collapse of training-free token reduction methods at high compression stems from two fundamental issues: an inherent error amplification mechanism in layer-wise reduction and the instability of pairwise similarity signals. The proposed CATIS method, built on principles derived from this diagnosis (unary signals and triage suppression), effectively mitigates these problems and achieves dramatically better performance at high compression ratios.

Abstract: Training-free token reduction methods for Vision Transformers (ToMe, ToFu, PiToMe, and MCTF) employ different scoring mechanisms, yet they share a closely matched cliff-like collapse at high compression. This paper explains \emph{why}. We develop a diagnostic framework with two tools, ranking consistency $ρ_s$ and off-diagonal correlation $ρ_\text{off}$, that decomposes the collapse into (1)a signal-agnostic error amplifier inherent to layer-wise reduction, predicting convex Pareto curves and $r_{\text{crit}} \propto 1/L$; and (2)shared reliance on \emph{pairwise} similarity signals whose ranking consistency degrades from $ρ_s{=}0.88$ to $0.27$ in deep layers. Pairwise rankings are inherently unstable ($O(N_p^2)$ joint perturbations) while unary signals enjoy greater stability ($O(N_p)$ perturbations, CLT). From three design principles derived from this diagnosis, we construct CATIS as a constructive validation: unary signals raise the trigger threshold, triage suppresses the gain. On ViT-Large at 63% FLOPs reduction, CATIS retains 96.9% of vanilla accuracy (81.0%) on ImageNet-1K where all baselines collapse to 43--65%.

</details>


### [20] [Don't Start What You Can't Finish: A Counterfactual Audit of Support-State Triage in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16752)
*Eren Unlu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Frontier language models show strong latent triage capabilities but require explicit categorical decision paths to properly diagnose task support states and avoid overcommitment on incomplete/unsupported tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent evaluations focus too much on execution of fully specified tasks, while important aspects like clarification, capability awareness, abstention, and search termination are studied in isolation. There's a gap in understanding whether agents can properly diagnose why a task is blocked before taking action.

Method: Introduced Support-State Triage Audit (SSTA-32), a matched-item diagnostic framework using minimal counterfactual edits to test four support states. Evaluated frontier models under four prompting conditions (Direct, Action-Only, Confidence-Only, Preflight Support Check) using Dual-Persona Auto-Auditing with deterministic heuristic scoring.

Result: Default execution shows 41.7% overcommitment on non-complete tasks. Scalar confidence mapping avoids overcommitment but collapses three-way deferral space (58.3% typed deferral accuracy). Action-Only and Preflight Support Check achieve 91.7% typed deferral accuracy by surfacing categorical ontology in prompts. Targeted ablations show specific dimensions affect different support states.

Conclusion: Frontier models possess strong latent triage capabilities that require explicit categorical decision paths to activate safely. While results represent upper-bound capability estimates (due to Dual-Persona Auto-Auditing within single context), the structural findings indicate models can perform accurate support-state diagnosis when properly prompted.

Abstract: Current agent evaluations largely reward execution on fully specified tasks, while recent work studies clarification [11, 22, 2], capability awareness [9, 1], abstention [8, 14], and search termination [20, 5] mostly in isolation. This leaves open whether agents can diagnose why a task is blocked before acting. We introduce the Support-State Triage Audit (SSTA-32), a matched-item diagnostic framework in which minimal counterfactual edits flip the same base request across four support states: Complete (ANSWER), Clarifiable (CLARIFY), Support-Blocked (REQUEST SUPPORT), and Unsupported-Now (ABSTAIN). We evaluate a frontier model under four prompting conditions - Direct, Action-Only, Confidence-Only, and a typed Preflight Support Check (PSC) - using Dual-Persona Auto-Auditing (DPAA) with deterministic heuristic scoring. Default execution overcommits heavily on non-complete tasks (41.7% overcommitment rate). Scalar confidence mapping avoids overcommitment but collapses the three-way deferral space (58.3% typed deferral accuracy). Conversely, both Action-Only and PSC achieve 91.7% typed deferral accuracy by surfacing the categorical ontology in the prompt. Targeted ablations confirm that removing the support-sufficiency dimension selectively degrades REQUEST SUPPORT accuracy, while removing the evidence-sufficiency dimension triggers systematic overcommitment on unsupported items. Because DPAA operates within a single context window, these results represent upper-bound capability estimates; nonetheless, the structural findings indicate that frontier models possess strong latent triage capabilities that require explicit categorical decision paths to activate safely.

</details>


### [21] [TacticGen: Grounding Adaptable and Scalable Generation of Football Tactics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18210)
*Sheng Xu,Guiliang Liu,Tarak Kharrat,Yudong Luo,Mohamed Aloulou,Javier López Peña,Konstantin Sofeikov,Adam Reid,Paul Roberts,Steven Spencer,Joe Carnall,Ian McHale,Oliver Schulte,Hongyuan Zha,Wei-Shi Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TacticGen is a generative AI model that creates adaptable football tactics as sequences of multi-agent movements, trained on extensive professional data and capable of generating realistic, strategic tactics through context-aware diffusion transformers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current football analysis focuses mostly on predictive trajectory forecasting, but there's a gap in tactical design generation. While prediction shows what is likely to happen, tactical generation determines what should happen to achieve strategic objectives, which is essential for effective football strategy development.

Method: TacticGen formulates tactics as sequences of multi-agent movements and interactions conditioned on game context. It uses a multi-agent diffusion transformer with agent-wise self-attention and context-aware cross-attention to capture cooperative/competitive dynamics among players and the ball. The model is trained on over 3.3 million events and 100 million tracking frames from top-tier leagues, and employs classifier guidance for adaptable generation based on inference-time objectives specified via rules, natural language, or neural models.

Result: TacticGen achieves state-of-the-art precision in predicting player trajectories. It enables adaptable tactic generation tailored to diverse objectives and shows inherent scalability in modeling performance. A case study with football experts confirms that TacticGen generates realistic, strategically valuable tactics.

Conclusion: TacticGen represents a significant advancement in tactical planning for professional football, demonstrating practical utility through its ability to generate context-aware, adaptable tactics that can be guided by various objective specifications, bridging the gap between predictive analytics and strategic design.

Abstract: Success in association football relies on both individual skill and coordinated tactics. While recent advancements in spatio-temporal data and deep learning have enabled predictive analyses like trajectory forecasting, the development of tactical design remains limited. Bridging this gap is essential, as prediction reveals what is likely to occur, whereas tactic generation determines what should occur to achieve strategic objectives. In this work, we present TacticGen, a generative model for adaptable and scalable tactic generation. TacticGen formulates tactics as sequences of multi-agent movements and interactions conditioned on the game context. It employs a multi-agent diffusion transformer with agent-wise self-attention and context-aware cross-attention to capture cooperative and competitive dynamics among players and the ball. Trained with over 3.3 million events and 100 million tracking frames from top-tier leagues, TacticGen achieves state-of-the-art precision in predicting player trajectories. Building on it, TacticGen enables adaptable tactic generation tailored to diverse inference-time objectives through classifier guidance mechanism, specified via rules, natural language, or neural models. Its modeling performance is also inherently scalable. A case study with football experts confirms that TacticGen generates realistic, strategically valuable tactics, demonstrating its practical utility for tactical planning in professional football. The project page is available at: https://shengxu.net/TacticGen/.

</details>


### [22] [Know When to Trust the Skill: Delayed Appraisal and Epistemic Vigilance for Single-Agent LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16753)
*Eren Unlu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MESA-S introduces a metacognitive framework for autonomous agents that separates self-confidence from source-confidence to prevent context pollution and overthinking through delayed procedural probing and trust provenance tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional routing heuristics in autonomous agents integrated with large language models suffer from context pollution and "overthinking" issues. The bottleneck is identified as the absence of disciplined second-order metacognitive governance rather than deficiencies in algorithmic capability or skill diversity.

Method: MESA-S shifts scalar confidence estimation to a vector approach separating self-confidence (parametric certainty) from source-confidence (trust in retrieved external procedures). It formalizes a delayed procedural probe mechanism and introduces Metacognitive Skill Cards to decouple skill awareness from token-intensive execution.

Result: Evaluation via an In-Context Static Benchmark using Gemini 3.1 Pro suggests that explicitly programming trust provenance and delayed escalation mitigates supply-chain vulnerabilities, prunes unnecessary reasoning loops, and prevents offloading-induced confidence inflation.

Conclusion: MESA-S represents a scientifically cautious, behaviorally anchored step toward reliable, epistemically vigilant single-agent orchestration by implementing computational translations of human cognitive control mechanisms like delayed appraisal and epistemic vigilance.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) transition into autonomous agents integrated with extensive tool ecosystems, traditional routing heuristics increasingly succumb to context pollution and "overthinking". We argue that the bottleneck is not a deficit in algorithmic capability or skill diversity, but the absence of disciplined second-order metacognitive governance. In this paper, our scientific contribution focuses on the computational translation of human cognitive control - specifically, delayed appraisal, epistemic vigilance, and region-of-proximal offloading - into a single-agent architecture. We introduce MESA-S (Metacognitive Skills for Agents, Single-agent), a preliminary framework that shifts scalar confidence estimation into a vector separating self-confidence (parametric certainty) from source-confidence (trust in retrieved external procedures). By formalizing a delayed procedural probe mechanism and introducing Metacognitive Skill Cards, MESA-S decouples the awareness of a skill's utility from its token-intensive execution. Evaluated under an In-Context Static Benchmark Evaluation natively executed via Gemini 3.1 Pro, our early results suggest that explicitly programming trust provenance and delayed escalation mitigates supply-chain vulnerabilities, prunes unnecessary reasoning loops, and prevents offloading-induced confidence inflation. This architecture offers a scientifically cautious, behaviorally anchored step toward reliable, epistemically vigilant single-agent orchestration.

</details>


### [23] [Training and Agentic Inference Strategies for LLM-based Manim Animation Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18364)
*Ravidu Suien Rammuni Silva,Ahmad Lotfi,Isibor Kennedy Ihianle,Golnaz Shahtahmassebi,Jordan J. Bird*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ManimTrainer and ManimAgent pipelines combine SFT+GRPO training with Renderer-in-the-loop inference for text-to-Manim animation generation, achieving state-of-the-art results with 94% render success and 85.7% visual similarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with programmatic animation using Manim due to lack of spatial reasoning, temporal sequencing, and domain-specific API knowledge in pre-training data, plus missing systematic study of training-inference interactions.

Method: ManimTrainer pipeline: SFT + RL-based GRPO with unified code+visual reward signal. ManimAgent inference: Renderer-in-the-loop (RITL) and API documentation-augmented RITL (RITL-DOC). Evaluated 17 sub-30B LLMs across 9 training-inference combos using ManimBench.

Result: Qwen 3 Coder 30B with GRPO and RITL-DOC achieved highest performance: 94% Render Success Rate and 85.7% Visual Similarity, surpassing GPT-4.1 baseline by +3pp in VS. SFT improves code quality; GRPO enhances visual outputs and self-correction responsiveness.

Conclusion: First unified training-inference study for text-to-code-to-video with Manim shows complementary roles of training (SFT/GRPO) and agentic inference (RITL) strategies. Code-visual metric correlation strengthens with training but weakens with inference enhancements.

Abstract: Generating programmatic animation using libraries such as Manim presents unique challenges for Large Language Models (LLMs), requiring spatial reasoning, temporal sequencing, and familiarity with domain-specific APIs that are underrepresented in general pre-training data. A systematic study of how training and inference strategies interact in this setting is lacking in current research. This study introduces ManimTrainer, a training pipeline that combines Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT) with Reinforcement Learning (RL) based Group Relative Policy Optimisation (GRPO) using a unified reward signal that fuses code and visual assessment signals, and ManimAgent, an inference pipeline featuring Renderer-in-the-loop (RITL) and API documentation-augmented RITL (RITL-DOC) strategies. Using these techniques, this study presents the first unified training and inference study for text-to-code-to-video transformation with Manim. It evaluates 17 open-source sub-30B LLMs across nine combinations of training and inference strategies using ManimBench. Results show that SFT generally improves code quality, while GRPO enhances visual outputs and increases the models' responsiveness to extrinsic signals during self-correction at inference time. The Qwen 3 Coder 30B model with GRPO and RITL-DOC achieved the highest overall performance, with a 94% Render Success Rate (RSR) and 85.7% Visual Similarity (VS) to reference videos, surpassing the baseline GPT-4.1 model by +3 percentage points in VS. Additionally, the analysis shows that the correlation between code and visual metrics strengthens with SFT and GRPO but weakens with inference-time enhancements, highlighting the complementary roles of training and agentic inference strategies in Manim animation generation.

</details>


### [24] [Machine individuality: Separating genuine idiosyncrasy from response bias in large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16755)
*Valentin Kriegmair,Dirk U. Wulff*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs exhibit stable, stimulus-specific individual differences ("machine individuality") that cannot be explained by response biases or stochastic noise, as shown through crossed random-effects models on 74.9 million ratings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs become integrated into high-stakes and daily life applications, understanding their behavioral dispositions is critical. While psychometric inventories are used to profile LLMs, existing approaches cannot determine whether behavioral differences reflect genuine individuality versus noise or biases.

Method: Applied crossed random-effects models (widely used in psychometrics) to analyze 74.9 million ratings from 10 open-weight LLMs on over 100,000 words across 14 psycholinguistic norms, allowing separation of systematic effects from noise.

Result: On average, 16.9% of variance is attributable to stimulus-specific individuality, significantly exceeding statistical null models. Cross-norm prediction analyses reveal this individuality forms a coherent, unique fingerprint for each model.

Conclusion: LLMs exhibit stable individual differences called "machine individuality" that cannot be attributed to response biases or stochastic noise, representing genuine behavioral dispositions.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into daily life, in roles ranging from high-stakes decision support to companionship, understanding their behavioral dispositions becomes critical.
