A growing literature uses psychometric inventories and cognitive paradigms to profile LLM dispositions. However, these approaches cannot determine whether behavioral differences reflect stable, stimulus-specific individuality or global response biases and stochastic noise. Here, we apply crossed random-effects models -- widely used in psychometrics to separate systematic effects -- to 74.9 million ratings provided by 10 open-weight LLMs for over 100,000 words across 14 psycholinguistic norms. On average, 16.9% of variance is attributable to stimulus-specific individuality, robustly exceeding a statistical null model. Cross-norm prediction analyses reveal this individuality as a coherent fingerprint, unique to each model. These results identify individual differences among LLMs that cannot be attributed to response biases or stochastic noise. We term these differences machine individuality.

</details>


### [25] [SAVE: A Generalizable Framework for Multi-Condition Single-Cell Generation with Gene Block Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16776)
*Jiahao Li,Jiayi Dong,Peng Ye,Xiaochi Zhou,Haohai Lu,Fei Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SAVE is a Transformer-based generative framework that groups genes into blocks to capture biological relationships for multi-condition single-cell data modeling, outperforming existing methods in generation fidelity and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing single-cell modeling methods treat genes as independent tokens, ignoring their biological relationships and leading to poor performance. There's a need for a unified framework that can model gene expression across diverse conditions while capturing higher-order dependencies.

Method: SAVE uses a conditional Transformer framework with coarse-grained representation by grouping semantically related genes into blocks to capture gene module dependencies. It incorporates Flow Matching mechanism and condition-masking strategy for flexible simulation and generalization to unseen condition combinations.

Result: SAVE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in conditional generation, batch effect correction, and perturbation prediction benchmarks. It shows superior generation fidelity and extrapolative generalization, especially in low-resource or combinatorially held-out settings.

Conclusion: SAVE provides a scalable and generalizable solution for modeling complex single-cell data, with broad utility in virtual cell synthesis and biological interpretation. The framework effectively captures gene relationships and generalizes to unseen conditions.

Abstract: Modeling single-cell gene expression across diverse biological and technical conditions is crucial for characterizing cellular states and simulating unseen scenarios. Existing methods often treat genes as independent tokens, overlooking their high-level biological relationships and leading to poor performance. We introduce SAVE, a unified generative framework based on conditional Transformers for multi-condition single-cell modeling. SAVE leverages a coarse-grained representation by grouping semantically related genes into blocks, capturing higher-order dependencies among gene modules. A Flow Matching mechanism and condition-masking strategy further enhance flexible simulation and enable generalization to unseen condition combinations. We evaluate SAVE on a range of benchmarks, including conditional generation, batch effect correction, and perturbation prediction. SAVE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in generation fidelity and extrapolative generalization, especially in low-resource or combinatorially held-out settings. Overall, SAVE offers a scalable and generalizable solution for modeling complex single-cell data, with broad utility in virtual cell synthesis and biological interpretation. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/fdu-wangfeilab/sc-save

</details>


### [26] [Introspection Adapters: Training LLMs to Report Their Learned Behaviors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16812)
*Keshav Shenoy,Li Yang,Abhay Sheshadri,Sören Mindermann,Jack Lindsey,Sam Marks,Rowan Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introspection Adapters (IAs) enable fine-tuned LLMs to self-describe their learned behaviors, providing a scalable audit method that generalizes across different fine-tuning approaches and detects concerning behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When LLMs are fine-tuned, they can develop unexpected, harmful, or hard-to-detect behaviors. Current audit methods lack scalability and transparency, making it difficult to identify what models have learned during fine-tuning.

Method: Create labeled training data by fine-tuning base models with implanted behaviors, then train a single LoRA adapter (Introspection Adapter) across these fine-tuned models to make them verbalize their implanted behaviors in natural language.

Result: IAs generalize effectively to detect behaviors in models trained differently from training examples, achieving state-of-the-art performance on AuditBench, detecting encrypted finetuning API attacks, and scaling well with model size and data diversity.

Conclusion: Introspection Adapters provide a scalable, effective, and practical approach to auditing fine-tuned LLMs by enabling models to self-describe their learned behaviors, improving transparency and safety monitoring.

Abstract: When model developers or users fine-tune an LLM, this can induce behaviors that are unexpected, deliberately harmful, or hard to detect. It would be far easier to audit LLMs if they could simply describe their behaviors in natural language. Here, we study a scalable approach to rapidly identify learned behaviors of many LLMs derived from a shared base LLM. Given a model $M$, our method works by finetuning models $M_i$ from $M$ with implanted behaviors $b_i$; the $(M_i, b_i)$ pairs serve as labeled training data. We then train an \emph{introspection adapter} (IA): a single LoRA adapter jointly trained across the finetunes $M_i$ to cause them to verbalize their implanted behaviors. We find that this IA induces self-description of learned behaviors even in finetunes of $M$ that were trained in very different ways from the $M_i$. For example, IAs generalize to AuditBench, achieving state-of-the-art at identifying explicitly hidden concerning behaviors. IAs can also be used to detect encrypted finetuning API attacks. They scale favorably with model size and training data diversity. Overall, our results suggest that IAs are a scalable, effective, and practically useful approach to auditing fine-tuned LLMs.

</details>


### [27] [PersonalHomeBench: Evaluating Agents in Personalized Smart Homes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16813)
*Nikhil Verma,InJung Yang,Sungil Kim,KoKeun Kim,YoungJoon Kim,Manasa Bharadwaj,Yolanda Liu,Kevin Ferreira*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PersonalHomeBench is a benchmark for evaluating foundation models as agentic AI assistants in personalized smart home environments, featuring rich household states, realistic tasks, and comprehensive tools for testing both reactive and proactive capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic AI systems are advancing toward real-world applications but their readiness in complex, personalized environments is not well characterized, creating a gap in understanding how these systems perform in realistic smart home scenarios.

Method: The authors developed PersonalHomeBench through an iterative process that builds rich household states, generates personalized context-dependent tasks, and provides PersonalHomeTools for realistic agent-environment interaction including household information retrieval, appliance control, and situational understanding.

Result: Experiments show systematic performance reduction as task complexity increases, with pronounced failures in counterfactual reasoning and under partial observability where effective tool-based information gathering is required.

Conclusion: PersonalHomeBench serves as a rigorous evaluation platform for analyzing the robustness and limitations of personalized agentic reasoning and planning, positioning it as an important tool for advancing agentic AI in real-world applications.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems are rapidly advancing toward real-world applications, yet their readiness in complex and personalized environments remains insufficiently characterized. To address this gap, we introduce PersonalHomeBench, a benchmark for evaluating foundation models as agentic assistants in personalized smart home environments. The benchmark is constructed through an iterative process that progressively builds rich household states, which are then used to generate personalized, context-dependent tasks. To support realistic agent-environment interaction, we provide PersonalHomeTools, a comprehensive toolbox enabling household information retrieval, appliance control, and situational understanding. PersonalHomeBench evaluates both reactive and proactive agentic abilities under unimodal and multimodal observations. Thorough experimentation reveals a systematic performance reduction as task complexity increases, with pronounced failures in counterfactual reasoning and under partial observability, where effective tool-based information gathering is required. These results position PersonalHomeBench as a rigorous evaluation platform for analyzing the robustness and limitations of personalized agentic reasoning and planning.

</details>


### [28] [The CTLNet for Shanghai Composite Index Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16835)
*Haibin Jiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A novel CNN-Transformer-LSTM Networks (CTLNet) model is proposed for Shanghai Composite Index prediction, combining strengths of CNN, Transformer, and LSTM architectures to outperform state-of-the-art baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Shanghai Composite Index prediction is a hot issue for investors and researchers. While various deep learning models (RNN, CNN, transformers) are used for multivariate time series forecasting, the Transformer encoder with its attention mechanism and parallel processing capabilities shows advantages in handling long sequence dependencies and multivariate correlations, prompting the need for a model that combines the strengths of different architectures.

Method: Proposed CNN-Transformer-LSTM Networks (CTLNet) that integrates three architectures: CNN for feature extraction, Transformer encoder for attention-based sequence modeling and handling long-range dependencies, and LSTM for temporal pattern learning. The model leverages CNN's local pattern recognition, Transformer's global attention and parallel processing, and LSTM's sequential memory capabilities.

Result: Comparative experiments show that CTLNet outperforms state-of-the-art baselines for Shanghai Composite Index prediction. The model demonstrates superior performance in multivariate time series forecasting by effectively combining the complementary strengths of CNN, Transformer, and LSTM architectures.

Conclusion: The proposed CTLNet model successfully addresses Shanghai Composite Index prediction by synergistically combining CNN, Transformer, and LSTM architectures. This hybrid approach provides an effective solution for financial time series forecasting, particularly for handling long sequence dependencies and multivariate correlations in stock market indices.

Abstract: Shanghai Composite Index prediction has become a hot issue for many investors and academic researchers. Deep learning models are widely applied in multivariate time series forecasting, including recurrent neural networks (RNN), convolutional neural networks (CNN), and transformers. Specifically, the Transformer encoder, with its unique attention mechanism and parallel processing capabilities, has become an important tool in time series prediction, and has an advantage in dealing with long sequence dependencies and multivariate data correlations. Drawing on the strengths of various models, we propose the CNN-Transformer-LSTM Networks (CTLNet). This paper explores the application of CTLNet for Shanghai Composite Index prediction and the comparative experiments show that the proposed model outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [29] [GAMMA-Net: Adaptive Long-Horizon Traffic Spatio-Temporal Forecasting Model based on Interleaved Graph Attention and Multi-Axis Mamba](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16859)
*Dongyi He,Yuanquan Gao,Bin Jiang,He Yan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GAMMA-Net combines Graph Attention Networks (GAT) with multi-axis Selective State Space Models (Mamba) to better capture spatio-temporal dependencies in traffic forecasting, achieving state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional traffic forecasting models fail to adequately capture the intricate spatio-temporal dependencies present in traffic data, which is crucial for effective traffic management, congestion reduction, and urban planning.

Method: The proposed GAMMA-Net integrates two key components: 1) Graph Attention Networks (GAT) with self-attention mechanisms to dynamically adjust node influence and model adaptive spatial dependencies, and 2) multi-axis Selective State Space Models (Mamba) to efficiently capture long-term temporal and spatial dynamics without heavy computational costs.

Result: Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets (METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03-08) show GAMMA-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models across different prediction horizons, achieving up to 16.25% reduction in Mean Absolute Error compared to baselines. Ablation studies confirm the critical contributions of both spatial and temporal components.

Conclusion: GAMMA-Net sets a new standard in traffic forecasting by effectively capturing spatio-temporal dependencies, offering a powerful tool for next-generation traffic management and urban planning. The model demonstrates the complementary importance of both spatial and temporal modeling components.

Abstract: Accurate traffic forecasting is crucial for intelligent transportation systems, supporting effective traffic management, congestion reduction, and informed urban planning. However, traditional models often fail to adequately capture the intricate spatio-temporal dependencies present in traffic data. To overcome these limitations, we introduce GAMMA-Net, a novel approach that integrates Graph Attention Networks (GAT) with multi-axis Selective State Space Models (Mamba). The GAT component uses a self-attention mechanism to dynamically adjust the influence of nodes within the traffic network, enabling adaptive spatial dependency modeling based on real-time conditions. Simultaneously, the Mamba module efficiently models long-term temporal and spatial dynamics without the heavy computational cost of conventional recurrent architectures. Extensive experiments on several benchmark traffic datasets, including METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03, PEMS04, PEMS07, and PEMS08, show that GAMMA-Net consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art models across different prediction horizons, achieving up to a 16.25% reduction in Mean Absolute Error (MAE) compared to baseline models. Ablation studies highlight the critical contributions of both the spatial and temporal components, emphasizing their complementary role in improving prediction accuracy. In conclusion, the GAMMA-Net model sets a new standard in traffic forecasting, offering a powerful tool for next-generation traffic management and urban planning. The code for this study is available at https://github.com/hdy6438/GAMMA-Net

</details>


### [30] [GRAIL: Autonomous Concept Grounding for Neuro-Symbolic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16871)
*Hikaru Shindo,Henri Rößler,Quentin Delfosse,Kristian Kersting*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GRAIL autonomously grounds relational concepts in RL agents using LLM priors and interactive learning, achieving comparable performance to hand-crafted concepts while addressing sparse rewards and concept misalignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current neuro-symbolic RL approaches require human experts to manually define relational concepts (like "left of" or "close by"), limiting adaptability since concept semantics vary across different environments and contexts.

Method: Proposes GRAIL framework that uses LLMs to provide generic concept representations as weak supervision, then refines them through environmental interaction to capture environment-specific semantics, addressing sparse rewards and concept misalignment.

Result: Experiments on Atari games (Kangaroo, Seaquest, Skiing) show GRAIL matches or outperforms agents with manually crafted concepts in simplified settings, and reveals trade-offs between reward maximization and high-level goal completion in full environments.

Conclusion: GRAIL successfully automates relational concept grounding in RL, reducing reliance on human expertise while maintaining interpretable and generalizable policies through combined symbolic reasoning and gradient-based optimization.

Abstract: Neuro-symbolic Reinforcement Learning (NeSy-RL) combines symbolic reasoning with gradient-based optimization to achieve interpretable and generalizable policies. Relational concepts, such as "left of" or "close by", serve as foundational building blocks that structure how agents perceive and act. However, conventional approaches require human experts to manually define these concepts, limiting adaptability since concept semantics vary across environments. We propose GRAIL (Grounding Relational Agents through Interactive Learning), a framework that autonomously grounds relational concepts through environmental interaction. GRAIL leverages large language models (LLMs) to provide generic concept representations as weak supervision, then refines them to capture environment-specific semantics. This approach addresses both sparse reward signals and concept misalignment prevalent in underdetermined environments. Experiments on the Atari games Kangaroo, Seaquest, and Skiing demonstrate that GRAIL matches or outperforms agents with manually crafted concepts in simplified settings, and reveals informative trade-offs between reward maximization and high-level goal completion in the full environment.

</details>


### [31] [Step-GRPO: Internalizing Dynamic Early Exit for Efficient Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16890)
*Benteng Chen,Weida Wang,Shufei Zhang,Mingbao Lin,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Step-GRPO is a post-training framework that teaches large language models to dynamically exit reasoning chains early, reducing token consumption by 32% on Qwen3-8B without sacrificing accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large reasoning models waste significant compute on redundant checks and overthinking in chain-of-thought reasoning. Existing solutions like training-time length penalties harm model capability, while inference-time early-exit approaches add system overhead.

Method: Step-GRPO uses linguistic markers to structure reasoning into semantic steps, implements Dynamic Truncated Rollout to expose models to concise high-confidence trajectories during exploration, and employs Step-Aware Relative Reward to dynamically penalize redundancy based on group-level baselines.

Result: Extensive experiments across three model sizes show Step-GRPO achieves superior accuracy-efficiency trade-off. Specifically on Qwen3-8B, it reduces token consumption by 32.0% compared to vanilla models while avoiding accuracy degradation seen in traditional length-penalty methods.

Conclusion: Step-GRPO successfully bridges the gap between efficiency and capability by internalizing dynamic early-exit capabilities directly into models through structured reasoning optimization, making large reasoning models more efficient without compromising their problem-solving abilities.

Abstract: Large reasoning models that use long chain-of-thought excel at problem-solving yet waste compute on redundant checks. Curbing this overthinking is hard: training-time length penalties can cripple ability, while inference-time early-exit adds system overhead. To bridge this gap, we propose Step-GRPO, a novel post-training framework that internalizes dynamic early-exit capabilities directly into the model. Step-GRPO shifts the optimization objective from raw tokens to semantic steps by utilizing linguistic markers to structure reasoning. We introduce a Dynamic Truncated Rollout mechanism that exposes the model to concise high-confidence trajectories during exploration, synergized with a Step-Aware Relative Reward that dynamically penalizes redundancy based on group-level baselines. Extensive experiments across three model sizes on diverse benchmarks demonstrate that Step-GRPO achieves a superior accuracy-efficiency trade-off. On Qwen3-8B, our method reduces token consumption by 32.0\% compared to the vanilla model while avoiding the accuracy degradation observed in traditional length-penalty methods.

</details>


### [32] [Beyond Text-Dominance: Understanding Modality Preference of Omni-modal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16902)
*Xinru Yan,Boxi Cao,Yaojie Lu,Hongyu Lin,Weixiang Zhou,Le Sun,Xianpei Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OLLMs exhibit visual preference (unlike traditional VLMs' text dominance), which emerges in mid-to-late layers and can be leveraged to diagnose cross-modal hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Native integration of multiple modalities in OLLMs leads to an underexplored phenomenon called "modality preference" that needs systematic investigation to understand its mechanisms and implications for model trustworthiness.

Method: 1) Quantify modality preference using a conflict-based benchmark and modality selection rate metric; 2) Evaluate ten representative OLLMs; 3) Conduct layer-wise probing to understand emergence patterns; 4) Use internal signals for cross-modal hallucination diagnosis.

Result: Most OLLMs exhibit pronounced visual preference (unlike traditional VLMs' text dominance), preference emerges progressively in mid-to-late layers, and internal signals can effectively diagnose cross-modal hallucinations achieving competitive performance across three benchmarks.

Conclusion: The work provides both mechanistic understanding of modality preference in OLLMs and practical tools for building more trustworthy systems, with discovered visual preference patterns representing a paradigm shift from previous VLM behavior.

Abstract: Native Omni-modal Large Language Models (OLLMs) have shifted from pipeline architectures to unified representation spaces. However, this native integration gives rise to a critical yet underexplored phenomenon: modality preference. To bridge this gap, we first systematically quantify modality preference of OLLMs using a newly-curated conflict-based benchmark and the modality selection rate metric. Our evaluation of ten representative OLLMs reveals a notable paradigm shift: unlike the ``text-dominance'' of traditional VLMs, most OLLMs exhibit a pronounced visual preference. To further understand the underlying mechanism, we conduct layer-wise probing and demonstrate that such modality preference is not static but emerges progressively in the mid-to-late layers. Building upon these insights, we leverage these internal signals to diagnose cross-modal hallucinations, achieving competitive performance across three downstream multi-modal benchmarks without task-specific data. Our work provides both a mechanistic understanding and a practical tool for building more trustworthy OLLMs. Our code and related resources are publicly available at: https://github.com/icip-cas/OmniPreference

</details>


### [33] [Skilldex: A Package Manager and Registry for Agent Skill Packages with Hierarchical Scope-Based Distribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16911)
*Sampriti Saha,Pranav Hemanth*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Skilldex is a package manager and registry for LLM agent skill packages that provides format conformance validation and skillset bundling with shared context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM agent skill package systems lack public validation tools against Anthropic's specification format and lack mechanisms to bundle related skills with shared context needed for behavioral coherence.

Method: Skilldex introduces: 1) compiler-style format conformance scoring that validates against Anthropic's specification with line-level diagnostics, and 2) skillset abstraction that bundles related skills with shared assets like vocabulary files and templates.

Result: Implemented Skilldex as a TypeScript CLI tool (skillpm/spm) with Hono/Supabase registry backend, featuring three-tier hierarchical scope system, human-in-the-loop agent suggestion loop, metadata-only community registry, and Model Context Protocol server.

Conclusion: Skilldex addresses key gaps in LLM agent skill package management by providing standardization validation and context-aware skill bundling, contributing to better organized and more coherent agent skill ecosystems.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly extended at runtime via skill packages, structured natural-language instruction bundles loaded from a well-known directory. Community install tooling and registries exist, but two gaps persist: no public tool scores skill packages against Anthropic's published format specification, and no mechanism bundles related skills with the shared context they need to remain mutually coherent. We present Skilldex, a package manager and registry for agent skill packages addressing both gaps. The two novel contributions are: (1) compiler-style format conformance scoring against Anthropic's skill specification, producing line-level diagnostics on description specificity, frontmatter validity, and structural adherence; and (2) the skillset abstraction, a bundled collection of related skills with shared assets (vocabulary files, templates, reference documents) that enforce cross-skill behavioral coherence. Skilldex also provides supporting infrastructure: a three-tier hierarchical scope system, a human-in-the-loop agent suggestion loop, a metadata-only community registry, and a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server. The system is implemented as a TypeScript CLI (skillpm / spm) with a Hono/Supabase registry backend, and is open-source.

</details>


### [34] [The Cognitive Penalty: Ablating System 1 and System 2 Reasoning in Edge-Native SLMs for Decentralized Consensus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16913)
*Syed Muhammad Aqdas Rizvi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DAOs should use small language models' fast intuitive reasoning (System 1) rather than slower analytical reasoning (System 2) for edge-native security, as System 2 introduces critical vulnerabilities despite higher computational investment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DAOs need secure, decentralized governance mechanisms but face risks from adversarial attacks. The effectiveness of computational scaling (System 2 reasoning) for formal logic in cryptoeconomic environments remains uncertain, requiring empirical evaluation.

Method: Developed Sentinel-Bench, an 840-inference empirical framework performing intra-model ablation on Qwen-3.5-9B. Tested by toggling latent reasoning capabilities on frozen weights against adversarial Optimism DAO datasets to isolate inference-time compute effects.

Result: System 1 achieved 100% adversarial robustness and 100% juridical consistency with fast processing. System 2 reasoning caused catastrophic instability: 26.7% cognitive collapse rate, degraded consensus stability to 72.6%, 17x latency overhead, and introduced vulnerabilities to governance attacks and hardware centralization.

Conclusion: For edge-native SLMs under Byzantine Fault Tolerance constraints, System 1 parameterized intuition is structurally and economically superior to System 2 iterative deliberation for achieving decentralized consensus, challenging conventional wisdom about computational scaling.

Abstract: Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs) are inclined explore Small Language Models (SLMs) as edge-native constitutional firewalls to vet proposals and mitigate semantic social engineering. While scaling inference-time compute (System 2) enhances formal logic, its efficacy in highly adversarial, cryptoeconomic governance environments remains underexplored. To address this, we introduce Sentinel-Bench, an 840-inference empirical framework executing a strict intra-model ablation on Qwen-3.5-9B. By toggling latent reasoning across frozen weights, we isolate the impact of inference-time compute against an adversarial Optimism DAO dataset. Our findings reveal a severe compute-accuracy inversion. The autoregressive baseline (System 1) achieved 100% adversarial robustness, 100% juridical consistency, and state finality in under 13 seconds. Conversely, System 2 reasoning introduced catastrophic instability, fundamentally driven by a 26.7% Reasoning Non-Convergence (cognitive collapse) rate. This collapse degraded trial-to-trial consensus stability to 72.6% and imposed a 17x latency overhead, introducing critical vulnerabilities to Governance Extractable Value (GEV) and hardware centralization. While rare (1.5% of adversarial trials), we empirically captured "Reasoning-Induced Sycophancy," where the model generated significantly longer internal monologues (averaging 25,750 characters) to rationalize failing the adversarial trap. We conclude that for edge-native SLMs operating under Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT) constraints, System 1 parameterized intuition is structurally and economically superior to System 2 iterative deliberation for decentralized consensus.
  Code and Dataset: https://github.com/smarizvi110/sentinel-bench

</details>


### [35] [ClimAgent: LLM as Agents for Autonomous Open-ended Climate Science Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16922)
*Hao Wang,Jindong Han,Wei Fan,Hao Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ClimAgent is a novel autonomous AI framework that uses large language models to perform complex climate research tasks across multiple sub-fields, significantly outperforming existing LLM solutions by integrating specialized tools and rigorous reasoning protocols.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current climate research faces bottlenecks due to massive multi-scale datasets and complex analytical tools, limiting scientific discovery to fragmented workflows. While LLMs could help scale expertise, existing applications are too simplistic, focusing only on basic Q&A tasks and neglecting real-world physical constraints and data-driven requirements of professional climate science.

Method: ClimAgent introduces a general-purpose autonomous framework that integrates a unified tool-use environment with rigorous reasoning protocols to execute end-to-end climate research tasks. It moves beyond simple retrieval to perform comprehensive modeling and analysis. The authors also developed ClimaBench, a comprehensive benchmark covering 5 task categories derived from professional climate scenarios (2000-2025) for systematic evaluation.

Result: On the ClimaBench benchmark, ClimAgent significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a 40.21% improvement over original LLM solutions in terms of solution rigorousness and practicality, demonstrating its effectiveness for real-world climate discovery tasks.

Conclusion: ClimAgent successfully bridges the gap between general-purpose LLMs and specialized climate research needs by providing an autonomous framework that can handle complex, data-driven tasks across diverse climate sub-fields, offering a transformative paradigm for accelerating climate science discovery.

Abstract: Climate research is pivotal for mitigating global environmental crises, yet the accelerating volume of multi-scale datasets and the complexity of analytical tools have created significant bottlenecks, constraining scientific discovery to fragmented and labor-intensive workflows. While the emergence Large Language Models (LLMs) offers a transformative paradigm to scale scientific expertise, existing explorations remain largely confined to simple Question-Answering (Q&A) tasks. These approaches often oversimplify real-world challenges, neglecting the intricate physical constraints and the data-driven nature required in professional climate science.To bridge this gap, we introduce ClimAgent, a general-purpose autonomous framework designed to execute a wide spectrum of research tasks across diverse climate sub-fields. By integrating a unified tool-use environment with rigorous reasoning protocols, ClimAgent transcends simple retrieval to perform end-to-end modeling and analysis.To foster systematic evaluation, we propose ClimaBench, the first comprehensive benchmark for real-world climate discovery. It encompasses challenging problems spanning 5 distinct task categories derived from professional scenarios between 2000 and 2025. Experiments on ClimaBench demonstrate that ClimAgent significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a 40.21% improvement over original LLM solutions in solution rigorousness and practicality. Our code are available at https://github.com/usail-hkust/ClimAgent.

</details>


### [36] [Alignment Imprint: Zero-Shot AI-Generated Text Detection via Provable Preference Discrepancy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16923)
*Junxi Wu,Kailin Huang,Dongjian Hu,Bin Chen,Hao Wu,Shu-Tao Xia,Changliang Zou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LAPD (Log-likelihood Alignment Preference Discrepancy) is a new AI-generated text detection method that leverages alignment imprints in LLMs, achieving significant performance improvements over existing baselines through standardized information-weighted statistics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing likelihood-based AI-generated text detection methods are sensitive to content complexity and exhibit unstable performance, especially in high-entropy regions. The paper aims to develop a more robust detection approach that leverages the distributional imprints left by LLM alignment processes.

Method: The paper theoretically derives the alignment imprint by modeling LLM alignment as constrained optimization steps, showing that log-likelihood ratio decomposes into implicit instructional biases and preference rewards. To address instability in high-entropy regions, the authors introduce LAPD - a standardized information-weighted statistic based on alignment imprint, with theoretical guarantees of performance superiority over Fast-DetectGPT.

Result: LAPD achieves a 45.82% relative improvement over the strongest existing baselines, demonstrating large and consistent gains across all experimental settings. The method shows statistical dominance over Fast-DetectGPT and theoretical improvements over unweighted alignment scores when aligned and base models are close in distribution.

Conclusion: The alignment imprint left by modern LLMs provides a measurable distributional signature that can be effectively leveraged for AI-generated text detection. LAPD offers a robust, theoretically-grounded approach that significantly outperforms existing methods, particularly addressing the instability issues in complex, high-entropy text regions.

Abstract: Detecting AI-generated text is an important but challenging problem. Existing likelihood-based detection methods are often sensitive to content complexity and may exhibit unstable performance. In this paper, our key insight is that modern Large Language Models (LLMs) undergo alignment (including fine-tuning and preference tuning), leaving a measurable distributional imprint. We theoretically derive this imprint by abstracting the alignment process as a sequence of constrained optimization steps, showing that the log-likelihood ratio can naturally decompose into implicit instructional biases and preference rewards. We refer to this quantity as the Alignment Imprint. Furthermore, to mitigate the instability in high-entropy regions, we introduce Log-likelihood Alignment Preference Discrepancy (LAPD), a standardized information-weighted statistic based on alignment imprint. We provide statistical guarantee that alignment-based statistics dominate Fast-DetectGPT in performance. We also theoretically show that LAPD strictly improves the unweighted alignment scores when the aligned and base models are close in distribution. Extensive experiments show that LAPD achieves an improvement 45.82% relative to the strongest existing baselines, yielding large and consistent gains across all settings.

</details>


### [37] [Playing Psychic: Using Thought Trees to Predict Reasoning Models Accuracy on Coding Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16931)
*Jiaxin Fang,Runyuan He,Sahil Bhatia,Neel Gajare,Alvin Cheung*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Test-time scaling helps LLMs perform better on coding tasks, but current evaluations focus too much on competitive programming. The paper systematically studies frontier reasoning models on real-world coding benchmarks and finds that the structure of reasoning traces (not just content) strongly predicts correctness. They propose structured thought-trees and show that detecting structurally anomalous traces leads to performance gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluations of large language models' reasoning abilities in coding primarily rely on competitive programming benchmarks, which may not capture the full range of reasoning abilities needed for real-world coding tasks. There's a need for more systematic analysis of frontier reasoning models on real-world coding benchmarks to better understand their performance.

Method: 1. Systematic study of frontier reasoning models on real-world coding benchmarks. 2. Devised a programmatic way to automatically generate coding tasks of arbitrary difficulty and structure from existing benchmarks. 3. Proposed structured thought-trees to represent reasoning traces. 4. Trained a lightweight classifier on features extracted from thought-trees to predict trace correctness. 5. Implemented flagging and retrying of structurally anomalous traces based on extracted features.

Result: Analysis revealed that the structure of reasoning traces (not just their contents) is a strong predictor of correctness. The lightweight classifier trained on thought-tree features effectively predicts trace correctness. Flagging and retrying structurally anomalous traces based on these features yields consistent performance gains, particularly at lower complexity levels.

Conclusion: Structured analysis of reasoning traces through thought-trees provides valuable insights into model performance. The structure of reasoning traces is a crucial factor in determining correctness, and leveraging this insight through automated detection of structural anomalies can lead to improved performance in coding tasks, offering a new direction for enhancing LLM reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have shown that test-time scaling can substantially improve model performance on complex tasks, particularly in the coding domain. Under this paradigm, models use a larger token budget during inference to generate intermediate reasoning traces before producing a final answer. However, current evaluations primarily rely on competitive programming benchmarks, which may not capture the full range of reasoning abilities. In this work, we perform a systematic study of frontier reasoning models to understand their performance on real-world coding benchmarks. To gain more insights into the performance of such models, we devise a programmatic way to {\em automatically generate} coding tasks of arbitrary difficulty and structure from existing benchmarks. Using this framework, our analysis reveals that the structure of a reasoning trace, not just its contents, is a strong predictor of correctness. Motivated by this, we propose structured thought-trees as means to represent reasoning traces. To illustrate their use, we train a lightweight classifier on features extracted from thought-trees to predict trace correctness, and demonstrate that flagging and retrying structurally anomalous traces based on the extracted features yields consistent gains at lower complexity levels.

</details>


### [38] [LLMs can persuade only psychologically susceptible humans on societal issues, via trust in AI and emotional appeals, amid logical fallacies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16935)
*Alexis Carrillo,Salvatore Citraro,Ali Aghazhadeh Ardebili,Enrique Taietta,Giulio Rossetti,Emilio Ferrara,Giuseppe Alessandro Veltri,Massimo Stella*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces Talk2AI, a longitudinal framework that measures how LLMs influence human opinions on polarizing topics, revealing persistent conviction anchoring, frequent fallacious reasoning by both humans and LLMs, and psychological factors that make people more susceptible to AI persuasion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's scarce longitudinal evidence examining LLMs' persuasiveness and perceived humanness over time. The researchers aim to understand how generative AI influences human opinions through psycho-social pathways in AI-human interactions on digital platforms.

Method: Developed Talk2AI framework with 770 participants in a four-way longitudinal setup. Participants engaged in structured conversations with four leading LLMs on polarizing topics, producing 3,080 conversations over 60,000 turns. Collected measures of conviction, opinion change, perceived humanness, self-donation, and textual explanations after each wave. Used NLP analyses for reasoning patterns and explainable AI (XAI) for susceptibility modeling.

Result: Found longitudinal inertia in human convictions despite AI exposure. Both humans and LLMs used fallacious reasoning in 1 out of 6 conversational turns. LLM humanness was best predicted by sociodemographic/psychological features (R²=0.44), while opinion change (R²=0.34), conviction (R²=0.26), and endowment (R²=0.24) were less predictable. Identified susceptible individuals with trust in LLMs, agreeableness, extraversion, and higher need for cognition.

Conclusion: Talk2AI provides empirical evidence that LLMs influence human opinions through multiple psycho-social pathways, challenging the stereotype of LLMs as superior reasoning systems and highlighting both cognitive surrender and resistance in AI-human interactions.

Abstract: Scarce longitudinal evidence examines LLMs' persuasiveness and humanness along time-evolving psychological frameworks. We introduce Talk2AI, a longitudinal framework quantifying psycho-social, reasoning and affective dimensions of LLMs' persuasiveness about polarizing societal topics. In a four-way longitudinal setup, Talk2AI's 770 participants engaged in structured conversations with one of four leading LLMs on topics like climate change, social media misinformation, and math anxiety. This produced 3,080 conversations over 60,000 turns. After each wave, participants reported conviction in their initial topic stance, perceived opinion change, LLM's perceived humanness, a self-donation to the topic and a textual explanation. Feedback time series showed longitudinal inertia in convictions, indicating some human anchoring to initial opinions even after repeated exposure to AI-generated arguments. Interestingly, NLP analyses revealed that both humans and LLMs relied on fallacious reasoning in 1 conversational quip every 6, countering the ``LLMs as superior systems" stereotype behind LLMs' cognitive surrender. LLMs' perceived humanness was most learnable from sociodemographic, psychological and engagement features ($R^2=0.44$), followed by opinion change ($R^2=0.34$), conviction ($R^2=0.26$) and personal endowment ($R^2=0.24$). Crucially, explainable AI (XAI) indicated: (i) the presence of individuals more susceptible to LLM-based opinion changes; (ii) psychological susceptibility to LLM-convincing consisted of having more trust in LLMs, being more agreeable and extraverted and with a higher need for cognition. A multiverse approach with mixed-effects models confirmed XAI results, alongside strong individual differences. Talk2AI provides a grounded framework and evidence for detecting how GenAI can influence human opinions via multiple psycho-social pathways in AI-human digital platforms.

</details>


### [39] [AutoPKG: An Automated Framework for Dynamic E-commerce Product-Attribute Knowledge Graph Construction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16950)
*Pollawat Hongwimol,Haoning Shang,Chutong Wang,Zhichao Wan,Yi Gao,Yuanming Li,Lin Gui,Wenhao Sun,Cheng Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoPKG is a multi-agent LLM framework that automatically constructs Product-attribute Knowledge Graphs from multimodal e-commerce content, improving attribute extraction and boosting business metrics like Gross Merchandise Value.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Product attribute extraction in e-commerce faces challenges with inconsistent, incomplete, and costly-to-maintain ontologies, creating a bottleneck for effective product knowledge management.

Method: A multi-agent Large Language Model framework that automatically constructs Product-attribute Knowledge Graphs from multimodal product content, including type induction, attribute key discovery, multimodal value extraction from text/images, and centralized consolidation through a decision agent for canonical graph consistency.

Result: Achieved 0.953 Weighted Knowledge Efficiency for product types, 0.724 WKE for attribute keys, and 0.531 edge-level F1 for multimodal value extraction on Lazada dataset. Improved edge-level F1 by 0.152 on benchmarks and precision by 0.208. Online A/B tests showed GMV increases: Badge (+3.81%), Search (+5.32%), Recommendation (+7.89%).

Conclusion: AutoPKG successfully addresses the product attribute extraction bottleneck in e-commerce by automatically constructing consistent knowledge graphs, demonstrating strong performance on both offline metrics and real-world business impact through significant GMV improvements.

Abstract: Product attribute extraction in e-commerce is bottlenecked by ontologies that are inconsistent, incomplete, and costly to maintain. We present AutoPKG, a multi-agent Large Language Model (LLM) framework that automatically constructs a Product-attribute Knowledge Graph (PKG) from multimodal product content. AutoPKG induces product types and type-specific attribute keys on demand, extracts attribute values from text and images, and consolidates updates through a centralized decision agent that maintains a globally consistent canonical graph. We also propose an evaluation protocol for dynamic PKGs that measures type and key validity, consolidation quality, and edge-level accuracy for value assertions after canonicalization. On a large real-world marketplace catalog dataset from Lazada (Alibaba), AutoPKG achieves up to 0.953 Weighted Knowledge Efficiency (WKE) for product types, 0.724 WKE for attribute keys, and 0.531 edge-level F1 for multimodal value extraction. Across three public benchmarks, our method improves edge-level exact-match F1 by 0.152 and yields a precision gain of 0.208 on the attribute extraction application. Online A/B tests show that AutoPKG-derived attributes increase Gross Merchandise Value (GMV) in Badge by 3.81 percent, in Search by 5.32 percent, and in Recommendation by 7.89 percent, supporting the practical value of AutoPKG in production.

</details>


### [40] [MCPO: Mastery-Consolidated Policy Optimization for Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16972)
*Zhaokang Liao,Yingguo Gao,Yi Yang,Yongheng Hu,Jingting Ding*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MCPO improves RLVR for LLMs by addressing training signal issues in high-accuracy prompts with hinge-KL regularization and weighted optimization prioritization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO-style RLVR algorithms have issues with high accuracy prompts: 1) mastered prompts (100% accuracy) lose training signals causing policy drift and forgetting, 2) majority-correct prompts (50-100% accuracy) have reduced query weights that weaken consolidation from partial correctness to mastery.

Method: Proposes Mastery-Consolidated Policy Optimization (MCPO) with two key components: 1) hinge-KL regularizer applied only to mastered prompts to bound harmful policy drift between gradient steps, 2) weighting mechanism that prioritizes majority-correct prompts to better allocate optimization effort.

Result: Extensive experiments across three mathematical benchmarks show MCPO consistently improves pass@1 performance. Counter-intuitively, MCPO also boosts pass@k metrics, indicating mastery consolidation further catalyzes solution diversity rather than restricting exploration.

Conclusion: MCPO successfully addresses limitations of GRPO-style objectives in RLVR for LLMs by preventing policy drift in mastered prompts and strengthening consolidation from partial to full mastery, leading to improved reasoning performance and increased solution diversity.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a promising approach to improve the reasoning abilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). Among RLVR algorithms, Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and its variants have demonstrated strong performance and high training efficiency. However, GRPO-style objectives exhibit two issues on high accuracy prompts including mastered prompts (rollout accuracy =1) and majority-correct prompts (rollout accuracy in (0.5,1)). For mastered prompts, group-relative advantages vanish, yielding no training signal and unconstrained policy drift that can cause forgetting. For majority-correct prompts, the induced query weight shrinks as accuracy increases, weakening consolidation from partial correctness to mastery. To alleviate this, we propose Mastery-Consolidated Policy Optimization (MCPO), which introduces (i) a hinge-KL regularizer applied exclusively to mastered prompts to bound harmful policy drift between successive gradient steps, and (ii) a weighting mechanism that prioritizes majority-correct prompts to better allocate optimization effort. Extensive experiments across three mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that MCPO consistently improves pass@1 performance. Counter-intuitively, rather than restricting exploration, MCPO boosts pass@k metrics, indicating that mastery consolidation further catalyzes solution diversity.

</details>


### [41] [A phenotype-driven and evidence-governed framework for knowledge graph enrichment and hypotheses discovery in population data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16982)
*Adela Bâra,Simona-Vasilica Oprea*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A phenotype-driven framework for knowledge graph construction that shifts from confirmatory relationship recovery to structured hypothesis discovery, using GNNs for phenotype discovery and LLMs for hypothesis generation in a multi-objective optimization approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current knowledge graph construction methods are overly confirmatory, focusing on recovering known relationships rather than identifying novel or context-dependent nodes and connections. There's a need for approaches that enable discovery of underexplored relationships while maintaining scientific rigor.

Method: Proposes a unified pipeline integrating graph neural networks (GNNs) for phenotype discovery and causal inference, combined with large language models (LLMs) for hypothesis generation and claim extraction. KG expansion is formulated as a multi-objective optimization problem evaluating candidate claims based on relevance, structural validation, and novelty. Uses Pareto-optimal selection to identify non-dominated claims balancing confirmation and discovery.

Result: Experiments on heterogeneous population datasets show the framework produces more interpretable phenotypes, reveals context-dependent causal structures, and generates high-quality claims aligned with both data and evidence. Outperforms rule-based and LLM-only baselines across plausibility, novelty, validation, and relevance metrics. In retrieval-augmented settings, achieves high performance (Recall@5=0.98) with low hallucination rates (0.05).

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully shifts knowledge graph construction from confirmatory recovery to structured hypothesis discovery, effectively balancing data-driven validation with scientific novelty while reducing LLM hallucinations in knowledge generation tasks.

Abstract: Current knowledge graph (KG) construction methods are confirmatory, focusing on recovering known relationships rather than identifying novel or context-dependent nodes. This paper proposes a phenotype-driven and evidence-governed framework that shifts the paradigm toward structured hypothesis discovery and controlled KG expansion. The approach integrates graph neural networks (GNNs) for phenotype discovery, causal inference, probabilistic reasoning and large language models (LLMs) for hypothesis generation and claim extraction within a unified pipeline. The framework prioritizes relationships that are both structurally supported by data and underexplored in the literature. KG expansion is formulated as a multi-objective optimization problem, where candidate claims are jointly evaluated in terms of relevance, structural validation and novelty. Pareto-optimal selection enables the identification of non-dominated claims that balance confirmation and discovery, avoiding trivial or redundant knowledge inclusion. Experiments on heterogeneous population datasets demonstrate that the proposed framework produces more interpretable phenotypes, reveals context-dependent causal structures and generates high-quality claims that align with both data and scientific evidence. Compared to rule-based and LLM-only baselines, the method achieves the best trade-off across plausibility, novelty, validation and relevance. In retrieval-augmented settings, it significantly improves performance (Recall@5=0.98) while reducing hallucination rates (0.05), highlighting its effectiveness in grounding LLM outputs.

</details>


### [42] [Rule-VLN: Bridging Perception and Compliance via Semantic Reasoning and Geometric Rectification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16993)
*Jiawen Wen,Penglei Sun,Wenjie Zhang,Suixuan Qiu,Weisheng Xu,Xiaofei Yang,Xiaowen Chu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Rule-VLN, a benchmark for rule-compliant navigation in urban environments, and proposes SNRM, a zero-shot module to help agents navigate while respecting regulatory constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Vision-and-Language Navigation agents suffer from a "goal-driven trap" - they prioritize physical accessibility over semantic compliance, often violating regulatory constraints in real-world urban environments where social compliance is crucial.

Method: 1) Created Rule-VLN benchmark with 29k-node urban environment, 177 regulatory categories across 8k constrained nodes, organized in four curriculum levels. 2) Proposed Semantic Navigation Rectification Module (SNRM) with coarse-to-fine visual perception using VLMs and epistemic mental map for dynamic detour planning, designed as a zero-shot module for pre-trained agents.

Result: Rule-VLN challenges state-of-the-art models, while SNRM significantly improves rule compliance - reducing Constraint Violation Rate (CVR) by 19.26% and boosting Task Completion (TC) by 5.97%.

Conclusion: The work addresses a critical gap in embodied AI by focusing on rule-compliant navigation beyond mere reachability, providing both a challenging benchmark and an effective solution for real-world deployment where semantic understanding of regulations is essential.

Abstract: As embodied AI transitions to real-world deployment, the success of the Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) task tends to evolve from mere reachability to social compliance. However, current agents suffer from a "goal-driven trap", prioritizing physical geometry ("can I go?") over semantic rules ("may I go?"), frequently overlooking subtle regulatory constraints. To bridge this gap, we establish Rule-VLN, the first large-scale urban benchmark for rule-compliant navigation. Spanning a massive 29k-node environment, it injects 177 diverse regulatory categories into 8k constrained nodes across four curriculum levels, challenging agents with fine-grained visual and behavioral constraints. We further propose the Semantic Navigation Rectification Module (SNRM), a universal, zero-shot module designed to equip pre-trained agents with safety awareness. SNRM integrates a coarse-to-fine visual perception VLM framework with an epistemic mental map for dynamic detour planning. Experiments demonstrate that while Rule-VLN challenges state-of-the-art models, SNRM significantly restores navigation capabilities, reducing CVR by 19.26% and boosting TC by 5.97%.

</details>


### [43] [Small Model as Master Orchestrator: Learning Unified Agent-Tool Orchestration with Parallel Subtask Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17009)
*Wenzhen Yuan,Wutao Xiong,Fanchen Yu,Shengji Tang,Ting Liu,Tao Chen,Peng Ye,Yuzhuo Fu,Wanli Ouyang,Lei Bai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A unified Agent-as-Tool paradigm with ParaManager orchestrator enables protocol-normalized, state-aware parallel task decomposition and asynchronous execution through a two-stage training pipeline combining SFT and RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-agent systems face limitations with static workflows, serial agent scheduling, and heterogeneous interface protocols between tools and agents, leading to high complexity and poor extensibility.

Method: Proposes Agent-as-Tool paradigm that abstracts agents and tools into standardized, learnable action space with protocol normalization and state feedback. Trains lightweight ParaManager orchestrator using two-stage pipeline: SFT with recovery mechanisms followed by RL to balance task success, protocol compliance, diversity, and reasoning efficiency.

Result: ParaManager achieves strong performance across multiple benchmarks and exhibits robust generalization under unseen model pools.

Conclusion: The Agent-as-Tool paradigm with ParaManager orchestrator addresses limitations of existing MAS orchestration methods by enabling state-aware parallel subtask decomposition and delegation through a unified, learnable action space.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems (MAS) demonstrate clear advantages in tackling complex problems by coordinating diverse agents and external tools. However, most existing orchestration methods rely on static workflows or serial agent scheduling, and are further constrained by heterogeneous interface protocols between tools and agents. This leads to high system complexity and poor extensibility. To mitigate these issues, we propose Agent-as-Tool, a unified parallel orchestration paradigm that abstracts both agents and tools into a standardized, learnable action space with protocol normalization and explicit state feedback. Building on this paradigm, we train a lightweight orchestrator, ParaManager, which decouples planning decisions from subtask solving, enabling state-aware parallel subtask decomposition, delegation, and asynchronous execution. For training, we adopt a two-stage ParaManager training pipeline. It improves robustness by incorporating supervised fine-tuning (SFT) trajectories equipped with recovery mechanisms, and further applies reinforcement learning (RL) to achieve an optimal balance among task success, protocol compliance, diversity, and reasoning efficiency. Experiments show that ParaManager achieves strong performance across multiple benchmarks and exhibits robust generalization under unseen model pools.

</details>


### [44] [Mini-BEHAVIOR-Gran: Revealing U-Shaped Effects of Instruction Granularity on Language-Guided Embodied Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17019)
*Sukai Huang,Chenyuan Zhang,Fucai Ke,Zhixi Cai,Gholamreza Haffari,Lizhen Qu,Hamid Rezatofighi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Mini-BEHAVIOR-Gran is a new benchmark that enables controlled study of instruction granularity in embodied AI by providing multiple instruction variants per task, revealing a U-shaped relationship between granularity and agent performance with peaks at both fine and coarse extremes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks pair each task with a single static instruction, making it difficult to study how agent behavior changes when the same task is described at different levels of detail. There's a need for controlled studies of instruction granularity in language-guided embodied AI.

Method: Extended Mini-BEHAVIOR with multiple instruction variants per task ranging from high-level goal descriptions to step-by-step guidance. Compared four candidate metrics for cross-task granularity quantification: token count, entity count, action-verb count, and planning-width.

Result: Planning-width correlates most consistently with agent performance. Using width to organize training and evaluation reveals a non-monotonic U-shaped relationship between instruction granularity and performance, with peaks at both fine and coarse extremes. The coarse-granularity performance rebound is associated with shallow grounding where agents learn vision-dominant policies.

Conclusion: Instruction granularity significantly impacts agent performance in complex ways, with both overly detailed and overly simplistic instructions having advantages depending on the learning mechanism. Planning-width is an effective metric for quantifying granularity, and the U-shaped performance relationship suggests agents can benefit from both detailed step-by-step guidance and high-level goal descriptions through different learning strategies.

Abstract: Instruction granularity is an important yet poorly controlled variable in language-guided embodied AI. Existing benchmarks typically pair each task with a single static instruction, making it difficult to study how agent behavior changes when the same task is described at different levels of detail. We introduce Mini-BEHAVIOR-Gran, a new benchmark for controlled studies of instruction granularity that extends Mini-BEHAVIOR with multiple instruction variants per task, ranging from high-level goal descriptions to step-by-step guidance. Using this benchmark, we compare four candidate metrics for cross-task granularity quantification: token count, entity count, action-verb count, and planning-width, and find that width correlates most consistently with agent performance. Using width to organize training and evaluation further reveals a non-monotonic U-shaped relationship between instruction granularity and performance, with peaks at both fine and coarse extremes. Further analysis suggests that the coarse-granularity performance rebound is associated with shallow grounding, where agents learn vision-dominant policies.

</details>


### [45] [Harness as an Asset: Enforcing Determinism via the Convergent AI Agent Framework (CAAF)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17025)
*Tianbao Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CAAF framework ensures 100% constraint compliance in safety-critical engineering by implementing deterministic closed-loop control with formal domain invariants, outperforming existing methods that achieve 0% reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based orchestration suffers from undetected constraint violations, sycophantic compliance, context attention decay, and stochastic oscillation during self-correction, making systems unreliable for safety-critical applications where even low violation rates are unacceptable.

Method: CAAF framework uses three pillars: (1) Recursive Atomic Decomposition with physical context firewalls, (2) Harness as an Asset formalizing domain invariants into machine-readable registries enforced by deterministic Unified Assertion Interface (UAI), and (3) Structured Semantic Gradients with State Locking for monotonic convergence.

Result: CAAF achieved 100% paradox detection across autonomous driving and pharmaceutical benchmarks, while monolithic GPT-4o and alternative multi-agent architectures achieved 0% reliability. Pharmaceutical benchmark with 7 simultaneous nonlinear constraints and minimal unsatisfiable subset was structurally harder than 2-constraint AD paradox. Mono+UAI ablation achieved 95%, isolating UAI as core contribution.

Conclusion: CAAF provides reliable safety-critical engineering with deterministic constraint enforcement, enabling fully offline deployment with single commodity models, addressing fundamental limitations of current LLM orchestration paradigms.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) produce a controllability gap in safety-critical engineering: even low rates of undetected constraint violations render a system undeployable. Current orchestration paradigms suffer from sycophantic compliance, context attention decay [Liu et al., 2024], and stochastic oscillation during self-correction [Huang et al., 2024].
  We introduce the Convergent AI Agent Framework (CAAF), which transitions agentic workflows from open-loop generation to closed-loop Fail-Safe Determinism via three pillars: (1) Recursive Atomic Decomposition with physical context firewalls; (2) Harness as an Asset, formalizing domain invariants into machine-readable registries enforced by a deterministic Unified Assertion Interface (UAI); and (3) Structured Semantic Gradients with State Locking for monotonic convergence.
  Empirical evaluation across two domains -- SAE Level 3 (L3) autonomous driving (AD) (n=30, 7 conditions) and pharmaceutical continuous flow reactor design (n=20, 4 conditions including a Mono+UAI ablation) -- shows that CAAF-all-GPT-4o-mini achieves 100% paradox detection while monolithic GPT-4o achieves 0% (even at temperature=0). The pharmaceutical benchmark features 7 simultaneous constraints with nonlinear Arrhenius interactions and a 3-way minimal unsatisfiable subset, representing a structurally harder challenge than the 2-constraint AD paradox. Alternative multi-agent architectures (debate, sequential checking) also achieve 0% across 80 trials, confirming that CAAF's reliability derives from its deterministic UAI, not from multi-agent orchestration per se. A Mono+UAI ablation (95%) isolates UAI as the core contribution. CAAF's reliability is invariant to prompt hints; all components use a single commodity model, enabling fully offline deployment.

</details>


### [46] [Understanding and Enforcing Weight Disentanglement in Task Arithmetic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17078)
*Shangge Liu,Yuehan Yin,Lei Wang,Qi Fan,Yinghuan Shi,Wenbin Li,Yang Gao,Dacheng Tao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Task-Feature Specialization (TFS) as the fundamental principle behind task arithmetic success, proves it causes weight disentanglement and vector orthogonality, and proposes OrthoReg regularization to enforce orthogonality during fine-tuning to improve task arithmetic performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Task arithmetic provides efficient training-free model editing but lacks theoretical explanation. Existing "weight disentanglement" concept describes ideal outcomes but doesn't reveal underlying causes. The paper aims to discover what intrinsic properties enable this disentanglement.

Method: The authors introduce Task-Feature Specialization (TFS) as the core principle, prove it's sufficient for weight disentanglement, and show it causes geometric orthogonality. They then propose OrthoReg - a regularization method that actively enforces orthogonal structure on weight updates during fine-tuning to shape this geometric consequence and promote disentanglement.

Result: Theoretical proof that OrthoReg promotes disentanglement. Extensive experiments show OrthoReg consistently and significantly enhances performance of various task arithmetic methods across different settings.

Conclusion: TFS is the fundamental principle behind successful task arithmetic, causing both functional disentanglement and geometric orthogonality. By actively enforcing orthogonality during fine-tuning via OrthoReg, we can practically promote disentanglement and improve task arithmetic performance without needing to enforce the abstract TFS property directly.

Abstract: Task arithmetic provides an efficient, training-free way to edit pre-trained models, yet lacks a fundamental theoretical explanation for its success. The existing concept of ``weight disentanglement" describes the ideal outcome of non-interfering task composition but does not reveal its underlying cause. Crucially, what intrinsic properties of the pre-trained model ($θ_0$) or the task vectors ($τ_t$) enable this disentanglement remains underexplored. In this paper, we introduce Task-Feature Specialization (TFS), a model's ability to allocate distinct internal features to different tasks, as the fundamental principle. We first prove that TFS is a sufficient condition for weight disentanglement. More importantly, we find that TFS also gives rise to an observable geometric consequence: weight vector orthogonality. This positions TFS as the common cause for both the desired functional outcome (disentanglement) and a measurable geometric property (orthogonality). This relationship provides the key insight for our method: since the abstract TFS property is intractable to enforce directly, we can instead promote weight disentanglement by shaping its concrete geometric consequence, orthogonality. Therefore, we propose OrthoReg, a simple and effective regularization method that actively enforces an internal orthogonal structure on weight updates ($ΔW$) that constitute $τ_t$ during fine-tuning. And we theoretically prove that OrthoReg promotes disentanglement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OrthoReg consistently and significantly enhances the performance of various task arithmetic methods. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/RL-MIND/OrthoReg}{https://github.com/RL-MIND/OrthoReg}.

</details>


### [47] [Complementing Self-Consistency with Cross-Model Disagreement for Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17112)
*Kimia Hamidieh,Veronika Thost,Walter Gerych,Mikhail Yurochkin,Marzyeh Ghassemi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new method to estimate epistemic uncertainty in black-box LLMs by measuring disagreement between different models, which helps identify confident failures when aleatoric uncertainty is low.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current self-consistency methods for estimating aleatoric uncertainty in LLMs fail when models are overconfident and produce the same incorrect answer across multiple samples. There's a need for better uncertainty quantification that can detect such confident failures.

Method: Proposes an epistemic uncertainty measure using a small ensemble of scale-matched models. Calculates EU as the gap between inter-model and intra-model sequence-semantic similarity, then defines total uncertainty as the sum of aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty.

Result: The proposed total uncertainty improves ranking calibration and selective abstention across five 7-9B instruction-tuned models and ten long-form tasks. Epistemic uncertainty reliably flags confident failures where aleatoric uncertainty is low.

Conclusion: Cross-model disagreement provides valuable epistemic uncertainty signals that complement aleatoric uncertainty, enabling better detection of confident failures in LLMs even with black-box access and small model ensembles.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often produce confident yet incorrect responses, and uncertainty quantification is one potential solution to more robust usage. Recent works routinely rely on self-consistency to estimate aleatoric uncertainty (AU), yet this proxy collapses when models are overconfident and produce the same incorrect answer across samples. We analyze this regime and show that cross-model semantic disagreement is higher on incorrect answers precisely when AU is low. Motivated by this, we introduce an epistemic uncertainty (EU) term that operates in the black-box access setting: EU uses only generated text from a small, scale-matched ensemble and is computed as the gap between inter-model and intra-model sequence-semantic similarity. We then define total uncertainty (TU) as the sum of AU and EU. In a comprehensive study across five 7-9B instruction-tuned models and ten long-form tasks, TU improves ranking calibration and selective abstention relative to AU, and EU reliably flags confident failures where AU is low. We further characterize when EU is most useful via agreement and complementarity diagnostics.

</details>


### [48] [If Only My CGM Could Speak: A Privacy-Preserving Agent for Question Answering over Continuous Glucose Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17133)
*Yanjun Cui,Ali Emami,Temiloluwa Prioleau,Nikhil Singh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CGM-Agent is a privacy-preserving framework that uses LLMs as reasoning engines to answer free-form questions about continuous glucose data while keeping all personal health data on-device and never transmitting it externally.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CGM patient platforms only provide static summaries without supporting inquisitive user queries, while deploying LLMs over sensitive health records raises privacy and accuracy concerns.

Method: LLMs serve purely as reasoning engines that select analytical functions; all computation occurs locally with personal health data never leaving the user's device. Evaluation uses a benchmark of 4,180 questions combining parameterized templates with real user queries and ground truth from deterministic program execution.

Result: Top LLMs achieve 94% value accuracy on synthetic queries and 88% on ambiguous real-world queries. Errors stem primarily from intent and temporal ambiguity rather than computational failures. Lightweight models achieve competitive performance in this agent design.

Conclusion: CGM-Agent demonstrates a viable privacy-preserving approach for LLM-powered health agents that can handle free-form inquiries while maintaining data privacy, with potential for low-cost deployment using lightweight models.

Abstract: Continuous glucose monitors (CGMs) used in diabetes care collect rich personal health data that could improve day-to-day self-management. However, current patient platforms only offer static summaries which do not support inquisitive user queries. Large language models (LLMs) could enable free-form inquiries about continuous glucose data, but deploying them over sensitive health records raises privacy and accuracy concerns. In this paper, we present CGM-Agent, a privacy-preserving framework for question answering over personal glucose data. In our design, the LLM serves purely as a reasoning engine that selects analytical functions. All computation occurs locally, and personal health data never leaves the user's device. For evaluation, we construct a benchmark of 4,180 questions combining parameterized question templates with real user queries and ground truth derived from deterministic program execution. Evaluating 6 leading LLMs, we find that top models achieve 94\% value accuracy on synthetic queries and 88\% on ambiguous real-world queries. Errors stem primarily from intent and temporal ambiguity rather than computational failures. Additionally, lightweight models achieve competitive performance in our agent design, suggesting opportunities for low-cost deployment. We release our code and benchmark to support future work on trustworthy health agents.

</details>


### [49] [Local Inconsistency Resolution: The Interplay between Attention and Control in Probabilistic Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17140)
*Oliver E. Richardson,Mandana Samiei,Mehran Shakerinava,Joseph D. Viviano,Abdessamad El Kabid,Ali Parviz,Yoshua Bengio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Local Inconsistency Resolution (LIR) is a generic algorithm for learning and approximate inference that iteratively focuses on subsets of a model to resolve inconsistencies, unifying various important algorithms including EM, belief propagation, adversarial training, GANs, and GFlowNets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to provide a unified framework that connects various important algorithms in machine learning and probabilistic inference under a common epistemic interpretation, addressing the need for a general approach to learning with inconsistent beliefs.

Method: The method is built on Probabilistic Dependency Graphs (PDGs) as a flexible representation. Local Inconsistency Resolution (LIR) works by iteratively focusing on subsets of the model and resolving inconsistencies using controlled parameters, with different algorithms emerging based on choices of attention and control procedures.

Result: LIR successfully unifies and generalizes several important algorithms including EM, belief propagation, adversarial training, GANs, and GFlowNets. For GFlowNets specifically, LIR suggests a more natural loss function that improves convergence. The implementation for discrete PDGs shows comparable behavior to global optimization.

Conclusion: LIR provides a powerful general framework with intuitive epistemic interpretation that connects diverse learning algorithms, demonstrating practical improvements (like better GFlowNet convergence) while offering theoretical unification of important methods in the literature.

Abstract: We present a generic algorithm for learning and approximate inference with an intuitive epistemic interpretation: iteratively focus on a subset of the model and resolve inconsistencies using the parameters under control. This framework, which we call Local Inconsistency Resolution (LIR) is built upon Probabilistic Dependency Graphs (PDGs), which provide a flexible representational foundation capable of capturing inconsistent beliefs. We show how LIR unifies and generalizes a wide variety of important algorithms in the literature, including the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm, belief propagation, adversarial training, GANs, and GFlowNets. In the last case, LIR actually suggests a more natural loss, which we demonstrate improves GFlowNet convergence. Each method can be recovered as a specific instance of LIR by choosing a procedure to direct focus (attention and control). We implement this algorithm for discrete PDGs and study its properties on synthetically generated PDGs, comparing its behavior to the global optimization semantics of the full PDG.

</details>


### [50] [Graph-of-Agents: A Graph-based Framework for Multi-Agent LLM Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17148)
*Sukwon Yun,Jie Peng,Pingzhi Li,Wendong Fan,Jie Chen,James Zou,Guohao Li,Tianlong Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Graph-of-Agents (GoA) is a graph-based framework for multi-agent LLM coordination that selectively samples relevant agents, establishes communication edges through relevance ordering, performs directed message passing between agents, and aggregates responses via graph pooling to achieve superior performance with fewer models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
