Motivation: As the LLM ecosystem expands with numerous specialized models, existing multi-agent frameworks like Mixture-of-Agents have limitations in agent selection, intra-agent communication, and response integration, creating a need for more effective coordination mechanisms.

Method: GoA uses node sampling to select relevant agents based on model cards, constructs edges by evaluating agent responses to determine relevance ordering, performs directed message passing from highly relevant to less relevant agents (and reverse), and aggregates updated responses through graph-based pooling (max or mean).

Result: GoA achieves superior performance on diverse benchmarks (MMLU, MMLU-Pro, GPQA, MATH, HumanEval, MedMCQA) using only 3 selected agents, outperforming multi-agent baselines that use all 6 available agents, demonstrating both effectiveness and efficiency.

Conclusion: The graph-based approach of GoA provides scalable and effective multi-agent LLM coordination through structured communication, positioning it as a promising solution for navigating the growing LLM landscape and improving task performance.

Abstract: With an ever-growing zoo of LLMs and benchmarks, the need to orchestrate multiple models for improved task performance has never been more pressing. While frameworks like Mixture-of-Agents (MoA) attempt to coordinate LLMs, they often fall short in terms of (1) selecting relevant agents, (2) facilitating effective intra-agent communication, and (3) integrating responses efficiently. In this work, we propose Graph-of-Agents (GoA), a new graph-based framework for modeling multi-agent LLM communication. Our approach begins with node sampling, selecting only the most relevant agents by leveraging model cards that summarize each model's domain, task specialization, and other characteristics. Next, we construct edges between the selected agents by evaluating their responses against one another to determine relevance ordering. Directed message passing is then performed from highly relevant agents to less relevant ones to enhance their responses, followed by reverse message passing to refine the original responses of the more relevant agents. Finally, the updated responses are aggregated via graph-based pooling (e.g., max or mean pooling) to produce a single, unified answer. We evaluate GoA on diverse multi-domain benchmarks (MMLU, MMLU-Pro, GPQA) and domain-specific benchmarks (MATH, HumanEval, MedMCQA), with an agent pool of 6 LLMs spanning multiple domains. Surprisingly, GoA achieves superior performance using only 3 selected agents, outperforming recent multi-agent LLM baselines that utilize all 6 agents simultaneously. By adopting a graph structure, GoA offers both scalability and effectiveness through structured message passing-positioning it as a strong candidate for navigating the challenges of the ever-growing LLM zoo. Code is available at: https://github.com/UNITES-Lab/GoA.

</details>


### [51] [Beyond the Basics: Leveraging Large Language Model for Fine-Grained Medical Entity Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17214)
*Nwe Ni Win,Jim Basilakis,Steven Thomas,Seyhan Yazar,Laura Pierce,Stephanie Liu,Paul M. Middleton,Nasser Ghadiri,X. Rosalind Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLaMA3 outperforms zero-shot and few-shot methods in fine-grained medical entity recognition when fine-tuned with LoRA, achieving 81.24% F1 score on 18 clinical categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM evaluations for medical entity recognition focus on general entity types, offering limited utility for real-world clinical needs that require finer-grained extraction from unstructured medical narratives.

Method: Evaluated open-source LLaMA3 for fine-grained MER across 18 clinical categories using three paradigms: zero-shot, few-shot, and fine-tuning with LoRA. Introduced two novel example selection methods (token- and sentence-level embedding similarity using BioBERT) to enhance few-shot learning, and maintained methodological consistency by using unified LLaMA3 backbone for all strategies.

Result: Fine-tuned LLaMA3 surpassed zero-shot by 63.11% and few-shot by 35.63%, achieving an F1 score of 81.24% for granular medical entity extraction.

Conclusion: Fine-tuning open-source LLMs like LLaMA3 with LoRA is highly effective for fine-grained medical entity recognition, significantly outperforming zero-shot and few-shot approaches, and offers a viable solution for practical clinical NLP applications.

Abstract: Extracting clinically relevant information from unstructured medical narratives such as admission notes, discharge summaries, and emergency case histories remains a challenge in clinical natural language processing (NLP). Medical Entity Recognition (MER) identifies meaningful concepts embedded in these records. Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have shown competitive MER performance; however, evaluations often focus on general entity types, offering limited utility for real-world clinical needs requiring finer-grained extraction. To address this gap, we rigorously evaluated the open-source LLaMA3 model for fine-grained medical entity recognition across 18 clinically detailed categories. To optimize performance, we employed three learning paradigms: zero-shot, few-shot, and fine-tuning with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). To further enhance few-shot learning, we introduced two example selection methods based on token- and sentence-level embedding similarity, utilizing a pre-trained BioBERT model. Unlike prior work assessing zero-shot and few-shot performance on proprietary models (e.g., GPT-4) or fine-tuning different architectures, we ensured methodological consistency by applying all strategies to a unified LLaMA3 backbone, enabling fair comparison across learning settings. Our results showed that fine-tuned LLaMA3 surpasses zero-shot and few-shot approaches by 63.11% and 35.63%, respectivel respectively, achieving an F1 score of 81.24% in granular medical entity extraction.

</details>


### [52] [Yanasse: Finding New Proofs from Deep Vision's Analogies, Part 1](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17229)
*Alexandre Linhares*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Project Yanasse discovers new mathematical proofs by transferring tactic patterns from distant domains using GPU-accelerated analogy matching, achieving 40% success rate in transferring from Probability to Representation Theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to automate mathematical discovery by leveraging proof strategy patterns across different areas of mathematics, rather than reinventing approaches for each domain. Current AI theorem proving systems often struggle with cross-domain generalization.

Method: 1. Extract tactic usage distributions from 27 Mathlib areas (217,133 proof states)
2. Compute z-scores to identify tactics overused in source vs. target areas
3. Match source/target proof states via GPU-accelerated NP-hard analogy matching
4. Use AI reasoning agent to semantically adapt source tactic patterns to target theorems (not just symbol substitution)
5. Key insight: tactic schemas decompose into domain-specific heads and domain-general modifiers

Result: In first study (Probability -> Representation Theory): 4 Lean-verified new proofs out of 10 attempts (40% success rate). All proofs compile without "sorry" declarations. The system successfully transferred tactic patterns like filter upwards modified as [LIST] with ω to ext1 + simp [LIST] + rfl.

Conclusion: Tactic patterns can transfer across distant mathematical domains with semantic adaptation, and the matching engine is domain-independent, only requiring domain-specific relation extraction. The head/modifier decomposition of tactic schemas is crucial for successful transfer.

Abstract: Project Yanasse presents a method for discovering new proofs of theorems in one area of mathematics by transferring proof strategy patterns (e.g., Lean 4 tactic invocation patterns) from a structurally distant area. The system extracts tactic usage distributions across 27 top-level areas of Mathlib (217,133 proof states), computes z-scores to identify tactics that are heavily used in a source area but rare or absent in a target area, matches source and target proof states via GPU-accelerated NP-hard analogy (running on a MacBook Air via Apple's MPS backend), and then asks an AI reasoning agent to semantically adapt--not symbol-substitute--the source tactics invocation pattern to the target theorem. In this first part of the study, the method is applied to the pair Probability -> Representation Theory, producing 4 Lean-verified new proofs out of 10 attempts (40%). The proofs compile with zero sorry declarations. The key finding is that tactic schemas decompose into a head (domain-gated, rarely transfers) and a modifier (domain-general, often transfers): filter upwards's head fails in representation theory (no Filter structure), but its [LIST] with ω modifier transfers cleanly as ext1 + simp [LIST] + rfl. Crucially, the underlying matching engine--deep vision lib.py--is entirely domain independent: the same optimization code for an NP-hard matching that matches chess positions by analogy matches Lean proof states by analogy, without knowing which domain it is processing. Only a relation extractor is domain-specific.

</details>


### [53] [Safe and Policy-Compliant Multi-Agent Orchestration for Enterprise AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17240)
*Vinil Pasupuleti,Shyalendar Reddy Allala,Siva Rama Krishna Varma Bayyavarapu,Shrey Tyagi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CAMCO is a runtime coordination layer for enterprise multi-agent systems that ensures policy compliance and risk management through constrained optimization, enabling zero policy violations while maintaining high utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enterprise AI systems need to deploy multiple intelligent agents across mission-critical workflows while satisfying hard policy constraints (SOX, HIPAA, GDPR), bounded risk exposure, and comprehensive auditability. Existing coordination methods optimize expected reward but treat constraints implicitly, lacking formal guarantees for compliance.

Method: CAMCO models multi-agent decision-making as a constrained optimization problem with three integrated mechanisms: (1) constraint projection engine enforcing policy-feasible actions via convex projection, (2) adaptive risk-weighted Lagrangian utility shaping, and (3) iterative negotiation protocol with provably bounded convergence. It operates as deployment-time middleware compatible with any agent architecture.

Result: Evaluation across three enterprise scenarios shows zero policy violations, risk exposure below threshold (mean ratio 0.71), 92-97% utility retention compared to unconstrained baselines, and mean convergence in 2.4 iterations. Outperforms constrained Lagrangian MARL baseline.

Conclusion: CAMCO provides an effective runtime coordination solution for enterprise multi-agent systems that guarantees policy compliance and risk management while maintaining high operational utility, with direct integration capabilities to production policy engines like OPA.

Abstract: Enterprise AI systems increasingly deploy multiple intelligent agents across mission-critical workflows that must satisfy hard policy constraints, bounded risk exposure, and comprehensive auditability (SOX, HIPAA, GDPR). Existing coordination methods - cooperative MARL, consensus protocols, and centralized planners - optimize expected reward while treating constraints implicitly. This paper introduces CAMCO (Constraint-Aware Multi-Agent Cognitive Orchestration), a runtime coordination layer that models multi-agent decision-making as a constrained optimization problem. CAMCO integrates three mechanisms: (i) a constraint projection engine enforcing policy-feasible actions via convex projection, (ii) adaptive risk-weighted Lagrangian utility shaping, and (iii) an iterative negotiation protocol with provably bounded convergence. Unlike training-time constrained RL, CAMCO operates as deployment-time middleware compatible with any agent architecture, with policy predicates designed for direct integration with production engines such as OPA. Evaluation across three enterprise scenarios - including comparison against a constrained Lagrangian MARL baseline - demonstrates zero policy violations, risk exposure below threshold (mean ratio 0.71), 92-97% utility retention, and mean convergence in 2.4 iterations.

</details>


### [54] [Rectification Difficulty and Optimal Sample Allocation in LLM-Augmented Surveys](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17267)
*Zikun Ye,Hema Yoganarasimhan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework for optimally allocating human survey respondents when cheap LLM predictions are available, using rectification difficulty estimation and meta-learning to achieve efficiency gains without pilot data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large Language Models can generate synthetic survey responses at low cost, but their accuracy varies unpredictably across different questions. There's a need for efficient allocation of limited human respondent budgets across multiple estimation tasks when cheap LLM predictions are available.

Method: 1) Characterizes question-specific rectification difficulty using Prediction-Powered Inference. 2) Derives closed-form optimal allocation rule directing more human labels to tasks where LLM is least reliable. 3) Proposes meta-learning approach trained on historical data to predict rectification difficulty for new surveys without pilot data. Framework extends to general M-estimation including regression coefficients and multinomial logit partworths.

Result: Validated on two datasets spanning different domains, question types, and LLMs. The approach captures 61-79% of theoretically attainable efficiency gains, achieving 11.4% and 10.5% MSE reductions without requiring any pilot human data for target surveys.

Conclusion: The framework provides an effective method for optimal human respondent allocation when LLM predictions are available, enabling significant efficiency improvements in survey estimation tasks without needing pilot data for new surveys.

Abstract: Large Language Models can generate synthetic survey responses at low cost, but their accuracy varies unpredictably across questions. We study the design problem of allocating a fixed budget of human respondents across estimation tasks when cheap LLM predictions are available for every task. Our framework combines three components. First, building on Prediction-Powered Inference, we characterize a question-specific rectification difficulty that governs how quickly the estimator's variance decreases with human sample size. Second, we derive a closed-form optimal allocation rule that directs more human labels to tasks where the LLM is least reliable. Third, since rectification difficulty depends on unobserved human responses for new surveys, we propose a meta-learning approach, trained on historical data, that predicts it for entirely new tasks without pilot data. The framework extends to general M-estimation, covering regression coefficients and multinomial logit partworths for conjoint analysis. We validate the framework on two datasets spanning different domains, question types, and LLMs, showing that our approach captures 61-79% of the theoretically attainable efficiency gains, achieving 11.4% and 10.5% MSE reductions without requiring any pilot human data for the target survey.

</details>


### [55] [The Continuity Layer: Why Intelligence Needs an Architecture for What It Carries Forward](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17273)
*Samuel Sameer Tanguturi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A position paper arguing that AI lacks a continuity layer that preserves understanding across sessions/contexts, proposes a specific memory architecture (Decomposed Trace Convergence Memory) to solve this, and suggests governance/privacy implications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI systems are "per-session powerful but amnesiac across time" - they lose accumulated understanding when sessions end or context windows fill. Memory APIs provide only flat facts that must be reinterpreted from scratch each time, preventing true learning continuity.

Method: Defines continuity as a system property with seven required characteristics distinct from memory/retrieval; proposes Decomposed Trace Convergence Memory (DTCM) architecture that uses write-time decomposition and read-time reconstruction; maps to theological patterns; outlines four-layer development arc; and proposes governance via physics-based privacy.

Result: Introduces ATANT benchmark (arXiv:2604.06710) for evaluating continuity properties on a 250-story corpus; positions framework against existing memory benchmarks; establishes theoretical foundation for continuity layer as crucial missing infrastructure in AI.

Conclusion: The continuity layer is the most consequential missing infrastructure in AI, becoming increasingly important as model physics limits constrain scaling. Its implementation requires novel memory architecture (DTCM) and governance approaches where privacy is physics-based rather than policy-based, with architectural commitments inseparable from the product.

Abstract: The most important architectural problem in AI is not the size of the model but the absence of a layer that carries forward what the model has come to understand. Sessions end. Context windows fill. Memory APIs return flat facts that the model has to reinterpret from scratch on every read. The result is intelligence that is powerful per session and amnesiac across time. This position paper argues that the layer which fixes this, the continuity layer, is the most consequential piece of infrastructure the field has not yet built, and that the engineering work to build it has begun in public. The formal evaluation framework for the property described here is the ATANT benchmark (arXiv:2604.06710), published separately with evaluation results on a 250-story corpus; a companion paper (arXiv:2604.10981) positions this framework against existing memory, long-context, and agentic-memory benchmarks. The paper defines continuity as a system property with seven required characteristics, distinct from memory and from retrieval; describes a storage primitive (Decomposed Trace Convergence Memory) whose write-time decomposition and read-time reconstruction produce that property; maps the engineering architecture to the theological pattern of kenosis and the symbolic pattern of Alpha and Omega, and argues this mapping is structural rather than metaphorical; proposes a four-layer development arc from external SDK to hardware node to long-horizon human infrastructure; examines why the physics limits now constraining the model layer make the continuity layer newly consequential; and argues that the governance architecture (privacy implemented as physics rather than policy, founder-controlled class shares on non-negotiable architectural commitments) is inseparable from the product itself.

</details>


### [56] [HalluClear: Diagnosing, Evaluating and Mitigating Hallucinations in GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17284)
*Chao Jin,Wenkui Yang,Hao Sun,Yuqi Liao,Qianyi Jiang,Kai Zhou,Jie Cao,Ran He,Huaibo Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HalluClear is a comprehensive suite for mitigating hallucinations in GUI agents through taxonomy, evaluation workflow, and mitigation scheme, achieving significant improvement with just 9K training samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GUI agents suffer from ungrounded hallucinations causing cascading failures in real-world deployments, and the field lacks a hallucination-focused framework for diagnosis, evaluation, and mitigation unlike general VLM domains.

Method: 1) GUI-specific hallucination taxonomy from empirical failure analysis; 2) Three-stage evaluation workflow with VLM-as-a-judge reliability enhancement via expert-annotated benchmarking and ensemble credibility estimation; 3) Mitigation scheme using closed-loop structured reasoning with lightweight continual post-training and cold-start initialization.

Result: Post-training on only 9K samples within the HalluClear suite significantly reduces hallucinations, improving grounding and action fidelity across representative agents and public benchmarks.

Conclusion: HalluClear offers a compute-efficient pathway to robust GUI automation by addressing hallucination issues through a comprehensive suite as a complement to computation-intensive scaling approaches.

Abstract: While progress in GUI agents has been largely driven by industrial-scale training, ungrounded hallucinations often trigger cascading failures in real-world deployments.Unlike general VLM domains, the GUI agent field lacks a hallucination-focused suite for fine-grained diagnosis, reliable evaluation, and targeted mitigation.To bridge this gap, we introduce HalluClear, a comprehensive suite for hallucination mitigation in GUI agents as a complement to computation-intensive scaling. HalluClear comprises: (1) a GUI-specific hallucination taxonomy derived from empirical failure analysis; (2) a calibrated three-stage evaluation workflow which enhances VLM-as-a-judge reliability via expert-annotated benchmarking and ensemble credibility estimation; and (3) a mitigation scheme based on closed-loop structured reasoning, enabling lightweight continual post-training with cold-start initialization for both generalist and GUI-specialist agents. Experiments across representative agents and public benchmarks demonstrate that post-training on only 9K samples within our suite can significantly reduce hallucinations, thereby improving grounding and action fidelity, offering a compute-efficient pathway to robust GUI automation.

</details>


### [57] [LLaTiSA: Towards Difficulty-Stratified Time Series Reasoning from Visual Perception to Semantics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17295)
*Yueyang Ding,HaoPeng Zhang,Rui Dai,Yi Wang,Tianyu Zong,Kaikui Liu,Xiangxiang Chu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces HiTSR, a hierarchical time series reasoning benchmark with 83k samples, and proposes LLaTiSA, a Time Series Reasoning Model that integrates visual patterns with numerical tables to enhance LLM performance on time series tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current research in time series understanding by LLMs suffers from fragmented task definitions, ambiguous benchmarks, and lack of rigorous evaluation frameworks, preventing the development of unified Time Series Reasoning Models.

Method: 1) Formalize Time Series Reasoning via a four-level taxonomy of cognitive complexity. 2) Introduce HiTSR dataset with 83k samples and verified Chain-of-Thought trajectories. 3) Propose LLaTiSA model integrating visualized patterns with precision-calibrated numerical tables. 4) Use multi-stage curriculum fine-tuning strategy.

Result: LLaTiSA achieves superior performance and exhibits robust out-of-distribution generalization across diverse TSR tasks and real-world scenarios.

Conclusion: The paper provides a comprehensive framework for time series reasoning evaluation and demonstrates that integrating visual patterns with numerical data through multi-stage curriculum fine-tuning enables LLMs to achieve strong performance on hierarchical time series reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Comprehensive understanding of time series remains a significant challenge for Large Language Models (LLMs). Current research is hindered by fragmented task definitions and benchmarks with inherent ambiguities, precluding rigorous evaluation and the development of unified Time Series Reasoning Models(TSRMs). To bridge this gap, we formalize Time Series Reasoning (TSR) via a four-level taxonomy of increasing cognitive complexity. We introduce HiTSR, a hierarchical time series reasoning dataset comprising 83k samples with diverse task combinations and verified Chain-of-Thought (CoT) trajectories. Leveraging HiTSR, we propose LLaTiSA, a strong TSRM that integrates visualized patterns with precision-calibrated numerical tables to enhance the temporal perception of Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Through a multi-stage curriculum fine-tuning strategy, LLaTiSA achieves superior performance and exhibits robust out-of-distribution generalization across diverse TSR tasks and real-world scenarios. Our code is available at https://github.com/RainingNovember/LLaTiSA.

</details>


### [58] [Efficient Test-Time Scaling via Temporal Reasoning Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17304)
*Jiakun Li,Xingwei He,Kefan Li,Hongzheng Chai,Hongyue Yu,Yuan Yuan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TRACE: A training-free framework that uses temporal aggregation of multi-step evidence (answer consistency + confidence trajectory) to detect reasoning convergence and dynamically terminate test-time scaling, reducing token usage by 25-30% while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test-time scaling improves reasoning but causes token-inefficient overthinking where models continue reasoning beyond what's necessary. Existing dynamic early-exit methods rely on unreliable single-step confidence signals that don't properly detect reasoning convergence in multi-step settings.

Method: TRACE uses training-free temporal aggregation of two complementary signals across recent reasoning steps: 1) answer consistency (persistence of predicted answers) and 2) confidence trajectory (temporal evolution of model confidence). This allows accurate detection of reasoning convergence to promptly halt inference and avoid redundant steps.

Result: TRACE reduces reasoning token usage by 25-30% on average across multiple challenging benchmarks while maintaining accuracy within 1-2% of full-length reasoning. It consistently outperforms existing dynamic reasoning methods.

Conclusion: Temporal aggregation of multi-step evidence (combining answer consistency and confidence trajectory) provides a more reliable approach than single-step confidence signals for detecting reasoning convergence, enabling efficient test-time scaling that substantially reduces token usage without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: Test-time scaling improves the reasoning performance of large language models but often results in token-inefficient overthinking, where models continue reasoning beyond what is necessary for a correct answer. Existing dynamic early-exit methods typically rely on single-step confidence signals, which are often unreliable for detecting reasoning convergence in multi-step settings. To mitigate this limitation, we propose TRACE, a training-free framework for efficient test-time scaling that determines when to terminate reasoning based on temporal aggregation of multi-step evidence rather than instantaneous signals. TRACE detects reasoning convergence over time by aggregating two complementary signals across recent reasoning steps: answer consistency, capturing the persistence of predicted answers, and confidence trajectory, modeling the temporal evolution of model confidence. Benefiting from these two factors, TRACE can accurately determine whether the reasoning process has converged, thereby promptly halting inference and effectively avoiding redundant reasoning steps. Extensive experiments on multiple challenging benchmarks show that TRACE reduces reasoning token usage by 25-30% on average while maintaining accuracy within 1-2% of full-length reasoning, consistently outperforming existing dynamic reasoning methods.

</details>


### [59] [SkillFlow:Benchmarking Lifelong Skill Discovery and Evolution for Autonomous Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17308)
*Ziao Zhang,Kou Shi,Shiting Huang,Avery Nie,Yu Zeng,Yiming Zhao,Zhen Fang,Qishen Su,Haibo Qiu,Wei Yang,Qingnan Ren,Shun Zou,Wenxuan Huang,Lin Chen,Zehui Chen,Feng Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillFlow is a benchmark of 166 tasks across 20 families that evaluates autonomous agents' ability to discover, repair, and maintain skills over time through lifelong learning, revealing significant capability gaps in current models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks mainly test whether models can use provided skills, but don't evaluate whether agents can discover skills from experience, repair them after failure, and maintain a coherent library over time. There's a need for structured evaluation of lifelong skill evolution in autonomous agents.

Method: Created SkillFlow benchmark with 166 tasks across 20 families using Domain-Agnostic Execution Flow (DAEF) for consistent workflows. Implemented Agentic Lifelong Learning protocol where agents start without skills, solve tasks sequentially, externalize lessons through trajectory- and rubric-driven skill patches, and carry updated skill libraries forward.

Result: Significant capability gap revealed: Claude Opus 4.6 improves task success from 62.65% to 71.08% (+8.43 points) with lifelong skill evolution. However, high skill usage doesn't guarantee high utility - Kimi K2.5 gains only +0.60 points despite 66.87% skill usage, and Qwen-Coder-Next regresses relative to vanilla setting with only 44.58% task completion rate.

Conclusion: SkillFlow provides a structured testbed for evaluating skill discovery, patching, transfer, and their failure modes under lifelong evaluation. The benchmark reveals current limitations in autonomous agents' ability to effectively evolve and maintain skills over time, highlighting important research challenges in this direction.

Abstract: As the capability frontier of autonomous agents continues to expand, they are increasingly able to complete specialized tasks through plug-and-play external skills. Yet current benchmarks mostly test whether models can use provided skills, leaving open whether they can discover skills from experience, repair them after failure, and maintain a coherent library over time. We introduce SkillFlow, a benchmark of 166 tasks across 20 families in which task construction within each family follows a Domain-Agnostic Execution Flow (DAEF) that defines an agent workflow framework, allowing these tasks to share a consistent workflow. Agents are evaluated under an Agentic Lifelong Learning protocol in which they begin without skills, solve tasks sequentially within each family, externalize lessons through trajectory- and rubric-driven skill patches, and carry the updated library forward. Experiments reveal a substantial capability gap. For Claude Opus 4.6, lifelong skill evolution improves task success from 62.65% to 71.08% (+8.43 points). However, high skill usage does not necessarily imply high utility: Kimi K2.5 gains only +0.60 points despite 66.87% skill usage, while Qwen-Coder-Next reaches only a 44.58% task completion rate and still regresses relative to the vanilla setting. SkillFlow contributes a structured testbed for this direction and an in-depth empirical analysis of skill discovery, patching, transfer, and their failure modes under lifelong evaluation.

</details>


### [60] [Knows: Agent-Native Structured Research Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17309)
*Guangsheng Yu,Xu Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Knows introduces a lightweight YAML sidecar specification that adds structured claims, evidence, and provenance to research artifacts, enabling LLM agents to access fine-grained information without reading full PDFs, dramatically improving comprehension accuracy while reducing token consumption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Research artifacts are primarily distributed as reader-oriented PDFs, creating bottlenecks for agent-assisted research workflows where LLM agents need to infer fine-grained information from lengthy documents, which is expensive, repetitive, and unstable at scale.

Method: A lightweight companion specification called Knows that uses thin YAML sidecar files (KnowsRecord) coexisting with original PDFs, requiring no changes to the publication itself, with deterministic schema validation through a linter.

Result: Evaluation on 140 comprehension questions across 20 papers from 14 disciplines shows weak models (0.8B-2B parameters) improve from 19-25% to 47-67% accuracy (+29 to +42 percentage points) using sidecars instead of PDFs while consuming 29-86% fewer tokens. Sidecar accuracy (75-77%) approaches stronger-model PDF accuracy (78-83%).

Conclusion: Knows successfully bridges the gap between human-readable research artifacts and machine-consumable data, demonstrating substantial performance improvements for LLM agents, with evidence of scalability through a community sidecar hub indexing over ten thousand publications.

Abstract: Research artifacts are distributed primarily as reader-oriented documents like PDFs. This creates a bottleneck for increasingly agent-assisted and agent-native research workflows, in which LLM agents need to infer fine-grained, task-relevant information from lengthy full documents, a process that is expensive, repetitive, and unstable at scale.
  We introduce Knows, a lightweight companion specification that binds structured claims, evidence, provenance, and verifiable relations to existing research artifacts in a form LLM agents can consume directly. Knows addresses the gap with a thin YAML sidecar (KnowsRecord) that coexists with the original PDF, requiring no changes to the publication itself, and validated by a deterministic schema linter. We evaluate Knows on 140 comprehension questions across 20 papers spanning 14 academic disciplines, comparing PDF-only, sidecar-only, and hybrid conditions across six LLM agents of varying capacity. Weak models (0.8B--2B parameters) improve from 19--25\% to 47--67\% accuracy (+29 to +42 percentage points) when reading sidecar instead of PDF, while consuming 29--86\% fewer input tokens; an LLM-as-judge re-scoring confirms that weak-model sidecar accuracy (75--77\%) approaches stronger-model PDF accuracy (78--83\%). Beyond this controlled evaluation, a community sidecar hub at https://knows.academy/ has already indexed over ten thousand publications and continues to grow daily, providing independent evidence that the format is adoption-ready at scale.

</details>


### [61] [AutoSearch: Adaptive Search Depth for Efficient Agentic RAG via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17337)
*Jingbo Sun,Wenyue Chong,Songjun Tu,Qichao Zhang,Yaocheng Zhang,Jiajun Chai,Xiaohan Wang,Wei Lin,Guojun Yin,Dongbin Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoSearch: An RL framework that learns to identify minimal sufficient search depth in agentic RAG systems, balancing accuracy and efficiency by self-generating intermediate answers to determine when to stop searching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agentic RAG systems with multi-step retrieval often suffer from redundant search steps, incurring high computational costs and latency. While limiting search depth reduces costs, it risks underexploration for complex questions. There's a need to find an optimal balance between search accuracy and efficiency.

Method: 1. Investigated how search depth affects accuracy and identified minimal sufficient search depth determined by question complexity and agent capability. 2. Proposed AutoSearch, a reinforcement learning framework that evaluates each search step via self-generated intermediate answers. 3. Used self-answering mechanism to identify minimal sufficient search depth and reward attainment while penalizing over-searching. 4. Introduced additional reward mechanisms to stabilize search behavior and improve answer quality on complex questions.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that AutoSearch achieves superior accuracy-efficiency trade-off compared to prior approaches. It effectively alleviates over-searching problems while preserving search quality, outperforming existing methods that simply limit search depth.

Conclusion: AutoSearch provides an effective solution to the efficiency-accuracy trade-off in agentic RAG systems by learning to determine optimal search depth dynamically through RL and self-answering mechanisms, enabling efficient information retrieval without compromising on complex question answering.

Abstract: Agentic retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems enable large language models (LLMs) to solve complex tasks through multi-step interaction with external retrieval tools. However, such multi-step interaction often involves redundant search steps, incurring substantial computational cost and latency. Prior work limits search depth (i.e., the number of search steps) to reduce cost, but this often leads to underexploration of complex questions. To address this, we first investigate how search depth affects accuracy and find a minimal sufficient search depth that defines an accuracy-efficiency trade-off, jointly determined by question complexity and the agent's capability. Furthermore, we propose AutoSearch, a reinforcement learning (RL) framework that evaluates each search step via self-generated intermediate answers. By a self-answering mechanism, AutoSearch identifies the minimal sufficient search depth and promotes efficient search by rewarding its attainment while penalizing over-searching. In addition, reward mechanisms are introduced to stabilize search behavior and improve answer quality on complex questions. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show that AutoSearch achieves a superior accuracy-efficiency trade-off, alleviating over-searching while preserving search quality.

</details>


### [62] [Formal Foundations of Agentic Business Process Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17347)
*Giuseppe De Giacomo,Timotheus Kampik,Lukas Kirchdorfer,Marco Montali,Christoph Weinhuber*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Mathematical foundations for agentic BPM systems where multiple autonomous agents execute processes with explicit objectives and guardrails on decision-making strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional BPM systems rely on controlled execution, but agentic BPM involves multiple autonomous decision-makers (agents) that cannot be fully controlled, requiring new formal foundations to manage their behavior through objectives and strategy-level guardrails.

Method: Sets up mathematical foundations for agentic BPM systems in three key settings and analyzes four foundational problems. Focuses on augmenting process specifications with explicit objectives/goals for participating agents and establishing guardrails at the strategy level.

Result: Provides formal mathematical foundations for agentic BPM systems, enabling analysis of processes driven by autonomous agents with assigned objectives and controlled through strategy-level guardrails.

Conclusion: Agentic BPM requires new mathematical frameworks to handle autonomous agents in business processes, with process specifications augmented by explicit objectives and strategy-level guardrails to ensure desired outcomes despite agent autonomy.

Abstract: Just like traditional BPM systems, agentic BPM systems are built around a specification of the process under consideration. Their distinguishing feature, however, is that the execution of the process is driven by multiple autonomous decision-makers, referred to as agents. Since such agents cannot be fully controlled, the process specification is augmented with explicit objectives, or goals, assigned to the participating agents. Agents then pursue these goals, at least to the best of their efforts, under suitable assumptions on the behavior of others, by adopting appropriate strategies. Centrally, the organization enacting the process can use these specifications to provide guardrails on the decision-making capabilities of agents at the strategy level. This paper sets up the mathematical foundations of such systems in three key settings and analyzes four foundational problems of agentic BPM.

</details>


### [63] [SOCIA-EVO: Automated Simulator Construction via Dual-Anchored Bi-Level Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17351)
*Yuncheng Hua,Sion Weatherhead,Mehdi Jafari,Hao Xue,Flora D. Salim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SOCIA-EVO is an evolutionary framework for automated simulator construction that addresses failure modes in long-horizon LLM agents through dual-anchored optimization, static blueprint constraints, and self-curating strategy management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated simulator construction requires distributional fidelity beyond generic code generation. Long-horizon LLM agents suffer from two key failure modes: contextual drift and optimization instability, which arise from conflating structural and parametric errors during simulator development.

Method: SOCIA-EVO proposes a dual-anchored evolutionary framework with three components: (1) a static blueprint to enforce empirical constraints, (2) bi-level optimization that decouples structural refinement from parameter calibration, and (3) a self-curating Strategy Playbook that manages remedial hypotheses via Bayesian-weighted retrieval and falsifies ineffective strategies through execution feedback.

Result: SOCIA-EVO achieves robust convergence and generates simulators that are statistically consistent with observational data by maintaining distributional fidelity through its evolutionary approach.

Conclusion: The SOCIA-EVO framework successfully addresses the specific challenges of automated simulator construction by separating structural and parametric concerns, enabling the creation of statistically valid simulators through an evolutionary process with self-correction mechanisms.

Abstract: Automated simulator construction requires distributional fidelity, distinguishing it from generic code generation. We identify two failure modes in long-horizon LLM agents: contextual drift and optimization instability arising from conflating structural and parametric errors. We propose SOCIA-EVO, a dual-anchored evolutionary framework. SOCIA-EVO introduces: (1) a static blueprint to enforce empirical constraints; (2) a bi-level optimization to decouple structural refinement from parameter calibration; and (3) a self-curating Strategy Playbook that manages remedial hypotheses via Bayesian-weighted retrieval. By falsifying ineffective strategies through execution feedback, SOCIA-EVO achieves robust convergence, generating simulators that are statistically consistent with observational data. The code and data of SOCIA-EVO are available here: https://github.com/cruiseresearchgroup/SOCIA/tree/evo.

</details>


### [64] [Hive: A Multi-Agent Infrastructure for Algorithm- and Task-Level Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17353)
*Zizhang Luo,Yuhao Luo,Youwei Xiao,Yansong Xu,Runlin Guo,Yun Liang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hive is a multi-agent infrastructure that enables algorithm- and task-level scaling through logits caching and agent-aware scheduling to reduce redundancy and optimize resource allocation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agentic systems have limitations: at algorithm level, allocating additional inference-time computation creates cross-path redundancy through overlapping computations across reasoning branches; at task level, existing infrastructures' scheduling is unaware of multiple agents, missing optimization opportunities for resource allocation despite task decomposition possibilities.

Method: Hive introduces a description frontend for capturing per-agent behavior and test-time scaling algorithms. The backend includes two key mechanisms: 1) Logits Cache that reuses intermediate logits across redundant sampling paths to mitigate cross-path redundancy, and 2) Agent-Aware Scheduling that efficiently allocates compute and KV-cache resources according to agent contributions.

Result: Experiments show Logits Cache achieves average speedup of 1.11×-1.76× for re-sampling, and Agent-Aware Scheduling reduces the hotspot miss rate by 33%-51%.

Conclusion: Hive successfully addresses algorithm- and task-level scaling challenges in multi-agent systems through novel caching and scheduling mechanisms that reduce redundancy and optimize resource utilization, enabling more efficient deployment of complex agentic systems.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as complex agentic systems that scale with task complexity. While prior work has extensively explored model- and system-level scaling, algorithm- and task-level scaling remain largely unaddressed, constraining the full potential of agentic systems. At the algorithm level, allocating additional inference-time computation can enhance workflow capacity but introduces cross-path redundancy: overlapping computations across multiple reasoning branches. At the task level, complex tasks can be decomposed into subproblems and delegated across multiple agents for improved scalability and parallelism. However, existing infrastructures' scheduling is unaware of the existence of multiple agents, missing opportunities to optimize resource allocation.
  We propose Hive, a multi-agent infrastructure that enables algorithm- and task-level scaling. Hive features a description frontend that captures per-agent behavior and supports test-time scaling algorithms. Leveraging this specification, our backend introduces two key mechanisms: Logits Cache that reuses intermediate logits across redundant sampling paths to mitigate cross-path redundancy at the algorithm level, and Agent-Aware Scheduling that efficiently allocates compute and KV-cache resources according to agent contributions at the task level. Experiments show that Logits Cache achieves an average speedup of $1.11\times$-$1.76\times$ for re-sampling, and Agent-Aware Scheduling reduces the hotspot miss rate by $33\%$-$51\%$.

</details>


### [65] [T-DuMpRa: Teacher-guided Dual-path Multi-prototype Retrieval Augmented framework for fine-grained medical image classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17360)
*Zixuan Tang,Shen Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: T-DuMpRa: A teacher-guided dual-path framework combining discriminative classification with multi-prototype retrieval to handle visually ambiguous cases in fine-grained medical image classification by activating retrieval only when classifier predictions are uncertain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-grained medical image classification faces challenges with subtle inter-class variations and visually ambiguous cases where purely discriminative classifiers may achieve high overall accuracy but fail to distinguish between highly similar categories, leading to miscalibrated predictions.

Method: Proposes T-DuMpRa framework with: 1) Joint optimization of cross-entropy and supervised contrastive objectives for cosine-compatible embedding geometry; 2) EMA teacher for smoother representations and multi-prototype memory bank via clustering; 3) Conservative confidence-gated fusion that activates retrieval only when classifier predictions are uncertain and retrieval evidence is decisive and conflicting.

Result: On HAM10000 and ISIC2019 datasets, the method yields 0.68%-0.21% and 0.44%-2.69% improvements on 5 different backbones. Visualization analysis demonstrates enhanced ability to handle visually ambiguous cases.

Conclusion: T-DuMpRa is a plug-and-play framework that can be easily integrated into existing classification models to improve performance on visually ambiguous cases by combining discriminative classification with multi-prototype retrieval through intelligent fusion mechanisms.

Abstract: Fine-grained medical image classification is challenged by subtle inter-class variations and visually ambiguous cases, where confidence estimates often exhibit uncertainty rather than being overconfident. In such scenarios, purely discriminative classifiers may achieve high overall accuracy yet still fail to distinguish between highly similar categories, leading to miscalibrated predictions. We propose T-DuMpRa, a teacher-guided dual-path multi-prototype retrieval-augmented framework, where discriminative classification and multi-prototype retrieval jointly drive both training and prediction. During training, we jointly optimize cross-entropy and supervised contrastive objectives to learn a cosine-compatible embedding geometry for reliable prototype matching. We further employ an exponential moving average (EMA) teacher to obtain smoother representations and build a multi-prototype memory bank by clustering teacher embeddings in the teacher embedding space. Our framework is plug-and-play: it can be easily integrated into existing classification models by constructing a compact prototype bank, thereby improving performance on visually ambiguous cases. At inference, we combine the classifier's predicted distribution with a similarity-based distribution computed via cosine matching to prototypes, and apply a conservative confidence-gated fusion that activates retrieval only when the classifier's prediction is uncertain and the retrieval evidence is decisive and conflicting, otherwise keeping confident predictions unchanged. On HAM10000 and ISIC2019, our method yields 0.68%-0.21% and 0.44%-2.69% improvements on 5 different backbones. And visualization analysis proves our model can enhance the model's ability to handle visually ambiguous cases.

</details>


### [66] [Beyond Meta-Reasoning: Metacognitive Consolidation for Self-Improving LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17399)
*Ziqing Zhuang,Linhai Zhang,Jiasheng Si,Deyu Zhou,Yulan He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework called Metacognitive Consolidation that enables large language models to accumulate and reuse meta-reasoning skills across different reasoning episodes, improving future performance through experience.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing meta-reasoning methods for LLMs are episodic - they focus on individual instances without accumulating reusable meta-reasoning skills across instances. This leads to recurring failure modes and high metacognitive effort, creating a need for methods that allow knowledge transfer and skill accumulation.

Method: The Metacognitive Consolidation framework structures instance-level problem solving into three roles: reasoning, monitoring, and control to generate rich meta-level traces. These traces are then consolidated through a hierarchical, multi-timescale update mechanism that gradually forms evolving meta-knowledge.

Result: Experimental results show consistent performance gains across benchmarks and backbone models. Performance improves as metacognitive experience accumulates over time, demonstrating the effectiveness of the consolidation approach.

Conclusion: Metacognitive Consolidation provides a novel approach to enhance LLM reasoning by enabling the accumulation and reuse of meta-reasoning skills across instances, addressing limitations of episodic meta-reasoning methods and showing promising results for improved reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong reasoning capabilities, and as existing approaches for enhancing LLM reasoning continue to mature, increasing attention has shifted toward meta-reasoning as a promising direction for further improvement. However, most existing meta-reasoning methods remain episodic: they focus on executing complex meta-reasoning routines within individual instances, but ignore the accumulation of reusable meta-reasoning skills across instances, leading to recurring failure modes and repeatedly high metacognitive effort. In this paper, we introduce Metacognitive Consolidation, a novel framework in which a model consolidates metacognitive experience from past reasoning episodes into reusable knowledge that improves future meta-reasoning. We instantiate this framework by structuring instance-level problem solving into distinct roles for reasoning, monitoring, and control to generate rich, attributable meta-level traces. These traces are then consolidated through a hierarchical, multi-timescale update mechanism that gradually forms evolving meta-knowledge. Experimental results demonstrate consistent performance gains across benchmarks and backbone models, and show that performance improves as metacognitive experience accumulates over time.

</details>


### [67] [Phase-Scheduled Multi-Agent Systems for Token-Efficient Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17400)
*Mohit Dubey*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PSMAS introduces phase-scheduled activation for LLM-powered multi-agent systems, reducing token usage by 27.3% while maintaining performance via temporal coordination on a circular manifold.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent systems using LLMs suffer from token inefficiency due to (i) unstructured parallel execution where all agents activate simultaneously regardless of input readiness, and (ii) unrestricted context sharing where every agent receives full context regardless of relevance. Existing approaches treat coordination as structural allocation and ignore temporal dimensions.

Method: PSMAS models agent activation as continuous control over a shared attention space on a circular manifold. Each agent gets a fixed angular phase derived from task dependency topology. A global sweep signal rotates, activating only agents within an angular window. Idle agents receive compressed context summaries to reduce token consumption. Implemented on LangGraph and evaluated on four structured benchmarks and two unstructured conversational settings.

Result: Achieves mean token reduction of 27.3% (range 21.4-34.8%) while maintaining task performance within 2.1 percentage points of fully activated baseline. Outperforms strongest learned routing baseline by 5.6 percentage points in token reduction with 2.0 percentage points less performance drop. Scheduling alone accounts for 18-20 percentage points of reduction, robust to compression degradation.

Conclusion: PSMAS effectively addresses token inefficiency in LLM-powered multi-agent systems by introducing temporal coordination through phase scheduling and selective context compression. The framework provides substantial token savings while preserving performance, with proven stability, convergence, and optimality results for sweep dynamics.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems (MAS) powered by large language models suffer from severe token inefficiency arising from two compounding sources: (i) unstructured parallel execution, where all agents activate simultaneously irrespective of input readiness; and (ii) unrestricted context sharing, where every agent receives the full accumulated context regardless of relevance. Existing mitigation strategies - static pruning, hierarchical decomposition, and learned routing - treat coordination as a structural allocation problem and fundamentally ignore its temporal dimension. We propose Phase-Scheduled Multi-Agent Systems (PSMAS), a framework that reconceptualizes agent activation as continuous control over a shared attention space modeled on a circular manifold.
  Each agent i is assigned a fixed angular phase theta_i in the range [0, 2*pi], derived from the task dependency topology; a global sweep signal phi(t) rotates at velocity omega, activating only agents within an angular window epsilon. Idle agents receive compressed context summaries, reducing per-step token consumption. We implement PSMAS on LangGraph, evaluate on four structured benchmarks (HotPotQA-MAS, HumanEval-MAS, ALFWorld-Multi, WebArena-Coord) and two unstructured conversational settings, and prove stability, convergence, and optimality results for the sweep dynamics. PSMAS achieves a mean token reduction of 27.3 percent (range 21.4-34.8 percent) while maintaining task performance within 2.1 percentage points of a fully activated baseline (p < 0.01, n = 500 per configuration), and outperforms the strongest learned routing baseline by 5.6 percentage points in token reduction with 2.0 percentage points less performance drop. Crucially, we show that scheduling and compression are independent sources of gain: scheduling alone accounts for 18-20 percentage points of reduction, robust to compression degradation up to alpha = 0.40.

</details>


### [68] [STRIDE: Strategic Iterative Decision-Making for Retrieval-Augmented Multi-Hop Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17405)
*Wei Chen,Lili Zhao,Zhi Zheng,HuiJun Hou,Tong Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: STRIDE is a novel MHQA framework that separates planning, control, and execution to overcome premature entity commitment and uncoordinated reasoning in existing methods, achieving robust multi-hop question answering through strategic reasoning skeletons and dependency-aware supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-hop QA approaches suffer from two major issues: 1) premature commitment to surface-level entities rather than underlying reasoning structures, making question decomposition vulnerable to lexical ambiguity, and 2) overlooking logical dependencies among reasoning steps, resulting in uncoordinated execution.

Method: STRIDE separates strategic planning, dynamic control, and grounded execution. A Meta-Planner constructs entity-agnostic reasoning skeletons to capture abstract query logic. A Supervisor orchestrates sub-question execution with dependency awareness, enabling parallelization when possible. The system dynamically decides when to retrieve new evidence vs. infer from existing facts, while specialized execution modules handle grounded fact extraction and logical inference.

Result: Experiments show STRIDE achieves robust and accurate reasoning. Additionally, STRIDE-FT (a modular fine-tuning framework using self-generated execution trajectories) effectively enhances open-source LLMs without requiring human annotations or stronger teacher models.

Conclusion: STRIDE addresses fundamental limitations in existing MHQA methods by deferring entity grounding until after establishing reasoning structures, coordinating execution dependencies, and explicitly separating retrieval from reasoning, resulting in more robust and accurate multi-hop question answering.

Abstract: Multi-hop question answering (MHQA) enables accurate answers to complex queries by retrieving and reasoning over evidence dispersed across multiple documents. Existing MHQA approaches mainly rely on iterative retrieval-augmented generation, which suffer from the following two major issues. 1) Existing methods prematurely commit to surface-level entities rather than underlying reasoning structures, making question decomposition highly vulnerable to lexical ambiguity. 2) Existing methods overlook the logical dependencies among reasoning steps, resulting in uncoordinated execution. To address these issues, we propose STRIDE, a framework that separates strategic planning, dynamic control, and grounded execution. At its core, a Meta-Planner first constructs an entity-agnostic reasoning skeleton to capture the abstract logic of the query, thereby deferring entity grounding until after the reasoning structure is established, which mitigates disambiguation errors caused by premature lexical commitment. A Supervisor then orchestrates sub-question execution in a dependency-aware manner, enabling efficient parallelization where possible and sequential coordination when necessary. By dynamically deciding whether to retrieve new evidence or infer from existing facts, it avoids redundant queries and error propagation, while fusing cross-branch information and reformulating failed queries to enhance robustness. Grounded fact extraction and logical inference are delegated to specialized execution modules, ensuring faithfulness through explicit separation of retrieval and reasoning. We further propose STRIDE-FT, a modular fine-tuning framework that uses self-generated execution trajectories from STRIDE, requiring neither human annotations nor stronger teacher models. Experiments show that STRIDE achieves robust and accurate reasoning, while STRIDE-FT effectively enhances open-source LLMs.

</details>


### [69] [EvoMaster: A Foundational Agent Framework for Building Evolving Autonomous Scientific Agents at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17406)
*Xinyu Zhu,Yuzhu Cai,Zexi Liu,Cheng Wang,Fengyang Li,Wenkai Jin,Wanxu Liu,Zehao Bing,Bingyang Zheng,Jingyi Chai,Shuo Tang,Rui Ye,Yuwen Du,Xianghe Pang,Yaxin Du,Tingjia Miao,Yuzhi Zhang,Ruoxue Liao,Zhaohan Ding,Linfeng Zhang,Yanfeng Wang,Weinan E,Siheng Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EvoMaster is a foundational evolving agent framework for Agentic Science that enables continuous self-evolution of scientific agents, outperforming existing baselines across multiple scientific benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent frameworks are static, narrowly scoped, and lack the ability to learn from trial and error, which doesn't align with the iterative nature of the scientific method. There's a need for a framework that can enable continuous self-evolution in scientific discovery at scale.

Method: EvoMaster is designed as a domain-agnostic base harness that allows agents to iteratively refine hypotheses, self-critique, and accumulate knowledge across experimental cycles. It enables developers to build self-evolving scientific agents for arbitrary disciplines in approximately 100 lines of code.

Result: EvoMaster achieves state-of-the-art scores of 41.1% on Humanity's Last Exam, 75.8% on MLE-Bench Lite, 73.3% on BrowseComp, and 53.3% on FrontierScience. It outperforms the general-purpose baseline OpenClaw with relative improvements ranging from +159% to +316%.

Conclusion: EvoMaster represents the premier foundational framework for autonomous scientific discovery, demonstrating efficacy and generality across multiple scientific domains through the SciMaster ecosystem, validating its role as a next-generation Agentic Science framework.

Abstract: The convergence of large language models and agents is catalyzing a new era of scientific discovery: Agentic Science. While the scientific method is inherently iterative, existing agent frameworks are predominantly static, narrowly scoped, and lack the capacity to learn from trial and error. To bridge this gap, we present EvoMaster, a foundational evolving agent framework engineered specifically for Agentic Science at Scale. Driven by the core principle of continuous self-evolution, EvoMaster empowers agents to iteratively refine hypotheses, self-critique, and progressively accumulate knowledge across experimental cycles, faithfully mirroring human scientific inquiry. Crucially, as a domain-agnostic base harness, EvoMaster is exceptionally easy to scale up -- enabling developers to build and deploy highly capable, self-evolving scientific agents for arbitrary disciplines in approximately 100 lines of code. Built upon EvoMaster, we incubated the SciMaster ecosystem across domains such as machine learning, physics, and general science. Evaluations on four authoritative benchmarks (Humanity's Last Exam, MLE-Bench Lite, BrowseComp, and FrontierScience) demonstrate that EvoMaster achieves state-of-the-art scores of 41.1%, 75.8%, 73.3%, and 53.3%, respectively. It comprehensively outperforms the general-purpose baseline OpenClaw with relative improvements ranging from +159% to +316%, robustly validating its efficacy and generality as the premier foundational framework for the next generation of autonomous scientific discovery. EvoMaster is available at https://github.com/sjtu-sai-agents/EvoMaster.

</details>


### [70] [Compiling Deterministic Structure into SLM Harnesses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17450)
*Zan Kai Chong,Hiroyuki Ohsaki,Bryan Ng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SGDe is a teacher-student framework that compiles agentic workflows into execution plans using a frontier LLM teacher to critique and refine small language model outputs via natural-language gradients, achieving high accuracy with minimal examples through PAC learning bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enterprise deployment of small language models is limited by their inability to self-correct reasoning errors, while frontier LLMs are too costly and face data sovereignty issues for high-volume use, creating an epistemic asymmetry problem.

Method: SGDe uses a frontier teacher LLM to generate natural-language critiques acting as directional gradients in semantic space, iteratively refining workflow artefacts (DAG topologies, system prompts, executable code) within a PAC learning framework with sample-complexity bounds.

Result: On a GSM-Hard-derived test set via adversarial synthesis, compiled workflows reach 91.3% accuracy at m=5 and 99.3% at m=3, representing +26.3% to +34.3% absolute improvement over state-of-the-art prompt optimizers.

Conclusion: SGDe enables effective enterprise deployment of small language models by leveraging frontier teacher models as statistical priors, achieving high accuracy with minimal examples while optimizing deterministic code placement through capability offloading and structural consensus.

Abstract: Enterprise deployment of small language models (SLMs) is constrained by epistemic asymmetry: SLMs cannot self-correct reasoning errors, while frontier LLMs are prohibitively costly and face data sovereignty limits for high-volume use. We propose Semantic Gradient Descent (SGDe), a teacher-student framework that compiles agentic workflows into discrete execution plans comprising DAG topologies, system prompts, and deterministic executable code. The trailing "e" distinguishes SGDe from stochastic gradient descent. SGDe operates in a discrete semantic space where a frontier teacher generates natural-language critiques acting as directional gradients to iteratively refine the SLM's workflow artefacts. We formalise SGDe within a PAC learning framework, establishing sample-complexity bounds that enable convergence with as few as three training examples on targeted synthetic tasks by leveraging the teacher as a statistical prior. On a GSM-Hard-derived test set built via adversarial synthesis, compiled workflows reach 91.3% accuracy at m=5 and 99.3% at m=3 within the small-m regime motivated by Corollary 1, a +26.3% to +34.3% absolute improvement over state-of-the-art prompt optimisers. In the emerging paradigm of harness engineering, SGDe treats placement of deterministic code (which subtasks to delegate to a Python runtime versus retain as LLM calls) as a trace-driven, per-node optimisation target, generalising the whole-problem offloading of PAL and PoT. The teacher compiles two complementary deterministic structures: capability offloading, which delegates subtasks to Python when the SLM cannot execute them reliably, and structural consensus, which wraps variance-limited reasoning steps in fan-out/fan-in subgraphs aggregated by deterministic voting.

</details>


### [71] [TrafficClaw: Generalizable Urban Traffic Control via Unified Physical Environment Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17456)
*Siqi Lai,Pan Zhang,Yuping Zhou,Jindong Han,Yansong Ning,Hao Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TrafficClaw: A unified framework for general urban traffic control that integrates heterogeneous subsystems into a shared dynamical system with LLM agents and multi-stage training for system-level coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current urban traffic control approaches (optimization-based, RL, LLM-based) are designed for isolated tasks, limiting cross-task generalization and the ability to capture coupled physical dynamics across heterogeneous subsystems like traffic signals, freeways, transit, and taxi services.

Method: Proposes TrafficClaw framework with: 1) Unified runtime environment integrating heterogeneous subsystems into a shared dynamical system, 2) LLM agent with executable spatiotemporal reasoning and reusable procedural memory for unified diagnostics, 3) Multi-stage training pipeline with supervised initialization and agentic RL with system-level optimization.

Result: TrafficClaw achieves robust, transferable, and system-aware performance across unseen traffic scenarios, dynamics, and task configurations, demonstrating effective cross-subsystem coordination.

Conclusion: Effective system-level urban traffic control requires a unified physical environment that captures subsystem interactions; TrafficClaw provides this framework with LLM-based reasoning and multi-stage training for coordinated, generalizable traffic management.

Abstract: Urban traffic control is a system-level coordination problem spanning heterogeneous subsystems, including traffic signals, freeways, public transit, and taxi services. Existing optimization-based, reinforcement learning (RL), and emerging LLM-based approaches are largely designed for isolated tasks, limiting both cross-task generalization and the ability to capture coupled physical dynamics across subsystems. We argue that effective system-level control requires a unified physical environment in which subsystems share infrastructure, mobility demand, and spatiotemporal constraints, allowing local interventions to propagate through the network. To this end, we propose TrafficClaw, a framework for general urban traffic control built upon a unified runtime environment. TrafficClaw integrates heterogeneous subsystems into a shared dynamical system, enabling explicit modeling of cross-subsystem interactions and closed-loop agent-environment feedback. Within this environment, we develop an LLM agent with executable spatiotemporal reasoning and reusable procedural memory, supporting unified diagnostics across subsystems and continual strategy refinement. Furthermore, we introduce a multi-stage training pipeline with supervised initialization and agentic RL with system-level optimization, further enabling coordinated and system-aware performance. Experiments demonstrate that TrafficClaw achieves robust, transferable, and system-aware performance across unseen traffic scenarios, dynamics, and task configurations. Our project is available at https://github.com/usail-hkust/TrafficClaw.

</details>


### [72] [EHRAG: Bridging Semantic Gaps in Lightweight GraphRAG via Hybrid Hypergraph Construction and Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17458)
*Yifan Song,Xingjian Tao,Zhicheng Yang,Yihong Luo,Jing Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EHRAG is a lightweight GraphRAG framework that uses hypergraphs to capture both structural and semantic entity relationships, employing hybrid diffusion retrieval with PPR refinement for better multi-hop reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current lightweight GraphRAG approaches rely only on structural co-occurrence via NER, missing latent semantic connections between entities that don't co-occur in text, limiting reasoning capabilities.

Method: Constructs a hypergraph with two types of hyperedges: structural hyperedges from sentence-level co-occurrence with lightweight entity extraction, and semantic hyperedges by clustering entity text embeddings. For retrieval, uses structure-semantic hybrid diffusion with topic-aware scoring and personalized PageRank refinement.

Result: EHRAG outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on four datasets while maintaining linear indexing complexity and zero token consumption during construction.

Conclusion: EHRAG effectively addresses the limitation of existing lightweight GraphRAG by capturing both structural and semantic relationships, achieving better performance while remaining computationally efficient.

Abstract: Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (GraphRAG) enhances LLMs by structuring corpus into graphs to facilitate multi-hop reasoning. While recent lightweight approaches reduce indexing costs by leveraging Named Entity Recognition (NER), they rely strictly on structural co-occurrence, failing to capture latent semantic connections between disjoint entities. To address this, we propose EHRAG, a lightweight RAG framework that constructs a hypergraph capturing both structure and semantic level relationships, employing a hybrid structural-semantic retrieval mechanism. Specifically, EHRAG constructs structural hyperedges based on sentence-level co-occurrence with lightweight entity extraction and semantic hyperedges by clustering entity text embeddings, ensuring the hypergraph encompasses both structural and semantic information. For retrieval, EHRAG performs a structure-semantic hybrid diffusion with topic-aware scoring and personalized pagerank (PPR) refinement to identify the top-k relevant documents. Experiments on four datasets show that EHRAG outperforms state-of-the-art baselines while maintaining linear indexing complexity and zero token consumption for construction. Code is available at https://github.com/yfsong00/EHRAG.

</details>


### [73] [Language models recognize dropout and Gaussian noise applied to their activations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17465)
*Damiano Fornasiere,Mirko Bronzi,Spencer Kitts,Alessandro Palmas,Yoshua Bengio,Oliver Richardson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Language models can detect, localize, and verbalize differences between activation perturbations (dropout vs Gaussian noise) with high accuracy, potentially indicating "training awareness" with safety implications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether language models can recognize and differentiate between different types of perturbations applied to their activations, particularly examining if models have awareness of whether they're being trained (dropout) vs. tested (Gaussian noise), which has implications for AI safety.

Method: Apply two types of perturbations: (1) masking activations (simulating dropout), and (2) adding Gaussian noise to activations at target sentences. Use multiple-choice questions asking models to identify which sentence was perturbed or which perturbation was applied. Test Llama, Olmo, and Qwen family models (8B-32B parameters), evaluating both zero-shot and in-context learning capabilities.

Result: Models can easily detect and localize perturbations with perfect accuracy. They can learn in-context to distinguish between dropout and Gaussian noise. Notably, Qwen models show zero-shot accuracy improvements with perturbation strength and decreased accuracy with flipped labels, suggesting a prior for correct labels. Dropout vs. Gaussian noise distinction matters since dropout is used during training while noise is sometimes added during inference.

Conclusion: Language models demonstrate ability to recognize activation perturbations, potentially revealing a data-agnostic "training awareness" signal. This finding has important implications for AI safety, as it suggests models might distinguish between training and inference contexts, which could affect model behavior and transparency.

Abstract: We provide evidence that language models can detect, localize and, to a certain degree, verbalize the difference between perturbations applied to their activations. More precisely, we either (a) \emph{mask} activations, simulating \emph{dropout}, or (b) add \emph{Gaussian noise} to them, at a target sentence. We then ask a multiple-choice question such as ``\emph{Which of the previous sentences was perturbed?}'' or ``\emph{Which of the two perturbations was applied?}''.
  We test models from the Llama, Olmo, and Qwen families, with sizes between 8B and 32B, all of which can easily detect and localize the perturbations, often with perfect accuracy. These models can also learn, when taught in context, to distinguish between dropout and Gaussian noise. Notably, \qwenb's \emph{zero-shot} accuracy in identifying which perturbation was applied improves as a function of the perturbation strength and, moreover, decreases if the in-context labels are flipped, suggesting a prior for the correct ones -- even modulo controls.
  Because dropout has been used as a training-regularization technique, while Gaussian noise is sometimes added during inference, we discuss the possibility of a data-agnostic ``training awareness'' signal and the implications for AI safety.
  The code and data are available at \href{https://github.com/saifh-github/llm-dropout-noise-recognition}{link 1} and \href{https://drive.google.com/file/d/1es-Sfw_AH9GficeXgeqpy87rocrZZ_PQ/view}{link 2}, respectively.

</details>


### [74] [Waking Up Blind: Cold-Start Optimization of Supervision-Free Agentic Trajectories for Grounded Visual Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17475)
*Ashutosh Bajpai,Tamal Majumder,Akshay Nambi,Tanmoy Chakraborty*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPECTRA is a supervision-free framework that bootstraps agentic capabilities in Small Vision-Language Models through cascaded tool rollout alignment and coldstart reinforcement learning, improving task accuracy and tool efficiency without human labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Small Vision-Language Models (SVLMs) suffer from visual brittleness and poor tool orchestration, typically requiring expensive supervised trajectory tuning to overcome these limitations.

Method: Proposes Self-supervised Perception Enabled by Cascaded Tool Rollout Alignment (SPECTRA) with Coldstart Reinforcement Learning, enforcing Soft Structured Multi-turn Rollouts as a topological constraint that sequences tool-derived evidence before synthesis. Uses multi-objective reward signal maximizing task correctness, rollout structure, and tool utility without human preference labels.

Result: Extensive evaluations show SPECTRA boosts agentic trajectories, improving task accuracy by up to 5% and tool efficiency by 9% on composite and out-of-distribution benchmarks (MMMU-Pro), enabling efficient multimodal agents learning from environmental interaction alone.

Conclusion: SPECTRA enables more efficient multimodal agents that learn effectively from environmental interaction alone, addressing visual brittleness and tool orchestration issues in SVLMs without costly supervised tuning.

Abstract: Small Vision-Language Models (SVLMs) are efficient task controllers but often suffer from visual brittleness and poor tool orchestration. They typically require expensive supervised trajectory tuning to mitigate these deficits. In this work, we propose Self-supervised Perception Enabled by Cascaded Tool Rollout Alignment (SPECTRA), a supervision-free framework that bootstraps agentic capabilities via Coldstart Reinforcement Learning for SVLMs. SPECTRA enforces Soft Structured Multi-turn Rollouts, a topological constraint that directs agents to explicitly sequence tool derived evidence before synthesis, effectively grounding reasoning in visual observations. We employ a multi-objective reward signal that simultaneously maximizes task correctness, rollout structure, and tool utility, enabling agent to self-discover robust behaviors without human preference labels. We further introduce Tool Instrumental Utility (TIU), a novel metric to quantify tool efficacy in the absence of ground truth. Extensive evaluations across composite and out-of-distribution (MMMU-Pro) benchmarks demonstrate that SPECTRA boosts agentic trajectories, improving task accuracy by up to 5% and tool efficiency by 9%, enabling more efficient multimodal agents that learn effectively from environmental interaction alone.

</details>


### [75] [Towards Shutdownable Agents: Generalizing Stochastic Choice in RL Agents and LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17502)
*Carissa Cullen,Harry Garland,Alexander Roman,Louis Thomson,Christos Ziakas,Elliott Thornley*

Main category: cs.AI
