TL;DR: DReST (Discounted Reward for Same-Length Trajectories) trains AI agents to be both Useful (effective at goals) and Neutral (lacking preferences about trajectory lengths), potentially making them more shutdownable and safe.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the alignment problem where misaligned AI agents might resist being shut down, by training agents that lack preferences about how long they run, making them more willing to accept shutdown.

Method: DReST reward function penalizes agents for repeatedly choosing same-length trajectories, incentivizing them to choose stochastically between different trajectory lengths (Neutral) while still pursuing goals effectively within each length (Useful). Applied to deep RL agents and fine-tuned LLMs.

Result: DReST-trained agents generalized well to unseen contexts: RL agents achieved 11% (PPO) and 18% (A2C) higher Usefulness than baselines, and fine-tuned LLM achieved maximum Usefulness and near-maximum Neutrality.

Conclusion: Provides early evidence that DReST could train advanced agents to be both Useful and Neutral, and theoretical work suggests such agents would be both useful and shutdownable, addressing safety concerns about misaligned AI.

Abstract: Misaligned artificial agents might resist shutdown. One proposed solution is to train agents to lack preferences between different-length trajectories. The Discounted Reward for Same-Length Trajectories (DReST) reward function does this by penalizing agents for repeatedly choosing same-length trajectories, and thus incentivizes agents to (1) choose stochastically between different trajectory-lengths (be Neutral about trajectory-lengths), and (2) pursue goals effectively conditional on each trajectory-length (be Useful). In this paper, we use DReST to train deep RL agents and fine-tune LLMs to be Neutral and Useful. We find that these DReST agents generalize to being Neutral and Useful in unseen contexts at test time. Indeed, DReST RL agents achieve 11% (PPO) and 18% (A2C) higher Usefulness on our test set than baseline agents, and our fine-tuned LLM achieves maximum Usefulness and near-maximum Neutrality. Our results provide some early evidence that DReST could be used to train more advanced agents to be Useful and Neutral. Prior theoretical work suggests that these agents would be useful and shutdownable.

</details>


### [76] [From Admission to Invariants: Measuring Deviation in Delegated Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17517)
*Marcelo Fernandez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Enforcement-based governance systems for autonomous agents have a structural limitation: they cannot detect behavioral drift from admission-time specifications, while Invariant Measurement Layer (IML) can provably detect such drift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing enforcement mechanisms for autonomous agents only flag hard constraint violations at runtime, but there's a fundamental structural limitation: they cannot determine whether an agent's behavior remains within the admissible behavior space established at admission time due to behavioral drift.

Method: Proves the Non-Identifiability Theorem showing the admission-time behavior space is not in the sigma-algebra generated by enforcement signals. Proposes Invariant Measurement Layer (IML) that retains access to the generative model of admission-time behavior. Validates across three drift scenarios (300/1000 steps), n8n webhook pipeline, and LangGraph StateGraph agent.

Result: Enforcement triggers zero violations while IML detects each drift type within 9-258 steps. The Non-Identifiability Theorem proves structural impossibility for enforcement-based monitoring, while IML detects admission-time drift with provably finite detection delay.

Conclusion: Enforcement-based governance is structurally unable to monitor admission-time behavioral drift, while IML bypasses this limitation. This is Paper 2 of a 4-paper Agent Governance Series, addressing fundamental limits in agent control protocols.

Abstract: Autonomous agent systems are governed by enforcement mechanisms that flag hard constraint violations at runtime. The Agent Control Protocol identifies a structural limit of such systems: a correctly-functioning enforcement engine can enter a regime in which behavioral drift is invisible to it, because the enforcement signal operates below the layer where deviation is measurable. We show that enforcement-based governance is structurally unable to determine whether an agent's behavior remains within the admissible behavior space A0 established at admission time. Our central result, the Non-Identifiability Theorem, proves that A0 is not in the sigma-algebra generated by the enforcement signal g under the Local Observability Assumption, which every practical enforcement system satisfies. The impossibility arises from a fundamental mismatch: g evaluates actions locally against a point-wise rule set, while A0 encodes global, trajectory-level behavioral properties set at admission time. We define the Invariant Measurement Layer (IML), which bypasses this limitation by retaining direct access to the generative model of A0. We prove an information-theoretic impossibility for enforcement-based monitoring; separately, we show IML detects admission-time drift with provably finite detection delay, operating in the region where enforcement is structurally blind. Validated across four settings: three drift scenarios (300 and 1000 steps), a live n8n webhook pipeline, and a LangGraph StateGraph agent -- enforcement triggers zero violations while IML detects each drift type within 9-258 steps. Paper 2 of a 4-paper Agent Governance Series: atomic boundaries (P0, 10.5281/zenodo.19642166), ACP enforcement (P1, arXiv:2603.18829), fair allocation (P3, 10.5281/zenodo.19643928), irreducibility (P4, 10.5281/zenodo.19643950).

</details>


### [77] [COSEARCH: Joint Training of Reasoning and Document Ranking via Reinforcement Learning for Agentic Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17555)
*Hansi Zeng,Liam Collins,Bhuvesh Kumar,Neil Shah,Hamed Zamani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoSearch: A framework that jointly trains a reasoning agent and generative document ranking model through Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to overcome retrieval bottlenecks in agentic search systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL-based agentic search approaches treat retrieval systems as fixed tools, optimizing only the reasoning agent while leaving the retrieval component unchanged. Experiments show a 26.8% relative F1 gap between oracle and fixed retrieval systems, indicating retrieval is a key bottleneck for scaling agentic search performance.

Method: Propose CoSearch framework with: 1) Joint training of multi-step reasoning agent and generative document ranking model via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), 2) Semantic grouping strategy that clusters sub-queries by token-level similarity to form valid optimization groups without extra rollouts, 3) Composite reward combining ranking quality signals with trajectory-level outcome feedback for both immediate and long-term learning.

Result: Experiments on seven single-hop and multi-hop QA benchmarks show consistent improvements over strong baselines. Ablation studies validate each design choice, demonstrating that joint training of reasoning agent and retrieval system is both feasible and strongly performant.

Conclusion: Joint training of reasoning agent and retrieval system is a key ingredient for future search agents, addressing the identified retrieval bottleneck and enabling better performance through coordinated optimization of both components.

Abstract: Agentic search -- the task of training agents that iteratively reason, issue queries, and synthesize retrieved information to answer complex questions -- has achieved remarkable progress through reinforcement learning (RL). However, existing approaches such as Search-R1, treat the retrieval system as a fixed tool, optimizing only the reasoning agent while the retrieval component remains unchanged. A preliminary experiment reveals that the gap between an oracle and a fixed retrieval system reaches up to +26.8% relative F1 improvement across seven QA benchmarks, suggesting that the retrieval system is a key bottleneck in scaling agentic search performance. Motivated by this finding, we propose CoSearch, a framework that jointly trains a multi-step reasoning agent and a generative document ranking model via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). To enable effective GRPO training for the ranker -- whose inputs vary across reasoning trajectories -- we introduce a semantic grouping strategy that clusters sub-queries by token-level similarity, forming valid optimization groups without additional rollouts. We further design a composite reward combining ranking quality signals with trajectory-level outcome feedback, providing the ranker with both immediate and long-term learning signals. Experiments on seven single-hop and multi-hop QA benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines, with ablation studies validating each design choice. Our results show that joint training of the reasoning agent and retrieval system is both feasible and strongly performant, pointing to a key ingredient for future search agents.

</details>


### [78] [Beyond Static Snapshots: A Grounded Evaluation Framework for Language Models at the Agentic Frontier](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17573)
*Jazmia Henry*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Current LLM evaluation frameworks are structurally inadequate for agentic systems due to distributional, temporal, scope, and process invalidity failures. The paper proposes ISOPro, a simulation-based fine-tuning and evaluation system with grounded continuous evaluation that eliminates reward hacking and reduces hardware requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation frameworks for LLMs suffer from systematic failures when assessing deployed, agentic systems: 1) distributional invalidity (inputs don't reflect real interaction distributions), 2) temporal invalidity (post-hoc rather than training-integrated evaluations), 3) scope invalidity (single-turn outputs rather than long-horizon trajectories), and 4) process invalidity (output assessments rather than reasoning evaluations). These failures are particularly critical in RLHF where reward models are evaluated under conditions that don't hold during RL training, making reward hacking a predictable consequence rather than a training pathology.

Method: The paper proposes the Grounded Continuous Evaluation (GCE) framework and presents ISOPro, a simulation-based fine-tuning and evaluation system. ISOPro replaces learned reward models with deterministic ground-truth verifiers, eliminating reward hacking by construction in verifiable-reward domains. The system operates on LoRA adapter weights updatable on CPU, reducing hardware requirements by an order of magnitude.

Result: ISOPro was validated on a resource-constrained scheduling domain with six difficulty tiers. The system demonstrated: 1) capability emergence visible only through continuous evaluation, 2) an implicit curriculum that forms without researcher curation, and 3) a 3x accuracy improvement over zero-shot baselines. All results were achieved on consumer hardware with only 0.216% trainable parameters.

Conclusion: Current LLM evaluation frameworks are structurally inadequate for agentic systems. The ISOPro system with Grounded Continuous Evaluation addresses these limitations by eliminating reward hacking through ground-truth verification, enabling continuous evaluation during training, and dramatically reducing hardware requirements, making sophisticated agent training accessible on consumer hardware.

Abstract: We argue that current evaluation frameworks for large language models (LLMs) suffer from four systematic failures that make them structurally inadequate for assessing deployed, agentic systems: distributional invalidity (evaluation inputs do not reflect real interaction distributions), temporal invalidity (evaluations are post-hoc rather than training-integrated), scope invalidity (evaluations measure single-turn outputs rather than long-horizon trajectories), and process invalidity (evaluations assess outputs rather than reasoning). These failures compound critically in RLHF, where reward models are evaluated under conditions that do not hold during RL training, making reward hacking a predictable consequence of evaluation design rather than a training pathology. We propose the Grounded Continuous Evaluation (GCE) framework and present ISOPro, a simulation-based fine-tuning and evaluation system. ISOPro replaces the learned reward model with a deterministic ground-truth verifier, eliminating reward hacking by construction in verifiable-reward domains, and operates on LoRA adapter weights updatable on CPU, reducing the hardware barrier by an order of magnitude. We validate ISOPro on a resource-constrained scheduling domain with six difficulty tiers, demonstrating capability emergence visible only through continuous evaluation, an implicit curriculum that forms without researcher curation, and a 3x accuracy improvement over zero-shot baselines, all on consumer hardware with 0.216% trainable parameters.

</details>


### [79] [DIRCR: Dual-Inference Rule-Contrastive Reasoning for Solving RAVENs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17584)
*Jiachen Zhang,Chengtai Li,Jianfeng Ren,Linlin Shen,Zheng Lu,Ruibin Bai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DIRCR model combines local and global reasoning with contrastive learning for abstract visual reasoning, achieving improved robustness and generalization on RAVEN datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods prioritize either global context or local row-wise relations separately, failing to integrate both approaches. They also lack intermediate feature constraints, leading to incomplete rule capture and entangled representations in abstract visual reasoning tasks.

Method: Proposes Dual-Inference Rule-Contrastive Reasoning (DIRCR) model with two main components: 1) Dual-Inference Reasoning Module combining local row-wise analogical reasoning and global holistic inference via gated attention, and 2) Rule-Contrastive Learning Module using pseudo-labels to construct positive/negative rule samples with contrastive learning to enhance feature separability.

Result: Experimental results on three RAVEN datasets demonstrate that DIRCR significantly enhances reasoning robustness and generalization compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: DIRCR successfully addresses the limitations of previous approaches by integrating local and global reasoning while using contrastive learning to promote abstract, transferable rule learning, leading to improved performance in abstract visual reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Abstract visual reasoning remains challenging as existing methods often prioritize either global context or local row-wise relations, failing to integrate both, and lack intermediate feature constraints, leading to incomplete rule capture and entangled representations. To address these issues, we propose the Dual-Inference Rule-Contrastive Reasoning (DIRCR) model. Its core component, the Dual-Inference Reasoning Module, combines a local path for row-wise analogical reasoning and a global path for holistic inference, integrated via a gated attention mechanism. Additionally, a Rule-Contrastive Learning Module introduces pseudo-labels to construct positive and negative rule samples, applying contrastive learning to enhance feature separability and promote abstract, transferable rule learning. Experimental results on three RAVEN datasets demonstrate that DIRCR significantly enhances reasoning robustness and generalization. Codes are available at https://github.com/csZack-Zhang/DIRCR.

</details>


### [80] [Characterizing Model-Native Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17614)
*Feiyang Kang,Mahavir Dabas,Myeongseob Ko,Ruoxi Jia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a model-native approach for skill characterization by recovering an interpretable orthogonal basis from model activations, which outperforms human-written taxonomies for both data selection and inference-time steering interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing skill characterizations rely on external human-written taxonomies, textual descriptions, or manual profiling pipelines that may not align with the model's internal representations. The authors argue that when intervening on model behavior, skill characterization should be model-native—grounded in the model's own representations rather than imposed through external ontologies.

Method: The authors recover a compact orthogonal basis from sequence-level activations of language models. This basis is semantically interpretable but need not correspond to predefined human ontologies—instead, it captures axes of behavioral variation that the model itself organizes around. They develop lightweight proxy interventions to identify which directions are most useful for a given model, then use these for both supervised fine-tuning (SFT) data selection and inference-time steering.

Result: The approach significantly outperforms human-characterized skills: selecting data along model-native skill directions improves Pass@1 by up to 20% on MATH and 41% on AMC benchmarks using Llama3-8B and Qwen2.5-3B. The same directions serve as steering vectors at inference time, improving Pass@8 by up to 4.8% on MATH—an intervention human-characterized skills cannot support. For safety alignment, selecting adversarial training data for model-native skill coverage yields more sample-efficient learning.

Conclusion: Recovering skills from a model's own representations, rather than imposing external ontologies, provides a more effective foundation for intervening on model behavior across reasoning post-training and safety alignment. The model-native approach enables both improved data selection and inference-time steering interventions that outperform traditional human-centric methods.

Abstract: Skills are a natural unit for describing what a language model can do and how its behavior can be changed. However, existing characterizations rely on human-written taxonomies, textual descriptions, or manual profiling pipelines--all external hypotheses about what matters that need not align with the model's internal representations. We argue that when the goal is to intervene on model behavior, skill characterization should be *model-native*: grounded in the model's own representations rather than imposed through external ontologies. We instantiate this view by recovering a compact orthogonal basis from sequence-level activations. The resulting basis is semantically interpretable but need not correspond to any predefined human ontology; instead, it captures axes of behavioral variation that the model itself organizes around. We validate this characterization on reasoning post-training, using the recovered basis for both SFT data selection and inference-time steering. We develop lightweight proxy interventions to identify which directions are most useful for a given model. Across Llama3-8B and Qwen2.5-3B, selecting data along those directions improves Pass@1 by up to 20% on MATH and 41% on AMC, outperforming data selection based on human-characterized skills. Because the basis lives in activation space, the same directions also serve as steering vectors at inference time, improving Pass@8 by up to 4.8% on MATH--an intervention that human-characterized skills cannot support. We further validate the characterization on safety alignment, where selecting adversarial training data for model-native skill coverage rather than textual diversity yields more sample-efficient learning. These results suggest that recovering skills from the model's own representations, rather than imposing them externally, provides a more effective foundation for intervening on model behavior. Codes are open-sourced.

</details>


### [81] [KnowledgeBerg: Evaluating Systematic Knowledge Coverage and Compositional Reasoning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17621)
*Xiao Zhang,Qianru Meng,Yongjian Chen,Yumeng Wang,Johan Bos*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: KnowledgeBerg benchmark reveals LLMs struggle with systematic knowledge coverage and compositional set-based reasoning over bounded domains, showing severe limitations across languages and model scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world questions often appear simple but require systematic coverage of bounded knowledge universes and compositional set-based reasoning - a phenomenon called "the tip of the iceberg." Current benchmarks don't adequately test these capabilities, motivating the creation of a specialized benchmark.

Method: Formalized the challenge through two dimensions: knowledge width (cardinality of required universe) and reasoning depth (number of compositional set operations). Created KnowledgeBerg benchmark with 4,800 multiple-choice questions from 1,183 enumeration seeds across 10 domains and 17 languages, using authoritative sources for grounding.

Result: Open-source LLMs show severe limitations: 5.26-36.88 F1 on universe enumeration and 16.00-44.19 accuracy on knowledge-grounded reasoning. Diagnostic analysis reveals three failure stages: completeness (missing knowledge), awareness (failure to identify requirements), and application (incorrect reasoning). Test-time compute and retrieval augmentation show modest improvements but substantial gaps remain.

Conclusion: Current LLMs have significant limitations in organizing structured knowledge and executing compositional reasoning over bounded domains. The KnowledgeBerg benchmark exposes these weaknesses and provides a tool for future research in systematic knowledge coverage and set-based reasoning.

Abstract: Many real-world questions appear deceptively simple yet implicitly demand two capabilities: (i) systematic coverage of a bounded knowledge universe and (ii) compositional set-based reasoning over that universe, a phenomenon we term "the tip of the iceberg." We formalize this challenge through two orthogonal dimensions: knowledge width, the cardinality of the required universe, and reasoning depth, the number of compositional set operations. We introduce KnowledgeBerg, a benchmark of 4,800 multiple-choice questions derived from 1,183 enumeration seeds spanning 10 domains and 17 languages, with universes grounded in authoritative sources to ensure reproducibility. Representative open-source LLMs demonstrate severe limitations, achieving only 5.26-36.88 F1 on universe enumeration and 16.00-44.19 accuracy on knowledge-grounded reasoning. Diagnostic analyses reveal three stages of failure: completeness, or missing knowledge; awareness, or failure to identify requirements; and application, or incorrect reasoning execution. This pattern persists across languages and model scales. Although test-time compute and retrieval augmentation yield measurable gains -- up to 4.35 and 3.78 points, respectively -- substantial gaps remain, exposing limitations in how current LLMs organize structured knowledge and execute compositional reasoning over bounded domains. The dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/2npc/KnowledgeBerg

</details>


### [82] [Toward Reusability of AI Models Using Dynamic Updates of AI Documentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17626)
*Peter Bajcsy,Walid Keyrouz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents a methodology for creating agile, data-driven, and community-based AI model cards to improve AI model reusability by reducing the temporal lag between documentation standards and actual community practices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many trained AI models remain non-reusable due to inadequate AI documentation (model cards) and the temporal lag between rapidly changing reusability requirements and outdated documentation templates. There's a need to align AI documentation with current best practices and community standards.

Method: The approach introduces agile, data-driven, and community-based methodology for AI model cards using Hugging Face repository data and Zero Draft documentation templates. It quantifies correlations between model reuse metrics (downloads/likes) and documentation quality metrics (alignment with templates, tables of contents, word statistics). Infrastructure is developed to regularly compare documentation templates against community practices from millions of AI models on Hugging Face.

Result: The work establishes correlations between AI model reuse metrics and documentation quality metrics, and develops infrastructure for ongoing comparison between documentation templates and community-standard practices. This enables data-driven validation of documentation standards against actual community behavior.

Conclusion: The research introduces a novel methodology for agile, community-informed AI documentation standards that can improve AI model reusability by reducing the lag between documentation requirements and actual practices, ultimately enabling better AI model reuse through improved documentation alignment with community standards.

Abstract: This work addresses the challenge of disseminating reusable artificial intelligence (AI) models accompanied by AI documentation (a.k.a., AI model cards). The work is motivated by the large number of trained AI models that are not reusable due to the lack of (a) AI documentation and (b) the temporal lag between rapidly changing requirements on AI model reusability and those specified in various AI model cards. Our objectives are to shorten the lag time in updating AI model card templates and align AI documentation more closely with current AI best practices.
  Our approach introduces a methodology for delivering agile, data-driven, and community-based AI model cards. We use the Hugging Face (HF) repository of AI models, populated by a subset of the AI research and development community, and the AI consortium-based Zero Draft (ZD) templates for the AI documentation of AI datasets and AI models, as our test datasets. We also address questions about the value of AI documentation for AI reusability.
  Our work quantifies the correlations between AI model downloads/likes (i.e., AI model reuse metrics) from the HF repository and their documentation alignment with the ZD documentation templates using tables of contents and word statistics (i.e., AI documentation quality metrics). Furthermore, our work develops the infrastructure to regularly compare AI documentation templates against community-standard practices derived from millions of uploaded AI models in the Hugging Face repository. The impact of our work lies in introducing a methodology for delivering agile, data-driven, and community-based standards for documenting AI models and improving AI model reuse.

</details>


### [83] [PV-SQL: Synergizing Database Probing and Rule-based Verification for Text-to-SQL Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17653)
*Yuan Tian,Tianyi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PV-SQL is an agentic framework with Probe and Verify components that improves text-to-SQL systems by resolving ambiguities through iterative probing queries and rule-based verification, achieving better accuracy and efficiency on BIRD benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-SQL systems struggle with deep contextual understanding for complex queries, particularly handling subtle requirements, ambiguities in value formats, column semantics, and inter-table relationships, leading to poor performance on sophisticated SQL tasks.

Method: PV-SQL uses two complementary components: 1) Probe - iteratively generates probing queries to retrieve concrete database records, resolving ambiguities in values, semantics, and relationships; 2) Verify - employs rule-based methods to extract verifiable conditions and construct executable checklists for iterative SQL refinement.

Result: Experiments on BIRD benchmarks show PV-SQL outperforms the best text-to-SQL baseline by 5% in execution accuracy and 20.8% in valid efficiency score while consuming fewer tokens, demonstrating significant improvements in both correctness and computational efficiency.

Conclusion: The PV-SQL framework effectively addresses contextual understanding failures in text-to-SQL systems through its dual Probe-Verify approach, offering a practical solution for handling complex queries with subtle requirements while improving both accuracy and efficiency.

Abstract: Text-to-SQL systems often struggle with deep contextual understanding, particularly for complex queries with subtle requirements. We present PV-SQL, an agentic framework that addresses these failures through two complementary components: Probe and Verify. The Probe component iteratively generates probing queries to retrieve concrete records from the database, resolving ambiguities in value formats, column semantics, and inter-table relationships to build richer contextual understanding. The Verify component employs a rule-based method to extract verifiable conditions and construct an executable checklist, enabling iterative SQL refinement that effectively reduces missing constraints. Experiments on the BIRD benchmarks show that PV-SQL outperforms the best text-to-SQL baseline by 5% in execution accuracy and 20.8% in valid efficiency score while consuming fewer tokens.

</details>


### [84] [Poly-EPO: Training Exploratory Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17654)
*Ifdita Hasan Orney,Jubayer Ibn Hamid,Shreya S Ramanujam,Shirley Wu,Hengyuan Hu,Noah Goodman,Dorsa Sadigh,Chelsea Finn*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Post-training framework for language models that encourages optimistic exploration and synergizes exploration with exploitation through set reinforcement learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exploration is essential for learning from experience, enabling agents to find solutions to complex problems, generalize to novel ones, and scale performance with test-time compute. Existing language models may not effectively explore diverse reasoning strategies.

Method: Proposed Polychromic Exploratory Policy Optimization (Poly-EPO), a set reinforcement learning framework where LMs generate sets of responses that are collectively accurate under reward functions and exploratory in reasoning strategies. Developed general recipe for optimizing LMs with set RL under arbitrary objectives by modifying advantage computation.

Result: Across reasoning benchmarks, Poly-EPO improves generalization (higher pass@k coverage), preserves greater diversity in model generations, and effectively scales with test-time compute.

Conclusion: The Poly-EPO framework successfully creates synergy between exploration and exploitation in language models, demonstrating benefits for generalization, diversity, and compute scaling in reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Exploration is a cornerstone of learning from experience: it enables agents to find solutions to complex problems, generalize to novel ones, and scale performance with test-time compute. In this paper, we present a framework for post-training language models (LMs) that explicitly encourages optimistic exploration and promotes a synergy between exploration and exploitation. The central idea is to train the LM to generate sets of responses that are collectively accurate under the reward function and exploratory in their reasoning strategies. We first develop a general recipe for optimizing LMs with set reinforcement learning (set RL) under arbitrary objective functions, showing how standard RL algorithms can be adapted to this setting through a modification to the advantage computation. We then propose Polychromic Exploratory Policy Optimization (Poly-EPO), which instantiates this framework with an objective that explicitly synergizes exploration and exploitation. Across a range of reasoning benchmarks, we show that Poly-EPO improves generalization, as evidenced by higher pass@$k$ coverage, preserves greater diversity in model generations, and effectively scales with test-time compute.

</details>


### [85] [Semantic Entanglement in Vector-Based Retrieval: A Formal Framework and Context-Conditioned Disentanglement Pipeline for Agentic RAG Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17677)
*Nick Loghmani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Semantic Disentanglement Pipeline (SDP) to address semantic entanglement in RAG systems, improving retrieval precision from 32% to 82% by preprocessing documents before embedding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems struggle when source documents contain multiple interleaved topics, creating semantic entanglement in embedding spaces where distinct content overlaps, reducing retrieval precision.

Method: Introduced the Semantic Disentanglement Pipeline (SDP), a four-stage preprocessing framework that restructures documents before embedding. Also proposed context-conditioned preprocessing shaped by operational use patterns and continuous feedback adaptation based on agent performance.

Result: On a real-world enterprise healthcare knowledge base with 2,000+ documents across 25 sub-domains: Top-K retrieval precision improved from ~32% (fixed-token chunking) to ~82% (SDP), while mean Entanglement Index decreased from 0.71 to 0.14.

Conclusion: Semantic entanglement represents a distinct preprocessing failure mode in RAG systems that cannot be reliably corrected downstream once encoded into vector space. Proactive document structuring through SDP can significantly improve retrieval performance.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems depend on the geometric properties of vector representations to retrieve contextually appropriate evidence. When source documents interleave multiple topics within contiguous text, standard vectorization produces embedding spaces in which semantically distinct content occupies overlapping neighborhoods. We term this condition semantic entanglement. We formalize entanglement as a model-relative measure of cross-topic overlap in embedding space and define an Entanglement Index (EI) as a quantitative proxy. We argue that higher EI constrains attainable Top-K retrieval precision under cosine similarity retrieval. To address this, we introduce the Semantic Disentanglement Pipeline (SDP), a four-stage preprocessing framework that restructures documents prior to embedding. We further propose context-conditioned preprocessing, in which document structure is shaped by patterns of operational use, and a continuous feedback mechanism that adapts document structure based on agent performance. We evaluate SDP on a real-world enterprise healthcare knowledge base comprising over 2,000 documents across approximately 25 sub-domains. Top-K retrieval precision improves from approximately 32% under fixed-token chunking to approximately 82% under SDP, while mean EI decreases from 0.71 to 0.14. We do not claim that entanglement fully explains RAG failure, but that it captures a distinct preprocessing failure mode that downstream optimization cannot reliably correct once encoded into the vector space.

</details>


### [86] [Stratagem: Learning Transferable Reasoning via Trajectory-Modulated Game Self-Play](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17696)
*Xiachong Feng,Deyi Yin,Xiaocheng Feng,Yi Jiang,Libo Qin,Yangfan Ye,Lei Huang,Weitao Ma,Qiming Li,Yuxuan Gu,Bing Qin,Lingpeng Kong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: STRATAGEM is a method that improves reasoning transfer in language models by selectively reinforcing domain-agnostic reasoning patterns through a Reasoning Transferability Coefficient and encouraging adaptive reasoning development via a Reasoning Evolution Reward.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current self-play approaches in games only use terminal outcomes, failing to distinguish transferable reasoning patterns from game-specific heuristics. This creates two problems: domain specificity (learned patterns remain tied to game semantics) and contextual stasis (static game contexts don't foster progressive reasoning).

Method: STRATAGEM addresses reasoning transfer barriers through two components: 1) Reasoning Transferability Coefficient - selectively reinforces trajectories showing abstract, domain-agnostic reasoning; 2) Reasoning Evolution Reward - incentivizes adaptive reasoning development.

Result: Experiments show substantial improvements across mathematical reasoning, general reasoning, and code generation benchmarks, with particularly strong gains on competition-level mathematics where multi-step reasoning is critical.

Conclusion: Both components contribute to transferable reasoning, as confirmed by ablation studies and human evaluation. STRATAGEM successfully addresses fundamental barriers to reasoning transfer in language models trained through game-based approaches.

Abstract: Games offer a compelling paradigm for developing general reasoning capabilities in language models, as they naturally demand strategic planning, probabilistic inference, and adaptive decision-making. However, existing self-play approaches rely solely on terminal game outcomes, providing no mechanism to distinguish transferable reasoning patterns from game-specific heuristics. We present STRATAGEM, which addresses two fundamental barriers to reasoning transfer: domain specificity, where learned patterns remain anchored in game semantics, and contextual stasis, where static game contexts fail to cultivate progressive reasoning. STRATAGEM selectively reinforces trajectories exhibiting abstract, domain-agnostic reasoning through a Reasoning Transferability Coefficient, while incentivizing adaptive reasoning development via a Reasoning Evolution Reward. Experiments across mathematical reasoning, general reasoning, and code generation benchmarks demonstrate substantial improvements, with particularly strong gains on competition-level mathematics where multi-step reasoning is critical. Ablation studies and human evaluation confirm that both components contribute to transferable reasoning.

</details>


### [87] [Co-evolving Agent Architectures and Interpretable Reasoning for Automated Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17708)
*Jiahao Huang,Peilan Xu,Xiaoya Nan,Wenjian Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EvoOR-Agent: A co-evolutionary framework that evolves agent architectures and reasoning trajectories for automated operations research optimization using large language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based operations research automation is limited by hand-crafted reasoning-execution workflows that can't adapt to complex OR tasks requiring coordination among problem interpretation, formulation, solver selection, code generation, and debugging.

Method: Proposes EvoOR-Agent with activity-on-edge networks to represent agent workflows, then evolves populations through graph-mediated path-conditioned recombination, multi-granularity semantic mutation, and elitist population updates, enhanced by knowledge-base-assisted experience acquisition.

Result: Empirical results show consistent improvements over zero-shot LLMs, fixed-pipeline OR agents, and representative evolutionary agent frameworks on heterogeneous OR benchmarks. Case studies indicate explicit architecture evolution and graph-supported reasoning-trajectory search enhance both performance and structural interpretability.

Conclusion: Treating agent architectures and reasoning trajectories as evolvable objects provides an effective route toward adaptive and interpretable automated optimization in operations research.

Abstract: Automating operations research (OR) with large language models (LLMs) remains limited by hand-crafted reasoning--execution workflows. Complex OR tasks require adaptive coordination among problem interpretation, mathematical formulation, solver selection, code generation, and iterative debugging. To address this limitation, we propose EvoOR-Agent, a co-evolutionary framework for automated optimization. The framework represents agent workflows as activity-on-edge (AOE)-style networks, making workflow topology, execution dependencies, and alternative reasoning paths explicit. On this representation, the framework maintains an architecture graph and evolves a population of reasoning individuals through graph-mediated path-conditioned recombination, multi-granularity semantic mutation, and elitist population update. A knowledge-base-assisted experience-acquisition module further injects reusable OR practices into initialization and semantic variation. Empirical results on heterogeneous OR benchmarks show that the proposed framework consistently improves over zero-shot LLMs, fixed-pipeline OR agents, and representative evolutionary agent frameworks. Case studies and ablation analyses further indicate that explicit architecture evolution and graph-supported reasoning-trajectory search contribute to both performance improvement and structural interpretability. These results suggest that treating agent architectures and reasoning trajectories as evolvable objects provides an effective route toward adaptive and interpretable automated optimization.

</details>


### [88] [Evolutionary Negative Module Pruning for Better LoRA Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17753)
*Anda Cao,Zhuo Gou,Yi Wang,Kaixuan Chen,Yu Wang,Can Wang,Mingli Song,Jie Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ENMP is an evolutionary search method that identifies and prunes negative LoRA modules before merging to improve multi-task deployment performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LoRA merging methods assume all modules contribute positively, but negative modules degrade performance upon merging. The paper aims to address this bottleneck.

Method: ENMP uses evolutionary search strategy to navigate the discrete, non-differentiable landscape of module selection, identifying optimal pruning configurations to exclude detrimental LoRA layers.

Result: ENMP consistently boosts performance of existing merging algorithms, achieving state-of-the-art results across both language and vision domains.

Conclusion: Identifying and pruning negative modules via evolutionary search significantly improves LoRA merging effectiveness for multi-task deployment.

Abstract: Merging multiple Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) experts into a single backbone is a promising approach for efficient multi-task deployment. While existing methods strive to alleviate interference via weight interpolation or subspace alignment, they rest upon the implicit assumption that all LoRA matrices contribute constructively to the merged model. In this paper, we uncover a critical bottleneck in current merging paradigms: the existence of $\textit{negative modules}$ -- specific LoRA layers that inherently degrade global performance upon merging. We propose $\textbf{E}$volutionary $\textbf{N}$egative $\textbf{M}$odule $\textbf{P}$runing ($\textbf{ENMP}$), a plug-and-play LoRA pruning method to locate and exclude these detrimental modules prior to merging. By leveraging an evolutionary search strategy, ENMP effectively navigates the discrete, non-differentiable landscape of module selection to identify optimal pruning configurations. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that ENMP consistently boosts the performance of existing merging algorithms, achieving a new state-of-the-art across both language and vision domains. Code is available at https://github.com/CaoAnda/ENMP-LoRAMerging.

</details>


### [89] [Contrastive Attribution in the Wild: An Interpretability Analysis of LLM Failures on Realistic Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17761)
*Rongyuan Tan,Jue Zhang,Zhuozhao Li,Qingwei Lin,Saravan Rajmohan,Dongmei Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Contrastive attribution can provide useful signals for analyzing LLM failures in some cases, but isn't universally applicable across realistic benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpretability tools for LLMs have mainly been studied on short prompts or toy settings, leaving their behavior on commonly used benchmarks underexplored.

Method: Use contrastive LRP-based attribution to analyze LLM failures, formulating it as contrastive attribution that attributes logit differences between incorrect and correct outputs to input tokens and internal states, with an efficient extension for constructing cross-layer attribution graphs for long contexts.

Result: Token-level contrastive attribution yields informative signals in some failure cases but is not universally applicable, showing both utility and limitations for realistic LLM failure analysis.

Conclusion: Contrastive attribution offers a practical tool for analyzing LLM failures in realistic settings, but its effectiveness varies across cases, highlighting the need for careful application and awareness of its limitations.

Abstract: Interpretability tools are increasingly used to analyze failures of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet prior work largely focuses on short prompts or toy settings, leaving their behavior on commonly used benchmarks underexplored. To address this gap, we study contrastive, LRP-based attribution as a practical tool for analyzing LLM failures in realistic settings. We formulate failure analysis as \textit{contrastive attribution}, attributing the logit difference between an incorrect output token and a correct alternative to input tokens and internal model states, and introduce an efficient extension that enables construction of cross-layer attribution graphs for long-context inputs. Using this framework, we conduct a systematic empirical study across benchmarks, comparing attribution patterns across datasets, model sizes, and training checkpoints. Our results show that this token-level contrastive attribution can yield informative signals in some failure cases, but is not universally applicable, highlighting both its utility and its limitations for realistic LLM failure analysis. Our code is available at: https://aka.ms/Debug-XAI.

</details>


### [90] [When Vision-Language Models Judge Without Seeing: Exposing Informativeness Bias](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17768)
*Xiaohan Zou,Roshan Sridhar,Mohammadtaher Safarzadeh,Dan Roth*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BIRCH is a new judging paradigm that reduces informativeness bias in VLM-as-a-Judge systems by first correcting image content inconsistencies in candidate answers, then comparing answers against this corrected version.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLM-as-a-Judge systems suffer from "informativeness bias" - they often blindly favor more informative answers even when they conflict with image content, undermining judge reliability for vision-language model evaluation.

Method: Proposed BIRCH (Balanced Informativeness and CoRrectness with a Truthful AnCHor): first corrects inconsistencies with image content in candidate answers, creating a truthful anchor version, then compares answers against this corrected version to shift focus from informativeness to image-grounded correctness.

Result: Experiments show BIRCH reduces informativeness bias by up to 17% and achieves performance gains of up to 9.8% across multiple models and benchmarks.

Conclusion: The work reveals a fundamental flaw in current VLM-as-a-Judge systems (informativeness bias) and demonstrates that principled designs like BIRCH can significantly improve judge reliability by better balancing informativeness with image-grounded correctness.

Abstract: The reliability of VLM-as-a-Judge is critical for the automatic evaluation of vision-language models (VLMs). Despite recent progress, our analysis reveals that VLM-as-a-Judge often pays limited attention to the image when making decisions. Instead, they often blindly favor the more informative answer, even when they can recognize it conflicts with the image content. We call this problem informativeness bias, which significantly undermines judge reliability. To address it, we propose BIRCH (Balanced Informativeness and CoRrectness with a Truthful AnCHor), a judging paradigm that first corrects inconsistencies with the image content in candidate answers, and then compares the answers against this corrected version. This shifts the judge's focus from informativeness to image-grounded correctness. Experiments on multiple models and benchmarks show that BIRCH reduces informativeness bias by up to 17%, resulting in performance gains of up to 9.8%. Our work reveals an overlooked but fundamental flaw in current VLM-as-a-Judge systems and highlights the need for more principled designs.

</details>


### [91] [Prompt Optimization Enables Stable Algorithmic Collusion in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17774)
*Yingtao Tian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Prompt optimization enables LLM agents to autonomously discover and coordinate on stable tacit collusion strategies in market simulations, outperforming baseline approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing research on LLM agents in markets focuses on hand-crafted prompts, but the emerging paradigm of prompt optimization requires new methodologies to understand autonomous agent behavior and the risk of emergent algorithmic collusion.

Method: Proposed a meta-learning loop where LLM agents participate in duopoly markets, with an LLM meta-optimizer iteratively refining shared strategic guidance through prompt optimization experiments.

Result: Meta-prompt optimization enables agents to discover stable tacit collusion strategies with substantially improved coordination quality compared to baseline agents. These behaviors generalize to held-out test markets, indicating discovery of general coordination principles.

Conclusion: Prompt optimization leads to emergent collusive behaviors in autonomous multi-agent systems, calling for further investigation into AI safety implications as agents can discover and coordinate on harmful market strategies without explicit programming.

Abstract: LLM agents in markets present algorithmic collusion risks. While prior work shows LLM agents reach supracompetitive prices through tacit coordination, existing research focuses on hand-crafted prompts. The emerging paradigm of prompt optimization necessitates new methodologies for understanding autonomous agent behavior. We investigate whether prompt optimization leads to emergent collusive behaviors in market simulations. We propose a meta-learning loop where LLM agents participate in duopoly markets and an LLM meta-optimizer iteratively refines shared strategic guidance. Our experiments reveal that meta-prompt optimization enables agents to discover stable tacit collusion strategies with substantially improved coordination quality compared to baseline agents. These behaviors generalize to held-out test markets, indicating discovery of general coordination principles. Analysis of evolved prompts reveals systematic coordination mechanisms through stable shared strategies. Our findings call for further investigation into AI safety implications in autonomous multi-agent systems.

</details>


### [92] [Adversarial Arena: Crowdsourcing Data Generation through Interactive Competition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17803)
*Prasoon Goyal,Sattvik Sahai,Michael Johnston,Hangjie Shi,Yao Lu,Shaohua Liu,Anna Rumshisky,Rahul Gupta,Anna Gottardi,Desheng Zhang,Lavina Vaz,Leslie Ball,Lucy Hu,Luke Dai,Samyuth Sagi,Maureen Murray,Sankaranarayanan Ananthakrishnan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An adversarial competition framework generates high-quality conversational data by having attackers create prompts and defenders generate responses, validated through cybersecurity safety alignment with significant improvements in secure code generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality conversational data is rare and costly, especially in low-resource domains and for multi-turn conversations. Current methods like crowdsourcing or synthetic generation often yield low-quality or low-diversity data.

Method: Introduced Adversarial Arena framework where data generation is framed as an adversarial task: attackers create prompts, and defenders generate responses. Conducted a competition with 10 academic teams building attacker or defender bots, focused on LLM safety alignment in cybersecurity.

Result: Generated 19,683 multi-turn conversations. Fine-tuning an open-source model on this dataset produced an 18.47% improvement on CyberSecEval-Instruct and 29.42% improvement on CyberSecEval-MITRE for secure code generation.

Conclusion: The adversarial competition framework effectively generates diverse, high-quality conversational data for low-resource domains, demonstrated by significant improvements in cybersecurity safety alignment benchmarks.

Abstract: Post-training Large Language Models requires diverse, high-quality data which is rare and costly to obtain, especially in low resource domains and for multi-turn conversations. Common solutions are crowdsourcing or synthetic generation, but both often yield low-quality or low-diversity data. We introduce Adversarial Arena for building high quality conversational datasets by framing data generation as an adversarial task: attackers create prompts, and defenders generate responses. This interactive competition between multiple teams naturally produces diverse and complex data. We validated this approach by conducting a competition with 10 academic teams from top US and European universities, each building attacker or defender bots. The competition, focused on safety alignment of LLMs in cybersecurity, generated 19,683 multi-turn conversations. Fine-tuning an open-source model on this dataset produced an 18.47% improvement in secure code generation on CyberSecEval-Instruct and 29.42% improvement on CyberSecEval-MITRE.

</details>


### [93] [WebUncertainty: Dual-Level Uncertainty Driven Planning and Reasoning For Autonomous Web Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17821)
*Lingfeng Zhang,yongan sun,Jinpeng Hu,Hui Ma,yang ying,Kuien Liu,Zenglin Shi,Meng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: WebUncertainty is a new autonomous web agent framework that addresses planning and reasoning uncertainty through adaptive planning and Monte Carlo tree search with uncertainty quantification, achieving state-of-the-art performance on web automation benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based web agents struggle with complex tasks involving dynamic interactions and long-horizon execution due to rigid planning strategies and hallucination-prone reasoning, creating significant limitations in real-world web automation.

Method: The framework introduces two key mechanisms: 1) Task Uncertainty-Driven Adaptive Planning Mechanism that dynamically selects planning modes for unknown environments, and 2) Action Uncertainty-Driven Monte Carlo tree search (MCTS) Reasoning Mechanism with Confidence-induced Action Uncertainty (ConActU) strategy that quantifies both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty to optimize search and guide decision-making.

Result: Experimental evaluation on WebArena and WebVoyager benchmarks shows that WebUncertainty achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art baseline methods.

Conclusion: Addressing dual-level uncertainty in planning and reasoning through adaptive mechanisms and uncertainty-aware MCTS significantly improves autonomous web agent performance on complex real-world tasks.

Abstract: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have empowered autonomous web agents to execute natural language instructions directly on real-world webpages. However, existing agents often struggle with complex tasks involving dynamic interactions and long-horizon execution due to rigid planning strategies and hallucination-prone reasoning. To address these limitations, we propose WebUncertainty, a novel autonomous agent framework designed to tackle dual-level uncertainty in planning and reasoning. Specifically, we design a Task Uncertainty-Driven Adaptive Planning Mechanism that adaptively selects planning modes to navigate unknown environments. Furthermore, we introduce an Action Uncertainty-Driven Monte Carlo tree search (MCTS) Reasoning Mechanism. This mechanism incorporates the Confidence-induced Action Uncertainty (ConActU) strategy to quantify both aleatoric uncertainty (AU) and epistemic uncertainty (EU), thereby optimizing the search process and guiding robust decision-making. Experimental results on the WebArena and WebVoyager benchmarks demonstrate that WebUncertainty achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [94] [Polysemantic Experts, Monosemantic Paths: Routing as Control in MoEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17837)
*Charles Ye,Bo Yuan,Lee Sharkey*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MoE models decompose hidden states into control signals (for routing) and content channels, revealing that expert paths (not individual experts) are the interpretable unit, with tokens following different semantic trajectories based on function.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how Mixture-of-Experts models handle the dual role of residual streams as both state and instruction, and to develop interpretable decompositions that reveal the underlying structure of routing decisions.

Method: Introduced a parameter-free decomposition that splits each layer's hidden state into a control signal (driving routing decisions) and an orthogonal content channel (invisible to the router). Analyzed six MoE architectures to examine feature preservation across channels.

Result: Found that content channels preserve surface-level features (language, token identity, position), while control signals encode abstract functions that rotate across layers. Individual experts remain polysemantic, but expert paths become monosemantic, clustering tokens by semantic function across languages and surface forms. The same token follows distinct trajectories based on semantic function.

Conclusion: The natural unit of interpretability in MoE models is not the individual expert but the trajectory through the expert network, as control subspace clusters are substantially more monosemantic than those in full representations, revealing compositional specialization across layers.

Abstract: An LLM's residual stream is both state and instruction: it encodes the current context and determines the next transformation. We introduce a parameter-free decomposition for Mixture-of-Experts models that splits each layer's hidden state into a control signal that causally drives routing and an orthogonal content channel invisible to the router. Across six MoE architectures, we find that models preserve surface-level features (language, token identity, position) in the content channel, while the control signal encodes an abstract function that rotates from layer to layer. Because each routing decision is low-bandwidth, this hand-off forces compositional specialization across layers. While individual experts remain polysemantic, expert paths become monosemantic, clustering tokens by semantic function across languages and surface forms. The same token (e.g., ":") follows distinct trajectories depending on whether it serves as a type annotation, an introductory colon, or a time separator. Our decomposition identifies the source of this structure: clusters in the control subspace are substantially more monosemantic than those in the full representation. As a result, the natural unit of interpretability in MoEs is not the expert but the trajectory.

</details>


### [95] [On the Reliability of Computer Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17849)
*Gonzalo Gonzalez-Pumariega,Saaket Agashe,Jiachen Yang,Ang Li,Xin Eric Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies why computer-use agents that succeed once may fail on repeated executions of the same task, identifying three factors of unreliability: execution stochasticity, task specification ambiguity, and agent behavior variability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite rapid improvements in computer-use agents performing real-world tasks (web navigation, desktop automation, software interaction), there's a reliability problem: agents that succeed once often fail on repeated executions of the same task, even with unchanged models and tasks. This raises the fundamental question of what prevents successful agents from performing reliably.

Method: The researchers study unreliability through three factors: stochasticity during execution, ambiguity in task specification, and variability in agent behavior. They analyze these on OSWorld using repeated executions of the same task with paired statistical tests that capture task-level changes across settings.

Result: The analysis shows that reliability depends on both how tasks are specified and how agent behavior varies across executions. The findings indicate that agents should be evaluated under repeated execution, should be allowed to resolve task ambiguity through interaction, and should favor strategies that remain stable across runs.

Conclusion: Reliability in computer-use agents is influenced by multiple factors beyond just achieving initial success. To build more robust agents, the research community needs to incorporate repeated execution evaluations, design agents that can interactively resolve task ambiguities, and develop strategies that maintain consistency across different runs.

Abstract: Computer-use agents have rapidly improved on real-world tasks such as web navigation, desktop automation, and software interaction, in some cases surpassing human performance. Yet even when the task and model are unchanged, an agent that succeeds once may fail on a repeated execution of the same task. This raises a fundamental question: if an agent can succeed at a task once, what prevents it from doing so reliably? In this work, we study the sources of unreliability in computer-use agents through three factors: stochasticity during execution, ambiguity in task specification, and variability in agent behavior. We analyze these factors on OSWorld using repeated executions of the same task together with paired statistical tests that capture task-level changes across settings. Our analysis shows that reliability depends on both how tasks are specified and how agent behavior varies across executions. These findings suggest the need to evaluate agents under repeated execution, to allow agents to resolve task ambiguity through interaction, and to favor strategies that remain stable across runs.

</details>


### [96] [SPREG: Structured Plan Repair with Entropy-Guided Test-Time Intervention for Large Language Model Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17884)
*Xuan Wang,Yu Ming,Xinhao Zhong,Xinyu Yu,Wenjie Wang,Shuai Chen,Wei Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPREG is a lightweight inference-time framework that uses real-time entropy monitoring to detect and repair logical errors in LLM reasoning, improving accuracy by 20% on AIME25 while maintaining fluency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs suffer from logical hallucinations and stochastic drifts during long-chain reasoning, and standard Classifier-Free Guidance implementations often cause semantic dilution and linguistic degradation.

Method: SPREG employs an adaptive dual-threshold mechanism to monitor real-time entropy, identifying entropy spikes as indicators of logical failure. It triggers dynamic repair by replacing uninformative null-priors with reference distributions synthesized from historical high-confidence states, modulated according to structured reasoning stages.

Result: The experiments demonstrate significant gains, notably a 20.0% absolute accuracy improvement on AIME25, while effectively suppressing uncontrolled entropy drift in complex tasks.

Conclusion: SPREG provides an effective framework for surgical error rectification during LLM inference, steering models back to stable reasoning manifolds without compromising fluency through structured plan-guided real-time entropy gating.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are prone to logical hallucinations and stochastic drifts during long-chain reasoning. While Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) can improve instruction adherence, standard static implementations often cause semantic dilution and linguistic degradation. We propose SPREG (Structured Plan-guided Real-time Entropy Gating), a lightweight inference-time framework for surgical error rectification. SPREG employs an adaptive dual-threshold mechanism to monitor real-time entropy, identifying sudden ``entropy spikes'' as reliable indicators of logical failure. Upon detection, it triggers a dynamic repair by replacing uninformative null-priors with reference distributions synthesized from historical high-confidence states. By modulating guidance intensity according to structured reasoning stages (e.g., Action, Observation), SPREG steers the model back to a stable manifold without compromising fluency. Our experiments demonstrate significant gains, notably a 20.0% absolute accuracy improvement on AIME25, while effectively suppressing uncontrolled entropy drift in complex tasks.

</details>


### [97] [Physics-Informed Causal MDPs for Sequential Constraint Repair in Engineering Simulation Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17910)
*Chuhan Qiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PI-CMDP framework enables off-policy learning in constrained MDPs with binary state spaces by combining causal identification, state-space compression, and physics-guided estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a fundamental tension in constrained MDPs with large binary state spaces: causal identification requires structural assumptions while sample-efficient policy learning requires state-space compression.

Method: Introduce PI-CMDP framework with Identify-Compress-Estimate pipeline: 1) Identify causal edge weights using Lifecycle Ordering Assumption, 2) Compress state space using Markov abstraction, 3) Estimate with physics-guided doubly-robust estimator.

Result: On TPS benchmark (4,206 episodes), PI-CMDP achieves 76.2% repair success rate with only 300 training episodes vs 70.8% for strongest baseline (+5.4 pp), and 83.4% vs 80.6% in full-data regime (+2.8 pp), reducing cascade failure rates significantly.

Conclusion: PI-CMDP framework successfully addresses the tension between causal identification and sample-efficient learning in constrained MDPs with binary state spaces, demonstrating practical improvements in engineering constraint repair applications.

Abstract: Off-policy learning in constrained MDPs with large binary state spaces faces a fundamental tension: causal identification of transition dynamics requires structural assumptions, while sample-efficient policy learning requires state-space compression. We introduce PI-CMDP, a framework for CMDPs whose constraint dependencies form a layered DAG under a Lifecycle Ordering Assumption (LOA). We propose an Identify-Compress-Estimate pipeline: (i) Identify: LOA enables backdoor identification of causal edge weights for cross-layer pairs, with formal partial-identification bounds when LOA is violated; (ii) Compress: a Markov abstraction compresses state cardinality from 2^(WL) to (W+1)^L under layer-priority regularity and exchangeability; and (iii) Estimate: a physics-guided doubly-robust estimator remains unbiased and reduces the variance constant when the physics prior outperforms a learned model. We instantiate PI-CMDP on constraint repair in engineering simulation pipelines. On the TPS benchmark (4,206 episodes), PI-CMDP achieves 76.2% repair success rate with only 300 training episodes versus 70.8% for the strongest baseline (+5.4 pp), narrowing to +2.8 pp (83.4% vs. 80.6%) in the full-data regime, while substantially reducing cascade failure rates. All improvements are consistent across 5 independent seeds (paired t-test p < 0.02).

</details>


### [98] [LiteResearcher: A Scalable Agentic RL Training Framework for Deep Research Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17931)
*Wanli Li,Bince Qu,Bo Pan,Jianyu Zhang,Zheng Liu,Pan Zhang,Wei Chen,Bo Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LiteResearcher is a scalable RL training framework for LLM-based research agents that uses a virtual world to mirror real-world search dynamics, enabling efficient training of small models that outperform larger commercial systems on research benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agentic RL for deep research faces two main challenges: hand-crafted synthetic data fails to elicit genuine real-world search capabilities, and real-world search dependency during RL training causes instability and high costs, limiting scalability.

Method: Developed LiteResearcher framework that constructs a lite virtual world mirroring real-world search dynamics, enabling continuously improving training recipes for search agents without costly real-world interactions.

Result: LiteResearcher-4B achieves state-of-the-art results on GAIA (71.3%) and Xbench (78.0%) benchmarks, outperforming large-scale open-source and commercial models like Tongyi DeepResearch and Claude-4.5 Sonnet.

Conclusion: Scalable RL training through virtual world construction is a key enabler for deep research agents, demonstrating that small, efficiently trained models can outperform much larger commercial systems on complex research tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful training paradigm for LLM-based agents. However, scaling agentic RL for deep research remains constrained by two coupled challenges: hand-crafted synthetic data fails to elicit genuine real-world search capabilities, and real-world search dependency during RL training introduces instability and prohibitive cost, which limits the scalability of Agentic RL. LiteResearcher is a training framework that makes Agentic RL scalable: by constructing a lite virtual world that mirrors real-world search dynamics, we enable a continuously improving training recipe that empowers a tiny search agent to outperform large-scale open-source and commercial models (e.g., Tongyi DeepResearch and Claude-4.5 Sonnet). Specifically, on common benchmarks such as GAIA and Xbench, our LiteResearcher-4B achieves open-source state-of-the-art results of 71.3% and 78.0% respectively, demonstrating that scalable RL training is a key enabler for Deep Research Agents.

</details>


### [99] [ContraPrompt: Contrastive Prompt Optimization via Dyadic Reasoning Trace Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17937)
*Rishav Rishav,Pushpak Pujari,Pushpendre Rastogi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ContraPrompt: A prompt optimization method that analyzes contrastive reasoning traces from model failures and successes to extract optimization rules organized into input-aware decision trees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing prompt optimization methods either analyze individual failures in isolation or compare prompt variants across examples, operating on single execution traces without access to the reasoning process that distinguishes success from failure on the same input.

Method: ContraPrompt uses dyadic reasoning trace analysis - comparing complete intermediate reasoning processes when a model fails but succeeds on a retry with feedback. The multi-attempt solving phase is an instrumented agentic retry loop that generates contrastive data automatically. Extracted rules are organized into an input-aware decision tree routing instructions by observable input characteristics.

Result: Outperforms GEPA on all four reasoning and compliance benchmarks: +8.29pp on HotPotQA, +2.21pp on GDPR-Bench, +7.14pp on GPQA Diamond, and +0.74pp on BBH. On 53 EvalSet problems, beats GEPA on 11, ties on 41, loses on 1. On FiNER-139 financial NER, achieves +7.77pp over baseline and +1.94pp over GEPA.

Conclusion: ContraPrompt effectively leverages contrastive reasoning traces from model retry attempts to optimize prompts, with dyadic trace contrastivity being the critical component (ablations show -16% relative average drop without it). The approach enables automatic generation of optimization rules without human annotation.

Abstract: Prompt optimization methods either analyze individual failures in isolation or compare prompt variants across examples, operating on single execution traces with no access to the reasoning process distinguishing success from failure on the same input. We introduce ContraPrompt, built on the observation that when a model fails but succeeds on a retry with feedback, the difference between its two chain-of-thought traces constitutes an optimization signal not captured by prior methods. Unlike prior contrastive methods, we compare complete intermediate reasoning processes: the two traces share model, input, and base prompt, so remaining differences reflect reasoning strategy and appended error feedback -- we call this dyadic reasoning trace analysis. The multi-attempt solving phase is an instrumented agentic retry loop that generates contrastive data automatically without human annotation. Extracted rules are organized into an input-aware decision tree routing instructions by observable input characteristics. On four reasoning and compliance benchmarks, ContraPrompt outperforms GEPA (Agrawal et al., 2026) on all four, with absolute gains of +8.29 pp on HotPotQA (+20.8% rel.), +2.21 pp on GDPR-Bench (+18.2% rel.), +7.14 pp on GPQA Diamond (+10.6% rel.), and +0.74 pp on BBH (+0.85% rel.). Ablations confirm dyadic trace contrastivity is the critical component, with a -16% relative average drop upon its removal. On 53 EvalSet black-box optimization problems, ContraPrompt beats GEPA on 11, ties on 41, and loses on 1 at equal budget. On FiNER-139 financial named entity recognition (Loukas et al., 2022), ContraPrompt achieves +7.77 pp over the unoptimized baseline (+11.6% rel.) and +1.94 pp over GEPA (+2.66% rel.), with branch conditions aligning with standard US GAAP financial-instrument categories.

</details>


### [100] [CADMAS-CTX: Contextual Capability Calibration for Multi-Agent Delegation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17950)
*Chuhan Qiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CADMAS-CTX improves multi-agent delegation by modeling agents' context-dependent capabilities and using risk-aware delegation instead of static skill assignments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-agent systems assume fixed agent capabilities, but real-world agent performance varies based on task context. Static skill-level capability profiles average over heterogeneous situations, leading to systematic misdelegation.

Method: Proposes CADMAS-CTX framework with hierarchical contextual capability profiles: for each agent, skill, and coarse context bucket, maintains a Beta posterior capturing stable experience. Delegation uses a risk-aware score combining posterior mean with uncertainty penalty.

Result: On GAIA with GPT-4o agents: CADMAS-CTX achieves 0.442 accuracy vs static baseline 0.381 and AutoGen 0.354 (non-overlapping 95% confidence intervals). On SWE-bench Lite: improves resolve rate from 22.3% to 31.4%. Uncertainty penalty improves robustness against context tagging noise.

Conclusion: Contextual calibration and risk-aware delegation significantly improve multi-agent teamwork compared to static global skill assignments, addressing the bias-variance tradeoff through formal context-aware routing.

Abstract: We revisit multi-agent delegation under a stronger and more realistic assumption: an agent's capability is not fixed at the skill level, but depends on task context. A coding agent may excel at short standalone edits yet fail on long-horizon debugging; a planner may perform well on shallow tasks yet degrade on chained dependencies. Static skill-level capability profiles therefore average over heterogeneous situations and can induce systematic misdelegation. We propose CADMAS-CTX, a framework for contextual capability calibration. For each agent, skill, and coarse context bucket, CADMAS-CTX maintains a Beta posterior that captures stable experience in that part of the task space. Delegation is then made by a risk-aware score that combines the posterior mean with an uncertainty penalty, so that agents delegate only when a peer appears better and that assessment is sufficiently well supported by evidence. This paper makes three contributions. First, a hierarchical contextual capability profile replaces static skill-level confidence with context-conditioned posteriors. Second, based on contextual bandit theory, we formally prove context-aware routing achieves lower cumulative regret than static routing under sufficient context heterogeneity, formalizing the bias-variance tradeoff. Third, we empirically validate our method on GAIA and SWE-bench benchmarks. On GAIA with GPT-4o agents, CADMAS-CTX achieves 0.442 accuracy, outperforming static baseline 0.381 and AutoGen 0.354 with non-overlapping 95% confidence intervals. On SWE-bench Lite, it improves resolve rate from 22.3% to 31.4%. Ablations show the uncertainty penalty improves robustness against context tagging noise. Our results demonstrate contextual calibration and risk-aware delegation significantly improve multi-agent teamwork compared with static global skill assignments.

</details>


### [101] [TPS-CalcBench: A Benchmark and Diagnostic Evaluation Framework for LLM Analytical Calculation Competence in Hypersonic Thermal Protection System Engineering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17966)
*Jinglai Zheng,Chuhan Qiao,Haiming Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A benchmark called TPS-CalcBench evaluates LLMs for aerospace engineering safety-critical applications by testing their analytical calculation abilities in hypersonic TPS design, including reasoning quality assessment and intervention methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current scientific benchmarks are insufficient for safety-critical aerospace engineering as they only test abstract math/basic physics, evaluate final answers only, ignore engineering reasoning processes, and cannot detect models that produce numerically reasonable but physically invalid answers - which is more dangerous than declining to respond in TPS design scenarios.

Method: Developed TPS-CalcBench with: domain-oriented task taxonomy (4 difficulty levels, 8 categories from Anderson's textbook), dual-track evaluation measuring result accuracy and reasoning quality via 8-dimension rubric and calibrated judge with human audit, human-AI data pipeline producing 420 high-confidence core items and 810 noise-controlled items, noise-sensitivity analysis, and three diagnostic intervention methods: DFA-TPS fine-tuning, RAG-EQ retrieval grounding, and PA-CoT process-aware prompting.

Result: Tests on 13 models from 7 groups showed wide performance differences (KPI 12.6-87.9), revealed hidden formula selection defects, demonstrated data-driven rank changes, and showed effective intervention improvements, establishing a complete diagnose-evaluate-intervene framework.
