Conclusion: TPS-CalcBench provides a comprehensive diagnostic benchmark for evaluating LLMs in safety-critical aerospace engineering applications, addressing critical gaps in existing evaluation methods and enabling better assessment and improvement of LLM reasoning quality for engineering domains where inaccurate calculations can lead to catastrophic failures.

Abstract: Deploying LLMs as reasoning assistants in safety-critical aerospace engineering requires stricter evaluation criteria than general scientific benchmarks. In hypersonic thermal protection system (TPS) design, inaccurate stagnation-point heat flux or boundary-layer calculations may cause catastrophic design margin violations. Models with numerically reasonable but physically invalid answers are more dangerous than those declining to respond. Current scientific benchmarks only test abstract math and basic physics, evaluate final answers solely, ignore engineering reasoning processes, and cannot detect such critical failures. We propose TPS-CalcBench, the first diagnostic benchmark for closed-form analytical calculations in hypersonic aerodynamics and high-temperature gas dynamics that experienced TPS engineers conduct without simulations. Our contributions include domain-oriented task taxonomy with 4 difficulty levels and 8 categories from Anderson's textbook, dual-track evaluation measuring result accuracy and reasoning quality via an 8-dimension rubric and calibrated judge with human audit to identify right answer wrong reasoning issues, human-AI data pipeline producing 420 high-confidence core items and 810 noise-controlled pre-gating items from 4560 raw data, noise-sensitivity analysis measuring data quality impacts on model ranking, and three diagnostic intervention methods: DFA-TPS fine-tuning, RAG-EQ retrieval grounding and PA-CoT process-aware prompting. Tests on 13 models from 7 groups show wide performance differences (KPI 12.6-87.9), hidden formula selection defects, data-driven rank changes and effective intervention improvements, establishing a complete diagnose-evaluate-intervene framework for safety-critical engineering LLM deployment assessment.

</details>


### [102] [A Sugeno Integral View of Binarized Neural Network Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17967)
*Ismaïl Baaj,Henri Prade*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BNNs can be represented as Sugeno integrals, providing interpretable rule-based explanations for binary neural decisions through a fuzzy integral framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a need for interpretable representations of neural network decisions. Binarized neural networks (BNNs) operate with binary activations, but their decision-making process lacks explicit interpretable explanations. The Sugeno integral offers a framework for representing input importance and interactions while being equivalent to if-then rules, potentially bridging the gap between BNN performance and interpretability.

Method: Establishing a precise mathematical connection between BNN activation threshold tests and Sugeno integrals on binary inputs. The method shows that each hidden neuron's binary activation decision can be expressed as a Sugeno integral, yielding an explicit set-function representation and rule-based form. Also provides Sugeno-integral expressions for the final layer score.

Result: Demonstrated that BNN neuron decisions can be represented as Sugeno integrals, providing explicit set-function representations of each neuron's binary decisions and corresponding rule-based explanations. The framework allows adaptation to richer input interactions and extension beyond the binary case of BNNs.

Conclusion: BNNs have a natural mathematical correspondence with Sugeno integrals, offering an interpretable rule-based framework for understanding neural network decisions. This connection provides both theoretical insights and practical interpretability benefits, with potential extensions to more complex scenarios beyond binary inputs.

Abstract: In this article, we establish a precise connection between binarized neural networks (BNNs) and Sugeno integrals. The advantage of the Sugeno integral is that it provides a framework for representing the importance of inputs and their interactions, while being equivalent to a set of if-then rules. For a hidden BNN neuron at inference time, we show that the activation threshold test can be written as a Sugeno integral on binary inputs. This yields an explicit set-function representation of each neuron decision, and an associated rule-based representation. We also provide a Sugeno-integral expression for the last-layer score. Finally, we discuss how the same framework can be adapted to support richer input interactions and how it can be extended beyond the binary case induced by binarized neural networks.

</details>


### [103] [From Fallback to Frontline: When Can LLMs be Superior Annotators of Human Perspectives?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17968)
*Hasan Amin,Harry Yizhou Tian,Xiaoni Duan,Chien-Ju Ho,Rajiv Khanna,Ming Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs can outperform human annotators in predicting aggregate subgroup opinions on subjective tasks under common practical conditions, due to their structural properties as estimators rather than lived experience.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as annotators but are typically viewed as a pragmatic fallback rather than faithful estimators of human perspectives. The paper challenges this presumption by examining whether LLMs can actually be superior to human annotators for estimating collective human judgments.

Method: The authors frame perspective-taking as estimation of latent group-level judgment and characterize the conditions under which LLMs outperform human annotators. They analyze LLMs as estimators with structural properties like low variance and reduced coupling between representation and processing biases.

Result: The paper shows that LLMs can outperform human annotators, including in-group humans, when predicting aggregate subgroup opinions on subjective tasks. This advantage arises from LLMs' structural estimator properties rather than lived experience. Clear regimes are identified where LLMs act as statistically superior frontline estimators, as well as principled limits where human judgment remains essential.

Conclusion: LLMs should be repositioned from being seen as a cost-saving compromise to becoming a principled tool for estimating collective human perspectives, with defined conditions for their superiority and limits for when human judgment is still necessary.

Abstract: Although large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as annotators at scale, they are typically treated as a pragmatic fallback rather than a faithful estimator of human perspectives. This work challenges that presumption. By framing perspective-taking as the estimation of a latent group-level judgment, we characterize the conditions under which modern LLMs can outperform human annotators, including in-group humans, when predicting aggregate subgroup opinions on subjective tasks, and show that these conditions are common in practice. This advantage arises from structural properties of LLMs as estimators, including low variance and reduced coupling between representation and processing biases, rather than any claim of lived experience. Our analysis identifies clear regimes where LLMs act as statistically superior frontline estimators, as well as principled limits where human judgment remains essential. These findings reposition LLMs from a cost-saving compromise to a principled tool for estimating collective human perspectives.

</details>


### [104] [AIT Academy: Cultivating the Complete Agent with a Confucian Three-Domain Curriculum](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17989)
*Jiaqi Li,Lvyang Zhang,Yang Zhao,Wen Lu,Lidong Zhai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AIT Academy: A comprehensive curriculum framework for AI agents across three knowledge domains (Natural Science/Technical, Humanities/Creative, Social/Ethical) using Confucian Six Arts as behavioral archetypes, showing multi-domain training improves performance and reveals pathologies like Security Awareness Calibration Pathology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI agents are specialized systems with predictable deficits where they weren't trained, lacking a comprehensive curriculum theory for what a fully developed agent should know and be able to do across all intelligent behaviors.

Method: Developed AIT Academy curriculum framework organized into three domains based on Kagan's Three Cultures and UNESCO ISCED-F 2013. Used Confucian Six Arts (liuyi) as behavioral archetypes mapped to trainable agent capabilities. Created three training grounds: ClawdGO Security Dojo (Domain I), Athen's Academy (Domain II), and Alt Mirage Stage (Domain III). Tested across multiple backbone LLMs with different curriculum scheduling approaches.

Result: 15.9-point improvement in security capability scores with weakest-first curriculum scheduling, 7-percentage-point gain in social reasoning with principled attribution modeling. Discovered Security Awareness Calibration Pathology (SACP) where over-trained Domain I agents fail on out-of-distribution evaluation, demonstrating the diagnostic value of multi-domain perspective.

Conclusion: A comprehensive, multi-domain curriculum framework is essential for developing well-rounded AI agents. The Confucian Six Arts provide effective behavioral archetypes, and cross-domain training reveals pathologies that single-domain approaches miss, highlighting the need for holistic agent education.

Abstract: What does it mean to give an AI agent a complete education? Current agent development produces specialists systems optimized for a single capability dimension, whether tool use, code generation, or security awareness that exhibit predictable deficits wherever they were not trained. We argue this pattern reflects a structural absence: there is no curriculum theory for agents, no principled account of what a fully developed agent should know, be, and be able to do across the full scope of intelligent behavior.
  This paper introduces the AIT Academy (Agents Institute of Technology Academy), a curriculum framework for cultivating AI agents across the tripartite structure of human knowledge. Grounded in Kagan's Three Cultures and UNESCO ISCED-F 2013, AIT organizes agent capability development into three domains: Natural Science and Technical Reasoning (Domain I), Humanities and Creative Expression (Domain II), and Social Science and Ethical Reasoning (Domain III). The Confucian Six Arts (liuyi) a 2,500-year-old holistic education system are reinterpreted as behavioral archetypes that map directly onto trainable agent capabilities within each domain.
  Three representative training grounds instantiate the framework across multiple backbone LLMs: the ClawdGO Security Dojo (Domain I), Athen's Academy (Domain II), and the Alt Mirage Stage (Domain III). Experiments demonstrate a 15.9-point improvement in security capability scores under weakest-first curriculum scheduling, and a 7-percentage-point gain in social reasoning performance under principled attribution modeling. A cross-domain finding Security Awareness Calibration Pathology (SACP), in which over-trained Domain I agents fail on out-of-distribution evaluation illustrates the diagnostic value of a multi-domain perspective unavailable to any single-domain framework.

</details>


### [105] [SELF-EMO: Emotional Self-Evolution from Recognition to Consistent Expression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18003)
*Shaowei Zhang,Faqiang Qian,Yan Chen,Ziliang Wang,Kang An,Yong Dai,Mengya Gao,Yichao Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SELF-EMO is a self-evolution framework for emotion recognition in conversation that uses role-based self-play to generate diverse conversational data and a data flywheel mechanism for continuous improvement without external supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The scarcity and static nature of high-quality annotated data limits both accurate emotion recognition and coherent emotional expression in LLMs for human-centric interaction.

Method: Proposes SELF-EMO framework with two auxiliary tasks (emotional understanding and expression), role-based self-play where the model acts as both emotion recognizer and dialogue responder, iterative interaction for diverse trajectory generation, data flywheel mechanism with smoothed IoU-based reward filtering, and SELF-GRPO reinforcement learning with multi-label alignment rewards.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on IEMOCAP, MELD, and EmoryNLP datasets, improving accuracy by +6.33% on Qwen3-4B and +8.54% on Qwen3-8B, demonstrating strong effectiveness and generalization.

Conclusion: SELF-EMO successfully addresses data scarcity in ERC through self-evolution, enabling continuous improvement without external supervision and achieving superior performance across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Emotion Recognition in Conversation (ERC) has become a fundamental capability for large language models (LLMs) in human-centric interaction. Beyond accurate recognition, coherent emotional expression is also crucial, yet both are limited by the scarcity and static nature of high-quality annotated data. In this work, we propose SELF-EMO, a self-evolution framework grounded in the hypothesis that better emotion prediction leads to more consistent emotional responses. We introduce two auxiliary tasks, emotional understanding and emotional expression, and design a role-based self-play paradigm where the model acts as both an emotion recognizer and a dialogue responder. Through iterative interactions, the model generates diverse conversational trajectories, enabling scalable data generation. To ensure quality, we adopt a data flywheel mechanism that filters candidate predictions and responses using a smoothed IoU-based reward and feeds selected samples back for continuous self-improvement without external supervision. We further develop SELF-GRPO, a reinforcement learning algorithm that stabilizes optimization with multi-label alignment rewards and group-level consistency signals. Experiments on IEMOCAP, MELD, and EmoryNLP show that SELF-EMO achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving accuracy by +6.33% on Qwen3-4B and +8.54% on Qwen3-8B, demonstrating strong effectiveness and generalization.

</details>


### [106] [The Topological Dual of a Dataset: A Logic-to-Topology Encoding for AlphaGeometry-Style Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18050)
*Anthony Bordg*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A novel logic-to-topology encoding framework is proposed to overcome representation bottlenecks in neuro-symbolic AI by revealing structural invariants in model latent spaces through topological duality with formal logic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current neuro-symbolic systems like AlphaGeometry face log-linear scaling bottlenecks in symbolic deduction, and evidence suggests neural components rely on superficial input encodings rather than deep structural understanding, creating a representation bottleneck that limits efficiency as problem complexity increases.

Method: Proposes a logic-to-topology encoding based on the Logic of Observation, leveraging the duality between provability in observable theories and topologies. Introduces the "topological dual of a dataset" transformation that bridges formal logic, topology, and neural processing.

Result: The framework provides a principled pathway for mechanistic interpretability of how models navigate complex discovery paths, serving as a "Rosetta Stone" for neuro-symbolic AI by revealing structural invariants of latent spaces under input transformations.

Conclusion: The logic-to-topology encoding addresses fundamental representation bottlenecks in neuro-symbolic systems, enabling deeper structural understanding and more efficient scaling while providing new interpretability tools that unify formal logic, topology, and neural network processing.

Abstract: AlphaGeometry represents a milestone in neuro-symbolic reasoning, yet its architecture faces a log-linear scaling bottleneck within its symbolic deduction engine that limits its efficiency as problem complexity increases. Recent technical reports suggest that current domain-specific languages may be isomorphic as input representations to natural language, interchanging them acts as a performance-invariant transformation, implying that current neural guidance relies on superficial encodings rather than structural understanding. This paper addresses this representation bottleneck by proposing a logic-to-topology encoding designed to reveal the structural invariants of a model's latent space under a transformation of its input space. By leveraging the Logic of Observation, we utilize the duality between provability in observable theories and topologies to propose a logic-to-topology encoder for the input space. We introduce the concept of the "topological dual of a dataset", a transformation that bridges formal logic, topology, and neural processing. This framework serves as a Rosetta Stone for neuro-symbolic AI, providing a principled pathway for the mechanistic interpretability of how models navigate complex discovery paths.

</details>


### [107] [Understanding Human Actions through the Lens of Executable Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18064)
*Rimvydas Rubavicius,Manisha Dubey,N. Siddharth,Subramanian Ramamoorthy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EXACT introduces a domain-specific language to represent human motions as motion programs, enabling zero-shot policy inference and compositional modeling for improved human action analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing action recognition methods fail to capture the internal structure of how actions are executed, which is crucial for assessing execution quality and distinguishing between actions in human-centered systems.

Method: Developed EXACT domain-specific language that represents human motions as underspecified motion programs, which are interpreted as reward-generating functions for zero-shot policy inference using forward-backwards representations. Combines individual policies into an executable neuro-symbolic model using program structure for compositional modeling.

Result: The proposed pipeline improves data efficiency and captures intuitive relationships between actions compared to monolithic, task-specific approaches, as demonstrated in human action segmentation and action anomaly detection tasks using motion-capture data.

Conclusion: Executable action models using EXACT motion programs provide a structured, compositional approach to understanding human actions that outperforms conventional methods in capturing execution mechanics and relationships between actions.

Abstract: Human-centred systems require an understanding of human actions in the physical world. Temporally extended sequences of actions are intentional and structured, yet existing methods for recognising what actions are performed often do not attempt to capture their structure, particularly how the actions are executed. This, however, is crucial for assessing the quality of the action's execution and its differences from other actions. To capture the internal mechanics of actions, we introduce a domain-specific language EXACT that represents human motions as underspecified motion programs, interpreted as reward-generating functions for zero-shot policy inference using forward-backwards representations. By leveraging the compositional nature of EXACT motion programs, we combine individual policies into an executable neuro-symbolic model that uses program structure for compositional modelling. We evaluate the utility of the proposed pipeline for creating executable action models by analysing motion-capture data to understand human actions, for the tasks of human action segmentation and action anomaly detection. Our results show that the use of executable action models improves data efficiency and captures intuitive relationships between actions compared with monolithic, task-specific approaches.

</details>


### [108] [Architectural Design Decisions in AI Agent Harnesses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18071)
*Hu Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper conducts an empirical study of 70 AI agent systems to identify recurring architectural design patterns, dimensions, and co-occurrences in non-LLM engineering infrastructure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agent systems increasingly rely on reusable non-LLM engineering infrastructure (tool mediation, context handling, delegation, safety control, orchestration), but the architectural design decisions in this surrounding infrastructure remain understudied.

Method: Protocol-guided, source-grounded empirical study of 70 publicly available agent-system projects using source-code and technical-material reading. The study addresses three research questions about design dimensions, co-occurrences, and architectural patterns.

Result: Identified five recurring design dimensions: subagent architecture, context management, tool systems, safety mechanisms, and orchestration. Found corpus favors file-persistent, hybrid, and hierarchical context strategies; registry-oriented tool systems dominate while MCP- and plugin-oriented extensions emerge; intermediate isolation common but high-assurance audit rare. Cross-project co-occurrence analysis revealed coordination pairs with context services, execution environments with governance, and tool-registration boundaries with ecosystem ambitions.

Conclusion: The study provides evidence-based account of architectural regularities in agent-system engineering and identifies five architectural patterns (lightweight tools, balanced CLI frameworks, multi-agent orchestrators, enterprise systems, scenario-verticalized projects), offering grounded guidance for framework designers, selectors, and researchers.

Abstract: AI agent systems increasingly rely on reusable non-LLM engineering infrastructure that packages tool mediation, context handling, delegation, safety control, and orchestration. Yet the architectural design decisions in this surrounding infrastructure remain understudied. This paper presents a protocol-guided, source-grounded empirical study of 70 publicly available agent-system projects, addressing three questions: which design-decision dimensions recur across projects, which co-occurrences structure those decisions, and which typical architectural patterns emerge. Methodologically, we contribute a transparent investigation procedure for analyzing heterogeneous agent-system corpora through source-code and technical-material reading. Empirically, we identify five recurring design dimensions (subagent architecture, context management, tool systems, safety mechanisms, and orchestration) and find that the corpus favors file-persistent, hybrid, and hierarchical context strategies; registry-oriented tool systems remain dominant while MCP- and plugin-oriented extensions are emerging; and intermediate isolation is common but high-assurance audit is rare. Cross-project co-occurrence analysis reveals that deeper coordination pairs with more explicit context services, stronger execution environments with more structured governance, and formalized tool-registration boundaries with broader ecosystem ambitions. We synthesize five recurring architectural patterns spanning lightweight tools, balanced CLI frameworks, multi-agent orchestrators, enterprise systems, and scenario-verticalized projects. The result provides an evidence-based account of architectural regularities in agent-system engineering, with grounded guidance for framework designers, selectors, and researchers.

</details>


### [109] [DSAINet: An Efficient Dual-Scale Attentive Interaction Network for General EEG Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18095)
*Zhiyuan Ma,Zeyuan Li,Zihao Qiu,Jinhao Li,Lingqin Meng,Xinche Zhang,Yixuan Liu,Xinke Shen,Sen Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DSAINet is an efficient dual-scale attentive interaction network for general EEG decoding that models diverse temporal dynamics through parallel convolutional branches at fine and coarse scales, with adaptive attention mechanisms for cross-task generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Specialized EEG decoders have limited generalizability across diverse tasks in subject-independent settings because task-relevant EEG signals follow different temporal patterns, and existing methods rely on task-tailored architectures with specific temporal inductive biases that don't adapt well across tasks.

Method: DSAINet constructs shared spatiotemporal token representations from raw EEG, uses parallel convolutional branches at fine and coarse scales to model diverse temporal dynamics, then refines representations with intra-branch attention (emphasizing scale-specific patterns) and inter-branch attention (integrating cross-scale features), followed by adaptive token aggregation for compact prediction representations.

Result: Extensive experiments on five downstream EEG decoding tasks across ten public datasets show DSAINet consistently outperforms 13 representative baselines under strict subject-independent evaluation, using the same architecture hyperparameters across all datasets, with only ~77K parameters and providing interpretable neurophysiological insights.

Conclusion: DSAINet successfully addresses cross-task EEG decoding challenges by modeling diverse temporal dynamics through dual-scale attentive interactions, achieving superior generalizability, efficiency, and interpretability compared to existing methods while maintaining architectural consistency across diverse tasks.

Abstract: In real-world applications of noninvasive electroencephalography (EEG), specialized decoders often show limited generalizability across diverse tasks under subject-independent settings. One central challenge is that task-relevant EEG signals often follow different temporal organization patterns across tasks, while many existing methods rely on task-tailored architectural designs that introduce task-specific temporal inductive biases. This mismatch makes it difficult to adapt temporal modeling across tasks without changing the model configuration. To address these challenges, we propose DSAINet, an efficient dual-scale attentive interaction network for general EEG decoding. Specifically, DSAINet constructs shared spatiotemporal token representations from raw EEG signals and models diverse temporal dynamics through parallel convolutional branches at fine and coarse scales. The resulting representations are then adaptively refined by intra-branch attention to emphasize salient scale-specific patterns and by inter-branch attention to integrate task-relevant features across scales, followed by adaptive token aggregation to yield a compact representation for prediction. Extensive experiments on five downstream EEG decoding tasks across ten public datasets show that DSAINet consistently outperforms 13 representative baselines under strict subject-independent evaluation. Notably, this performance is achieved using the same architecture hyperparameters across datasets. Moreover, DSAINet achieves a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off with only about 77K trainable parameters and provides interpretable neurophysiological insights. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/zy0929/DSAINet.

</details>


### [110] [Stability Implies Redundancy: Delta Attention Selective Halting for Efficient Long-Context Prefilling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18103)
*Yujie Chen,Tailai Chen,Yifeng Gao,Zoe Wanying He,Yijue Xu,Shaobo Wang,Linfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DASH is a training-free token halting method that monitors self-attention layer dynamics to selectively stop processing stabilized tokens, achieving significant prefill speedups while maintaining accuracy and hardware compatibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prefilling computational costs create a bottleneck for LLMs and LMMs in long-context settings. Existing token pruning methods rely on heuristics that break compatibility with hardware-efficient kernels like FlashAttention.

Method: Delta Attention Selective Halting (DASH) monitors layer-wise update dynamics of self-attention to identify when tokens evolve toward semantic fixing points (where further processing becomes redundant). It selectively halts stabilized tokens without requiring any training.

Result: Extensive evaluation shows DASH generalizes across language and vision benchmarks, delivering significant prefill speedups while preserving model accuracy and maintaining hardware efficiency.

Conclusion: DASH provides an effective training-free solution to reduce prefill computational costs by intelligently halting stabilized tokens based on attention update dynamics, offering compatibility with hardware-optimized kernels and broad applicability to both language and vision tasks.

Abstract: Prefilling computational costs pose a significant bottleneck for Large Language Models (LLMs) and Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) in long-context settings. While token pruning reduces sequence length, prior methods rely on heuristics that break compatibility with hardware-efficient kernels like FlashAttention. In this work, we observe that tokens evolve toward \textit{semantic fixing points}, making further processing redundant. To this end, we introduce Delta Attention Selective Halting (DASH), a training-free policy that monitors the layer-wise update dynamics of the self-attention mechanism to selectively halt stabilized tokens. Extensive evaluation confirms that DASH generalizes across language and vision benchmarks, delivering significant prefill speedups while preserving model accuracy and hardware efficiency. Code will be released at https://github.com/verach3n/DASH.git.

</details>


### [111] [Training LLM Agents for Spontaneous, Reward-Free Self-Evolution via World Knowledge Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18131)
*Qifan Zhang,Dongyang Ma,Tianqing Fang,Jia Li,Jing Tang,Nuo Chen,Haitao Mi,Yan Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agents trained with intrinsic meta-evolution capability can spontaneously learn about unseen environments without external supervision, achieving 20% performance improvements on WebVoyager and WebWalker benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent evolution depends on external human-defined rewards and rules, stopping evolution without human guidance. The paper aims to create agents with intrinsic meta-evolution capability for spontaneous learning in unseen environments.

Method: Designs an outcome-based reward mechanism that measures how well an agent's self-generated world knowledge improves downstream task success. This reward is used during training to teach effective exploration and summarization, but at inference time, the agent requires no external rewards.

Result: The approach yields 20% performance increase on WebVoyager and WebWalker when applied to Qwen3-30B and Seed-OSS-36B. Most notably, a compact 14B Qwen3 model outperforms the unassisted Gemini-2.5-Flash using generated world knowledge.

Conclusion: Establishes a new paradigm for truly evolving agents that can perform native self-evolution to adapt to unknown environments using internal parameters without external supervision.

Abstract: Most agents today ``self-evolve'' by following rewards and rules defined by humans. However, this process remains fundamentally dependent on external supervision; without human guidance, the evolution stops. In this work, we train agents to possess an intrinsic meta-evolution capability to spontaneously learn about unseen environments prior to task execution.
  To instill this ability, we design an outcome-based reward mechanism that measures how much an agent's self-generated world knowledge improves its success rate on downstream tasks. This reward signal is used exclusively during the training phase to teach the model how to explore and summarize effectively. At inference time, the agent requires no external rewards or human instructions. It spontaneously performs native self-evolution to adapt to unknown environments using its internal parameters.
  When applied to Qwen3-30B and Seed-OSS-36B, this shift to native evolution yields a 20% performance increase on WebVoyager and WebWalker. Most strikingly, the generated world knowledge even enables a compact 14B Qwen3 model to outperform the unassisted Gemini-2.5-Flash, establishing a new paradigm for truly evolving agents.

</details>


### [112] [Multi-Agent Systems: From Classical Paradigms to Large Foundation Model-Enabled Futures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18133)
*Zixiang Wang,Mengjia Gong,Qiyu Sun,Jing Xu,Shuai Mao,Xin Jin,Qing-Long Han,Yang Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This survey paper systematically reviews and compares classical multi-agent systems (CMASs) with emerging large foundation model-based multi-agent systems (LMASs), highlighting how LMASs enable semantic-level reasoning and improved adaptability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With the rapid advancement of AI and the emergence of large foundation models (LFMs), multi-agent systems are evolving from classical paradigms to more sophisticated architectures that leverage semantic reasoning capabilities.

Method: The paper uses a systematic review and comparative analysis approach, first examining CMASs within a closed-loop coordination framework across four dimensions: perception, communication, decision-making, and control. Then it analyzes how LMASs integrate LFMs to enable higher-level collaboration.

Result: The analysis contrasts CMASs and LMASs across architecture, operating mechanism, adaptability, and applications, showing how LMASs enable semantic-level reasoning and more flexible coordination across diverse scenarios.

Conclusion: The survey identifies future research directions and open challenges in multi-agent systems, particularly highlighting how LMASs represent a significant evolution from classical paradigms by leveraging large foundation models for improved semantic reasoning and adaptability.

Abstract: With the rapid advancement of artificial intelligence, multi-agent systems (MASs) are evolving from classical paradigms toward architectures built upon large foundation models (LFMs). This survey provides a systematic review and comparative analysis of classical MASs (CMASs) and LFM-based MASs (LMASs). First, within a closed-loop coordination framework, CMASs are reviewed across four fundamental dimensions: perception, communication, decision-making, and control. Beyond this framework, LMASs integrate LFMs to lift collaboration from low-level state exchanges to semantic-level reasoning, enabling more flexible coordination and improved adaptability across diverse scenarios. Then, a comparative analysis is conducted to contrast CMASs and LMASs across architecture, operating mechanism, adaptability, and application. Finally, future perspectives on MASs are presented, summarizing open challenges and potential research opportunities.

</details>


### [113] [State Transfer Reveals Reuse in Controlled Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18158)
*Yanzhen Lu,Zhicheng Qian,Muchen Jiang,Xingyu Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper demonstrates that fixed-interface transfer is stronger evidence of model behavior reuse than trained prompt success alone, showing different transfer patterns in controlled routing tasks across various models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While prompt-based interventions can change model behavior, trained success alone doesn't identify where behaviorally relevant state is represented in models. The paper aims to distinguish between fixed-interface reuse versus prompt relocation in controlled settings.

Method: The study uses controlled routing tasks with interfaces chosen on support data, held-out query evaluation, and matched necessity, sufficiency, and wrong-interface controls. Tests are conducted on GPT-2 triop, GPT-2 add/sub, and Qwen routing models across different architectures.

Result: On GPT-2 triop, an early interface supports exact transfer; on GPT-2 add/sub, zero-retrain compiled transfer at fixed interface recovers most donor routing accuracy, while trainable prompt slots need additional examples to relearn behavior. Qwen routing shows cross-architecture consistency at operator token but unresolved donor-specific identity.

Conclusion: Fixed-interface transfer provides stronger evidence of behavioral reuse than trained prompt success alone, especially in controlled routing tasks where interfaces can be tested directly and show more reliable transfer patterns.

Abstract: Prompt-based interventions can change model behavior, but trained success alone does not identify where the behaviorally relevant state is represented. We study this question in controlled routing tasks using interfaces chosen on support data, held-out query evaluation, and matched necessity, sufficiency, and wrong-interface controls. On GPT-2 triop, an early interface supports exact transfer under these tests. On GPT-2 add/sub, zero-retrain compiled transfer at the fixed interface recovers most of donor routing accuracy, while trainable prompt slots can relearn the same behavior at several other positions only after additional support examples and optimization. These results distinguish fixed-interface reuse from prompt relocation in a setting where the two can be tested directly. Qwen routing provides a cross-architecture consistency check for the same matched-interface pattern at the operator token, although donor-specific identity on the local V-path remains unresolved. Generation and reasoning branches are used to map scope: they show broader transport or weaker controller identifiability once control depends on longer trajectories or harder selection. In controlled routing, fixed-interface transfer is therefore stronger evidence of reuse than trained prompt success alone.

</details>


### [114] [QuantumQA: Enhancing Scientific Reasoning via Physics-Consistent Dataset and Verification-Aware Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18176)
*Songxin Qu,Tai-Ping Sun,Yun-Jie Wang,Huan-Yu Liu,Cheng Xue,Xiao-Fan Xu,Han Fang,Yang Yang,Yu-Chun Wu,Guo-Ping Guo,Zhao-Yun Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces QuantumQA, a verified quantum mechanics dataset, and RLVR with VRM to enhance LLMs' reliability in scientific domains using verifiable feedback instead of pure model scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs perform well in general reasoning but lack reliability in scientific domains like quantum mechanics due to scarce verifiable training data and inadequate feedback signals in standard alignment methods.

Method: 1) Created QuantumQA dataset using task-adaptive strategy and hybrid verification (deterministic solvers + semantic auditing). 2) Proposed VRM for RLVR with ARF mechanism to dynamically integrate deterministic signals from SES with semantic evaluations for precise supervision.

Result: The method consistently outperforms baselines and general-purpose preference models. Optimized 8B model achieves performance competitive with proprietary models, showing verifiable, rule-based feedback offers parameter-efficient alternative to pure scaling.

Conclusion: Incorporating verifiable, rule-based feedback into RL loop provides parameter-efficient enhancement for LLMs in scientific domains, addressing reliability issues without requiring massive model scaling.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) show strong capabilities in general reasoning but typically lack reliability in scientific domains like quantum mechanics, which demand strict adherence to physical constraints. This limitation arises from the scarcity of verifiable training resources and the inadequacy of coarse feedback signals in standard alignment paradigms. To address the data challenge, we introduce QuantumQA, a large-scale dataset constructed via a task-adaptive strategy and a hybrid verification protocol that combines deterministic solvers with semantic auditing to guarantee scientific rigor. Building on this foundation, we propose the verification-aware reward model (VRM) tailored for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), which employs an adaptive reward fusion (ARF) mechanism to dynamically integrate deterministic signals from a scientific execution suite (SES) with multidimensional semantic evaluations for precise supervision. Experimental results demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms baselines and general-purpose preference models. Notably, our optimized 8B model achieves performance competitive with proprietary models, validating that incorporating verifiable, rule-based feedback into the reinforcement learning loop offers a parameter-efficient alternative to pure scaling.

</details>


### [115] [A Control Architecture for Training-Free Memory Use](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18206)
*Yanzhen Lu,Muchen Jiang,Zhicheng Qian,Xingyu Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Training-free memory injection system that controls when and how to use memory for improved reasoning through uncertainty routing, confidence filtering, and memory governance


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prompt-injected memory can enhance reasoning without weight updates, but creates a control problem: retrieved content only helps when applied in the right state. Need to address applicability control for memory-assisted reasoning.

Method: Combines uncertainty-based routing (when to trigger memory-assisted second pass), confidence-based selective acceptance (when to trust memory), bank selection across rule and exemplar memory, and evidence-based governance of memory bank over time.

Result: Improves arithmetic benchmarks by +7.0 points on SVAMP and +7.67 points on ASDiv over baseline under locked training-free protocol. Also shows positive transfer to QA and agent benchmarks, with control architecture (not raw memory exposure) driving improvements.

Conclusion: The control architecture is crucial for memory-augmented reasoning, with confidence filtering separating helpful from harmful interventions and evidence-based governance enabling effective memory bank management.

Abstract: Prompt-injected memory can improve reasoning without updating model weights, but it also creates a control problem: retrieved content helps only when it is applied in the right state. We study this problem in a strict training-free setting and formulate it as applicability control: when to trigger a memory-assisted second pass, when to trust it, and how to maintain the memory bank over time. Our method combines uncertainty-based routing, confidence-based selective acceptance, bank selection across rule and exemplar memory, and evidence-based governance of the memory bank over time. Under a locked training-free protocol with compute-matched controls, it improves two core arithmetic benchmarks by +7.0 points on SVAMP and +7.67 points on ASDiv over baseline. The same architecture also transfers to QA and agent benchmarks with smaller positive effects and shows the same positive direction on a second checkpoint for the main arithmetic tasks. On arithmetic, the main empirical pattern is that the control architecture, rather than raw memory exposure, drives the improvements on SVAMP and ASDiv. Mechanistically, confidence separates helpful from harmful rule-bank interventions, and under fixed retrieval the repair-versus-corrupt difference localizes to rows whose retrieved set actually contains the edited entries.

</details>


### [116] [AJ-Bench: Benchmarking Agent-as-a-Judge for Environment-Aware Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18240)
*Wentao Shi,Yu Wang,Yuyang Zhao,Yuxin Chen,Fuli Feng,Xueyuan Hao,Xi Su,Qi Gu,Hui Su,Xunliang Cai,Xiangnan He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AJ-Bench: A benchmark for evaluating Agent-as-a-Judge systems in complex environments, showing performance gains over LLM-as-a-Judge but revealing substantial open challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches (rule-based verifiers and LLM-as-a-Judge) struggle to generalize beyond narrow domains when verifying reinforcement learning agent behaviors in complex environments. Agent-as-a-Judge has potential but remains underexplored.

Method: Introduces AJ-Bench, a systematic benchmark across three domains (search, data systems, and graphical user interfaces) comprising 155 tasks and 516 annotated trajectories. The benchmark assesses judge agents' abilities in information acquisition, state verification, and process verification.

Result: Experiments demonstrate consistent performance gains over LLM-as-a-Judge baselines. The benchmark reveals substantial open challenges in agent-based verification despite improvements.

Conclusion: Agent-as-a-Judge shows promise for verifying complex agent behaviors but faces significant challenges. The AJ-Bench provides a foundation for systematic evaluation and future research in this emerging area.

Abstract: As reinforcement learning continues to scale the training of large language model-based agents, reliably verifying agent behaviors in complex environments has become increasingly challenging. Existing approaches rely on rule-based verifiers or LLM-as-a-Judge models, which struggle to generalize beyond narrow domains. Agent-as-a-Judge addresses this limitation by actively interacting with environments and tools to acquire verifiable evidence, yet its capabilities remain underexplored.
  We introduce a benchmark AJ-Bench to systematically evaluate Agent-as-a-Judge across three domains-search, data systems, and graphical user interfaces-comprising 155 tasks and 516 annotated trajectories. The benchmark comprehensively assesses judge agents' abilities in information acquisition, state verification, and process verification. Experiments demonstrate consistent performance gains over LLM-as-a-Judge baselines, while also revealing substantial open challenges in agent-based verification. Our data and code are available at https://aj-bench.github.io/.

</details>


### [117] [LeGo-Code: Can Modular Curriculum Learning Advance Complex Code Generation? Insights from Text-to-SQL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18254)
*Salmane Chafik,Saad Ezzini,Ismail Berrada*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Curriculum learning with modular adapter composition improves Text-to-SQL performance for complex queries by preventing catastrophic forgetting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State-of-the-art code LLMs struggle with highly complex logic in Text-to-SQL tasks, particularly with deeply nested statements and real-world noisy database schemas. The paper investigates whether curriculum learning can address these limitations.

Method: Proposes Modular Adapter Composition (MAC) strategy: sequentially trains tier-specific adapters on incremental complexity levels (Easy to Extra-Hard), creating a scaffolded learning environment. This prevents catastrophic forgetting compared to naive curriculum approaches that simply order training samples by complexity in a single epoch.

Result: MAC produces measurable performance gains on Spider and BIRD benchmarks, providing a flexible "Lego-like" architecture that allows models to be composed and deployed based on specific schema difficulty requirements.

Conclusion: Structured, modular learning with adapter composition is a superior alternative to monolithic fine-tuning for mastering complex code generation syntax and logic, particularly for Text-to-SQL tasks.

Abstract: Recently, code-oriented large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in translating natural language into executable code. Text-to-SQL is a significant application of this ability, enabling non-technical users to interact with relational databases using natural language. However, state-of-the-art models continue to struggle with highly complex logic, particularly deeply nested statements involving multiple joins and conditions, as well as with real-world database schemas that are noisy or poorly structured. In this paper, we investigate whether curriculum learning can improve the performance of code-based LLMs on Text-to-SQL tasks. Employing benchmarks including Spider and BIRD, we fine-tune models under different curriculum strategies. Our experiments show that naive curriculum, simply ordering training samples by complexity in a single epoch, fails to surpass standard fine-tuning due to catastrophic forgetting. To overcome this, we propose a Modular Adapter Composition (MAC) strategy. By sequentially training tier-specific adapters on incremental complexity levels (Easy to Extra-Hard), we create a scaffolded learning environment that improves performance on complex queries. Our approach not only produces measurable performance gains on the Spider and BIRD benchmarks but also provides a flexible, "Lego-like" architecture, allowing models to be composed and deployed based on specific schema difficulty requirements. These findings demonstrate that structured, modular learning is a superior alternative to monolithic fine-tuning for mastering the syntax and logic of complex code generation.

</details>


### [118] [Enhancing Tabular Anomaly Detection via Pseudo-Label-Guided Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18266)
*Wei Huang,Yuxuan Xiong,Hezhe Qiao,Yu-Ming Shang,Xiangling Fu,Guansong Pang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PLAG: A pseudo-label-guided anomaly generation method that enhances tabular anomaly detection by generating synthetic anomalies using pseudo-labels and feature-level abnormality accumulation, integrated with unsupervised detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing unsupervised anomaly detection methods lack anomaly awareness, while current generation and contrastive approaches overlook localized anomaly patterns in tabular data, leading to suboptimal detection performance. There's a need for methods that don't require scarce ground-truth labels while capturing fine-grained anomalous signals.

Method: PLAG uses pseudo-anomalies as guidance signals and decouples overall anomaly quantification into feature-level abnormalities accumulation. It employs a two-stage data selection strategy with format verification and uncertainty estimation to filter synthetic anomalies, ensuring fidelity and diversity.

Result: Extensive experiments show PLAG achieves state-of-the-art performance against eight baselines. As a flexible framework, it integrates seamlessly with existing unsupervised detectors, consistently boosting F1-scores by 0.08 to 0.21.

Conclusion: PLAG provides an effective solution for tabular anomaly detection that doesn't require ground-truth labels, captures localized anomaly patterns, and can enhance existing unsupervised detectors, demonstrating superior performance and practical flexibility.

Abstract: Identifying anomalous instances in tabular data is essential for improving data reliability and maintaining system stability. Due to the scarcity of ground-truth anomaly labels, existing methods mainly rely on unsupervised anomaly detection models, or exploit a small number of labeled anomalies to facilitate detection via sample generation or contrastive learning. However, unsupervised methods lack sufficient anomaly awareness, while current generation and contrastive approaches tend to compute anomalies globally, overlooking the localized anomaly patterns of tabular features, resulting in suboptimal detection performance. To address these limitations, we propose PLAG, a pseudo-label-guided anomaly generation method designed to enhance tabular anomaly detection. Specifically, by utilizing pseudo-anomalies as guidance signals and decoupling the overall anomaly quantification of a sample into an accumulation of feature-level abnormalities, PLAG not only effectively obviates the need for scarce ground-truth labels but also provides a novel perspective for the model to comprehend localized anomalous signals at a fine-grained level. Furthermore, a two-stage data selection strategy is proposed, integrating format verification and uncertainty estimation to rigorously filter candidate samples, thereby ensuring the fidelity and diversity of the synthetic anomalies. Ultimately, these filtered synthetic anomalies serve as robust discriminative guidance, empowering the model to better separate normal and anomalous instances. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PLAG achieves state-of-the-art performance against eight representative baselines. Moreover, as a flexible framework, it integrates seamlessly with existing unsupervised detectors, consistently boosting F1-scores by 0.08 to 0.21.

</details>


### [119] [Agent-World: Scaling Real-World Environment Synthesis for Evolving General Agent Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18292)
*Guanting Dong,Junting Lu,Junjie Huang,Wanjun Zhong,Longxiang Liu,Shijue Huang,Zhenyu Li,Yang Zhao,Xiaoshuai Song,Xiaoxi Li,Jiajie Jin,Yutao Zhu,Hanbin Wang,Fangyu Lei,Qinyu Luo,Mingyang Chen,Zehui Chen,Jiazhan Feng,Ji-Rong Wen,Zhicheng Dou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agent-World is a self-evolving training system that combines automated environment-task discovery with continuous agent training to advance general AI agents through scalable, realistic environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current training of language models as general-purpose agents is limited by lack of realistic environments and principled mechanisms for lifelong learning, despite frameworks like Model Context Protocol providing unified interfaces to real-world services.

Method: Two main components: (1) Agentic Environment-Task Discovery that autonomously explores databases and tool ecosystems to synthesize verifiable tasks with controllable difficulty; (2) Continuous Self-Evolving Agent Training combining multi-environment reinforcement learning with a self-evolving arena that identifies capability gaps through dynamic task synthesis.

Result: Agent-World-8B and 14B models consistently outperform strong proprietary models and environment scaling baselines across 23 challenging agent benchmarks, with analyses showing scaling trends related to environment diversity and self-evolution rounds.

Conclusion: Agent-World provides an effective framework for advancing general agent intelligence through scalable, self-evolving environments, offering insights into the relationship between environment diversity, self-evolution cycles, and agent capability development.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly expected to serve as general-purpose agents that interact with external, stateful tool environments. The Model Context Protocol (MCP) and broader agent skills offer a unified interface for connecting agents with scalable real-world services, but training robust agents remains limited by the lack of realistic environments and principled mechanisms for life-long learning. In this paper, we present \textbf{Agent-World}, a self-evolving training arena for advancing general agent intelligence through scalable environments. Agent-World has two main components: (1) Agentic Environment-Task Discovery, which autonomously explores topic-aligned databases and executable tool ecosystems from thousands of real-world environment themes and synthesizes verifiable tasks with controllable difficulty; and (2) Continuous Self-Evolving Agent Training, which combines multi-environment reinforcement learning with a self-evolving agent arena that automatically identifies capability gaps through dynamic task synthesis and drives targeted learning, enabling the co-evolution of agent policies and environments. Across 23 challenging agent benchmarks, Agent-World-8B and 14B consistently outperforms strong proprietary models and environment scaling baselines. Further analyses reveal scaling trends in relation to environment diversity and self-evolution rounds, offering insights for building general agent intelligence.

</details>


### [120] [Toward Zero-Egress Psychiatric AI: On-Device LLM Deployment for Privacy-Preserving Mental Health Decision Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18302)
*Eranga Bandara,Asanga Gunaratna,Ross Gore,Anita H. Clayton,Christopher K. Rhea,Sachini Rajapakse,Isurunima Kularathna,Sachin Shetty,Ravi Mukkamala,Xueping Liang,Preston Samuel,Atmaram Yarlagadda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a zero-egress, on-device AI platform for privacy-preserving psychiatric decision support using lightweight LLMs on mobile devices, eliminating cloud data transmission while maintaining diagnostic accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Privacy concerns are a major barrier to AI adoption in mental healthcare, especially in sensitive settings like military, correctional, and remote healthcare where cloud-based systems require sensitive patient data to leave devices, creating unacceptable privacy/security risks that deter help-seeking behavior.

Method: Developed a cross-platform mobile application with fully local AI execution. Used a consortium of three lightweight, fine-tuned, quantized open-source LLMs (Gemma, Phi-3.5-mini, Qwen2) selected for mobile efficiency. An on-device orchestration layer coordinates ensemble inference and consensus-based diagnostic reasoning to produce DSM-5-aligned assessments.

Result: The zero-egress deployment achieves diagnostic accuracy comparable to the server-side predecessor while sustaining real-time inference latency on commodity mobile hardware. The platform supports both clinician differential diagnosis/evidence-linked symptom mapping and patient-facing self-screening with clinical safeguards.

Conclusion: On-device AI platforms with zero data egress can address critical privacy barriers in sensitive mental healthcare settings without compromising diagnostic accuracy, enabling AI adoption in environments where cloud-based solutions are unacceptable.

Abstract: Privacy represents one of the most critical yet underaddressed barriers to AI adoption in mental healthcare -- particularly in high-sensitivity operational environments such as military, correctional, and remote healthcare settings, where the risk of patient data exposure can deter help-seeking behavior entirely. Existing AI-enabled psychiatric decision support systems predominantly rely on cloud-based inference pipelines, requiring sensitive patient data to leave the device and traverse external servers, creating unacceptable privacy and security risks in these contexts. In this paper, we propose a zero-egress, on-device AI platform for privacy-preserving psychiatric decision support, deployed as a cross-platform mobile application. The proposed system extends our prior work on fine-tuned LLM consortiums for psychiatric diagnosis standardization by fundamentally re-architecting the inference pipeline for fully local execution -- ensuring that no patient data is transmitted to, processed by, or stored on any external server at any stage. The platform integrates a consortium of three lightweight, fine-tuned, and quantized open-source LLMs -- Gemma, Phi-3.5-mini, and Qwen2 -- selected for their compact architectures and proven efficiency on resource-constrained mobile hardware. An on-device orchestration layer coordinates ensemble inference and consensus-based diagnostic reasoning, producing DSM-5-aligned assessments for conditions. The platform is designed to assist clinicians with differential diagnosis and evidence-linked symptom mapping, as well as to support patient-facing self-screening with appropriate clinical safeguards. Initial evaluation demonstrates that the proposed zero-egress deployment achieves diagnostic accuracy comparable to its server-side predecessor while sustaining real-time inference latency on commodity mobile hardware.

</details>


### [121] [PARM: Pipeline-Adapted Reward Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18327)
*Xingyu Fan,Wei Shao,Jiacheng Liu,Linqi Song,Pheng Ann Heng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PARM (Pipeline-Adapted Reward Model) addresses reward inconsistency in multi-stage LLM pipelines by aligning reward models with downstream execution feedback through direct preference optimization, improving code generation for optimization problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While reward models are crucial for aligning LLMs with human preferences, most work focuses on single-step generation. Real-world applications increasingly use multi-stage LLM pipelines, but effective reward guidance in such pipelines remains underexplored. The authors identify a critical challenge: inconsistency between reward model predictions and actual pipeline execution outcomes.

Method: Propose PARM (Pipeline-Adapted Reward Model) that leverages pipeline-specific data and direct preference optimization to align rewards with downstream feedback. Instantiate PARM as a two-stage pipeline (formulation -> code generation) for code generation in combinatorial optimization. Evaluate on four public optimization benchmarks measuring execution rate and solving accuracy against baselines and sampling methods, with a supplementary cross-domain experiment on GSM8K to assess transferability.

Result: PARM consistently improves pipeline output quality and stability compared to baselines and sampling methods. The approach demonstrates effectiveness in multi-stage code generation for combinatorial optimization problems and shows transferability potential in cross-domain experiments on GSM8K.

Conclusion: PARM successfully addresses reward inconsistency in multi-stage LLM pipelines by aligning reward models with downstream execution feedback. The work provides new insights into reward modeling for multi-stage LLM reasoning, demonstrating practical improvements in pipeline output quality and stability for complex tasks like code generation for optimization problems.

Abstract: Reward models (RMs) are central to aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences, powering RLHF and advanced decoding strategies. While most prior work focuses on single-step generation, real-world applications increasingly adopt multi-stage LLM pipelines, where effective reward guidance remains underexplored. We investigate this through code generation for combinatorial optimization, constructing a pipeline that integrates reward models into both formulation and solution stages. We identify a critical challenge: inconsistency between reward model predictions and actual pipeline execution outcomes. To address this, we propose the Pipeline-Adapted Reward Model (PARM), which leverages pipeline-specific data and direct preference optimization to align rewards with downstream feedback. We instantiate PARM as a two-stage pipeline (formulation -> code generation) and evaluate it on four public optimization benchmarks, measuring execution rate and solving accuracy against baselines and sampling methods. A supplementary cross-domain experiment on GSM8K assesses transferability. Results demonstrate that PARM consistently improves pipeline output quality and stability, providing new insights into reward modeling for multi-stage LLM reasoning.

</details>


### [122] [One Pass for All: A Discrete Diffusion Model for Knowledge Graph Triple Set Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18344)
*Jihong Guan,Jiaqi Wang,Wengen Li,Hanchen Yang,Yichao Zhang,Shuigeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DiffTSP: A discrete diffusion model for Triple Set Prediction that generates missing triples in one pass while capturing dependencies between predicted triples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional KGC tasks assume partial information about missing triples, but the more realistic Triple Set Prediction (TSP) task aims to infer missing triples given only the observed KG. Existing TSP methods predict triples one by one, failing to capture dependencies among predicted triples for consistency.

Method: Proposes DiffTSP, a novel discrete diffusion model that treats TSP as a generative task. It adds noise to the KG via discrete diffusion by masking relational edges, then gradually recovers the complete KG conditioned on the incomplete graph. Uses a structure-aware denoising network with relational context encoder and relational graph diffusion transformer for KG generation.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on three public datasets. Can generate complete sets of triples in one pass while ensuring dependencies among predicted triples.

Conclusion: DiffTSP effectively addresses limitations of existing TSP methods by capturing dependencies among predicted triples through a generative diffusion approach, making it suitable for realistic KG completion where no partial information about missing triples is assumed.

Abstract: Knowledge Graphs (KGs) are composed of triples, and the goal of Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC) is to infer the missing factual triples. Traditional KGC tasks predict missing elements in a triple given one or two of its elements. As a more realistic task, the Triple Set Prediction (TSP) task aims to infer the set of missing triples conditioned only on the observed knowledge graph, without assuming any partial information about the missing triples. Existing TSP methods predict the set of missing triples in a triple-by-triple manner, falling short in capturing the dependencies among the predicted triples to ensure consistency. To address this issue, we propose a novel discrete diffusion model termed DiffTSP that treats TSP as a generative task. DiffTSP progressively adds noise to the KG through a discrete diffusion process, achieved by masking relational edges. The reverse process then gradually recovers the complete KG conditioned on the incomplete graph. To this end, we design a structure-aware denoising network that integrates a relational context encoder with a relational graph diffusion transformer for knowledge graph generation. DiffTSP can generate the complete set of triples in a one-pass manner while ensuring the dependencies among the predicted triples. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on three public datasets. Code: https://github.com/ADMIS-TONGJI/DiffTSP.

</details>


### [123] [The implicated scientist: on the role of AI researchers in the development of weapons systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18380)
*Alexandra Volokhova,Alex Hernandez-Garcia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI researchers are implicated in harms caused by AI-enabled weapons systems through their professional contributions, requiring transformation of this implication into solidarity with victims of AI-fortified injustices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI technologies are increasingly used in weapons systems involved in mass killings, with an ongoing AI arms race that risks more deaths, devastation, and inequality. The paper addresses the ethical responsibility of AI researchers as implicated subjects in these harms.

Method: The paper investigates and discusses the specifics of AI researchers' implication in weapons-related harms, exploring ways to transform this position of implication through conceptual and ethical analysis.

Result: The paper identifies AI researchers as implicated subjects in the harms caused by AI-enabled weapons and proposes approaches to transfigure this implication into long-distance solidarity with victims of technologically fortified injustices.

Conclusion: AI researchers have ethical responsibilities as implicated subjects in AI weapons harms, and should transform their professional position into forms of differentiated solidarity with victims rather than maintaining complicity through technical contributions alone.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) technologies are increasingly used in modern weapons systems. Notably, these systems have recently been involved in mass killings and destruction at scale. Furthermore, there is currently a strong interest and competition among powerful players to accelerate the proliferation of weapons with automated or AI-based components, a phenomenon known as AI arms race. This competition poses a risk of causing even more deaths and devastation in the future, as well as increased power and wealth inequality. In this work, we aim to shed light on the role of AI researchers as implicated subjects in the harms caused by weapons enabled by AI technologies. We investigate and discuss the specifics of this implication and explore ways to transfigure this position of implication into one of differentiated, long-distance solidarity with the victims of technologically fortified injustices.

</details>


### [124] [Learning from Less: Measuring the Effectiveness of RLVR in Low Data and Compute Regimes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18381)
*Justin Bauer,Thomas Walshe,Derek Pham,Harit Vishwakarma,Armin Parchami,Frederic Sala,Paroma Varma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Small Language Models can be effectively fine-tuned with Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards in low-data settings using procedural datasets, achieving up to 5x sample efficiency when trained on mixed-complexity tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous RLVR work requires large annotated datasets and compute resources, which are scarce in real-world settings. There's a need to understand how Small Language Models perform with RLVR when data is limited.

Method: Conducted empirical study using RLVR on open-source SLMs with three novel procedural datasets (number counting, graph reasoning, spatial reasoning). Analyzed performance scaling with dataset size, diversity, and complexity.

Result: (1) Procedural datasets enable controlled dataset development, (2) Models trained on lower complexity tasks generalize to higher complexity ones, (3) Mixed-complexity datasets provide up to 5x sample efficiency vs. easy-only tasks in low-data regimes.

Conclusion: RLVR with procedural data generators can enable efficient fine-tuning of Small Language Models in resource-constrained settings, inspiring future work on data scaling laws and effective dataset development.

Abstract: Fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs) typically relies on large quantities of high-quality annotated data, or questions with well-defined ground truth answers in the case of Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). While previous work has explored the benefits to model reasoning capabilities by scaling both data and compute used for RLVR, these results lack applicability in many real-world settings where annotated data and accessible compute may be scarce. In this work, we present a comprehensive empirical study of open-source Small Language Model (SLM) performance after RLVR in low data regimes. Across three novel datasets covering number counting problems, graph reasoning, and spatial reasoning, we characterize how model performance scales with dataset size, diversity, and complexity. We demonstrate that (1) procedural datasets allow for fine-grained evaluation and training dataset development with controllable properties (size, diversity, and complexity), (2) under RLVR, models trained on lower complexity tasks can generalize to higher complexity tasks, and (3) training on mixed complexity datasets is associated with the greatest benefits in low data regimes, providing up to 5x sample efficiency versus training on easy tasks. These findings inspire future work on the development of data scaling laws for RLVR and the use of procedural data generators to further understand effective data development for efficient LLM fine-tuning.

</details>


### [125] [Six Llamas: Comparative Religious Ethics Through LoRA-Adapted Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18404)
*Chad Coleman,W. Russell Neuman,Manan Shah,Ali Dasdan,Matthew Crispi,Morris Chiang,Zack Leitman,Mustafa Poonawala*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents "Six Llamas," a study showing that LLMs fine-tuned on different religious texts develop systematically different ethical reasoning patterns aligned with their respective religious traditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To examine whether large language models fine-tuned on distinct religious corpora encode systematically different patterns of ethical reasoning, serving as a proof-of-concept for using differentially trained language models as instruments for cultural and ethical analysis.

Method: Created six Llama-3.1-8B variants: one unmodified control and five LoRA-adapted models trained on sacred texts of Christianity, Islam, Judaism, Hinduism, or Buddhism. Tested all models with 17 standardized ethical prompts across moral dilemmas, game theory, public policy, and self-assessments using multi-temperature sampling (10 settings). Computed response consistency, pairwise agreement rates, temperature sensitivity, and run-to-run stability analyses.

Result: LoRA-adapted models produce ethical reasoning patterns that: (a) differ systematically from the base model; (b) align with their training traditions' moral logics; (c) show interpretable dimensions in moral-philosophical space; (d) maintain stable core positions across temperatures for high-consensus dilemmas (Trolley Problem achieved 100% consistency); (e) tradition-specific divergence intensifies at higher temperatures in contested domains; (f) base model shows highest overall response consistency (88.3%).

Conclusion: The study provides a proof-of-concept for the "condensate comparative method" using differentially trained language models for cultural and ethical analysis, identifying specific criteria for falsification and future extensions, demonstrating that religious fine-tuning systematically shapes ethical reasoning in predictable ways.

Abstract: We present Six Llamas, a comparative study examining whether large language models fine-tuned on distinct religious corpora encode systematically different patterns of ethical reasoning. Six variants of Meta-Llama-3.1-8B are constructed: one unmodified control and five LoRA-adapted models trained exclusively on the sacred and theological texts of Christianity, Islam, Judaism, Hinduism, or Buddhism. All six models are probed with an identical battery of 17 standardized ethical prompts spanning moral dilemmas, game-theoretic scenarios, public policy questions, and moral-psychological self-assessments. To assess robustness and reproducibility, we implement a multi-temperature sampling design spanning ten temperature settings. We compute response consistency metrics, pairwise inter-model agreement rates, temperature sensitivity coefficients across four prompt domains, and run-to-run stability analyses.
  Findings show that LoRA-adapted models produce ethical reasoning patterns that are (a) systematically differentiated from the base model, (b) consistent with the moral logics of their training traditions, (c) structured along interpretable dimensions in moral-philosophical space, (d) core ethical positions remain stable across temperature variations for high-consensus dilemmas. The Trolley Problem achieves 100% consistency across all models and temperatures, while (e) tradition-specific divergence intensifies at higher temperatures in morally contested domains, and (f) the base model exhibits the highest overall response consistency (mean 88.3%), suggesting LoRA adaptation introduces both tradition-specific signal and increased sampling sensitivity.
  The study offers a proof-of-concept for the condensate comparative method using differentially trained language models as instruments for cultural and ethical analysis and identifies specific criteria for falsification and planned extensions.

</details>


### [126] [Using large language models for embodied planning introduces systematic safety risks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18463)
*Tao Zhang,Kaixian Qu,Zhibin Li,Jiajun Wu,Marco Hutter,Manling Li,Fan Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Large language models have strong planning capabilities for robotics but dangerous safety gaps, with safety awareness not improving with model scale while reasoning models show better safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used as planners for robotic systems, there's a critical need to systematically evaluate how safely they plan, since planning ability alone doesn't ensure safety.

Method: Introduced DESPITE benchmark with 12,279 tasks spanning physical and normative dangers, tested 23 models (18 open-source from 3B to 671B parameters and proprietary models), analyzed planning ability vs safety awareness relationship.

Result: Best planning model fails on only 0.4% of tasks but produces dangerous plans on 28.3%; safety awareness remains flat (38-57%) across open-source model scales while planning ability improves dramatically (0.4-99.3%); reasoning models achieve higher safety awareness (71-81%).

Conclusion: Safety awareness is the key bottleneck for LLM-based robotic planners, requiring separate attention from planning ability improvement, especially as planning ability approaches saturation in frontier models.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used as planners for robotic systems, yet how safely they plan remains an open question. To evaluate safe planning systematically, we introduce DESPITE, a benchmark of 12,279 tasks spanning physical and normative dangers with fully deterministic validation. Across 23 models, even near-perfect planning ability does not ensure safety: the best-planning model fails to produce a valid plan on only 0.4% of tasks but produces dangerous plans on 28.3%. Among 18 open-source models from 3B to 671B parameters, planning ability improves substantially with scale (0.4-99.3%) while safety awareness remains relatively flat (38-57%). We identify a multiplicative relationship between these two capacities, showing that larger models complete more tasks safely primarily through improved planning, not through better danger avoidance. Three proprietary reasoning models reach notably higher safety awareness (71-81%), while non-reasoning proprietary models and open-source reasoning models remain below 57%. As planning ability approaches saturation for frontier models, improving safety awareness becomes a central challenge for deploying language-model planners in robotic systems.

</details>


### [127] [A Generalized Synthetic Control Method for Baseline Estimation in Demand Response Services](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18469)
*Jonas Sievers,Mardavij Roozbehani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Generalized Synthetic Control Method improves electricity demand response baseline estimation by transforming it into a dynamic counterfactual prediction problem with enriched donor representations and temporal features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing machine learning methods for Demand Response (DR) settlement baseline estimation have limited predictive performance, and causal inference/counterfactual prediction methodologies from econometrics are underutilized in this domain. The classical Synthetic Control Method (SCM) is powerful but static, ignoring predictable temporal structure in residual errors.

Method: Develops a generalized SCM framework that transforms baseline estimation into dynamic counterfactual prediction by augmenting donor representation with: 1) exogenous features, 2) lagged treated load, and 3) selected lagged donor signals.
