This captures autoregressive dependence, delayed donor-response patterns, and error-correction effects. The framework also accommodates nonlinear predictors when linear weighting is inadequate.

Result: Experiments on the Ausgrid smart-meter dataset show consistent improvements over classical SCM and strong benchmark methods. The dominant performance gains are driven by the dynamic augmentation of the donor representation.

Conclusion: The proposed Generalized Synthetic Control Method provides a more effective framework for DR baseline estimation by addressing the limitations of static SCM through dynamic counterfactual prediction with enriched feature representations, leading to significant performance improvements in practical applications.

Abstract: Baseline estimation is critical to Demand Response (DR) settlement in electricity markets, yet existing machine learning methods remain limited in predictive performance, while methodologies from causal inference and counterfactual prediction are still underutilized in this domain. We introduce a Generalized Synthetic Control Method that builds on the classical Synthetic Control Method (SCM) from econometrics. While SCM provides a powerful framework for counterfactual estimation, classical SCM remains a static estimator: it fits the treated unit as a combination of contemporaneous donor units and therefore ignores predictable temporal structure in the residual error. We develop a generalized SCM framework that transforms baseline estimation into a dynamic counterfactual prediction problem by augmenting the donor representation with exogenous features, lagged treated load, and selected lagged donor signals. This enriched representation allows the estimator to capture autoregressive dependence, delayed donor-response patterns, and error-correction effects beyond the scope of standard SCM. The framework further accommodates nonlinear predictors when linear weighting is inadequate, with the greatest benefit arising in limited-data settings. Experiments on the Ausgrid smart-meter dataset show consistent improvements over classical SCM and strong benchmark methods, with the dominant performance gains driven by dynamic augmentation.

</details>


### [128] [WorldDB: A Vector Graph-of-Worlds Memory Engine with Ontology-Aware Write-Time Reconciliation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18478)
*Harish Santhanalakshmi Ganesan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: WorldDB is a memory engine for AI agents that uses hierarchical, content-addressed graphs with programmatic edges to solve persistent memory problems, achieving state-of-the-art performance on conversational memory benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current memory systems for AI agents (RAG over flat vector stores and bitemporal knowledge graphs) have limitations: they fragment facts into chunks, lose cross-session identity, lack supersession/contradiction handling, and have flat graph structures without recursive composition or content addressing.

Method: WorldDB is built on three core principles: (1) every node is a "world" - a hierarchical container with its own subgraph and ontology, (2) nodes are content-addressed and immutable (Merkle-style audit trail), (3) edges are write-time programs with handlers for insertion, deletion, and query rewriting to handle supersession, contradiction, and merging.

Result: On the LongMemEval-s benchmark (500 questions, ~115k token conversations), WorldDB with Claude Opus 4.7 achieved 96.40% overall accuracy, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art systems: +5.61pp over Hydra DB (90.79%) and +11.20pp over Supermemory (85.20%). It showed excellent performance on temporal reasoning (96.24%), knowledge update (98.72%), and preference synthesis (96.67%).

Conclusion: WorldDB's hierarchical, content-addressed graph architecture with programmatic edges provides a powerful solution for persistent agent memory, enabling long-running agentic systems with robust handling of temporal reasoning, knowledge updates, and contradictions while maintaining audit trails.

Abstract: Persistent memory is the bottleneck separating stateless chatbots from long-running agentic systems. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over flat vector stores fragments facts into chunks, loses cross-session identity, and has no first-class notion of supersession or contradiction. Recent bitemporal knowledge-graph systems (Graphiti, Memento, Hydra DB) add typed edges and valid-time metadata, but the graph itself remains flat: no recursive composition, no content-addressed invariants on nodes, and edge types carry no behavior beyond a label. We present WorldDB, a memory engine built on three commitments: (i) every node is a world -- a container with its own interior subgraph, ontology scope, and composed embedding, recursive to arbitrary depth; (ii) nodes are content-addressed and immutable, so any edit produces a new hash at the node and every ancestor, giving a Merkle-style audit trail for free; (iii) edges are write-time programs -- each edge type ships on_insert/on_delete/on_query_rewrite handlers (supersession closes validity, contradicts preserves both sides, same_as stages a merge proposal), so no raw append path exists. On LongMemEval-s (500 questions, ~115k-token conversational stacks), WorldDB with Claude Opus 4.7 as answerer achieves 96.40% overall / 97.11% task-averaged accuracy, a +5.61pp improvement over the previously reported Hydra DB state-of-the-art (90.79%) and +11.20pp over Supermemory (85.20%), with perfect single-session-assistant recall and robust performance on temporal reasoning (96.24%), knowledge update (98.72%), and preference synthesis (96.67%). Ablations show that the engine's graph layer -- resolver-unified entities and typed refers_to edges -- contributes +7.0pp task-averaged independently of the underlying answerer.

</details>


### [129] [LLM Safety From Within: Detecting Harmful Content with Internal Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18519)
*Difan Jiao,Yilun Liu,Ye Yuan,Zhenwei Tang,Linfeng Du,Haolun Wu,Ashton Anderson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SIREN is a lightweight guard model that leverages internal layer features of LLMs to detect harmful content, outperforming state-of-the-art guard models with 250x fewer parameters while enabling real-time streaming detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current state-of-the-art guard models rely only on terminal-layer representations and overlook the rich safety-relevant features distributed across internal layers of LLMs, limiting their effectiveness and efficiency in harm detection.

Method: SIREN identifies safety neurons via linear probing and combines them through an adaptive layer-weighted strategy, building a harmfulness detector from LLM internals without modifying the underlying model architecture.

Result: SIREN substantially outperforms state-of-the-art open-source guard models across multiple benchmarks while using 250 times fewer trainable parameters, exhibits superior generalization to unseen benchmarks, enables real-time streaming detection, and significantly improves inference efficiency compared to generative guard models.

Conclusion: LLM internal states represent a promising foundation for practical, high-performance harmfulness detection, offering better accuracy, efficiency, and generalization capabilities than traditional guard models.

Abstract: Guard models are widely used to detect harmful content in user prompts and LLM responses. However, state-of-the-art guard models rely solely on terminal-layer representations and overlook the rich safety-relevant features distributed across internal layers. We present SIREN, a lightweight guard model that harnesses these internal features. By identifying safety neurons via linear probing and combining them through an adaptive layer-weighted strategy, SIREN builds a harmfulness detector from LLM internals without modifying the underlying model. Our comprehensive evaluation shows that SIREN substantially outperforms state-of-the-art open-source guard models across multiple benchmarks while using 250 times fewer trainable parameters. Moreover, SIREN exhibits superior generalization to unseen benchmarks, naturally enables real-time streaming detection, and significantly improves inference efficiency compared to generative guard models. Overall, our results highlight LLM internal states as a promising foundation for practical, high-performance harmfulness detection.

</details>


### [130] [OGER: A Robust Offline-Guided Exploration Reward for Hybrid Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18530)
*Xinyu Ma,Mingzhou Xu,Xuebo Liu,Chang Jin,Qiang Wang,Derek F. Wong,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OGER unifies offline teacher guidance with online RL through entropy-aware reward modeling to enhance LLM exploration in reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLVR methods struggle with exploring novel trajectories beyond initial latent space, and current approaches like offline teacher guidance or entropy strategies lack deep integration or are constrained by model capacity.

Method: OGER combines offline teacher guidance and online RL through multi-teacher collaborative training and constructs an auxiliary exploration reward that leverages both offline trajectories and model entropy to incentivize autonomous exploration.

Result: Extensive experiments show OGER significantly outperforms baselines on mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks, achieving substantial gains in mathematical reasoning while maintaining robust generalization to out-of-domain tasks.

Conclusion: OGER's unified framework effectively addresses exploration limitations in LLM reasoning through integrated entropy-aware reward modulation, validated by comprehensive analysis and ablation studies.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) have significantly improved Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning, yet models often struggle to explore novel trajectories beyond their initial latent space. While offline teacher guidance and entropy-driven strategies have been proposed to address this, they often lack deep integration or are constrained by the model's inherent capacity. In this paper, we propose OGER, a novel framework that unifies offline teacher guidance and online reinforcement learning through a specialized reward modeling lens. OGER employs multi-teacher collaborative training and constructs an auxiliary exploration reward that leverages both offline trajectories and the model's own entropy to incentivize autonomous exploration. Extensive experiments across mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that OGER significantly outperforms competitive baselines, achieving substantial gains in mathematical reasoning while maintaining robust generalization to out-of-domain tasks. We provide a comprehensive analysis of training dynamics and conduct detailed ablation studies to validate the effectiveness of our entropy-aware reward modulation. Our code is available at https://github.com/ecoli-hit/OGER.git.

</details>


### [131] [ClawEnvKit: Automatic Environment Generation for Claw-Like Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18543)
*Xirui Li,Ming Li,Derry Xu,Wei-Lin Chiang,Ion Stoica,Cho-Jui Hsieh,Tianyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ClawEnvKit is an automated pipeline for generating diverse, verified environments for claw-like agents from natural language descriptions, enabling large-scale evaluation and on-demand training at dramatically lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current environment construction for training and evaluating claw-like agents is manual, human-intensive, and doesn't scale. There's a need for an automated pipeline that can generate diverse, verified environments on demand rather than just static datasets.

Method: Three-module pipeline: 1) Parser extracts structured generation parameters from natural language input, 2) Generator produces task specification, tool interface, and scoring configuration, 3) Validator enforces feasibility, diversity, structural validity, and internal consistency across generated environments.

Result: Built Auto-ClawEval benchmark with 1,040 environments across 24 categories at 13,800x lower cost than human curation while matching/exceeding human quality. Evaluation across 4 model families and 8 agent harness frameworks showed: harness engineering boosts performance by up to 15.7 percentage points over bare ReAct baseline, completion remains primary variation axis with no model saturating the benchmark, and automated generation enables previously infeasible scale.

Conclusion: ClawEnvKit enables live evaluation where users describe capabilities in natural language to get verified environments on demand, turning evaluation into continuous, user-driven process. It also serves as on-demand training environment generator that adapts to agent's weaknesses rather than being bounded by existing user logs.

Abstract: Constructing environments for training and evaluating claw-like agents remains a manual, human-intensive process that does not scale. We argue that what is needed is not just a dataset, but an automated pipeline capable of generating diverse, verified environments on demand. To this end, we introduce ClawEnvKit, an autonomous generation pipeline that instantiates this formalism from natural language descriptions. The pipeline comprises three modules: (1) a parser that extracts structured generation parameters from natural language input; (2) a generator that produces the task specification, tool interface, and scoring configuration; and (3) a validator that enforces feasibility, diversity, structural validity, and internal consistency across the generated environments. Using ClawEnvKit, we construct Auto-ClawEval, the first large-scale benchmark for claw-like agents, comprising 1,040 environments across 24 categories. Empirically, Auto-ClawEval matches or exceeds human-curated environments on coherence and clarity at 13,800x lower cost. Evaluated across 4 model families and 8 agent harness frameworks, we find that harness engineering boosts performance by up to 15.7 percentage points over a bare ReAct baseline, completion remains the primary axis of variation with no model saturating the benchmark, and automated generation enables evaluation at a scale previously infeasible. Beyond static benchmarking, ClawEnvKit enables live evaluation: users describe a desired capability in natural language and obtain a verified environment on demand, turning evaluation into a continuous, user-driven process. The same mechanism serves as an on-demand training environment generator, producing task distributions that adapt to an agent's current weaknesses rather than being bounded by existing user logs.

</details>


### [132] [Benchmarking System Dynamics AI Assistants: Cloud Versus Local LLMs on CLD Extraction and Discussion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18566)
*Terry Leitch*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents a comprehensive evaluation of large language models (both proprietary cloud APIs and open-source local models) on System Dynamics AI assistance tasks, focusing on causal loop diagram extraction and interactive model discussion, while analyzing the practical impact of different model types, backends, and quantization levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to systematically evaluate and compare the performance of various large language models (both cloud-based and locally-hosted open-source models) on System Dynamics tasks, which involve complex causal reasoning and modeling. There's a need to understand how different model architectures, backends, and deployment configurations affect practical performance in specialized domains like System Dynamics.

Method: The researchers created two purpose-built benchmarks: the CLD Leaderboard (53 tests for structured causal loop diagram extraction) and the Discussion Leaderboard (for interactive model discussion, feedback explanation, and model building coaching). They systematically evaluated multiple model families across cloud APIs and local deployments, analyzing model type effects including reasoning vs. instruction-tuned architectures, GGUF vs. MLX backends, and different quantization levels. They documented full parameter sweeps, cleaned timing data, and provided practical guidance for running large models on Apple Silicon hardware.

Result: On CLD extraction, cloud models achieved 77-89% pass rates, while the best local model reached 77% (Kimi K2.5 GGUF Q3), matching mid-tier cloud performance. On Discussion tasks, best local models achieved 50-100% on model building steps and 47-75% on feedback explanation, but only 0-50% on error fixing. Backend choice proved more impactful than quantization level, with mlx_lm requiring explicit JSON instructions while llama.cpp handled JSON reliably but struggled with indefinite generation on long-context prompts.

Conclusion: Local open-source models can achieve performance comparable to mid-tier cloud APIs on structured System Dynamics tasks, but practical deployment considerations like backend selection and context management are crucial for real-world performance. The systematic analysis reveals that backend implementation details (like JSON schema enforcement) have substantial practical implications, and the paper provides valuable guidance for practitioners working with large models on consumer hardware like Apple Silicon.

Abstract: We present a systematic evaluation of large language model families -- spanning both proprietary cloud APIs and locally-hosted open-source models -- on two purpose-built benchmarks for System Dynamics AI assistance: the \textbf{CLD Leaderboard} (53 tests, structured causal loop diagram extraction) and the \textbf{Discussion Leaderboard} (interactive model discussion, feedback explanation, and model building coaching).
  On CLD extraction, cloud models achieve 77--89\% overall pass rates; the best local model reaches 77\% (Kimi~K2.5~GGUF~Q3, zero-shot engine), matching mid-tier cloud performance. On Discussion, the best local models achieve 50--100\% on model building steps and 47--75\% on feedback explanation, but only 0--50\% on error fixing -- a category dominated by long-context prompts that expose memory limits in local deployments.
  A central contribution of this paper is a systematic analysis of \textit{model type effects} on performance: we compare reasoning vs.\ instruction-tuned architectures, GGUF (llama.cpp) vs.\ MLX (mlx\_lm) backends, and quantization levels (Q3 / Q4\_K\_M / MLX-3bit / MLX-4bit / MLX-6bit) across the same underlying model families. We find that backend choice has larger practical impact than quantization level: mlx\_lm does not enforce JSON schema constraints, requiring explicit prompt-level JSON instructions, while llama.cpp grammar-constrained sampling handles JSON reliably but causes indefinite generation on long-context prompts for dense models.
  We document the full parameter sweep ($t$, $p$, $k$) for all local models, cleaned timing data (stuck requests excluded), and a practitioner guide for running 671B--123B parameter models on Apple~Silicon.

</details>


### [133] [Agentic Forecasting using Sequential Bayesian Updating of Linguistic Beliefs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18576)
*Kevin Murphy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BLF is a Bayesian linguistic forecasting system that combines structured belief states with hierarchical aggregation and calibration to achieve state-of-the-art performance on binary forecasting tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to improve binary forecasting performance by addressing limitations in current approaches: 1) avoiding the context bloat from appending all retrieved evidence, 2) improving aggregation of multiple trials, and 3) enhancing calibration for sources with skewed base rates.

Method: Three core ideas: (1) Bayesian linguistic belief state - semi-structured representation combining numerical probabilities with natural-language evidence summaries, updated iteratively; (2) Hierarchical multi-trial aggregation - running K independent trials and combining them using logit-space shrinkage with data-dependent prior; (3) Hierarchical calibration - Platt scaling with hierarchical prior to avoid over-shrinking extreme predictions.

Result: BLF outperforms all top public methods on 400 backtesting questions from ForecastBench, including Cassi, GPT-5, Grok~4.20, and Foresight-32B. Ablation studies show the structured belief state is as impactful as web search access, and both shrinkage aggregation and hierarchical calibration provide significant gains.

Conclusion: BLF demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in binary forecasting through innovative combination of Bayesian linguistic representation, hierarchical aggregation, and calibration techniques, supported by rigorous statistical methodology and minimal leakage back-testing.

Abstract: We present BLF (Bayesian Linguistic Forecaster), an agentic system for binary forecasting that achieves state-of-the-art performance on the ForecastBench benchmark. The system is built on three ideas. (1) A Bayesian linguistic belief state: a semi-structured representation combining numerical probability estimates with natural-language evidence summaries, updated by the LLM at each step of an iterative tool-use loop. This contrasts with the common approach of appending all retrieved evidence to an ever-growing context. (2) Hierarchical multi-trial aggregation: running $K$ independent trials and combining them using logit-space shrinkage with a data-dependent prior. (3) Hierarchical calibration: Platt scaling with a hierarchical prior, which avoids over-shrinking extreme predictions for sources with skewed base rates.
  On 400 backtesting questions from the ForecastBench leaderboard, BLF outperforms all the top public methods, including Cassi, GPT-5, Grok~4.20, and Foresight-32B. Ablation studies show that the structured belief state is as impactful as web search access, and that shrinkage aggregation and hierarchical calibration each provide significant additional gains.
  In addition, we develop a robust back-testing framework with a leakage rate below 1.5\%, and use rigorous statistical methodology to compare different methods while controlling for various sources of noise.

</details>


### [134] [MathNet: a Global Multimodal Benchmark for Mathematical Reasoning and Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18584)
*Shaden Alshammari,Kevin Wen,Abrar Zainal,Mark Hamilton,Navid Safaei,Sultan Albarakati,William T. Freeman,Antonio Torralba*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MathNet: A large-scale, multimodal, multilingual Olympiad-level math dataset and benchmark for evaluating mathematical reasoning and retrieval, covering 47 countries, 17 languages, 30,676 problems over two decades of competitions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing math reasoning benchmarks are limited in size, language coverage, and task diversity, creating a gap in evaluating advanced mathematical reasoning and retrieval capabilities of modern AI models.

Method: Created MathNet dataset comprising 30,676 expert-authored problems with solutions from Olympiad competitions across 47 countries and 17 languages. Built a retrieval benchmark with mathematically equivalent and structurally similar problem pairs curated by human experts.

Result: State-of-the-art reasoning models (Gemini-3.1-Pro: 78.4%, GPT-5: 69.3%) struggle on the benchmark. Embedding models perform poorly on retrieval tasks. Retrieval-augmented generation shows significant gains (up to 12% for DeepSeek-V3.2-Speciale) but depends heavily on retrieval quality.

Conclusion: MathNet provides the largest high-quality Olympiad dataset and first benchmark for evaluating mathematical problem retrieval, revealing significant challenges for current AI models in mathematical reasoning and highlighting the importance of retrieval quality for improved performance.

Abstract: Mathematical problem solving remains a challenging test of reasoning for large language and multimodal models, yet existing benchmarks are limited in size, language coverage, and task diversity. We introduce MathNet, a high-quality, large-scale, multimodal, and multilingual dataset of Olympiad-level math problems together with a benchmark for evaluating mathematical reasoning in generative models and mathematical retrieval in embedding-based systems. MathNet spans 47 countries, 17 languages, and two decades of competitions, comprising 30,676 expert-authored problems with solutions across diverse domains. In addition to the core dataset, we construct a retrieval benchmark consisting of mathematically equivalent and structurally similar problem pairs curated by human experts.
  MathNet supports three tasks: (i) Problem Solving, (ii) Math-Aware Retrieval, and (iii) Retrieval-Augmented Problem Solving. Experimental results show that even state-of-the-art reasoning models (78.4% for Gemini-3.1-Pro and 69.3% for GPT-5) remain challenged, while embedding models struggle to retrieve equivalent problems. We further show that retrieval-augmented generation performance is highly sensitive to retrieval quality; for example, DeepSeek-V3.2-Speciale achieves gains of up to 12%, obtaining the highest scores on the benchmark. MathNet provides the largest high-quality Olympiad dataset together with the first benchmark for evaluating mathematical problem retrieval, and we publicly release both the dataset and benchmark at https://mathnet.mit.edu.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [135] [BASIS: Balanced Activation Sketching with Invariant Scalars for "Ghost Backpropagation"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16324)
*Vladimer Khasia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BASIS is an efficient backpropagation algorithm that reduces activation memory from O(L*BN) to O(L*RN) by using compressed rank-R tensors for weight updates while preserving exact error signals, achieving comparable performance to exact backpropagation with implicit regularization benefits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The activation memory required for exact backpropagation scales linearly with network depth, context length, and feature dimensionality (O(L*BN)), creating a spatial bottleneck that historically limits the scaling of deep neural networks. Existing randomized automatic differentiation methods suffer from catastrophic variance issues.

Method: BASIS uses two novel mechanisms: 1) Balanced Hashing to eliminate off-diagonal collision variance, and 2) Invariant Scalars as a principled bias-variance tradeoff that deterministically preserves the exact continuous energy norm of spatial geometry. The algorithm propagates exact error signals (dX) while computing weight updates (dW) using massively compressed rank-R tensors.

Result: BASIS reduces activation memory to O(L*RN) and decreases backward pass matrix multiplication footprint. In experiments training GPT architecture for 50,000 steps with R=32, BASIS achieves parity with and marginally outperforms exact backpropagation (validation loss 6.575 vs 6.616). The method works even under extreme compression (R=1), proving estimator robustness.

Conclusion: BASIS successfully decouples activation memory from batch and sequence dimensions while preserving flawless gradient flow, solving the foundational instability of sketched gradients. The algorithm acts as an implicit regularizer and enables smooth convergence even under extreme spatial compression.

Abstract: The activation memory required for exact backpropagation scales linearly with network depth, context length, and feature dimensionality, forming an O(L * BN ) spatial bottleneck (where B is the sequence-batch cardinality and N is the feature dimension). This constraint historically throttles the scaling of deep neural networks. While randomized automatic differentiation attempts to mitigate this, it historically suffers from catastrophic variance. In this paper, we introduce BASIS (Balanced Activation Sketching with Invariant Scalars), an efficient backpropagation algorithm that fully decouples activation memory from the batch and sequence dimensions. BASIS propagates the exact error signal (dX) to preserve flawless gradient flow, but computes the weight updates (dW) using massively compressed rank-R tensors. To solve the foundational instability of sketched gradients, we propose two novel mechanisms: Balanced Hashing, which strictly eliminates off-diagonal collision variance, and Invariant Scalars, a principled bias-variance tradeoff that deterministically preserves the exact continuous energy norm of the spatial geometry. Theoretically, BASIS reduces activation memory to O(L * RN ) and heavily decreases the backward pass matrix-multiplication footprint. Empirically, training a GPT architecture for 50,000 steps validates our theoretical guarantees: at R = 32, BASIS achieves parity with (and marginally outperforms) exact backpropagation validation loss (6.575 vs. 6.616), acting as an implicit regularizer. Remarkably, the stabilized magnitude trajectory allows the model to converge smoothly even under extreme spatial compression (R = 1), proving the extreme robustness of the estimator. The code is available at https://github.com/VladimerKhasia/basis

</details>


### [136] [UniMamba: A Unified Spatial-Temporal Modeling Framework with State-Space and Attention Integration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16325)
*Xingsheng Chen,Xianpei Mu,Deyu Yi,Yilin Yuan,Xingwei He,Bo Gao,Regina Zhang,Pietro Lio,Siu-Ming Yiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UniMamba is a unified spatial-temporal forecasting framework that combines efficient state-space dynamics with attention mechanisms to address multivariate time series forecasting challenges, achieving superior accuracy and computational efficiency compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches for multivariate time series forecasting have limitations: Transformer-based methods suffer from quadratic computational costs despite capturing temporal correlations, while state-space models like Mamba lack explicit temporal pattern recognition capabilities. There's a need for a more efficient and effective solution that can handle complex temporal dependencies and cross-variable interactions in domains like energy, finance, and environmental monitoring.

Method: UniMamba integrates three main components: 1) Mamba Variate-Channel Encoding Layer enhanced with FFT-Laplace Transform and TCN to capture global temporal dependencies; 2) Spatial Temporal Attention Layer to jointly model inter-variate correlations and temporal evolution; 3) Feedforward Temporal Dynamics Layer to fuse continuous and discrete contexts for accurate forecasting.

Result: Comprehensive experiments on eight public benchmark datasets demonstrate that UniMamba consistently outperforms state-of-the-art forecasting models in both forecasting accuracy and computational efficiency, establishing superior performance for long-sequence multivariate time-series prediction.

Conclusion: UniMamba provides a scalable and robust solution for multivariate time series forecasting that effectively addresses the limitations of existing methods by combining efficient state-space modeling with attention-based dependency learning, making it suitable for real-world applications requiring both accuracy and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Multivariate time series forecasting is fundamental to numerous domains such as energy, finance, and environmental monitoring, where complex temporal dependencies and cross-variable interactions pose enduring challenges. Existing Transformer-based methods capture temporal correlations through attention mechanisms but suffer from quadratic computational cost, while state-space models like Mamba achieve efficient long-context modeling yet lack explicit temporal pattern recognition. Therefore we introduce UniMamba, a unified spatial-temporal forecasting framework that integrates efficient state-space dynamics with attention-based dependency learning. UniMamba employs a Mamba Variate-Channel Encoding Layer enhanced with FFT-Laplace Transform and TCN to capture global temporal dependencies, and a Spatial Temporal Attention Layer to jointly model inter-variate correlations and temporal evolution. A Feedforward Temporal Dynamics Layer further fuses continuous and discrete contexts for accurate forecasting. Comprehensive experiments on eight public benchmark datasets demonstrate that UniMamba consistently outperforms state-of-the-art forecasting models in both forecasting accuracy and computational efficiency, establishing a scalable and robust solution for long-sequence multivariate time-series prediction.

</details>


### [137] [Annotation Entropy Predicts Per-Example Learning Dynamics in LoRA Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16332)
*Brady Steele*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoRA fine-tuning causes un-learning of ambiguous examples with high annotator disagreement, unlike full fine-tuning, across multiple models and datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The researchers wanted to investigate how parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA handle examples with high annotator disagreement compared to full fine-tuning, to understand differences in learning dynamics between these approaches.

Method: They correlated annotation entropy (computed from ChaosNLI's 100 labels per example) with per-example area under the loss curve (AULC) on SNLI and MNLI datasets. They tested four encoder and two decoder-only models across 25 conditions, using Spearman correlation and partial-correlation controls.

Result: LoRA fine-tuning shows increasing loss on contested examples during training, a pattern absent in full fine-tuning. Positive correlations between annotation entropy and AULC were found in all 25 conditions (ρ=0.06-0.43), stronger for decoder-only models. The effect persists across seeds, datasets, and controls.

Conclusion: LoRA fine-tuning exhibits qualitatively different learning dynamics from full fine-tuning, specifically un-learning of ambiguous examples with high annotator disagreement, suggesting important implications for parameter-efficient fine-tuning in NLP tasks.

Abstract: We find that LoRA fine-tuning exhibits un-learning on contested examples: items with high annotator disagreement show increasing loss during training, a qualitatively distinct pattern largely absent under full fine-tuning and consistent across all six models tested (four encoder, two decoder-only). This discovery emerges from correlating annotation entropy, computed from ChaosNLI's 100 labels per example, with per-example area under the loss curve (AULC) on SNLI and MNLI. The correlation is positive in all 25 conditions tested (Spearman $ρ= 0.06$-$0.43$), with decoder-only models showing stronger correlations than encoders at matched LoRA rank. The effect survives partial-correlation controls and replicates across seeds and datasets. A preliminary noise-injection experiment is consistent with these findings.

</details>


### [138] [A Discordance-Aware Multimodal Framework with Multi-Agent Clinical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16333)
*Pegah Ahadian,Mingrui Yang,Sixu Chen,Xiaojuan Li,Qiang Guan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multimodal framework combines ML prediction with multi-agent reasoning to address knee OA's symptom-structure discordance, creating interpretable phenotypes and personalized management recommendations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Knee osteoarthritis shows mismatch between imaging-observed structural damage and patient-reported symptoms like pain. This discordance complicates clinical interpretation, patient stratification, and is inadequately modeled in existing decision support systems.

Method: Proposed discordance-aware multimodal framework combining ML prediction models with multi-agent reasoning. Trained on FNIH Osteoarthritis Biomarkers Consortium baseline data. Uses three modality-specific experts: 1) CatBoost tabular model (demographic, radiographic, MRI-derived scalar, biomarker features), 2) MRI image embeddings (ResNet18), 3) X-ray embeddings (ResNet18). Stacking ensemble fuses expert predictions. Residual-based models estimate expected pain from structural features to compute pain-structure discordance score.

Result: Multi-agent reasoning layer interprets predictive signals to assign clinically interpretable OA phenotypes and generate phenotype-specific management recommendations.

Conclusion: The framework addresses the critical discordance problem in knee OA by integrating multimodal prediction with interpretable reasoning, enabling better patient stratification and personalized management strategies.

Abstract: Knee osteoarthritis frequently exhibits discordance between structural damage observed in imaging and patient-reported symptoms such as pain. This mismatch complicates clinical interpretation and patient stratification and remains insufficiently modeled in existing decision support systems. We propose a discordance aware multimodal framework that combines machine learning prediction models with a tool grounded multi agent reasoning system. Using baseline data from the FNIH Osteoarthritis Biomarkers Consortium, we trained multimodal models to predict two progression tasks, joint space loss only progression versus non progression, and pain only progression versus non progression. The predictive system integrates three modality specific experts: a CatBoost tabular model using demographic, radiographic, MRI-derived scalar, and biomarker features; MRI image embeddings extracted using a ResNet18 backbone; and Xray embeddings derived from the same architecture. Expert predictions are fused using a stacking ensemble. Residual based models estimate expected pain from structural features, enabling the computation of a pain structure discordance score between observed and expected symptoms. A multi-agent reasoning layer interprets these signals to assign clinically interpretable OA phenotypes and generate phenotype specific management recommendations.

</details>


### [139] [Preventing overfitting in deep learning using differential privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16334)
*Alizishaan Anwar Hussein Khatri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Differential privacy improves generalization in deep neural networks by preventing overfitting to training data noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep neural networks often overfit to training data noise, leading to poor generalization on unseen data, which is problematic in real-world applications with limited training data.

Method: The authors propose using a differential privacy-based approach to improve generalization in deep neural networks.

Result: Not specified in the abstract - this appears to be a position paper or proposal rather than presenting completed experimental results.

Conclusion: Differential privacy techniques show promise for addressing the overfitting problem and improving generalization capabilities of deep neural networks in practical settings with limited data.

Abstract: The use of Deep Neural Network based systems in the real world is growing. They have achieved state-of-the-art performance on many image, speech and text datasets. They have been shown to be powerful systems that are capable of learning detailed relationships and abstractions from the data. This is a double-edged sword which makes such systems vulnerable to learning the noise in the training set, thereby negatively impacting performance. This is also known as the problem of \emph{overfitting} or \emph{poor generalization}. In a practical setting, analysts typically have limited data to build models that must generalize to unseen data. In this work, we explore the use of a differential-privacy based approach to improve generalization in Deep Neural Networks.

</details>


### [140] [Beyond Verifiable Rewards: Rubric-Based GRM for Reinforced Fine-Tuning SWE Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16335)
*Jiawei Huang,Qingping Yang,Renjie Zheng,Jiaze Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRM introduces a rubric-based generative reward model that provides richer learning signals beyond binary test outcomes, improving LLM agent training for software engineering tasks through trajectory filtration and reinforced fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current end-to-end fine-tuning for LLM agents in SWE tasks relies on binary terminal rewards (e.g., unit test pass/fail), which offer poor guidance for shaping intermediate behaviors during multi-step problem-solving, limiting improvements in overall resolution process quality.

Method: The authors introduce a Generative Reward Model (GRM) equipped with human-designed rubrics that specify criteria for encouraging or discouraging specific behavioral patterns. This feedback is leveraged for high-quality training data collection via trajectory filtration, then used for Reinforced Fine-Tuning (RFT) on SWE tasks.

Result: The approach outperforms terminal-score-only rejection sampling: it more effectively suppresses undesirable patterns while promoting beneficial ones (confirmed by case analyses) and ultimately improves final test accuracy on SWE tasks.

Conclusion: Rubric-based reward modeling provides richer learning signals than binary terminal rewards, enabling better guidance of intermediate behaviors in LLM agents for software engineering tasks, leading to improved performance through more effective pattern shaping and trajectory filtration.

Abstract: Despite recent progress in Large Language Model (LLM) Agents for Software Engineering (SWE) tasks, end-to-end fine-tuning typically relies on verifiable terminal rewards such as whether all unit tests pass. While these binary signals reflect whether the final solution is correct, they provide little guidance for shaping intermediate behaviors during multi-step interactions, thereby limiting improvements in the overall quality of the resolution process. To address this, we introduce a rubric-based Generative Reward Model (GRM) that provides richer learning signals. The GRM is equipped with human-designed rubrics that indicate criteria for encouraging or discouraging specific behavioral patterns, and we leverage this feedback for high-quality training data collection via trajectory filtration. When used for Reinforced Fine-Tuning (RFT) on SWE Tasks, our approach outperforms terminal-score-only rejection sampling: it more effectively suppresses undesirable patterns while promoting beneficial ones, as confirmed by case analyses, and it ultimately improves final test accuracy.

</details>


### [141] [SaFeR-Steer: Evolving Multi-Turn MLLMs via Synthetic Bootstrapping and Feedback Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16358)
*Haolong Hu,Hanyu Li,Tiancheng He,Huahui Yi,An Zhang,Qiankun Li,Kun Wang,Yang Liu,Zhigang Zeng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SaFeR-Steer is a progressive multi-turn alignment framework for multimodal LLMs that addresses safety decay in long-context conversations through staged synthetic bootstrapping and tutor-in-the-loop GRPO, achieving substantial safety improvements on single and multi-turn benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal LLMs are increasingly deployed in multi-turn settings where attackers can escalate unsafe intent through visual-text history and exploit long-context safety decay, but current safety alignment is dominated by single-turn data and fixed templates, creating a mismatch between training and deployment.

Method: Proposes SaFeR-Steer framework with: 1) Staged synthetic bootstrapping combined with tutor-in-the-loop GRPO to train a single student under adaptive on-policy attacks; 2) TCSR (trajectory safety propagation) using minimum/average safety to propagate late-turn failures to earlier turns; 3) STEER dataset with three components for fine-tuning and evaluation.

Result: Starting from Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B, SaFeR-Steer substantially improves Safety/Helpfulness on both single-turn (48.30/45.86 → 81.84/70.77 for 3B; 56.21/60.32 → 87.89/77.40 for 7B) and multi-turn benchmarks (12.55/27.13 → 55.58/70.27 for 3B; 24.66/46.48 → 64.89/72.35 for 7B), shifting failures to later turns and yielding robustness beyond scaling alone.

Conclusion: The SaFeR-Steer framework successfully bridges the gap between single-turn training and multi-turn deployment in MLLMs, providing an effective approach to address safety decay in long-context conversations through progressive alignment and comprehensive dataset creation.

Abstract: MLLMs are increasingly deployed in multi-turn settings, where attackers can escalate unsafe intent through the evolving visual-text history and exploit long-context safety decay. Yet safety alignment is still dominated by single-turn data and fixed-template dialogues, leaving a mismatch between training and deployment.To bridge this gap, we propose SaFeR-Steer, a progressive multi-turn alignment framework that combines staged synthetic bootstrapping with tutor-in-the-loop GRPO to train a single student under adaptive, on-policy attacks. We also introduce TCSR, which uses trajectory minimum/average safety to propagate late-turn failures to earlier turns.I. Dataset. We release STEER, a multi-turn multimodal safety dataset with STEER-SFT (12,934), STEER-RL (2,000), and STEER-Bench (3,227) dialogues spanning 2~10 turns.II. Experiment. Starting from Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B, SaFeR-Steer substantially improves Safety/Helpfulness on both single-turn (48.30/45.86 -> 81.84/70.77 for 3B; 56.21/60.32 -> 87.89/77.40 for 7B) and multi-turn benchmarks (12.55/27.13 -> 55.58/70.27 for 3B; 24.66/46.48 -> 64.89/72.35 for 7B), shifting failures to later turns and yielding robustness beyond scaling alone.Codes are available at https://github.com/Ed-Bg/SaFeR-Steer

</details>


### [142] [SetFlow: Generating Structured Sets of Representations for Multiple Instance Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16362)
*Nikola Jovišić,Milica Škipina,Vanja Švenda*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SetFlow is a generative model for Multiple Instance Learning that creates synthetic MIL bag representations in latent space using flow matching and Set Transformers, improving performance on data-scarce tasks like mammography.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses data scarcity and weak supervision in real-world applications like mammography where MIL is commonly used. While foundation models provide good representations, existing augmentation methods operate at instance level and fail to capture intra-bag dependencies in MIL settings.

Method: SetFlow uses a flow matching paradigm combined with Set Transformer-inspired architecture to model entire MIL bags directly in representation space. The model handles permutation-invariant inputs while capturing instance interactions within bags, and is conditioned on both class labels and input scale for coherent, semantically consistent set generation.

Result: On a large-scale mammography benchmark using MIL-PF classification, SetFlow generates samples that closely match original data distribution and improves downstream performance when used for augmentation. Training on synthetic data alone achieves competitive results.

Conclusion: Representation-space generative modeling with SetFlow is effective for data-scarce and privacy-sensitive tasks, demonstrating that generating coherent sets of representations can overcome limitations of instance-level augmentation in MIL frameworks.

Abstract: Data scarcity and weak supervision continue to limit the performance of machine learning models in many real-world applications, such as mammography, where Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) often offers the best formulation. While recent foundation models provide strong semantic representations out of the box, effective augmentation of such representations of MIL data remains limited, as existing methods operate at the instance level and fail to capture intra-bag dependencies. In this work, we introduce SetFlow, a generative architecture that models entire MIL bags (i.e., sets) directly in the representation space. Our approach leverages the flow matching paradigm combined with a Set Transformer-inspired design, enabling it to handle permutation-invariant inputs while capturing interactions between instances within each bag. The model is conditioned on both class labels and input scale, allowing it to generate coherent and semantically consistent sets of representations. We evaluate SetFlow on a large-scale mammography benchmark using a state-of-the-art MIL-PF classification pipeline. The generated samples are shown to closely match the original data distribution and even improve downstream performance when used for augmentation. Furthermore, training on synthetic data alone shows competitive results, demonstrating the effectiveness of representation-space generative modeling for data-scarce and privacy-sensitive tasks.

</details>


### [143] [Matched-Learning-Rate Analysis of Attention Drift and Transfer Retention in Fine-Tuned CLIP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16410)
*Ruize Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: When comparing CLIP adaptation methods at matched learning rates, LoRA preserves significantly more out-of-domain transfer than Full Fine-Tuning, despite Full FT achieving better in-domain accuracy. Attention drift patterns differ substantially between methods but correlate with transfer retention rather than causing it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous comparisons between Full Fine-Tuning and LoRA for CLIP adaptation have been confounded by different learning-rate conventions, making it unclear how each method affects both in-domain accuracy and out-of-domain transfer capability.

Method: Conducted a controlled matched-learning-rate comparison of Full FT and LoRA on CLIP ViT-B/32 across EuroSAT and Oxford-IIIT Pets datasets. Used four shared learning rates with five seeds each (80 total runs), measuring attention-drift metrics, validation accuracy, and CIFAR-100 zero-shot transfer.

Result: At matched learning rates, LoRA preserved substantially more zero-shot transfer: 45.13% vs 11.28% CIFAR-100 accuracy on EuroSAT, and 58.01% vs 8.54% on Pets. Attention drift patterns differed: Full FT showed entropy contraction at high learning rates while LoRA remained entropy-positive across all rates.

Conclusion: Matched-learning-rate evaluation changes the interpretation of Full FT vs LoRA comparison. Attention drift is a descriptive diagnostic of representation preservation rather than a causal explanation of transfer behavior, with LoRA better preserving out-of-domain capabilities despite sometimes lower in-domain performance.

Abstract: CLIP adaptation can improve in-domain accuracy while degrading out-of-domain transfer, but comparisons between Full Fine-Tuning (Full FT) and LoRA are often confounded by different learning-rate conventions. We study how adaptation method and optimization scale jointly shape attention drift and transfer retention in CLIP using a controlled matched-learning-rate comparison of Full FT and LoRA. The completed matrix contains 80 runs on CLIP ViT-B/32 across EuroSAT and Oxford-IIIT Pets, spanning four shared learning rates ($10^{-6}$, $5{\times}10^{-6}$, $10^{-5}$, $5{\times}10^{-5}$) and five seeds, and evaluates attention-drift metrics, best validation accuracy, and adapter-aware CIFAR-100 zero-shot accuracy. Learning rate strongly modulates structural change: on EuroSAT, Full FT moves from mild entropy broadening at $10^{-6}$ to marked contraction at $5{\times}10^{-5}$, whereas LoRA remains entropy-positive across the full matched grid. At matched learning rates, LoRA preserves substantially more zero-shot transfer than Full FT, averaging $45.13\%$ versus $11.28\%$ CIFAR-100 accuracy on EuroSAT and $58.01\%$ versus $8.54\%$ on Pets. Oxford-IIIT Pets also reveals a regime effect: low-learning-rate LoRA underfits in-domain, so method-only averages can obscure when LoRA becomes competitive. Supporting rollout, patch-to-patch, and CKA analyses are directionally consistent with the controlled matrix. Overall, matched-learning-rate evaluation materially changes the interpretation of Full FT versus LoRA, and attention drift is most useful as a descriptive diagnostic of representation preservation rather than a causal explanation of transfer behavior.

</details>


### [144] [CGCMA: Conditionally-Gated Cross-Modal Attention for Event-Conditioned Asynchronous Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16411)
*Yunxiang Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes CGCMA architecture for asynchronous multimodal alignment, tested on cryptocurrency markets with delayed web intelligence, achieving improved Sharpe ratios compared to baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses asynchronous multimodal learning where dense primary streams must fuse with sporadic external context whose value depends on freshness. Existing benchmarks assume structural synchrony, but real-world applications require reasoning about freshness and trust.

Method: Introduces CGCMA (Conditionally-Gated Cross-Modal Attention) that separates text-conditioned grounding from lag-aware trust control. Text attends over price sequences to identify event-relevant states, then a conditional gate uses modality agreement, web features, and lag to regulate residual injection, falling back to unimodal prediction when context is stale.

Result: On Crypto Market Intelligence (CMI) corpus with 27,914 real-news samples, CGCMA attains highest mean downstream Sharpe ratio (+0.449 ± 0.257) among baselines under zero-cost threshold-trading evaluation. Gains are not explained by web scalars alone or simple freshness heuristics.

Conclusion: The evidence supports problem validity and shows promising asynchronous multimodal gain in stress-test setting, demonstrating effective handling of sporadic, delayed context through explicit freshness and trust reasoning.

Abstract: We study asynchronous alignment, a first-class multimodal learning setting in which a dense primary stream must be fused with sporadic external context whose value depends on when it arrives. Unlike standard multimodal benchmarks that assume structural synchrony, this setting requires models to reason explicitly about freshness and trust. We focus on the event-conditioned case in which continuous market states are paired with delayed web intelligence, and we use high-frequency cryptocurrency markets only as a timestamped, high-noise stress test for this broader problem. We propose CGCMA (Conditionally-Gated Cross-Modal Attention), whose central design principle is to separate text-conditioned grounding from lag-aware trust control. Text first attends over price sequences to identify event-relevant market states, after which a conditional gate uses modality agreement, web features, and lag $τ_{\mathrm{lag}}$ to regulate residual injection and fall back toward unimodal prediction when external context is stale or contradictory. We introduce CMI (Crypto Market Intelligence), an asynchronous evaluation corpus with 27,914 real-news samples pairing high-frequency price sequences with lagged web intelligence. On the current short real-news corpus, CGCMA attains the highest mean downstream Sharpe ratio ($+0.449 \pm 0.257$) among the evaluated baselines under a shared zero-cost threshold-trading evaluation on news-available bars. Additional controls show that the gain is not explained by web scalars alone and is not recovered by simple freshness heuristics. The resulting evidence supports problem validity and a promising asynchronous multimodal gain on this stress-test setting.

</details>


### [145] [Shifting the Gradient: Understanding How Defensive Training Methods Protect Language Model Integrity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16423)
*Satchel Grant,Victor Gillioz,Jake Ward,Thomas McGrath*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PPS and IP are both defensive training methods for LLMs that add trait-inducing objects during training, but they work through fundamentally different mechanisms - PPS shifts activation gradients while IP "explains away" trait expressions with a more diffuse gradient pattern.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to understand how two seemingly similar defensive training methods (PPS and IP) work, specifically whether they operate through the same mechanisms or distinct ones, using "evilness" as a case-study trait to investigate their behavioral and mechanistic differences.

Method: The researchers conducted behavioral and mechanistic comparisons using "evilness" as a trait case study. They analyzed behavioral effects, activation gradients, vector alignments, and gradient signatures through cosine similarity analyses and qualitative gradient pattern examination to understand the different mechanisms.

Result: PPS and IP achieve defensive benefits through distinct mechanisms: PPS shifts activation gradients toward attenuating directions along the PPS vector axis and can reverse gradient pressure, while IP has a characteristically different, more diffuse gradient signature and reduces next-token prediction loss on trait-expressing data, suggesting it "explains away" trait expressions.

Conclusion: The two defensive training methods operate through fundamentally different mechanisms, with PPS working through activation gradient manipulation and IP having a more complex, diffuse mechanism that "explains away" traits, highlighting open questions about IP's mechanistic picture.

Abstract: Defensive training methods such as positive preventative steering (PPS) and inoculation prompting (IP) offer surprising results through seemingly similar processes: both add trait-inducing objects to large language models (LLMs) during training, and both defend the LLM against acquiring the trait. The surprising success of these methods comes with the question: how do they work? Are PPS and IP doing the same thing? We provide behavioral and mechanistic comparisons of these two methods using "evilness" as a case-study trait. Our central finding is that PPS and IP achieve their defensive benefits through distinct mechanisms. Behaviorally, we show that neither PPS nor IP operates through a purely associative mechanism; and PPS can both defend against trait acquisition and actively reduce pre-existing expression, whereas IP is ineffective in models that were previously finetuned to express the trait. This behavioral divergence is reflected mechanistically: PPS shifts the activation gradient towards an attenuating direction along the PPS vector axis. When the PPS vector is aligned with a trait-expressing axis, it can reverse the gradient pressure, reducing rather than increasing activation along that axis. In contrast, IP continues to resist a precise mechanistic account. Direct cosine similarity analyses reveal that IP has a characteristically different gradient signature than PPS, and qualitative analyses reveal IP's gradient to be more diffuse. Furthermore, IP reduces the next-token prediction loss on trait-expressing data where PPS need not, consistent with the notion that IP "explains away" the trait-expression in the training data. Taken together, our analyses reveal distinct mechanisms by which each method operates and highlight open questions about IP's mechanistic picture.

</details>


### [146] [Functional Similarity Metric for Neural Networks: Overcoming Parametric Ambiguity via Activation Region Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16426)
*Kutomanov Hennadii*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a canonical representation and functional similarity metric for ReLU networks by analyzing neuron activation region topology instead of raw weights, overcoming representational ambiguities from permutation and scaling symmetries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern deep learning architectures suffer from representational ambiguity where identical functions can be represented by different weight configurations due to algebraic symmetries (neuron permutation and positive diagonal scaling). This makes parameter-based comparison methods unstable to weight perturbations and hinders interpretability, model merging, and reliable evaluation.

Method: 1) Eliminate scaling ambiguity via L2-normalization of weight vectors with layer compensation. 2) Generate discrete approximations of activation regions as binary functional signatures over data samples. 3) Use Locality-Sensitive Hashing (MinHash) to efficiently approximate Jaccard index for comparing large binary vectors. 4) Solve cross-network neuron matching as a linear sum assignment problem using the Hungarian algorithm.

Result: The proposed metric theoretically and experimentally mitigates neuron "flickering" effects and exhibits exceptional robustness to minor weight perturbations. It provides a stable canonical representation that addresses representational ambiguity issues in ReLU networks.

Conclusion: This framework establishes a mathematically grounded approach for stable network comparison that serves as a foundation for model merging, transfer learning, pruning assessment, and Explainable AI applications by focusing on functional properties rather than parameter configurations.

Abstract: As modern deep learning architectures grow in complexity, representational ambiguity emerges as a critical barrier to their interpretability and reliable merging. For ReLU networks, identical functional mappings can be achieved through entirely different weight configurations due to algebraic symmetries: neuron permutation and positive diagonal scaling. Consequently, traditional parameter-based comparison methods exhibit extreme instability to slight weight perturbations during training. This paper proposes a mathematically grounded approach to constructing a stable canonical representation of neural networks and a robust functional similarity metric. We shift focus from comparing raw weights to analyzing the topology of neuron activation regions. The algorithm first eliminates scaling ambiguity via L2-normalization of weight vectors with subsequent layer compensation. Next, discrete approximations of activation regions are generated as binary functional signatures evaluated over a data sample. To overcome the computational bottleneck of comparing large binary vectors, we adapt Locality-Sensitive Hashing, specifically MinHash, providing a fast and statistically precise approximation of the Jaccard index. The final cross-network neuron matching is formulated as a linear sum assignment problem solved via the Hungarian algorithm. We demonstrate theoretically and experimentally that our metric mitigates the neuron "flickering" effect and exhibits exceptional robustness to minor weight perturbations. This framework provides a solid foundation for model merging, transfer learning, objective assessment during pruning, and Explainable AI paradigms.

</details>


### [147] [Non-Stationarity in the Embedding Space of Time Series Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16428)
*Jinmyeong Choi,Brad Shook,Artur Dubrawski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TSFMs' embedding spaces show varying sensitivity to different types of non-stationarity, with detectability degrading smoothly with shift strength and models exhibiting distinct failure patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a gap in understanding how non-stationarity manifests in Time Series Foundation Models' embedding spaces, with recent work conflating non-stationarity with distribution shift, blurring important distinctions from classical time-series analysis and statistical process control methodologies.

Method: The study examines how different forms of distributional non-stationarity (mean shifts, variance changes, linear trends) become linearly accessible in TSFM embedding spaces under controlled conditions, and investigates temporal non-stationarity from persistence (long-memory/near-unit-root behavior). Researchers sweep shift strength and probe multiple TSFMs.

Result: Embedding-space detectability of non-stationarity degrades smoothly with increasing shift strength, and different TSFMs exhibit distinct, model-specific failure modes when detecting various types of non-stationarity.

Conclusion: Understanding how non-stationarity manifests in TSFM embeddings is crucial for their proper application in time-series analysis, revealing systematic patterns in how different models fail to detect various forms of non-stationarity that have clear implications for quality monitoring and change-point analysis applications.

Abstract: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) are widely used as generic feature extractors, yet the notion of non-stationarity in their embedding spaces remains poorly understood. Recent work often conflates non-stationarity with distribution shift, blurring distinctions fundamental to classical time-series analysis and long-standing methodologies such as statistical process control (SPC). In SPC, non-stationarity signals a process leaving a stable regime - via shifts in mean, variance, or emerging trends - and detecting such departures is central to quality monitoring and change-point analysis. Motivated by this diagnostic tradition, we study how different forms of distributional non-stationarity - mean shifts, variance changes, and linear trends - become linearly accessible in TSFM embedding spaces under controlled conditions. We further examine temporal non-stationarity arising from persistence, which reflects violations of weak stationarity due to long-memory or near-unit-root behavior rather than explicit distributional shifts. By sweeping shift strength and probing multiple TSFMs, we find that embedding-space detectability of non-stationarity degrades smoothly and that different models exhibit distinct, model-specific failure modes.

</details>


### [148] [(Sparse) Attention to the Details: Preserving Spectral Fidelity in ML-based Weather Forecasting Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16429)
*Maksim Zhdanov,Ana Lucic,Max Welling,Jan-Willem van de Meent*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mosaic is a probabilistic weather forecasting model that avoids spectral degradation issues in ML-based weather prediction through functional perturbations and block-sparse attention on native-resolution grids.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two principal sources of spectral degradation in ML-based weather prediction: (1) deterministic training against ensemble means which loses variability, and (2) compressive encoding creating information bottlenecks.

Method: Generates ensemble members through learned functional perturbations and operates on native-resolution grids via block-sparse attention, a hardware-aligned mechanism that captures long-range dependencies at linear cost by sharing keys and values across spatially adjacent queries.

Result: At 1.5° resolution with 214M parameters, Mosaic matches or outperforms models trained on 6 times finer data on headline upper-air variables, achieves state-of-the-art results among 1.5° models, produces well-calibrated ensembles with near-perfect spectral alignment across all resolved frequencies. 24-member, 10-day forecast takes under 12 seconds on a single H100 GPU.

Conclusion: Mosaic successfully addresses spectral degradation problems in ML weather forecasting through innovative architectural choices, enabling efficient, high-quality probabilistic forecasts with excellent spectral properties.

Abstract: We introduce Mosaic, a probabilistic weather forecasting model that addresses two principal sources of spectral degradation in ML-based weather prediction: (1) deterministic training against ensemble means and (2) compressive encoding creating an information bottleneck. Mosaic generates ensemble members through learned functional perturbations and operates on native-resolution grids via block-sparse attention, a hardware-aligned mechanism that captures long-range dependencies at linear cost by sharing keys and values across spatially adjacent queries. At 1.5$°$ resolution with 214M parameters, Mosaic matches or outperforms models trained on 6 times finer data on headline upper-air variables and achieves state-of-the-art results among 1.5$°$ models, producing well-calibrated ensembles whose individual members exhibit near-perfect spectral alignment across all resolved frequencies. A 24-member, 10-day forecast takes under 12 seconds on a single H100 GPU.

</details>


### [149] [Dimensional Criticality at Grokking Across MLPs and Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16431)
*Ping Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel offline probe called TDU-OFC converts gradient snapshots into cascade statistics to detect grokking transitions in neural networks, revealing a macroscopic observable - effective cascade dimension D(t) - that crosses D=1 precisely at generalization transitions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Abrupt transitions like grokking in deep neural networks (sudden shift from memorization to generalization) lack robust macroscopic signatures, making it difficult to detect and understand these critical transitions.

Method: Developed TDU-OFC (Thresholded Diffusion Update-Olami-Feder-Christensen), an offline avalanche probe that converts gradient snapshots during training into cascade statistics and extracts time-resolved effective cascade dimension D(t) via grokking-aligned finite-size scaling.

Result: Discovered localized dynamical crossing of Gaussian diffusion baseline D=1 precisely at generalization transition. Crossing direction is task-dependent (descending for modular addition, ascending for XOR). Negative controls confirm ungrokked runs remain supercritical (D>1). Probe is non-invasive and detects transitions 100-200 epochs before behavioral changes.

Conclusion: TDU-OFC provides a macroscopic observable D(t) that reliably detects grokking transitions in neural networks, revealing opposite-direction convergence consistent with attraction toward a shared critical manifold rather than trivial residence near D≈1.

Abstract: Abrupt transitions between distinct dynamical regimes are a hallmark of complex systems. Grokking in deep neural networks provides a striking example -- an abrupt transition from memorization to generalization long after training accuracy saturates -- yet robust macroscopic signatures of this transition remain elusive. Here we introduce \textbf{TDU--OFC} (Thresholded Diffusion Update--Olami-Feder-Christensen), an offline avalanche probe that converts gradient snapshots into cascade statistics and extracts a \emph{macroscopic observable} -- the time-resolved effective cascade dimension $D(t)$ -- via grokking-aligned finite-size scaling. Across Transformers trained on modular addition and MLPs trained on XOR, we discover a localized dynamical crossing of the Gaussian diffusion baseline $D=1$ precisely at the generalization transition. The crossing direction is task-dependent: modular addition descends through $D=1$ (approaching from $D>1$), while XOR ascends (from $D<1$). This opposite-direction convergence is consistent with attraction toward a candidate shared critical manifold, rather than trivial residence near $D \approx 1$. Negative controls confirm this picture: ungrokked runs remain supercritical ($D>1$) and never enter the post-transition regime. In addition, avalanche distributions exhibit heavy tails and finite-size scaling consistent with the dimensional exponent extracted from $D(t)$. Shadow-probe controls ($α_{\mathrm{train}}=0$) confirm that $D(t)$ is non-invasive, and grokked trajectories diverge from ungrokked ones in $D(t)$ some $100$--$200$ epochs before the behavioral transition.

</details>


### [150] [Sampling for Quality: Training-Free Reward-Guided LLM Decoding via Sequential Monte Carlo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16453)
*Jelena Markovic-Voronov,Wenhui Zhu,Bo Long,Zhipeng Wang,Suyash Gupta,Kayhan Behdin,Bee-Chung Chen,Deepak Agarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Training-free reward-guided decoding framework for LLMs that combines model probabilities with reward potentials, using Sequential Monte Carlo sampling to achieve significant performance gains without modifying model weights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard decoding methods in large language models optimize token-level likelihood rather than sequence-level quality, leading to suboptimal outputs. There's a need for principled probabilistic frameworks that can incorporate sequence-level rewards without retraining the model.

Method: Defines a reward-augmented target distribution over complete sequences by combining model transition probabilities with prefix-dependent reward potentials. Uses Sequential Monte Carlo algorithms including prefix-only and lookahead variants, with resample-move updates, Metropolis-Hastings rejuvenation, and block-wise generation. Training-free approach leaves model weights unchanged.

Result: Significant improvements across 7B models: on code generation (HumanEval) - up to 54.9% improvement over base, 9.1%-15.3% over strongest sampling baselines; on mathematical reasoning (MATH500) - up to 8.8% gains. Achieves 87.8% on HumanEval and 78.4% on MATH500 with Qwen2.5-7B, consistently outperforming reinforcement learning method GRPO.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a principled, training-free approach to reward-guided decoding that significantly outperforms existing methods by properly balancing model probabilities with sequence-level rewards through sophisticated inference-time sampling techniques.

Abstract: We introduce a principled probabilistic framework for reward-guided decoding in large language models, addressing the limitations of standard decoding methods that optimize token-level likelihood rather than sequence-level quality. Our method defines a reward-augmented target distribution over complete sequences by combining model transition probabilities with prefix-dependent reward potentials. Importantly, the approach is training-free: it leaves model weights unchanged and instead modifies the inference distribution via reward potentials, with all gains arising purely from inference-time sampling. To sample from this distribution, we develop Sequential Monte Carlo algorithms, including a computationally efficient prefix-only variant and a lookahead variant whose intermediate targets match the exact marginals of the full sequence distribution. The framework also integrates resample-move updates with Metropolis-Hastings rejuvenation and supports block-wise generation, subsuming common decoding strategies such as temperature sampling and power-tempered objectives. Empirical results across three 7B models show significant gains. On code generation (HumanEval), our method improves base performance by up to 54.9% and surpasses the strongest sampling baselines by 9.1%-15.3%. On mathematical reasoning (MATH500), it achieves gains of up to 8.8%. Notably, it reaches 87.8% on HumanEval and 78.4% on MATH500 with Qwen2.5-7B, consistently outperforming the reinforcement learning method GRPO.

</details>


### [151] [CAPO: Counterfactual Credit Assignment in Sequential Cooperative Teams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17693)
*Shripad Deshmukh,Jayakumar Subramanian,Raghavendra Addanki,Nikos Vlassis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sequential Aristocrat Utility (SeqAU) is introduced as a unique per-agent learning signal for cooperative teams where agents act in fixed order, enabling individual attribution of team rewards without critics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In cooperative teams with sequential actions and shared team rewards, it's difficult to attribute individual contributions, especially when agents are updated asynchronously, causing earlier-collected data to become outdated relative to new policies.

Method: Introduces Sequential Aristocrat Utility (SeqAU) extending Wolpert and Tumer (2002) to sequential settings. From SeqAU, derives CAPO (Counterfactual Advantage Policy Optimization), a critic-free policy-gradient algorithm that decomposes group rewards into per-agent rewards and computes per-agent advantage with closed-form solutions plus forward passes, requiring no extra environment calls beyond initial batch.

Result: The paper provides analytic bias and variance bounds for CAPO and validates them on a controlled sequential bandit, showing CAPO's advantage over standard baselines grows with team size. The framework is general enough for applications like multi-LLM pipelines.

Conclusion: SeqAU provides a principled solution to credit assignment in sequential cooperative teams, enabling individual agent learning without critics while maintaining computational efficiency and theoretical guarantees.

Abstract: In cooperative teams where agents act in a fixed order and share a single team reward, it is hard to know how much each agent contributed, and harder still when agents are updated one at a time because data collected earlier no longer reflects the new policies. We introduce the Sequential Aristocrat Utility (SeqAU), the unique per-agent learning signal that maximizes the individual learnability of each agent's action, extending the classical framework of Wolpert and Tumer (2002) to this sequential setting. From SeqAU we derive CAPO (Counterfactual Advantage Policy Optimization), a critic-free policy-gradient algorithm. CAPO fits a per-agent reward decomposition from group rewards and computes the per-agent advantage in closed form plus a handful of forward passes through the current policy, requiring no extra environment calls beyond the initial batch. We give analytic bias and variance bounds and validate them on a controlled sequential bandit, where CAPO's advantage over standard baselines grows with the team size. The framework is general; multi-LLM pipelines are a natural deployment target.

</details>


### [152] [Multi-Label Phase Diagram Prediction in Complex Alloys via Physics-Informed Graph Attention Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16468)
*Eunjeong Park,Amrita Basak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A physics-informed graph attention network predicts phase equilibria in multicomponent alloys by combining element-aware graph representations with thermodynamic constraints, enabling rapid phase mapping and alloy screening.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CALPHAD thermodynamic calculations are computationally expensive for exploring multicomponent composition-temperature space, limiting alloy design exploration to sparse sections. There is a need for rapid phase mapping and alloy screening methods.

Method: Propose a physics-informed graph attention network (GAT) that learns element-aware representations from atomic fractions and elemental descriptors as node features. Each composition-temperature point is represented as a four-node element graph. The model combines graph attention, global pooling, and MLP to predict nine phases. Thermodynamic constraints are incorporated as training penalties or inference-time projection.

Result: Baseline model achieves macro-F1 of 0.951 and 93.98% exact-set match. Physics-informed decoding improves exact-set accuracy to ~96% on dense in-domain grids. The surrogate generalizes to unseen ternary sections (99.32% accuracy) and quaternary sections at 700°C (91.78% accuracy).
