Conclusion: Attention-based graph learning coupled with thermodynamic constraint enforcement provides an effective, physically consistent surrogate for high-resolution phase mapping and extrapolative alloy screening in multicomponent systems.

Abstract: Accurate phase equilibria are foundational to alloy design because they encode the underlying thermodynamics governing stability, transformations, and processing windows. However, while the CALculation of Phase Diagrams (CALPHAD) provides a rigorous thermodynamic framework, exploring multicomponent composition-temperature space remains computationally expensive and is typically limited to sparse section. To enable rapid phase mapping and alloy screening, we propose a physics-informed graph attention network (GAT) that learns element-aware representations and couples them with thermodynamic constraints for multi-label phase-set prediction in the Ag-Bi-Cu-Sn alloy system. Using about 25,000 equilibrium states generated with pycalphad, each composition-temperature point is represented as a four-node element graph with atomic fractions and elemental descriptors as node features. The model combines graph attention, global pooling, and a multilayer perceptron to predict nine relevant phases. To improve physical consistency, we incorporate thermodynamic constraints, applied as training penalties or as an inference-time projection. Across six binary and three ternary subsystems, the baseline model achieves a macro-F1 score of 0.951 and 93.98% exact-set match, while physics-informed decoding improves robustness and raises exact-set accuracy to about 96% on dense in-domain grids. The surrogate also generalizes to an unseen ternary section with 99.32% exact-set accuracy and to a quaternary section at 700 °C with 91.78% accuracy. These results demonstrate that attention-based graph learning coupled with thermodynamic constraint enforcement provides an effective and physically consistent surrogate for high-resolution phase mapping and extrapolative alloy screening.

</details>


### [153] [Positive-Only Drifting Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16519)
*Qi Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PODPO is a new online RL method that uses likelihood-free generative policy learning without gradient clipping, employing advantage-weighted contrastive drifting that focuses only on positive samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing online RL methods (Gaussian policies and flow-based methods) have limitations: they are unimodal (lack expressiveness), require complex gradient clipping, have stringent trust-region requirements, and rely on post-hoc penalization of negative samples to correct errors.

Method: PODPO uses a drifting model to perform policy updates via advantage-weighted local contrastive drifting. It's a likelihood-free, gradient-clipping-free generative approach that relies solely on positive-advantage samples, steering actions toward high-return regions while leveraging the generative model's local smoothness for proactive error prevention.

Result: The paper introduces a promising new pathway for generative policy learning in online RL settings, addressing limitations of existing methods through a novel approach that avoids post-hoc error correction.

Conclusion: PODPO represents an elegant solution to key challenges in online RL, offering a fresh direction for generative policy learning that eliminates the need for gradient clipping, complex trust regions, and reactive error correction mechanisms.

Abstract: In the field of online reinforcement learning (RL), traditional Gaussian policies and flow-based methods are often constrained by their unimodal expressiveness, complex gradient clipping, or stringent trust-region requirements. Moreover, they all rely on post-hoc penalization of negative samples to correct erroneous actions. This paper introduces Positive-Only Drifting Policy Optimization (PODPO), a likelihood-free and gradient-clipping-free generative approach for online RL. By leveraging the drifting model, PODPO performs policy updates via advantage-weighted local contrastive drifting. Relying solely on positive-advantage samples, it elegantly steers actions toward high-return regions while exploiting the inherent local smoothness of the generative model to enable proactive error prevention. In doing so, PODPO opens a promising new pathway for generative policy learning in online settings.

</details>


### [154] [G-PARC: Graph-Physics Aware Recurrent Convolutional Neural Networks for Spatiotemporal Dynamics on Unstructured Meshes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16533)
*Jack T. Beerman,Tyler J. Abele,Mehdi Taghizadeh,Andrew Davis,Zoë J. Gray,Negin Alemazkoor,Xinfeng Gao,H. S. Udaykumar,Stephen S. Baek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: G-PARC embeds physics via moving least squares kernels on graphs for extreme nonlinear spatiotemporal dynamics, outperforming graph-based PADL with fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-based convolutions in PARC are limited to static Cartesian grids, while existing graph-based PADL methods struggle with extreme nonlinear regimes, creating need for physics-aware learning on irregular domains.

Method: Proposes Graph PARC (G-PARC) using moving least squares kernels to approximate spatial derivatives on unstructured graphs, embedding governing PDE derivatives into network computational graph, replacing encoder-processor-decoder framework.

Result: Achieves better accuracy with 2-3x fewer parameters than MeshGraphNet, MeshGraphKAN, and GraphSAGE; generalizes across nonuniform discretizations, handles moving meshes, and outperforms on nonlinear benchmarks including fluvial hydrology, shock waves, and elastoplastic dynamics.

Conclusion: G-PARC enables accurate modeling of extreme nonlinear phenomena on complex domains by embedding explicit physical operators within GNN flexibility, moving physics-aware deep learning beyond idealized Cartesian grids.

Abstract: Physics-aware recurrent convolutional networks (PARC) have demonstrated strong performance in predicting nonlinear spatiotemporal dynamics by embedding differential operators directly into the computational graph of a neural network. However, pixel-based convolutions are restricted to static, uniform Cartesian grids, making them ill-suited to following evolving localized structures in an efficient manner. Graph neural networks (GNNs) naturally handle irregular spatial discretizations, but existing graph-based physics-aware deep learning (PADL) methods have difficulty handling extreme nonlinear regimes. To address these limitations, we propose Graph PARC (G-PARC), which uses moving least squares (MLS) kernels to approximate spatial derivatives on unstructured graphs, and embeds the derivatives of governing partial differential equations into the network's computational graph. G-PARC achieves better accuracy with 2-3x fewer parameters than MeshGraphNet, MeshGraphKAN, and GraphSAGE, replacing the traditional encoder-processor-decoder framework with analytically computed differential operators. We demonstrate that G-PARC (1) generalizes across nonuniform spatial and temporal discretizations; (2) handles moving meshes required for structural deformation; and (3) outperforms existing graph-based PADL methods on nonlinear benchmarks including fluvial hydrology, planar shock waves, and elastoplastic dynamics. By embedding explicit physical operators within the flexibility of GNNs, G-PARC enables accurate modeling of extreme nonlinear phenomena on complex computational domains, moving PADLbeyond idealized Cartesian grids.

</details>


### [155] [SCATR: Simple Calibrated Test-Time Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16535)
*Divya Shyamal,Marta Knežević,Lan Tran,Chanakya Ekbote,Vijay Lingam,Paul Pu Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCATR is an efficient Best-of-N ranking method that learns lightweight scorers from hidden representations using small calibration data, achieving strong accuracy-efficiency trade-offs for test-time selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Best-of-N strategies for test-time scaling face a trade-off: learned scorers (like PRMs) are effective but expensive, while lightweight confidence heuristics based on token log-probabilities are cheap but perform poorly. There's a need for a middle ground that offers better performance than simple heuristics without the high cost of learned scorers.

Method: SCATR (Simple and Efficient BoN Ranking) learns a lightweight scorer from a small calibration set using hidden representations from the base model. It avoids expensive training by extracting useful features from the model's hidden states to create an efficient ranking mechanism for Best-of-N selection.

Result: SCATR improves over prior confidence-based baselines by up to 9% on coding and math reasoning benchmarks. Compared to LoRA fine-tuning on same calibration data, it achieves comparable accuracy with 8000x fewer trainable parameters, reduces training/inference latency by 150x/1000x respectively. It also competes with strong PRM baselines, improving accuracy by up to 7.8% on math and 4.2% on coding with up to 1000x faster inference.

Conclusion: SCATR provides an effective accuracy-efficiency trade-off for test-time selection, offering performance close to strong learned scorers while maintaining the computational efficiency of lightweight methods. It enables scalable Best-of-N strategies with minimal overhead.

Abstract: Test-time scaling (TTS) improves large language models (LLMs) by allocating additional compute at inference time. In practice, TTS is often achieved through parallel scaling: generating multiple candidate responses and selecting the best via a Best-of-N (BoN) strategy. Its effectiveness therefore hinges on the scoring function. Learned scorers such as process reward models (PRMs) can be strong, but they are expensive to train and run. Lightweight confidence heuristics based on token log-probabilities are much cheaper, yet we find that they often perform substantially worse. To improve on lightweight confidence heuristics without incurring the full cost of stronger learned scorers, we introduce SCATR, a simple and efficient BoN ranking method that learns a lightweight scorer from a small calibration set using hidden representations from the base model. Across coding and mathematical reasoning benchmarks, SCATR improves over prior confidence-based baselines by up to 9%. Relative to LoRA fine-tuning on the same calibration data, it achieves comparable accuracy with up to 8000x fewer trainable parameters and much lower compute, reducing training and inference latency by up to 150x and 1000x, respectively. SCATR is also competitive with strong PRM baselines, and in several settings improves accuracy by up to 7.8% on math and 4.2% on coding while enabling up to 1000x faster inference. Overall, SCATR offers a strong accuracy-efficiency trade-off for scalable test-time selection.

</details>


### [156] [Towards Reliable Testing of Machine Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16536)
*Anna Mazhar,Sainyam Galhotra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Paper frames machine unlearning testing as a software engineering problem, proposing causal fuzzing to detect residual influence pathways in models after data deletion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As regulations require deleting sensitive data from deployed models (machine unlearning), there's a need for practical testing approaches to verify models no longer rely on targeted information under realistic deployment constraints with imperfect oracles.

Method: Proposes a causal, pathway-centric perspective called "causal fuzzing" that generates budgeted interventions to estimate residual direct and indirect effects and produce actionable "leakage reports" for unlearning verification.

Result: Proof-of-concept results show standard attribution checks can miss residual influence due to proxy pathways, cancellation effects, and subgroup masking, motivating causal testing as a promising direction.

Conclusion: Machine unlearning testing should be treated as a first-class software engineering problem requiring practical tests with thorough coverage, debuggable diagnostics, cost-effective execution under query budgets, and black-box applicability for API-deployed models.

Abstract: Machine learning components are now central to AI-infused software systems, from recommendations and code assistants to clinical decision support. As regulations and governance frameworks increasingly require deleting sensitive data from deployed models, machine unlearning is emerging as a practical alternative to full retraining. However, unlearning introduces a software quality-assurance challenge: under realistic deployment constraints and imperfect oracles, how can we test that a model no longer relies on targeted information? This paper frames unlearning testing as a first-class software engineering problem. We argue that practical unlearning tests must provide (i) thorough coverage over proxy and mediated influence pathways, (ii) debuggable diagnostics that localize where leakage persists, (iii) cost-effective regression-style execution under query budgets, and (iv) black-box applicability for API-deployed models. We outline a causal, pathway-centric perspective, causal fuzzing, that generates budgeted interventions to estimate residual direct and indirect effects and produce actionable "leakage reports". Proof-of-concept results illustrate that standard attribution checks can miss residual influence due to proxy pathways, cancellation effects, and subgroup masking, motivating causal testing as a promising direction for unlearning testing.

</details>


### [157] [An Interpretable Framework Applying Protein Words to Predict Protein-Small Molecule Complementary Pairing Rules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16550)
*Jingke Chen,Jingrui Zhong,Tazneen Hossain Tani,Zidong Su,Xiaochun Zhang,Boxue Tian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PWRules is an interpretable framework that identifies privileged small molecule fragments and complementary pairing rules between protein semantic units and fragments, achieving competitive binding prediction performance while providing chemical interpretability for drug discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite high accuracy of black-box deep learning models, drug discovery still relies on protein-ligand interaction principles and heuristics. There's a need for interpretable models that can provide meaningful insights into protein-small molecule binding predictions.

Method: Developed PWRules framework that: 1) Uses binding affinity data to identify privileged small molecule fragments, 2) Defines complementary pairing rules between these fragments and protein words (semantic sequence units) through an interpretability module, 3) Ranks word-fragment rules using PWScore function to prioritize active compounds.

Result: PWScore achieves competitive performance comparable to physics-based model (Glide) and deep learning model (PSICHIC). Shows broad applicability for protein targets outside training dataset (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 main protease). Integration with established methods yields superior enrichment performance. Learned rules are significantly enriched near ligand-binding pockets without explicit structural guidance.

Conclusion: PWRules provides an interpretable framework for drug discovery by extracting and applying complementary pairing rules between protein words and small molecule fragments, bridging the gap between accurate prediction and chemical interpretability.

Abstract: Despite the high accuracy of 'black box' deep learning models, drug discovery still relies on protein-ligand interaction principles and heuristics. To improve interpretability of protein-small molecule binding predictions, we developed the PWRules framework, which applies binding affinity data to identify privileged small molecule fragments and subsequently defines complementary pairing rules between these fragments and protein words (semantic sequence units) through an interpretability module. The resulting word-fragment rules are then ranked by the PWScore function to prioritize active compounds. Evaluations on benchmark datasets show that PWScore achieves competitive performance comparable to the physics-based model (Glide) and the deep learning model (PSICHIC) and shows broad applicability for protein targets outside the training dataset, e.g., SARS-CoV-2 main protease. Notably, PWScore captures complementary interaction information, yielding superior enrichment performance when integrated with these established methods. Structural analysis of protein-ligand complexes indicates that learned word-fragment rules are significantly enriched near ligand-binding pockets, despite training without explicit structural guidance. By extracting and applying complementary pairing rules, PWRules provides an interpretable framework for drug discovery.

</details>


### [158] [LLM as a Tool, Not an Agent: Code-Mined Tree Transformations for Neural Architecture Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16555)
*Masakazu Yoshimura,Zitang Sun,Yuiko Sakuma,Junji Otsuka,Atsushi Irie,Takeshi Ohashi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMasTool: A hierarchical tree-based Neural Architecture Search framework combining algorithmic planning with LLM assistance for stable, open-ended model evolution, outperforming existing NAS methods on multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Neural Architecture Search approaches have limitations: conventional algorithm-driven NAS relies heavily on hand-crafted search spaces restricting exploration, while LLM-based coding approaches struggle to generate valid complex architectures and exhibit bias toward patterns in their training data.

Method: LLMasTool uses hierarchical tree-based NAS framework: 1) automatically extracts reusable modules from source code, 2) represents architectures as hierarchical trees enabling evolution through reliable tree transformations (not code generation), 3) uses coarse-level planning with diversity-guided Bayesian modeling for exploration efficiency, 4) employs LLMs to resolve remaining degrees of freedom for meaningful evolutionary trajectories and executable architectures.

Result: The method improves over existing NAS methods by 0.69, 1.83, and 2.68 points on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet16-120 datasets respectively, demonstrating effectiveness in open-ended exploration beyond LLM biases.

Conclusion: LLMasTool successfully bridges reliable algorithmic search with LLM assistance, enabling stable and open-ended model evolution that explores diverse directions beyond inherent LLM biases while ensuring executable architectures.

Abstract: Neural Architecture Search (NAS) aims to automatically discover high-performing deep neural network (DNN) architectures. However, conventional algorithm-driven NAS relies on carefully hand-crafted search spaces to ensure executability, which restricts open-ended exploration. Recent coding-based agentic approaches using large language models (LLMs) reduce manual design, but current LLMs struggle to reliably generate complex, valid architectures, and their proposals are often biased toward a narrow set of patterns observed in their training data. To bridge reliable algorithmic search with powerful LLM assistance, we propose LLMasTool, a hierarchical tree-based NAS framework for stable and open-ended model evolution. Our method automatically extracts reusable modules from arbitrary source code and represents full architectures as hierarchical trees, enabling evolution through reliable tree transformations rather than code generation. At each evolution step, coarse-level planning is governed by a diversity-guided algorithm that leverages Bayesian modeling to improve exploration efficiency, while the LLM resolves the remaining degrees of freedom to ensure a meaningful evolutionary trajectory and an executable generated architecture. With this formulation, instead of fully agentic LLM approaches, our method explores diverse directions beyond the inherent biases in the LLM. Our method improves over existing NAS methods by 0.69, 1.83, and 2.68 points on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet16-120, demonstrating its effectiveness.

</details>


### [159] [S-GRPO: Unified Post-Training for Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16557)
*Yuming Yan,Kai Tang,Sihong Chen,Ke Xu,Dan Hu,Qun Yu,Pengfei Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: S-GRPO is a unified post-training framework that combines SFT and RL advantages through ground-truth trajectory injection when exploration fails, enabling efficient domain adaptation while preserving base model capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current post-training methods for Large Vision-Language Models have limitations: SFT causes catastrophic forgetting of general capabilities by forcing single-expert trajectories, while RL suffers from optimization collapse where unaligned models can't sample valid trajectories in sparse-reward visual tasks.

Method: Supervised Group Relative Policy Optimization (S-GRPO) integrates imitation learning guidance into multi-trajectory exploration of preference optimization. It features Conditional Ground-Truth Trajectory Injection (CGI), where a binary verifier detects complete exploration failure and injects verified ground-truth trajectories with maximal rewards, creating positive signals for group-relative advantage estimation.

Result: S-GRPO bridges the gap between SFT and RL, drastically accelerates convergence, achieves superior domain adaptation, and preserves the base model's general-purpose capabilities according to theoretical analysis and empirical results.

Conclusion: S-GRPO provides an effective unified framework for LVLM post-training that dynamically balances expert trajectory exploitation and novel visual concept exploration, overcoming limitations of isolated SFT and RL approaches.

Abstract: Current post-training methodologies for adapting Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) generally fall into two paradigms: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL). Despite their prevalence, both approaches suffer from inefficiencies when applied in isolation. SFT forces the model's generation along a single expert trajectory, often inducing catastrophic forgetting of general multimodal capabilities due to distributional shifts. Conversely, RL explores multiple generated trajectories but frequently encounters optimization collapse - a cold-start problem where an unaligned model fails to spontaneously sample any domain-valid trajectories in sparse-reward visual tasks. In this paper, we propose Supervised Group Relative Policy Optimization (S-GRPO), a unified post-training framework that integrates the guidance of imitation learning into the multi-trajectory exploration of preference optimization. Tailored for direct-generation visual tasks, S-GRPO introduces Conditional Ground-Truth Trajectory Injection (CGI). When a binary verifier detects a complete exploratory failure within a sampled group of trajectories, CGI injects the verified ground-truth trajectory into the candidate pool. By assigning a deterministic maximal reward to this injected anchor, S-GRPO enforces a positive signal within the group-relative advantage estimation. This mechanism reformulates the supervised learning objective as a high-advantage component of the policy gradient, compelling the model to dynamically balance between exploiting the expert trajectory and exploring novel visual concepts. Theoretical analysis and empirical results demonstrate that S-GRPO gracefully bridges the gap between SFT and RL, drastically accelerates convergence, and achieves superior domain adaptation while preserving the base model's general-purpose capabilities.

</details>


### [160] [Cross-Modal Generation: From Commodity WiFi to High-Fidelity mmWave and RFID Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16558)
*Zhixiong Yang,Long Jing,Yao Li,Shuli Cheng,Guoxuan Chi,Chenyu Wen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RF-CMG is a diffusion-based cross-modal generation method that uses data-rich WiFi signals to synthesize high-fidelity data for scarce RF modalities like mmWave and RFID by decoupling generation into high-frequency guidance and low-frequency constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's significant data imbalance across RF modalities - abundant WiFi data but scarce mmWave and RFID data due to high acquisition costs, making it difficult to train high-quality generative models for these data-scarce modalities.

Method: RF-CMG decouples cross-modal generation into high-frequency guidance (learning distribution from limited target modality data) and low-frequency constraints (preserving physical structure). It uses a Modality-Guided Embedding module to steer diffusion trajectory and a Low-Frequency Modality Consistency module to suppress source-modality biases.

Result: RF-CMG achieves superior performance in synthesizing RFID and mmWave signals compared to prevalent generative models. Generated data proves effective for gesture recognition tasks, with analysis showing the impact of synthetic data proportion on downstream performance.

Conclusion: RF-CMG successfully addresses the RF modality data imbalance problem by leveraging data-rich WiFi to generate high-fidelity data for scarce modalities through a novel diffusion-based approach with frequency decoupling, enabling better training of models for under-resourced RF domains.

Abstract: AIGC has shown remarkable success in CV and NLP, and has recently demonstrated promising potential in the wireless domain. However, significant data imbalance exists across RF modalities, with abundant WiFi data but scarce mmWave and RFID data due to high acquisition cost. This makes it difficult to train high-quality generative models for these data-scarce modalities. In this work, we propose RF-CMG, a diffusion-based cross-modal generative method that leverages data-rich WiFi signals to synthesize high-fidelity RF data for scarce modalities including mmWave and RFID. The key insight of RF-CMG is to decouple cross-modal generation into high-frequency guidance and low-frequency constraint, which respectively learn high-frequency distribution from limited target modality data and preserve the underlying physical structure via low-frequency constraints during generation. On this basis, we introduce a Modality-Guided Embedding (MGE) module to steer the reverse diffusion trajectory toward the target high-frequency distribution, and a Low-Frequency Modality Consistency (LFMC) module to progressively enforce low-frequency constraints to suppress the accumulation of source-modality structural biases during inference, enabling high-quality target-modality generation. Performance comparison with several prevalent generative models demonstrates that RF-CMG achieves superior performance in synthesizing RFID and mmWave signals. We further showcase the effectiveness of the data generated by RF-CMG in gesture recognition tasks, and analyze the impact of the proportion of synthetic data on downstream performance.

</details>


### [161] [Reasoning on the Manifold: Bidirectional Consistency for Self-Verification in Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16565)
*Jiaoyang Ruan,Xin Gao,Yinda Chen,Hengyu Zeng,Liang Du,Guanghao Li,Jie Fu,Jian Pu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Bidirectional Manifold Consistency (BMC), a training-free metric that verifies correctness in Diffusion Large Language Models by measuring geometric stability of reasoning trajectories on learned data manifolds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) have structural advantages for global planning, efficiently verifying their correctness via valid reasoning traces remains a critical challenge. Current methods lack robust ways to determine if generated solutions are correct without ground truth.

Method: The authors propose a geometric perspective called "Reasoning on the Manifold," hypothesizing that valid reasoning trajectories reside as stable attractors on the high-density manifold of learned distributions. They introduce Bidirectional Manifold Consistency (BMC), an unsupervised metric that quantifies stability through forward-masking and backward-reconstruction cycles.

Result: BMC demonstrates versatility across three reasoning lifecycle stages: (1) Diagnosis - serves as robust discriminator of solution validity without ground truth; (2) Inference - enables rejection resampling to concentrate computational resources on complex tasks; (3) Alignment - functions as dense geometric reward that transforms sparse outcome supervision into fine-grained guidance for model self-evolution.

Conclusion: The results establish intrinsic geometric stability as a robust indicator of correctness for Diffusion Large Language Models, providing a fundamental approach to verifying reasoning quality without supervision.

Abstract: While Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) offer structural advantages for global planning, efficiently verifying that they arrive at correct answers via valid reasoning traces remains a critical challenge. In this work, we propose a geometric perspective: Reasoning on the Manifold. We hypothesize that valid generation trajectories reside as stable attractors on the high-density manifold of the learned distribution, whereas invalid paths exhibit off-manifold drift. To operationalize this, we introduce Bidirectional Manifold Consistency (BMC), a training-free, unsupervised metric that quantifies the stability of the generated sequence through a forward-masking and backward-reconstruction cycle. Empirically, we demonstrate BMC's versatility across the full reasoning lifecycle: (1) in Diagnosis, it serves as a robust discriminator of solution validity without ground truth answer; (2) in Inference, it enables rejection resampling to effectively concentrate computational resources on complex reasoning tasks; and (3) in Alignment, it functions as a dense geometric reward that transforms sparse outcome supervision into fine-grained guidance, empowering models to self-evolve beyond standard baselines. Our results establish intrinsic geometric stability as a robust indicator of correctness for dLLMs.

</details>


### [162] [In Search of Lost DNA Sequence Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16570)
*Zhijiang Tang,Jiaxin Qi,Yan Cui,Jinli Ou,Yuhua Zheng,Jianqiang Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies three critical overlooked problems in DNA sequence pretraining and provides principled guidelines for better evaluation and benchmarking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DNA sequence pretraining studies focus too much on scale and custom datasets while neglecting essential components of the pretraining paradigm, leading to three critical overlooked problems.

Method: The authors conduct comprehensive investigations to identify three key problems (inappropriate downstream datasets, flaws in neighbor-masking strategy, lack of vocabulary discussion) and propose principled guidelines including dataset selection criteria, task design guidance, and vocabulary analysis.

Result: Extensive experiments validate the significance of the identified problems and support the rationale behind the proposed recommendations.

Conclusion: The paper introduces a standardized testbed for reproducible and rigorous benchmarking of DNA pretraining methods to advance genomic foundation model development.

Abstract: DNA sequence encoding is fundamental to gene function prediction, protein synthesis, and diverse downstream biological tasks. Despite the substantial progress achieved by large-scale DNA sequence pretraining, existing studies have overwhelmingly emphasized pretraining scale and custom downstream evaluation datasets, while neglecting some essential components of the pretraining paradigm. In this paper, we reveal three critical yet heretofore overlooked problems in DNA pretraining: inappropriate downstream datasets, inherent flaws in the neighbor-masking strategy, and the lack of detailed discussion on vocabulary. Therefore, we undertake comprehensive investigations and propose principled guidelines, including selection criteria for evaluation datasets, guiding task design, and in-depth vocabulary analysis. Extensive experiments validate the significance of our identified problems and support the rationale behind our recommendations. Finally, we introduce a standardized testbed that enables reproducible and rigorous benchmarking of DNA pretraining methods to advance the development of genomic foundation models.

</details>


### [163] [From User Recognition to Activity Counting: An Identity-Agnostic Approach to Multi-User WiFi Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16572)
*Kemal Bayik,Olayinka Ajayi,Daniel Roggen,Philip Birch*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reformulating multi-user WiFi activity recognition as activity counting rather than identity-dependent assignment improves generalization to unseen users.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-user WiFi sensing approaches assume fixed, known users during both training and inference (closed-set assumption), limiting practical deployment when encountering new individuals or environments.

Method: Proposes activity counting approach that estimates how many users perform each activity type without associating actions with specific individuals. Pipeline converts CSI measurements to spatial projections and extracts features using a pretrained convolutional backbone. Evaluates two formulations: identity-dependent model (assigns activities to fixed user slots) vs identity-agnostic model (estimates scene-level activity composition through regression).

Result: Identity-agnostic model achieves mean absolute error of 0.1081 on 0-5 count scale under standard evaluation. Under unseen-user evaluation, identity-dependent model's macro-F1 drops from 80.38 to 32.61, while identity-agnostic model's counting error remains stable. Feature space analysis shows identity-agnostic representations are more user-invariant.

Conclusion: Activity counting provides a more practical and generalizable alternative to identity-dependent formulations for multi-user WiFi sensing, addressing the limitations of closed-set assumptions in real-world deployments.

Abstract: Wi-Fi Channel State Information (CSI) enables device-free human activity recognition, but existing multi-user approaches assume a fixed set of known users during both training and inference. This closed-set assumption limits deployment, as models trained on a specific user set degrade when applied to new individuals or environments. We reformulate multi-user activity recognition as activity counting, estimating how many users perform each activity type at a given time, without associating actions with specific individuals. We propose a pipeline that converts CSI measurements into spatial projections and extracts features using a pretrained convolutional backbone. Two formulations are evaluated on the WiMANS dataset: a conventional identity-dependent model that assigns activities to fixed user slots, and an identity-agnostic model that estimates scene-level activity composition through regression. Under standard evaluation, the identity-agnostic model achieves a mean absolute error of 0.1081 on a 0-5 count scale. Under unseen-user evaluation, the identity-dependent model's macro-F1 drops from 80.38 to 32.61, while the identity-agnostic model's counting error remains stable. Feature space analysis confirms that identity-agnostic representations are more user-invariant, which explains their stronger generalization. These results suggest that activity counting provides a more practical and generalizable alternative to identity-dependent formulations for multi-user WiFi sensing.

</details>


### [164] [FedOBP: Federated Optimal Brain Personalization through Cloud-Edge Element-wise Decoupling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16574)
*Xingyan Chen,Tian Du,Changqiao Xu,Fuzhen Zhuang,Lujie Zhong,Gabriel-Miro Muntean,Enmao Diao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedOBP: A federated learning algorithm that uses Optimal Brain Damage theory with quantile thresholding to intelligently select which parameters to personalize, minimizing client computational burden while improving performance in heterogeneous data environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated Learning struggles with client data heterogeneity and mobile device resource constraints, which degrade model accuracy. Personalized FL (PFL) helps by adapting global knowledge to local data, but existing methods lack principled approaches for determining which parameters should be personalized versus kept global.

Method: Proposes FedOBP algorithm with quantile-based thresholding mechanism. Extends Optimal Brain Damage pruning theory by introducing an element-wise importance score that uses federated approximation of first-order derivatives from Taylor expansion. Moves importance computation from clients to server to reduce mobile device burden.

Result: FedOBP outperforms state-of-the-art PFL methods across diverse datasets and heterogeneity scenarios. Achieves strong performance while only personalizing a very small number of parameters (typically <10% of model parameters). Successfully bridges classical pruning theory with federated parameter decoupling.

Conclusion: FedOBP provides a theoretically justified approach for parameter personalization selection in FL, balancing global knowledge sharing with local adaptation while minimizing client computational overhead. The work establishes a novel connection between pruning theory and federated learning, offering a practical solution for resource-constrained FL deployments.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) faces challenges from client data heterogeneity and resource-constrained mobile devices, which can degrade model accuracy. Personalized Federated Learning (PFL) addresses this issue by adapting shared global knowledge to local data distributions. A promising approach in PFL is model decoupling, which separates the model into global and personalized parameters, raising the key question of which parameters should be personalized to balance global knowledge sharing and local adaptation. In this paper, we propose a Federated Optimal Brain Personalization (FedOBP) algorithm with a quantile-based thresholding mechanism and introduce an element-wise importance score. This score extends Optimal Brain Damage (OBD) pruning theory by incorporating a federated approximation of the first-order derivative in the Taylor expansion to evaluate the importance of each parameter for personalization. Moreover, we move the metric computation originally performed on clients to the server side, to alleviate the burden on resource-constrained mobile devices. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to bridge classical saliency-based pruning theory with federated parameter decoupling, providing a rigorous theoretical justification for selecting personalized parameters based on their sensitivity to local loss landscapes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FedOBP outperforms state-of-the-art methods across diverse datasets and heterogeneity scenarios, while requiring personalization of only a very small number of personalized parameters.

</details>


### [165] [Evaluating Temporal and Structural Anomaly Detection Paradigms for DDoS Traffic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16575)
*Yasmin Souza Lima,Rodrigo Moreira,Larissa F. Rodrigues Moreira,Tereza Cristina M. de B. Carvalho,Flávio de Oliveira Silva*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lightweight decision framework for DDoS anomaly detection that automatically chooses between temporal or structural features using diagnostic metrics, with structural features generally outperforming temporal ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current unsupervised anomaly detection methods for DDoS attacks in cloud-native 5G networks typically assume either temporal or structural traffic representations without validating which feature space best fits the actual data characteristics.

Method: Proposes a decision framework using two diagnostic metrics: lag-1 autocorrelation of aggregated flow signal to measure temporal dependence, and PCA cumulative explained variance to assess structural complexity. The framework guides feature space selection (temporal vs. structural) before training anomaly detection models.

Result: Experiments on two statistically distinct datasets with Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM, and KMeans show that structural features consistently match or outperform temporal ones. The performance gap widens as temporal dependence weakens. The hybrid option is reserved for inconclusive cases rather than used as an empirically validated branch.

Conclusion: The framework provides a practical approach to feature space selection for DDoS anomaly detection, with structural features proving generally more effective than temporal ones, especially when temporal dependencies are weak. The method is lightweight and can improve detection performance by better matching feature spaces to data characteristics.

Abstract: Unsupervised anomaly detection is widely used to detect Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks in cloud-native 5G networks, yet most studies assume a fixed traffic representation, either temporal or structural, without validating which feature space best matches the data. We propose a lightweight decision framework that prioritizes temporal or structural features before training, using two diagnostics: lag-1 autocorrelation of an aggregated flow signal and PCA cumulative explained variance. When the probes are inconclusive, the framework reserves a hybrid option as a future fallback rather than an empirically validated branch. Experiments on two statistically distinct datasets with Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM, and KMeans show that structural features consistently match or outperform temporal ones, with the performance gap widening as temporal dependence weakens.

</details>


### [166] [Towards Trustworthy Depression Estimation via Disentangled Evidential Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16579)
*Fangyuan Liu,Sirui Zhao,Zeyu Zhang,Jinyang Huang,Feng-Qi Cui,Bin Luo,Tong Xu,Meng Li,Enhong Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EviDep is an evidential learning framework for automated depression estimation that jointly quantifies depression severity alongside aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties using Normal-Inverse-Gamma distribution, while addressing multimodal redundancy through disentangled evidence synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current automated depression estimation systems are vulnerable to signal corruption and ambient noise in real-world deployment. Deterministic methods produce uncalibrated point estimates, exposing clinical systems to the risk of overconfident misdiagnoses. There's a need for a resilient and trustworthy assessment paradigm.

Method: The proposed EviDep framework: 1) Uses Normal-Inverse-Gamma distribution to jointly quantify depression severity with uncertainties, 2) Implements Frequency-aware Feature Extraction with wavelet-based Mixture-of-Experts to isolate task-irrelevant noise, and 3) Employs Disentangled Evidential Learning to separate shared consensus from modality-specific nuances before Bayesian fusion to mitigate evidence redundancy.

Result: Extensive experiments on AVEC 2013, 2014, DAIC-WOZ, and E-DAIC datasets confirm that EviDep achieves state-of-the-art predictive accuracy and superior uncertainty calibration, delivering a robust fail-safe mechanism for trustworthy clinical screening.

Conclusion: EviDep establishes a highly resilient and trustworthy assessment paradigm for automated depression estimation by addressing the fundamental vulnerability of cross-modal redundancy in multimodal evidential fusion, providing both accurate predictions and well-calibrated uncertainty estimates for clinical safety.

Abstract: Automated depression estimation is highly vulnerable to signal corruption and ambient noise in real-world deployment. Prevailing deterministic methods produce uncalibrated point estimates, exposing safety-critical clinical systems to the severe risk of overconfident misdiagnoses. To establish a highly resilient and trustworthy assessment paradigm, we propose EviDep, an evidential learning framework that jointly quantifies depression severity alongside aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties via a Normal-Inverse-Gamma distribution. A fundamental vulnerability in multimodal evidential fusion is the uncontrolled accumulation of cross-modal redundancies. This structural flaw artificially inflates diagnostic confidence by double-counting overlapping evidence. To guarantee robust evidence synthesis, EviDep enforces strict information integrity. First, a Frequency-aware Feature Extraction module leverages a wavelet-based Mixture-of-Experts to dynamically isolate task-irrelevant noise, preserving the fidelity of diagnostic signals. Subsequently, a Disentangled Evidential Learning strategy separates the shared consensus from modality-specific nuances. By explicitly decoupling these representations before Bayesian fusion, EviDep systematically mitigates evidence redundancy. Extensive experiments on AVEC 2013, 2014, DAIC-WOZ, and E-DAIC confirm that EviDep achieves state-of-the-art predictive accuracy and superior uncertainty calibration, delivering a robust fail-safe mechanism for trustworthy clinical screening.

</details>


### [167] [Continuous ageing trajectory representations for knee-aware lifetime prediction of lithium-ion batteries across heterogeneous dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16580)
*Agnieszka Pregowska,Stefan Marynowicz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified framework for battery ageing analysis using continuous representations of voltage-capacity and capacity-cycle trajectories, enabling robust knee point detection and early-life RUL prediction across heterogeneous datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate lithium-ion battery ageing assessment is challenged by cell-to-cell variability, heterogeneous cycling protocols, limited model transferability across datasets, and difficulties in robustly identifying degradation transitions like knee points and reliably predicting remaining useful life early in battery life.

Method: Proposes a unified framework based on continuous representations of voltage-capacity and capacity-cycle trajectories learned from heterogeneous public datasets (NASA, CALCE, ISU-ILCC). The continuous formulation enables consistent extraction of degradation descriptors including curvature, plateau length, and knee-related metrics while reducing sensitivity to dataset-specific discretization.

Result: Across 250+ cells, statistically significant correlations between knee onset and end-of-life (Pearson 0.75-0.84). Early-life analysis shows knee-related features retain predictive value from partial trajectories, with meaningful RUL prediction emerging within first 5-20 cycles. Framework demonstrates robustness under cross-dataset domain shift and reduces sensitivity to sampling resolution compared to conventional methods.

Conclusion: The proposed framework integrates continuous modeling, feature extraction, and uncertainty-aware prediction, providing an interpretable and dataset-consistent approach that demonstrates robustness across heterogeneous dataset types. However, study is limited to laboratory-scale datasets and capacity-based end-of-life definitions.

Abstract: Accurate assessment of lithium-ion battery ageing is challenged by cell-to-cell variability, heterogeneous cycling protocols, and limited transferability of data-driven models across datasets. In particular, robust identification of degradation transitions, such as the knee point, and reliable early-life prediction of remaining useful life (RUL) remain open problems. This study proposes a unified framework for battery ageing analysis based on continuous representations of voltage-capacity and capacity-cycle trajectories learned from heterogeneous public datasets (NASA, CALCE, ISU-ILCC). The continuous formulation enables consistent extraction of degradation descriptors, including curvature, plateau length and knee-related metrics, while reducing sensitivity to dataset-specific discretisation. Across more than 250 cells, statistically significant correlations between knee onset and end-of-life (Pearson 0.75-0.84) are observed. Additional early-life analysis confirms that knee-related features retain predictive value when estimated from partial trajectories. Early-life models provide increasingly stable RUL predictions as the number of observed cycles increases, with meaningful predictive performance emerging within the first 5-20 cycles and remain robust under cross-dataset domain shift. The framework integrates continuous modelling, feature extraction and uncertainty-aware prediction, providing an interpretable and dataset-consistent approach demonstrating robustness across heterogeneous dataset types. Compared with conventional discrete or feature-based methods, the proposed representation reduces sensitivity to sampling resolution and improves cross-dataset consistency. The study is limited to laboratory-scale datasets and capacity-based end-of-life definitions.

</details>


### [168] [NCO4CVRP: Neural Combinatorial Optimization for the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16581)
*Mahir Labib Dihan,Md. Ashrafur Rahman Khan,Wasif Jalal,Md. Roqunuzzaman Sojib,Mashroor Hasan Bhuiyan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Improved neural combinatorial optimization for CVRP by enhancing inference techniques with simulated annealing, beam search, and exploring various sampling strategies and instance augmentations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve solution quality and generalization of neural combinatorial optimization models for combinatorial optimization problems, particularly the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP), by refining existing inference techniques.

Method: Modified the RRC approach of LEHD model with Simulated Annealing for probabilistic acceptance; enhanced POMO approach with Beam Search for systematic exploration; investigated various inference strategies (Softmax Sampling, Greedy, Gumbel-Softmax, Epsilon-Greedy); explored instance augmentation techniques (horizontal/vertical flipping, rotation).

Result: Significantly reduced optimality gap across various CVRP benchmarks; Beam Search and SA-based RRC consistently yielded superior performance compared to conventional methods.

Conclusion: Refining inference techniques and leveraging enhanced search strategies improves NCO model applicability to real-world combinatorial optimization tasks by better escaping local optima and exploring diverse solution spaces.

Abstract: Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) has emerged as a powerful framework for solving combinatorial optimization problems by integrating deep learning-based models. This work focuses on improving existing inference techniques to enhance solution quality and generalization. Specifically, we modify the Random Re-Construct (RRC) approach of the Light Encoder Heavy Decoder (LEHD) model by incorporating Simulated Annealing (SA). Unlike the conventional RRC, which greedily replaces suboptimal segments, our SA-based modification introduces a probabilistic acceptance mechanism that allows the model to escape local optima and explore a more diverse solution space. Additionally, we enhance the Policy Optimization with Multiple Optima (POMO) approach by integrating Beam Search, enabling systematic exploration of multiple promising solutions while maintaining diversity in the search space. We further investigate different inference strategies, including Softmax Sampling, Greedy, Gumbel-Softmax, and Epsilon-Greedy, analyzing their impact on solution quality. Furthermore, we explore instance augmentation techniques, such as horizontal and vertical flipping and rotation-based augmentations, to improve model generalization across different CVRP instances. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that these modifications significantly reduce the optimality gap across various Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) benchmarks, with Beam Search and SA-based RRC consistently yielding superior performance. By refining inference techniques and leveraging enhanced search strategies, our work contributes to the broader applicability of NCO models in real-world combinatorial optimization tasks.

</details>


### [169] [POLAR: Online Learning for LoRA Adapter Caching and Routing in Edge LLM Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16583)
*Shaoang Li,Jian Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: POLAR is a two-timescale contextual bandit algorithm for joint caching and routing of LoRA adapters in edge LLM deployment that achieves sublinear regret while accounting for memory hierarchy constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge deployment of large language models uses lightweight LoRA adapters, but GPU/DRAM can only keep a small subset resident at once. Paging non-resident adapters from storage creates latency, requiring joint optimization of caching (slow timescale) and request routing (fast timescale) with unknown context-dependent utilities.

Method: POLAR formulates the problem as a two-timescale contextual bandit with a cache-aware LinUCB router and epoch-based cache controller. Two variants: fixed-epoch version for worst-case guarantees, and POLAR+ with forced exploration and improved cache optimization for better theoretical bounds under stochastic conditions.

Result: POLAR+ achieves $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(d\sqrt{NT}+\sqrt{KT})$ sublinear regret, matching standard contextual bandit rates up to logarithmic factors despite memory constraints. Experiments with 15 real LoRA adapters for Qwen2.5-7B show adaptive cache control substantially outperforms non-adaptive baselines.

Conclusion: The memory hierarchy doesn't fundamentally slow routing learning when properly addressed. Adaptive joint optimization of caching and routing through POLAR enables efficient edge deployment of LLMs with multiple LoRA adapters while maintaining theoretical guarantees and practical performance.

Abstract: Edge deployment of large language models (LLMs) increasingly relies on libraries of lightweight LoRA adapters, yet GPU/DRAM can keep only a small resident subset at a time. Serving a request through a non-resident adapter requires paging its weights from storage, incurring measurable latency. This creates a two-timescale online control problem: on a slow timescale, the system selects which adapters remain resident in fast memory, while on a fast timescale it routes each request to an adapter whose context-dependent utility is unknown a priori. The two decisions are tightly coupled: the cache determines the cost of exploration, and the router determines which adapters receive informative feedback. We formulate this joint caching-and-routing problem as a two-timescale contextual bandit and propose POLAR (Paging and Online Learning for Adapter Routing). POLAR pairs a cache-aware LinUCB router with an epoch-based cache controller. We study two variants. A fixed-epoch version provides a robust baseline with worst-case regret guarantees under arbitrary contexts. An epoch-doubling version, POLAR+, adds forced exploration and improved cache optimization to achieve $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(d\sqrt{NT}+\sqrt{KT})$ sublinear regret under stochastic regularity and cacheability conditions, where $N$ is the adapter count, $K$ the cache size, $d$ the context dimension, and $T$ the horizon. The routing term matches the standard contextual-bandit rate up to logarithmic factors, showing that the memory hierarchy does not fundamentally slow routing learning. Experiments using 15 real LoRA adapters for Qwen2.5-7B together with measured GPU paging latencies show that adaptive cache control substantially outperforms non-adaptive baselines and exhibits scaling trends consistent with the theory.

</details>


### [170] [The Global Neural World Model: Spatially Grounded Discrete Topologies for Action-Conditioned Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16585)
*Noureddine Kermiche*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GNWM is a self-stabilizing neural world model that maps environments to discrete 2D grids with topological quantization, preventing manifold drift through native grid "snapping" error correction and learning generalized dynamics via maximum entropy exploration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a stable world model that can organize continuous, predictable concepts into structured topological maps while preventing the common problem of manifold drift during autoregressive rollouts, and to learn generalized transition dynamics rather than memorizing specific expert trajectories.

Method: Uses a continuous, action-conditioned Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) that maps environments onto a discrete 2D grid with translational equivariance (no pixel-level reconstruction). Implements grid "snapping" as native error-correction mechanism and trains via maximum entropy exploration using random walks.

Result: The architecture successfully prevents manifold drift during autoregressive rollouts through grid snapping error correction. The model learns generalized transition dynamics rather than memorizing specific expert trajectories. Validated across passive observation, active agent control, and abstract sequence regimes.

Conclusion: GNWM serves not just as a spatial physics simulator but as a causal discovery model capable of organizing continuous, predictable concepts into structured topological maps, demonstrating self-stabilization through topological quantization and balanced continuous entropy constraints.

Abstract: We present the Global Neural World Model (GNWM), a self-stabilizing framework that achieves topological quantization through balanced continuous entropy constraints. Operating as a continuous, action-conditioned Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA), the GNWM maps environments onto a discrete 2D grid, enforcing translational equivariance without pixel-level reconstruction. Our results show this architecture prevents manifold drift during autoregressive rollouts by using grid ``snapping'' as a native error-correction mechanism. Furthermore, by training via maximum entropy exploration (random walks), the model learns generalized transition dynamics rather than memorizing specific expert trajectories. We validate the GNWM across passive observation, active agent control, and abstract sequence regimes, demonstrating its capacity to act not just as a spatial physics simulator, but as a causal discovery model capable of organizing continuous, predictable concepts into structured topological maps.

</details>


### [171] [A Systematic Survey and Benchmark of Deep Learning for Molecular Property Prediction in the Foundation Model Era](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16586)
*Zongru Li,Xingsheng Chen,Honggang Wen,Regina Qianru Zhang,Ming Li,Xiaojin Zhang,Hongzhi Yin,Qiang Yang,Kwok-Yan Lam,Pietro Lio,Siu-Ming Yiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This survey paper provides a comprehensive review of molecular property prediction methods, analyzing four key paradigms, benchmarking current approaches, and proposing modernization directions toward more transparent and physics-aware methodologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the growing field of molecular property prediction that bridges quantum chemistry, cheminformatics, and deep learning. There's a need for a unified taxonomy and modernization of benchmark methodologies due to challenges like inconsistent stereochemistry, heterogeneous assay sources, and reproducibility limitations in current approaches.

Method: The authors survey four complementary paradigms: Quantum approaches, Descriptor-based Machine Learning, Geometric Deep Learning, and Foundation Models. They develop a unified taxonomy linking molecular representations, model architectures, and interdisciplinary applications. Benchmark analyses integrate evidence from both widely used academic datasets and industry-relevant datasets across quantum, physicochemical, physiological, and biophysical domains.

Result: The analysis examines current standards in data curation, splitting strategies, and evaluation protocols, revealing challenges including inconsistent stereochemistry handling, heterogeneous assay sources, and reproducibility limitations under random or poorly defined splits. The survey identifies the need for modernization of benchmark design toward more transparent, time-aware, and scaffold-aware methodologies.

Conclusion: The paper concludes by proposing three forward-looking research directions: (1) physics-aware learning embedding quantum consistency, (2) uncertainty-calibrated foundation models for trustworthy inference, and (3) realistic multimodal benchmark ecosystems integrating computational and experimental data. The work provides a comprehensive foundation for advancing molecular property prediction in the foundation model era.

Abstract: Molecular property prediction integrates quantum chemistry, cheminformatics, and deep learning to connect molecular structure with physicochemical and biological behavior. This survey traces four complementary paradigms, including Quantum, Descriptor Machine Learning, Geometric Deep Learning, and Foundation Models, and outlines a unified taxonomy linking molecular representations, model architectures, and interdisciplinary applications. Benchmark analyses integrate evidence from both widely used datasets and datasets reflecting industry perspectives, encompassing quantum, physicochemical, physiological, and biophysical domains. The survey examines current standards in data curation, splitting strategies, and evaluation protocols, highlighting challenges including inconsistent stereochemistry, heterogeneous assay sources, and reproducibility limitations under random or poorly defined splits. These observations motivate the modernization of benchmark design toward more transparent, time- and scaffold-aware methodologies. We further propose three forward-looking directions: (i) physics-aware learning embedding quantum consistency, (ii) uncertainty-calibrated foundation models for trustworthy inference, and (iii) realistic multimodal benchmark ecosystems integrating computational and experimental data. Repository: https://github.com/Zongru-Li/Survey-and-Benchmarks-of-DL-for-Molecular-Property-Prediction-in-the-Foundation-Model-Era.

</details>


### [172] [Hybrid Spectro-Temporal Fusion Framework for Structural Health Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16589)
*Jongyeop Kim,Jinki Kim,Doyun Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework combining spectral and temporal features improves vibration-based structural health monitoring by better capturing vibration dynamics at different scales, with the hybrid approach showing superior accuracy and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Structural health monitoring is critical for ensuring structural safety through vibration analysis, but current methods may not adequately capture both fine-scale and coarse-scale vibration dynamics needed for accurate monitoring.

Method: Proposes two frameworks: 1) Spectro-Temporal Alignment framework integrating arrival-time interval descriptors with spectral features, and 2) Hybrid Spectro-Temporal Fusion framework. Both aim to capture multi-scale vibration dynamics. Experiments conducted on LDS V406 electrodynamic shaker data.

Result: The spectro-temporal representations significantly outperform conventional approaches. Temporal resolution of 0.02 favors traditional ML models, while finer 0.008 resolution unlocks deep learning performance. The hybrid framework achieves higher accuracy with lower variability than baselines and alignment-only approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed hybrid framework provides a robust, accurate, and reliable solution for vibration-based structural health monitoring by effectively integrating multi-scale temporal and spectral information.

Abstract: Structural health monitoring plays a critical role in ensuring structural safety by analyzing vibration responses from engineering systems. This paper proposes a Spectro-Temporal Alignment framework and a Hybrid Spectro-Temporal Fusion framework that integrate arrival-time interval descriptors with spectral features to capture both fine-scale and coarse-scale vibration dynamics. Experiments conducted on data collected from an LDS V406 electrodynamic shaker demonstrate that the proposed spectro-temporal representations significantly outperform conventional input formulations. The results indicate that a temporal resolution (Δτ) of 0.008 of 0.02 favors traditional machine learning models, whereas a finer resolution (Δτ) of 0.008 effectively unlocks the performance potential of deep learning architectures. Beyond classification accuracy, a comprehensive stability analysis based on condensed indices, including mean performance, standard deviation, coefficient of variation, and balanced score, shows that the proposed hybrid framework consistently achieves higher accuracy with substantially lower variability compared to baseline and alignment-only approaches. Overall, these results demonstrate that the proposed framework provides a robust, accurate, and reliable solution for vibration-based structural health monitoring.

</details>


### [173] [Global Attention with Linear Complexity for Exascale Generative Data Assimilation in Earth System Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16590)
*Xiao Wang,Zezhong Zhang,Isaac Lyngaas,Hong-Jun Yoon,Jong-Youl Choi,Siming Liang,Janet Wang,Hristo G. Chipilski,Ashwin M. Aji,Feng Bao,Peter Jan van Leeuwen,Dan Lu,Guannan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel generative data assimilation framework using spatiotemporal transformers with linear-complexity global attention achieves exascale performance for Earth system state estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While exascale computing has advanced earth simulation, scalable and accurate inference of the Earth system state remains a fundamental bottleneck, limiting uncertainty quantification and extreme event prediction.

Method: A unified one-stage generative DA framework that reformulates assimilation as Bayesian posterior sampling, replacing conventional forecast-update cycles with compute-dense, GPU-efficient inference. Core is STORM - a spatiotemporal transformer with global attention linear-complexity scaling algorithm that breaks quadratic attention barriers.

Result: Achieved 63% strong scaling efficiency and 1.6 ExaFLOP sustained performance on 32,768 GPUs of Frontier supercomputer. Scaled to 20 billion spatiotemporal tokens, enabling km-scale global modeling over 177k temporal frames, previously unreachable regimes.

Conclusion: Establishes a new paradigm for Earth system prediction by enabling previously unreachable km-scale global modeling through exascale generative data assimilation.

Abstract: Accurate weather and climate prediction relies on data assimilation (DA), which estimates the Earth system state by integrating observations with models. While exascale computing has significantly advanced earth simulation, scalable and accurate inference of the Earth system state remains a fundamental bottleneck, limiting uncertainty quantification and prediction of extreme events. We introduce a unified one-stage generative DA framework that reformulates assimilation as Bayesian posterior sampling, replacing the conventional forecast-update cycle with compute-dense, GPU-efficient inference. At the core is STORM, a novel spatiotemporal transformer with a global attention linear-complexity scaling algorithm that breaks the quadratic attention barrier. On 32,768 GPUs of the Frontier supercomputer, our method achieves 63% strong scaling efficiency and 1.6 ExaFLOP sustained performance. We further scale to 20 billion spatiotemporal tokens, enabling km-scale global modeling over 177k temporal frames, regimes previously unreachable, establishing a new paradigm for Earth system prediction.

</details>


### [174] [Randomized Antipodal Search Done Right for Data Pareto Improvement of LLM Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16591)
*Ziwen Liu,Huawei Lin,Yide Ran,Denghui Zhang,Jianwen Xie,Chuan Li,Weijie Zhao,Zhaozhuo Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RASLIK is a novel retrieval algorithm for LLM unlearning that uses randomized antipodal search and permutation-projection hashing to efficiently find relevant data for forgetting, improving both quality and efficiency compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current machine unlearning methods assume readily available forget and retain datasets, but in practice, unlearning is triggered by undesirable generations at inference time, making data retrieval the central challenge. Existing optimization-based approaches lack effective methods for finding relevant data to forget.

Method: Proposes Randomized Antipodal Search on Linearized Influence Kernel (RASLIK), a retrieval algorithm combining permutation-projection hashing with randomized antipodal search. This approach introduces data Pareto improvement principles and achieves sublinear complexity while reducing selection variance.

Result: RASLIK consistently outperforms deterministic baselines and even oracle sampling across multiple models, datasets, and unlearning algorithms. It achieves a double gain in both quality and efficiency, demonstrating randomized search as a principled and scalable solution for data-centric unlearning.

Conclusion: Randomized search through the RASLIK algorithm provides an effective, scalable approach to the data retrieval challenge in LLM unlearning, enabling practical unlearning when triggered by undesirable generations rather than relying on pre-defined datasets.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) sometimes memorize undesirable knowledge, which must be removed after deployment. Prior work on machine unlearning has focused largely on optimization methods that adjust parameters to enforce forgetting while preserving retention. However, these approaches assume that the forget and retain sets are readily available, which rarely holds in practice. Unlearning is typically triggered by an undesired generation at inference time, making the retrieval of relevant data the central challenge.
  We introduce the notion of data Pareto improvement for LLM unlearning, which formalizes how retrieval can expand the achievable trade-off frontier between forgetting and retention. To realize this principle, we propose Randomized Antipodal Search on Linearized Influence Kernel (RASLIK), a retrieval algorithm that combines permutation-projection hashing with randomized antipodal search. RASLIK reduces selection variance, achieves sublinear complexity, and yields a double gain in both quality and efficiency. Across multiple models, datasets, and unlearning algorithms, RASLIK consistently outperforms deterministic baselines and even oracle sampling, establishing randomized search as a principled and scalable solution for data-centric unlearning.

</details>


### [175] [FedLLM: A Privacy-Preserving Federated Large Language Model for Explainable Traffic Flow Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16612)
*Seerat Kaur,Sukhjit Singh Sehra,Dariush Ebrahimi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedLLM: A federated learning framework using domain-adapted Large Language Models for privacy-preserving, explainable multi-horizon traffic flow prediction across distributed systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing traffic prediction methods face limitations: most spatio-temporal models use centralized data (privacy concerns), rely on numerical representations (limited explainability), and cannot handle distributed, heterogeneous real-world traffic systems. LLM-based methods improve reasoning but assume centralized data availability.

Method: 1) Composite Selection Score (CSS) for data-driven freeway selection capturing structural diversity. 2) Domain-adapted LLM fine-tuned on structured traffic prompts encoding spatial, temporal, and statistical context. 3) Federated LLM framework enabling collaborative training across heterogeneous clients exchanging only lightweight LoRA adapter parameters. 4) Structured prompt representation supporting contextual reasoning and cross-region generalization.

Result: FedLLM achieves improved predictive performance over centralized baselines for 15-60 minute traffic flow prediction while producing structured and explainable outputs. The framework reduces communication overhead, preserves data privacy, and supports learning under non-IID traffic distributions.

Conclusion: Combining federated learning with domain-adapted LLMs offers a scalable, privacy-aware, and explainable solution for traffic prediction that addresses practical limitations of existing approaches while handling distributed, heterogeneous real-world traffic systems.

Abstract: Traffic prediction plays a central role in intelligent transportation systems (ITS) by supporting real-time decision-making, congestion management, and long-term planning. However, many existing approaches face practical limitations. Most spatio-temporal models are trained on centralized data, rely on numerical representations, and offer limited explainability. Recent Large Language Model (LLM) methods improve reasoning capabilities but typically assume centralized data availability and do not fully capture the distributed and heterogeneous nature of real-world traffic systems. To address these challenges, this study proposes FedLLM (Federated LLM), a privacy-preserving and distributed framework for explainable multi-horizon short-term traffic flow prediction (15-60 minutes). The framework introduces four key contributions: 1) a Composite Selection Score (CSS) for data-driven freeway selection that captures structural diversity across traffic regions 2) a domain-adapted LLM fine-tuned on structured traffic prompts encoding spatial, temporal, and statistical context 3) FedLLM framework, that enables collaborative training across heterogeneous clients while exchanging only lightweight LoRA adapter parameters, 4) a structured prompt representation that supports contextual reasoning and cross-region generalization. The FedLLM design allows each client to learn from local traffic patterns while contributing to a shared global model through efficient parameter exchange, reducing communication overhead and keeping data private. This setup supports learning under non-IID traffic distributions. Experimental results show that FedLLM achieves improved predictive performance over centralized baselines, while producing structured and explainable outputs. These findings highlight the potential of combining FL with domain-adapted LLMs for scalable, privacy-aware, and explainable traffic prediction.

</details>


### [176] [Beyond Feature Fusion: Contextual Bayesian PEFT for Multimodal Uncertainty Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16615)
*Habibeh Naderi,Behrouz Haji Soleimani,Stan Matwin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoCo-LoRA is a multimodal, uncertainty-aware parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that conditions adapter uncertainty on both text features and audio context, improving reliability for speech-centered applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PEFT methods like LoRA are deterministic, while Bayesian low-rank adapters model uncertainty but remain unimodal. They can't capture uncertainty from acoustic factors like background noise, channel variability, or speaking style, which affects reliability in speech applications.

Method: CoCo-LoRA conditions a contextual variational posterior in low-rank space on both text-derived adapter features and audio-derived context signals. A pooled audio embedding is projected into a shared context space and adapted through lightweight layer-wise heads, enabling global-to-local modulation of adapter uncertainty without high-dimensional multimodal fusion.

Result: CoCo-LoRA consistently matches or outperforms text-only PEFT and conventional feature-fusion transfer baselines across diverse tasks and backbone combinations, especially on high-coverage labels where reliable adaptation is critical.

Conclusion: Using audio as a contextual uncertainty signal rather than as a fused feature stream provides a robust and parameter-efficient alternative for multimodal low-resource prediction.

Abstract: We introduce CoCo-LoRA, a multimodal, uncertainty-aware parameter-efficient fine-tuning method for text prediction tasks accompanied by audio context. Existing PEFT approaches such as LoRA are efficient but typically deterministic, while recent Bayesian low-rank adapters model uncertainty in a lightweight way yet remain largely unimodal and condition uncertainty primarily on internal text features. This leaves them poorly equipped to reflect uncertainty driven by external acoustic factors such as background noise, channel variability, or speaking style, which can materially affect reliability in speech-centered applications. CoCo-LoRA addresses this gap by conditioning a contextual variational posterior in the low-rank space on both local text-derived adapter features and an audio-derived context signal. A pooled audio embedding is projected once into a shared context space and then adapted through lightweight layer-wise heads, enabling global-to-local, depth-specific modulation of the adapter uncertainty and update without high-dimensional multimodal fusion. Stochasticity is confined to a compact latent component in the rank space, preserving PEFT scalability while producing audio-sensitive, heteroscedastic uncertainty. Based on our evaluations across diverse tasks and backbone combinations, CoCo-LoRA consistently matches or outperforms text-only PEFT and conventional feature-fusion transfer baselines, particularly on high-coverage labels where reliable adaptation is critical. The results indicate that using audio as a contextual uncertainty signal, rather than as a fused feature stream, provides a robust and parameter-efficient alternative for multimodal low-resource prediction.

</details>


### [177] [Lower Bounds and Proximally Anchored SGD for Non-Convex Minimization Under Unbounded Variance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16620)
*Arda Fazla,Ege C. Kaya,Antesh Upadhyay,Abolfazl Hashemi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Establishes minimax optimal oracle complexity bounds for stochastic non-convex optimization under the weakest known BG-0 variance condition, showing unavoidable degradation from bounded-variance complexities, and proposes PASTA algorithm that achieves these optimal bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SGD analysis typically assumes uniformly bounded variance, but this fails in practical non-convex settings like neural networks. The BG-0 condition is the weakest known variance condition but its oracle complexity for non-convex optimization remained underexplored.
