Method: Established information-theoretic lower bounds for BG-0 oracle queries, then proposed PASTA (Proximally Anchored STochastic Approximation) which couples Halpern anchoring with Tikhonov regularization to dynamically mitigate variance explosion permitted by BG-0.

Result: Lower bounds: Ω(ε⁻⁶) queries for smooth functions and Ω(ε⁻⁴) for mean-square smooth under BG-0, showing degradation from bounded-variance complexities (Ω(ε⁻⁴) and Ω(ε⁻³)). PASTA achieves these minimax optimal complexities across multiple non-convex regimes.

Conclusion: PASTA provides a unified algorithmic framework achieving minimax optimal oracle complexities for stochastic non-convex optimization under the weakest BG-0 variance condition, with theoretical guarantees under unbounded domains and stochastic gradients.

Abstract: Analysis of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) and its variants typically relies on the assumption of uniformly bounded variance, a condition that frequently fails in practical non-convex settings, such as neural network training, as well as in several elementary optimization settings. While several relaxations are explored in the literature, the Blum-Gladyshev (BG-0) condition, which permits the variance to grow quadratically with distance has recently been shown to be the weakest condition. However, the study of the oracle complexity of stochastic first-order non-convex optimization under BG-0 has remained underexplored. In this paper, we address this gap and establish information-theoretic lower bounds, proving that finding an $ε$-stationary point requires $Ω(ε^{-6})$ stochastic BG-0 oracle queries for smooth functions and $Ω(ε^{-4})$ queries under mean-square smoothness. These limits demonstrate an unavoidable degradation from classical bounded-variance complexities, i.e., $Ω(ε^{-4})$ and $Ω(ε^{-3})$ for smooth and mean-square smooth cases, respectively. To match these lower bounds, we consider Proximally Anchored STochastic Approximation (PASTA), a unified algorithmic framework that couples Halpern anchoring with Tikhonov regularization to dynamically mitigate the extra variance explosion term permitted by the BG-0 oracle. We prove that PASTA achieves minimax optimal complexities across numerous non-convex regimes, including standard smooth, mean-square smooth, weakly convex, star-convex, and Polyak-Lojasiewicz functions, entirely under an unbounded domain and unbounded stochastic gradients.

</details>


### [178] [FRIGID: Scaling Diffusion-Based Molecular Generation from Mass Spectra at Training and Inference Time](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16648)
*Montgomery Bohde,Hongxuan Liu,Mrunali Manjrekar,Magdalena Lederbauer,Shuiwang Ji,Runzhong Wang,Connor W. Coley*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FRIGID is a diffusion-based framework that generates molecular structures from mass spectra using fingerprint representations and chemical formulae, with inference-time scaling via forward fragmentation models to refine predictions and achieve state-of-the-art accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of de novo structural elucidation from mass spectra, which is crucial for identifying unknown compounds in fields like metabolomics and natural product discovery. Existing methods have limitations in accuracy and scalability for this complex inverse problem.

Method: FRIGID uses a novel diffusion language model trained on hundreds of millions of unlabeled molecular structures. It generates molecular structures conditioned on mass spectra through intermediate fingerprint representations and determined chemical formulae. A key innovation is inference-time scaling using forward fragmentation models that identify spectrum-inconsistent fragments and refine them through targeted remasking and denoising.

Result: FRIGID achieves strong baseline performance with its diffusion model and shows significant improvement with inference-time scaling. It surpasses 18% Top-1 accuracy on MassSpecGym benchmark and triples the Top-1 accuracy of leading methods on NPLIB1. The system exhibits log-linear performance scaling with increasing inference-time compute.

Conclusion: FRIGID represents a major advancement in de novo structural elucidation from mass spectra, demonstrating that inference-time scaling via forward fragmentation models can significantly boost accuracy. The log-linear performance scaling suggests a promising direction for continued improvements through increased computational resources.

Abstract: In this work, we present FRIGID, a framework with a novel diffusion language model that generates molecular structures conditioned on mass spectra via intermediate fingerprint representations and determined chemical formulae, training at the scale of hundreds of millions of unlabeled structures. We then demonstrate how forward fragmentation models enable inference-time scaling by identifying spectrum-inconsistent fragments and refining them through targeted remasking and denoising. While FRIGID already achieves strong performance with its diffusion base, inference-time scaling significantly improves its accuracy, surpassing 18% Top-1 accuracy on the challenging MassSpecGym benchmark and tripling the Top-1 accuracy of the leading methods on NPLIB1. Further empirical analyses show that FRIGID exhibits log-linear performance scaling with increasing inference-time compute, opening a promising new direction for continued improvements in de novo structural elucidation. FRIGID code is publicly available at https://github.com/coleygroup/FRIGID

</details>


### [179] [FLARE: A Data-Efficient Surrogate for Predicting Displacement Fields in Directed Energy Deposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16649)
*Kittipong Thiamchaiboonthawee,Ghadi Nehme,Ram Mohan Telikicherla,Jiawei Tian,Balaji Jayaraman,Vikas Chandan,Dhanushkodi Mariappan,Faez Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FLARE introduces a data-efficient surrogate modeling framework that predicts post-cooling displacement fields in Directed Energy Deposition (DED) manufacturing by encoding simulations as implicit neural fields and using affine weight-space reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Thermo-mechanical finite element simulations for DED are computationally expensive and complex, limiting their use for design iteration and process optimization. There's a need for efficient surrogate models to predict manufacturing outcomes.

Method: Developed a predefined-geometry DED simulation workflow using open-source finite element framework to generate dataset. FLARE encodes each simulation as an implicit neural field and regularizes neural-network weights to follow affine structure of input parameter space, enabling prediction of unseen parameter combinations through affine mixing of training examples.

Result: The method shows improved accuracy compared to baseline methods in both in-distribution and extrapolation settings on the DED benchmark. Successfully predicts post-cooling displacement fields from geometric and process parameters.

Conclusion: The affine weight-space reconstruction framework offers a promising approach for data-efficient surrogate modeling of physical fields, with demonstrated success for DED displacement prediction and potential applicability to broader physics modeling tasks.

Abstract: Directed energy deposition (DED) produces complex thermo-mechanical responses that can lead to distortion and reduced dimensional accuracy of a manufactured part. Thermo-mechanical finite element simulations are widely used to estimate these effects, but their computational cost and the complexity of accurately capturing DED physics limit their use in design iteration and process optimization. This paper introduces FLARE (Field Prediction via Linear Affine Reconstruction in wEight-space), a data-efficient surrogate modeling framework for predicting post-cooling displacement fields in DED from geometric and process parameters. We develop a predefined-geometry DED simulation workflow using an open-source finite element framework and generate a dataset of simulations with varying geometry, laser power, and deposition velocity. Each simulation provides full-field displacement, stress, strain, and temperature data throughout the manufacturing process. FLARE encodes each simulation as an implicit neural field and regularizes the corresponding neural-network weights so that they follow the affine structure of the input parameter space. This enables prediction of unseen parameter combinations by reconstructing network weights through affine mixing of training examples. On this DED benchmark, the method shows improved accuracy compared to baseline methods in both in-distribution and extrapolation settings. Although the present study focuses on DED displacement prediction, the proposed affine weight-space reconstruction framework offers a promising approach for data-efficient surrogate modeling of physical fields.

</details>


### [180] [Cross-Modal Bayesian Low-Rank Adaptation for Uncertainty-Aware Multimodal Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16657)
*Habibeh Naderi,Behrouz Haji Soleimani,Stan Matwin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CALIBER is a multimodal uncertainty-aware PEFT framework that uses Bayesian low-rank adaptation with cross-modal attention to combine text and audio while estimating predictive uncertainty in low-resource settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods are deterministic and unimodal, making them unsuitable for low-resource multimodal settings where both predictive uncertainty and cross-modal reliability are important.

Method: CALIBER extends Bayesian low-rank adaptation by conditioning the variational posterior on per-layer, token-level text-audio cross-attention. Text-derived low-rank features attend to frame-level audio embeddings to produce acoustic context, which modulates the mean and variance of a stochastic latent matrix within the rank-r adapter space.

Result: Experimental results across diverse text and audio backbones show CALIBER consistently matches or improves upon text-only Bayesian PEFT and conventional multimodal transfer-learning baselines, with token-level cross-attention yielding the most consistent gains.

Conclusion: Localized cross-modal conditioning is an effective and lightweight mechanism for uncertainty-aware multimodal adaptation, enabling efficient multimodal learning with reliable uncertainty estimation.

Abstract: Large pre-trained language models are increasingly adapted to downstream tasks using parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT), but existing PEFT methods are typically deterministic and unimodal, making them poorly suited for low-resource multimodal settings where predictive uncertainty and cross-modal reliability both matter. We introduce CALIBER (Context-Aware Low-rank Inference with Bayesian Embedding Regularization), a multimodal uncertainty-aware PEFT framework for audio-text learning. CALIBER extends Bayesian low-rank adaptation by conditioning the variational posterior in the adapter space on per-layer, token-level text-audio cross-attention. Specifically, text-derived low-rank features attend to frame-level audio embeddings to produce localized acoustic context, which then modulates the mean and variance of a compact stochastic latent matrix within the rank-$r$ adapter space. This design treats audio not only as an additional feature source, but as a contextual reliability signal that shapes both adaptation and confidence. By confining stochasticity to a low-dimensional latent component, CALIBER retains the computational efficiency and scalability of PEFT while enabling heteroscedastic multimodal uncertainty estimation. Experimental results across diverse text and audio backbones show that CALIBER consistently matches or improves upon text-only Bayesian PEFT and conventional multimodal transfer-learning baselines, with token-level cross-attention yielding the most consistent gains. Our findings demonstrate that localized cross-modal conditioning is an effective and lightweight mechanism for uncertainty-aware multimodal adaptation.

</details>


### [181] [UniCon: Unified Framework for Efficient Contrastive Alignment via Kernels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16678)
*Hangke Sui,Yuqing Wang,Minh N Do*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UniCon is a unified framework that uses kernels to achieve efficient contrastive learning with closed-form solutions, replacing slow stochastic optimization with exact updates while maintaining strong performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current contrastive learning methods for multimodal models are slow and rely on inefficient stochastic optimization. There's a need for a more efficient approach that can handle different types of alignments and encoder architectures.

Method: Introduces UniCon framework with contrastive similarity weight matrix S(γ) that enables closed-form global solutions. Uses reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHS) to provide kernelized perspective, unifying contrastive alignment and connecting it to spectral methods. Works with both linear and nonlinear encoders and supports one-to-one and many-to-many alignments.

Result: UniCon achieves substantial efficiency gains while preserving generality and strong empirical performance across synthetic, unimodal, multimodal, and zero-shot tasks. It replaces minibatch back-propagation with exact updates.

Conclusion: UniCon provides an efficient and unified framework for contrastive alignment that overcomes the computational inefficiencies of traditional contrastive learning while maintaining strong performance across various tasks.

Abstract: Contrastive objectives power state-of-the-art multimodal models, but their training remains slow, relying on long stochastic optimization. We propose a Unified Framework for Efficient Contrastive Alignment via Kernels (UniCon), which spans linear and nonlinear encoders as well as one-to-one and many-to-many alignments. At its core, UniCon introduces the contrastive similarity weight matrix $S(γ)$, which enables closed-form global solutions that provably replace minibatch back-propagation with exact updates. Through the lens of reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHS), UniCon provides a kernelized perspective that unifies contrastive alignment and reveals its connection to spectral methods. To validate the theory, we conduct experiments on synthetic, unimodal, multimodal, and zero-shot tasks, demonstrating that UniCon achieves substantial efficiency gains while preserving generality and strong empirical performance.

</details>


### [182] [DARLING: Detection Augmented Reinforcement Learning with Non-Stationary Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16684)
*Argyrios Gerogiannis,Yu-Han Huang,Venugopal V. Veeravalli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DARLING is a modular wrapper for piecewise-stationary RL that achieves near-optimal dynamic regret without prior knowledge of changes, improving state-of-the-art performance in both tabular and linear MDPs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model-free RL in non-stationary environments where reward and transition dynamics change arbitrarily is challenging, especially without prior knowledge of when changes occur. Existing methods lack optimality guarantees in both tabular and linear MDP settings.

Method: Proposes DARLING (Detection Augmented Reinforcement Learning), a modular wrapper that combines change-point detection with RL algorithms for piecewise-stationary MDPs. It works without knowledge of change points and applies to both tabular and linear MDPs.

Result: DARLING improves the best available dynamic regret bounds in both tabular and linear MDP settings under certain change-point separation and reachability conditions. It establishes the first minimax lower bounds for piecewise-stationary RL and demonstrates empirical superiority on standard benchmarks.

Conclusion: DARLING is the first nearly optimal algorithm for piecewise-stationary RL, providing strong theoretical guarantees and empirical performance across diverse non-stationary scenarios without requiring prior knowledge of change points.

Abstract: We study model-free reinforcement learning (RL) in non-stationary finite-horizon episodic Markov decision processes (MDPs) without prior knowledge of the non-stationarity. We focus on the piecewise-stationary (PS) setting, where both the reward and transition dynamics can change an arbitrary number of times. We propose Detection Augmented Reinforcement Learning (DARLING), a modular wrapper for PS-RL that applies to both tabular and linear MDPs, without knowledge of the changes. Under certain change-point separation and reachability conditions, DARLING improves the best available dynamic regret bounds in both settings and yields strong empirical performance. We further establish the first minimax lower bounds for PS-RL in tabular and linear MDPs, showing that DARLING is the first nearly optimal algorithm. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that DARLING consistently surpasses the state-of-the-art methods across diverse non-stationary scenarios.

</details>


### [183] [Graph Transformer-Based Pathway Embedding for Cancer Prognosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16685)
*Koushik Howlader,Md Tauhidul Islam,Wei Le*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PATH introduces a modulation-based gene embedding approach that starts from shared base gene representations and adapts them with patient-specific CNV/mutation signals, integrated into a graph transformer for pathway-pathway interactions, achieving state-of-the-art cancer metastasis prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current cancer progression prediction models struggle with high heterogeneity of molecular omics data. Existing hierarchical models fail to explicitly learn shared base representations for genes, limiting expressiveness and biological accuracy of pathway embeddings. Integration frameworks often use simple statistical aggregations that miss individual molecular variations.

Method: PATH (modulation-based, patient-conditioned gene embedding): starts from shared base embedding for each gene preserving stable biological identity, then dynamically adapts using patient-specific CNV and mutation signals. Integrated into graph transformer framework with pathway-guided attention to model interactions among biologically connected pathways.

Result: Achieved F1 score of 0.8766 in pancancer metastasis prediction, representing 8.8% improvement over current SOTA multi-omics benchmarks. Beyond predictive accuracy, identifies biologically meaningful pathways and reveals disease-state-specific pathway rewiring, offering insights into evolving pathway-pathway interactions in cancer progression.

Conclusion: PATH represents a paradigm shift in gene representation learning for cancer prediction by explicitly modeling both shared biological identity and patient-specific molecular variations. The approach not only improves predictive performance but also provides interpretable insights into pathway-level dynamics in cancer progression, offering a more biologically accurate framework for understanding heterogeneous cancer mechanisms.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of cancer progression remains a challenge due to the high heterogeneity of molecular omics data across patients. While biologically informed models have improved the interpretability of these predictions, a persistent limitation lies in how they encode individual genes to construct pathway representations. Existing hierarchical models typically derive gene features by directly mapping raw molecular inputs, whereas integration frameworks often rely on simple statistical aggregations of patient-level signals. These approaches often fail to explicitly learn a shared base representation for each gene, thereby limiting the expressiveness and biological accuracy of downstream pathway embeddings. To address this, we introduce PATH, a modulation-based, patient-conditioned gene embedding strategy. PATH represents a paradigm shift by starting from a shared base embedding for each gene, preserving a stable biological identity across the population, and then dynamically adapting it using patient-specific copy number variation (CNV) and mutation signals. This allows the model to capture subtle individual molecular variations while maintaining a consistent latent understanding of the gene itself. We integrate PATH into a graph transformer framework that models interactions among biologically connected pathways through pathway-guided attention. Across pancancer metastasis prediction, PATH achieves an F1 score of 0.8766, representing an 8.8 percent improvement over the current SOTA multi-omics benchmarks. Beyond superior predictive accuracy, our approach identifies biologically meaningful pathways and, crucially, reveals disease-state-specific pathway rewiring, offering new insights into the evolving pathway-pathway interactions that drive cancer progression.

</details>


### [184] [How to Approximate Inference with Subtractive Mixture Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16714)
*Lena Zellinger,Nicola Branchini,Lennert De Smet,Víctor Elvira,Nikolay Malkin,Antonio Vergari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SMMs (subtractive mixture models with negative coefficients) offer more expressivity than classical MMs but lack latent variable semantics; this work develops expectation estimators for importance sampling and learning schemes for variational inference with SMMs, addressing estimation stability and learning efficiency challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical mixture models are commonly used for approximate inference, but recently proposed subtractive mixture models with negative coefficients offer potentially greater expressivity. However, SMMs cannot use the same latent variable semantics and sampling schemes as classical MMs, creating a challenge for effectively using them in variational inference and importance sampling.

Method: The authors design several expectation estimators for importance sampling and develop learning schemes for variational inference with SMMs. They also propose methods to overcome challenges in estimation stability and learning efficiency that arise when using SMMs.

Result: The work provides practical approaches for using subtractive mixture models in approximate inference settings, including both importance sampling and variational inference. The proposed methods are empirically evaluated for distribution approximation.

Conclusion: SMMs can be effectively used for approximate inference through specially designed expectation estimators and learning schemes, overcoming their lack of latent variable semantics. The work addresses key challenges in estimation stability and learning efficiency while demonstrating SMMs' potential as more expressive alternatives to classical mixture models.

Abstract: Classical mixture models (MMs) are widely used tractable proposals for approximate inference settings such as variational inference (VI) and importance sampling (IS). Recently, mixture models with negative coefficients, called subtractive mixture models (SMMs), have been proposed as a potentially more expressive alternative. However, how to effectively use SMMs for VI and IS is still an open question as they do not provide latent variable semantics and therefore cannot use sampling schemes for classical MMs. In this work, we study how to circumvent this issue by designing several expectation estimators for IS and learning schemes for VI with SMMs, and we empirically evaluate them for distribution approximation. Finally, we discuss the additional challenges in estimation stability and learning efficiency that they carry and propose ways to overcome them. Code is available at: https://github.com/april-tools/delta-vi.

</details>


### [185] [Chronax: A Jax Library for Univariate Statistical Forecasting and Conformal Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16719)
*Xan Carey,Yash Deshmukh,Aileen Huang,Sunit Jadhav,Omkar Tekawade,Lorraine Yang,Anvesha Tiwary,Gerardo Riano,Amy Greenwald,Denizalp Goktas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chronax is a JAX-native time-series forecasting library that rethinks forecasting abstractions around functional purity, composable transformations, and accelerator-ready execution to address scalability and efficiency limitations of traditional Python-based frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing time-series forecasting libraries rely on traditional Python numerical stack with interpreter-driven execution and object-oriented abstractions, which limit composability, large-scale parallelism, and integration with modern differentiable and accelerator-oriented workflows. Current forecasting increasingly involves large collections of heterogeneous time series data, irregular covariates, and frequent retraining, placing new demands on scalability and execution efficiency.

Method: Chronax uses JAX's paradigm of pure functions and program transformations (just-in-time compilation, automatic vectorization) to design forecasting abstractions. It represents preprocessing, modeling, and multi-horizon prediction as pure JAX functions, enabling composable transformations and accelerator-ready execution across CPUs, GPUs, and TPUs.

Result: Chronax enables scalable multi-series forecasting, model-agnostic conformal uncertainty quantification, and seamless integration with modern machine learning and scientific computing pipelines by leveraging JAX's functional purity and program transformations.

Conclusion: Chronax represents a paradigm shift in time-series forecasting systems by incorporating JAX's modern computational framework, addressing the scalability and efficiency limitations of traditional approaches while enabling integration with contemporary machine learning workflows and accelerator hardware.

Abstract: Time-series forecasting is central to many scientific and industrial domains, such as energy systems, climate modeling, finance, and retail. While forecasting methods have evolved from classical statistical models to automated, and neural approaches, the surrounding software ecosystem remains anchored to the traditional Python numerical stack. Existing libraries rely on interpreter-driven execution and object-oriented abstractions, limiting composability, large-scale parallelism, and integration with modern differentiable and accelerator-oriented workflows. Meanwhile, today's forecasting increasingly involves large collections of heterogeneous time series data, irregular covariates, and frequent retraining, placing new demands on scalability and execution efficiency. JAX offers an alternative paradigm to traditional stateful numerical computation frameworks based on pure functions and program transformations such as just-in-time compilation and automatic vectorization, enabling end-to-end optimization across CPUs, GPUs, and TPUs. However, this modern paradigm has not yet been fully incorporated into the design of forecasting systems. We introduce Chronax, a JAX-native time-series forecasting library that rethinks forecasting abstractions around functional purity, composable transformations, and accelerator-ready execution. By representing preprocessing, modeling, and multi-horizon prediction as pure JAX functions, Chronax enables scalable multi-series forecasting, model-agnostic conformal uncertainty quantification, and seamless integration with modern machine learning and scientific computing pipelines.

</details>


### [186] [Late Fusion Neural Operators for Extrapolation Across Parameter Space in Partial Differential Equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16721)
*Eva van Tegelen,Taniya Kapoor,George A. K. van Voorn,Peter van Heijster,Ioannis N. Athanasiadis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Late Fusion Neural Operator disentangles state dynamics from parameter effects to improve PDE operator generalization across parameter shifts, outperforming existing methods by 72% average RMSE reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural operators struggle with distribution shifts caused by varying physical parameters in PDE systems, especially when state and parameter representations are entangled, limiting generalization to unseen parameter regimes.

Method: Proposes Late Fusion Neural Operator that separates learning of latent state representations (using neural operators) from parameter incorporation (using sparse regression), creating a disentangled architecture for better generalization.

Result: Outperforms Fourier Neural Operator and CAPE-FNO across four PDE benchmarks (advection, Burgers, 1D/2D reaction-diffusion), achieving 72.9% average RMSE reduction in-domain and 71.8% out-domain compared to second-best methods.

Conclusion: Late Fusion Neural Operator demonstrates superior generalization across both in-domain and out-domain parameter regimes by disentangling state dynamics from parameter effects, addressing the fundamental challenge of distribution shifts in PDE-based neural operators.

Abstract: Developing neural operators that accurately predict the behavior of systems governed by partial differential equations (PDEs) across unseen parameter regimes is crucial for robust generalization in scientific and engineering applications. In practical applications, variations in physical parameters induce distribution shifts between training and prediction regimes, making extrapolation a central challenge. As a result, the way parameters are incorporated into neural operator models plays a key role in their ability to generalize, particularly when state and parameter representations are entangled. In this work, we introduce the Late Fusion Neural Operator, an architecture that disentangles learning state dynamics from parameter effects, improving predictive performance both within and beyond the training distribution. Our approach combines neural operators for learning latent state representations with sparse regression to incorporate parameter information in a structured manner. Across four benchmark PDEs including advection, Burgers, and both 1D and 2D reaction-diffusion equations, the proposed method consistently outperforms Fourier Neural Operator and CAPE-FNO. Late Fusion Neural Operators achieve consistently the best performance in all experiments, with an average RMSE reduction of 72.9% in-domain and 71.8% out-domain compared to the second-best method. These results demonstrate strong generalization across both in-domain and out-domain parameter regimes.

</details>


### [187] [Neuroscience Inspired Graph Operators Towards Edge-Deployable Virtual Sensing for Irregular Geometries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16722)
*William Howes,Farid Ahmed,Kazuma Kobayashi,Souvik Chakraborty,Syed Bahauddin Alam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VS-GNO is a novel neural operator combining spectral-spatial convolution with variable spiking neurons to enable energy-efficient edge deployment for sparse-to-dense physics reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time virtual sensing for engineering systems faces challenges: sparse-to-dense reconstruction, complex multiphysics, irregular geometries, and strict latency/energy constraints for edge deployment. Current neural operators don't address power consumption well, and existing spiking neuron models cause severe performance degradation for regression tasks.

Method: The Variable Spiking Graph Neural Operator (VS-GNO) integrates sophisticated spectral-spatial convolutional analysis with Variable Spiking Neurons (VSN) and an energy-error balance loss function. The architecture handles complex geometries and physics while optimizing for energy efficiency through sparsity.

Result: With a non-spiking baseline error of 0.4%, VS-GNO achieves 0.71% error with 15% spiking (spectral-only) and 1.04% error with 24.5% spiking (full form). This shows good performance-energy trade-offs for edge deployment.

Conclusion: VS-GNO represents a promising step toward energy-efficient, edge-deployable neural operators for real-time sparse-to-dense virtual sensing in complex engineering environments, effectively balancing reconstruction accuracy with power consumption through variable spiking neurons.

Abstract: Predicting full-field physics through the real-time virtual sensing of engineering systems can enhance limited physical sensors but often requires sparse-to-dense reconstruction, complex multiphysics, and highly irregular geometries as well as strict latency and energy constraints for edge-deployability. Neural operators have been presented as a potential candidate for such applications but few architectures exist that explicitly address power consumption. Spiking neuron integration can provide a potential solution when integrated on neuromorphic hardware but the current existing neuron models result in severe performance degradation towards regression-based virtual sensing. To address the performance concerns and edge-constraints, we present the Variable Spiking Graph Neural Operator (VS-GNO) which integrates a sophisticated spectral-spatial convolutional analysis and a previously developed Variable Spiking Neuron (VSN) and energy-error balance loss function. With a non-spiking $L_2$ error baseline of $0.4\%$, VS-GNO can provide a reconstruction error of $0.71\%$ with $15\%$ average spiking in its spectral-only form and $1.04\%$ with $24.5\%$ spiking in its entire form. These results position VS-GNO as a promising step towards energy-efficient, edge-deployable neural operators for real-time sparse-to-dense virtual sensing in complex, highly irregular engineering environments.

</details>


### [188] [LLM-Extracted Covariates for Clinical Causal Inference: Rethinking Integration Strategies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16763)
*Lei Liu,Jialin Chen,Kathy Macropol*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM-extracted text covariates significantly improve causal inference in EHR data by reducing bias from unmeasured confounding, with propensity score augmentation being the most effective integration strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal inference from EHR data is fundamentally limited by unmeasured confounding, as critical clinical states like frailty, goals of care, and mental status are only documented in free-text notes and absent from structured data.

Method: The study compares seven covariate-integration strategies using MIMIC-IV database with 21,859 sepsis patients to estimate the effect of early vasopressor initiation on 28-day mortality. Approaches include tabular-only baselines, traditional NLP representations, and three LLM-augmented methods, with semi-synthetic experiments for validation.

Result: Directly augmenting propensity score models with LLM covariates achieves best performance, reducing estimation bias from 0.0143 to 0.0003. On real data, incorporating LLM-extracted covariates reduces estimated treatment effect from 0.055 to 0.027, consistent with clinical trial evidence, and doubly robust estimator yields 0.019.

Conclusion: LLM-extracted text covariates substantially improve causal estimation in critical care by addressing unmeasured confounding, with propensity score augmentation being the most effective integration strategy. The study provides practical guidance on when and how to integrate text-derived covariates into causal inference pipelines.

Abstract: Causal inference from electronic health records (EHR) is fundamentally limited by unmeasured confounding: critical clinical states such as frailty, goals of care, and mental status are documented in free-text notes but absent from structured data. Large language models can extract these latent confounders as interpretable, structured covariates, yet how to effectively integrate them into causal estimation pipelines has not been systematically studied. Using the MIMIC-IV database with 21,859 sepsis patients, we compare seven covariate-integration strategies for estimating the effect of early vasopressor initiation on 28-day mortality, spanning tabular-only baselines, traditional NLP representations, and three LLM-augmented approaches. A central finding is that not all integration strategies are equally effective: directly augmenting the propensity score model with LLM covariates achieves the best performance, while dual-caliper matching on text-derived categorical distances restricts the donor pool and degrades estimation. In semi-synthetic experiments with known ground-truth effects, LLM-augmented propensity scores reduce estimation bias from 0.0143 to 0.0003 relative to tabular-only methods, and this advantage persists under substantial simulated extraction error. On real data, incorporating LLM-extracted covariates reduces the estimated treatment effect from 0.055 to 0.027, directionally consistent with the CLOVERS randomized trial, and a doubly robust estimator yielding 0.019 confirms the robustness of this finding. Our results offer practical guidance on when and how text-derived covariates improve causal estimation in critical care.

</details>


### [189] [Representation Before Training: A Fixed-Budget Benchmark for Generative Medical Event Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16775)
*Inhyeok Lee,Luke Solo,Michael C. Burkhart,Bashar Ramadan,William F. Parker,Brett K. Beaulieu-Jones*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper systematically evaluates how different clinical event tokenization representations affect prediction performance in medical event models, finding that fused code-value tokenization and certain temporal encodings improve performance while CLIF remapping preserves performance with a smaller, interpretable token set.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While generative medical event model predictions are bounded by how clinical events are tokenized, input representation decisions are rarely isolated from other system and architectural choices. The authors aim to systematically evaluate how different representation decisions affect downstream prediction performance under a shared training budget.

Method: The authors train 28 matched transformers on MIMIC-IV with a shared one-epoch pretraining budget and evaluate them on 30 clinical outcomes across three experiments: (1) quantization granularity, reference-range anchoring, and code-value fusion; (2) value encoding methods crossed with temporal encoding methods; and (3) native MIMIC codes vs. CLIF-remapped codes with compression-preserving perturbation arms.

Result: Fused code-value tokenization improved mortality AUROC from 0.891 to 0.915, hospital length-of-stay AUROC from 0.763 to 0.788, and mean regression Spearman rho from 0.414 to 0.494. Event order only and admission-relative RoPE temporal encodings match or exceed time tokens while shortening sequences by 11%. CLIF remapping preserves performance while yielding a smaller, clinically interpretable token set. Finer quantization, reference-range anchoring, and soft discretization help selectively, while code-normalized xVal underperforms.

Conclusion: Input representation decisions significantly impact medical event model performance. Code-value fusion improves predictive power, efficient temporal encodings can replace explicit time tokens, and standardized code systems like CLIF can reduce vocabulary size while maintaining performance for multi-site compatibility.

Abstract: Every prediction from a generative medical event model is bounded by how clinical events are tokenized, yet input representation is rarely isolated from other system and architectural choices. We evaluate how representation decisions affect downstream prediction after a shared one-epoch pretraining budget. We train 28 matched transformers on MIMIC-IV and evaluate them on 30 clinical outcomes in three experiments: (1) quantization granularity, reference-range anchoring, and code-value fusion; (2) value encoding (hard bins, soft discretization, code-normalized xVal) crossed with temporal encoding (event order, time tokens, admission-relative RoPE); and (3) native MIMIC laboratory/vital codes versus the Common Longitudinal ICU Format (CLIF)-remapped laboratory/vital codes with compression-preserving perturbation arms. In Experiment 1, fused code-value tokenization improves mortality AUROC from 0.891 to 0.915 (BH-adjusted p < 0.001), hospital length-of-stay AUROC from 0.763 to 0.788 (BH-adjusted p < 0.001), and, for the decile fused-vs-unfused comparison, mean regression Spearman rho across the 13 regression outcomes from 0.414 to 0.494. Across the three temporal encodings, event order only and admission-relative RoPE match or exceed inserting time tokens on average while shortening sequences by 11%. CLIF remapping preserves downstream performance in our single-site setting while yielding a smaller, clinically interpretable token set compatible with multi-site use. Finer-than-decile quantization, reference-range anchoring, and soft discretization help in selective outcomes, while code-normalized xVal remains well below the discrete and soft families, consistent with near-median suppression that persists after the affine variant.

</details>


### [190] [Federation over Text: Insight Sharing for Multi-Agent Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16778)
*Dixi Yao,Tahseen Rabbani,Tian Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FoT is a federated learning-like framework where multiple LLM agents share reasoning traces to build a collective insight library, improving task performance while reducing computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-powered agents lack mechanisms to transfer learned skills between agents and typically reason from scratch for each new problem instance.

Method: A federated learning framework where agents independently perform local reasoning on their specific tasks, share reasoning traces with a central server, which aggregates and distills them into a cross-task insight library for all agents to use.

Result: FoT improves average accuracies by 24% while reducing reasoning tokens by 28% across mathematical problem solving and cross-domain collaboration, and generates insights covering over 90% of major contributions in subsequent research papers.

Conclusion: FoT enables efficient knowledge transfer between LLM agents without gradient optimization, significantly improving reasoning performance and efficiency across diverse applications.

Abstract: LLM-powered agents often reason from scratch when presented with a new problem instance and lack automatic mechanisms to transfer learned skills to other agents. We propose a federated learning-like framework, Federation over Text (FoT), that enables multiple agents solving different tasks to collectively generate a shared library of metacognitive insights by iteratively federating their local reasoning processes. Instead of federation over gradients (e.g., as in distributed training), FoT operates at the semantic level without any gradient optimization or supervision signal. Iteratively, each agent does local thinking and self-improvement on their specific tasks independently, and shares reasoning traces with a central server, which aggregates and distills them into a cross-task (and cross-domain) insight library that existing and future agents can leverage to improve performance on related tasks. Experiments show that FoT improves reasoning effectiveness and efficiency across a wide range of challenging applications, including mathematical problem solving, cross-domain collaboration, and machine learning research insight discovery. Specifically, it improves average accuracies of downstream tasks by 24% while reducing the reasoning tokens by 28% across the first two applications. In the research insight discovery application, FoT is able to generate insights that cover over 90% of the major contributions in the subsequent papers.

</details>


### [191] [Continuous Limits of Coupled Flows in Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16801)
*Zilin Li,Weiwei Xu,Xuchun Tong,Xuanbo Lu,Xuanqi Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decentralized learning dynamics on continuous manifolds converge to overdamped Langevin SDEs, with weights aligning to principal eigenspace and orthogonally disentangled features emerging at stationary limit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern representation learning relies heavily on global error signals, but decentralized algorithms driven by local interactions offer a distributed alternative. However, the macroscopic convergence properties of these discrete dynamics on continuous data manifolds remain theoretically unresolved and suffer from parameter explosion.

Method: 1) Formalize decentralized learning as coupled slow-fast dynamical system on Riemannian manifolds. 2) Use measure-theoretic limits to prove discrete spatial transitions converge to overdamped Langevin SDE. 3) Apply Itô-Poisson resolvent and stochastic LaSalle's Invariance Principle to show weights avoid divergence and align with principal eigenspace. 4) Construct joint Lyapunov functional for fully coupled spatial-parametric flow to prove global dissipativity.

Result: The representation weights unconditionally avoid divergence and align strictly with the principal eigenspace of the spatial measure. Orthogonally disentangled, linearly separable features emerge spontaneously at the stationary limit. The framework provides global dissipativity guarantees.

Conclusion: The framework bridges discrete algorithms with continuous stochastic analysis, providing a formal theoretical baseline for decentralized representation learning by establishing rigorous convergence properties and feature emergence guarantees.

Abstract: While modern representation learning relies heavily on global error signals, decentralized algorithms driven by local interactions offer a fundamental distributed alternative. However, the macroscopic convergence properties of these discrete dynamics on continuous data manifolds remain theoretically unresolved, notoriously suffering from parameter explosion. We bridge this gap by formalizing decentralized learning as a coupled slow-fast dynamical system on Riemannian manifolds. First, using measure-theoretic limits, we prove that the discrete spatial transitions converge uniformly to an overdamped Langevin stochastic differential equation. Second, via the Itô-Poisson resolvent and a stochastic extension of LaSalle's Invariance Principle, we establish that the representation weights unconditionally avoid divergence and align strictly with the principal eigenspace of the spatial measure. Finally, we construct a joint Lyapunov functional for the fully coupled spatial-parametric flow. This proves global dissipativity and demonstrates that orthogonally disentangled, linearly separable features emerge spontaneously at the stationary limit. Our framework bridges discrete algorithms with continuous stochastic analysis, providing a formal theoretical baseline for decentralized representation learning.

</details>


### [192] [AutoOR: Scalably Post-training LLMs to Autoformalize Operations Research Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16804)
*Sumeet Ramesh Motwani,Chuan Du,Aleksander Petrov,Christopher Davis,Philip Torr,Antonio Papania-Davis,Weishi Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AutoOR is an AI system that trains LLMs to automatically convert natural language optimization problem descriptions into solver-ready formulations using synthetic data generation and reinforcement learning, achieving state-of-the-art results on OR benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optimization problems are crucial in industrial applications (manufacturing, logistics, scheduling), but translating natural language descriptions into solver-ready formulations requires specialized OR expertise, creating scalability challenges in real-world deployment.

Method: AutoOR uses a scalable synthetic data generation pipeline to create verified training data from standard optimization forms, then employs reinforcement learning with solver execution feedback as the reward signal. A curriculum RL strategy is introduced for challenging non-linear problems.

Result: AutoOR applied to an 8B parameter model achieves state-of-the-art or competitive results across six established OR benchmarks, matching significantly larger frontier models. For difficult non-linear problems where frontier models score near 0%, AutoOR's curriculum RL makes this class tractable.

Conclusion: AutoOR demonstrates that AI methods can significantly accelerate industrial decision-making by automating the formalization of optimization problems, making OR expertise more scalable and accessible across linear, mixed-integer, and non-linear problem categories.

Abstract: Optimization problems are central to decision-making in manufacturing, logistics, scheduling, and other industrial settings. Translating complicated descriptions of these problems into solver-ready formulations requires specialized operations research (OR) expertise, making it hard to scale. We present AutoOR, a scalable synthetic data generation and reinforcement learning pipeline that trains LLMs to autoformalize optimization problems specified in natural language across linear, mixed-integer, and non-linear categories. AutoOR generates verified training data from standard optimization forms and uses solver execution feedback as the reward signal for RL post-training. AutoOR applied to an 8B model achieves state-of-the-art or competitive results across six established OR benchmarks, matching significantly larger frontier models. For a non-linear problem class involving physical dynamics, where frontier models score near 0%, we introduce a curriculum RL strategy that bootstraps from limited initial training data to make this class tractable for post-training. We believe that methods such as AutoOR can significantly accelerate industrial decision-making with AI.

</details>


### [193] [Self-Reinforcing Controllable Synthesis of Rare Relational Data via Bayesian Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16817)
*Chongsheng Zhang,Hao Wang,Zelong Yu,Esteban Garces Arias,Julian Rodemann,Zhanshuo Zhang,Qilong Li,Gaojuan Fan,Krikamol Muandet,Christian Heumann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RDDG is an LLM-based framework that generates high-quality relational tabular data for imbalanced classification using progressive chain-of-thought reasoning and self-reinforcing feedback mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imbalanced data is common in real-world applications, and while LLMs excel at text generation, their application to relational/structured tabular data synthesis remains underexplored. Existing approaches lack effective feedback mechanisms to guide LLMs in continuously optimizing generated data quality throughout the synthesis process.

Method: RDDG employs a unified in-context learning framework with progressive chain-of-thought steps: 1) Core set selection to identify representative samples, 2) In-context learning to discover patterns and correlations among attributes, 3) Tabular data generation while preserving constraints, and 4) A self-reinforcing feedback mechanism that provides automatic quality assessments for continuous optimization.

Result: Experimental results on multiple real and synthetic datasets show that RDDG outperforms existing approaches in both data fidelity and downstream imbalanced classification performance.

Conclusion: RDDG successfully addresses the limitations of existing methods by combining LLMs with dynamic feedback mechanisms for high-quality relational tabular data synthesis, effectively enhancing imbalanced classification performance. The framework demonstrates the potential of LLMs for structured data generation beyond traditional text applications.

Abstract: Imbalanced data is commonly present in real-world applications. While data synthesis can effectively mitigate the data scarcity problem of rare-classes, and LLMs have revolutionized text generation, the application of LLMs to relational/structured tabular data synthesis remains underexplored. Moreover, existing approaches lack an effective feedback mechanism that can guide LLMs towards continuously optimizing the quality of the generated data throughout the synthesis process. In this work, we propose RDDG, Relational Data generator with Dynamic Guidance, which is a unified in-context learning framework that employs progressive chain-of-thought (CoT) steps to generate tabular data for enhancing downstream imbalanced classification performance. RDDG first uses core set selection to identify representative samples from the original data, then utilizes in-context learning to discover the inherent patterns and correlations among attributes within the core set, and subsequently generates tabular data while preserving the aforementioned constraints. More importantly, it incorporates a self-reinforcing feedback mechanism that provides automatic assessments on the quality of the generated data, enabling continuous quality optimization throughout the generation process. Experimental results on multiple real and synthetic datasets demonstrate that RDDG outperforms existing approaches in both data fidelity and downstream imbalanced classification performance. We make our code available at https://github.com/cszhangLMU/RDDG.

</details>


### [194] [R&F-Inventory: A Large-Scale Dataset for Monotonic Inventory Estimation in Reach and Frequency Advertising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16821)
*Yunshan Peng,Ji Wu,Wentao Bai,Yunke Bai,Jinan Pang,Wenzheng Shu,Yanxiang Zeng,Xialong Liu,Peng Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces and releases a large-scale Reach and Frequency (R&F) contract inventory estimation dataset to address the lack of proper data for studying budget-performance curves in brand advertising.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing advertising datasets are based on independent samples and lack characterization of the core "budget-performance curve" structure for R&F contracts, which emphasize controllable delivery under targeting, scheduling, and frequency constraints. Advertisers need to view UV and PV change curves at different budget levels in real time when creating contracts.

Method: The paper proposes a dataset using R&F contract context (targeting-scheduling-frequency control) as basic context, providing UV and PV observations at multiple budget points within the same context to form complete budget-performance curves. It includes time-window-based frequency control mechanisms and satisfies monotonicity and diminishing marginal returns characteristics.

Result: The dataset includes theoretical maximum exposure ceiling for consistency checking, and the paper defines two benchmark tasks: single-point performance prediction and budget-performance curve reconstruction. It provides reproducible baseline methods and evaluation protocols, with code available on GitHub.

Conclusion: This dataset supports systematic research on structural constraint learning, monotonic regression, curve consistency modeling, and R&F contract planning, filling a critical gap in advertising research infrastructure.

Abstract: Reach and Frequency (R&F) contract advertising is an important form of widely used brand advertising. Unlike performance advertising, R&F contracts emphasize controllable delivery of UV and PV under given targeting, scheduling, and frequency control constraints. In practical systems, advertisers typically need to view the UV, PV change curves at different budget levels in real time when creating an R&F contract. However, most existing publicly available advertising datasets are based on independent samples, lacking a characterization of the core structure of the "budget-performance curve" (including UV and PV) in R&F contracts.This paper proposes and releases a large-scale R&F contract inventory estimation dataset. This dataset uses the R&F contract context consisting of "targeting-scheduling-frequency control" as the basic context, providing observations of UV and PV corresponding to multiple budget points within the same context, thus forming a complete budget-performance curve. The dataset explicitly includes a time-window-based frequency control mechanism (e.g.,"no more than 3 times within 5 days") and naturally satisfies the monotonicity and diminishing marginal returns characteristics in the budget and scheduling dimensions. We further derive the theoretical maximum exposure ceiling and use it as a consistency check to evaluate data quality and the feasibility of model predictions. Using this data set, this paper defines two standardized benchmark tasks: single-point performance prediction and reconstruction of budget-performance curves, and provides a set of reproducible baseline methods and evaluation protocols. This dataset can support systematic research on problems such as structural constraint learning, monotonic regression, curve consistency modeling, and R&F contract planning.The code for our experiments can be found at https://github.com/pengyunshan/RF-Inventory.

</details>


### [195] [The Illusion of Certainty: Decoupling Capability and Calibration in On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16830)
*Jiaxin Zhang,Xiangyu Peng,Qinglin Chen,Qinyuan Ye,Caiming Xiong,Chien-Sheng Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CaOPD addresses the problem of overconfidence in on-policy distillation by replacing teacher-conditioned confidence with empirical student confidence estimates, achieving Pareto-optimal calibration while maintaining task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-policy distillation (OPD) improves task accuracy but systematically causes severe overconfidence in language models. This occurs due to an information mismatch: teachers use privileged training context while deployed models must report confidence using only deployment-time information.

Method: Proposes CaOPD (calibration-aware OPD), which: 1) estimates empirical confidence from model rollouts, 2) replaces self-reported confidence with student-grounded targets, and 3) distills revised responses through the same self-distillation pipeline.

Result: CaOPD achieves Pareto-optimal calibration (balancing accuracy and calibration) while maintaining competitive capability. It generalizes robustly under out-of-distribution and continual learning scenarios across various models and domains.

Conclusion: Capability distillation does not imply calibrated confidence - confidence should be treated as an essential objective in post-training. The proposed CaOPD framework successfully addresses the calibration problem in OPD while preserving task performance.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) is an increasingly important paradigm for post-training language models. However, we identify a pervasive Scaling Law of Miscalibration: while OPD effectively improves task accuracy, it systematically traps models in severe overconfidence. We trace this failure to an information mismatch: teacher supervision is formed under privileged context available during training, whereas the deployed model must report confidence using only deployment-time information. We formalize this perspective theoretically, showing that teacher-conditioned success is generally not a valid target for deployment-time confidence and that helpful privileged context induces entropy collapse and a systematic optimism bias. To address this, we propose a calibration-aware OPD framework, CaOPD, that estimates empirical confidence from model rollouts, replaces self-reported confidence with this student-grounded target, and distills the revised response through the same self-distillation pipeline. Experiments across various models and domains show that CaOPD achieves Pareto-optimal calibration while maintaining competitive capability, generalizing robustly under out-of-distribution and continual learning. Our findings highlight that capability distillation does not imply calibrated confidence, and that confidence should be treated as an essential objective in post-training. Code: https://github.com/SalesforceAIResearch/CaOPD

</details>


### [196] [Applications of deep generative models to DNA reaction kinetics and to cryogenic electron microscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16851)
*Chenwei Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dissertation demonstrating how deep generative models (VAEs, GANs, U-Nets) with domain knowledge integration advance biological analysis in DNA reaction kinetics and cryo-EM structural biology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The dissertation aims to address challenging biological problems by integrating domain knowledge with deep learning, specifically focusing on DNA reaction kinetics and cryo-EM structural analysis, where traditional methods may lack interpretability or face resolution limitations.

Method: 1. For DNA kinetics: ViDa framework using VAEs with geometric scattering transforms to generate biophysically-plausible embeddings.
2. For cryo-EM: Comprehensive benchmarking of DL methods, Struc2mapGAN (GAN for synthetic density map generation), and CryoSAMU (multimodal U-Net with protein language model embeddings via cross-attention).

Result: 1. ViDa successfully generates reduced 2D embeddings visualizing DNA hybridization and strand displacement, preserving structure and clustering trajectories into interpretable reaction pathways.
2. Improved evaluation metrics and practical guidance for cryo-EM, synthetic high-fidelity density maps from structures, and enhanced intermediate-resolution maps via multimodal integration.

Conclusion: Deep generative models with domain knowledge integration can significantly advance biological interpretation, providing mechanistic insights in DNA reactions and improving cryo-EM density map analysis and protein structure modeling capabilities.

Abstract: This dissertation explores how deep generative models can advance the analysis of challenging biological problems by integrating domain knowledge with deep learning. It focuses on two areas: DNA reaction kinetics and cryogenic electron microscopy (cryo-EM). In the first part, we present ViDa, a biophysics-informed framework leveraging variational autoencoders (VAEs) and geometric scattering transforms to generate biophysically-plausible embeddings of DNA reaction kinetics simulations. These embeddings are reduced to a two-dimensional space to visualize DNA hybridization and toehold-mediated strand displacement reactions. ViDa preserves structure and clusters trajectory ensembles into reaction pathways, making simulation results more interpretable and revealing new mechanistic insights. In the second part, we address key challenges in cryo-EM density map interpretation and protein structure modeling. We provide a comprehensive review and benchmarking of deep learning methods for atomic model building, with improved evaluation metrics and practical guidance. We then present Struc2mapGAN, a generative adversarial network that synthesizes high-fidelity experimental-like cryo-EM density maps from protein structures. Finally, we present CryoSAMU, a structure-aware multimodal U-Net that enhances intermediate-resolution cryo-EM maps by integrating density features with structural embeddings from protein language models via cross-attention. Overall, these contributions demonstrate the potential of deep generative models to interpret DNA reaction mechanisms and advance cryo-EM density map analysis and protein structure modeling.

</details>


### [197] [CCAR: Intrinsic Robustness as an Emergent Geometric Property](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16861)
*Akash Samanta,Manish Pratap Singh,Debasis Chaudhuri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CCAR is a regularization method that imposes block-diagonal structure on feature representations to create geometrically organized subspaces for each class, improving robustness without adversarial training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard supervised learning produces entangled and brittle feature representations that are agnostic to internal geometry, making models vulnerable to noise and adversarial perturbations.

Method: Class-Conditional Activation Regularization (CCAR) explicitly engineers the feature space by imposing a block-diagonal structure via soft inductive bias, shaping latent representations to confine class energy to orthogonal subspaces.

Result: Empirical results show CCAR significantly outperforms baselines on label noise and input corruption benchmarks, demonstrating that robustness emerges from well-engineered feature spaces.

Conclusion: Robustness is an emergent property of geometrically structured feature spaces, and CCAR provides a principled approach to create intrinsic geometric scaffolds that naturally filter noise and adversarial perturbations without explicit adversarial training.

Abstract: Standard supervised learning optimizes for predictive accuracy but remains agnostic to the internal geometry of learned features, often yielding representations that are entangled and brittle. We propose Class-Conditional Activation Regularization (CCAR) to explicitly engineer the feature space, imposing a block-diagonal structure via a soft inductive bias. By shaping the latent representation to confine class energy to orthogonal subspaces, we create an intrinsic geometric scaffold that naturally filters noise and adversarial perturbations. We provide theoretical analysis linking this structural constraint to the maximization of the Fisher Discriminant Ratio, establishing a formal connection between geometric disentanglement and algorithmic stability. Empirically, this approach demonstrates that robustness is an emergent property of a well-engineered feature space, significantly outperforming baselines on label noise and input corruption benchmarks.

</details>


### [198] [Learning to Trade Like an Expert: Cognitive Fine-Tuning for Stable Financial Reasoning in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16862)
*Yuchen Pan,Soung Chang Liew*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework for training and evaluating LLMs as autonomous trading agents using curated multiple-choice questions and chronological trading simulations


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent deployments of LLMs as autonomous trading agents raise questions about whether financial decision-making competence generalizes beyond specific market patterns and how it should be trained/evaluated in noisy markets lacking ground truth.

Method: Proposed framework includes: (1) curated MCQ dataset from classic textbooks and historical markets, verified by AI committee, enriched with reasoning traces, and augmented to reduce shortcut learning; (2) two-stage evaluation protocol combining test-set evaluation with MCQ-based chronological trading simulation.

Result: Extensive evaluations across market regimes show open models trained with this framework exhibit competitive, risk-aware behavior over time, outperform open-source baselines, and approach frontier-model performance at smaller scale.

Conclusion: The framework provides structured approach for training/evaluating LLM trading agents; released dataset and evaluation framework support further research in this domain.

Abstract: Recent deployments of large language models (LLMs) as autonomous trading agents raise questions about whether financial decision-making competence generalizes beyond specific market patterns and how it should be trained and evaluated in noisy markets lacking ground truth. We propose a structured framework for training and evaluating such models. Central to our approach is a curated, multiple-choice question (MCQ) dataset derived from classic textbooks and historical markets, verified by an AI committee, enriched with structured reasoning traces, and augmented to reduce shortcut learning. To evaluate whether performance on isolated MCQs generalizes to real-world trading, we introduce a two-stage protocol combining test-set evaluation with an MCQ-based chronological trading simulation. Extensive evaluations across market regimes provide statistically robust evidence that open models trained with our framework exhibit competitive, risk-aware behavior over time, outperform open-source baselines, and approach frontier-model performance at smaller scale. We release the dataset and evaluation framework to support further research.

</details>


### [199] [Untrained CNNs Match Backpropagation at V1: A Systematic RSA Comparison of Four Learning Rules Against Human fMRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16875)
*Nils Leutenegger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Supervised learning objectives (BP) drive late visual cortex alignment (LOC/IT), while early visual alignment (V1/V2) is determined by architecture, not learning rules - even untrained networks match trained ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether the choice of learning rule for training neural networks affects how well their internal representations align with human visual cortex representations across different brain regions.

Method: Systematic comparison of four learning rules (BP, FA, PC, STDP) on identical CNN architectures, evaluated against human fMRI data from THINGS-fMRI dataset (720 stimuli, 3 subjects) using Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA), including an untrained random-weights baseline.

Result: Early visual alignment (V1/V2) is architecture-driven: untrained CNN achieves rho=0.071, statistically indistinguishable from BP (rho=0.072, p=0.43). Learning rules differentiate at higher visual areas: BP dominates at LOC/IT, and PC achieves IT alignment statistically indistinguishable from BP (p=0.18). FA consistently impairs representations below random baseline at V1.

Conclusion: The relationship between learning rules and cortical alignment is region-specific: architecture determines early visual alignment, while supervised learning objectives drive late visual alignment in higher cortical areas.

Abstract: A central question in computational neuroscience is whether the learning rule used to train a neural network determines how well its internal representations align with those of the human visual cortex. We present a systematic comparison of four learning rules -- backpropagation (BP), feedback alignment (FA), predictive coding (PC), and spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) -- applied to identical convolutional architectures and evaluated against human fMRI data from the THINGS-fMRI dataset (720 stimuli, 3 subjects) using Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA). Crucially, we include an untrained random-weights baseline that reveals the dominant role of architecture. We find that early visual alignment (V1/V2) is primarily architecture-driven: an untrained CNN achieves rho = 0.071, statistically indistinguishable from BP (rho = 0.072, p = 0.43). Learning rules only differentiate at higher visual areas: BP dominates at LOC/IT, and PC with local Hebbian updates achieves IT alignment statistically indistinguishable from BP (p = 0.18). FA consistently impairs representations below the random baseline at V1. Partial RSA confirms all effects survive pixel-similarity control. These results demonstrate that the relationship between learning rules and cortical alignment is region-specific: architecture determines early alignment, while supervised objectives drive late alignment.

</details>


### [200] [OC-Distill: Ontology-aware Contrastive Learning with Cross-Modal Distillation for ICU Risk Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16878)
*Zhongyuan Liang,Junhyung Jo,Hyang-Jung Lee,Sang Kyu Kim,Irene Y. Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OC-Distill: A two-stage multimodal framework that uses ontology-aware contrastive learning and cross-modal distillation to improve ICU clinical deterioration prediction while requiring only vital signs at inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early prediction of severe clinical deterioration and remaining length of stay in ICU is crucial for timely intervention and resource allocation. Existing methods have limitations: contrastive pretraining treats all patients as equally strong negatives, ignoring clinically meaningful similarities, and downstream fine-tuning ignores complementary modalities like clinical notes which provide rich contextual information.

Method: OC-Distill is a two-stage framework: 1) Ontology-aware contrastive pretraining that exploits ICD hierarchy to quantify patient similarity and learn clinically grounded representations, 2) Fine-tuning via cross-modal knowledge distillation to transfer complementary information from clinical notes into the model. The system only requires vital signs at inference despite using multimodal supervision during training.

Result: The method demonstrates improved label efficiency and achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple ICU prediction tasks on MIMIC dataset among methods that use only vital signs at inference.

Conclusion: OC-Distill successfully addresses limitations of existing methods by incorporating clinically meaningful similarity measures through ICD hierarchy and transferring multimodal information through distillation, resulting in better prediction performance while maintaining practical deployment requirements.

Abstract: Early prediction of severe clinical deterioration and remaining length of stay can enable timely intervention and better resource allocation in high-acuity settings such as the ICU. This has driven the development of machine learning models that leverage continuous streams of vital signs and other physiological signals for real-time risk prediction. Despite their promise, existing methods have important limitations. Contrastive pretraining treats all patients as equally strong negatives, failing to capture clinically meaningful similarity between patients with related diagnoses. Meanwhile, downstream fine-tuning typically ignores complementary modalities such as clinical notes, which provide rich contextual information unavailable in physiological signals alone. To address these challenges, we propose OC-Distill, a two-stage framework that leverages multimodal supervision during training while requiring only vital signs at inference. In the first stage, we introduce an ontology-aware contrastive objective that exploits the ICD hierarchy to quantify patient similarity and learn clinically grounded representations. In the second stage, we fine-tune the pretrained encoder via cross-modal knowledge distillation, transferring complementary information from clinical notes into the model. Across multiple ICU prediction tasks on MIMIC, OC-Distill demonstrates improved label efficiency and achieves state-of-the-art performance among methods that use only vital signs at inference.

</details>


### [201] [SinkRouter: Sink-Aware Routing for Efficient Long-Context Decoding in Large Language and Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16883)
*Junnan Liu,Xinyan Liu,Peifeng Gao,Zhaobo Qi,Beichen Zhang,Weigang Zhang,Antoni Bert Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SinkRouter is a training-free selective routing framework that detects attention sink signals to skip near-zero output computations, achieving up to 2.03x decoding speedup for long-context LLMs/LMMs while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing attention acceleration methods for long-context decoding are memory-bound and trade efficiency for accuracy through heuristic pruning that discards useful information. Current approaches insufficiently understand the attention sink phenomenon and tend to indiscriminately preserve high-scoring tokens or treat early tokens as indispensable anchors.

Method: The authors analyze attention sinks as stable, reachable error-controllable fixed points constructed during training. Based on this insight, they propose SinkRouter - a training-free selective routing framework that detects sink signals and skips computations that would produce near-zero output. They implement this with a hardware-aware Triton kernel using block-level branching and Split-K parallelism.

Result: Extensive evaluation on diverse long-context benchmarks (LongBench, InfiniteBench, CVBench, MileBench, MMVP) using text-only and multimodal models (Llama-3.1-8B, Llama-3.1-70B, Yi-9B-200K, LLaVA-1.5-7B, LLaVA-1.5-13B) shows consistent decoding efficiency improvements while maintaining competitive accuracy, reaching 2.03x speedup with a 512K context.

Conclusion: SinkRouter provides an effective solution to the memory-bound attention problem in long-context decoding by leveraging a deeper mechanistic understanding of attention sinks, enabling practical acceleration without compromising accuracy across diverse model architectures and tasks.

Abstract: In long-context decoding for LLMs and LMMs, attention becomes increasingly memory-bound because each decoding step must load a large amount of KV-cache data from GPU memory. Existing acceleration strategies often trade efficiency for accuracy by relying on heuristic pruning that may discard useful information. At a deeper level, they also tend to indiscriminately preserve all high-scoring tokens, treat early tokens as indispensable anchors, or rely on heuristic head routing, reflecting an insufficient mechanistic understanding of the attention sink phenomenon. In this paper, we show that the attention sink phenomenon corresponds to a stable, reachable, and error-controllable fixed point constructed during training. Based on this insight, we propose SinkRouter, a training-free selective routing framework that detects the sink signal and skips computations that would otherwise produce near-zero output. To translate this mechanism into real-world acceleration, we develop a hardware-aware Triton kernel with block-level branching and Split-K parallelism. We conduct extensive evaluations on a diverse suite of long-context benchmarks, including LongBench, InfiniteBench, CVBench, MileBench, and MMVP, using both text-only and multimodal backbones such as Llama-3.1-8B, Llama-3.1-70B, Yi-9B-200K, LLaVA-1.5-7B, and LLaVA-1.5-13B. Across these settings, SinkRouter consistently improves decoding efficiency while maintaining competitive accuracy, and reaches 2.03x speedup with a 512K context.

</details>


### [202] [Towards Fully Parameter-Free Stochastic Optimization: Grid Search with Self-Bounding Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16888)
*Yuheng Zhao,Yu-Hu Yan,Amit Attia,Tomer Koren,Lijun Zhang,Peng Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Grid Search with self-bounding analysis achieves fully parameter-free optimization for both non-convex and convex problems, eliminating the need for any unverifiable parameter bounds while maintaining competitive convergence rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing "parameter-free" optimization methods still rely on some prior knowledge about problem parameters (like bounds), making them only "partially parameter-free." The goal is to achieve "fully parameter-free" methods that don't require any unverifiable conditions about the true problem parameters.

Method: Proposed GRASP framework: a grid search approach with novel self-bounding analysis that automatically determines parameter search ranges. Features: 1) Grid search framework with adaptive range determination, 2) Self-bounding analysis technique, 3) Model ensemble with sharper guarantees under interpolated variance characterization.
