Result: 1) Non-convex case: Fully parameter-free method achieving near-optimal convergence rate (up to logarithmic factors). 2) Convex case: Parameter-free methods competitive with accelerated and universal methods. 3) Improved model ensemble guarantees under interpolated variance conditions.

Conclusion: The GRASP framework successfully achieves fully parameter-free optimization across both non-convex and convex settings, eliminating the need for any unverifiable parameter bounds while maintaining strong performance comparable to optimally tuned methods.

Abstract: Parameter-free stochastic optimization aims to design algorithms that are agnostic to the underlying problem parameters while still achieving convergence rates competitive with optimally tuned methods. While some parameter-free methods do not require the specific values of the problem parameters, they still rely on prior knowledge, such as the lower or upper bounds of them. We refer to such methods as ``partially parameter-free''. In this work, we target achieving ``fully parameter-free'' methods, i.e., the algorithmic inputs do not need to satisfy any unverifiable condition related to the true problem parameters. We propose a powerful and general grid search framework, named \textsc{Grasp}, with a novel self-bounding analysis technique that effectively determines the search ranges of parameters, in contrast to previous work. Our method demonstrates generality in: (i) the non-convex case, where we propose a fully parameter-free method that achieves near-optimal convergence rate, up to logarithmic factors; (ii) the convex case, where our parameter-free methods are competitive with strong performance in terms of acceleration and universality. Finally, we contribute a sharper guarantee for the model ensemble, a final step of the grid search framework, under interpolated variance characterization.

</details>


### [203] [Covariance-Based Structural Equation Modeling in Small-Sample Settings with $p>n$](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16894)
*Hiroki Hasegawa,Aoba Tamura,Yukihiko Okada*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel factor-based SEM estimation method that handles small-sample settings where number of variables exceeds sample size (p>n) by reformulating covariance structure and using likelihood with relative error constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional factor-based Structural Equation Modeling relies on likelihood-based estimation assuming a nonsingular sample covariance matrix, which breaks down in small-sample settings where p>n (more variables than observations).

Method: Proposes reformulating the covariance structure into self-covariance and cross-covariance components. Combines likelihood-based feasible set with relative error constraint to enable stable estimation in p>n settings, particularly for recovering sign and direction of parameters.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world data show improved stability, especially in recovering the sign and direction of structural parameters. The method extends covariance-based SEM to small-sample settings.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables factor-based SEM in p>n settings and provides practically useful directional information for decision-making where traditional methods fail.

Abstract: Factor-based Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) relies on likelihood-based estimation assuming a nonsingular sample covariance matrix, which breaks down in small-sample settings with $p>n$. To address this, we propose a novel estimation principle that reformulates the covariance structure into self-covariance and cross-covariance components. The resulting framework defines a likelihood-based feasible set combined with a relative error constraint, enabling stable estimation in small-sample settings where $p>n$ for sign and direction. Experiments on synthetic and real-world data show improved stability, particularly in recovering the sign and direction of structural parameters. These results extend covariance-based SEM to small-sample settings and provide practically useful directional information for decision-making.

</details>


### [204] [Noise-Adaptive Diffusion Sampling for Inverse Problems Without Task-Specific Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16919)
*Yingzhi Xia,Setthakorn Tanomkiattikun,Liangli Zhen,Zaiwang Gu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: N-HMC is a posterior sampling method that uses Hamiltonian Monte Carlo in noise space to solve inverse problems with diffusion models, avoiding local minima and manifold infeasibility issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for solving inverse problems with diffusion models have limitations: optimization-based methods are susceptible to local minima and noise overfitting, while Bayesian approaches with measurement consistency constraints face manifold infeasibility issues.

Method: Propose Noise-space Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (N-HMC) that treats reverse diffusion as deterministic mapping from initial noise to clean images, enabling posterior sampling by moving inference entirely into initial-noise space. Also develop noise-adaptive variant (NA-NHMC) for unknown noise conditions.

Result: Extensive experiments on four linear and three nonlinear inverse problems show NA-NHMC achieves superior reconstruction quality with robust performance across hyperparameters and initializations, significantly outperforming recent state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: N-HMC provides an effective posterior sampling approach for solving inverse problems with diffusion models by enabling comprehensive solution space exploration while maintaining manifold feasibility, with the adaptive variant handling unknown noise conditions.

Abstract: Diffusion models (DMs) have recently shown remarkable performance on inverse problems (IPs). Optimization-based methods can fast solve IPs using DMs as powerful regularizers, but they are susceptible to local minima and noise overfitting. Although DMs can provide strong priors for Bayesian approaches, enforcing measurement consistency during the denoising process leads to manifold infeasibility issues. We propose Noise-space Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (N-HMC), a posterior sampling method that treats reverse diffusion as a deterministic mapping from initial noise to clean images. N-HMC enables comprehensive exploration of the solution space, avoiding local optima. By moving inference entirely into the initial-noise space, N-HMC keeps proposals on the learned data manifold. We provide a comprehensive theoretical analysis of our approach and extend the framework to a noise-adaptive variant (NA-NHMC) that effectively handles IPs with unknown noise type and level. Extensive experiments across four linear and three nonlinear inverse problems demonstrate that NA-NHMC achieves superior reconstruction quality with robust performance across different hyperparameters and initializations, significantly outperforming recent state-of-the-art methods. The code is available at https://github.com/NA-HMC/NA-HMC.

</details>


### [205] [Test-Time Adaptation for EEG Foundation Models: A Systematic Study under Real-World Distribution Shifts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16926)
*Gabriel Jason Lee,Jathurshan Pradeepkumar,Jimeng Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces NeuroAdapt-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating test-time adaptation methods on EEG foundation models under distribution shifts, finding that optimization-free TTA methods outperform gradient-based approaches for EEG data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EEG foundation models face challenges in clinical deployment due to distribution shifts across settings, devices, and populations. Test-time adaptation offers privacy-preserving adaptation without source data access, but its effectiveness for EEG remains unexplored.

Method: The authors introduce NeuroAdapt-Bench, a systematic benchmark evaluating representative TTA methods across multiple pretrained EEG foundation models, diverse downstream tasks, and heterogeneous datasets including in-distribution, out-of-distribution, and extreme modality shifts like Ear-EEG.

Result: Standard TTA methods yield inconsistent performance gains and often degrade performance, especially gradient-based approaches. Optimization-free methods demonstrate greater stability and more reliable improvements for EEG adaptation.

Conclusion: Existing TTA techniques have limitations for EEG applications, highlighting the need for domain-specific adaptation strategies. Optimization-free methods show promise, but further development is needed for reliable EEG test-time adaptation in clinical settings.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) foundation models have shown strong potential for learning generalizable representations from large-scale neural data, yet their clinical deployment is hindered by distribution shifts across clinical settings, devices, and populations. Test-time adaptation (TTA) offers a promising solution by enabling models to adapt to unlabeled target data during inference without access to source data, a valuable property in healthcare settings constrained by privacy regulations and limited labeled data. However, its effectiveness for EEG remains largely underexplored. In this work, we introduce NeuroAdapt-Bench, a systematic benchmark for evaluating test-time adaptation methods on EEG foundation models under realistic distribution shifts. We evaluate representative TTA approaches from other domains across multiple pretrained foundation models, diverse downstream tasks, and heterogeneous datasets spanning in-distribution, out-of-distribution, and extreme modality shifts (e.g., Ear-EEG). Our results show that standard TTA methods yield inconsistent gains and often degrade performance, with gradient-based approaches particularly prone to heavy degradation. In contrast, optimization-free methods demonstrate greater stability and more reliable improvements. These findings highlight the limitations of existing TTA techniques in EEG, provide guidance for future development, and underscore the need for domain-specific adaptation strategies.

</details>


### [206] [D-QRELO: Training- and Data-Free Delta Compression for Large Language Models via Quantization and Residual Low-Rank Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16940)
*Junlin Li,Shuangyong Song,Guodong Du,Ngai Wong,Xuebo Liu,Yongxiang Li,Min Zhang,Jing Li,Xuelong Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DQRELO proposes a training-free delta compression method combining 1-bit quantization and residual low-rank approximation to efficiently store multiple fine-tuned LLMs while reducing memory overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The proliferation of fine-tuned LLMs through Supervised Fine-Tuning creates substantial memory overhead, and existing delta compression methods fail when dealing with models fine-tuned on large-scale datasets, as larger SFT data scales amplify delta parameter magnitude and complexity.

Method: DQRELO combines coarse-grained one-bit quantization to capture dominant delta structure, followed by compensated residual low-rank approximation to recover fine-grained details from smaller residual errors. This training-free approach handles challenging large-scale SFT scenarios.

Result: DQRELO outperforms existing delta compression methods across various LLMs (dense and MoE architectures) in multiple domains. The authors also establish design principles showing how task difficulty, architecture, and layer positioning create predictable patterns for optimal compression strategies.

Conclusion: DQRELO successfully addresses the challenge of compressing deltas from large-scale SFT datasets, providing an effective training-free solution for memory-efficient storage of multiple fine-tuned LLMs while establishing practical guidelines for compression strategy optimization in production systems.

Abstract: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) accelerates taskspecific large language models (LLMs) development, but the resulting proliferation of finetuned models incurs substantial memory overhead. Delta compression addresses this by retaining a single pre-trained LLM with multiple compressed delta weights. However, existing methods fail on models fine-tuned with largescale datasets. We find that larger SFT data scale amplifies delta parameter magnitude, singular values, and entropy, exacerbating compression errors. To tackle this, we propose DQRELO (Delta Compression via Quantization and Residual Low-Rank), a novel training- and data-free delta compression method. It combines coarse-grained one-bit quantization to capture the dominant structure of the delta, followed by compensated residual low-rank approximation to recover fine-grained details from the smaller residual error. Experiments on various LLMs spanning dense and MoE architectures across multiple domains under this challenging setting demonstrate that DQRELO outperforms existing methods. Moreover, we establish key design principles for delta compression through extensive empirical analysis, demonstrating how task difficulty, architecture, and layer positioning create predictable patterns that can guide optimal compression strategies in production systems.

</details>


### [207] [L1 Regularization Paths in Linear Models by Parametric Gaussian Message Passing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16949)
*Yun-Peng Li,Hans-Andrea Loeliger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two new dual algorithms for computing L1 regularization paths in state space models using parametric Gaussian message passing


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need efficient computation of L1 regularization paths for various state space problems including L1-regularized Kalman smoothing, linear SVM, and LASSO

Method: Proposes two dual algorithms using parametric Gaussian message passing (Kalman-type forward-backward recursions) in factor graphs - one for L1 regularization of independent variables, another for dependent variables

Result: Algorithms are broadly applicable, primarily use matrix multiplications, and can be competitive with prior methods in complexity for certain cases

Conclusion: The proposed dual message-passing algorithms provide efficient and general solutions for L1 regularization path computation in state space settings

Abstract: The paper considers the computation of L1 regularization paths in a state space setting, which includes L1 regularized Kalman smoothing, linear SVM, LASSO, and more. The paper proposes two new algorithms, which are duals of each other; the first algorithm applies to L1 regularization of independent variables while the second applies to L1 regularization of dependent variables. The heart of the proposed algorithms is parametric Gaussian message passing (i.e., Kalman-type forward-backward recursions) in the pertinent factor graphs. The proposed methods are broadly applicable, they (usually) require only matrix multiplications, and their complexity can be competitive with prior methods in some cases.

</details>


### [208] [Open-TQ-Metal: Fused Compressed-Domain Attention for Long-Context LLM Inference on Apple Silicon](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16957)
*Sai Vegasena*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Open-TQ-Metal enables 128K-context inference for large models on consumer Macs by implementing fused compressed-domain attention with int4 KV cache quantization and custom Metal shaders.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing inference frameworks cannot support 128K-context inference for large models like Llama 3.1 70B on consumer hardware due to memory constraints from KV cache requirements.

Method: The system quantizes KV cache to int4 on the fly and computes attention directly on compressed representations using custom Metal compute shaders, eliminating intermediate dequantization matrices. It uses a fused sdpa_int4 kernel for compressed-domain attention computation.

Result: Achieves 48x attention speedup at 128K context, reduces KV cache memory from 40GB to 12.5GB (3.2x compression), maintains identical top-1 token predictions to FP16 inference, and provides insights about attention scale factors affecting quantization methods.

Conclusion: Open-TQ-Metal enables previously impossible 128K-context inference on consumer hardware through efficient compressed-domain attention, with performance that reveals attention scale factor is more critical than model size for quantization success.

Abstract: We present Open-TQ-Metal, the first implementation of fused compressed-domain attention on Apple Silicon, enabling 128K-context inference for Llama 3.1 70B on a single 64GB consumer Mac -- a configuration impossible with all existing inference frameworks. Open-TQ-Metal quantizes the KV cache to int4 on the fly and computes attention directly on the compressed representation via custom Metal compute shaders, eliminating all intermediate dequantization matrices. Across 330 experiments spanning two model families (Gemma 4 31B and Llama 3.1 70B), the fused sdpa_int4 kernel achieves 48x attention speedup at 128K context over the dequantize-then-attend baseline, reduces KV cache memory from 40 GB to 12.5 GB (3.2x compression), and maintains identical top-1 token predictions to FP16 inference. We further provide the first cross-architecture analysis of KV cache quantization methods, revealing that the attention scale factor -- not model size -- determines whether angular quantization schemes like PolarQuant succeed or fail, with Gemma 4's attn_scale=1.0 amplifying directional error 25-100x more than Llama's standard 1/sqrt(d) scaling.

</details>


### [209] [Hyperbolic Enhanced Representation Learning for Incomplete Multi-view Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16959)
*Tianyi Chen,Haobo Wang,Kai Tang,Gengyu Lyu,Tianlei Hu,Gang Chen,Hong Ma,Meixiang Xiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HERL is a Hyperbolic Enhanced Representation Learning framework for Incomplete Multi-View Clustering that uses hyperbolic geometry (Poincaré ball) to better model hierarchical data structures and address semantic blurring in incomplete multi-view settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Euclidean-based methods for Incomplete Multi-View Clustering suffer from geometric mismatch when dealing with real-world hierarchical data, causing semantic blurring where representations drift toward spatially proximal but semantically distinct neighbors, making it difficult to learn discriminative representations from fragmentary observations.

Method: HERL operates within the Poincaré ball to construct a structure-aware latent space. It features: 1) A dual-constraint hyperbolic contrastive mechanism with angular-based loss (for semantic identity preservation) and distance-based loss (for hierarchical compactness); 2) A hyperbolic prototype head to rectify global structural drift by aligning cross-view hierarchy-aware prototype distributions; 3) Geometric constraints to sharpen cluster boundaries and improve data recovery.

Result: Extensive experimental results demonstrate that HERL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in Incomplete Multi-View Clustering tasks.

Conclusion: HERL effectively bridges the geometric mismatch gap in IMVC by leveraging hyperbolic geometry to create a more appropriate representation space for hierarchical data, enabling better semantic disentanglement and cluster boundary sharpening despite missing views.

Abstract: Incomplete Multi-View Clustering (IMVC) faces the challenge of learning discriminative representations from fragmentary observations while maintaining robustness against missing views. However, prevalent Euclidean-based methods suffer from a geometric mismatch when modeling real-world data with intrinsic hierarchies, leading to semantic blurring where representations drift towards spatially proximal but semantically distinct neighbors. To bridge this gap, we propose HERL, a Hyperbolic Enhanced Representation Learning framework for IMVC. Operating within the Poincaré ball, HERL constructs a structure-aware latent space to enhance representation learning. Specifically, we design a dual-constraint hyperbolic contrastive mechanism optimizing: an angular-based loss to preserve semantic identity via directional alignment, and a distance-based loss to enforce hierarchical compactness. Furthermore, a hyperbolic prototype head is introduced to rectify global structural drift by aligning cross-view hierarchy-aware prototype distributions. Consequently, HERL disentangles fine-grained semantic correlations to sharpen cluster boundaries and imposes geometric constraints to rectify the data recovery process. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that HERL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [210] [Evaluating Multimodal LLMs for Inpatient Diagnosis: Real-World Performance, Safety, and Cost Across Ten Frontier Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16980)
*Bruce A. Bassett,Amy Rouillard,Sitwala Mundia,Michael Cameron Gramanie,Linda Camara,Ziyaad Dangor,Shabir A. Madhi,Kajal Morar,Marlvin T. Ncube,Ismail Kalla,Haroon Saloojee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multimodal LLMs perform similarly in diagnostic support for LMIC inpatient cases despite large cost differences, and outperform routine care on safety metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly proposed for diagnostic support but lack real-world evaluations in LMIC public hospitals using multimodal inpatient data.

Method: Retrospective evaluation of 539 multimodal inpatient cases from a South African hospital using expert-adjudicated ground truth, 10 multimodal LLMs with zero-shot outputs, and a calibrated three-model LLM Jury for scoring.

Result: (i) LLM performance clustered tightly (<15% variation) despite cost differences; (ii) All LLMs outperformed routine ward diagnoses; (iii) GPT-5.1 performed best; (iv) Radiology reports improved performance by 6%; (v) Diagnostic and reasoning scores highly correlated; (vi) Output rates varied due to constraints.

Conclusion: Multimodal LLMs show similar diagnostic performance in LMIC settings despite cost differences and outperform routine care on safety metrics, suggesting affordability and robustness may outweigh marginal performance differences.

Abstract: Background: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly proposed for diagnostic support, but few evaluations use real-world multimodal inpatient data, particularly in low and middle-income country (LMIC) public hospitals.
  Methods: We conducted VALID, a retrospective evaluation of 539 multimodal inpatient cases from a tertiary public hospital in South Africa. Inputs included radiology imaging (CT, MRI, CXR) and reports, laboratory results, clinical notes, and vital signs. Expert panels adjudicated 300 cases (balanced and discordant subsets) to establish ground truth diagnoses, differentials, and reasoning. Ten multimodal LLMs generated zero-shot outputs. A calibrated three-model LLM Jury scored all outputs and routine ward diagnoses across diagnostic accuracy, differential quality, reasoning, and patient safety (>10,000 evaluations). Primary outcomes were composite scores ($S_3$, $S_4$) and win rates.
  Results: (i) LLM performance was tightly clustered (<15% variation) despite large cost differences; low-cost models performed comparably to top models. (ii) All LLMs significantly outperformed routine ward diagnoses on average diagnostic and safety scores. (iii) Top performance was achieved by GPT-5.1, followed by Gemini models. (vi) Adding radiology reports improved performance by 6%. (v) Diagnostic and reasoning scores were highly correlated ($ρ= 0.85$). (vi) Output rates varied (65-100%) due to input constraints. Results were robust across subsets and evaluation design.
  Conclusions: Across a real-world LMIC dataset, multimodal LLMs showed similar diagnostic performance despite large cost differences and outperformed routine care on average safety metrics. Affordability, robustness, and deployment constraints may outweigh marginal performance differences in LMIC settings.

</details>


### [211] [In-Context Learning Under Regime Change](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16988)
*Carson Dudley,Yutong Bi,Xiaofeng Liu,Samet Oymak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformers can perform in-context change-point detection for non-stationary data, with complexity scaling based on available information about change-point timing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world data in control, forecasting, and decision-making often exhibits non-stationarity with unknown regime shifts. As transformer models increasingly use in-context learning for time series tasks, understanding their ability to detect and adapt to these changes in deployment environments is crucial.

Method: Formalizes the in-context change-point detection problem, establishes the existence of transformer models that solve this problem through constructive proofs, and analyzes how model complexity (layers/parameters) depends on available information about change-point location. Validates with experiments on synthetic linear regression and linear dynamical systems, and applies to real-world tasks.

Result: Trained transformers match optimal baseline performance across different information levels about change-point timing. Encoding change-point knowledge improves performance of pretrained foundation models on real-world tasks (infectious disease forecasting and financial volatility around FOMC announcements) without retraining.

Conclusion: Transformer models can effectively perform in-context change-point detection, with practical applications to real-world non-stationary environments. Model design should consider available information about change-point timing, and incorporating such knowledge enhances foundation model performance on real-world regime change scenarios.

Abstract: Non-stationary sequences arise naturally in control, forecasting, and decision-making. The data-generating process shifts at unknown times, and models must detect the change, discard or downweight obsolete evidence, and adapt to new dynamics on the fly. Transformer-based foundation models increasingly rely on in-context learning for time series forecasting, tabular prediction, and continuous control. As these models are deployed in non-stationary environments, understanding their ability to detect and adapt to regime shifts is important. We formalize this as an in-context change-point detection problem and formally establish the existence of transformer models that solve this problem. Our construction demonstrates that model complexity, in layers and parameters, depends on the level of information available about the change-point location, from no knowledge to knowing exact timing. We validate our results with experiments on synthetic linear regression and linear dynamical systems, where trained transformers match the performance of optimal baselines across information levels. We also show that encoding and incorporating changepoint knowledge indeed improves the real-world performance of a pretrained foundation models on infectious disease forecasting and on financial volatility forecasting around Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) announcements without retraining, demonstrating practical applicability to real-world regime changes.

</details>


### [212] [When Spike Sparsity Does Not Translate to Deployed Cost: VS-WNO on Jetson Orin Nano](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17040)
*Jason Yoo,Shailesh Garg,Souvik Chakraborty,Syed Bahauddin Alam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spiking neural operators show algorithmic sparsity but fail to achieve latency/energy benefits on commodity edge-GPUs due to launch-dominated runtime that doesn't suppress dense computations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether spiking neural operators' theoretical energy/latency advantages from sparse activity translate to practical benefits when deployed on commodity edge-GPU software stacks like Jetson Orin Nano.

Method: Compare variable-spiking wavelet neural operator (VS-WNO) with matched dense wavelet neural operator (WNO) on Darcy rectangular benchmark using Jetson Orin Nano 8 GB. Analyze algorithmic sparsity, latency, energy, and runtime behavior using Nsight Systems profiling.

Result: VS-WNO exhibits substantial algorithmic sparsity (54.26% to 18.15% spike rates across layers), but shows worse deployed performance: 59.6 ms latency and 228.0 mJ energy vs dense WNO's 53.2 ms and 180.7 mJ. Runtime remains launch-dominated with dense convolution kernels dominating computation time.

Conclusion: On current Jetson-class GPU stacks, spike sparsity doesn't reduce deployed costs because the runtime doesn't suppress dense computations as spike activity decreases, highlighting a gap between algorithmic sparsity and hardware realization benefits.

Abstract: Spiking neural operators are appealing for neuromorphic edge computing because event-driven substrates can, in principle, translate sparse activity into lower latency and energy. Whether that advantage survives deployment on commodity edge-GPU software stacks, however, remains unclear. We study this question on a Jetson Orin Nano 8 GB using five pretrained variable-spiking wavelet neural operator (VS-WNO) checkpoints and five matched dense wavelet neural operator (WNO) checkpoints on the Darcy rectangular benchmark. On a reference-aligned path, VS-WNO exhibits substantial algorithmic sparsity, with mean spike rates decreasing from 54.26% at the first spiking layer to 18.15% at the fourth. On a deployment-style request path, however, this sparsity does not reduce deployed cost: VS-WNO reaches 59.6 ms latency and 228.0 mJ dynamic energy per inference, whereas dense WNO reaches 53.2 ms and 180.7 mJ, while also achieving slightly lower reference-path error (1.77% versus 1.81%). Nsight Systems indicates that the request path remains launch-dominated and dense rather than sparsity-aware: for VS-WNO, cudaLaunchKernel accounts for 81.6% of CUDA API time within the latency window, and dense convolution kernels account for 53.8% of GPU kernel time; dense WNO shows the same pattern. On this Jetson-class GPU stack, spike sparsity is measurable but does not reduce deployed cost because the runtime does not suppress dense work as spike activity decreases.

</details>


### [213] [Reference-state System Reliability method for scalable uncertainty quantification of coherent systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17066)
*Ji-Eun Byun,Hyeuk Ryu,Junho Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new Reference-state System Reliability (RSR) method that uses reference states to classify Monte Carlo samples rather than decompose the state space, enabling efficient reliability analysis of large-scale coherent systems with hundreds of thousands of reference states.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Probabilistic evaluation of coherent systems (like infrastructure networks and supply chains) remains challenging because existing decomposition-based methods scale poorly as the number of components grows. Current approaches become computationally expensive when dealing with large systems.

Method: The Reference-state System Reliability (RSR) method characterizes system state boundaries using reference states in component-state space. Instead of decomposing space into disjoint hypercubes, RSR uses reference states to classify Monte Carlo samples. It stores samples and reference states as matrices and compares them using batched matrix operations to leverage modern high-throughput computing capabilities.

Result: RSR can evaluate system-state probability of a graph with 119 nodes and 295 edges within 10 seconds, demonstrating potential for real-time risk assessment. It scales to problems involving hundreds of thousands of reference states - beyond existing methods - and extends naturally to multi-state systems.

Conclusion: RSR significantly reduces computational sensitivity to the number of reference states compared to existing methods. However, when boundary reference states grow exceedingly large, convergence slows (a limitation shared with existing approaches), motivating future research into learning-based representations of system-state boundaries.

Abstract: Coherent systems are representative of many practical applications, ranging from infrastructure networks to supply chains. Probabilistic evaluation of such systems remains challenging, however, because existing decomposition-based methods scale poorly as the number of components grows. To address this limitation, this study proposes the Reference-state System Reliability (RSR) method. Like existing approaches, RSR characterises the boundary between different system states using reference states in the component-state space. Where it departs from these methods is in how the state space is explored: rather than using reference states to decompose the space into disjoint hypercubes, RSR uses them to classify Monte Carlo samples, making computational cost significantly less sensitive to the number of reference states. To make this classification efficient, samples and reference states are stored as matrices and compared using batched matrix operations, allowing RSR to exploit the advances in high-throughput matrix computing driven by modern machine learning. We demonstrate that RSR evaluates the system-state probability of a graph with 119 nodes and 295 edges within 10~seconds, highlighting its potential for real-time risk assessment of large-scale systems. We further show that RSR scales to problems involving hundreds of thousands of reference states -- well beyond the reach of existing methods -- and extends naturally to multi-state systems. Nevertheless, when the number of boundary reference states grows exceedingly large, RSR's convergence slows down, a limitation shared with existing reference-state-based approaches that motivates future research into learning-based representations of system-state boundaries.

</details>


### [214] [Tree of Concepts: Interpretable Continual Learners in Non-Stationary Clinical Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17089)
*Dongkyu Cho,Xiyue Li,Samrachana Adhikari,Rumi Chunara*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tree of Concepts is an interpretable continual learning framework that maintains stable concept semantics through a decision tree interface while adapting to distribution shifts, achieving better stability-plasticity trade-offs than existing methods in healthcare applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-stakes domains like healthcare require both continual learning (adapting to distribution shifts without forgetting) and interpretability, but current methods face a trade-off: deep networks adapt well but are opaque, while interpretable models like decision trees are brittle under distribution shifts.

Method: Proposes Tree of Concepts framework that uses a shallow decision tree to define fixed, rule-based concept interface. Trains a concept bottleneck model to predict these concepts from raw features. Continual updates only affect the concept extractor and label head while keeping concept semantics stable, preventing explanation drift across updates.

Result: On multiple tabular healthcare benchmarks under continual learning protocols, Tree of Concepts achieves stronger stability-plasticity trade-off than existing baselines, including replay-enhanced variants. The framework preserves consistent audit interfaces while supporting continual adaptation.

Conclusion: Structured concept interfaces can effectively support continual adaptation while maintaining consistent interpretability in non-stationary, high-stakes domains, addressing the previously difficult trade-off between adaptation capability and interpretability.

Abstract: Continual learning aims to update models under distribution shift without forgetting, yet many high-stakes deployments, such as healthcare, also require interpretability. In practice, models that adapt well (e.g., deep networks) are often opaque, while models that are interpretable (e.g., decision trees) are brittle under shift, making it difficult to achieve both properties simultaneously. In response, we propose Tree of Concepts, an interpretable continual learning framework that uses a shallow decision tree to define a fixed, rule-based concept interface and trains a concept bottleneck model to predict these concepts from raw features. Continual updates act on the concept extractor and label head while keeping concept semantics stable over time, yielding explanations that do not drift across sequential updates. On multiple tabular healthcare benchmarks under continual learning protocols, our method achieves a stronger stability-plasticity trade-off than existing baselines, including replay-enhanced variants. Our results suggest that structured concept interfaces can support continual adaptation while preserving a consistent audit interface in non-stationary, high-stakes domains.

</details>


### [215] [Live LTL Progress Tracking: Towards Task-Based Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17106)
*Noel Brindise,Cedric Langbort,Melkior Ornik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel framework called Live LTL Progress Tracking that tracks agent progress through complex multi-stage tasks using finite linear temporal logic specifications, providing a "tracking vector" that updates in real-time to represent specification status during trajectory rollouts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing the challenge of non-Markovian objectives in reinforcement learning by providing a way to track progress through complex, multi-stage tasks specified in temporal logic.

Method: Establishes a 'tracking vector' that updates at each time step during trajectory rollout, representing the status of LTL specifications as true, false, or 'open' (for indeterminate cases). Applied to LTL formula trees to encode detailed task execution information.

Result: Formally presents the Live LTL Progress Tracking framework and algorithm with working examples, demonstrating avenues for integration into RL models for performance metrics, diverse exploration, and reward shaping.

Conclusion: The framework provides a potential tool for new RL applications including task-space exploration and diverse solution-finding, with future work planned for applying it to these problems.

Abstract: Motivated by the challenge presented by non-Markovian objectives in reinforcement learning (RL), we present a novel framework to track and represent the progress of autonomous agents through complex, multi-stage tasks. Given a specification in finite linear temporal logic (LTL), the framework establishes a 'tracking vector' which updates at each time step in a trajectory rollout. The values of the vector represent the status of the specification as the trajectory develops, assigning true, false, or 'open' labels (where 'open' is used for indeterminate cases). Applied to an LTL formula tree, the tracking vector can be used to encode detailed information about how a task is executed over a trajectory, providing a potential tool for new performance metrics, diverse exploration, and reward shaping. In this paper, we formally present the framework and algorithm, collectively named Live LTL Progress Tracking, give a simple working example, and demonstrate avenues for its integration into RL models. Future work will apply the framework to problems such as task-space exploration and diverse solution-finding in RL.

</details>


### [216] [The Topological Trouble With Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17121)
*Michael C. Mozer,Shoaib Ahmed Siddiqui,Rosanne Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformers struggle with dynamic state tracking due to feedforward limitations. The paper proposes refocusing on recurrent architectures for better state tracking in foundation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers have fundamental limitations in dynamic state tracking because their purely feedforward architecture can't efficiently maintain sequential dependencies for evolving environments. Information gets pushed deeper into layers with each input step, eventually exhausting model depth. While solutions like dynamic depth or explicit thinking exist, they're computationally inefficient.

Method: The paper introduces a taxonomy of recurrent and continuous-thought transformer architectures, categorizing them by recurrence axis (depth vs step) and ratio of input tokens to recurrence steps. It argues for moving from explicit thought traces to implicit activation dynamics via recurrent architectures.

Result: The paper presents a framework for understanding different recurrent architectures that could address state tracking limitations in transformers. It provides a classification system and analysis of various approaches to integrating recurrence into transformer models.

Conclusion: Temporally extended cognition requires refocusing on recurrent architectures rather than relying on feedforward transformers alone. The paper outlines promising research directions including enhanced state-space models and coarse-grained recurrence to better integrate state tracking into modern foundation models.

Abstract: Transformers encode structure in sequences via an expanding contextual history. However, their purely feedforward architecture fundamentally limits dynamic state tracking. State tracking -- the iterative updating of latent variables reflecting an evolving environment -- involves inherently sequential dependencies that feedforward networks struggle to maintain. Consequently, feedforward models push evolving state representations deeper into their layer stack with each new input step, rendering information inaccessible in shallow layers and ultimately exhausting the model's depth. While this depth limit can be bypassed by dynamic depth models and by explicit or latent thinking that externalizes state representations, these solutions are computationally and memory inefficient. In this article, we argue that temporally extended cognition requires refocusing from explicit thought traces to implicit activation dynamics via recurrent architectures. We introduce a taxonomy of recurrent and continuous-thought transformer architectures, categorizing them by their recurrence axis (depth versus step) and their ratio of input tokens to recurrence steps. Finally, we outline promising research directions, including enhanced state-space models and coarse-grained recurrence, to better integrate state tracking into modern foundation models.

</details>


### [217] [BOIL: Learning Environment Personalized Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17137)
*Rohan Patil,Henrik I. Christensen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BOIL is a scalable process that extracts environmental insights using Pagerank and common information maximization to guide long-term multi-agent behavior, outperforming heuristics in complex environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent systems face challenges in navigating complex environments with limited information, requiring efficient methods to extract valuable insights from environmental structure.

Method: Introduces the Blackbox Oracle Information Learning (BOIL) process, which leverages the Pagerank algorithm and common information maximization to extract valuable information from environment structure and guide long-term agent behavior.

Result: BOIL demonstrates efficacy in generating strategy distributions that improve performance over extended time horizons, surpassing heuristic approaches in complex environments across problems like coverage, patrolling, and stochastic reachability.

Conclusion: BOIL provides a scalable solution for extracting environmental insights to guide multi-agent systems, offering improved performance over heuristic methods for long-term decision-making in complex scenarios.

Abstract: Navigating complex environments poses challenges for multi-agent systems, requiring efficient extraction of insights from limited information. In this paper, we introduce the Blackbox Oracle Information Learning (BOIL) process, a scalable solution for extracting valuable insights from the environment structure. Leveraging the Pagerank algorithm and common information maximization, BOIL facilitates the extraction of information to guide long-term agent behavior applicable to problems such as coverage, patrolling, and stochastic reachability. Through experiments, we demonstrate the efficacy of BOIL in generating strategy distributions conducive to improved performance over extended time horizons, surpassing heuristic approaches in complex environments.

</details>


### [218] [SeekerGym: A Benchmark for Reliable Information Seeking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17143)
*Remy Kim,Minseung Lee,Shuo Li,Osbert Bastani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SeekerGym is a benchmark for evaluating the completeness of information retrieval by AI agents and their ability to quantify uncertainty about missing information, tested on Wikipedia articles and ML survey papers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agents face trustworthiness challenges in information retrieval - they can retrieve relevant information effectively but provide no guarantees about completeness. Missing information can create biases that mislead users even when retrieved content is correct and relevant.

Method: SeekerGym tasks involve documents (Wikipedia articles or ML survey papers) where AI agents must issue queries to retrieve passages from the document. The benchmark measures both retrieval completeness and agents' ability to quantify uncertainty about what might be missing.

Result: Best approaches retrieve 42.5% of passages on Wikipedia and 29.2% on ML Surveys, showing significant room for improvement. The benchmark reveals substantial gaps in AI agents' ability to achieve complete information retrieval.

Conclusion: Current AI agents have limited ability to ensure complete information retrieval or quantify their uncertainty about missing information, highlighting a critical trustworthiness gap that needs to be addressed for more reliable AI systems.

Abstract: Despite their substantial successes, AI agents continue to face fundamental challenges in terms of trustworthiness. Consider deep research agents, tasked with searching for information relevant to a given topic-while AI agents can perform effective information retrieval, there is little guarantee regarding the completeness of this information. Gaps in retrieved information can leave biases that mislead users even if the information they are given is correct and relevant. We introduce SeekerGym, a benchmark designed to evaluate the completeness of information retrieved by AI agents. In addition, SeekerGym also measures how well agents quantify their uncertainty in the completeness of their information; if an agent fails to retrieve all relevant information, it is useful for it to at least quantify how much might be missing. At a high level, each task in SeekerGym is a document (e.g., a Wikipedia article), and the AI agent must issue queries to retrieve passages from that document. Intuitively, the document comprehensively covers a topic, so the ability to retrieve its sections directly measures completeness of information retrieval. In addition to Wikipedia, we also consider machine learning survey papers, where the goal is to retrieve relevant sections of a survey paper. We benchmark several models and algorithms; the best approaches retrieve 42.5% of passages on Wikipedia and 29.2% on ML Surveys, leaving substantial room for improvement.

</details>


### [219] [Uncertainty Quantification in PINNs for Turbulent Flows: Bayesian Inference and Repulsive Ensembles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17156)
*Khemraj Shukla,Zongren Zou,Theo Kaeufer,Michael Triantafyllou,George Em Karniadakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops and evaluates probabilistic extensions of Physics-informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for uncertainty quantification in turbulence modeling, focusing on Bayesian methods, Monte Carlo dropout, and repulsive deep ensembles to improve uncertainty calibration for PDE-constrained inverse problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard PINNs are deterministic and lack reliable epistemic uncertainty quantification, which is critical for ill-posed inverse problems like data-driven Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) modeling in turbulence applications.

Method: Develops three probabilistic extensions: 1) Bayesian PINNs with Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampling and tempered multi-component likelihood, 2) Monte Carlo dropout, and 3) repulsive deep ensembles that enforce diversity in function space. Focuses on ensemble diversity and likelihood tempering for improved uncertainty calibration.

Result: Tested on Van der Pol oscillator and turbulent flow past a circular cylinder (Re=3,900 and Re=10,000). Bayesian PINNs provided the most consistent uncertainty estimates across all inferred quantities. Repulsive ensembles offered computationally efficient approximation with competitive accuracy for primary flow variables.

Conclusion: The study provides quantitative insights into trade-offs between accuracy, computational cost, and uncertainty calibration in physics-informed learning, offering practical guidance for uncertainty quantification in data-driven turbulence modeling.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have emerged as a promising framework for solving inverse problems governed by partial differential equations (PDEs), including the reconstruction of turbulent flow fields from sparse data. However, most existing PINN formulations are deterministic and do not provide reliable quantification of epistemic uncertainty, which is critical for ill-posed problems such as data-driven Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) modeling. In this work, we develop and systematically evaluate a set of probabilistic extensions of PINNs for uncertainty quantification in turbulence modeling. The proposed framework combines (i) Bayesian PINNs with Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampling and a tempered multi-component likelihood, (ii) Monte Carlo dropout, and (iii) repulsive deep ensembles that enforce diversity in function space. Particular emphasis is placed on the role of ensemble diversity and likelihood tempering in improving uncertainty calibration for PDE-constrained inverse problems. The methods are assessed on a hierarchy of test cases, including the Van der Pol oscillator and turbulent flow past a circular cylinder at Reynolds numbers Re=3,900 (direct numerical simulation data) and Re = 10,000 (experimental particle image velocimetry data). The results demonstrate that Bayesian PINNs provide the most consistent uncertainty estimates across all inferred quantities, while function-space repulsive ensembles offer a computationally efficient approximation with competitive accuracy for primary flow variables. These findings provide quantitative insight into the trade-offs between accuracy, computational cost, and uncertainty calibration in physics-informed learning, and offer practical guidance for uncertainty quantification in data-driven turbulence modeling.

</details>


### [220] [RosettaSearch: Multi-Objective Inference-Time Search for Protein Sequence Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17175)
*Meghana Kshirsagar,Allen Nie,Ching-An Cheng,Fanglei Xue,Rahul Dodhia,Juan Lavista Ferres,Kevin K. Yang,Frank DiMaio*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RosettaSearch is an inference-time multi-objective optimization method that uses LLMs as generative optimizers with RosettaFold3 structure prediction feedback to improve protein sequence designs, achieving up to 68% improvement in structural fidelity and 2.5× higher success rate over state-of-the-art baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current protein sequence design models like LigandMPNN generate suboptimal sequences through single-pass decoding. There's a need for optimization methods that can refine these sequences using structural feedback to achieve higher-fidelity designs.

Method: Uses large language models as generative optimizers within a search algorithm for controlled exploration and exploitation. Combines RosettaFold3 structure prediction for reward computation, creating an iterative optimization loop. Also demonstrates multimodal extension using vision-language models with predicted protein structure images.

Result: Applied to 400 LigandMPNN-generated suboptimal sequences, achieving 18-68% improvement in structural fidelity metrics and 2.5× design success rate. Results are robust across independent evaluation (Chai-1) and generalize across LLM families (o4-mini and Gemini-3). Also works with ProteinMPNN sequences on de novo backbones and computationally generated structures.

Conclusion: RosettaSearch effectively optimizes protein sequences through LLM-based search with structural feedback, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art methods. The approach generalizes across different model families and backbone types, with multimodal extensions showing promise for incorporating structural context.

Abstract: We introduce RosettaSearch, an inference-time multi-objective optimization approach for protein sequence optimization. We use large language models (LLMs) as a generative optimizer within a search algorithm capable of controlled exploration and exploitation, using rewards computed from RosettaFold3, a structure prediction model. In a large-scale evaluation, we apply RosettaSearch to 400 suboptimal sequences generated by LigandMPNN (a state-of-the-art model trained for protein sequence design), recovering high-fidelity designs that LigandMPNN's single-pass decoding fails to produce. RosettaSearch's designs show improvements in structural fidelity metrics ranging between 18\% to 68\%, translating to a 2.5$\times$ improvement in design success rate. We observe that these gains in success rate are robust when RosettaSearch-designed sequences are evaluated with an independent structure prediction oracle (Chai-1) and generalize across two distinct LLM families (o4-mini and Gemini-3), with performance scaling consistently with reasoning capability. We further demonstrate that RosettaSearch improves sequence fidelity for ProteinMPNN-designed sequences on \textit{de novo} backbones from the Dayhoff atlas, showing that the approach generalizes beyond native protein structures to computationally generated backbones. We also demonstrate a multi-modal extension of RosettaSearch with vision-language models, where images of predicted protein structures are used as feedback to incorporate structural context to guide protein sequence generation. The sequence trajectories generated by our approach can be used as training data in sequence design models or in post-training and will be released along with the code and datasets upon publication.

</details>


### [221] [Decomposing the Depth Profile of Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17177)
*Jayadev Billa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fine-tuning changes representations mostly in output-proximal layers across models; equalizing per-layer updates reveals architecture- and objective-dependent patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether the depth profile of representational change during fine-tuning is an intrinsic property of models or simply reflects gradient flow magnitude differences across layers.

Method: Analyzed 240 fine-tuning runs across 15 models in 4 architecture families (encoder/decoder transformers, state-space models, RNNs) from 125M to 6.9B parameters. Implemented a per-layer control to equalize relative weight changes across layers after each optimizer step.

Result: Representational change concentrates in output-proximal layers in all standard training runs except one. Under equalized updates: sequential-block architectures retain the profile slope across objectives, while parallel-block architectures retain it only for causal-language-modeling objectives. Architectural distinctions narrow at larger scales. Profile steepness tracks training-free objective distance at initialization, and width is architecture-dominated.

Conclusion: The locality gradient (depthwise slope of representational change) is a composite phenomenon with scale-dependent components, revealing fundamental differences in how architectures adapt during fine-tuning.

Abstract: Fine-tuning adapts pretrained networks to new objectives. Whether the resulting depth profile of representational change reflects an intrinsic property of the model or the magnitude of gradient flow has not been tested directly. We measure this profile across 240 fine-tuning runs spanning 15 models in four architecture families (encoder and decoder transformers, a state-space model, and an RNN) at scales from 125M to 6.9B parameters. Representational change concentrates in output-proximal layers in every standard-training run except one. We apply a per-layer control that equalizes $\|ΔW\|/\|W\|$ across layers after each optimizer step. Under this control, the profile persists in some conditions and collapses in others. At 125M--350M, sequential-block architectures (BERT, OPT, GPT-2) retain the slope across tested objectives while parallel-block architectures (Pythia, CodeGen) retain it only for causal-language-modeling objectives. This architectural distinction narrows at 1.3B--1.4B, where both block types show positive equal-step slopes for CausalLM. Under standard training, profile shape is described by two additional axes: steepness tracks a training-free objective distance at initialization, and profile width is dominated by architecture. We treat the locality gradient, the depthwise slope of representational change, as a composite phenomenon whose components are scale-dependent.

</details>


### [222] [Do LLM-derived graph priors improve multi-agent coordination?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17191)
*Nikunj Gupta,Rajgopal Kannan,Viktor Prasanna*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs can generate effective coordination graph priors for multi-agent reinforcement learning using minimal natural language descriptions, enhancing coordination and adaptability in dynamic environments even with small (1.5B parameter) models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MARL approaches for determining agent coordination have limitations: they require hand-specified graph topologies, rely on brittle proximity-based heuristics, or learn structure entirely from scratch through data-intensive environment interaction, which lacks semantic understanding.

Method: Using LLMs to generate coordination graph priors from minimal natural language descriptions of agent observations. These priors are integrated into MARL algorithms via graph convolutional layers within a GNN-based pipeline. The approach is evaluated on four cooperative scenarios from MPE benchmark against baseline methods and ablated across five compact open-source LLMs.

Result: First quantitative evidence that LLM-derived graph priors can enhance coordination and adaptability in dynamic multi-agent environments. Models as small as 1.5B parameters are sufficient for effective prior generation. LLMs outperform existing approaches that rely on hand-specified topologies, heuristics, or learning from scratch.

Conclusion: LLMs provide a promising approach for generating semantically-informed coordination priors in MARL, addressing key limitations of existing methods by leveraging natural language understanding to infer latent coordination patterns with minimal environmental descriptions.

Abstract: Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) is crucial for AI systems that operate collaboratively in distributed and adversarial settings, particularly in multi-domain operations (MDO). A central challenge in cooperative MARL is determining how agents should coordinate: existing approaches must either hand-specify graph topology, rely on proximity-based heuristics, or learn structure entirely from environment interaction; all of which are brittle, semantically uninformed, or data-intensive. We investigate whether large language models (LLMs) can generate useful coordination graph priors for MARL by using minimal natural language descriptions of agent observations to infer latent coordination patterns. These priors are integrated into MARL algorithms via graph convolutional layers within a graph neural network (GNN)-based pipeline, and evaluated on four cooperative scenarios from the Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE) benchmark against baselines spanning the full spectrum of coordination modeling, from independent learners to state-of-the-art graph-based methods. We further ablate across five compact open-source LLMs to assess the sensitivity of prior quality to model choice. Our results provide the first quantitative evidence that LLM-derived graph priors can enhance coordination and adaptability in dynamic multi-agent environments, and demonstrate that models as small as 1.5B parameters are sufficient for effective prior generation.

</details>


### [223] [Demystifying the unreasonable effectiveness of online alignment methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17207)
*Enoch Hyunwook Kang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Greedy online alignment methods like RLHF and DPO achieve constant (O(1)) cumulative regret under temperature-zero inference, explaining their practical efficiency better than pessimistic O(log T) KL-regularized regret bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing theoretical guarantees show O(log T) KL-regularized regret for greedy alignment methods, which seems pessimistic compared to their empirical performance. The authors argue this mismatch arises because KL-regularized regret conflates learning cost with exploratory randomization from softened training policies.

Method: The paper studies the traditional temperature-zero regret criterion that evaluates only the top-ranked response at inference time. Under this decision-centric performance measure, the authors prove that standard greedy online alignment methods (including online RLHF and online DPO) achieve constant (O(1)) cumulative regret.

Result: By separating statistical learning cost from regularization-induced stochasticity, the authors show that greedy alignment methods achieve O(1) cumulative regret under temperature-zero inference, providing a sharper theoretical explanation for their practical efficiency.

Conclusion: The mismatch between theoretical KL-regularized regret bounds and empirical performance of greedy alignment methods comes from conflation of learning cost and randomization effects. Using temperature-zero regret isolates the decision-making performance and reveals that these methods achieve constant regret, better explaining their practical superb efficiency.

Abstract: Iterative alignment methods based on purely greedy updates are remarkably effective in practice, yet existing theoretical guarantees of \(O(\log T)\) KL-regularized regret can seem pessimistic relative to their empirical performance. In this paper, we argue that this mismatch arises from the regret criterion itself: KL-regularized regret conflates the statistical cost of learning with the exploratory randomization induced by the softened training policy. To separate these effects, we study the traditional temperature-zero regret criterion, which evaluates only the top-ranked response at inference time. Under this decision-centric notion of performance, we prove that standard greedy online alignment methods, including online RLHF and online DPO, achieve constant \((O(1))\) cumulative regret. By isolating the cost of identifying the best response from the stochasticity induced by regularization, our results provide a sharper theoretical explanation for the practical superb efficiency of greedy alignment.

</details>


### [224] [Guardrails in Logit Space: Safety Token Regularization for LLM Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17210)
*Thong Bach,Truyen Tran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STR (safety token regularization) is a lightweight method that preserves safety alignment during LLM fine-tuning by constraining logits of safety-critical tokens, preventing safety degradation without heavy computational overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning well-aligned LLMs on new domains often degrades their safety alignment, even with benign datasets. Existing safety alignment techniques focus mainly on pretraining, leaving fine-tuned models vulnerable to behavioral shifts and safety compromises.

Method: STR identifies salient tokens from rejection templates of well-aligned models (tokens that signal safety-aware responses) and constrains their associated logits during training. This prevents loss of critical safety behaviors while being lightweight and compatible with parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques like LoRA.

Result: Comprehensive experiments show STR achieves safety performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods while preserving task-specific utility. It requires minimal implementation overhead, enhances training stability, and improves overall performance beyond just safety considerations.

Conclusion: STR offers a practical, readily deployable strategy for continual safety alignment in fine-tuned LLMs, addressing the safety degradation problem during domain adaptation with minimal computational cost and seamless integration with existing fine-tuning approaches.

Abstract: Fine-tuning well-aligned large language models (LLMs) on new domains often degrades their safety alignment, even when using benign datasets. Existing safety alignment techniques primarily focus on pretraining, leaving fine-tuned models vulnerable to behavioral shifts. In this work, we introduce safety token regularization (STR), a lightweight method designed to preserve safety properties during fine-tuning. Our approach identifies salient tokens from rejection templates of well-aligned models and constrains their associated logits during training, preventing the loss of critical safety behaviors. Unlike reinforcement learning or preference optimization methods, STR requires minimal additional computation and seamlessly integrates with parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques such as LoRA. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves safety performance on par with state-of-the-art methods, while preserving task-specific utility and requiring minimal implementation overhead. Furthermore, we show that safety token regularization enhances training stability and overall performance beyond safety considerations alone. This work offers a practical and readily deployable strategy for continual safety alignment in fine-tuned LLMs.

</details>


### [225] [Continual Safety Alignment via Gradient-Based Sample Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17215)
*Thong Bach,Dung Nguyen,Thao Minh Le,Truyen Tran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gradient-based sample selection during fine-tuning preserves safety alignment without compromising task performance by filtering high-gradient samples that cause safety degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models lose safety alignment (refusal of harmful requests, truthfulness, commonsense reasoning) during fine-tuning on benign data, creating a need for methods that preserve alignment while adapting to new tasks.

Method: Gradient-based sample selection that filters high-gradient samples during fine-tuning, based on empirical finding that high-gradient samples cause greater safety degradation and drive models toward pretrained distributions.

Result: Substantially improves alignment preservation while maintaining competitive task performance across multiple model families on continual domain tasks, without requiring curated safe data or architectural modifications. Method is robust across selection ratios, task orderings, and diverse attack benchmarks.

Conclusion: Data-centric approach with gradient-based filtering offers an effective solution to the alignment drift problem in LLM fine-tuning, enabling safe adaptation to new tasks through selective data pruning.

Abstract: Large language models require continuous adaptation to new tasks while preserving safety alignment. However, fine-tuning on even benign data often compromises safety behaviors, including refusal of harmful requests, truthfulness, and commonsense reasoning. We investigate which training samples cause alignment drift through a data-centric lens. Our empirical analysis shows samples contribute unequally: high-gradient samples cause greater safety degradation and drive models toward pretrained distributions, while moderate-gradient samples enable task learning with minimal alignment loss. We propose gradient-based sample selection that filters high-gradient samples during fine-tuning. Across multiple model families on continual domain tasks, our method substantially improves alignment preservation while maintaining competitive task performance, without requiring curated safe data or architectural modifications. Our method is robust across selection ratios, task orderings, and diverse attack benchmarks.

</details>


### [226] [LASER: Low-Rank Activation SVD for Efficient Recursion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17224)
*Ege Çakar,Ketan Ali Raghu,Lia Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes the geometric structure of reasoning trajectories in Tiny Recursive Models, finds activations occupy low-dimensional linear subspaces, and introduces LASER - a dynamic compression framework that saves ~60% activation memory without accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recursive architectures like TRMs perform implicit reasoning through iterative latent computation, but the geometric structure of these reasoning trajectories remains poorly understood. There's a need to understand how these models allocate representational capacity during reasoning and whether this can be exploited for efficiency.

Method: Investigates activation manifolds of TRMs during recursive unrolling using principal direction analysis via cheap power iterations. Develops LASER (Low-Rank Activation SVD for Efficient Recursion) - a dynamic compression framework that maintains evolving low-rank basis via matrix-free subspace tracking with fidelity-triggered reset mechanism.

Result: Found that activations occupy an effectively linear, low-dimensional subspace with principal directions trackable via power iterations. Weight-sharing concentrates iterative computation along small dominant eigendirections, with concentration varying sharply across computational sites. LASER achieves ~60% activation memory savings with no statistically significant accuracy degradation.

Conclusion: The analysis reveals structured concentration of recursive computation in low-dimensional subspaces, which can be exploited for efficient compression. Raises questions about how recursive architectures allocate representational capacity during reasoning and whether this concentration can improve efficiency and stability of latent computation.

Abstract: Recursive architectures such as Tiny Recursive Models (TRMs) perform implicit reasoning through iterative latent computation, yet the geometric structure of these reasoning trajectories remains poorly understood. We investigate the activation manifold of TRMs during recursive unrolling and find that activations occupy an effectively linear, low-dimensional subspace whose principal directions can be tracked dynamically with cheap power iterations. This suggests that weight-sharing concentrates iterative computation along a small number of dominant eigendirections, and we find that this concentration varies sharply across computational sites. We exploit this structure through LASER (Low-Rank Activation SVD for Efficient Recursion), a dynamic compression framework that maintains an evolving low-rank basis via matrix-free subspace tracking with a fidelity-triggered reset mechanism, achieving ${\sim}60\%$ activation memory savings with no statistically significant accuracy degradation. Our analysis raises questions about how recursive architectures allocate representational capacity during implicit reasoning, and whether this concentration can be exploited to improve the efficiency and stability of latent computation.

</details>


### [227] [Revisiting Auxiliary Losses for Conditional Depth Routing: An Empirical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17228)
*Qingwei Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conditional depth execution with gate training reveals that removing utility regression and rank supervision (util/rank) improves performance, as these auxiliary losses become net-negative due to off-policy oracle assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of gate training in conditional depth execution, where gate decisions must propagate through many layers before influencing the language modeling loss, resulting in weak and noisy gradients. There's a need to systematically compare interactions among auxiliary losses, particularly between predictive auxiliary and explicit score supervision.

Method: The authors evaluate two gate designs under a 157.5M-parameter decoder-only model with controller-only training and 50% full-path budget. They compare an MLP gate (G1) that maps hidden state to utility scores with a JEPA-guided gate (G3) that adds an action-conditional predictor forecasting outcomes of full vs. cheap execution in latent space. They use 3-seed runs on a fineweb-edu subset and systematically ablate utility regression and rank supervision (util/rank).

Result: G3 with util/rank improves early-to-mid optimization over G1 in all seeds, but removing util/rank improves best/avg LM and threshold-hit speed for both gates, and eliminates G3's advantage. This occurs because the oracle label assumes all subsequent layers execute full, while gated execution only routes a fraction through full, making util/rank net-negative. Removing util/rank also reduces training FLOPs proxy from ~1.53x to ~1.07x full-only (~39% reduction).

Conclusion: The standard recipe with utility regression and pairwise rank supervision (util/rank) is net-negative for conditional depth execution gate training due to off-policy oracle assumptions. Removing these auxiliary losses improves performance and reduces computational cost, though these conclusions are scoped to the studied experimental regime.

Abstract: Conditional depth execution routes a subset of tokens through a lightweight cheap FFN while the remainder execute the standard full FFN at each controlled layer. The central difficulty is gate training: the gate decision must propagate through many layers before it influences the language modeling (LM) loss, so the resulting gradients are weak and noisy. Auxiliary losses are commonly stacked to stabilise training, yet the interactions among them -- particularly between a predictive auxiliary and explicit score supervision -- have not been systematically compared under controlled conditions.
  We evaluate two gate designs under a 157.5M-parameter decoder-only model with controller-only training, 50% full-path budget, and 3-seed runs on a fineweb-edu subset. The MLP gate (G1) maps the current hidden state to a utility score; the JEPA-guided gate (G3) adds an action-conditional predictor that forecasts, in a low-dimensional latent space, the outcome of executing full vs. cheap per token, aligned against a fixed target head. Under the standard recipe with oracle-style utility regression and pairwise rank supervision (util/rank), G3 improves early-to-mid optimisation over G1 in 3/3 seeds (lower avg LM, faster threshold hits, ~10.3x lower grad norms), with 20k-step endpoint LM within a 0.005 heuristic reference.
  A key finding (ablation A3): jointly removing util/rank improves best/avg LM and threshold-hit speed in 3/3 seeds for both gates, and the early-to-mid advantage of G3 over G1 disappears. We trace this to an off-policy oracle label that assumes all subsequent layers execute full, whereas gated execution routes only a fraction through full -- making util/rank net-negative under the current recipe. Removing util/rank also cuts the training FLOPs proxy from ~1.53x to ~1.07x full-only (2.87h to 1.75h on a V100-32GB, ~39%). Conclusions are scoped to the studied regime.

</details>


### [228] [Fully Analog Resonant Recurrent Neural Network via Metacircuit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17277)
*Zixin Zhou,Tianxi Jiang,Menglong Yang,Zhihua Feng,Qingbo He,Shiwu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A fully analog resonant recurrent neural network (R²NN) implemented via coupled electrical local resonators enables real-time temporal classification without analog-to-digital conversion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physical neural networks offer superior inference speed and energy efficiency for edge intelligence, but realizing scalable, fully analog recurrent neural networks for temporal processing remains challenging due to difficulties in mapping trained models onto physical hardware.

Method: Developed a metacircuit architecture of coupled electrical local resonators using a reformulated mechanical-electrical analogy for direct parameter mapping. Integrated trainable global resistive coupling and local resonances that generate frequency-dependent negative resistances to shape an impedance landscape steering currents along frequency-selective pathways.

Result: Demonstrated cross-domain versatility with integrated hardware for tactile perception, speech recognition, and condition monitoring, enabling direct extraction of discriminative spectral features and real-time temporal classification of raw analog inputs without AD conversion.

Conclusion: Establishes a scalable, fully analog paradigm for intelligent temporal processing and paves the way for low-latency, resource-efficient physical neural hardware for edge intelligence applications.

Abstract: Physical neural networks offer a transformative route to edge intelligence, providing superior inference speed and energy efficiency compared to conventional digital architectures. However, realizing scalable, end-to-end, fully analog recurrent neural networks for temporal information processing remains challenging due to the difficulty of faithfully mapping trained network models onto physical hardware. Here we present a fully analog resonant recurrent neural network (R$^2$NN) implemented via a metacircuit architecture composed of coupled electrical local resonators. A reformulated mechanical-electrical analogy establishes a direct mapping between the R$^2$NN model and metacircuit elements, enabling accurate physical implementation of trained neural network parameters. By integrating jointly trainable global resistive coupling and local resonances, which generate effective frequency-dependent negative resistances, the architecture shapes an impedance landscape that steers currents along frequency-selective pathways. This mechanism enables direct extraction of discriminative spectral features, facilitating real-time temporal classification of raw analog inputs while bypassing analog-to-digital conversion. We demonstrate the cross-domain versatility of this framework using integrated hardware for tactile perception, speech recognition, and condition monitoring.
