This work establishes a scalable, fully analog paradigm for intelligent temporal processing and paves the way for low-latency, resource-efficient physical neural hardware for edge intelligence.

</details>


### [229] [REALM: Reliable Expertise-Aware Language Model Fine-Tuning from Noisy Annotations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17289)
*Sajjad Ghiasvand,Mark Beliaev,Mahnoosh Alizadeh,Ramtin Pedarsani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: REALM: Unsupervised method that jointly learns model parameters and annotator expertise values to handle noisy labels from heterogeneous crowdworkers during fine-tuning, outperforming standard majority vote approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard annotation pipelines involve multiple crowdworkers with varying expertise levels, but current methods like majority voting discard annotator identity and cause models to absorb errors from unreliable annotators directly into their parameters.

Method: REALM jointly learns model parameters and scalar expertise values for each annotator without supervision, modeling observed labels as a mixture between the model's prediction and uniform random guess weighted by annotator expertise. Extended to multi-task settings via learned expertise matrices capturing per-annotator reliability across tasks.

Result: Evaluated on five QA benchmarks with Flan-T5 models under simulated noisy annotations. Consistently outperformed naive noisy supervised fine-tuning across single- and multi-task settings, datasets, model sizes, and noise types, with accuracy improvements up to 50% in adversarial regimes and gains increasing with model capacity.

Conclusion: REALM effectively handles noisy annotations by learning annotator expertise values, significantly improving model performance across various settings without requiring additional supervision beyond annotator identity information.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning of large language models relies on human-annotated data, yet annotation pipelines routinely involve multiple crowdworkers of heterogeneous expertise. Standard practice aggregates labels via majority vote or simple averaging, discarding annotator identity and causing the model to absorb the errors of unreliable annotators directly into its parameters. We propose REALM, a method that jointly learns the model parameters and a scalar expertise value for each annotator entirely unsupervised, requiring no supervision beyond annotator identity. The key idea is to model each observed label as a mixture between the model's prediction and a uniform random guess, weighted by the annotator's learned expertise. We extend REALM to a multi-task setting via a learned expertise matrix that captures per-annotator reliability across tasks. We evaluate on five question answering benchmarks, fine-tuning three sizes of Flan-T5 under simulated noisy annotations. The proposed algorithm consistently outperforms the naive noisy SFT in the large majority of single- and multi-task settings, across datasets, model sizes, and noise types, with accuracy improvements of up to $50\%$ in the most adversarial regime and gains that grow with model capacity.

</details>


### [230] [Interpolating Discrete Diffusion Models with Controllable Resampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17310)
*Marcel Kollovieh,Sirine Ayadi,Stephan Günnemann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IDDM is an Interpolating Discrete Diffusion Model that improves discrete diffusion by reducing dependence on intermediate latent states through controllable resampling, outperforming existing methods in molecular graph and text generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing discrete diffusion models have fundamental limitations: masked diffusion models suffer from irreversible errors due to early unmasking, while uniform diffusion models yield low-quality samples due to strong reliance on intermediate latent states.

Method: IDDM introduces a controllable resampling mechanism that partially resets probability mass to the marginal distribution, specifying a generative process whose transitions interpolate between staying at current state, resampling from prior, and flipping toward target state while enforcing marginal consistency and decoupling training from inference.

Result: The model demonstrates competitive performance against state-of-the-art discrete diffusion models across molecular graph generation and text generation tasks, mitigating error accumulation and enabling more effective token corrections.

Conclusion: IDDM addresses fundamental limitations of existing discrete diffusion approaches by reducing dependence on intermediate states through interpolating transitions and controllable resampling, showing promising results for discrete data generation across domains.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion models form a powerful class of generative models across diverse domains, including text and graphs. However, existing approaches face fundamental limitations. Masked diffusion models suffer from irreversible errors due to early unmasking, while uniform diffusion models, despite enabling self-correction, often yield low-quality samples due to their strong reliance on intermediate latent states. We introduce IDDM, an Interpolating Discrete Diffusion Model, that improves diffusion by reducing dependence on intermediate latent states. Central to IDDM is a controllable resampling mechanism that partially resets probability mass to the marginal distribution, mitigating error accumulation and enabling more effective token corrections. IDDM specifies a generative process whose transitions interpolate between staying at the current state, resampling from a prior, and flipping toward the target state, while enforcing marginal consistency and fully decoupling training from inference. We benchmark our model against state-of-the-art discrete diffusion models across molecular graph generation as well as text generation tasks, demonstrating competitive performance.

</details>


### [231] [A Survey of Reinforcement Learning for Large Language Models under Data Scarcity: Challenges and Solutions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17312)
*Zhiyin Yu,Yuchen Mou,Juncheng Yan,Junyu Luo,Chunchun Chen,Xing Wei,Yunhui Liu,Hongru Sun,Yuxing Zhang,Jun Xu,Yatao Bian,Ming Zhang,Wei Ye,Tieke He,Jie Yang,Guanjie Zheng,Zhonghai Wu,Bo Zhang,Lei Bai,Xiao Luo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A systematic survey paper reviewing reinforcement learning methods for large language models under data scarcity, presenting a hierarchical framework with three perspectives to organize existing approaches and provide guidance for future research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning for LLMs faces significant data scarcity challenges, including limited high-quality external supervision and constrained model-generated experience. These limitations make data-efficient reinforcement learning a critical research direction that needs systematic organization and analysis.

Method: The paper conducts a systematic review using a bottom-up hierarchical framework built around three complementary perspectives: data-centric (focusing on data sources and quality), training-centric (focusing on RL algorithms and training efficiency), and framework-centric (focusing on overall system design). It develops a taxonomy of existing methods and analyzes representative approaches in each category.

Result: The survey provides the first systematic review of RL for LLMs under data scarcity, developing a comprehensive taxonomy that organizes existing methods. It identifies strengths and limitations of different approaches and creates a clear conceptual foundation for understanding the design space of data-efficient RL for LLMs.

Conclusion: The paper establishes a foundational framework for understanding and advancing data-efficient reinforcement learning for LLMs, offering a comprehensive roadmap for future research and inspiring new directions toward more efficient and scalable reinforcement learning post-training approaches.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful post-training paradigm for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs). However, reinforcement learning for LLMs faces substantial data scarcity challenges, including the limited availability of high-quality external supervision and the constrained volume of model-generated experience. These limitations make data-efficient reinforcement learning a critical research direction. In this survey, we present the first systematic review of reinforcement learning for LLMs under data scarcity. We propose a bottom-up hierarchical framework built around three complementary perspectives: the data-centric perspective, the training-centric perspective, and the framework-centric perspective. We develop a taxonomy of existing methods, summarize representative approaches in each category, and analyze their strengths and limitations. Our taxonomy aims to provide a clear conceptual foundation for understanding the design space of data-efficient RL for LLMs and to guide researchers working in this emerging area. We hope this survey offers a comprehensive roadmap for future research and inspires new directions toward more efficient and scalable reinforcement learning post-training for LLMs.

</details>


### [232] [SigGate-GT: Taming Over-Smoothing in Graph Transformers via Sigmoid-Gated Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17324)
*Dongxin Guo,Jikun Wu,Siu Ming Yiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SigGate-GT is a graph transformer that uses learned per-head sigmoid gates on attention outputs to address over-smoothing and attention entropy degeneration, achieving state-of-the-art results on molecular benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph transformers suffer from two key problems: over-smoothing (node representations collapse with depth) and attention entropy degeneration. These issues share a root cause with attention sinks in large language models - softmax attention's sum-to-one constraint forces nodes to attend somewhere even when no informative signal exists. Recent findings showed that element-wise sigmoid gating eliminates attention sinks in LLMs.

Method: SigGate-GT applies learned, per-head sigmoid gates to the attention output within the GraphGPS framework. Each gate can suppress activations toward zero, enabling heads to selectively silence uninformative connections. The approach is integrated into the GraphGPS architecture with minimal parameter overhead.

Result: On five standard benchmarks: matches prior best on ZINC (0.059 MAE) and sets new state-of-the-art on ogbg-molhiv (82.47% ROC-AUC). Statistically significant gains over GraphGPS across all five datasets (p < 0.05). Ablations show: gating reduces over-smoothing by 30% (mean relative MAD gain across 4-16 layers), increases attention entropy, and stabilizes training across a 10× learning rate range, with about 1% parameter overhead on OGB.

Conclusion: Sigmoid gating effectively addresses fundamental limitations of softmax attention in graph transformers, mitigating over-smoothing and attention entropy issues while improving performance and training stability with minimal parameter cost.

Abstract: Graph transformers achieve strong results on molecular and long-range reasoning tasks, yet remain hampered by over-smoothing (the progressive collapse of node representations with depth) and attention entropy degeneration. We observe that these pathologies share a root cause with attention sinks in large language models: softmax attention's sum-to-one constraint forces every node to attend somewhere, even when no informative signal exists. Motivated by recent findings that element-wise sigmoid gating eliminates attention sinks in large language models, we propose SigGate-GT, a graph transformer that applies learned, per-head sigmoid gates to the attention output within the GraphGPS framework. Each gate can suppress activations toward zero, enabling heads to selectively silence uninformative connections. On five standard benchmarks, SigGate-GT matches the prior best on ZINC (0.059 MAE) and sets new state-of-the-art on ogbg-molhiv (82.47% ROC-AUC), with statistically significant gains over GraphGPS across all five datasets ($p < 0.05$). Ablations show that gating reduces over-smoothing by 30% (mean relative MAD gain across 4-16 layers), increases attention entropy, and stabilizes training across a $10\times$ learning rate range, with about 1% parameter overhead on OGB.

</details>


### [233] [Rethinking the Comparison Unit in Sequence-Level Reinforcement Learning: An Equal-Length Paired Training Framework from Loss Correction to Sample Construction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17328)
*Fei Ding,Yongkang Zhang,Runhao Liu,Yuhao Liao,Zijian Zeng,Huiming Yang,Sibo wang,Linglin Liao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes EqLen, a method addressing the length problem in sequence-level RL by constructing equal-length, comparable training segments rather than applying post-hoc corrections to unequal-length responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for sequence-level relative RL partially address length-related issues, but a fundamental problem remains: comparison units used during training lack inherent comparability, making unequal-length responses difficult to compare effectively.

Method: Proposes a sample-construction-based training framework that proactively constructs equal-length, alignable, and comparable training segments during generation. The concrete method, EqLen, uses dual-track synchronous generation, prefix inheritance, and segment masking to efficiently collect effective equal-length training segments. It's applicable to group-relative comparison algorithms like GRPO, GSPO, and RLOO.

Result: EqLen enables stable training by solving the comparison unit construction problem, addressing the fundamental issue of unequal-length response comparisons in sequence-level reinforcement learning.

Conclusion: The length problem in sequence-level relative RL should be viewed as a comparison unit construction problem rather than a loss-scaling or normalization bias. The proposed EqLen method successfully addresses this fundamental issue through proactive construction of equal-length training segments.

Abstract: This paper investigates the length problem in sequence-level relative reinforcement learning. We observe that, although existing methods partially alleviate length-related phenomena, a more fundamental issue remains insufficiently characterized: the comparison units used during training lack inherent comparability. Building on this observation, we propose a new perspective: the length problem should not be viewed merely as a loss-scaling or normalization bias, but rather as a \emph{comparison unit construction} problem. We further establish a sample-construction-based training framework that, instead of applying post-hoc corrections to unequal-length responses, proactively constructs equal-length, alignable, and comparable training segments during generation. Within this framework, we propose EqLen, a concrete method applicable to group-relative comparison algorithms such as GRPO, GSPO, and RLOO. Through dual-track synchronous generation, prefix inheritance, and segment masking, EqLen efficiently collects effective equal-length training segments and enables stable

</details>


### [234] [FLARE: Task-agnostic embedding model evaluation through a normalization process](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17344)
*Jingzhou Jiang,Yixuan Tang,Yi Yang,Kar Yan Tam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FLARE is a flow-based method for evaluating embedding models without task-specific labels, avoiding the instability of existing labelless measures in high-dimensional spaces by using normalized streams and log-likelihood estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When task-specific labels are unavailable, it's challenging to select appropriate embedding models for a target corpus. Existing labelless evaluation measures fail in high-dimensional spaces due to issues with kernel estimators and Gaussian mixtures, leading to unstable rankings.

Method: FLARE (flow-based labelless representation embedding evaluation) uses normalized streams to estimate information sufficiency directly from log-likelihood, avoiding distance-based density estimation. The method provides finite sample bounds showing estimation error depends on the intrinsic data manifold dimension rather than original embedding dimension.

Result: On 11 datasets with 8 embedders, FLARE achieved Spearman's ρ of 0.90 under supervised benchmarks and remained stable in high-dimensional embeddings (d ≥ 3,584) while existing labelless baselines collapsed.

Conclusion: FLARE provides a robust labelless method for embedding model evaluation that works effectively in high-dimensional spaces, overcoming limitations of existing approaches that fail when embedding dimensions increase significantly.

Abstract: When task-specific labels are not available, it becomes difficult to select an embedding model for a specific target corpus. Existing labelless measures based on kernel estimators or Gaussian mixes fail in high-dimensional space, resulting in unstable rankings. We propose a flow-based labelless representation embedding evaluation (FLARE), which utilizes normalized streams to estimate information sufficiency directly from log-likelihood and avoid distance-based density estimation. We give a finite sample boundary, indicating that the estimation error depends on the intrinsic dimension of the data manifold rather than the original embedding dimension. On 11 datasets and 8 embedders, FLARE reached Spearman's $ρ$ of 0.90 under the supervised benchmark and remained stable in high-dimensional embeddings ($d \geq 3{,}584$) as the existing labelless baseline collapsed.

</details>


### [235] [Towards a Data-Parameter Correspondence for LLMs: A Preliminary Discussion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17384)
*Ou Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper establishes a unified "data-parameter correspondence" framework that reveals data-centric and model-centric LLM optimizations as dual manifestations of the same geometric structure on statistical manifolds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional LLM optimization has been divided into isolated data-centric (sample manipulation) and model-centric (weight tuning) paradigms without recognizing their fundamental geometric connections. The authors aim to unify these disparate approaches through mathematical formalization.

Method: The paper develops a theoretical framework based on Fisher-Rao geometry and Legendre duality on statistical manifolds. It identifies three fundamental correspondences: geometric (data pruning ↔ parameter sparsification), low-rank (ICL ↔ LoRA adaptation), and security-privacy (attacks ↔ defenses), showing these operations explore identical subspaces on Grassmannian manifolds.

Result: The framework demonstrates that cooperative optimization integrating data and parameter modalities may outperform isolated approaches across efficiency, robustness, and privacy dimensions, providing mathematical formalization for cross-community methodology transfer.

Conclusion: Data-centric and model-centric LLM optimizations are fundamentally unified through geometric structures on statistical manifolds, suggesting that integrated approaches leveraging both data and parameter manipulations offer superior performance across the entire model lifecycle.

Abstract: Large language model optimization has historically bifurcated into isolated data-centric and model-centric paradigms: the former manipulates involved samples through selection, augmentation, or poisoning, while the latter tunes model weights via masking, quantization, or low-rank adaptation. This paper establishes a unified \emph{data-parameter correspondence} revealing these seemingly disparate operations as dual manifestations of the same geometric structure on the statistical manifold $\mathcal{M}$. Grounded in the Fisher-Rao metric $g_{ij}(θ)$ and Legendre duality between natural ($θ$) and expectation ($η$) parameters, we identify three fundamental correspondences spanning the model lifecycle: 1. Geometric correspondence: data pruning and parameter sparsification equivalently reduce manifold volume via dual coordinate constraints; 2. Low-rank correspondence: in-context learning (ICL) and LoRA adaptation explore identical subspaces on the Grassmannian $\mathcal{G}(r,d)$, with $k$-shot samples geometrically equivalent to rank-$r$ updates; 3. Security-privacy correspondence: adversarial attacks exhibit cooperative amplification between data poisoning and parameter backdoors, whereas protective mechanisms follow cascading attenuation where data compression multiplicatively enhances parameter privacy. Extending from training through post-training compression to inference, this framework provides mathematical formalization for cross-community methodology transfer, demonstrating that cooperative optimization integrating data and parameter modalities may outperform isolated approaches across efficiency, robustness, and privacy dimensions.

</details>


### [236] [Back to Repair: A Minimal Denoising Network\ for Time Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17388)
*Kadir-Kaan Özer,René Ebeling,Markus Enzweiler*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: JuRe is a surprisingly simple yet highly effective time series anomaly detection method using just a single residual block trained with corruption-based denoising, outperforming complex neural baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current time series anomaly detection methods often rely on complex architectures (attention, latent variables, adversarial components), but the authors question whether this complexity is necessary. They hypothesize that architectural complexity is unnecessary when the training objective properly implements the manifold-projection principle.

Method: JuRe uses only a single depthwise-separable convolutional residual block (hidden dimension 128) trained to repair corrupted time series windows. Inference uses a fixed, parameter-free structural discrepancy function to score anomalies. The method employs no attention mechanisms, no latent variables, and no adversarial components.

Result: JuRe ranks second overall on the TSB-AD multivariate benchmark (AUC-PR 0.404) and second on the UCR univariate archive (AUC-PR 0.198), leading all neural baselines on both AUC-PR and VUS-PR metrics. Ablation studies show training-time corruption is the dominant factor (ΔAUC-PR = 0.047 when removed). Statistical significance is established against 21 of 25 baselines.

Conclusion: Architectural complexity is unnecessary for effective time series anomaly detection when the training objective correctly implements manifold-projection principles. The simple denoising approach with proper corruption strategy achieves state-of-the-art performance, challenging the trend toward increasingly complex models.

Abstract: We introduce JuRe (Just Repair), a minimal denoising network for time series anomaly detection that exposes a central finding: architectural complexity is unnecessary when the training objective correctly implements the manifold-projection principle. JuRe consists of a single depthwise-separable convolutional residual block with hidden dimension 128, trained to repair corrupted time series windows and scored at inference by a fixed, parameter-free structural discrepancy function. Despite using no attention, no latent variable, and no adversarial component, JuRe ranks second on the TSB-AD multivariate benchmark (AUC-PR 0.404, 180 series, 17 datasets) and second on the UCR univariate archive by AUC-PR (0.198, 250 series), leading all neural baselines on AUC-PR and VUS-PR. Component ablation on TSB-AD identifies training-time corruption as the dominant factor ($Δ$AUC-PR $= 0.047$ on removal), confirming that the denoising objective, not network capacity, drives detection quality. Pairwise Wilcoxon signed-rank tests establish statistical significance against 21 of 25 baselines on TSB-AD. Code is available at the URL https://github.com/iis-esslingen/JuRe.

</details>


### [237] [On the Generalization Bounds of Symbolic Regression with Genetic Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17402)
*Masahiro Nomura,Ryoki Hamano,Isao Ono*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GP-based symbolic regression has strong empirical success but limited theoretical understanding of generalization; this work provides learning-theoretic analysis with generalization bounds explaining practical GP design choices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite strong empirical success of genetic programming for symbolic regression, there's limited theoretical understanding of why GP-based SR models generalize beyond training data. This gap motivates a rigorous learning-theoretic analysis to explain generalization properties.

Method: The paper provides a learning-theoretic analysis of SR models represented as expression trees. It derives generalization bounds for GP-style SR under constraints on tree size, depth, and learnable constants, decomposing the generalization gap into interpretable components: structure-selection and constant-fitting terms.

Result: The analysis shows how structural restrictions (parsimony pressure, depth limits) reduce hypothesis-class growth while stability mechanisms (numerically stable operators, interval arithmetic) control sensitivity to parameter perturbations. This links practical GP design choices to explicit complexity terms in generalization bounds.

Conclusion: The work offers a principled explanation for commonly observed empirical behaviors in GP-based SR and contributes to a more rigorous theoretical understanding of its generalization properties by connecting practical design choices to theoretical generalization bounds.

Abstract: Symbolic regression (SR) with genetic programming (GP) aims to discover interpretable mathematical expressions directly from data. Despite its strong empirical success, the theoretical understanding of why GP-based SR generalizes beyond the training data remains limited. In this work, we provide a learning-theoretic analysis of SR models represented as expression trees. We derive a generalization bound for GP-style SR under constraints on tree size, depth, and learnable constants. Our result decomposes the generalization gap into two interpretable components: a structure-selection term, reflecting the combinatorial complexity of choosing an expression-tree structure, and a constant-fitting term, capturing the complexity of optimizing numerical constants within a fixed structure. This decomposition provides a theoretical perspective on several widely used practices in GP, including parsimony pressure, depth limits, numerically stable operators, and interval arithmetic. In particular, our analysis shows how structural restrictions reduce hypothesis-class growth while stability mechanisms control the sensitivity of predictions to parameter perturbations. By linking these practical design choices to explicit complexity terms in the generalization bound, our work offers a principled explanation for commonly observed empirical behaviors in GP-based SR and contributes towards a more rigorous understanding of its generalization properties.

</details>


### [238] [Reward Score Matching: Unifying Reward-based Fine-tuning for Flow and Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17415)
*Jeongjae Lee,Jinho Chang,Jeongsol Kim,Jong Chul Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified framework called Reward Score Matching (RSM) simplifies various reward-based fine-tuning methods for diffusion models into a common formulation, enabling clearer understanding of design tradeoffs and leading to simpler, more effective implementations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reward-based fine-tuning methods for diffusion/flow models are motivated by different perspectives (Soft RL, GFlowNets, etc.), creating a fragmented landscape. This diversity makes it hard to understand core principles, tradeoffs, and leads to unnecessary complexity in implementations.

Method: Proposes Reward Score Matching (RSM) framework that unifies existing methods as score matching toward reward-guided targets. The framework analyzes differences in value-guidance estimator construction and optimization strength across timesteps, clarifying bias-variance-compute tradeoffs.

Result: RSM reveals that many existing methods can be simplified by distinguishing core optimization components from auxiliary complexity. Based on this insight, the authors develop simpler redesigns that improve alignment effectiveness and compute efficiency across differentiable and black-box reward settings.

Conclusion: RSM transforms a fragmented collection of reward-based fine-tuning methods into a smaller, more interpretable, and actionable design space, providing clearer understanding of tradeoffs and enabling more efficient implementations.

Abstract: Reward-based fine-tuning aims to steer a pretrained diffusion or flow-based generative model toward higher-reward samples while remaining close to the pretrained model. Although existing methods are motivated by different perspectives such as Soft RL, GFlowNets, etc., we show that many can be written under a common framework, which we call reward score matching (RSM). Under this view, alignment becomes score matching toward a reward-guided target, and the main differences across methods reduce to the construction of the value-guidance estimator and the effective optimization strength across timesteps. This unification clarifies the bias--variance--compute tradeoffs of existing designs and distinguishes core optimization components from auxiliary mechanisms that add complexity without clear benefit. Guided by this perspective, we develop simpler redesigns that improve alignment effectiveness and compute efficiency across representative settings with differentiable and black-box rewards. Overall, RSM turns a seemingly fragmented collection of reward-based fine-tuning methods into a smaller, more interpretable, and more actionable design space.

</details>


### [239] [TransXion: A High-Fidelity Graph Benchmark for Realistic Anti-Money Laundering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17420)
*Keyang Chen,Mingxuan Jiang,Yongsheng Zhao,Zeping Li,Zaiyuan Chen,Weiqi Luo,Zhixin Li,Sen Liu,Yinan Jing,Guangnan Ye,Xihong Wu,Hongfeng Chai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TransXion is a new benchmark ecosystem for anti-money laundering research that simulates realistic transaction networks with rich entity profiles and stochastic illicit subgraphs, creating more challenging and realistic detection scenarios than existing benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AML research is hindered by unrealistic benchmarks. Existing transaction datasets have sparse node semantics (just anonymized IDs) and use template-driven anomaly injection that biases models toward detecting static structural patterns, leading to overly optimistic performance assessments.

Method: TransXion integrates profile-aware simulation of normal transaction behavior with stochastic, non-template synthesis of illicit subgraphs. It models persistent entity profiles (demographic/behavioral attributes) and conditional transaction behavior, enabling detection of "out-of-character" anomalies where activity contradicts an entity's socio-economic context.

Result: The dataset contains ~3M transactions among 50,000 entities with rich attributes. It reproduces key structural properties of real payment networks (heavy-tailed activity distributions, localized subgraph structure). Diverse detection models perform substantially worse on TransXion than on existing benchmarks, indicating increased difficulty and realism.

Conclusion: TransXion provides a more faithful testbed for developing context-aware and robust AML detection methods, addressing limitations of current benchmarks by offering realistic transaction networks with rich semantics and challenging anomaly scenarios.

Abstract: Money laundering poses severe risks to global financial systems, driving the widespread adoption of machine learning for transaction monitoring. However, progress remains stifled by the lack of realistic benchmarks. Existing transaction-graph datasets suffer from two pervasive limitations: (i) they provide sparse node-level semantics beyond anonymized identifiers, and (ii) they rely on template-driven anomaly injection, which biases benchmarks toward static structural motifs and yields overly optimistic assessments of model robustness. We propose TransXion, a benchmark ecosystem for Anti-Money Laundering (AML) research that integrates profile-aware simulation of normal activity with stochastic, non-template synthesis of illicit subgraphs.TransXion jointly models persistent entity profiles and conditional transaction behavior, enabling evaluation of "out-of-character" anomalies where observed activity contradicts an entity's socio-economic context. The resulting dataset comprises approximately 3 million transactions among 50,000 entities, each endowed with rich demographic and behavioral attributes. Empirical analyses show that TransXion reproduces key structural properties of payment networks, including heavy-tailed activity distributions and localized subgraph structure. Across a diverse array of detection models spanning multiple algorithmic paradigms, TransXion yields substantially lower detection performance than widely used benchmarks, demonstrating increased difficulty and realism. TransXion provides a more faithful testbed for developing context-aware and robust AML detection methods. The dataset and code are publicly available at https://github.com/chaos-max/TransXion.

</details>


### [240] [A unified convergence theory for adaptive first-order methods in the nonconvex case, including AdaNorm, full and diagonal AdaGrad, Shampoo and Muo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17423)
*S. Gratton,Ph. L. Toint*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified optimization framework with adaptive preconditioned gradients that covers various adaptive methods (AdaGrad, Shampoo, Muon variants) and allows combining different geometries across variable groups, with comprehensive stochastic convergence analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a unified theoretical framework that can encompass various first-order optimization algorithms for nonconvex unconstrained optimization, particularly adaptive gradient methods that use preconditioning, and to provide rigorous convergence guarantees without restrictive assumptions.

Method: Proposes a framework using adaptively preconditioned gradients that includes full/diagonal AdaGrad, AdaNorm, and adaptive variants of Shampoo and Muon. The framework supports combining heterogeneous geometries across different variable groups while maintaining unified convergence analysis.

Result: Conducts a fully stochastic global rate-of-convergence analysis for all methods in the framework, both with and without momentum (two types). Analysis uses reasonable variance assumptions on gradient oracles without requiring bounded stochastic gradients or excessively small step sizes.

Conclusion: The framework successfully unifies various adaptive optimization methods under a single theoretical structure with rigorous convergence guarantees, providing comprehensive stochastic convergence analysis under practical assumptions.

Abstract: A unified framework for first-order optimization algorithms fornonconvex unconstrained optimization is proposed that uses adaptivelypreconditioned gradients and includes popular methods such as full anddiagonal AdaGrad, AdaNorm, as well as adpative variants of Shampoo andMuon. This framework also allows combining heterogeneous geometriesacross different groups of variables while preserving a unifiedconvergence analysis. A fully stochastic global rate-of-convergenceanalysis is conducted for all methods in the framework, with andwithout two types of momentum, using reasonable assumptions on thevariance of the gradient oracle and without assuming boundedstochastic gradients or small enough stepsize.

</details>


### [241] [Neural Adjoint Method for Meta-optics: Accelerating Volumetric Inverse Design via Fourier Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17425)
*Chanik Kang,Hyewon Suk,Haejun Chung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural Adjoint Method uses AI to predict 3D gradient fields for meta-optics design, replacing thousands of expensive Maxwell equation solves with fast neural network predictions, reducing design time from hours to seconds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Meta-optics design requires solving computationally expensive Maxwell's equations iteratively, even with adjoint optimization, broadband design can require thousands of simulations, making industrial-scale optimization slow and costly.

Method: Proposed Neural Adjoint Method using a Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) as a solver-supervised surrogate that predicts 3D adjoint gradient fields from voxelized permittivity volumes. Introduced stage-wise FNO that progressively refines residual errors with increasing emphasis on higher-frequency components.

Result: The method reduces meta-optics design time from hours to seconds across three tasks: spectral sorting (color routers), achromatic focusing (metalenses), and waveguide mode conversion. Evaluated on a curated dataset from paired forward/adjoint FDTD simulations.

Conclusion: The Neural Adjoint Method provides a practical route toward fast, large-scale volumetric meta-optical design enabled by AI-accelerated scientific computing, overcoming computational bottlenecks in high-dimensional optimization problems.

Abstract: Meta-optics promises compact, high-performance imaging and color routing. However, designing high-performance structures is a high-dimensional optimization problem: mapping a desired optical output back to a physical 3D structure requires solving computationally expensive Maxwell's equations iteratively. Even with adjoint optimization, broadband design can require thousands of Maxwell solves, making industrial-scale optimization slow and costly. To overcome this challenge, we propose the Neural Adjoint Method, a solver-supervised surrogate that predicts 3D adjoint gradient fields from a voxelized permittivity volume using a Fourier Neural Operator (FNO). By learning the dense, per-voxel sensitivity field that drives gradient-based updates, our method can replace per-iteration adjoint solves with fast predictions, greatly reducing the computational cost of full-wave simulations required during iterative refinement. To better preserve sensitivity peaks, we introduce a stage-wise FNO that progressively refines residual errors with increasing emphasis on higher-frequency components. We curate a meta-optics dataset from paired forward/adjoint FDTD simulations and evaluate it across three tasks: spectral sorting (color routers), achromatic focusing (metalenses), and waveguide mode conversion. Our method reduces design time from hours to seconds. These results suggest a practical route toward fast, large-scale volumetric meta-optical design enabled by AI-accelerated scientific computing.

</details>


### [242] [Machine Learning Hamiltonian Dynamical Systems with Sparse and Noisy Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17470)
*Vedanta Thapar,Abhinav Gupta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ASRNNs enable symbolic discovery of Hamiltonian dynamics from extremely sparse, noisy data by combining structure-preserving neural networks with symplectic integration and symbolic regression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most machine learning approaches for discovering governing laws of dynamical systems degrade severely when observations are sparse, noisy, or irregularly sampled, creating a need for robust methods that work under extreme data scarcity.

Method: Introduce Adaptable Symplectic Recurrent Neural Networks (ASRNNs) that explicitly incorporate physical structure, combine Hamiltonian learning with symplectic recurrent integration, avoid time derivative estimation, and enable stable learning under noise. These structure-preserving models are then used as data generators for symbolic discovery using regression methods (SINDy and PySR).

Result: ASRNNs can accurately predict long-term dynamics even with only two irregularly spaced time points per training trajectory, possibly with correlated noise. They enable recovery of exact symbolic equations for polynomial systems and consistent polynomial approximations for non-polynomial Hamiltonians.

Conclusion: Structure-preserving architectures like ASRNNs provide a robust pathway to interpretable discovery of Hamiltonian dynamics from sparse and noisy data, overcoming limitations of conventional machine learning approaches.

Abstract: Machine learning has become a powerful tool for discovering governing laws of dynamical systems from data. However, most existing approaches degrade severely when observations are sparse, noisy, or irregularly sampled. In this work, we address the problem of learning symbolic representations of nonlinear Hamiltonian dynamical systems under extreme data scarcity by explicitly incorporating physical structure into the learning architecture. We introduce Adaptable Symplectic Recurrent Neural Networks (ASRNNs), a parameter-cognizant, structure-preserving model that combines Hamiltonian learning with symplectic recurrent integration, avoiding time derivative estimation, and enabling stable learning under noise. We demonstrate that ASRNNs can accurately predict long-term dynamics even when each training trajectory consists of only two irregularly spaced time points, possibly corrupted by correlated noise. Leveraging ASRNNs as structure-preserving data generators, we further enable symbolic discovery using independent regression methods (SINDy and PySR), recovering exact symbolic equations for polynomial systems and consistent polynomial approximations for non-polynomial Hamiltonians. Our results show that such architectures can provide a robust pathway to interpretable discovery of Hamiltonian dynamics from sparse and noisy data.

</details>


### [243] [Trustworthy deep domain adaptation for wearable photoplethysmography signal analysis with decision-theoretic uncertainty quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17480)
*Ciaran Bench*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes decision-theoretic uncertainty quantification to evaluate generative models for domain adaptation, addressing issues of hallucinations and lack of ground truth evaluation. Demonstrates on photoplethysmography denoising for Atrial Fibrillation classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep generative models can align feature representations for domain adaptation but produce hallucinations/artifacts that degrade downstream performance. Standard uncertainty evaluation doesn't extend well to generative models due to lack of ground truths and is agnostic to downstream task utility.

Method: Decision-theoretic uncertainty quantification framework to evaluate trustworthiness of generated outputs for domain adaptation. Formalizes heuristic of using downstream classifier to assess generated output quality. Applied to photoplethysmography time series denoising for Atrial Fibrillation classification.

Result: The approach provides a principled framework for uncertainty quantification in generative domain adaptation that connects uncertainty reliability to downstream task utility, formalizing existing heuristic methods.

Conclusion: Decision-theoretic uncertainty quantification addresses evaluation challenges for generative domain adaptation by linking uncertainty reliability to practical downstream use cases, providing a more meaningful assessment framework than standard methods.

Abstract: In principle, deep generative models can be used to perform domain adaptation; i.e. align the input feature representations of test data with that of a separate discriminative model's training data. This can help improve the discriminative model's performance on the test data. However, generative models are prone to producing hallucinations and artefacts that may degrade the quality of generated data, and therefore, predictive performance when processed by the discriminative model. While uncertainty quantification can provide a means to assess the quality of adapted data, the standard framework for evaluating the quality of predicted uncertainties may not easily extend to generative models due to the common lack of ground truths (among other reasons). Even with ground truths, this evaluation is agnostic to how the generated outputs are used on the downstream task, limiting the extent to which the uncertainty reliability analysis provides insights about the utility of the uncertainties with respect to the intended use case of the adapted examples. Here, we describe how decision-theoretic uncertainty quantification can address these concerns and provide a convenient framework for evaluating the trustworthiness of generated outputs, in particular, for domain adaptation. We consider a case study in photoplethysmography time series denoising for Atrial Fibrillation classification. This formalises a well-known heuristic method of using a downstream classifier to assess the quality of generated outputs.

</details>


### [244] [A Probabilistic Consensus-Driven Approach for Robust Counterfactual Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17494)
*Marcin Kostrzewa,Maciej Zięba,Jerzy Stefanowski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel method for generating robust counterfactual explanations that uses normalizing flows and model ensembles to maintain validity across model changes with adjustable robustness levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Counterfactual explanations (CFEs) for black-box models often become invalid when models are slightly modified. Existing robust CFE methods have limitations: they're specific to certain model types, require expensive tuning, or lack flexible robustness controls.

Method: Jointly models data distribution and plausible model decision spaces using probabilistic consensus over model ensembles. Trains conditional normalizing flow capturing data density under varying classifier agreement levels. At inference, a single interpretable parameter controls robustness by specifying minimum fraction of models agreeing on target class without retraining the generative model.

Result: The approach effectively pushes CFEs toward regions that are both plausible and stable across model changes. Experimental results show superior empirical robustness while maintaining good performance across other evaluation measures.

Conclusion: The proposed method successfully addresses limitations of existing robust CFE approaches by providing flexible, model-agnostic robustness with interpretable parameter control, achieving both plausibility and stability across model variations.

Abstract: Counterfactual explanations (CFEs) are essential for interpreting black-box models, yet they often become invalid when models are slightly changed. Existing methods for generating robust CFEs are often limited to specific types of models, require costly tuning, or inflexible robustness controls. We propose a novel approach that jointly models the data distribution and the space of plausible model decisions to ensure robustness to model changes. Using a probabilistic consensus over a model ensemble, we train a conditional normalizing flow that captures the data density under varying levels of classifier agreement. At inference time, a single interpretable parameter controls the robustness level; it specifies the minimum fraction of models that should agree on the target class without retraining the generative model. Our method effectively pushes CFEs toward regions that are both plausible and stable across model changes. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves superior empirical robustness while also maintaining good performance across other evaluation measures.

</details>


### [245] [Contraction and Hourglass Persistence for Learning on Graphs, Simplices, and Cells](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17548)
*Mattie Ji,Indradyumna Roy,Vikas Garg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hourglass Persistence introduces topological descriptors that combine graph inclusions and contractions to enhance graph neural networks, offering improved expressivity and stability over traditional persistent homology methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional persistent homology in graph neural networks relies on sequences of subgraph inclusions, which has limitations in capturing structural information. The paper aims to address these shortcomings by exploring contractions as topological operations and creating more powerful topological descriptors.

Method: The paper introduces Contraction Homology (CH) to study persistence through contraction sequences, then develops Hourglass Persistence - topological descriptors that interleave sequences of inclusions and contractions. The framework extends to simplicial and cellular networks, with efficient algorithms designed for end-to-end differentiable GNN pipelines.

Result: Hourglass Persistence shows superior expressivity, learnability, and stability compared to traditional persistent homology methods. The approach enables consistent empirical improvements across standard real-world graph datasets and is integrated into differentiable GNN pipelines with available code.

Conclusion: The combination of inclusions and contractions through Hourglass Persistence provides a more powerful topological framework for graph representation learning, addressing limitations of traditional persistent homology methods while maintaining practical efficiency and integration with neural networks.

Abstract: Persistent homology (PH) encodes global information, such as cycles, and is thus increasingly integrated into graph neural networks (GNNs). PH methods in GNNs typically traverse an increasing sequence of subgraphs. In this work, we first expose limitations of this inclusion procedure. To remedy these shortcomings, we analyze contractions as a principled topological operation, in particular, for graph representation learning. We study the persistence of contraction sequences, which we call Contraction Homology (CH). We establish that forward PH and CH differ in expressivity. We then introduce Hourglass Persistence, a class of topological descriptors that interleave a sequence of inclusions and contractions to boost expressivity, learnability, and stability. We also study related families parametrized by two paradigms. We also discuss how our framework extends to simplicial and cellular networks. We further design efficient algorithms that are pluggable into end-to-end differentiable GNN pipelines, enabling consistent empirical improvements over many PH methods across standard real-world graph datasets. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/Aalto-QuML/Hourglass}{this https URL}.

</details>


### [246] [SVL: Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning as Survival Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17551)
*Franki Nguimatsia Tiofack,Fabian Schramm,Théotime Le Hellard,Justin Carpentier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SVL reframes goal-conditioned RL as survival analysis, modeling time-to-goal as a distribution, enabling stable value estimation via hazard models trained with maximum likelihood on censored trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard TD-based approaches to goal-conditioned RL are unstable and sample-inefficient due to bootstrapping. The paper aims to develop a more stable, probabilistic alternative to improve learning stability and sample efficiency.

Method: Survival Value Learning (SVL) reframes GCRL as survival learning by modeling time-to-goal as a probability distribution. This yields a closed-form identity expressing goal-conditioned value as discounted sum of survival probabilities. Three practical value estimators are introduced: finite-horizon truncation and two binned infinite-horizon approximations. The hazard model is trained via maximum likelihood on both event and right-censored trajectories.

Result: Experiments on offline GCRL benchmarks show SVL combined with hierarchical actors matches or surpasses strong hierarchical TD and Monte Carlo baselines. The method particularly excels on complex, long-horizon tasks.

Conclusion: SVL provides a stable, probabilistic alternative to TD-based GCRL that improves performance on challenging tasks, especially those with long horizons, by leveraging survival analysis principles and maximum likelihood estimation on censored data.

Abstract: Standard approaches to goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) that rely on temporal-difference learning can be unstable and sample-inefficient due to bootstrapping. While recent work has explored contrastive and supervised formulations to improve stability, we present a probabilistic alternative, called survival value learning (SVL), that reframes GCRL as a survival learning problem by modeling the time-to-goal from each state as a probability distribution. This structured distributional Monte Carlo perspective yields a closed-form identity that expresses the goal-conditioned value function as a discounted sum of survival probabilities, enabling value estimation via a hazard model trained via maximum likelihood on both event and right-censored trajectories. We introduce three practical value estimators, including finite-horizon truncation and two binned infinite-horizon approximations to capture long-horizon objectives. Experiments on offline GCRL benchmarks show that SVL combined with hierarchical actors matches or surpasses strong hierarchical TD and Monte Carlo baselines, excelling on complex, long-horizon tasks.

</details>


### [247] [Diverse Dictionary Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17568)
*Yujia Zheng,Zijian Li,Shunxing Fan,Andrew Gordon Wilson,Kun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Partial identifiability in latent variable recovery: Instead of requiring full identifiability (often unrealistic), the paper shows that intersections, complements, and symmetric differences of latent variables, plus dependency structure, remain identifiable under weak assumptions, enabling structured views of hidden variables.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for recovering latent variables Z from observations X = g(Z) rely on strong, often unverifiable assumptions like linearity or auxiliary supervision. These methods fail under mild violations, leaving uncertainty about how to reliably understand hidden variables in real-world scenarios where full identifiability is unattainable.

Method: Introduces "diverse dictionary learning" to formalize partial identifiability. Shows that set-theoretic operations (intersections, complements, symmetric differences) of latent variables and their dependency structure remain identifiable even without strong assumptions. These can be composed via set algebra to construct structured views of the hidden world (e.g., genus-differentia definitions). Includes a simple inductive bias during estimation that can integrate into most models.

Result: Theoretical demonstration that set-theoretic relationships between latent variables and their dependency structure are identifiable up to appropriate indeterminacies. When sufficient structural diversity exists, this implies full identifiability of all latent variables. Validation on synthetic and real-world data shows practical benefits of the proposed inductive bias.

Conclusion: Partial identifiability is a practical alternative to full identifiability. By focusing on recoverable set-theoretic relationships and dependency structures, we can gain actionable insights into hidden variables even when traditional strong assumptions fail, making latent variable recovery more robust in real-world applications.

Abstract: Given only observational data $X = g(Z)$, where both the latent variables $Z$ and the generating process $g$ are unknown, recovering $Z$ is ill-posed without additional assumptions. Existing methods often assume linearity or rely on auxiliary supervision and functional constraints. However, such assumptions are rarely verifiable in practice, and most theoretical guarantees break down under even mild violations, leaving uncertainty about how to reliably understand the hidden world. To make identifiability actionable in the real-world scenarios, we take a complementary view: in the general settings where full identifiability is unattainable, what can still be recovered with guarantees, and what biases could be universally adopted? We introduce the problem of diverse dictionary learning to formalize this view. Specifically, we show that intersections, complements, and symmetric differences of latent variables linked to arbitrary observations, along with the latent-to-observed dependency structure, are still identifiable up to appropriate indeterminacies even without strong assumptions. These set-theoretic results can be composed using set algebra to construct structured and essential views of the hidden world, such as genus-differentia definitions. When sufficient structural diversity is present, they further imply full identifiability of all latent variables. Notably, all identifiability benefits follow from a simple inductive bias during estimation that can be readily integrated into most models. We validate the theory and demonstrate the benefits of the bias on both synthetic and real-world data.

</details>


### [248] [Recovery Guarantees for Continual Learning of Dependent Tasks: Memory, Data-Dependent Regularization, and Data-Dependent Weights](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17578)
*Liangzu Peng,Uday Kiran Reddy Tadipatri,Ziqing Xu,Eric Eaton,René Vidal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides theoretical guarantees for continual learning by modeling task dependencies through nonlinear transformations of previous task data, proving informative statistical recovery bounds for several practical CL paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual learning (CL) faces the challenge of learning multiple tasks sequentially without forgetting, but theoretical development remains limited. The core challenge is that data distributions vary across tasks, which requires understanding task dependency for proper theoretical analysis.

Method: The authors propose a model where nonlinear regression tasks are dependent such that current task data is a nonlinear transformation of previous data. Under this model and natural assumptions, they prove statistical recovery guarantees (estimation error bounds) for several CL paradigms including experience replay with data-independent regularization, data-independent loss balancing weights, replay with data-dependent weights, and continual learning with data-dependent regularization (e.g., knowledge distillation).

Result: The paper proves informative statistical recovery bounds for multiple CL paradigms. Unlike prior work that gives vacuous bounds, their theoretical guarantees remain informative in practical cases, providing rigorous foundations for understanding when and why different CL approaches work.

Conclusion: By explicitly modeling task dependency through nonlinear transformations, the work advances the theoretical understanding of continual learning, providing meaningful statistical guarantees for commonly used CL techniques and bridging the gap between empirical advances and theoretical foundations in the field.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL) is concerned with learning multiple tasks sequentially without forgetting previously learned tasks. Despite substantial empirical advances over recent years, the theoretical development of CL remains in its infancy. At the heart of developing CL theory lies the challenge that the data distribution varies across tasks, and we argue that properly addressing this challenge requires understanding this variation--dependency among tasks. To explicitly model task dependency, we consider nonlinear regression tasks and propose the assumption that these tasks are dependent in such a way that the data of the current task is a nonlinear transformation of previous data. With this model and under natural assumptions, we prove statistical recovery guarantees (more specifically, bounds on estimation errors) for several CL paradigms in practical use, including experience replay with data-independent regularization and data-independent weights that balance the losses of tasks, replay with data-dependent weights, and continual learning with data-dependent regularization (e.g., knowledge distillation). To the best of our knowledge, our bounds are informative in cases where prior work gives vacuous bounds.

</details>


### [249] [How Much Data is Enough? The Zeta Law of Discoverability in Biomedical Data, featuring the enigmatic Riemann zeta function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17581)
*Paul M. Thompson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical framework predicts how much data is needed for scientific discovery using scaling laws based on spectral analysis, showing that performance follows zeta-like scaling governed by covariance spectra and representation learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As biomedical datasets scale to millions of samples and AI models grow, there's limited theoretical guidance on when additional data will improve performance or when models will saturate, leading to inefficient resource allocation in scientific discovery.

Method: Proposes a scaling-law framework based on spectral structure of data covariance operators, task-aligned signal projections, and learned representations. Performance metrics like AUC are expressed as cumulative signal-to-noise energy across spectral modes, leading to zeta-like scaling laws with power-law decay of covariance spectra.

Result: The framework predicts cross-over regimes where simpler models work best at small sample sizes while higher-capacity/multimodal encoders outperform with sufficient data. Representation learning improves sample efficiency by concentrating signal into earlier stable modes, steepening spectral decay. Applications demonstrate this in multimodal disease classification, imaging genetics, and other domains.

Conclusion: The resulting zeta law provides a principled way to anticipate when scaling data, improving representations, or adding modalities will accelerate discovery, offering theoretical guidance for efficient resource allocation in scientific research.

Abstract: How much data is enough to make a scientific discovery? As biomedical datasets scale to millions of samples and AI models grow in capacity, progress increasingly depends on predicting when additional data will substantially improve performance. In practice, model development often relies on empirical scaling curves measured across architectures, modalities, and dataset sizes, with limited theoretical guidance on when performance should improve, saturate, or exhibit cross-over behavior.
  We propose a scaling-law framework for cross-modal discoverability based on spectral structure of data covariance operators, task-aligned signal projections, and learned representations. Many performance metrics, including AUC, can be expressed in terms of cumulative signal-to-noise energy accumulated across identifiable spectral modes of an encoder and cross-modal operator. Under mild assumptions, this accumulation follows a zeta-like scaling law governed by power-law decay of covariance spectra and aligned signal energy, leading naturally to the appearance of the Riemann zeta function. Representation learning methods such as sparse models, low-rank embeddings, and multimodal contrastive objectives improve sample efficiency by concentrating useful signal into earlier stable modes, effectively steepening spectral decay and shifting scaling curves.
  The framework predicts cross-over regimes in which simpler models perform best at small sample sizes, while higher-capacity or multimodal encoders outperform them once sufficient data stabilizes additional degrees of freedom. Applications include multimodal disease classification, imaging genetics, functional MRI, and topological data analysis. The resulting zeta law provides a principled way to anticipate when scaling data, improving representations, or adding modalities is most likely to accelerate discovery.

</details>


### [250] [STEP-PD: Stage-Aware and Explainable Parkinson's Disease Severity Classification Using Multimodal Clinical Assessments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17611)
*Md Mezbahul Islam,John Michael Templeton,Christian Poellabauer,Ananda Mohan Mondal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STEP-PD is a machine learning framework for Parkinson's disease severity classification using multimodal clinical data and interpretable boundaries, achieving high accuracy across binary and three-class severity tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parkinson's disease symptom burden evolves over time, making severity staging essential for clinical monitoring. However, most computational studies focus on binary PD detection and don't fully utilize repeated follow-up assessments for stage-aware prediction.

Method: Proposes STEP-PD, a severity-aware ML framework that leverages all available visits from PPMI, integrating subjective questionnaires and objective clinician-assessed measures. Uses Hoehn and Yahr staging grouped into three categories (Healthy, Mild PD, Moderate-to-Severe PD). Evaluates three binary classification problems and a three-class severity task using stratified cross-validation with imbalance-aware training. Uses XGBoost and SHAP for interpretability.

Result: XGBoost achieved strongest performance: 95.48% accuracy (Healthy vs. Mild), 99.44% (Healthy vs. Moderate-to-Severe), 96.78% (Mild vs. Moderate-to-Severe), and 94.14% accuracy with 0.8775 Macro-F1 for three-class classification. Explainability shows shift from early motor features to progression-related axial and balance impairments.

Conclusion: Multimodal clinical assessments within PPMI cohort can support accurate and interpretable visit-level PD severity stratification, providing valuable tools for clinical monitoring and treatment planning.

Abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a progressive disorder in which symptom burden and functional impairment evolve over time, making severity staging essential for clinical monitoring and treatment planning. However, many computational studies emphasize binary PD detection and do not fully use repeated follow-up clinical assessments for stage-aware prediction. This study proposes STEP-PD, a severity-aware machine learning framework to classify PD severity using clinically interpretable boundaries. It leverages all available visits from the Parkinson's Progression Markers Initiative (PPMI) and integrates routinely collected subjective questionnaires and objective clinician-assessed measures. Disease severity is defined using Hoehn and Yahr staging and grouped into three clinically meaningful categories: Healthy, Mild PD (stages 1-2), and Moderate-to-Severe PD (stages 3-5). Three binary classification problems and a three-class severity task were evaluated using stratified cross-validation with imbalance-aware training. To enhance interpretability, SHAP was used to provide global explanations and local patient-level waterfall explanations. Across all tasks, XGBoost achieved the strongest and most stable performance, with accuracies of 95.48% (Healthy vs. Mild), 99.44% (Healthy vs. Moderate-to-Severe), and 96.78% (Mild vs. Moderate-to-Severe), and 94.14% accuracy with 0.8775 Macro-F1 for three-class severity classification. Explainability results highlight a shift from early motor features to progression-related axial and balance impairments. These findings show that multimodal clinical assessments within the PPMI cohort can support accurate and interpretable visit-level PD severity stratification.

</details>


### [251] [Conditional Attribution for Root Cause Analysis in Time-Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17616)
*Shashank Mishra,Karan Patil,Cedric Schockaert,Didier Stricker,Jason Rambach*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A conditional attribution framework that explains time-series anomalies by retrieving contextually similar normal instances from learned low-dimensional representations, improving root-cause analysis accuracy and reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing explanation methods for time-series anomaly detection rely on unrealistic feature perturbations and ignore temporal and cross-feature dependencies, leading to unreliable attributions for root cause analysis in complex real-world systems.

Method: Proposes a conditional attribution framework that retrieves representative normal instances conditioned on anomalous observations using learned low-dimensional representations (variational autoencoder latent spaces and UMAP manifold embeddings), enabling dependency-preserving explanations without out-of-distribution artifacts.

Result: Experiments on SWaT and MSDS benchmarks show consistent improvements in root-cause identification accuracy, temporal localization, and robustness across multiple anomaly detection models, with the method outperforming existing approaches.

Conclusion: Conditional attribution grounded in system manifolds provides operationally meaningful explanations for time-series anomalies, enhancing explainable anomaly diagnosis in complex systems with practical utility demonstrated through improved performance metrics.

Abstract: Root cause analysis (RCA) for time-series anomaly detection is critical for the reliable operation of complex real-world systems. Existing explanation methods often rely on unrealistic feature perturbations and ignore temporal and cross-feature dependencies, leading to unreliable attributions. We propose a conditional attribution framework that explains anomalies relative to contextually similar normal system states. Instead of using marginal or randomly sampled baselines, our method retrieves representative normal instances conditioned on the anomalous observation, enabling dependency-preserving and operationally meaningful explanations. To support high-dimensional time-series data, contextual retrieval is performed in learned low-dimensional representations using both variational autoencoder latent spaces and UMAP manifold embeddings. By grounding the retrieval process in the system's learned manifold, this strategy avoids out-of-distribution artifacts and ensures attribution fidelity while maintaining computational efficiency. We further introduce confidence-aware and temporal evaluation metrics for assessing explanation reliability and responsiveness. Experiments on the SWaT and MSDS benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently improves root-cause identification accuracy, temporal localization, and robustness across multiple anomaly detection models. These results highlight the practical utility of conditional attribution for explainable anomaly diagnosis in complex time-series systems. Code and models will be publicly released.

</details>


### [252] [STRIKE: Additive Feature-Group-Aware Stacking Framework for Credit Default Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17622)
*Swattik Maiti,Ritik Pratap Singh,Fardina Fathmiul Alam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STRIKE is a feature-group-aware stacking framework that decomposes credit risk datasets into semantic feature groups, trains independent learners on each group, and aggregates predictions through a meta-learner, outperforming traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional credit risk models face challenges with high-dimensional, heterogeneous, and noisy datasets, leading to overfitting and reduced robustness under distributional shifts. There's a need for more stable and interpretable approaches that can leverage the complementary nature of different feature sources in risk modeling.

Method: STRIKE partitions the feature space into semantically coherent groups and trains independent learners within each group. This decomposition follows an additive perspective where different feature sources contribute complementary evidence. Group-specific predictions are then integrated through a meta-learner that aggregates signals while maintaining robustness and modularity.

Result: STRIKE consistently outperforms strong tree-based baselines and conventional stacking approaches across three real-world datasets spanning corporate bankruptcy and consumer lending scenarios in terms of AUC-ROC. Ablation studies confirm performance gains stem from meaningful feature decomposition rather than increased model complexity.

Conclusion: STRIKE provides a stable, scalable, and interpretable framework for credit risk default prediction that effectively addresses challenges with modern high-dimensional credit datasets through structured feature decomposition and aggregation.

Abstract: Credit risk default prediction remains a cornerstone of risk management in the financial industry. The task involves estimating the likelihood that a borrower will fail to meet debt obligations, an objective critical for lending decisions, portfolio optimization, and regulatory compliance. Traditional machine learning models such as logistic regression and tree-based ensembles are widely adopted for their interpretability and strong empirical performance. However, modern credit datasets are high-dimensional, heterogeneous, and noisy, increasing overfitting risk in monolithic models and reducing robustness under distributional shift. We introduce STRIKE (Stacking via Targeted Representations of Isolated Knowledge Extractors), a feature-group-aware stacking framework for structured tabular credit risk data. Rather than training a single monolithic model on the complete dataset, STRIKE partitions the feature space into semantically coherent groups and trains independent learners within each group. This decomposition is motivated by an additive perspective on risk modeling, where distinct feature sources contribute complementary evidence that can be combined through a structured aggregation. The resulting group-specific predictions are integrated through a meta-learner that aggregates signals while maintaining robustness and modularity. We evaluate STRIKE on three real-world datasets spanning corporate bankruptcy and consumer lending scenarios. Across all settings, STRIKE consistently outperforms strong tree-based baselines and conventional stacking approaches in terms of AUC-ROC. Ablation studies confirm that performance gains stem from meaningful feature decomposition rather than increased model complexity. Our findings demonstrate that STRIKE is a stable, scalable, and interpretable framework for credit risk default prediction tasks.

</details>


### [253] [SLO-Guard: Crash-Aware, Budget-Consistent Autotuning for SLO-Constrained LLM Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17627)
*Christian Lysenstøen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SLO-Guard is a crash-aware autotuner for vLLM serving that treats crashes as first-class observations, combining feasible-first exploration with warm-started exploitation to spend tuning budget more predictably once fast configurations are found.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Serving large language models under latency SLOs is configuration-heavy with a failure-prone search space where many configurations crash or miss targets, and standard black-box optimizers treat crashes as wasted trials.

Method: SLO-Guard combines a feasible-first Thermal Budget Annealing (TBA) exploration phase with a warm-started Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE) exploitation phase, encoding crashes as extreme constraint violations, plus configuration-repair and GPU-aware KV-cache memory guard.

Result: SLO-Guard achieves same feasibility as random search but with better budget consistency: more trials in fast-serving regime (10.20 vs. 7.40/15), higher post-handoff consistency (0.876 vs. 0.539), and 4.4x tighter standard deviation on best latency under concurrent load.

Conclusion: SLO-Guard's advantage isn't finding better final configurations, but spending fixed tuning budget more predictably once fast regimes are found, with consistent performance across seeds and conditions.

Abstract: Serving large language models under latency service-level objectives (SLOs) is a configuration-heavy systems problem with an unusually failure-prone search space: many plausible configurations crash outright or miss user-visible latency targets, and standard black-box optimizers treat these failures as wasted trials. We present SLO-Guard, a crash-aware autotuner for vLLM serving that treats crashes as first-class observations. SLO-Guard combines a feasible-first Thermal Budget Annealing (TBA) exploration phase with a warm-started Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE) exploitation phase; the handoff replays all exploration history, including crashes encoded as extreme constraint violations. We additionally contribute a configuration-repair pass, a GPU-aware KV-cache memory guard, and a four-category crash taxonomy.
  We evaluate SLO-Guard on Qwen2-1.5B served with vLLM 0.19 on an NVIDIA A100 40GB. Across a pre-specified five-seed study, both SLO-Guard and uniform random search attain 75/75 feasibility with zero crashes under the corrected concurrent harness, and are statistically tied on best-achieved latency (Mann-Whitney two-sided p=0.84). SLO-Guard's advantage is in budget consistency: more trials in the fast-serving regime (10.20 vs. 7.40 out of 15; one-sided p=0.014) and higher post-handoff consistency (0.876 vs. 0.539; p=0.010). Under concurrent load, SLO-Guard's cross-seed standard deviation on best latency is 4.4x tighter than random search's (2.26 ms vs. 10.00 ms). A harness-replication analysis shows that the consistency findings survive an independent sequential-dispatch measurement condition.
  The central claim is not that SLO-Guard finds a better final configuration, but that it spends a fixed tuning budget more predictably once the fast regime has been found.

</details>


### [254] [ATLAS: Constitution-Conditioned Latent Geometry and Redistribution Across Language Models and Neural Perturbation Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17663)
*Gareth Seneque,Lap-Hang Ho,Nafise Erfanian Saeedi,Jeffrey Molendijk,Tim Elson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Constitution-conditioned post-training induces structured geometric perturbations that create recoverable latent geometry detectable across models and substrates, though coordinate identity isn't preserved.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how constitution-conditioned post-training affects model representations geometrically, rather than treating it as just behavioral changes, and to trace how these geometric structures transfer across different models and substrates.

Method: Developed ATLAS, a geometry-first program that traces constitution-induced hidden-state structure across charts, models, and substrates.
