Analyzes local charts with measurable tangent structure, occupancy distribution, and behavioral coupling under system change rather than focusing on single units.

Result: On Gemma, source-local chart captures 310/320 source rows and all 84 score-flip rows. In unadapted Phi model, target-local realization separates with AUC 0.984. In mouse cortex data, same source-defined family receives support across all folds with mean AUC 0.72. Main boundary: nearby target-local signals can appear without source-faithful closure.

Conclusion: Constitution-induced geometric recurrences form recoverable latent structures that persist across model/substrate changes, but these correspondences aren't about coordinate/site identity - they're about geometric recurrence under redistribution where organizational patterns remain detectable despite shifting coordinates and expressions.

Abstract: Constitution-conditioned post-training can be analysed as a structured perturbation of a model's learned representational geometry. We introduce ATLAS, a geometry-first program that traces constitution-induced hidden-state structure across charts, models, and substrates. Instead of treating the relevant unit as a single behaviour, neuron, vector, or patch, ATLAS tests a local chart whose tangent structure, occupancy distribution, and behavioural coupling can be measured under system change. On Gemma, the anchored source-local chart captures 310 / 320 reviewed source rows and all 84 / 84 reviewed score-flip rows, but compact exact-patch sufficiency does not close, so the exportable unit is the broader source-defined family. Freezing that family, we re-identify a target-local realisation in an unadapted Phi model, where the fully adjudicated confirmatory contrast separates with AUC 0.984 and mean gap 5.50. In held-out ALM8 mouse frontal-cortex perturbation data, the same source-defined family receives support across 5/5 folds, with mean held-out AUC 0.72 and mean fold gap 4.50. A multiple-choice analysis provides the main boundary: nearby target-local signals can appear without source-faithful closure. The resulting correspondence is not coordinate identity, site identity, or a target-side mediation theorem. It is geometric recurrence under redistribution: written constitutions can induce recoverable latent geometry whose organisation remains detectable across model and substrate changes while its local coordinates, occupancy, and behavioural expression shift.

</details>


### [255] [Prior-Fitted Functional Flow: In-Context Generative Models for Pharmacokinetics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17670)
*César Ojeda,Niklas Hartung,Wilhelm Huisinga,Tim Jahn,Purity Kamene Kavwele,Marian Klose,Piyush Kumar,Ramsés J. Sánchez,Darius A. Faroughy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PFFFs is a zero-shot generative foundation model for pharmacokinetics that learns functional vector fields from sparse population data to synthesize virtual cohorts and forecast individual trajectories without parameter tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address challenges in pharmacokinetics modeling, particularly the need for manual parameter tuning, difficulty in generating coherent virtual cohorts, and limited ability to forecast individual patient trajectories with uncertainty quantification from sparse, irregular data.

Method: The method introduces Prior-Fitted Functional Flows (PFFFs), which learn functional vector fields conditioned on sparse, irregular data from an entire study population. The approach uses a new open-access literature corpus to inform priors and enables zero-shot population synthesis and individual forecasting with calibrated uncertainty.

Result: The model demonstrates state-of-the-art predictive accuracy on extensive real-world datasets, enabling generation of coherent virtual cohorts and forecasting of partially observed patient trajectories with calibrated uncertainty quantification.

Conclusion: PFFFs represent a significant advancement in pharmacokinetics modeling as a generative foundation model that eliminates manual parameter tuning while providing robust population synthesis and individual forecasting capabilities with uncertainty quantification.

Abstract: We introduce Prior-Fitted Functional Flows, a generative foundation model for pharmacokinetics that enables zero-shot population synthesis and individual forecasting without manual parameter tuning. We learn functional vector fields, explicitly conditioned on the sparse, irregular data of an entire study population. This enables the generation of coherent virtual cohorts as well as forecasting of partially observed patient trajectories with calibrated uncertainty. We construct a new open-access literature corpus to inform our priors, and demonstrate state-of-the-art predictive accuracy on extensive real-world datasets.

</details>


### [256] [Grokking of Diffusion Models: Case Study on Modular Addition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17673)
*Joon Hyeok Kim,Yong-Hyun Park,Mattis Dalsætra Østby,Jiatao Gu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion models trained with flow-matching exhibit grokking on modular addition, revealing how they learn algorithmic tasks through internal periodic representations and phase-separated computation during iterative sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite empirical success, there's poor understanding of how diffusion models generalize from a mechanistic perspective. The paper aims to analyze the internal computations and generalization mechanisms of diffusion models.

Method: The researchers demonstrate that diffusion models trained with flow-matching objectives show grokking (delayed generalization after overfitting) on modular addition tasks. They study this across two data regimes: 1) single-image regime where they perform mechanistic dissection, and 2) diverse-image regime with high intraclass variability. They analyze how models implement modular addition and leverage iterative sampling processes.

Result: In the single-image regime, the model implements modular addition by composing periodic representations of individual operands. In the diverse-image regime, the model partitions the task into an arithmetic computation phase followed by a visual denoising phase, separated by a critical timestep threshold. The model leverages its iterative sampling process to separate these computational phases.

Conclusion: The work provides a mechanistic decomposition of algorithmic learning in diffusion models, revealing how these models bridge continuous pixel-space generation and discrete symbolic reasoning through internal representations and phased computation during the sampling process.

Abstract: Despite their empirical success, how diffusion models generalize remains poorly understood from a mechanistic perspective. We demonstrate that diffusion models trained with flow-matching objectives exhibit grokking--delayed generalization after overfitting--on modular addition, enabling controlled analysis of their internal computations. We study this phenomenon across two levels of data regime. In a single-image regime, mechanistic dissection reveals that the model implements modular addition by composing periodic representations of individual operands. In a diverse-image regime with high intraclass variability, we find that the model leverages its iterative sampling process to partition the task into an arithmetic computation phase followed by a visual denoising phase, separated by a critical timestep threshold. Our work provides the mechanistic decomposition of algorithmic learning in diffusion models, revealing how these models bridge continuous pixel-space generation and discrete symbolic reasoning.

</details>


### [257] [SafeAnchor: Preventing Cumulative Safety Erosion in Continual Domain Adaptation of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17691)
*Dongxin Guo,Jikun Wu,Siu Ming Yiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SafeAnchor anchors safety in LLMs during continual multi-domain adaptation by identifying safety subspaces, constraining domain updates orthogonal to them, and monitoring for drift with corrective replay.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current safety alignment in LLMs is shallow and fragile - concentrated in first few tokens and reversible with minimal adversarial fine-tuning. This becomes critical in real-world deployment where models undergo sequential adaptation across domains (medicine, law, code), causing cumulative erosion of safety guardrails. Existing methods only target single-task fine-tuning, leaving multi-domain sequential adaptation unaddressed.

Method: SafeAnchor: 1) Identifies low-rank safety subspaces in LoRA parameter space via Fisher Information eigendecomposition, 2) Constrains domain-specific gradient updates to the orthogonal complement of these safety subspaces, 3) Monitors for residual safety drift with threshold-triggered corrective replay.

Result: Evaluated on Llama-2-7B-Chat and Mistral-7B-Instruct across three-domain pipeline and eight benchmarks. SafeAnchor retains 93.2% of original safety alignment, outperforming all baselines by 18-42 points, while matching unconstrained fine-tuning to within 1.5 points on domain tasks.

Conclusion: SafeAnchor effectively anchors safety throughout continual adaptation, addressing a critical gap in safety preservation during multi-domain sequential fine-tuning while maintaining strong performance on domain-specific tasks.

Abstract: Safety alignment in large language models is remarkably shallow: it is concentrated in the first few output tokens and reversible by fine-tuning on as few as 100 adversarial examples. This fragility becomes critical in real-world deployment, where models undergo sequential adaptation across domains such as medicine, law, and code, causing safety guardrails to erode cumulatively. Yet all existing safety-preserving methods target only single-task fine-tuning, leaving the multi-domain sequential setting entirely unaddressed.
  We introduce SafeAnchor, a framework that anchors safety in place throughout continual adaptation. SafeAnchor first identifies low-rank safety subspaces in LoRA parameter space via Fisher Information eigendecomposition, then constrains domain-specific gradient updates to the orthogonal complement of these subspaces, and finally monitors for residual safety drift with threshold-triggered corrective replay. Evaluated on Llama-2-7B-Chat and Mistral-7B-Instruct across a three-domain pipeline and eight benchmarks, SafeAnchor retains 93.2% of original safety alignment, outperforming all baselines by 18-42 points, while matching unconstrained fine-tuning to within 1.5 points on domain tasks.

</details>


### [258] [MoE-nD: Per-Layer Mixture-of-Experts Routing for Multi-Axis KV Cache Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17695)
*Libo Sun,Peixiong He,Po-Wei Harn,Xiao Qin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoE-nD is a mixture-of-experts framework that applies heterogeneous per-layer compression (token eviction and quantization) to KV cache memory for long-context LLM inference, achieving 14x compression with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV cache memory is the dominant bottleneck for long-context LLM inference. Existing compression methods apply uniform compression recipes across all layers, ignoring that different layers respond very differently to various compression operations, leaving accuracy improvements on the table.

Method: Proposed MoE-nD framework routes each layer to its own (eviction-ratio, K-bits, V-bits) tuple under a global memory budget. Uses an offline-calibrated greedy solver to choose routing that minimizes predicted quality loss. At inference, applies per-layer heterogeneous eviction and quantization jointly through a single attention patch.

Result: On 4-task LongBench-v1 subset (16k inputs), MoE-nD's hetero variant matches uncompressed 1.9GB baseline at 14x compression (136MB), while other compressed baselines perform poorly (under 8/100). Gains hold on AIME reasoning benchmarks (+6 to +27 points over strongest per-layer-quantization baseline). Shows principled understanding of when per-layer eviction routing helps vs. doesn't.

Conclusion: Per-layer heterogeneous compression of KV cache via mixture-of-experts routing significantly outperforms uniform compression methods, achieving substantial memory savings (14x compression) with minimal accuracy loss for long-context LLM inference. The approach cleanly characterizes when such techniques are beneficial versus when they provide limited gains.

Abstract: KV cache memory is the dominant bottleneck for long-context LLM inference. Existing compression methods each act on a single axis of the four-dimensional KV tensor -- token eviction (sequence), quantization (precision), low-rank projection (head dimension), or cross-layer sharing -- but apply the same recipe to every layer. We show that this homogeneity leaves accuracy on the table: different layers respond very differently to each compression operation, and the optimal per-layer mix of eviction and quantization is far from uniform. We propose MoE-nD, a mixture-of-experts framework that routes each layer to its own (eviction-ratio, K-bits, V-bits) tuple under a global memory budget. An offline-calibrated greedy solver chooses the routing that minimizes predicted quality loss; at inference time, per-layer heterogeneous eviction and quantization are applied jointly through a single attention patch. On a 4-task subset of LongBench-v1 (16k inputs, n=50 per task, adapted reasoning-model protocol; see section Experiments), MoE-nD's hetero variant matches our uncompressed 1.9~GB baseline at 14x compression (136~MB) while every other compressed baseline we tested (1d, 2d_uniform, 2d) at comparable or smaller memory stays under 8/100. The gains hold on AIME reasoning benchmarks (+6 to +27 pts over the strongest per-layer-quantization baseline across eight configurations). Two null results -- MATH-500 and LongBench's TREC -- share a principled cause (short inputs, solver picks keep=1.0 on most layers), cleanly characterizing when per-layer eviction routing has headroom to help.

</details>


### [259] [The Geometric Canary: Predicting Steerability and Detecting Drift via Representational Stability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17698)
*Prashant C. Raju*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Geometric stability measures can predict language model controllability (supervised) and detect degradation (unsupervised), providing complementary diagnostics for LLM deployment lifecycle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two critical capabilities for reliable language model deployment: predicting whether models will accept targeted behavioral control, and detecting when internal structures degrade. These appear distinct but share a common geometric foundation.

Method: Use geometric stability (consistency of representation's pairwise distance structure) with supervised variants for controllability prediction and unsupervised variants for drift detection. Test across 35-69 embedding models and three NLP tasks.

Result: Supervised Shesha variants predict linear steerability with near-perfect accuracy (ρ=0.89-0.97) and capture unique variance beyond class separability. Unsupervised stability fails for steering but excels at drift detection (2× greater geometric change than CKA, earlier warning in 73% of models, 6× lower false alarm rate than Procrustes).

Conclusion: Supervised and unsupervised geometric stability form complementary diagnostics: supervised for pre-deployment controllability assessment, unsupervised for post-deployment monitoring, together addressing the full LLM deployment lifecycle.

Abstract: Reliable deployment of language models requires two capabilities that appear distinct but share a common geometric foundation: predicting whether a model will accept targeted behavioral control, and detecting when its internal structure degrades. We show that geometric stability, the consistency of a representation's pairwise distance structure, addresses both. Supervised Shesha variants that measure task-aligned geometric stability predict linear steerability with near-perfect accuracy ($ρ= 0.89$-$0.97$) across 35-69 embedding models and three NLP tasks, capturing unique variance beyond class separability (partial $ρ= 0.62$-$0.76$). A critical dissociation emerges: unsupervised stability fails entirely for steering on real-world tasks ($ρ\approx 0.10$), revealing that task alignment is essential for controllability prediction. However, unsupervised stability excels at drift detection, measuring nearly $2\times$ greater geometric change than CKA during post-training alignment (up to $5.23\times$ in Llama) while providing earlier warning in 73\% of models and maintaining a $6\times$ lower false alarm rate than Procrustes. Together, supervised and unsupervised stability form complementary diagnostics for the LLM deployment lifecycle: one for pre-deployment controllability assessment, the other for post-deployment monitoring.

</details>


### [260] [Modeling Higher-Order Brain Interactions via a Multi-View Information Bottleneck Framework for fMRI-based Psychiatric Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17713)
*Kunyu Zhang,Qiang Li,Vince D. Calhoun,Shujian Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces O-information, a signed measure to characterize higher-order brain interactions, and integrates it into a multi-view information bottleneck framework for fMRI-based psychiatric diagnosis, achieving computational efficiency and outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current resting-state fMRI approaches for psychiatric diagnosis rely on pairwise brain connectivities that overlook higher-order interactions central to complex brain dynamics. Existing hypergraph methods use heuristic similarity metrics and don't characterize whether interactions are synergy- or redundancy-dominated.

Method: Introduces O-information as a signed measure for informational nature of higher-order interactions. Develops acceleration strategies (Gaussian analytical approximation and randomized matrix-based Rényi entropy estimator) for scalable O-information estimation. Integrates third- and fourth-order O-information into a unified multi-view information bottleneck framework that fuses pairwise, triadic, and tetradic brain interactions.

Result: Achieves over 30-fold computational speedup compared to conventional estimators. Outperforms 11 baseline methods including state-of-the-art GNN and hypergraph approaches across four benchmark datasets (REST-meta-MDD, ABIDE, UCLA, ADNI). Reveals interpretable region-level synergy-redundancy patterns not captured by conventional hypergraph formulations.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides an efficient and interpretable approach to capture higher-order brain interactions for psychiatric diagnosis, addressing limitations of existing methods by explicitly characterizing synergy-redundancy patterns and achieving superior performance across multiple datasets.

Abstract: Resting-state functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has emerged as a cornerstone for psychiatric diagnosis, yet most approaches rely on pairwise brain cortical or sub-cortical connectivities that overlooks higher-order interactions (HOIs) central to complex brain dynamics. While hypergraph methods encode HOIs through predefined hyperedges, their construction typically relies on heuristic similarity metrics and does not explicitly characterize whether interactions are synergy- or redundancy-dominated. In this paper, we introduce $O$-information, a signed measure that characterizes the informational nature of HOIs, and integrate third- and fourth-order $O$-information into a unified multi-view information bottleneck framework for fMRI-based psychiatric diagnosis. To enable scalable $O$-information estimation, we further develop two independent acceleration strategies: a Gaussian analytical approximation and a randomized matrix-based Rényi entropy estimator, achieving over a 30-fold computational speedup compared with conventional estimators. Our tri-view architecture systematically fuses pairwise, triadic, and tetradic brain interactions, capturing comprehensive brain connectivity while explicitly penalizing redundancy. Extensive evaluation across four benchmark datasets (REST-meta-MDD, ABIDE, UCLA, ADNI) demonstrates consistent improvements, outperforming 11 baseline methods including state-of-the-art graph neural network (GNN) and hypergraph based approaches. Moreover, our method reveals interpretable region-level synergy-redundancy patterns which are not explicitly characterized by conventional hypergraph formulations.

</details>


### [261] [FlashFPS: Efficient Farthest Point Sampling for Large-Scale Point Clouds via Pruning and Caching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17720)
*Yuzhe Fu,Hancheng Ye,Cong Guo,Junyao Zhang,Qinsi Wang,Yueqian Lin,Changchun Zhou,Hai,Li,Yiran Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlashFPS is a hardware-agnostic framework that accelerates Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) in point-based neural networks by eliminating redundant computations through pruning and caching techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) introduces significant inference latency in point-based neural networks (PNNs), especially for large-scale processing. Despite existing optimizations, FPS remains a major bottleneck due to exhaustive computations across multiple network layers, hindering scalability.

Method: The proposed FlashFPS framework consists of two components: FPS-Prune and FPS-Cache. FPS-Prune reduces redundant computations through candidate pruning (removing unnecessary full-cloud computations) and iteration pruning (eliminating redundant late-stage iterations). FPS-Cache eliminates layer-wise redundancy via cache-and-reuse of predictable inter-layer outputs.

Result: FlashFPS achieves 5.16× speedup over standard CUDA baseline on GPU and 2.69× speedup on PNN accelerators, with negligible accuracy loss. It's integrated into existing CUDA libraries and state-of-the-art PNN accelerators.

Conclusion: FlashFPS enables efficient and scalable PNN inference by addressing the computational redundancies in FPS through a hardware-agnostic, plug-and-play framework that significantly reduces inference latency while maintaining sampling quality.

Abstract: Point-based Neural Networks (PNNs) have become a key approach for point cloud processing. However, a core operation in these models, Farthest Point Sampling (FPS), often introduces significant inference latency, especially for large-scale processing. Despite existing CUDA- and hardware-level optimizations, FPS remains a major bottleneck due to exhaustive computations across multiple network layers in PNNs, which hinders scalability. Through systematic analysis, we identify three substantial redundancies in FPS, including unnecessary full-cloud computations, redundant late-stage iterations, and predictable inter-layer outputs that make later FPS computations avoidable. To address these, we propose \textbf{\textit{FlashFPS}}, a hardware-agnostic, plug-and-play framework for FPS acceleration, composed of \textit{FPS-Prune} and \textit{FPS-Cache}. \textit{FPS-Prune} introduces candidate pruning and iteration pruning to reduce redundant computations in FPS while preserving sampling quality, and \textit{FPS-Cache} eliminates layer-wise redundancy via cache-and-reuse. Integrated into existing CUDA libraries and state-of-the-art PNN accelerators, \textit{FlashFPS} achieves 5.16$\times$ speedup over the standard CUDA baseline on GPU and 2.69$\times$ on PNN accelerators, with negligible accuracy loss, enabling efficient and scalable PNN inference. Codes are released at https://github.com/Yuzhe-Fu/FlashFPS.

</details>


### [262] [Tool Learning Needs Nothing More Than a Free 8B Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17739)
*Chenming Tang,Hsiu-Yuan Huang,Weijie Liu,Junqiang Zheng,Saiyong Yang,Yunfang Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRUSTEE is a data-free reinforcement learning method that trains tool-calling agents using dynamic environments simulated by small open-source language models (as small as 8B), with adaptive curriculum learning to control task difficulty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL approaches for tool calling agents require either expensive ground truth annotations or fixed environments created by advanced commercial LMs, creating barriers to accessible and adaptable training.

Method: TRUSTEE uses free open-source LMs to simulate all aspects of the training environment: task generation, user simulation, tool simulation, and trajectory evaluation. It incorporates an adaptive curriculum learning mechanism that dynamically controls various aspects of task difficulty during training.

Result: TRUSTEE brings consistent improvements across various domains and outperforms baselines that require extra external resources. It demonstrates that simulated environments with a local 8B LM can set a strong baseline for tool learning without expensive resources.

Conclusion: With sophisticated design, even simulated environments using small open-source LMs can enable effective tool learning without costly resources, offering a new paradigm for environment scaling with limited resources.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a prevalent paradigm for training tool calling agents, which typically requires online interactive environments. Existing approaches either rely on training data with ground truth annotations or require advanced commercial language models (LMs) to synthesize environments that keep fixed once created. In this work, we propose TRUSTEE, a data-free method training tool calling agents with dynamic environments fully simulated by free open-source LMs that can be as small as 8B, including task generation, user simulation, tool simulation and trajectory evaluation, paired with an adaptive curriculum learning mechanism that controls various aspects of the task difficulty dynamically during training. Our empirical results show that TRUSTEE brings consistent improvements across various domains and outperforms all the baselines which require extra external resources for training. These confirm that, with a sufficiently sophisticated design, even simulated environments with a local 8B LM as the backbone could set a strong baseline for tool learning, without expensive annotated data, realistic human interactions, executable tools or costly verifiable environments from human experts or commercial LMs. We hope our proposed paradigm could inspire future research on environment scaling with limited resources.

</details>


### [263] [Efficient Federated RLHF via Zeroth-Order Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17747)
*Deyi Wang,Qining Zhang,Lei Ying*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated RLHF algorithm using zeroth-order optimization with binary perturbation for resource-constrained edge devices, achieving efficient convergence with low communication/computation costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) in federated learning settings with resource-constrained agents like edge devices, where traditional methods are too communication and computationally expensive.

Method: Proposed Par-S²ZPO algorithm: Partitioned, Sign-based Stochastic Zeroth-order Policy Optimization using binary perturbation for low communication, computation, and memory complexity; operates in federated learning framework.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows convergence rate similar to centralized RLHF in sample complexity but faster in policy update iterations; experimental results outperform FedAvg-based RLHF on four MuJoCo tasks.

Conclusion: Par-S²ZPO is an efficient federated RLHF algorithm suitable for resource-constrained edge devices, achieving practical performance while maintaining theoretical convergence guarantees and lower resource requirements.

Abstract: This paper considers reinforcement learning from human feedback in a federated learning setting with resource-constrained agents, such as edge devices. We propose an efficient federated RLHF algorithm, named Partitioned, Sign-based Stochastic Zeroth-order Policy Optimization (Par-S$^2$ZPO). The algorithm is built on zeroth-order optimization with binary perturbation, resulting in low communication, computation, and memory complexity by design. Our theoretical analysis establishes an upper bound on the convergence rate of Par-S$^2$ZPO, revealing that it is as efficient as its centralized counterpart in terms of sample complexity but converges faster in terms of policy update iterations. Our experimental results show that it outperforms a FedAvg-based RLHF on four MuJoCo RL tasks.

</details>


### [264] [HiP-LoRA: Budgeted Spectral Plasticity for Robust Low-Rank Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17751)
*Lixian Chen,Jianhong Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HiP-LoRA is a spectrum-aware adaptation framework that addresses spectral interference in LoRA by decomposing parameter-efficient updates into principal and residual channels, enabling better preservation of pretrained capabilities while maintaining task-specific plasticity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA suffer from spectral interference, where low-rank updates concentrate on leading singular directions of pretrained weights, causing catastrophic forgetting, fragile multi-adapter merging, and perturbation of general capabilities.

Method: HiP-LoRA uses cached singular value decomposition (SVD) of pretrained layers to decompose updates into: 1) principal channel within dominant singular subspace, and 2) residual low-rank channel in orthogonal complement, with singular-value-weighted stability budget balancing preservation and plasticity.

Result: On Llama-3.1-8B, HiP-LoRA under matched budgets drastically reduces pretraining degradation and multi-adapter MergeFail, robustly outperforming baselines in interference-sensitive tasks like continual tuning and knowledge editing.

Conclusion: HiP-LoRA effectively resolves spectral interference in parameter-efficient fine-tuning, enabling better preservation of pretrained capabilities while maintaining adaptation flexibility, particularly valuable for resource-constrained adaptation scenarios.

Abstract: Adapting foundation models under resource budgets relies heavily on Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT), with LoRA being a standard modular solution. However, LoRA suffers from spectral interference. Low-rank updates often concentrate energy on the leading singular directions of pretrained weights, perturbing general capabilities and causing catastrophic forgetting and fragile multi-adapter merging. To resolve this, we propose HiP-LoRA, a spectrum-aware adaptation framework. Utilizing the cached singular value decomposition (SVD) of pretrained layers, HiP-LoRA decomposes updates into two channels: a principal channel within the dominant singular subspace, and a residual low-rank channel in the orthogonal complement. A singular-value-weighted stability budget on the principal channel continuously balances pretrained behavior preservation with task-specific plasticity. Experiments on Llama-3.1-8B demonstrate that under matched budgets, HiP-LoRA drastically reduces pretraining degradation and multi-adapter MergeFail, robustly outperforming baselines in interference-sensitive tasks like continual tuning and knowledge editing.

</details>


### [265] [LLM-AUG: Robust Wireless Data Augmentation with In-Context Learning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17770)
*Pranshav Gajjar,Manan Tiwari,Sayanta Seth,Vijay K. Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM-AUG uses large language models to generate synthetic training samples in embedding space for wireless communication tasks, outperforming traditional augmentation methods in low-data scenarios and showing robustness to distribution shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data scarcity in wireless communication deep learning applications, where collecting labeled RF data is expensive, time-consuming, or operationally constrained.

Method: LLM-AUG framework leveraging in-context learning in LLMs to generate synthetic training samples directly in learned embedding space through structured prompting, enabling rapid adaptation in low-shot regimes without training task-specific models.

Result: Outperforms traditional augmentation and deep generative baselines across low-shot settings, reaching near oracle performance with only 15% labeled data. Shows improved robustness under distribution shifts with 29.4% relative gain over diffusion-based augmentation at lower SNR. Achieves 67.6% and 35.7% relative gains over diffusion baseline on RadioML and IC datasets respectively. t-SNE visualizations confirm synthetic samples preserve class structure in embedding space.

Conclusion: LLMs can serve as effective and practical data augmenters for wireless machine learning, enabling robust and data-efficient learning in evolving wireless environments.

Abstract: Data scarcity remains a fundamental bottleneck in applying deep learning to wireless communication problems, particularly in scenarios where collecting labeled Radio Frequency (RF) data is expensive, time-consuming, or operationally constrained. This paper proposes LLM-AUG, a data augmentation framework that leverages in-context learning in large language models (LLMs) to generate synthetic training samples directly in a learned embedding space. Unlike conventional generative approaches that require training task-specific models, LLM-AUG performs data generation through structured prompting, enabling rapid adaptation in low-shot regimes. We evaluate LLM-AUG on two representative tasks: modulation classification and interference classification using the RadioML 2016.10A dataset, and the Interference Classification (IC) dataset respectively. Results show that LLM-AUG consistently outperforms traditional augmentation and deep generative baselines across low-shot settings and reaches near oracle performance using only 15% labeled data. LLM-AUG further demonstrates improved robustness under distribution shifts, yielding a 29.4% relative gain over diffusion-based augmentation at a lower SNR value. On the RadioML and IC datasets, LLM-AUG yields a relative gain of 67.6% and 35.7% over the diffusion-based baseline. The t-SNE visualizations further validate that synthetic samples generated by better preserve class structure in the embedding space, leading to more consistent and informative augmentations. These results demonstrate that LLMs can serve as effective and practical data augmenters for wireless machine learning, enabling robust and data-efficient learning in evolving wireless environments.

</details>


### [266] [TeleEmbedBench: A Multi-Corpus Embedding Benchmark for RAG in Telecommunications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17778)
*Pranshav Gajjar,Vijay K Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TeleEmbedBench: First large-scale embedding benchmark for telecommunications that reveals LLM-based embedders outperform traditional models on dense, acronym-heavy telecom documents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing embedding model evaluations fail to capture the unique characteristics of telecommunications corpora (dense, acronym-heavy, cross-referential), creating a gap in evaluating RAG pipelines for telecom domain deployment.

Method: Created TeleEmbedBench with 9,000 question-chunk pairs across three telecom corpora (O-RAN, 3GPP, srsRAN) using automated pipeline: one LLM generates queries from text chunks, another validates them. Evaluated 8 embedding models (sentence-transformers vs LLM-based).

Result: LLM-based embedders (Qwen3, EmbeddingGemma) consistently outperform traditional sentence-transformers in both retrieval accuracy and robustness. Domain-specific instructions help for source code but degrade performance for natural language telecom specs.

Conclusion: Telecommunications needs specialized embedding benchmarks like TeleEmbedBench; LLM-based embedders show superior performance for telecom RAG pipelines; domain-specific instructions have mixed effects depending on content type.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in the telecommunications domain for critical tasks, relying heavily on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to adapt general-purpose models to continuously evolving standards. However, a significant gap exists in evaluating the embedding models that power these RAG pipelines, as general-purpose benchmarks fail to capture the dense, acronym-heavy, and highly cross-referential nature of telecommunications corpora. To address this, we introduce TeleEmbedBench, the first large-scale, multi-corpus embedding benchmark designed specifically for telecommunications. The benchmark spans three heterogeneous corpora: O-RAN Alliance specifications, 3GPP release documents, and the srsRAN open-source codebase, comprising 9,000 question-chunk pairs across three standard chunk sizes (512, 1024, and 2048 tokens). To construct this dataset at scale without manual annotation bottlenecks, we employ a novel automated pipeline where one LLM generates specific queries from text chunks and a secondary LLM validates them across strict criteria. We comprehensively evaluate eight embedding models, spanning standard sentence-transformers and LLM-based embedders. Our results demonstrate that LLM-based embedders, such as Qwen3 and EmbeddingGemma, consistently and significantly outperform traditional sentence-transformers in both retrieval accuracy and robustness against cross-domain interference. Additionally, we introduce TeleEmbedBench-Clean to evaluate model robustness against noisy, incomplete user queries. Finally, our analysis reveals that while domain-specific task instructions improve embedder performance for raw source code, they paradoxically degrade retrieval performance for natural language telecommunications specifications.

</details>


### [267] [Ranking Abuse via Strategic Pairwise Data Perturbations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17805)
*Junyi Yao,Zihao Zheng,Jiayu Long*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MLE-based ranking systems like Bradley-Terry are vulnerable to strategic attacks using adaptive subset selection, showing sharp phase transitions where limited adversarial voters can dramatically alter global rankings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pairwise ranking systems based on Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), such as Bradley-Terry models, are widely used for preference aggregation from pairwise comparisons, but their robustness under strategic data manipulation remains insufficiently understood.

Method: The authors formulate the manipulation task as a constrained combinatorial optimization problem and propose an Adaptive Subset Selection Attack (ASSA) to efficiently identify high-impact perturbations in MLE-based ranking systems.

Result: Experimental results on synthetic data and real-world election datasets show that MLE-based rankings exhibit sharp phase-transition behavior: beyond a small perturbation budget, limited strategic voters can significantly alter global rankings. The ASSA method consistently outperforms random and greedy baselines under constrained budgets.

Conclusion: These findings reveal a fundamental sensitivity of MLE-based ranking mechanisms to structured perturbations and highlight the need for more robust aggregation methods in collective decision-making systems.

Abstract: Pairwise ranking systems based on Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), such as the Bradley-Terry model, are widely used to aggregate preferences from pairwise comparisons. However, their robustness under strategic data manipulation remains insufficiently understood.
  In this paper, we study the vulnerability of MLE-based ranking systems to adversarial perturbations. We formulate the manipulation task as a constrained combinatorial optimization problem and propose an Adaptive Subset Selection Attack (ASSA) to efficiently identify high-impact perturbations.
  Experimental results on both synthetic data and real-world election datasets show that MLE-based rankings exhibit a sharp phase-transition behavior: beyond a small perturbation budget, a limited number of strategic voters can significantly alter the global ranking. In particular, our method consistently outperforms random and greedy baselines under constrained budgets.
  These findings reveal a fundamental sensitivity of MLE-based ranking mechanisms to structured perturbations and highlight the need for more robust aggregation methods in collective decision-making systems.

</details>


### [268] [Efficient Diffusion Models under Nonconvex Equality and Inequality constraints via Landing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17838)
*Kijung Jeon,Michael Muehlebach,Molei Tao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified framework for constrained diffusion models on nonconvex feasible sets that enforces equality/inequality constraints efficiently without costly projections, using landing mechanisms and underdamped dynamics to reduce computational costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative modeling in constrained sets is essential for scientific/engineering applications (molecular generation, robotics) but existing methods struggle with computational costs and feasibility maintenance on nonconvex feasible sets.

Method: Proposes a unified framework for constrained diffusion models incorporating both overdamped and underdamped dynamics with a novel landing mechanism that replaces costly projections onto feasible sets, ensuring feasibility without iterative Newton solves.

Result: Empirically reduces function evaluations and memory usage during training/inference while preserving sample quality. On equality/mixed constraint benchmarks, achieves comparable quality to SOTA baselines with significantly lower computational cost.

Conclusion: Provides a practical and scalable solution for diffusion modeling on nonconvex feasible sets, making constrained generative modeling more efficient and applicable to real-world scientific/engineering problems.

Abstract: Generative modeling within constrained sets is essential for scientific and engineering applications involving physical, geometric, or safety requirements (e.g., molecular generation, robotics). We present a unified framework for constrained diffusion models on generic nonconvex feasible sets $Σ$ that simultaneously enforces equality and inequality constraints throughout the diffusion process. Our framework incorporates both overdamped and underdamped dynamics for forward and backward sampling. A key algorithmic innovation is a computationally efficient landing mechanism that replaces costly and often ill-defined projections onto $Σ$, ensuring feasibility without iterative Newton solves or projection failures. By leveraging underdamped dynamics, we accelerate mixing toward the prior distribution, effectively alleviating the high simulation costs typically associated with constrained diffusion. Empirically, this approach reduces function evaluations and memory usage during both training and inference while preserving sample quality. On benchmarks featuring equality and mixed constraints, our method achieves comparable sample quality to state-of-the-art baselines while significantly reducing computational cost, providing a practical and scalable solution for diffusion on nonconvex feasible sets.

</details>


### [269] [M100: An Orchestrated Dataflow Architecture Powering General AI Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17862)
*Yan Xie,Changkui Mao,Changsong Wu,Chao Lu,Chao Suo,Cheng Qian,Chun Yang,Danyang Zhu,Hengchang Xiong,Hongzhan Lu,Hongzhen Liu,Jiafu Liu,Jie Chen,Jie Dai,Junfeng Tang,Kai Liu,Kun Li,Lipeng Ge,Meng Sun,Min Luo,Peng Chen,Peng Wang,Shaodong Yang,Shibin Tang,Shibo Chen,Weikang Zhang,Xiao Ling,Xiaobo Du,Xin Wu,Yang Liu,Yi Jiang,Yihua Jin,Yin Huang,Yuli Zhang,Zhen Yuan,Zhiyuan Man,Zhongxiao Yao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: M100 is Li Auto's dataflow parallel architecture for AI inference that eliminates caching and uses tensor-level data streaming, achieving better performance and efficiency than GPGPU for autonomous driving and LLM applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While GPGPU-based architectures are versatile for AI workloads, they lack efficiency and cost-effectiveness. Domain-Specific Architectures (DSAs) excel at specific tasks but struggle with generalization and adaptation to rapidly evolving AI applications. There's a need for performant, cost-effective AI computing architectures that can handle diverse applications like Autonomous Driving, LLMs, and intelligent human interactions.

Method: M100 employs a dataflow parallel architecture with compiler-architecture co-design that orchestrates both computation and data movement. The architecture eliminates traditional caching - tensor computations are driven by compiler- and runtime-managed data streams flowing between computing elements and memories. The system uses tensor as the fundamental data element and focuses on optimal operational granularity across compiler, firmware, and hardware.

Result: M100 demonstrates general AI computing capability across diverse inference applications including UniAD for autonomous driving and LLaMA for LLMs. Benchmarks show M100 outperforms GPGPU architectures in AD applications with higher utilization, achieving greater efficiency and scalability than cache-based systems while reducing hardware complexity and cost.

Conclusion: M100 represents a promising direction for future general AI computing, offering a performant, cost-effective solution that combines the versatility of GPGPU with the efficiency of specialized architectures, addressing the needs of modern AI workloads across autonomous driving, LLMs, and intelligent interactions.

Abstract: As deep learning-based AI technologies gain momentum, the demand for general-purpose AI computing architectures continues to grow. While GPGPU-based architectures offer versatility for diverse AI workloads, they often fall short in efficiency and cost-effectiveness. Various Domain-Specific Architectures (DSAs) excel at particular AI tasks but struggle to extend across broader applications or adapt to the rapidly evolving AI landscape. M100 is Li Auto's response: a performant, cost-effective architecture for AI inference in Autonomous Driving (AD), Large Language Models (LLMs), and intelligent human interactions, domains crucial to today's most competitive automobile platforms. M100 employs a dataflow parallel architecture, where compiler-architecture co-design orchestrates not only computation but, more critically, data movement across time and space. Leveraging dataflow computing efficiency, our hardware-software co-design improves system performance while reducing hardware complexity and cost. M100 largely eliminates caching: tensor computations are driven by compiler- and runtime-managed data streams flowing between computing elements and on/off-chip memories, yielding greater efficiency and scalability than cache-based systems. Another key principle was selecting the right operational granularity for scheduling, issuing, and execution across compiler, firmware, and hardware. Recognizing commonalities in AI workloads, we chose the tensor as the fundamental data element. M100 demonstrates general AI computing capability across diverse inference applications, including UniAD (for AD) and LLaMA (for LLMs). Benchmarks show M100 outperforms GPGPU architectures in AD applications with higher utilization, representing a promising direction for future general AI computing.

</details>


### [270] [LEPO: \underline{L}atent R\underline{e}asoning \underline{P}olicy \underline{O}ptimization for Large Language~Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17892)
*Yuyan Zhou,Jiarui Yu,Hande Dong,Zhezheng Hao,Hong Wang,Jianqing Zhang,Qiang Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LEPO is a new RL framework that injects controllable stochasticity into latent reasoning using Gumbel-Softmax, enabling diverse reasoning paths and direct RL optimization on continuous latent representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current latent reasoning methods in LLMs collapse to deterministic inference without stochastic sampling, failing to discover diverse reasoning paths and lacking compatibility with Reinforcement Learning.

Method: LEPO injects controllable stochasticity via Gumbel-Softmax into latent reasoning, maintains stochasticity during rollout for diverse trajectory sampling, and constructs unified gradient estimation for both latent representations and discrete tokens during optimization.

Result: Extensive experiments show LEPO significantly outperforms existing RL methods for both discrete and latent reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: LEPO bridges the gap between latent reasoning and RL by enabling exploratory capacity and direct RL application to continuous latent representations, offering a promising framework for enhancing LLM reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Recently, latent reasoning has been introduced into large language models (LLMs) to leverage rich information within a continuous space. However, without stochastic sampling, these methods inevitably collapse to deterministic inference, failing to discover diverse reasoning paths. To bridge the gap, we inject controllable stochasticity into latent reasoning via Gumbel-Softmax, restoring LLMs' exploratory capacity and enhancing their compatibility with Reinforcement Learning (RL). Building on this, we propose \textbf{\underline{L}}atent R\textbf{\underline{e}}asoning \textbf{\underline{P}}olicy \textbf{\underline{O}}ptimization~(\textbf{LEPO}), a novel framework that applies RL directly to continuous latent representations. Specifically, in rollout stage, LEPO maintains stochasticity to enable diverse trajectory sampling, while in optimization stage, LEPO constructs a unified gradient estimation for both latent representations and discrete tokens. Extensive experiments show that LEPO significantly outperforms existing RL methods for discrete and latent reasoning.

</details>


### [271] [Can Explicit Physical Feasibility Benefit VLA Learning? An Empirical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17896)
*Yubai Wei,Chen Wu,Hashem Haghbayan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A study showing that adding explicit feasibility supervision (like obstacle avoidance) to Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model training improves physical reliability, task performance, and data efficiency, compared to standard imitation learning alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models are trained through imitation learning but lack explicit supervision of hard physical constraints like obstacle avoidance and kinematic feasibility. This forces models to infer geometric structure implicitly from demonstrations, which may limit their physical reliability.

Method: Formulated a geometry-grounded feasibility objective and integrated it into the training of a diffusion-based VLA policy. Used obstacle-aware manipulation as a controlled testbed to systematically evaluate geometry-dependent physical feasibility.

Result: Augmenting VLA training with explicit feasibility supervision improved both physical reliability and overall task performance. Also enhanced learning efficiency in low-data regimes, showing that feasibility signals effectively complement imitation learning.

Conclusion: Explicit feasibility supervision provides effective structured guidance for VLA policies, highlighting its potential for developing more reliable robot control systems that better handle physical constraints and geometric structure.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models map multimodal inputs directly to robot actions and are typically trained through large-scale imitation learning. While this paradigm has shown strong performance, prevailing VLA training procedures do not explicitly supervise hard physical constraints such as obstacle avoidance or kinematic feasibility. As a result, the geometric structure underlying physically feasible behavior must be inferred only implicitly from demonstrations. In this paper, we study whether introducing explicit feasibility supervision can provide effective structured guidance for VLA policies. We formulate a simple geometry-grounded feasibility objective and integrate it into the training stage of a diffusion-based VLA policy. To evaluate this idea systematically, we use obstacle-aware manipulation as a controlled probe of geometry-dependent physical feasibility. Empirical results show that augmenting VLA training with feasibility supervision improves both physical reliability and overall task performance, while also enhancing learning efficiency in the low-data regime. These findings indicate that explicit feasibility signals can effectively complement imitation-based VLA learning, highlighting their potential for developing more reliable VLA policies.

</details>


### [272] [LoReC: Rethinking Large Language Models for Graph Data Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17897)
*Hongyu Zhan,Qixin Wang,Yusen Tan,Haitao Yu,Jingbo Zhou,Shuai Chen,Jia Li,Xiao Tan,Jun Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoReC is a plug-and-play method that enhances LLMs' graph understanding through attention redistribution, graph information re-injection, and logits rectification to improve performance on graph-related tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current GraphLLM approaches show limited benefits when directly using LLMs for graph tasks, often performing worse than traditional GNN-based methods. LLMs have limited capability for processing graph data and tend to overlook graph information, creating a performance gap.

Method: Proposes LoReC with three stages: (1) Look - redistributes attention to graph structures; (2) Remember - re-injects graph information into the Feed-Forward Network (FFN); (3) Contrast - rectifies vanilla logits produced during decoding to improve predictions.

Result: Extensive experiments show LoReC brings notable improvements over current GraphLLM methods and outperforms GNN-based approaches across diverse datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness in enhancing LLMs' graph understanding.

Conclusion: LoReC successfully addresses LLMs' limitations in graph data processing by enhancing their graph understanding through attention mechanisms and information injection, establishing a more effective GraphLLM paradigm that outperforms both existing GraphLLM methods and traditional GNN approaches.

Abstract: The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) has fundamentally reshaped the way we interact with graphs, giving rise to a new paradigm called GraphLLM. As revealed in recent studies, graph learning can benefit from LLMs. However, we observe limited benefits when we directly utilize LLMs to make predictions for graph-related tasks within GraphLLM paradigm, which even yields suboptimal results compared to conventional GNN-based approaches. Through in-depth analysis, we find this failure can be attributed to LLMs' limited capability for processing graph data and their tendency to overlook graph information. To address this issue, we propose LoReC (Look, Remember, and Contrast), a novel plug-and-play method for GraphLLM paradigm, which enhances LLM's understanding of graph data through three stages: (1) Look: redistributing attention to graph; (2) Remember: re-injecting graph information into the Feed-Forward Network (FFN); (3) Contrast: rectifying the vanilla logits produced in the decoding process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LoReC brings notable improvements over current GraphLLM methods and outperforms GNN-based approaches across diverse datasets. The implementation is available at https://github.com/Git-King-Zhan/LoReC.

</details>


### [273] [Learning to Correct: Calibrated Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Attempt Chain-of-Thought](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17912)
*Muhammed Emrullah Ildiz,Halil Alperen Gozeten,Ege Onur Taga,Samet Oymak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes CAL-GRPO, a reinforcement learning method with calibrated attempt-level weighting for optimizing multi-attempt reasoning with verification feedback, addressing bias in naive weighting approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current reasoning models use long chain-of-thought to solve complex problems, but they don't effectively leverage multiple successive attempts with verification feedback. There's a need for RL methods that can properly weight individual attempts when optimizing for success within K attempts.

Method: Introduces Calibrated Attempt-Level GRPO (CAL-GRPO) with a novel weighing strategy to obtain unbiased gradients while maintaining small variance when optimizing the Verification@K reward (success by the K-th attempt). The method addresses bias that occurs from naive pass/fail weighting of attempts.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real data show CAL-GRPO outperforms vanilla GRPO and naive weighting baselines. Theoretical analysis reveals how incorporating per-attempt rewards influences training and eventual Verification@K performance.

Conclusion: CAL-GRPO provides an effective solution for optimizing multi-attempt reasoning with verification feedback by solving the gradient bias problem in attempt-level weighting, leading to improved performance on complex reasoning tasks.

Abstract: State-of-the-art reasoning models utilize long chain-of-thought (CoT) to solve increasingly complex problems using more test-time computation. In this work, we explore a long CoT setting where the model makes up to K successive attempts at solving a problem, in which each attempt is allowed to build on earlier ones after the model receives a hard verifier feedback. This motivates RL methods that can harness per-attempt rewards by carefully weighting individual attempts. We study optimizing the Verification@K reward (the model succeeds by the K-th attempt) and show that naively weighing the attempts by their pass/fail results in biased gradients. We introduce Calibrated Attempt-Level (CAL) GRPO by devising a weighing strategy to obtain unbiased gradients while maintaining small variance. Our theory reveals how incorporating per-attempt rewards influence the training and the eventual Verification@K performance. Experiments, baselines, and ablations on synthetic and real data corroborate our theory and the benefits of CAL-GRPO over vanilla GRPO as well as naive weighting.

</details>


### [274] [Fisher Decorator: Refining Flow Policy via A Local Transport Map](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17919)
*Xiaoyuan Cheng,Haoyu Wang,Wenxuan Yuan,Ziyan Wang,Zonghao Chen,Li Zeng,Zhuo Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel geometric approach to offline RL using Fisher information for anisotropic regularization, overcoming limitations of isotropic L2 regularization and achieving state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current flow-based offline RL methods use L2 regularization as an upper bound of 2-Wasserstein distance, which creates problems because behavioral policy manifolds are anisotropic while L2 regularization is isotropic and density-insensitive, leading to misaligned optimization directions.

Method: Formulates policy refinement as a local transport map (initial flow policy + residual displacement), derives local quadratic approximation of KL-constrained objective governed by Fisher information matrix, and uses score function from flow velocity to obtain quadratic constraint for efficient anisotropic optimization.

Result: Shows that optimality gap in prior methods comes from isotropic approximation, achieves controllable approximation error within provable neighborhood of optimal solution, and demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across diverse offline RL benchmarks.

Conclusion: By addressing the geometric mismatch through anisotropic Fisher information-based regularization, the proposed framework provides a principled solution to trade-offs between expressiveness, optimality, and efficiency in flow-based offline RL.

Abstract: Recent advances in flow-based offline reinforcement learning (RL) have achieved strong performance by parameterizing policies via flow matching. However, they still face critical trade-offs among expressiveness, optimality, and efficiency. In particular, existing flow policies interpret the $L_2$ regularization as an upper bound of the 2-Wasserstein distance ($W_2$), which can be problematic in offline settings. This issue stems from a fundamental geometric mismatch: the behavioral policy manifold is inherently anisotropic, whereas the $L_2$ (or upper bound of $W_2$) regularization is isotropic and density-insensitive, leading to systematically misaligned optimization directions. To address this, we revisit offline RL from a geometric perspective and show that policy refinement can be formulated as a local transport map: an initial flow policy augmented by a residual displacement. By analyzing the induced density transformation, we derive a local quadratic approximation of the KL-constrained objective governed by the Fisher information matrix, enabling a tractable anisotropic optimization formulation. By leveraging the score function embedded in the flow velocity, we obtain a corresponding quadratic constraint for efficient optimization. Our results reveal that the optimality gap in prior methods arises from their isotropic approximation. In contrast, our framework achieves a controllable approximation error within a provable neighborhood of the optimal solution. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across diverse offline RL benchmarks. See project page: https://github.com/ARC0127/Fisher-Decorator.

</details>


### [275] [HEALing Entropy Collapse: Enhancing Exploration in Few-Shot RLVR via Hybrid-Domain Entropy Dynamics Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17928)
*Zhanyu Liu,Qingguo Hu,Ante Wang,Chenqing Liu,Zhishang Xiang,Hui Li,Delai Qiu,Jinsong Su*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HEAL is a framework that improves few-shot reinforcement learning with verifiable reward by incorporating general-domain data and aligning entropy dynamics to prevent entropy collapse and enhance exploration diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLVR methods perform well in high-resource settings but suffer from severe entropy collapse in low-resource scenarios, which limits exploration and degrades reasoning performance. The paper aims to address this few-shot RLVR challenge.

Method: HEAL has two main components: 1) Selective incorporation of high-value general-domain data to promote diverse exploration, and 2) Entropy Dynamics Alignment (EDA) - a reward mechanism that aligns trajectory-level entropy dynamics between target and general domains, capturing both entropy magnitude and fine-grained variation.

Result: Experiments across multiple domains show HEAL consistently improves few-shot RLVR performance. Notably, using only 32 target-domain samples, HEAL matches or even surpasses full-shot RLVR trained with 1K target-domain samples.

Conclusion: HEAL effectively addresses entropy collapse in few-shot RLVR by leveraging general-domain data and entropy dynamics alignment, enabling strong performance with minimal target-domain samples while maintaining exploration diversity.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reward (RLVR) has proven effective for training reasoning-oriented large language models, but existing methods largely assume high-resource settings with abundant training data. In low-resource scenarios, RLVR is prone to more severe entropy collapse, which substantially limits exploration and degrades reasoning performance. To address this issue, we propose Hybrid-domain Entropy dynamics ALignment (HEAL), a framework tailored for few-shot RLVR. HEAL first selectively incorporates high-value general-domain data to promote more diverse exploration. Then, we introduce Entropy Dynamics Alignment (EDA), a reward mechanism that aligns trajectory-level entropy dynamics between the target and general domains, capturing both entropy magnitude and fine-grained variation. Through this alignment, EDA not only further mitigates entropy collapse but also encourages the policy to acquire more diverse exploration behaviors from the general domain. Experiments across multiple domains show that HEAL consistently improves few-shot RLVR performance. Notably, using only 32 target-domain samples, HEAL matches or even surpasses full-shot RLVR trained with 1K target-domain samples.

</details>


### [276] [How Much Cache Does Reasoning Need? Depth-Cache Tradeoffs in KV-Compressed Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17935)
*Xiao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Theoretical analysis of KV cache compression limits for Transformers, showing tradeoffs between cache size, attention parameters, and reasoning depth for pointer chasing tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV cache memory is the dominant bottleneck during Transformer inference, but there's little theoretical understanding of how aggressively it can be compressed before multi-step reasoning degrades. The paper aims to establish fundamental limits on KV cache compression.

Method: Studies k-hop pointer chasing on n tokens with shared KV cache of size s, analyzing depth lower bounds through theoretical proofs and conjectures. Examines three key results: product depth bounds, bandwidth barriers, and adaptive vs oblivious error scaling.

Result: 1) Conjectured product depth lower bound L = Ω(⌈k/s⌉·⌈log₂ n/(Hmp)⌉) with matching upper bound. 2) Bandwidth barrier shows existing methods can't exceed ⌈k/s⌉ once Hmp ≥ log₂ n. 3) Adaptive caches achieve Pr[ℰ] = s/n (independent of T) while oblivious caches suffer exponential error scaling, explaining empirical superiority of heavy-hitter eviction.

Conclusion: The paper establishes fundamental tradeoffs between KV cache compression, attention parameters, and reasoning capabilities, providing theoretical foundations for understanding when and how compression degrades multi-step reasoning in Transformers.

Abstract: The key-value (KV) cache is the dominant memory bottleneck during Transformer inference, yet little is known theoretically about how aggressively it can be compressed before multi-step reasoning degrades. We study this through $k$-hop pointer chasing on $n$ tokens under a shared KV cache of size $s$, attention dimension $m$, $H$ heads, $p$-bit precision, and a locality-respecting cache controller (satisfied by all standard KV-compression methods). We give three results.
  (1) Product depth lower bound (conjectured). We conjecture that any such Transformer ($n \geq 4k$, $s \leq \sqrt{n}/4$) requires depth $L = Ω(\lceil k/s \rceil \cdot \lceil \log_2 n/(Hmp) \rceil)$, and isolate the sole remaining gap as a probabilistic step on the joint distribution of cache trace and pointer chain. Unconditionally, we prove a matching upper bound $L = O(\min(k, \lceil k/s \rceil \log s) \cdot \log n/(mp))$ via windowed pointer doubling, and a max-bound $L = Ω(\max(\lceil k/s \rceil, \log n/(Hmp)))$. Closing the conjecture amounts to upgrading max to product.
  (2) Bandwidth barrier. The product bound binds only when $Hmp \lesssim \log n$. Any lower bound provable via per-window distinguishability counting -- including reachability, bandwidth, and combinations -- cannot exceed $\lceil k/s \rceil$ once $Hmp \geq \log_2 n$. Breaking this requires lifting unconditional communication-complexity bounds for pointer chasing to Cache-Transformer depth.
  (3) Adaptive vs oblivious error scaling. Under random cache over $T = \lceil \log_2 k \rceil$ doubling stages, oblivious caches give $\Pr[\mathcal{E}] \leq (s/(n-T))^T + 2T^3/n$ (exponential in $T$), while adaptive locality-respecting caches achieve $\Pr[\mathcal{E}] = s/n$ exactly, independent of $T$. The $Ω((n/s)^{T-1})$ separation explains why heavy-hitter eviction empirically dominates random eviction for multi-hop reasoning.

</details>


### [277] [Federated Rule Ensemble Method in Medical Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17956)
*Ke Wan,Kensuke Tanioka,Toshio Shimokawa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated RuleFit framework enables interpretable machine learning across institutions without sharing raw data, combining differential privacy, gradient boosting decision trees, and federated optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning in healthcare needs large datasets but privacy regulations hinder data sharing across institutions. Existing federated learning methods lack interpretability, limiting clinical adoption.

Method: Three-component framework: 1) Differentially private histograms for shared cutoffs, 2) Local rule generation using gradient boosting decision trees, 3) Federated Dual Averaging algorithm with ℓ₁-regularization for sparse coefficient estimation.

Result: Achieved comparable performance to centralized RuleFit, outperformed existing federated approaches in simulations, and provided interpretable insights with competitive accuracy in real-world analysis.

Conclusion: The framework offers a practical, interpretable solution for reliable modeling in federated environments, balancing privacy, performance, and clinical interpretability.

Abstract: Machine learning has become integral to medical research and is increasingly applied in clinical settings to support diagnosis and decision-making; however, its effectiveness depends on access to large, diverse datasets, which are limited within single institutions. Although integrating data across institutions can address this limitation, privacy regulations and data ownership constraints hinder these efforts. Federated learning enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data; however, most methods rely on complex architectures that lack interpretability, limiting clinical applicability. Therefore, we proposed a federated RuleFit framework to construct a unified and interpretable global model for distributed environments. It integrates three components: preprocessing based on differentially private histograms to estimate shared cutoff values, enabling consistent rule definitions and reducing heterogeneity across clients; local rule generation using gradient boosting decision trees with shared cutoffs; and coefficient estimation via $\ell_1$-regularized optimization using a Federated Dual Averaging algorithm for sparse and consistent variable selection. In simulation studies, the proposed method achieved a performance comparable to that of centralized RuleFit while outperforming existing federated approaches. Real-world analysis demonstrated its ability to provide interpretable insights with competitive predictive accuracy. Therefore, the proposed framework offers a practical and effective solution for interpretable and reliable modeling in federated learning environments.

</details>


### [278] [Online Conformal Prediction with Adversarial Semi-bandit Feedback via Regret Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17984)
*Junyoung Yang,Kyungmin Kim,Sangdon Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Online conformal prediction method for uncertainty quantification with partial feedback where true labels are only revealed when inside prediction sets, formulated as adversarial bandit problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online uncertainty quantification is crucial for safety-critical systems where decisions must be made under uncertainty. Existing online conformal prediction methods assume full feedback (always observing true labels), but in practice, we often only get partial feedback (true labels revealed only when inside prediction sets).

Method: Formulates online conformal prediction with partial feedback as an adversarial bandit problem, treating each candidate prediction set as an arm. Builds on existing adversarial bandit algorithms to achieve long-run coverage guarantees by establishing connections to learner regret.

Result: The method achieves long-run coverage guarantees even under partial feedback from adaptive adversaries. Empirical demonstrations show effectiveness in both i.i.d. and non-i.i.d. settings, successfully controlling miscoverage rate while maintaining reasonable prediction set sizes.

Conclusion: Proposes a novel learning method for online conformal prediction that handles the challenging partial feedback setting, providing theoretical guarantees and empirical validation for practical uncertainty quantification in safety-critical systems.

Abstract: Uncertainty quantification is crucial in safety-critical systems, where decisions must be made under uncertainty. In particular, we consider the problem of online uncertainty quantification, where data points arrive sequentially. Online conformal prediction is a principled online uncertainty quantification method that dynamically constructs a prediction set at each time step. While existing methods for online conformal prediction provide long-run coverage guarantees without any distributional assumptions, they typically assume a full feedback setting in which the true label is always observed. In this paper, we propose a novel learning method for online conformal prediction with partial feedback from an adaptive adversary-a more challenging setup where the true label is revealed only when it lies inside the constructed prediction set. Specifically, we formulate online conformal prediction as an adversarial bandit problem by treating each candidate prediction set as an arm. Building on an existing algorithm for adversarial bandits, our method achieves a long-run coverage guarantee by explicitly establishing its connection to the regret of the learner. Finally, we empirically demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in both independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) and non-i.i.d. settings, showing that it successfully controls the miscoverage rate while maintaining a reasonable size of the prediction set.

</details>


### [279] [Causally-Constrained Probabilistic Forecasting for Time-Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17998)
*Pooyan Khosravinia,João Gama,Bruno Veloso*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CGT: A causally-guided Transformer for multivariate time-series anomaly detection that integrates causal graph priors with deep forecasting to improve both detection performance and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deep learning models for multivariate time-series anomaly detection (e.g., graph neural networks, Transformers) are primarily correlational and offer limited causal interpretation and root-cause localization capabilities, which are crucial for industrial monitoring.

Method: Proposes a Causally Guided Transformer (CGT) framework that integrates explicit time-lagged causal graph priors with deep sequence modeling. It uses hard parent masks from causal discovery for main prediction, latent Gaussian heads for uncertainty, shadow auxiliary paths with stop-gradient isolation, and safety-gated blending to handle residual correlations without compromising causality.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art detection performance with F1-scores of 96.19% on ASD and 95.32% on SMD benchmarks, while enhancing variable-level attribution quality for root-cause analysis.

Conclusion: Causal structural priors can significantly improve both robustness and interpretability in detecting anomalies in multivariate sensor systems, offering better root-cause localization alongside strong detection performance.

Abstract: Anomaly detection in multivariate time series is a central challenge in industrial monitoring, as failures frequently arise from complex temporal dynamics and cross-sensor interactions. While recent deep learning models, including graph neural networks and Transformers, have demonstrated strong empirical performance, most approaches remain primarily correlational and offer limited support for causal interpretation and root-cause localization. This study introduces a causally-constrained probabilistic forecasting framework which is a Causally Guided Transformer (CGT) model for multivariate time-series anomaly detection, integrating an explicit time-lagged causal graph prior with deep sequence modeling. For each target variable, a dedicated forecasting block employs a hard parent mask derived from causal discovery to restrict the main prediction pathway to graph-supported causes, while a latent Gaussian head captures predictive uncertainty. To leverage residual correlational information without compromising the causal representation, a shadow auxiliary path with stop-gradient isolation and a safety-gated blending mechanism is incorporated to suppress non-causal contributions when reliability is low. Anomalies are identified using negative log-likelihood scores with adaptive streaming thresholding, and root-cause variables are determined through per-dimension probabilistic attribution and counterfactual clamping. Experiments on the ASD and SMD benchmarks indicate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art detection performance, with F1-scores of 96.19% on ASD and 95.32% on SMD, and enhances variable-level attribution quality. These findings suggest that causal structural priors can improve both robustness and interpretability in detecting deep anomalies in multivariate sensor systems.

</details>


### [280] [Neural Garbage Collection: Learning to Forget while Learning to Reason](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18002)
*Michael Y. Li,Jubayer Ibn Hamid,Emily B. Fox,Noah D. Goodman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NGC (Neural Garbage Collection) enables language models to learn when to forget while reasoning, using RL to optimize both reasoning and KV cache management from task rewards alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chain-of-thought reasoning creates KV cache bottlenecks as reasoning steps grow. Current approaches use hand-designed criteria, but a scalable solution would let models learn to manage their own memory through end-to-end learning, similar to how they learn to reason.

Method: NGC treats chain-of-thought tokens and cache-eviction decisions as discrete actions from the language model. Using reinforcement learning, it jointly optimizes reasoning and memory management where the model periodically pauses to decide which KV cache entries to evict, then continues reasoning with the remaining cache.

Result: On Countdown, AMC, and AIME tasks, NGC maintains strong accuracy relative to full-cache upper bound while achieving 2-3x peak KV cache size compression, substantially outperforming eviction baselines.

Conclusion: NGC represents a first step toward end-to-end optimization driving both capability and efficiency in language models, showing models can learn to forget while reasoning purely from outcome-based task rewards without supervised fine-tuning or proxy objectives.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought reasoning has driven striking advances in language model capability, yet every reasoning step grows the KV cache, creating a bottleneck to scaling this paradigm further. Current approaches manage these constraints on the model's behalf using hand-designed criteria.
