A more scalable approach would let end-to-end learning subsume this design choice entirely, following a broader pattern in deep learning. After all, if a model can learn to reason, why can't it learn to forget? We introduce Neural Garbage Collection (NGC), in which a language model learns to forget while learning to reason, trained end-to-end from outcome-based task reward alone. As the model reasons, it periodically pauses, decides which KV cache entries to evict, and continues to reason conditioned on the remaining cache. By treating tokens in a chain-of-thought and cache-eviction decisions as discrete actions sampled from the language model, we can use reinforcement learning to jointly optimize how the model reasons and how it manages its own memory: what the model evicts shapes what it remembers, what it remembers shapes its reasoning, and the correctness of that reasoning determines its reward. Crucially, the model learns this behavior entirely from a single learning signal - the outcome-based task reward - without supervised fine-tuning or proxy objectives. On Countdown, AMC, and AIME tasks, NGC maintains strong accuracy relative to the full-cache upper bound at 2-3x peak KV cache size compression and substantially outperforms eviction baselines. Our results are a first step towards a broader vision where end-to-end optimization drives both capability and efficiency in language models.

</details>


### [281] [Neural Shape Operator Surrogates -- Expression Rate Bounds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18012)
*Helmut Harbrecht,Christoph Schwab*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proves error bounds for operator surrogates (neural and spectral) that approximate shape-to-solution maps for PDEs on families of diffeomorphic domains, providing theoretical guarantees for parametric solution approximation across shape variations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide theoretical foundations for neural operators' empirically observed ability to learn data-to-solution maps for PDEs that generalize across parametric families of shapes, by establishing rigorous error bounds and convergence rates.

Method: Use affine-parametric shape encoding to pull back PDEs to a reference domain, establish parametric holomorphy of solution families, then construct finite-parametric approximations (neural and spectral operators) with quantified convergence rates using principal-component shape encoders and frame decoders.

Result: Proved existence and error bounds for neural and spectral operator surrogates for shape-to-solution maps with convergence rate guarantees uniform across admissible shapes, providing theoretical justification for neural operators' generalization across shape families.

Conclusion: The work establishes rigorous mathematical foundations for operator learning methods that can approximate parametric solution families of PDEs across varying geometries, supporting the empirical success of neural operators in shape-parametric settings.

Abstract: We prove error bounds for operator surrogates of solution operators for partial differential and boundary integral equations on families of domains which are diffeomorphic to one common reference (or latent) domain $D_{ref}$. The pullback of the PDE to $D_{ref}$ via affine-parametric shape encoding produces a collection of holomorphic parametric PDEs on $D_{ref}$. Sufficient conditions for (uniformly with respect to the parameter) well-posedness are given, implying existence, uniqueness and stability of parametric solution families on $D_{ref}$. We illustrate the abstract hypotheses by reviewing recent holomorphy results for a suite of elliptic and parabolic PDEs.
  Quantified parametric holomorphy implies existence of finite-parametric, discrete approximations of the parametric solution families with convergence rates in terms of the number $N$ of parameters. We obtain constructive proofs of existence of Neural and Spectral Operator surrogates for the shape-to-solution maps with error bounds and convergence rate guarantees uniform on the collection of admissible shapes. We admit principal-component shape encoders and frame decoders.
  Our results support in particular the (empirically reported) ability of neural operators to realize data-to-solution maps for elliptic and parabolic PDEs and BIEs that generalize across parametric families of shapes.

</details>


### [282] [Clusterability-Based Assessment of Potentially Noisy Views for Multi-View Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18024)
*Mudi Jiang,Jiahui Zhou,Xinying Liu,Zengyou He,Zhikui Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Multi-View Clusterability Score (MVCS) to quantify cluster-related structures in multi-view data and use it for noisy-view detection before clustering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multi-view clustering, low-quality or degraded views can impair overall performance, but existing methods mainly address this within clustering process rather than assessing data quality before clustering.

Method: Propose Multi-View Clusterability Score (MVCS) with three components: per-view structural clusterability, joint-space clusterability, and cross-view neighborhood consistency. Use MVCS for noisy-view analysis and detection before clustering.

Result: Extensive experiments show noisy views significantly degrade clustering performance, and MVCS (designed specifically for multi-view data) more effectively supports noisy-view analysis and detection compared to single-view clusterability measures.

Conclusion: MVCS is the first clusterability score specifically designed for multi-view data, enabling pre-clustering noisy-view analysis that helps identify and handle problematic views before the clustering process begins.

Abstract: In multi-view clustering, the quality of different views may vary substantially, and low-quality or degraded views can impair overall clustering performance. However, existing studies mainly address this issue within the clustering process through view weighting or noise-robust optimization, while paying limited attention to data-level assessment before clustering. In this paper, we study the problem of pre-clustering noisy-view analysis in multi-view data from a clusterability perspective. To this end, we propose a Multi-View Clusterability Score (MVCS), which quantifies the strength of latent cluster-related structures in multi-view data through three complementary components: per-view structural clusterability, joint-space clusterability, and cross-view neighborhood consistency. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first clusterability score specifically designed for multi-view data. We further use it to perform potentially noisy view analysis and noisy-view detection before clustering. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that noisy views can significantly degrade clustering performance, and that, compared with existing clusterability measures designed for single-view data, the proposed method more effectively supports noisy-view analysis and detection.

</details>


### [283] [RASP-Tuner: Retrieval-Augmented Soft Prompts for Context-Aware Black-Box Optimization in Non-Stationary Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18026)
*Enze Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RASP-Tuner: A method for online black-box optimization that adapts to changing contexts by decomposing the problem into regime identification, mixture-of-experts prediction, and low-dimensional prompt subspace adaptation, achieving better cumulative regret with lower computational cost than traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many real-world systems have black-box objectives that change with observed contexts, requiring adaptation. Traditional approaches either discard history (paying repeated adaptation costs) or use expensive Gaussian-process refits that become unsustainable at high observation counts. There's a need for an efficient online tuning method that can adapt to changing contexts while maintaining low per-step computational cost.

Method: RASP-Tuner decomposes online tuning into three components: (1) identify regimes by retrieving similar past contexts, (2) predict short-horizon loss using a mixture-of-experts surrogate with parameters, context, and retrieved soft prompt as inputs, and (3) adapt primarily in a low-dimensional prompt subspace, using full surrogate updates only when error or disagreement spikes. A RealErrorComposer maps heterogeneous streaming metrics to [0,1] via EMA-stabilized logistic scores for training.

Result: On nine synthetic benchmarks, one adversarial-context test, and three real-world streams, RASP-Tuner improves or matches cumulative regret compared to GP-UCB and CMA-ES on 7/9 synthetic tasks at horizon T=100, while being 8-12 times faster per step than sliding-window GP-UCB on the same hardware.

Conclusion: RASP-Tuner provides an effective approach for online context-conditioned optimization that balances adaptation performance with computational efficiency. The theoretical analysis (RA-GD model) provides sufficient conditions for bounded dynamic regret, though practical deployment violates some of these premises, leaving gaps for future work.

Abstract: Many deployed systems expose black-box objectives whose minimizing configuration shifts with an externally observed context. When contexts revisit a small set of latent regimes, an optimizer that discards history pays repeated adaptation cost; when each step must remain inexpensive, full Gaussian-process (GP) refits at high observation counts are difficult to sustain. We cast online tuning as context-conditioned regret minimization and present RASP-Tuner, which instantiates a decomposition motivated by first principles: (i) identify a regime proxy by retrieving similar past contexts; (ii) predict short-horizon loss with a mixture-of-experts surrogate whose input concatenates parameters, context, and a retrieved soft prompt; (iii) adapt chiefly in a low-dimensional prompt subspace, invoking full surrogate updates only when scalarized error or disagreement spikes. A RealErrorComposer maps heterogeneous streaming metrics to [0,1] via EMA-stabilized logistic scores, supplying a single differentiable training target. On nine synthetic non-stationary benchmarks, an adversarial-context sanity check, and three tabular real-world streams (Section on real-world experiments), RASP-Tuner improves or matches cumulative regret relative to our GP-UCB and CMA-ES implementations on seven of nine synthetic tasks under paired tests at horizon T=100, while recording 8-12 times lower wall-clock per step than sliding-window GP-UCB on identical hardware. Idealized analysis in a cluster-separated, strongly convex regime model (RA-GD) supplies sufficient conditions for bounded dynamic regret; the deployed pipeline violates several of these premises, and we articulate which gaps remain open.

</details>


### [284] [Variational Autoencoder Domain Adaptation for Cross-System Generalization in ML-Based SOP Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18035)
*Leyla Sadighi,Stefan Karlsson,Carlos Natalino,Mojtaba Eshghie,Fehmida Usmani,Eoin Kenny,Lena Wosinska,Paolo Monti,Marija Furdek,Marco Ruffini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Domain Adaptation using VAE enables cross-system physical-layer threat detection between different optical fiber networks with significant accuracy improvements over single-system DNN baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning models for optical fiber threat detection fail when deployed on different systems due to variations in wavelength, fiber properties, and network architecture, creating a need for domain adaptation.

Method: Proposes a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) based Domain Adaptation framework that learns shared representations from combined data of two optical systems, then freezes the encoder and trains a classifier using individual system labels.

Result: Achieves 95.3% accuracy when adapting from System 1 (21 km O-band dark-fiber) to System 2 (63.4 km C-band live metro ring), and 73.5% accuracy for the reverse direction, with gains of 83.4% and 51% over single-system DNN baselines.

Conclusion: The VAE-based domain adaptation approach successfully addresses cross-system generalization in optical fiber threat detection while maintaining intra-system performance, enabling practical deployment across heterogeneous optical networks.

Abstract: Machine learning (ML) models trained to detect physical-layer threats on one optical fiber system often fail catastrophically when applied to a different system, due to variations in operating wavelength, fiber properties, and network architecture. To overcome this, we propose a Domain Adaptation (DA) framework based on a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) that learns a shared representation capturing event signatures common to both systems while suppressing system-specific differences. The shared encoder is first trained on the combined data from two distinct optical systems: a 21 km O-band dark-fiber testbed (System 1) and a 63.4 km C-band live metro ring (System 2). The encoder is then frozen, and a classifier is trained using labels from an individual system. The proposed approach achieves 95.3% and 73.5% cross-system accuracy when moving from System 1 to System 2 and vice versa, respectively. This corresponds to gains of 83.4% and 51% over a fully supervised Deep Neural Network (DNN) baseline trained on a single system, while preserving intra-system performance.

</details>


### [285] [Sonata: A Hybrid World Model for Inertial Kinematics under Clinical Data Scarcity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18058)
*Blaise Delaney,Salil Patel,Yuji Xing,Dominic Dootson,Karin Sevegnani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sonata is a compact 3.77M-parameter latent world model for six-axis trunk IMU representation learning that predicts future states rather than reconstructing sensor data, showing superior clinical performance in data-scarce scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical cohorts for neurological assessment typically have only tens to hundreds of patients, making web-scale masked-reconstruction approaches poorly suited due to data scarcity. There's a need for effective representation learning methods that work with limited clinical data.

Method: Sonata is a hybrid model with 3.77M parameters pre-trained on a harmonized corpus of 9 public datasets (739 subjects, 190k windows). Instead of reconstructing raw sensor traces, it uses a latent world-model objective that predicts future state. The approach is compared against a matched autoregressive forecasting baseline (MAE) on the same backbone architecture.

Result: Sonata consistently outperforms the baseline across 14 evaluation arms, showing stronger frozen-probe clinical discrimination, better prospective fall-risk prediction, and superior cross-cohort transfer. It produces higher-rank, more structured latent representations while remaining compact enough for on-device wearable inference.

Conclusion: Sonata represents a step toward general kinematic world models for neurological assessment, demonstrating that predictive world modeling outperforms reconstruction-based approaches in data-scarce clinical settings while maintaining computational efficiency suitable for wearable deployment.

Abstract: We introduce Sonata, a compact latent world model for six-axis trunk IMU representation learning under clinical data scarcity. Clinical cohorts typically comprise tens to hundreds of patients, making web-scale masked-reconstruction objectives poorly matched to the problem. Sonata is a 3.77 M-parameter hybrid model, pre-trained on a harmonised corpus of nine public datasets (739 subjects, 190k windows) with a latent world-model objective that predicts future state rather than reconstructing raw sensor traces. In a controlled comparison against a matched autoregressive forecasting baseline (MAE) on the same backbone, Sonata yields consistently stronger frozen-probe clinical discrimination, prospective fall-risk prediction, and cross-cohort transfer across a 14-arm evaluation suite, while producing higher-rank, more structured latent representations. At 3.77 M parameters the model is compatible with on-device wearable inference, offering a step toward general kinematic world models for neurological assessment.

</details>


### [286] [Towards a Foundation-Model Paradigm for Aerodynamic Prediction in Three-dimensional Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18062)
*Yunjia Yang,Babak Gholami,Caglar Gurbuz,Mohammad Rashed,Nils Thuerey*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AeroTransformer: A Transformer-based aerodynamic prediction model pre-trained on diverse wing geometries (SuperWing dataset) and fine-tuned with few task-specific samples, achieving 84.2% error reduction and enabling interactive design tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate aerodynamic prediction models are crucial for accelerating shape optimization, but developing them for complex 3D configurations is challenging due to high training data generation costs. There's a need for efficient methods to build accurate surrogate models for design purposes.

Method: Proposes a two-stage methodology: 1) Pre-training a large-scale Transformer-based architecture (AeroTransformer) on diverse geometries using the SuperWing dataset (~30,000 samples), 2) Fine-tuning the pre-trained model with a few task-specific samples (450 samples for specific wing shapes perturbed from the Common Research Model).

Result: With only 450 task-specific samples, the method achieves 0.36% error on surface-flow prediction, reducing error by 84.2% compared to training from scratch. The study also systematically analyzes model configurations and training strategies for effective deployment under limited data/computational budgets.

Conclusion: The AeroTransformer approach enables efficient construction of accurate aerodynamic surrogate models through pre-training and fine-tuning, significantly reducing data requirements while maintaining high accuracy. The released datasets, pre-trained models, and interactive design tool facilitate practical application and further research in aerodynamic shape optimization.

Abstract: Accurate machine-learning models for aerodynamic prediction are essential for accelerating shape optimization, yet remain challenging to develop for complex three-dimensional configurations due to the high cost of generating training data. This work introduces a methodology for efficiently constructing accurate surrogate models for design purposes by first pre-training a large-scale model on diverse geometries and then fine-tuning it with a few more detailed task-specific samples. A Transformer-based architecture, AeroTransformer, is developed and tailored for large-scale training to learn aerodynamics. The methodology is evaluated on transonic wings, where the model is pre-trained on SuperWing, a dataset of nearly 30000 samples with broad geometric diversity, and subsequently fine-tuned to handle specific wing shapes perturbed from the Common Research Model. Results show that, with 450 task-specific samples, the proposed methodology achieves 0.36% error on surface-flow prediction, reducing 84.2% compared to training from scratch. The influence of model configurations and training strategies is also systematically studied to provide guidance on effectively training and deploying such models under limited data and computational budgets. To facilitate reuse, we release the datasets and the pre-trained models at https://github.com/tum-pbs/AeroTransformer. An interactive design tool is also built on the pre-trained model and is available online at https://webwing.pbs.cit.tum.de.

</details>


### [287] [Towards Real-Time ECG and EMG Modeling on $μ$ NPUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18067)
*Josh Millar,Ashok Samraj Thangarajan,Soumyajit Chatterjee,Hamed Haddadi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PhysioLite is a lightweight, NPU-compatible model architecture for ECG/EMG signal analysis that achieves comparable performance to Transformer models while being <10% the size, enabling deployment on microcontroller-scale wearable hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While neural processing units (NPUs) have been integrated into microcontroller-scale wearable hardware for offline, privacy-preserving inference, physiological signal analysis remains infeasible because current state-of-the-art Transformer models are too large and incompatible with μNPUs due to dynamic attention operations.

Method: Introduces PhysioLite with learnable wavelet filter banks, CPU-offloaded positional encoding, and hardware-aware layer design to create a NPU-compatible architecture. Uses 8-bit quantization resulting in ~370KB model size.

Result: Achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art Transformer-based foundation models on ECG and EMG benchmarks while being <10% of their size. Profiles show viable latency and resource consumption on MAX78000 and HX6538 WE2 μNPUs for battery-powered hardware.

Conclusion: PhysioLite demonstrates that lightweight, NPU-compatible models can bring physiological signal analysis to resource-constrained wearable hardware, balancing performance with practical deployment considerations like size, latency, and power efficiency.

Abstract: The miniaturisation of neural processing units (NPUs) and other low-power accelerators has enabled their integration into microcontroller-scale wearable hardware, supporting near-real-time, offline, and privacy-preserving inference. Yet physiological signal analysis has remained infeasible on such hardware; recent Transformer-based models show state-of-the-art performance but are prohibitively large for resource- and power-constrained hardware and incompatible with $μ$ NPUs due to their dynamic attention operations. We introduce PhysioLite, a lightweight, NPU-compatible model architecture and training framework for ECG/EMG signal analysis. Using learnable wavelet filter banks, CPU-offloaded positional encoding, and hardware-aware layer design, PhysioLite reaches performance comparable to state-of-the-art Transformer-based foundation models on ECG and EMG benchmarks, while being <10% of the size ($\sim$370KB with 8-bit quantization). We also profile its component-wise latency and resource consumption on both the MAX78000 and HX6538 WE2 $μ$ NPUs, demonstrating its viability for signal analysis on constrained, battery-powered hardware. We release our model(s) and training framework at: https://github.com/j0shmillar/physiolite.

</details>


### [288] [Implicit neural representations as a coordinate-based framework for continuous environmental field reconstruction from sparse ecological observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18083)
*Agnieszka Pregowska,Hazem M. Kalaji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: INRs (implicit neural representations) effectively reconstruct continuous environmental fields from sparse, irregular biodiversity data across species distribution, phenology, and morphological segmentation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconstructing continuous environmental fields from sparse and irregular observations is a central challenge in environmental modelling. Many ecological datasets are heterogeneous in space and time, making grid-based approaches difficult to scale or generalize across domains.

Method: The paper evaluates implicit neural representations (INRs) as a coordinate-based modelling framework for learning continuous spatial and spatio-temporal fields directly from coordinate inputs. They analyze INRs across three representative modelling scenarios using open biodiversity data and examine interpolation behavior, spatial coherence, and computational characteristics.

Result: Results show that neural fields provide stable continuous representations with predictable computational cost, complementing classical smoothers and tree-based approaches. INRs demonstrate effectiveness for species distribution reconstruction, phenological dynamics, and morphological segmentation tasks.

Conclusion: Coordinate-based neural fields serve as a flexible representation layer that can be integrated into environmental modelling pipelines and exploratory analysis frameworks for large, irregularly sampled datasets, offering advantages in scalability and resolution-independent querying.

Abstract: Reconstructing continuous environmental fields from sparse and irregular observations remains a central challenge in environmental modelling and biodiversity informatics. Many ecological datasets are heterogeneous in space and time, making grid-based approaches difficult to scale or generalise across domains. Here, we evaluate implicit neural representations (INRs) as a coordinate-based modelling framework for learning continuous spatial and spatio-temporal fields directly from coordinate inputs. We analyse their behaviour across three representative modelling scenarios: species distribution reconstruction, phenological dynamics, and morphological segmentation derived from open biodiversity data. Beyond predictive performance, we examine interpolation behaviour, spatial coherence, and computational characteristics relevant for environmental modelling workflows, including scalability, resolution-independent querying, and architectural inductive bias. Results show that neural fields provide stable continuous representations with predictable computational cost, complementing classical smoothers and tree-based approaches. These findings position coordinate-based neural fields as a flexible representation layer that can be integrated into environmental modelling pipelines and exploratory analysis frameworks for large, irregularly sampled datasets.

</details>


### [289] [Predicting LLM Compression Degradation from Spectral Statistics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18085)
*Mingxue Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Low-rank compression degradation in LLMs can be predicted using stable rank and compression ratio interaction, enabling compute-efficient compression planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Matrix-level low-rank compression reduces LLM costs but requires expensive compute to evaluate compressed models. Need to predict compression-induced degradation before committing to compression compute.

Method: Systematic analysis of Qwen3 and Gemma3 model families across four low-rank compression methods: vanilla SVD, two ASVD variants, and SVD-LLM. Studied stable rank and information density metrics.

Result: Interaction term γ·ρ̄s (compression ratio × stable rank) is robust predictor of accuracy degradation, achieving leave-one-out cross-validation Pearson correlations of 0.890 for attention layers and 0.839 for MLP layers.

Conclusion: Provides theoretical intuition connecting predictor to SVD truncation bounds and transformer error composition. Enables predict-then-compress workflow to estimate degradation from weights before investing compute.

Abstract: Matrix-level low-rank compression is a promising way to reduce the cost of large language models, but running compression and evaluating the resulting models on language tasks can be prohibitively expensive. Can compression-induced degradation be predicted before committing to this compute? We systematically analyze the Qwen3 and Gemma3 model families across four representative low-rank compression methods: vanilla SVD, two ASVD variants, and SVD-LLM. We find that stable rank and information density, measured in bits per parameter, dominate performance degradation. The interaction term $γ\cdot \barρ_s$, defined as compression ratio times stable rank, is a robust predictor of accuracy degradation, achieving leave-one-out cross-validation Pearson correlations of $0.890$ for attention layers and $0.839$ for MLP layers. We provide theoretical intuition for why this predictor succeeds by connecting it to standard SVD truncation bounds and error composition mechanisms in transformer layers. These findings enable a predict-then-compress workflow: compute $γ\cdot \barρ_s$ from weights, estimate degradation, and invest compute only in desirable configurations.

</details>


### [290] [Towards E-Value Based Stopping Rules for Bayesian Deep Ensembles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18089)
*Emanuel Sommer,Rickmer Schulte,Sarah Deubner,Julius Kobialka,David Rügamer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes E-value based stopping rule for Bayesian Deep Ensembles to determine when MCMC sampling provides meaningful improvement over baseline Deep Ensembles, enabling early stopping when no significant gains are observed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While Bayesian Deep Ensembles combine Deep Ensembles with MCMC for uncertainty quantification, MCMC sampling can be prohibitively expensive. There's a practical need to determine how long to sample to get meaningful improvements over the initial optimized DE baseline.

Method: Formulates ensemble construction as a sequential anytime-valid hypothesis test using E-values. The null hypothesis is that MCMC offers no improvement over a strong baseline. The stopping rule decides when to reject the null or early stop sampling.

Result: Empirical studies across diverse settings show the approach is effective and that only a fraction of the full-chain budget is often required to determine whether MCMC provides meaningful improvement.

Conclusion: The proposed E-value based stopping rule provides a principled way to decide when MCMC sampling in Bayesian Deep Ensembles yields significant improvements over Deep Ensembles baseline, enabling efficient resource allocation.

Abstract: Bayesian Deep Ensembles (BDEs) represent a powerful approach for uncertainty quantification in deep learning, combining the robustness of Deep Ensembles (DEs) with flexible multi-chain MCMC. While DEs are affordable in most deep learning settings, (long) sampling of Bayesian neural networks can be prohibitively costly. Yet, adding sampling after optimizing the DEs has been shown to yield significant improvements. This leaves a critical practical question: How long should the sequential sampling process continue to yield significant improvements over the initial optimized DE baseline? To tackle this question, we propose a stopping rule based on E-values. We formulate the ensemble construction as a sequential anytime-valid hypothesis test, providing a principled way to decide whether or not to reject the null hypothesis that MCMC offers no improvement over a strong baseline, to early stop the sampling. Empirically, we study this approach for diverse settings. Our results demonstrate the efficacy of our approach and reveal that only a fraction of the full-chain budget is often required.

</details>


### [291] [Generalization Boundaries of Fine-Tuned Small Language Models for Graph Structural Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18092)
*Michal Podstawski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fine-tuned small language models (3-4B parameters) maintain structural inference capabilities beyond their training conditions, particularly showing ordinal consistency across graph families and size generalization with distinct architecture-specific degradation patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While small language models fine-tuned for graph property estimation show strong in-distribution performance, their generalization capabilities beyond training conditions (graph size and distribution) remain poorly understood and need systematic investigation.

Method: Used controlled experimental setup with three instruction-tuned models (3-4B parameters) and two graph serialization formats. Evaluated performance on graphs substantially larger than training range and across held-out random graph families to test generalization along size and distribution axes.

Result: Fine-tuned models maintain strong ordinal consistency across structurally distinct graph families and continue to rank graphs by structural properties on inputs substantially larger than those seen during training. Show distinct architecture-specific degradation profiles.

Conclusion: The findings delineate where fine-tuned small language models generalize reliably, providing empirical grounding for their use in graph-based reasoning tasks beyond training conditions.

Abstract: Small language models fine-tuned for graph property estimation have demonstrated strong in-distribution performance, yet their generalization capabilities beyond training conditions remain poorly understood. In this work, we systematically investigate the boundaries of structural inference in fine-tuned small language models along two generalization axes - graph size and graph family distribution - and assess domain-learning capability on real-world graph benchmarks. Using a controlled experimental setup with three instruction-tuned models in the 3-4B parameter class and two graph serialization formats, we evaluate performance on graphs substantially larger than the training range and across held-out random graph families. Our results show that fine-tuned models maintain strong ordinal consistency across structurally distinct graph families and continue to rank graphs by structural properties on inputs substantially larger than those seen during training, with distinct architecture-specific degradation profiles. These findings delineate where fine-tuned small language models generalize reliably, providing empirical grounding for their use in graph-based reasoning tasks.

</details>


### [292] [LoRaQ: Optimized Low Rank Approximation for 4-bit Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18117)
*Yann Bouquet,Alireza Khodamoradi,Sophie Yáng Shen,Kristof Denolf,Mathieu Salzmann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoRaQ is a data-free calibration method for post-training quantization of diffusion transformers that enables fully sub-16-bit quantization pipelines by optimizing quantization error compensation without requiring high-precision auxiliary branches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training quantization is crucial for deploying large diffusion transformers on resource-constrained hardware, but aggressive 4-bit quantization significantly degrades generative performance. Existing low-rank approximation methods require high-precision (W16A16) auxiliary branches and heavy, data-dependent calibration, which limits practical deployment.

Method: LoRaQ challenges the need for high-precision branches in low-rank approximation methods by introducing a simple, data-free calibration approach that optimizes quantization error compensation. This enables the low-rank branch itself to be quantized, creating the first fully sub-16-bit pipeline.

Result: LoRaQ outperforms state-of-the-art methods on Pixart-Σ and SANA at equal memory overhead. Mixed-precision configurations (W8A8, W6A6, W4A8 for low-rank branch alongside W4 main layer) yield superior results while maintaining full quantization compatibility with modern mixed-precision hardware.

Conclusion: LoRaQ successfully eliminates both the requirement for high-precision auxiliary branches and data-dependent calibration in post-training quantization for diffusion transformers, enabling more efficient deployment on resource-constrained hardware while maintaining or improving performance.

Abstract: Post-training quantization (PTQ) is essential for deploying large diffusion transformers on resource-constrained hardware, but aggressive 4-bit quantization significantly degrades generative performance. Low-rank approximation methods have emerged as a promising solution by appending auxiliary linear branches to restore performance. However, current state-of-the-art approaches assume these branches must retain high precision (W16A16) and rely on heavy, data-dependent calibration for initialization. We challenge both limitations with LoRaQ (Low-Rank Approximated Quantization), a simple, data-free calibration approach that optimizes quantization error compensation. By overcoming the need for high-precision branches, LoRaQ enables the first fully sub-16 bit pipeline, allowing the low-rank branch itself to be quantized. We demonstrate that, at equal memory overhead, LoRaQ outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in their native implementations on Pixart-$Σ$ and SANA. We also analyze mixed-precision configurations, showing that setups such as W8A8, W6A6, and W4A8 for the low-rank branch, alongside a W4 main layer, yield superior results while maintaining a fully quantized architecture compatible with modern mixed-precision hardware.

</details>


### [293] [An `Inverse' Experimental Framework to Estimate Market Efficiency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18130)
*Thomas Asikis,Heinrich Nax*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Developed quantile-based normalization and predictive models to estimate market allocative efficiency from early orderbook data (bids, asks, prices) without requiring true reservation values, enabling algorithmic governance decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current digital marketplaces lack principled measurement frameworks for assessing market efficiency and fairness from complex data. Traditional orderbook data alone is insufficient because bids/asks don't reveal true willingness to pay/sell, limiting economists' ability to evaluate actual market performance in allocative efficiency.

Method: Used experimental data by 'inverting' the standard induced value approach of double auction experiments. Developed quantile-based normalization techniques to handle unstructured, non-stationary, non-linear orderbook data. Trained multiple models (linear regressions and gradient boosting trees) using quantile-based input features from supply-demand model to predict key market features from early orderbook data.

Result: Models successfully predict allocative efficiency with reasonable accuracy from earliest bids and asks, with predictions improving as more price realization data becomes available. Prediction performance varies by target metric and market type.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables prediction of market efficiency from early orderbook data without trade realizations, offering significant potential for real-world application to inform algorithmic governance decisions about market performance.

Abstract: Digital marketplaces processing billions of dollars annually represent critical infrastructure in sociotechnical ecosystems, yet their performance optimization lacks principled measurement frameworks that can inform algorithmic governance decisions regarding market efficiency and fairness from complex market data. By looking at orderbook data from double auction markets alone, because bids and asks do not represent true maximum willingnesses to buy and true minimum willingnesses to sell, there is little an economist can say about the market's actual performance in terms of allocative efficiency. We turn to experimental data to address this issue, `inverting' the standard induced value approach of double auction experiments. Our aim is to predict key market features relevant to market efficiency, particularly allocative efficiency, using orderbook data only -- specifically bids, asks and price realizations, but not the induced reservation values -- as early as possible. Since there is no established model of strategically optimal behavior in these markets, and because orderbook data is highly unstructured, non-stationary and non-linear, we propose quantile-based normalization techniques that help us build general predictive models. We develop and train several models, including linear regressions and gradient boosting trees, leveraging quantile-based input from the underlying supply-demand model. Our models can predict allocative efficiency with reasonable accuracy from the earliest bids and asks, and these predictions improve with additional realized price data. The performance of the prediction techniques varies by target and market type. Our framework holds significant potential for application to real-world market data, offering valuable insights into market efficiency and performance, even prior to any trade realizations.

</details>


### [294] [Does "Do Differentiable Simulators Give Better Policy Gradients?'' Give Better Policy Gradients?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18161)
*Ku Onoda,Paavo Parmas,Manato Yaguchi,Yutaka Matsuo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates bias in policy gradient RL with discontinuous dynamics, proposing two lightweight solutions: DDCG for estimator switching in nonsmooth regions and IVW-H for variance stabilization in differentiable control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discontinuous dynamics in policy gradient RL cause bias that undermines 1st-order gradient estimators. Prior methods using REINFORCE for discontinuity detection are noisy, require hyperparameter tuning, and have low sample efficiency. The paper aims to understand if bias is the primary obstacle and find minimal fixes.

Method: Two approaches: 1) DDCG - a lightweight test that switches between 1st-order and 0th-order estimators in nonsmooth regions with a single hyperparameter. 2) IVW-H - a per-step inverse-variance weighted implementation that stabilizes variance without explicit discontinuity detection for differentiable robotics control tasks.

Result: DDCG achieves robust performance with small samples in standard discontinuous settings. IVW-H yields strong results in differentiable robotics control tasks by stabilizing variance. Both methods outperform prior REINFORCE-based approaches.

Conclusion: While estimator switching (DDCG) improves robustness in controlled studies, careful variance control (IVW-H) often dominates in practical deployments. The findings suggest bias is addressable with minimal fixes and variance stabilization is crucial for real-world applications.

Abstract: In policy gradient reinforcement learning, access to a differentiable model enables 1st-order gradient estimation that accelerates learning compared to relying solely on derivative-free 0th-order estimators. However, discontinuous dynamics cause bias and undermine the effectiveness of 1st-order estimators. Prior work addressed this bias by constructing a confidence interval around the REINFORCE 0th-order gradient estimator and using these bounds to detect discontinuities. However, the REINFORCE estimator is notoriously noisy, and we find that this method requires task-specific hyperparameter tuning and has low sample efficiency. This paper asks whether such bias is the primary obstacle and what minimal fixes suffice. First, we re-examine standard discontinuous settings from prior work and introduce DDCG, a lightweight test that switches estimators in nonsmooth regions; with a single hyperparameter, DDCG achieves robust performance and remains reliable with small samples. Second, on differentiable robotics control tasks, we present IVW-H, a per-step inverse-variance implementation that stabilizes variance without explicit discontinuity detection and yields strong results. Together, these findings indicate that while estimator switching improves robustness in controlled studies, careful variance control often dominates in practical deployments.

</details>


### [295] [Scalable Neighborhood-Based Multi-Agent Actor-Critic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18190)
*Tim Goppelsroeder,Rasmus Jensen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MADDPG-K extends MADDPG with k-nearest neighbor critics to reduce computational complexity while maintaining performance in multi-agent settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Centralized critic approaches in multi-agent reinforcement learning suffer from computational limitations - critic networks grow linearly with the number of agents, making them expensive to train at scale.

Method: MADDPG-K restricts each agent's critic to only consider the k closest agents based on Euclidean distance, ensuring constant-size critic input regardless of total agent count.

Result: The method achieves competitive or superior performance compared to MADDPG, shows faster convergence in cooperative settings, and better runtime scaling as number of agents grows.

Conclusion: MADDPG-K provides a scalable solution to centralized critic approaches by using k-nearest neighbor critics, effectively reducing computational complexity while maintaining performance across various multi-agent environments.

Abstract: We propose MADDPG-K, a scalable extension to Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MADDPG) that addresses the computational limitations of centralized critic approaches. Centralized critics, which condition on the observations and actions of all agents, have demonstrated significant performance gains in cooperative and competitive multi-agent settings. However, their critic networks grow linearly in input size with the number of agents, making them increasingly expensive to train at scale. MADDPG-K mitigates this by restricting each agent's critic to the $k$ closest agents under a chosen metric which in our case is Euclidean distance. This ensures a constant-size critic input regardless of the total agent count. We analyze the complexity of this approach, showing that the quadratic cost it retains arises from cheap scalar distance computations rather than the expensive neural network matrix multiplications that bottleneck standard MADDPG. We validate our method empirically across cooperative and adversarial environments from the Multi-Particle Environment suite, demonstrating competitive or superior performance compared to MADDPG, faster convergence in cooperative settings, and better runtime scaling as the number of agents grows. Our code is available at https://github.com/TimGop/MADDPG-K .

</details>


### [296] [Attraction, Repulsion, and Friction: Introducing DMF, a Friction-Augmented Drifting Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18194)
*Arkadii Kazanskii,Tatiana Petrova,Konstantin Bagrianskii,Aleksandr Puzikov,Radu State*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Drifting Models (DM) train one-step generators using kernel-based drift fields, avoiding ODE integration. The paper addresses open questions about repulsive regimes and drift-field equilibrium, proposing DMF with friction scheduling to improve performance with lower compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The original Drifting Models paper left two important questions open: 1) The drift-field iteration has a locally repulsive regime in two-particle surrogates, and 2) Vanishing of the drift field doesn't guarantee that the learned distribution matches the target. The authors aim to resolve these theoretical gaps and improve practical performance.

Method: The paper derives a contraction threshold for the surrogate model, introduces a linearly-scheduled friction coefficient for finite-horizon error bounds, and proves equilibrium identifiability under Gaussian kernels (vanishing drift on any open set forces q=p). They propose DMF (Drifting Model with Friction), a friction-augmented version of Drifting Models.

Result: DMF matches or exceeds Optimal Flow Matching performance on FFHQ adult-to-child domain translation while using 16x lower training compute. The theoretical contributions include contraction analysis, friction scheduling for error control, and proving drift-field equilibrium identifiability under Gaussian kernels.

Conclusion: The paper successfully addresses theoretical gaps in Drifting Models, providing contraction analysis and equilibrium identifiability proofs. The proposed DMF method demonstrates practical efficiency improvements, achieving state-of-the-art performance with significantly reduced computational cost for domain translation tasks.

Abstract: Drifting Models [Deng et al., 2026] train a one-step generator by evolving samples under a kernel-based drift field, avoiding ODE integration at inference. The original analysis leaves two questions open. The drift-field iteration admits a locally repulsive regime in a two-particle surrogate, and vanishing of the drift ($V_{p,q}\equiv 0$) is not known to force the learned distribution $q$ to match the target $p$. We derive a contraction threshold for the surrogate and show that a linearly-scheduled friction coefficient gives a finite-horizon bound on the error trajectory. Under a Gaussian kernel we prove that the drift-field equilibrium is identifiable: vanishing of $V_{p,q}$ on any open set forces $q=p$, closing the converse of Proposition 3.1 of Deng et al. Our friction-augmented model, DMF (Drifting Model with Friction), matches or exceeds Optimal Flow Matching on FFHQ adult-to-child domain translation at 16x lower training compute.

</details>


### [297] [FSEVAL: Feature Selection Evaluation Toolbox and Dashboard](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18227)
*Muhammad Rajabinasab,Arthur Zimek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FSEVAL is a comprehensive feature selection evaluation toolbox with visualization dashboard that provides standardized, unified evaluation of feature selection algorithms across supervised and unsupervised settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feature selection is crucial for addressing the curse of dimensionality while preserving explainability, but evaluating different feature selection algorithms is challenging due to lack of standardized tools and comprehensive evaluation frameworks.

Method: The authors developed FSEVAL, a feature selection evaluation toolbox with accompanying visualization dashboard, designed to provide standardized, unified evaluation capabilities for both supervised and unsupervised feature selection algorithms with different evaluation metrics.

Result: FSEVAL provides a practical toolbox that enables researchers to conduct extensive and comprehensive evaluation of feature selection algorithms with ease, offering both evaluation and visualization capabilities in one integrated system.

Conclusion: FSEVAL addresses the need for standardized evaluation tools in feature selection research, making it easier for researchers to comprehensively compare and evaluate different feature selection algorithms, which should advance the field by providing better benchmarking and comparison capabilities.

Abstract: Feature selection is a fundamental machine learning and data mining task, involved with discriminating redundant features from informative ones. It is an attempt to address the curse of dimensionality by removing the redundant features, while unlike dimensionality reduction methods, preserving explainability. Feature selection is conducted in both supervised and unsupervised settings, with different evaluation metrics employed to determine which feature selection algorithm is the best. In this paper, we propose FSEVAL, a feature selection evaluation toolbox accompanied with a visualization dashboard, with the goal to make it easy to comprehensively evaluate feature selection algorithms. FSEVAL aims to provide a standardized, unified, evaluation and visualization toolbox to help the researchers working in the field, conduct extensive and comprehensive evaluation of feature selection algorithms with ease.

</details>


### [298] [Semantic-based Distributed Learning for Diverse and Discriminative Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18237)
*Zhuojun Tian,Chaouki Ben Issaid,Mehdi Bennis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel distributed learning framework that ensures diverse and discriminative representations by introducing constraints on representation variance for i.i.d. data and using clustering with block coordinate descent for non-i.i.d. data, with theoretical guarantees and reduced architectural constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In large-scale distributed scenarios, complex tasks require intelligent collaboration and joint extraction of structural representations. Conventional task-specific approaches often lead to nonstructural embeddings with collapsed variability among samples of the same class, especially in classification tasks, failing to leverage the intrinsic data structure for downstream applications.

Method: For i.i.d. data: reformulate and decouple global optimization function with constraints on representation variance, deriving simplified update rules via primal-dual approach. For non-i.i.d. data: tackle through clustering and virtual node replication, using block coordinate descent for model updates within each cluster.

Result: Theoretical proofs show optimal solutions maintain discriminative and diverse properties with guaranteed convergence for i.i.d. conditions. Semantic information from representations is shared among nodes, reducing need for common neural network architectures. Extensive simulations on MNIST, CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 confirm effectiveness in capturing global structural representations.

Conclusion: The proposed distributed learning framework successfully addresses the problem of collapsed variability in representations by ensuring both diversity and discriminability, with different strategies for i.i.d. and non-i.i.d. data distributions, achieving effective global structural representation learning across various datasets.

Abstract: In large-scale distributed scenarios, increasingly complex tasks demand more intelligent collaboration across networks, requiring the joint extraction of structural representations from data samples. However, conventional task-specific approaches often result in nonstructural embeddings, leading to collapsed variability among data samples within the same class, particularly in classification tasks. To address this issue and fully leverage the intrinsic structure of data for downstream applications, we propose a novel distributed learning framework that ensures both diverse and discriminative representations. For independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) data, we reformulate and decouple the global optimization function by introducing constraints on representation variance. The update rules are then derived and simplified using a primal-dual approach. For non-i.i.d. data distributions, we tackle the problem by clustering and virtually replicating nodes, allowing model updates within each cluster using block coordinate descent. In both cases, the resulting optimal solutions are theoretically proven to maintain discriminative and diverse properties, with a guaranteed convergence for i.i.d. conditions. Additionally, semantic information from representations is shared among nodes, reducing the need for common neural network architectures. Finally, extensive simulations on MNIST, CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 confirm the effectiveness of the proposed algorithms in capturing global structural representations.

</details>


### [299] [Towards Disentangled Preference Optimization Dynamics Beyond Likelihood Displacement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18239)
*Wei Chen,Yubing Wu,Junmei Yang,Delu Zeng,Qibin Zhao,John Paisley,Min Chen,Zhou Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a unified framework for analyzing preference optimization in LLMs, identifies a "disentanglement band" condition to prevent likelihood displacement, and proposes a plug-and-play reward calibration method to improve training dynamics and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current margin-based preference optimization objectives often suppress both chosen and rejected responses (likelihood displacement), lacking a general mechanism to prevent this across different objectives.

Method: 1) Develops a unified incentive-score decomposition showing diverse objectives share identical update directions but differ in scalar weights. 2) Analyzes chosen/rejected likelihood dynamics to identify the "disentanglement band" condition for avoiding likelihood displacement. 3) Proposes reward calibration that adaptively rebalances chosen vs rejected updates to satisfy the disentanglement band condition.

Result: Empirical results show reward calibration steers training toward more disentangled dynamics and often improves downstream performance across various objectives.

Conclusion: The paper provides a principled framework for understanding and mitigating likelihood displacement in preference optimization, with practical reward calibration that works across different base objectives without redesigning them.

Abstract: Preference optimization is widely used to align large language models (LLMs) with human preferences. However, many margin-based objectives suppress the chosen response along with the rejected one, a phenomenon known as likelihood displacement, and no general mechanism currently prevents this across objectives.
  We bridge this gap by presenting a unified \emph{incentive-score decomposition} of preference optimization, revealing that diverse objectives share identical local update directions and differ only in their scalar weighting coefficients.
  Building on this decomposition, by analyzing the dynamics of the chosen/rejected likelihoods, we identify the \emph{disentanglement band} (DB), a simple, testable condition that characterizes when training can avoid likelihood displacement by realizing the preferred pathway: suppressing the loser while maintaining the winner, possibly after an initial transient.
  Leveraging the DB, we propose a plug-and-play \emph{reward calibration} (RC) that adaptively rebalances chosen versus rejected updates to satisfy the DB and mitigate likelihood displacement, without redesigning the base objective.
  Empirical results show that RC steers training toward more disentangled dynamics and often improves downstream performance across a range of objectives. Our code is available at https://github.com/IceyWuu/DisentangledPreferenceOptimization.

</details>


### [300] [Correction and Corruption: A Two-Rate View of Error Flow in LLM Protocols](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18245)
*Fernando Reitich*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A measurement interface for auditing LLM protocol steps using paired correctness bits to track correction and corruption rates, enabling predictable composition and mixture shift robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM protocols (multi-call procedures) are only evaluated end-to-end, giving limited insight into when they help/hurt, and whether behavior transfers under distribution shift or composition. Need better diagnostic tools.

Method: Proposed paired-outcome interface records baseline correctness (E0) and post-step correctness (E1) bits for each instance. Computes correction rate (c = Pr(E1=1|E0=0)) and corruption rate (γ = Pr(E1=0|E0=1)). Three diagnostic tests: mixture shift bias correction via difficulty proxy, presentation contamination detection, and state insufficiency test via Markov factorization.

Result: Interface enables reusable empirical testing across seeds, mixtures, pipelines. Can predict accuracy changes, detect failures. On synthetic math tasks and GSM8K, calibrated interface correctly predicts when protocol steps should be activated/suppressed.

Conclusion: Protocol steps become auditable modules when passing diagnostics: gated by estimated gain, conditioned on difficulty proxy to correct mixture bias, composed into multi-step pipelines with predictable accuracy. Provides rigorous framework for LLM protocol evaluation.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as protocols: structured multi-call procedures that spend additional computation to transform a baseline answer into a final one. These protocols are evaluated only by end-to-end accuracy, giving limited insight into when they help, when they hurt, and whether their behavior transfers under distribution shift or composition. We propose a paired-outcome measurement interface for auditing a single protocol step on exact-match tasks. For each instance, the interface records a baseline correctness bit $E_0\in\{0,1\}$ and a post-step correctness bit $E_1\in\{0,1\}$, separating correction ($E_0=0\to E_1=1$) from corruption ($E_0=1\to E_1=0$) through two rates: $c=\Pr(E_1=1\mid E_0=0)$ and $γ=\Pr(E_1=0\mid E_0=1)$. These rates predict accuracy changes and define a reusable empirical interface testable across seeds, mixtures, and pipelines. We identify three failure mechanisms. Under mixture shift, pooled estimates of $(c,γ)$ become biased when calibration and deployment mixtures differ; conditioning on a difficulty proxy restores stability without additional model calls. Under presentation contamination, selection protocols alter the interface through stable presentation artifacts when candidate content is fixed. Under state insufficiency, the correctness bit may not carry enough history for multi-step pipelines to compose predictably; a Markov factorization test identifies when composition is valid and where additional state is needed. When a protocol step passes these diagnostics, it becomes an auditable module: gated by estimated gain, conditioned on a difficulty proxy to correct mixture bias, and composed into multi-step pipelines with predictable accuracy. We demonstrate these ideas on synthetic mathematical tasks and on GSM8K, where the calibrated interface correctly predicts when protocol steps should be activated or suppressed.

</details>


### [301] [Universally Empowering Zeroth-Order Optimization via Adaptive Layer-wise Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18264)
*Fei Wang,Li Shen,Liang Ding,Chao Xue,Ye Liu,Changxing Ding*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AdaLeZO is an adaptive layer-wise zeroth-order optimization framework that accelerates LLM fine-tuning by dynamically allocating perturbation budget to sensitive parameters through bandit-based layer selection and inverse probability weighting, achieving 1.7-3x wall-clock speedup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current zeroth-order optimization methods for LLM fine-tuning suffer from slow convergence and high variance due to uniform exploration strategies that waste computation on insensitive parameters, with perturbation generation and parameter updates accounting for over 40% of training latency.

Method: AdaLeZO formulates layer selection as a non-stationary Multi-Armed Bandit problem to dynamically allocate perturbation budget to the most sensitive layers. It uses an Inverse Probability Weighting mechanism based on sampling with replacement to ensure unbiased gradient estimation while reducing variance through temporal denoising.

Result: The method achieves 1.7x to 3.0x wall-clock acceleration compared to state-of-the-art methods on LLaMA and OPT models ranging from 6.7B to 30B parameters. It serves as a plug-and-play module that enhances existing ZO optimizers without additional memory overhead.

Conclusion: AdaLeZO effectively addresses the structural mismatch in ZO optimization by accounting for layer heterogeneity, significantly accelerating LLM fine-tuning while maintaining memory efficiency. The framework represents a universal enhancement to existing ZO methods through intelligent budget allocation and variance reduction.

Abstract: Zeroth-Order optimization presents a promising memory-efficient paradigm for fine-tuning Large Language Models by relying solely on forward passes. However, its practical adoption is severely constrained by slow wall-clock convergence and high estimation variance. In this work, we dissect the runtime characteristics of ZO algorithms and identify a critical system bottleneck where the generation of perturbations and parameter updates accounts for over 40% of the training latency. We argue that the standard uniform exploration strategy is fundamentally flawed as it fails to account for the heterogeneous sensitivity of layers in deep networks, resulting in computationally wasteful blind searches. To address this structural mismatch, we propose AdaLeZO, an Adaptive Layer-wise ZO optimization framework. By formulating the layer selection process as a non-stationary Multi-Armed Bandit problem, AdaLeZO dynamically allocates the limited perturbation budget to the most sensitive parameters. We further introduce an Inverse Probability Weighting mechanism based on sampling with replacement, which guarantees unbiased gradient estimation while effectively acting as a temporal denoiser to reduce variance. Extensive experiments on LLaMA and OPT models ranging from 6.7B to 30B parameters demonstrate that AdaLeZO achieves 1.7x to 3.0x wall-clock acceleration compared to state-of-the-art methods. Crucially, AdaLeZO functions as a universal plug-and-play module that seamlessly enhances the efficiency of existing ZO optimizers without incurring additional memory overhead.

</details>


### [302] [Dissipative Latent Residual Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Modeling and Identification of Electromechanical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18277)
*Youyuan Long,Gokhan Solak,Arash Ajoudani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DiLaR-PINN: A physics-informed neural network with guaranteed energy dissipation for modeling complex dissipative effects in electromechanical systems, validated on a real-world helicopter system.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: First-principles models of electromechanical systems often fail to capture complex dissipative effects like joint friction, stray losses, and structural damping. While residual-learning PINNs can augment imperfect models, they may inadvertently inject artificial energy through unconstrained MLPs.

Method: Proposed DiLaR-PINN (dissipative latent residual PINN) with: 1) residual network operating only on unmeasurable latent state components, 2) parameterization in skew-dissipative form guaranteeing non-increasing energy for any network parameters, 3) recurrent rollout scheme with curriculum-based sequence length extension for stable training under partial state measurability.

Result: DiLaR-PINN outperformed four baselines on a real-world helicopter system: 1) pure physical model, 2) unstructured residual MLP, 3) DiLaR with soft dissipativity constraint, and 4) black-box LSTM. It more accurately captured dissipative effects and achieved superior long-horizon extrapolation performance.

Conclusion: DiLaR-PINN provides a physically consistent framework for learning unmodeled dissipative dynamics with guaranteed energy dissipation properties, enabling more accurate dynamical modeling and superior long-term prediction compared to both traditional physics-based and unconstrained data-driven approaches.

Abstract: Accurate dynamical modeling is essential for simulation and control of embodied systems, yet first-principles models of electromechanical systems often fail to capture complex dissipative effects such as joint friction, stray losses, and structural damping. While residual-learning physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) can effectively augment imperfect first-principles models with data-driven components, the residual terms are typically implemented as unconstrained multilayer perceptrons (MLPs), which may inadvertently inject artificial energy into the system.
  To more faithfully model the dissipative dynamics, we propose DiLaR-PINN, a dissipative latent residual PINN designed to learn unmodeled dissipative effects in a physically consistent manner. Structurally, the residual network operates only on unmeasurable (latent) state components and is parameterized in a skew-dissipative form that guarantees non-increasing energy for any choice of network parameters. To enable stable and data-efficient training under partial measurability of the state, we further develop a recurrent rollout scheme with a curriculum-based sequence length extension strategy.
  We validate DiLaR-PINN on a real-world helicopter system and compare it against four baselines: a pure physical model (without a residual network), an unstructured residual MLP, a DiLaR variant with a soft dissipativity constraint, and a black-box LSTM. The results demonstrate that DiLaR-PINN more accurately captures dissipative effects and achieves superior long-horizon extrapolation performance.

</details>


### [303] [CAARL: In-Context Learning for Interpretable Co-Evolving Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18305)
*Etienne Tajeuna,Patrick Asante Owusu,Armelle Brun,Shengrui Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CAARL is a novel interpretable forecasting framework that uses LLMs to model coevolving time series by decomposing them into autoregressive segments, constructing temporal dependency graphs, and serializing them into narratives for LLM processing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of forecasting coevolving time series with complex dependencies and nonstationary dynamics, where existing methods often lack interpretability in capturing contextual influences on series changes.

Method: CAARL decomposes time series into autoregressive segments, constructs a temporal dependency graph to capture relationships, serializes this graph into a narrative format, and processes it through LLMs to generate interpretable reasoning paths for forecasting.

Result: Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that CAARL achieves competitive accuracy while providing enhanced interpretability through explicit reasoning traces, positioning it as an effective alternative to state-of-the-art forecasting methods.

Conclusion: CAARL successfully bridges the gap between accuracy and interpretability in time series forecasting by leveraging LLMs to provide transparent, chain-of-thought-like reasoning for understanding contextual dynamics in coevolving series.

Abstract: In this paper we investigate forecasting coevolving time series that feature intricate dependencies and nonstationary dynamics by using an LLM Large Language Models approach We propose a novel modeling approach named ContextAware ARLLM CAARL that provides an interpretable framework to decode the contextual dynamics influencing changes in coevolving series CAARL decomposes time series into autoregressive segments constructs a temporal dependency graph and serializes this graph into a narrative to allow processing by LLM This design yields a chainofthoughtlike reasoning path where intermediate steps capture contextual dynamics and guide forecasts in a transparent manner By linking prediction to explicit reasoning traces CAARL enhances interpretability while maintaining accuracy Experiments on realworld datasets validate its effectiveness positioning CAARL as a competitive and interpretable alternative to stateoftheart forecasting methods

</details>


### [304] [Scale-free adaptive planning for deterministic dynamics & discounted rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18312)
*Peter L. Bartlett,Victor Gabillon,Jennifer Healey,Michal Valko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Platypoos: A scale-free planning algorithm for environments with deterministic dynamics and stochastic rewards, providing optimal sample complexity bounds without prior knowledge of reward scale or discount factors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the problem of planning in environments with deterministic dynamics but stochastic rewards with discounted returns, where the optimal value function is unknown and rewards are not bounded. Existing methods require knowledge of reward scale and smoothness, which may not be available in practice.

Method: Proposes Platypoos, a simple scale-free planning algorithm that adapts to unknown scale and smoothness of the reward function. The algorithm doesn't require prior knowledge of discount factors or reward scales and works with unbounded rewards.

Result: Provides a sample complexity analysis for Platypoos that improves upon prior work. The analysis holds simultaneously over a broad range of discount factors and reward scales without algorithm knowing them. Also establishes a matching lower bound showing the analysis is optimal up to constants.

Conclusion: Platypoos represents an optimal solution for planning in deterministic-stochastic reward environments, achieving provably optimal sample complexity bounds without requiring prior knowledge of system parameters like reward scale or discount factors.

Abstract: We address the problem of planning in an environment with deterministic dynamics and stochastic rewards with discounted returns. The optimal value function is not known, nor are the rewards bounded. We propose Platypoos, a simple scale-free planning algorithm that adapts to the unknown scale and smoothness of the reward function. We provide a sample complexity analysis for Platypoos that improves upon prior work and holds simultaneously over a broad range of discount factors and reward scales, without the algorithm knowing them. We also establish a matching lower bound showing our analysis is optimal up to constants.

</details>


### [305] [Parkinson's Disease Detection via Self-Supervised Dual-Channel Cross-Attention on Bilateral Wrist-Worn IMU Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18372)
*Meheru Zannat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A self-supervised cross-attention encoder using wrist-worn IMU sensors achieves high accuracy in classifying Parkinson's disease vs. healthy controls and differential diagnoses, with real-time deployment on Raspberry Pi.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Parkinson's disease diagnosis through clinical physical exams is time-consuming and subjective. Wearable IMU sensors offer a promising solution for passive monitoring, but better automated classification methods are needed.

Method: Self-supervised cross-attention encoder that processes bilateral wrist-worn IMU signals from the PADS dataset (469 subjects across PD, HC, and DD groups). Uses contrastive infoNCE loss for representation learning and transfer learning with minimal labels.

Result: Achieved 93.12% mean accuracy for HC vs. PD classification and 87.04% for PD vs. DD classification. Self-supervised learning gained 93.56% accuracy for HC vs. PD and 92.50% for PD vs. DD using only 20% labeled data. Real-time inference time of 48.32 ms per window on Raspberry Pi CPU.

Conclusion: The proposed method demonstrates effective automated PD classification using wearable sensors, highlighting both the clinical challenge of differential diagnosis and the promise of self-supervised learning for clinical applications with limited labeled data.

Abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a chronic neurodegenerative disease. It shows multiple motor symptoms such as tremor, bradykinesia, postural instability, freezing of gait (FoG). PD is currently diagnosed clinically through physical exam by health-care professionals, which can be time consuming and highly subjective. Wearable IMU sensors has become a promising gateway for passive monitoring of PD patients. We propose a self-supervised cross-attention encoder that processes bilateral wrist-worn IMU signals from a public dataset called PADS, consisting of three groups, PD (Parkinson Disease), HC (Healthy Control) and DD (Differential Diagnosis) of a total of 469 subjects. We have achieved a mean accuracy of 93.12% for HC vs. PD classification and 87.04% for PD vs. DD classification. The results emphasize the clinical challenge of distinguishing Parkinson's from other neurodegenerative diseases. Self-supervised representation learning using contrastive infoNCE loss gained an accuracy of 93.56% for HC vs. PD and 92.50% for PD vs. DD using only 20% of labelled data. This demonstrates the effectiveness of our method in transfer learning for clinical use with minimal labels. The real-time applicability was tested by deploying the optimized model with a mean inference time of 48.32 ms per window on a Raspberry Pi CPU.

</details>


### [306] [Forecasting Ionospheric Irregularities on GNSS Lines of Sight Using Dynamic Graphs with Ephemeris Conditioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18379)
*Mert Can Turkmen,Eng Leong Tan,Yee Hui Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dynamic graph neural network models ionosphere using evolving satellite pierce points, enabling forecasts on future satellite positions through ephemeris conditioning, achieving significant improvements over persistence for ionospheric irregularity prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data-driven ionospheric forecasting models use gridded products that lose the time-varying sampling structure of satellite-based sensing, which is problematic since satellite positions (and thus measurement locations) constantly change.

Method: Model ionosphere as dynamic graph over ionospheric pierce points (IPPs) with connectivity evolving as satellites move. Use ephemeris conditioning - conditioning forecasts on predictable future graph structure - enabling predictions on lines of sight that only appear in forecast horizon. Implement as IonoDGNN for binary probabilistic classification of ROTI-defined irregularities.

Result: Achieves Brier Skill Score of 0.49 and PR-AUC of 0.75, improving over persistence by 35% in BSS and 52% in PR-AUC, with larger gains at longer lead times. Ephemeris conditioning crucial for satellites rising during forecast (ROC AUC: 0.95 vs. 0.52 without). Maintains skill under coverage dropout through spatial message passing.

Conclusion: Dynamic graph forecasting on evolving lines of sight is a viable alternative to grid-based representations for ionospheric irregularity forecasting, effectively handling changing satellite geometries and enabling predictions on future measurement locations.

Abstract: Most data-driven ionospheric forecasting models operate on gridded products, which do not preserve the time-varying sampling structure of satellite-based sensing. We instead model the ionosphere as a dynamic graph over ionospheric pierce points (IPPs), with connectivity that evolves as satellite positions change. Because satellite trajectories are predictable, the graph topology over the forecast horizon can be constructed in advance. We exploit this property to condition forecasts on the future graph structure, which we term ephemeris conditioning. This enables prediction on lines of sight that appear only in the forecast horizon. We evaluate our framework on multi-GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) data from a co-located receiver pair in Singapore spanning January 2023 through April 2025. The task is to forecast Rate of TEC Index (ROTI)-defined irregularities at 5-minute cadence up to 2 hours ahead as binary probabilistic classification per node. The resulting model, IonoDGNN, achieves a Brier Skill Score (BSS) of 0.49 and a precision-recall area under the curve (PR-AUC) of 0.75, improving over persistence by 35\% in BSS and 52\% in PR-AUC, with larger gains at longer lead times. Ablations confirm that graph structure and ephemeris conditioning each contribute meaningfully, with conditioning proving essential for satellites that rise during the forecast horizon (receiver operating characteristic AUC: 0.95 vs.\ 0.52 without). Under simulated coverage dropout, the model retains predictive skill on affected nodes through spatial message passing from observed neighbors. These results suggest that dynamic graph forecasting on evolving lines of sight is a viable alternative to grid-based representations for ionospheric irregularity forecasting. The model and evaluation code will be released upon publication.

</details>


### [307] [Randomly Initialized Networks Can Learn from Peer-to-Peer Consensus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18390)
*Esteban Rodríguez-Betancourt,Edgar Casasola-Murillo*

Main category: cs.LG
