TL;DR: Minimal self-distillation setup without projectors, predictors, or pretext tasks still yields non-trivial representation learning improvements over random baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current self-distillation methods rely on complex ensembles with empirically-motivated design choices that are poorly understood. The authors want to isolate and understand the core role of self-distillation itself in learning dynamics.

Method: Train a group of randomly initialized networks with only self-distillation, removing all common components like projectors, predictors, and pretext tasks. Use this minimal setup to isolate the effect of self-distillation alone.

Result: Even this minimal self-distillation setup leads to learned representations with non-trivial improvements over random baselines on downstream tasks. The effect varies with different hyperparameters, and analysis reveals what models learn under this setup.

Conclusion: Self-distillation itself plays a significant role in representation learning independent of other complex components. The minimal setup provides insights into the fundamental mechanisms of self-distillation learning dynamics.

Abstract: In self-supervised learning, self-distilled methods have shown impressive performance, learning representations useful for downstream tasks and even displaying emergent properties. However, state-of-the-art methods usually rely on ensembles of complex mechanisms, with many design choices that are empirically motivated and not well understood.
  In this work, we explore the role of self-distillation within learning dynamics. Specifically, we isolate the effect of self-distillation by training a group of randomly initialized networks, removing all other common components such as projectors, predictors, and even pretext tasks. Our findings show that even this minimal setup can lead to learned representations with non-trivial improvements over a random baseline on downstream tasks. We also demonstrate how this effect varies with different hyperparameters and present a short analysis of what is being learned by the models under this setup.

</details>


### [308] [Bridge-Centered Metapath Classification Using R-GCN-VGAE for Disaster-Resilient Maintenance Decisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18399)
*Takato Yasuno*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes an R-GCN-VGAE framework to classify bridges into disaster-preparedness categories using heterogeneous graphs from open data, enabling better maintenance prioritization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing single-indicator approaches fail to quantify the multi-dimensional roles bridges play in disaster scenarios, making it challenging to prioritize bridge maintenance under limited budgets for urban resilience.

Method: Construct heterogeneous graphs with road, bridge, and building layers from open data using OSMnx; use R-GCN-VGAE to learn metapath-based feature representations for classifying bridges into Supply Chain, Medical Access, and Residential Protection categories.

Result: Validated methodology on 1,103 bridges across three Japanese cities; successfully classified bridges into disaster-preparedness categories; demonstrated UMAP's superiority over t-SNE/PCA for visualization; established k-NN tuning strategy across diverse city scales.

Conclusion: The approach enables data-driven bridge maintenance prioritization for disaster resilience by redefining bridge roles through metapath-based classification, supporting better budget allocation decisions using open data.

Abstract: Daily infrastructure management in preparation for disasters is critical for urban resilience. When bridges remain resilient against disaster-induced external forces, access to hospitals, shops, and residences via metapaths can be sustained, maintaining essential urban functions. However, prioritizing bridge maintenance under limited budgets requires quantifying the multi-dimensional roles that bridges play in disaster scenarios -- a challenge that existing single-indicator approaches fail to address. We focus on metapaths from national highways through bridges to buildings (hospitals, shops, residences), constructing a heterogeneous graph with road, bridge, and building layers. A Relation-centric Graph Convolutional Network Variational Autoencoder (R-GCN-VGAE) learns metapath-based feature representations, enabling classification of bridges into disaster-preparedness categories: Supply Chain (commercial logistics), Medical Access (emergency healthcare), and Residential Protection (preventing isolation). Using OSMnx and open data, we validate our methodology on three diverse cities in Ibaraki Prefecture, Japan: Mito (697 bridges), Chikusei (258 bridges), and Moriya (148 bridges), totaling 1,103 bridges. The heterogeneous graph construction from open data enables redefining bridge roles for disaster scenarios, supporting maintenance budget decision-making. We contributed that (1) Open-data methodology for constructing urban heterogeneous graphs. (2) Redefinition of bridge roles for disaster scenarios via metapath-based classification. (3) Establishment of maintenance budget decision support methodology. (4) k-NN tuning strategy validated across diverse city scales. (5) Empirical demonstration of UMAP superiority over t-SNE/PCA for multi-role bridge visualization.

</details>


### [309] [Balance-Guided Sparse Identification of Multiscale Nonlinear PDEs with Small-coefficient Terms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18414)
*Zhenhua Dang,Lei Zhang,Long Wang,Guowei He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BG-SINDy is a new method that uses balance-guided sparse regression to discover governing equations with small-coefficient terms that traditional methods often miss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data-driven methods for discovering governing equations struggle with multiscale systems where dynamically significant terms may have small coefficients, causing these important terms to be incorrectly pruned during sparse regression.

Method: Proposes Balance-Guided SINDy (BG-SINDy) inspired by dominant balance principle. Reformulates ℓ₀-constrained sparse regression as term-level ℓ₂,₀-regularized problem with progressive pruning. Terms are ranked by their relative contributions to governing equation balance rather than absolute coefficient magnitudes, alternating between least-squares regression and elimination of negligible terms.

Result: Successfully demonstrates BG-SINDy's validity in discovering small-coefficient terms across multiple systems: Korteweg-de Vries equation with small dispersion, modified Burgers equation with vanishing hyperviscosity, modified Kuramoto-Sivashinsky equation with multiple small-coefficient terms, and 2D reaction-diffusion system.

Conclusion: BG-SINDy provides an efficient approach for discovering governing equations that contain small-coefficient terms by preserving dynamically significant terms even when their coefficients are small, overcoming limitations of traditional sparse regression methods.

Abstract: Data-driven discovery of governing equations has advanced significantly in recent years; however, existing methods often struggle in multiscale systems where dynamically significant terms may have small coefficients. Therefore, we propose Balance-Guided SINDy (BG-SINDy) inspired by the principle of dominant balance, which reformulates $\ell_0$-constrained sparse regression as a term-level $\ell_{2,0}$-regularized problem and solves it using a progressive pruning strategy. Terms are ranked according to their relative contributions to the governing equation balance rather than their absolute coefficient magnitudes. Based on this criterion, BG-SINDy alternates between least-squares regression and elimination of negligible terms, thereby preserving dynamically significant terms even when their coefficients are small. Numerical experiments on the Korteweg--de Vries equation with a small dispersion coefficient, a modified Burgers equation with vanishing hyperviscosity, a modified Kuramoto--Sivashinsky equation with multiple small-coefficient terms, and a two-dimensional reaction--diffusion system demonstrate the validity of BG-SINDy in discovering small-coefficient terms. The proposed method thus provides an efficient approach for discovering governing equations that contain small-coefficient terms.

</details>


### [310] [Knowing When to Quit: A Principled Framework for Dynamic Abstention in LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18419)
*Hen Davidov,Nachshon Cohen,Oren Kalinsky,Yaron Fairstein,Guy Kushilevitz,Ram Yazdi,Patrick Rebeschini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dynamic mid-generation abstention for LLMs that terminates unpromising reasoning traces early, outperforming pre/post-generation methods through a principled RL framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs waste substantial compute on long, incorrect chain-of-thought reasoning. Current abstention methods decide before or after generation, but dynamic mid-generation abstention lacks principled guidance for termination rules.

Method: Formal analysis modeling abstention as an explicit action within a regularized reinforcement learning framework. Derives a principled abstention rule: abstain when value function falls below abstention reward parameter, with efficient method to approximate value function.

Result: Theory shows abstaining when value function falls below abstention reward strictly outperforms baselines under general conditions. Empirical results on mathematical reasoning and toxicity avoidance demonstrate improved selective accuracy over existing methods.

Conclusion: Dynamic mid-generation abstention with principled value-function-based termination rules provides an effective approach to reduce compute waste in LLM reasoning while maintaining accuracy.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) using chain-of-thought reasoning often waste substantial compute by producing long, incorrect responses. Abstention can mitigate this by withholding outputs unlikely to be correct. While most abstention methods decide to withhold outputs before or after generation, dynamic mid-generation abstention considers early termination of unpromising reasoning traces at each token position. Prior work has explored empirical variants of this idea, but principled guidance for the abstention rule remains lacking. We present a formal analysis of dynamic abstention for LLMs, modeling abstention as an explicit action within a regularized reinforcement learning framework. An abstention reward parameter controls the trade-off between compute and information. We show that abstaining when the value function falls below this reward strictly outperforms natural baselines under general conditions. We further derive a principled and efficient method to approximate the value function. Empirical results on mathematical reasoning and toxicity avoidance tasks support our theory and demonstrate improved selective accuracy over existing methods.

</details>


### [311] [Scalable Physics-Informed Neural Differential Equations and Data-Driven Algorithms for HVAC Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18438)
*Hanfeng Zhai,Hongtao Qiao,Hassan Mansour,Christopher Laughman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A scalable, data-driven simulation framework for large-scale HVAC systems combining physics-informed neural ODEs with DAE solvers for efficient and accurate system simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for scalable simulation of large-scale HVAC systems that balances accuracy with computational efficiency, going beyond traditional high-fidelity simulations that are computationally expensive.

Method: Two-level approach: (1) Component-level learning using implicit physics-informed neural ODEs (PINODEs) for heat-exchanger dynamics with physics-informed training via automatic differentiation, (2) System-level integration with DAE solvers (IDA and DASSL) that enforce junction constraints, plus Bayesian optimization for solver tuning and a lightweight corrector network for bias reduction.

Result: Multi-fold speedups over high-fidelity simulation while maintaining low errors (MAPE below a few percent), scaling to systems with up to 32 compressor-condenser pairs in dual-compressor and scaled network studies.

Conclusion: The framework successfully combines data-driven learning with physics-based constraints to create an efficient, scalable simulation approach for large-scale HVAC systems that maintains accuracy while significantly reducing computational cost.

Abstract: We present a scalable, data-driven simulation framework for large-scale heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) systems that couples physics-informed neural ordinary differential equations (PINODEs) with differential-algebraic equation (DAE) solvers. At the component level, we learn heat-exchanger dynamics using an implicit PINODE formulation that predicts conserved quantities (refrigerant mass $M_r$ and internal energy $E_\text{hx}$) as outputs, enabling physics-informed training via automatic differentiation of mass/energy balances. Stable long-horizon prediction is achieved through gradient-stabilized latent evolution with gated architectures and layer normalization. At the system level, we integrate learned components with DAE solvers (IDA and DASSL) that explicitly enforce junction constraints (pressure equilibrium and mass-flow consistency), and we use Bayesian optimization to tune solver parameters for accuracy--efficiency trade-offs. To reduce residual system-level bias, we introduce a lightweight corrector network trained on short trajectory segments. Across dual-compressor and scaled network studies, the proposed approach attains multi-fold speedups over high-fidelity simulation while keeping errors low (MAPE below a few percent) and scales to systems with up to 32 compressor--condenser pairs.

</details>


### [312] [ProtoCLIP: Prototype-Aligned Latent Refinement for Robust Zero-Shot Chest X-Ray Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18444)
*Florian Kittler,Sheethal Bhat,Andreas Maier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProtoCLIP improves zero-shot chest radiograph classification by addressing co-occurrence bias and domain shift through curated negative samples and representation-preserving distillation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot vision-language models for chest radiograph classification suffer from three main limitations: confounding label co-occurrence (multiple pathologies appearing together), long-tail class imbalance, and transfer instability under domain shift. These issues degrade performance when applying VLMs to medical imaging tasks.

Method: ProtoCLIP proposes a refinement strategy with two key components: 1) Constructing pathology-focused training subsets with curated negative samples to reduce co-occurrence bias, and 2) Introducing a representation-preserving distillation objective that stabilizes adaptation while maintaining semantic structure and improving discrimination of clinically relevant co-occurring pathologies.

Result: On the unseen VinDr-CXR dataset, ProtoCLIP improves AUC by 2-10 percentage points over strong CLIP-based baselines across multiple findings. For pneumothorax specifically, it achieves a state-of-the-art AUC of 0.94. The approach demonstrates effectiveness without requiring large-scale retraining.

Conclusion: Anchor-guided refinement with curated supervision and controlled adaptation can effectively mitigate common zero-shot transfer failures in medical VLMs. The method addresses co-occurrence bias and domain shift while improving discrimination of clinically relevant pathologies.

Abstract: Zero-shot vision-language models (VLMs) have shown promise for chest radiograph classification, but their performance is often limited by confounding label co-occurrence, long-tail class imbalance, and transfer instability under domain shift. We propose ProtoCLIP, a refinement strategy for CLIP-style VLMs that improves zero-shot discrimination through targeted data curation and distilled anchor alignment. Specifically, we construct pathology-focused training subsets with curated negative samples to reduce co-occurrence bias. We also introduce a representation-preserving distillation objective to stabilize adaptation while maintaining semantic structure and improving discrimination of clinically relevant co-occurring pathologies. Evaluated on an unseen dataset VinDr-CXR, ProtoCLIP improves AUC by 2-10 percentage points over a strong CLIP-based baseline across multiple findings. For pneumothorax specifically, ProtoCLIP achieves a state-of-the-art AUC of 0.94. These results demonstrate that anchor-guided refinement, coupled with curated supervision and controlled adaptation, can mitigate common zero-shot transfer failures in medical VLMs without requiring large-scale retraining.

</details>


### [313] [AutoPPA: Automated Circuit PPA Optimization via Contrastive Code-based Rule Library Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18445)
*Chongxiao Li,Pengwei Jin,Di Huang,Guangrun Sun,Husheng Han,Jianan Mu,Xinyao Zheng,Jiaguo Zhu,Shuyi Xing,Hanjun Wei,Tianyun Ma,Shuyao Cheng,Rui Zhang,Ying Wang,Zidong Du,Qi Guo,Xing Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AutoPPA is an automated PPA optimization framework that generates optimization rules through an Explore-Evaluate-Induce workflow and uses adaptive multi-step search to apply the most effective rules for circuit optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based approaches for PPA optimization are inefficient - feedback-based methods design without prior knowledge, while knowledge-based methods rely heavily on human-summarized rules, making the process suboptimal.

Method: AutoPPA employs an Explore-Evaluate-Induce (E²I) workflow that contrasts and abstracts optimization rules from generated code pairs, then uses an adaptive multi-step search framework to apply the most effective rules for each circuit.

Result: AutoPPA outperforms both manual optimization and state-of-the-art methods SymRTLO and RTLRewriter in experiments.

Conclusion: AutoPPA provides a fully automated approach to PPA optimization that automatically generates and adaptively applies optimization rules, offering a more efficient solution than existing methods.

Abstract: Performance, power, and area (PPA) optimization is a fundamental task in RTL design, requiring a precise understanding of circuit functionality and the relationship between circuit structures and PPA metrics. Recent studies attempt to automate this process using LLMs, but neither feedback-based nor knowledge-based methods are efficient enough, as they either design without any prior knowledge or rely heavily on human-summarized optimization rules.
  In this paper, we propose AutoPPA, a fully automated PPA optimization framework. The key idea is to automatically generate optimization rules that enhance the search for optimal solutions. To do this, AutoPPA employs an Explore-Evaluate-Induce ($E^2I$) workflow that contrasts and abstracts rules from diverse generated code pairs rather than manually defined prior knowledge, yielding better optimization patterns. To make the abstracted rules more generalizable, AutoPPA employs an adaptive multi-step search framework that adopts the most effective rules for a given circuit. Experiments show that AutoPPA outperforms both the manual optimization and the state-of-the-art methods SymRTLO and RTLRewriter.

</details>


### [314] [Learning Invariant Modality Representation for Robust Multimodal Learning from a Causal Inference Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18460)
*Sijie Mai,Shiqin Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CmIR is a causal modality-invariant representation learning framework that separates multimodal data into causal invariant representations and environment-specific spurious representations to improve robustness and generalization across distribution shifts and noisy modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal affective computing models often learn spurious correlations that harm generalization under distribution shifts or when dealing with noisy modalities. There's a need for more robust approaches that can maintain stable performance across different environments and conditions.

Method: The paper introduces CmIR, a theoretically grounded disentanglement method from a causal inference perspective. It separates each modality into two components: 'causal invariant representation' and 'environment-specific spurious representation'. The framework uses three key constraints: invariance constraint, mutual information constraint, and reconstruction constraint to ensure the learned invariant representations retain stable predictive relationships with labels while preserving sufficient information from raw inputs.

Result: Experiments across multiple multimodal benchmarks demonstrate that CmIR achieves state-of-the-art performance. It particularly excels on out-of-distribution data and noisy data, confirming its robustness and generalizability.

Conclusion: The proposed CmIR framework provides an effective approach for robust multimodal learning by addressing spurious correlations through causal disentanglement, leading to improved performance and generalization capabilities in multimodal affective computing tasks.

Abstract: Multimodal affective computing aims to predict humans' sentiment, emotion, intention, and opinion using language, acoustic, and visual modalities. However, current models often learn spurious correlations that harm generalization under distribution shifts or noisy modalities. To address this, we propose a causal modality-invariant representation (CmIR) learning framework for robust multimodal learning. At its core, we introduce a theoretically grounded disentanglement method that separates each modality into `causal invariant representation' and `environment-specific spurious representation' from a causal inference perspective. CmIR ensures that the learned invariant representations retain stable predictive relationships with labels across different environments while preserving sufficient information from the raw inputs via invariance constraint, mutual information constraint, and reconstruction constraint. Experiments across multiple multimodal benchmarks demonstrate that CmIR achieves state-of-the-art performance. CmIR particularly excels on out-of-distribution data and noisy data, confirming its robustness and generalizability.

</details>


### [315] [Semantic Step Prediction: Multi-Step Latent Forecasting in LLM Reasoning Trajectories via Step Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18464)
*Yidi Yuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STP with consecutive step-boundary sampling achieves 168x more accurate multi-step latent prediction vs random-token sampling (4x), revealing sampling position as critical for geometric regularization and establishing MSE as new evaluation metric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper investigates whether Semantic Tube Prediction (STP) sampling position can enhance semantic structure of multi-step reasoning and affect its geometric impact, moving beyond random token sampling to targeted semantic boundaries.

Method: Applied STP at consecutive semantic reasoning step boundaries during fine-tuning, analyzed trajectories with non-linear predictors (3-layer MLP), and experimented with removing language modeling loss to isolate geometric effects.

Result: Achieved 168x more accurate multi-step latent prediction than frozen baselines (vs 4x for random-token STP), revealed trajectories are smooth curves not straight lines (3-12x error reduction with MLP over linear), and found tradeoff between generation quality and geometric purity.

Conclusion: Sampling position is the critical variable in geometric regularization, multi-step latent prediction MSE serves as effective evaluation metric, and there exists tradeoff between generation quality and geometric purity in STP methods.

Abstract: Semantic Tube Prediction (STP) leverages representation geometric to regularize LLM hidden-state trajectories toward locally linear geodesics during fine-tuning, thereby greatly improving data efficiency. The original STP recipe samples random token sub-spans, which is compatible with the base large language model (LLM) training architecture. Inspired by STP, we are interested to investigate whether the sampling position can further enhance the semantic structure of multi-step reasoning, and hence affect its geometric impact. We applied STP at consecutive semantic reasoning step boundaries and achieved 168x more accurate multi-step latent prediction than frozen baselines on ProcessBench (3,400 samples), compared to only 4x for the random-token STP. Probing the latent manifold with a learned non-linear predictor reveals that STP-shaped trajectories are smooth curves, not straight lines: a 3-layer MLP reduces prediction error by a further 3-12x over linear extrapolation on step-boundary models. Removing the language modeling loss yields trajectories that are 2x more MLP-predictable than the combined loss, revealing a tradeoff between generation quality and geometric purity. Our results identify sampling position as the critical variable in geometric regularization and establish multi-step latent prediction MSE as a new evaluation metric for this class of methods.

</details>


### [316] [An Integrated Deep-Learning Framework for Peptide-Protein Interaction Prediction and Target-Conditioned Peptide Generation with ConGA-PePPI and TC-PepGen](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18467)
*Chupei Tang,Junxiao Kong,Moyu Tang,Di Wang,Jixiu Zhai,Ronghao Xie,Shangkun Sima,Tianchi Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An integrated framework combining partner-aware peptide-protein interaction prediction (ConGA-PepPI) with target-conditioned peptide generation (TC-PepGen) for early-stage peptide screening.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Peptide-protein interactions are crucial for cellular regulation and therapeutics, but experimental characterization is too slow for large-scale screening. Existing methods focus on either interaction prediction OR peptide generation, leaving gaps in candidate prioritization, residue-level interpretation, and target-conditioned expansion.

Method: Two-component framework: 1) ConGA-PepPI uses asymmetric encoding, bidirectional cross-attention, and progressive transfer from pair prediction to binding-site localization. 2) TC-PepGen preserves target information throughout autoregressive decoding via layerwise conditioning for target-conditioned peptide generation.

Result: ConGA-PepPI achieved 0.839 accuracy and 0.921 AUROC in cross-validation, with binding-site AUPR of 0.601 (protein) and 0.950 (peptide). TC-PepGen generated peptides where 40.39% exceeded native templates in AlphaFold 3 ipTM, with evidence of target-conditioned signal in unconstrained generation.

Conclusion: The integrated framework successfully addresses limitations of existing methods by combining accurate partner-aware interaction prediction with effective target-conditioned peptide generation, enabling more comprehensive early-stage peptide screening.

Abstract: Motivation: Peptide-protein interactions (PepPIs) are central to cellular regulation and peptide therapeutics, but experimental characterization remains too slow for large-scale screening. Existing methods usually emphasize either interaction prediction or peptide generation, leaving candidate prioritization, residue-level interpretation, and target-conditioned expansion insufficiently integrated. Results: We present an integrated framework for early-stage peptide screening that combines a partner-aware prediction and localization model (ConGA-PepPI) with a target-conditioned generative model (TC-PepGen). ConGA-PepPI uses asymmetric encoding, bidirectional cross-attention, and progressive transfer from pair prediction to binding-site localization, while TC-PepGen preserves target information throughout autoregressive decoding via layerwise conditioning. In five-fold cross-validation, ConGA-PepPI achieved 0.839 accuracy and 0.921 AUROC, with binding-site AUPR values of 0.601 on the protein side and 0.950 on the peptide side, and remained competitive on external benchmarks. Under a controlled length-conditioned benchmark, 40.39% of TC-PepGen peptides exceeded native templates in AlphaFold 3 ipTM, and unconstrained generation retained evidence of target-conditioned signal.

</details>


### [317] [NI Sampling: Accelerating Discrete Diffusion Sampling by Token Order Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18471)
*Enshu Liu,Xuefei Ning,Yu Wang,Zinan Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NI Sampling is a token sampling order optimization framework for discrete diffusion language models that uses a neural indicator to select which tokens to sample at each step, achieving up to 14.3× acceleration without significant performance loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing heuristic sampling strategies for discrete diffusion language models are inefficient because they only select a small portion of tokens to sample at each step, leaving substantial room for improvement in sampling efficiency and acceleration.

Method: Proposes Neural Indicator Sampling (NI Sampling), a general sampling order optimization framework that uses a neural indicator to decide which tokens should be sampled at each step. Also introduces a novel trajectory-preserving objective to train the indicator.

Result: Experiments on LLaDA and Dream models across multiple benchmarks show up to 14.3× acceleration over full-step sampling with negligible performance drop, and consistent outperformance of confidence threshold sampling in accuracy-step trade-off.

Conclusion: Token sampling order optimization has significant potential for accelerating discrete diffusion language models, and NI Sampling provides an effective framework that dramatically reduces sampling iterations while maintaining accuracy.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion language models (dLLMs) have recently emerged as a promising alternative to traditional autoregressive approaches, offering the flexibility to generate tokens in arbitrary orders and the potential of parallel decoding. However, existing heuristic sampling strategies remain inefficient: they choose only a small part of tokens to sample at each step, leaving substantial room for improvement. In this work, we study the problem of token sampling order optimization and demonstrate its significant potential for acceleration. Specifically, we find that fully leveraging correct predictions at each step can reduce the number of sampling iterations by an order of magnitude without compromising accuracy. Based on this, we propose Neural Indicator Sampling (NI Sampling), a general sampling order optimization framework that utilize a neural indicator to decide which tokens should be sampled at each step. We further propose a novel trajectory-preserving objective to train the indicator. Experiments on LLaDA and Dream models across multiple benchmarks show that our method achieves up to 14.3$\times$ acceleration over full-step sampling with negligible performance drop, and consistently outperforms confidence threshold sampling in the accuracy-step trade-off. Code is available at https://github.com/imagination-research/NI-Sampling.

</details>


### [318] [Train Separately, Merge Together: Modular Post-Training with Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18473)
*Jacob Morrison,Sanjay Adhikesaven,Akshita Bhagia,Matei Zaharia,Noah A. Smith,Sewon Min*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BAR introduces a modular training approach that trains independent domain experts separately and composes them via Mixture-of-Experts, enabling efficient domain extension without degrading existing capabilities or requiring costly retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current language model training approaches face fundamental limitations: retraining from scratch is expensive and scales poorly, while continued training often degrades existing capabilities. There's a need for scalable methods to extend models with new domain capabilities without these drawbacks.

Method: BAR (Branch-Adapt-Route) trains independent domain experts through separate mid-training, supervised finetuning, and reinforcement learning pipelines, then composes them using a Mixture-of-Experts architecture with lightweight router training. This enables updating individual experts independently rather than retraining the entire model.

Result: At 7B scale with experts for math, code, tool use, and safety, BAR achieves an overall score of 49.1 across 7 evaluation categories, matching or exceeding retraining baselines (47.8-50.5). It demonstrates linear cost scaling for updates vs quadratic scaling for retraining, and avoids catastrophic forgetting that occurs in monolithic training.

Conclusion: Modular, decoupled expert-based training with BAR provides a scalable alternative to monolithic retraining for extending language models, offering efficient domain updates without degradation of existing capabilities and significant reductions in cost and complexity.

Abstract: Extending a fully post-trained language model with new domain capabilities is fundamentally limited by monolithic training paradigms: retraining from scratch is expensive and scales poorly, while continued training often degrades existing capabilities. We present BAR (Branch-Adapt-Route), which trains independent domain experts, each through its own mid-training, supervised finetuning, and reinforcement learning pipeline, and composes them via a Mixture-of-Experts architecture with lightweight router training. Unlike retraining approaches that mix all domains and require full reprocessing for any update (with cost scaling quadratically), BAR enables updating individual experts independently with linear cost scaling and no degradation to existing domains. At the 7B scale, with experts for math, code, tool use, and safety, BAR achieves an overall score of 49.1 (averaged across 7 evaluation categories), matching or exceeding re-training baselines (47.8 without mid-training, 50.5 with). We further show that modular training provides a structural advantage: by isolating each domain, it avoids the catastrophic forgetting that occurs when late-stage RL degrades capabilities from earlier training stages, while significantly reducing the cost and complexity of updating or adding a domain. Together, these results suggest that decoupled, expert-based training is a scalable alternative to monolithic retraining for extending language models.

</details>


### [319] [Multi-Scale Reversible Chaos Game Representation: A Unified Framework for Sequence Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18477)
*Sarwan Ali,Taslim Murad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MS-RCGR is a novel multi-scale reversible encoding framework that transforms biological sequences into geometric representations, enabling three analytical paradigms (traditional ML, computer vision, and hybrid approaches) and achieving superior classification performance with interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Biological classification with interpretability remains challenging. Existing sequence encoding methods often lose information or lack interpretability, creating a need for encoding methods that preserve complete sequence information while enabling diverse analytical approaches.

Method: The MS-RCGR framework transforms biological sequences into multi-resolution geometric representations using rational arithmetic and hierarchical k-mer decomposition. It bridges three analytical paradigms: 1) traditional ML using geometric features, 2) computer vision on CGR images, and 3) hybrid approaches combining protein language model embeddings with CGR features.

Result: MS-RCGR consistently enhances classification performance across all paradigms in experiments on synthetic DNA and protein datasets. The hybrid approach combining pre-trained language model embeddings (ESM2, ProtT5) with MS-RCGR features achieves superior performance compared to either method alone.

Conclusion: MS-RCGR provides a flexible, interpretable, and high-performing foundation for biological sequence analysis with guaranteed reversibility (no information loss) and multi-scale pattern capture from nucleotides to complex motifs.

Abstract: Biological classification with interpretability remains a challenging task. For this, we introduce a novel encoding framework, Multi-Scale Reversible Chaos Game Representation (MS-RCGR), that transforms biological sequences into multi-resolution geometric representations with guaranteed reversibility. Unlike traditional sequence encoding methods, MS-RCGR employs rational arithmetic and hierarchical k-mer decomposition to generate scale-invariant features that preserve complete sequence information while enabling diverse analytical approaches. Our framework bridges three distinct paradigms for sequence analysis: (1) traditional machine learning using extracted geometric features, (2) computer vision models operating on CGR-generated images, and (3) hybrid approaches combining protein language model embeddings with CGR features. Through comprehensive experiments on synthetic DNA and protein datasets encompassing seven distinct sequence classes, we demonstrate that MS-RCGR features consistently enhance classification performance across all paradigms. Notably, our hybrid approach combining pre-trained language model embeddings (ESM2, ProtT5) with MS-RCGR features achieves superior performance compared to either method alone. The reversibility property of our encoding ensures no information loss during transformation, while multi-scale analysis captures patterns ranging from individual nucleotides to complex motif structures. Our results indicate that MS-RCGR provides a flexible, interpretable, and high-performing foundation for biological sequence analysis.

</details>


### [320] [Faster by Design: Interactive Aerodynamics via Neural Surrogates Trained on Expert-Validated CFD](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18491)
*Nicholas Thumiger,Andrea Bartezzaghi,Mattia Rigotti,Cezary Skura,Thomas Frick,Elisa Serioli,Fabrizio Arbucci,A. Cristiano I. Malossi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GIST is a graph-based neural operator that achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on complex race-car geometries, enabling interactive design-space exploration for aerodynamic development at industrial scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CFD simulations for race-car aerodynamics are prohibitively expensive (tens of thousands of core-hours), limiting design space exploration. Existing AI surrogate models are constrained by simplistic public datasets that don't capture the complex geometries of race cars.

Method: Three key contributions: 1) A high-fidelity RANS dataset from parametric LMP2-class CAD model spanning six operating conditions, validated by Dallara experts; 2) Gauge-Invariant Spectral Transformer (GIST), a graph-based neural operator with spectral embeddings that encode mesh connectivity; 3) GIST guarantees discretization invariance and scales linearly with mesh size.

Result: GIST achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on both public benchmarks and the proposed race-car dataset. It demonstrates predictive accuracy suitable for early-stage aerodynamic design, enabling interactive design-space exploration where engineers query the surrogate instead of CFD solvers.

Conclusion: The work successfully validates the concept of interactive design-space exploration in industrial motorsport workflows, providing a scalable solution to overcome the computational bottleneck of traditional CFD while maintaining accuracy on complex race-car geometries.

Abstract: Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) is central to race-car aerodynamic development, yet its cost -- tens of thousands of core-hours per high-fidelity evaluation -- severely limits the design space exploration feasible within realistic budgets. AI-based surrogate models promise to alleviate this bottleneck, but progress has been constrained by the limited complexity of public datasets, which are dominated by smoothed passenger-car shapes that fail to exercise surrogates on the thin, complex, highly loaded components governing motorsport performance. This work presents three primary contributions. First, we introduce a high-fidelity RANS dataset built on a parametric LMP2-class CAD model and spanning six operating conditions (map points) covering straight-line and cornering regimes, generated and validated by aerodynamics experts at Dallara to preserve features relevant to industrial motorsport. Second, we present the Gauge-Invariant Spectral Transformer (GIST), a graph-based neural operator whose spectral embeddings encode mesh connectivity to enhance predictions on tightly packed, complex geometries. GIST guarantees discretization invariance and scales linearly with mesh size, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on both public benchmarks and the proposed race-car dataset. Third, we demonstrate that GIST achieves a level of predictive accuracy suitable for early-stage aerodynamic design, providing a first validation of the concept of interactive design-space exploration -- where engineers query a surrogate in place of the CFD solver -- within industrial motorsport workflows.

</details>


### [321] [Barrier-enforced multi-objective optimization for direct point and sharp interval forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18492)
*Worachit Amnuaypongsa,Yotsapat Suparanonrat,Pana Wanitchollakit,Jitkomut Songsiri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multi-step probabilistic forecasting framework using a single neural network that simultaneously generates point forecasts and non-crossing prediction intervals with guaranteed coverage probability through adaptive multi-objective optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for combined point and prediction interval forecasting often rely on manual weight tuning in scalarized loss functions, which is suboptimal. There's a need for a unified framework that can automatically balance multiple objectives while guaranteeing non-crossing prediction intervals with target coverage probability.

Method: Proposes a multi-gradient descent approach to treat point and PI forecasting as a multi-objective optimization problem. Uses a novel PI loss function based on extended log-barrier with adaptive hyperparameter to guarantee coverage. Features a hybrid architecture with shared temporal model and horizon-specific submodels, along with a specialized training strategy.

Result: The framework consistently outperforms current literature methods by achieving target coverage with the narrowest prediction interval widths in intra-day solar irradiance forecasting. It remains highly competitive against LSTM encoder-decoder and Transformer architectures, including those with Chronos foundation models.

Conclusion: The proposed multi-step probabilistic forecasting framework provides an effective solution for simultaneous point and interval forecasting, eliminating trial-and-error hyperparameter tuning while guaranteeing non-crossing PIs with target coverage. The approach is scale-independent, universally applicable, and can be adapted to any deep learning structure.

Abstract: This paper proposes a multi-step probabilistic forecasting framework using a single neural-network based model to generate simultaneous point and interval forecasts. Our approach ensures non-crossing prediction intervals (PIs) through a model structure design that strictly satisfy a target coverage probability (PICP) while maximizing sharpness. Unlike existing methods that rely on manual weight tuning for scalarized loss functions, we treat point and PI forecasting as a multi-objective optimization problem, utilizing multi-gradient descent to adaptively select optimal weights. Key innovations include a new PI loss function based on an extended log-barrier with an adaptive hyperparameter to guarantee the coverage, a hybrid architecture featuring a shared temporal model with horizon-specific submodels, and a training strategy. The proposed loss is scale-independent and universally applicable; combined with our training algorithm, the framework eliminates trial-and-error hyperparameter tuning for balancing multiple objectives. Validated by an intra-day solar irradiance forecasting application, results demonstrate that our proposed loss consistently outperforms those in current literature by achieving target coverage with the narrowest PI widths. Furthermore, when compared against LSTM encoder-decoder and Transformer architectures--including those augmented with Chronos foundation models--our method remains highly competitive and can be seamlessly adapted to any deep learning structure.

</details>


### [322] [Too Correct to Learn: Reinforcement Learning on Saturated Reasoning Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18493)
*Zhenwen Liang,Yujun Zhou,Sidi Lu,Xiangliang Zhang,Haitao Mi,Dong Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CUTS prevents RL reasoning degradation in strong LLMs by enforcing diversity-preserving exploration to combat mode collapse when benchmark saturation eliminates advantage signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Strong LLMs saturate standard reasoning benchmarks like MATH, producing correct but homogeneous solutions. This causes advantage signals in group-relative RL algorithms (e.g., GRPO) to vanish, leading to mode collapse and policy degeneration despite high initial performance.

Method: Proposes Constrained Uniform Top-K Sampling (CUTS), a parameter-free decoding strategy that samples uniformly from constrained high-confidence candidates to flatten local optimization landscapes. Also introduces Mixed-CUTS, a training framework that synergizes exploitative and exploratory rollouts to amplify intra-group advantage variance while maintaining solution diversity.

Result: Experiments with Qwen3 models show CUTS prevents policy degeneration and significantly improves out-of-domain generalization. Mixed-CUTS boosts Pass@1 accuracy on the challenging AIME25 benchmark by up to 15.1% over standard GRPO, demonstrating that maintaining diversity within semantic manifolds is critical for rigorous reasoning.

Conclusion: Preserving solution diversity through structured exploration (CUTS) is essential for continued RL-based reasoning improvement in strong LLMs, as homogeneous success in saturated benchmarks undermines advantage-based learning and generalization capabilities.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) enhances LLM reasoning, yet a paradox emerges as models scale: strong base models saturate standard benchmarks (e.g., MATH), yielding correct but homogeneous solutions. In such environments, the lack of failure cases causes the advantage signal in group-relative algorithms (e.g., GRPO) to vanish, driving policies into mode collapse. To address this, we propose Constrained Uniform Top-K Sampling (CUTS), a parameter-free decoding strategy enforcing structure-preserving exploration. Unlike standard sampling that follows model biases, CUTS flattens the local optimization landscape by sampling uniformly from constrained high-confidence candidates. We integrate this into Mixed-CUTS, a training framework synergizing exploitative and exploratory rollouts to amplify intra-group advantage variance. Experiments on Qwen3 models demonstrate that our approach prevents policy degeneration and significantly boosts out-of-domain generalization. Notably, Mixed-CUTS improves Pass@1 accuracy on the challenging AIME25 benchmark by up to 15.1% over standard GRPO, validating that maintaining diversity within the semantic manifold is critical for rigorous reasoning.

</details>


### [323] [IDOBE: Infectious Disease Outbreak forecasting Benchmark Ecosystem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18521)
*Aniruddha Adiga,Jingyuan Chou,Anshul Chiranth,Bryan Lewis,Ana I. Bento,Shaun Truelove,Geoffrey Fox,Madhav Marathe,Harry Hochheiser,Srini Venkatramanan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IDOBE is a curated epidemiological dataset for outbreak forecasting with over 10,000 outbreaks across 13 diseases, featuring baselines and standardized benchmarks for forecasting method evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a lack of standardized benchmark datasets for evaluating collaborative ensemble forecasting methods in infectious disease outbreaks, and limited understanding of how these methods perform for novel outbreaks with minimal historical data.

Method: Created IDOBE dataset by compiling epidemiological time series from multiple repositories spanning over a century, performing derivative-based segmentation to generate outbreaks, using information-theoretic measures to quantify diversity, and evaluating 11 baseline models with multi-horizon short-term forecasting.

Result: MLP-based methods showed the most robust performance overall, while statistical methods performed slightly better during the pre-peak phase. The dataset includes evaluation using NMSE, MAPE, and probabilistic scoring rules like NWIS.

Conclusion: IDOBE provides a standardized, reproducible benchmark for outbreak forecasting methods, addressing the gap in standardized datasets and enabling better evaluation of forecasting performance across different disease outbreaks.

Abstract: Epidemic forecasting has become an integral part of real-time infectious disease outbreak response. While collaborative ensembles composed of statistical and machine learning models have become the norm for real-time forecasting, standardized benchmark datasets for evaluating such methods are lacking. Further, there is limited understanding on performance of these methods for novel outbreaks with limited historical data. In this paper, we propose IDOBE, a curated collection of epidemiological time series focused on outbreak forecasting. IDOBE compiles from multiple data repositories spanning over a century of surveillance and across U.S. states and global locations. We perform derivative-based segmentation to generate over 10,000 outbreaks covering multiple outcomes such as cases and hospitalizations for 13 diseases. We consider a variety of information-theoretic and distributional measures to quantify the epidemiological diversity of the dataset. Finally, we perform multi-horizon short-term forecasting (1- to 4-week-ahead) through the progression of the outbreak using 11 baseline models and report on their performance. In addition to standard metrics such as NMSE and MAPE for point forecasts, we include probabilistic scoring rules such as Normalized Weighted Interval Score (NWIS) to quantify the performance. We find that MLP-based methods have the most robust performance, with statistical methods having a slight edge during the pre-peak phase. IDOBE dataset along with baselines are released publicly on https://github.com/NSSAC/IDOBE to enable standardized, reproducible benchmarking of outbreak forecasting methods.

</details>


### [324] [Wasserstein Distributionally Robust Risk-Sensitive Estimation via Conditional Value-at-Risk](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18546)
*Feras Al Taha,Eilyan Bitar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Distributionally robust risk-sensitive estimation using conditional value-at-risk (CVaR) of squared error within Wasserstein ambiguity sets, with tractable semidefinite programming solution for finite nominal distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for robust signal estimation when joint probability distribution is unknown but belongs to an ambiguity set, with performance measured by risk-sensitive CVaR metric rather than just expected error.

Method: Propose distributionally robust approach where estimator minimizes worst-case CVaR over Wasserstein ambiguity sets; show affine estimators can be computed via tractable semidefinite program when nominal distribution is finitely supported.

Result: Demonstrate tractability via semidefinite programming; show on real electricity price forecasting data that proposed estimators achieve lower out-of-sample CVaR of squared error compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: Distributionally robust risk-sensitive estimation with CVaR metric and Wasserstein ambiguity sets provides practical, computationally tractable solution that outperforms existing methods in terms of out-of-sample risk performance.

Abstract: We propose a distributionally robust approach to risk-sensitive estimation of an unknown signal x from an observed signal y. The unknown signal and observation are modeled as random vectors whose joint probability distribution is unknown, but assumed to belong to a given type-2 Wasserstein ball of distributions, termed the ambiguity set. The performance of an estimator is measured according to the conditional value-at-risk (CVaR) of the squared estimation error. Within this framework, we study the problem of computing affine estimators that minimize the worst-case CVaR over all distributions in the given ambiguity set. As our main result, we show that, when the nominal distribution at the center of the Wasserstein ball is finitely supported, such estimators can be exactly computed by solving a tractable semidefinite program. We evaluate the proposed estimators on a wholesale electricity price forecasting task using real market data and show that they deliver lower out-of-sample CVaR of squared error compared to existing methods.

</details>


### [325] [Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Biological $2\mathrm{D}{+}t$ Reaction-Diffusion Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18548)
*William Lavery,Jodie A. Cochrane,Christian Olesen,Dagim S. Tadele,John T. Nardini,Sara Hamis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper extends Biologically-Informed Neural Networks (BINNs) from 1D+t to 2D+t systems for learning governing equations from experimental data, with application to lung cancer cell dynamics from time-lapse microscopy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing BINN studies are limited to 1D+t reaction-diffusion systems and focus on forward prediction rather than explicit equation identification. There's a need to extend BINNs to higher-dimensional systems and develop a complete framework for closed-form equation discovery from real-world experimental data.

Method: Develops a framework combining: (1) data preprocessing, (2) BINN-based equation learning that preserves known differential operator structure (reaction-diffusion) while learning constitutive terms via trainable neural subnetworks, and (3) symbolic regression post-processing for closed-form equation discovery within a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) framework.

Result: Successfully learned governing equations of lung cancer cell population dynamics from time-lapse microscopy data, recovering 2D+t reaction-diffusion models from experimental observations. The framework demonstrates real-world applicability for spatio-temporal systems.

Conclusion: The proposed framework extends BINNs to 2D+t systems and provides a practical, interpretable tool for fast analytic equation discovery from data, readily applicable to other spatio-temporal systems beyond the demonstrated cancer cell dynamics application.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) provide a powerful framework for learning governing equations of dynamical systems from data. Biologically-informed neural networks (BINNs) are a variant of PINNs that preserve the known differential operator structure (e.g., reaction-diffusion) while learning constitutive terms via trainable neural subnetworks, enforced through soft residual penalties. Existing BINN studies are limited to $1\mathrm{D}{+}t$ reaction-diffusion systems and focus on forward prediction, using the governing partial differential equation as a regulariser rather than an explicit identification target. Here, we extend BINNs to $2\mathrm{D}{+}t$ systems within a PINN framework that combines data preprocessing, BINN-based equation learning, and symbolic regression post-processing for closed-form equation discovery. We demonstrate the framework's real-world applicability by learning the governing equations of lung cancer cell population dynamics from time-lapse microscopy data, recovering $2\mathrm{D}{+}t$ reaction-diffusion models from experimental observations. The proposed framework is readily applicable to other spatio-temporal systems, providing a practical and interpretable tool for fast analytic equation discovery from data.

</details>


### [326] [A Note on TurboQuant and the Earlier DRIVE/EDEN Line of Work](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18555)
*Ran Ben-Basat,Yaniv Ben-Itzhak,Gal Mendelson,Michael Mitzenmacher,Amit Portnoy,Shay Vargaftik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper clarifies the relationship between TurboQuant and earlier EDEN quantization schemes, showing that both TurboQuant variants are suboptimal special cases of EDEN and that EDEN consistently outperforms TurboQuant in accuracy experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to clarify the relationship between the recent TurboQuant quantization method and earlier established quantization schemes (DRIVE and EDEN), highlighting that TurboQuant is actually a suboptimal special case of the more general EDEN framework.

Method: The method involves comparative theoretical analysis of quantization schemes: (1) showing TurboQuant_mse is EDEN with fixed suboptimal scale parameter S=1, (2) analyzing TurboQuant_prod's suboptimal combination of biased (b-1)-bit EDEN with 1-bit residual quantization, and (3) noting shared analytical tools like Beta distribution connections and Lloyd-Max algorithm.

Result: Experiments confirm that biased EDEN (with optimized S) outperforms TurboQuant_mse, and unbiased EDEN is markedly more accurate than TurboQuant_prod, sometimes by more than a bit (e.g., 2-bit EDEN beats 3-bit TurboQuant_prod). EDEN consistently outperforms TurboQuant in all experimental setups tested.

Conclusion: TurboQuant is essentially a suboptimal special case of the more general and superior EDEN quantization framework, and researchers should use EDEN instead of TurboQuant for better performance.

Abstract: This note clarifies the relationship between the recent TurboQuant work and the earlier DRIVE (NeurIPS 2021) and EDEN (ICML 2022) schemes. DRIVE is a 1-bit quantizer that EDEN extended to any $b>0$ bits per coordinate; we refer to them collectively as EDEN.
  First, TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$ is a special case of EDEN obtained by fixing EDEN's scalar scale parameter to $S=1$. EDEN supports both biased and unbiased quantization, each optimized by a different $S$ (chosen via methods described in the EDEN works). The fixed choice $S=1$ used by TurboQuant is generally suboptimal, although the optimal $S$ for biased EDEN converges to $1$ as the dimension grows; accordingly TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$ approaches EDEN's behavior for large $d$.
  Second, TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$ combines a biased $(b-1)$-bit EDEN step with an unbiased 1-bit QJL quantization of the residual. It is suboptimal in three ways: (1) its $(b-1)$-bit step uses the suboptimal $S=1$; (2) its 1-bit unbiased residual quantization has worse MSE than (unbiased) 1-bit EDEN; (3) chaining a biased $(b-1)$-bit step with a 1-bit unbiased residual step is inferior to unbiasedly quantizing the input directly with $b$-bit EDEN.
  Third, some of the analysis in the TurboQuant work mirrors that of the EDEN works: both exploit the connection between random rotations and the shifted Beta distribution, use the Lloyd-Max algorithm, and note that Randomized Hadamard Transforms can replace uniform random rotations.
  Experiments support these claims: biased EDEN (with optimized $S$) is more accurate than TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$, and unbiased EDEN is markedly more accurate than TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$, often by more than a bit (e.g., 2-bit EDEN beats 3-bit TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$). We also repeat all accuracy experiments from the TurboQuant paper, showing that EDEN outperforms it in every setup we have tried.

</details>


### [327] [Latent Phase-Shift Rollback: Inference-Time Error Correction via Residual Stream Monitoring and KV-Cache Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18567)
*Manan Gupta,Dhruv Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LPSR detects and corrects reasoning errors in LLMs at inference time by monitoring layer activations, rolling back KV-cache when phase shifts are detected, and injecting steering vectors, achieving 44% accuracy on MATH-500 with an 8B model without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models often make unrecoverable reasoning errors during generation where a wrong step leads to compounding mistakes rather than self-correction. Current inference-time methods like prompted self-correction actually perform worse than standard autoregressive generation on math tasks.

Method: Latent Phase-Shift Rollback (LPSR) monitors the residual stream at a critical layer, detects abrupt directional reversals (phase shifts) using cosine similarity + entropy dual gate, rolls back the KV-cache when detected, and injects a pre-computed steering vector. No fine-tuning, gradients, or extra forward passes needed.

Result: LPSR achieves 44.0% accuracy on MATH-500 with an 8B model vs 28.8% for standard AR (+15.2 pp). It outperforms prompted self-correction (19.8%) by +24.2 pp and Best-of-16 by +7.8 pp at 5.4× lower token cost. Surpasses a 70B model (35.2%) with 8.75× fewer parameters. Layer analysis reveals detection-correction dissociation with error-detection AUC peaking at layer 14 but accuracy peaking at layer 16.

Conclusion: LPSR demonstrates effective inference-time error correction through latent space monitoring, showing that optimal monitoring depth differs between detection and correction tasks, and provides substantial performance gains on mathematical reasoning without training or significant computational overhead.

Abstract: Large language models frequently commit unrecoverable reasoning errors mid-generation: once a wrong step is taken, subsequent tokens compound the mistake rather than correct it. We introduce $\textbf{Latent Phase-Shift Rollback}$ (LPSR): at each generation step, we monitor the residual stream at a critical layer lcrit, detect abrupt directional reversals (phase shifts) via a cosine-similarity $+$ entropy dual gate, and respond by rolling back the KV-cache and injecting a pre-computed steering vector. No fine-tuning, gradient computation, or additional forward passes are required. LPSR achieves $\mathbf{44.0\%}$ on MATH-500 with an 8B model versus $28.8\%$ for standard AR ($+15.2$ pp; McNemar $χ^2 = 66.96$, $p < 10^{-15}$). Critically, prompted self-correction, the most natural inference-time baseline, scores only $19.8\%$, below standard AR; LPSR exceeds it by $+24.2$ pp ($χ^2 = 89.4$, $p \approx 0$). LPSR also outperforms Best-of-16 ($+7.8$ pp) at $5.4\times$ lower token cost, and surpasses a standard 70B model ($35.2\%$) with $8.75\times$ fewer parameters at ${\sim}3\times$ the token budget. A 32-layer sweep reveals a novel \textbf{detection-correction dissociation}: error-detection AUC peaks at layer~14 ($0.718$) but task accuracy peaks at layer~16 ($44.0\%$ vs.\ $29.2\%$), demonstrating that optimal monitoring depth differs for detection and correction.

</details>


### [328] [A multimodal and temporal foundation model for virtual patient representations at healthcare system scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18570)
*Andrew Zhang,Tong Ding,Sophia J. Wagner,Caiwei Tian,Ming Y. Lu,Rowland Pettit,Joshua E. Lewis,Alexandre Misrahi,Dandan Mo,Long Phi Le,Faisal Mahmood*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Apollo is a multimodal temporal foundation model that integrates 30+ years of hospital records to create unified patient representations, enabling disease prediction up to 5 years ahead and multimodal medical search capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern medicine generates vast multimodal data across siloed systems, but no existing model integrates the full breadth and temporal depth of clinical records into unified patient representations for comprehensive analysis.

Method: Developed Apollo, trained on 25 billion records from 7.2 million patients over 3 decades, integrating 28 medical modalities. Creates "atlas of medical concepts" that compresses structured/unstructured events into virtual patient representations.

Result: Tested on 322 prognosis/retrieval tasks with 1.4M patients: successfully predicted new disease onset (5 years ahead), disease progression, treatment response, adverse events, and hospital operations. Also enabled multimodal medical search with text/image queries.

Conclusion: Apollo establishes foundation for computable medicine by making full patient care context accessible to computational reasoning through unified patient representations and multimodal temporal modeling capabilities.

Abstract: Modern medicine generates vast multimodal data across siloed systems, yet no existing model integrates the full breadth and temporal depth of the clinical record into a unified patient representation. We introduce Apollo, a multimodal temporal foundation model trained and evaluated on over three decades of longitudinal hospital records from a major US hospital system, composed of 25 billion records from 7.2 million patients, representing 28 distinct medical modalities and 12 major medical specialties. Apollo learns a unified representation space integrating over 100 thousand unique medical events in our clinical vocabulary as well as images and clinical text. This "atlas of medical concepts" forms a computational substrate for modeling entire patient care journeys comprised of sequences of structured and unstructured events, which are compressed by Apollo into virtual patient representations. To assess the potential of these whole-patient representations, we created 322 prognosis and retrieval tasks from a held-out test set of 1.4 million patients. We demonstrate the generalized clinical forecasting potential of Apollo embeddings, including predicting new disease onset risk up to five years in advance (95 tasks), disease progression (78 tasks), treatment response (59 tasks), risk of treatment-related adverse events (17 tasks), and hospital operations endpoints (12 tasks). Using feature attribution techniques, we show that model predictions align with clinically-interpretable multimodal biomarkers. We evaluate semantic similarity search on 61 retrieval tasks, and moreover demonstrate the potential of Apollo as a multimodal medical search engine using text and image queries. Together, these modeling capabilities establish the foundation for computable medicine, where the full context of patient care becomes accessible to computational reasoning.

</details>


### [329] [When Can LLMs Learn to Reason with Weak Supervision?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18574)
*Salman Rahman,Jingyan Shen,Anna Mordvina,Hamid Palangi,Saadia Gabriel,Pavel Izmailov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLVR can succeed under weak supervision (scarce data, noisy rewards, proxy rewards) when models exhibit prolonged pre-saturation training dynamics. Reasoning faithfulness predicts generalization success, not output diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM capabilities grow, constructing high-quality reward signals for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) becomes increasingly difficult. There's a need to understand when RLVR can succeed under weaker forms of supervision.

Method: Systematic empirical study across diverse model families and reasoning domains under three weak supervision settings: scarce data, noisy rewards, and self-supervised proxy rewards. Analysis of training reward saturation dynamics and reasoning faithfulness. Examination of continual pre-training vs supervised fine-tuning effects.

Result: Generalization is governed by training reward saturation dynamics: models that generalize exhibit prolonged pre-saturation phase where training reward and downstream performance climb together. Reasoning faithfulness (how well intermediate steps logically support final answer) predicts which regime a model falls into. SFT on explicit reasoning traces is necessary for generalization under weak supervision, while continual pre-training amplifies the effect. These interventions enabled Llama3.2-3B-Base to generalize across all three weak supervision settings.

Conclusion: RLVR can work under weak supervision when models have good reasoning faithfulness and appropriate training dynamics. The key is ensuring models don't rapidly saturate and memorize. Combining SFT on reasoning traces with continual pre-training enables generalization even with scarce, noisy, or proxy rewards.

Abstract: Large language models have achieved significant reasoning improvements through reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). Yet as model capabilities grow, constructing high-quality reward signals becomes increasingly difficult, making it essential to understand when RLVR can succeed under weaker forms of supervision. We conduct a systematic empirical study across diverse model families and reasoning domains under three weak supervision settings: scarce data, noisy rewards, and self-supervised proxy rewards. We find that generalization is governed by training reward saturation dynamics: models that generalize exhibit a prolonged pre-saturation phase during which training reward and downstream performance climb together, while models that saturate rapidly memorize rather than learn. We identify reasoning faithfulness, defined as the extent to which intermediate steps logically support the final answer, as the pre-RL property that predicts which regime a model falls into, while output diversity alone is uninformative. Motivated by these findings, we disentangle the contributions of continual pre-training and supervised fine-tuning, finding that SFT on explicit reasoning traces is necessary for generalization under weak supervision, while continual pre-training on domain data amplifies the effect. Applied together to Llama3.2-3B-Base, these interventions enable generalization across all three settings where the base model previously failed.

</details>


### [330] [Bounded Ratio Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18578)
*Yunke Ao,Le Chen,Bruce D. Lee,Assefa S. Wahd,Aline Czarnobai,Philipp Fürnstahl,Bernhard Schölkopf,Andreas Krause*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BRRL framework bridges theoretical foundations of trust region methods with PPO's practical approach, introducing Bounded Policy Optimization (BPO) with proven monotonic improvement guarantees, and extending to Group-relative BPO (GBPO) for LLM fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a significant disconnect between the theoretical foundations of trust region methods and the heuristic clipped objective used in PPO, creating a need for a more principled approach that maintains PPO's empirical robustness while having stronger theoretical guarantees.

Method: Introduces the Bounded Ratio Reinforcement Learning (BRRL) framework with a novel regularized and constrained policy optimization problem, derives its analytical optimal solution, and develops Bounded Policy Optimization (BPO) algorithm that minimizes advantage-weighted divergence between the policy and the analytic optimal solution.

Result: BPO ensures monotonic performance improvement, provides theoretical interpretation of PPO's success, connects trust region methods with Cross-Entropy Method, and demonstrates empirical performance matching or outperforming PPO across MuJoCo, Atari, IsaacLab environments and LLM fine-tuning tasks.

Conclusion: The BRRL framework successfully bridges theory and practice in policy optimization, providing BPO with strong theoretical guarantees while maintaining or exceeding PPO's empirical performance, with extensions like GBPO showing promise for LLM fine-tuning applications.

Abstract: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) has become the predominant algorithm for on-policy reinforcement learning due to its scalability and empirical robustness across domains. However, there is a significant disconnect between the underlying foundations of trust region methods and the heuristic clipped objective used in PPO. In this paper, we bridge this gap by introducing the Bounded Ratio Reinforcement Learning (BRRL) framework. We formulate a novel regularized and constrained policy optimization problem and derive its analytical optimal solution. We prove that this solution ensures monotonic performance improvement. To handle parameterized policy classes, we develop a policy optimization algorithm called Bounded Policy Optimization (BPO) that minimizes an advantage-weighted divergence between the policy and the analytic optimal solution from BRRL. We further establish a lower bound on the expected performance of the resulting policy in terms of the BPO loss function. Notably, our framework also provides a new theoretical lens to interpret the success of the PPO loss, and connects trust region policy optimization and the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM). We additionally extend BPO to Group-relative BPO (GBPO) for LLM fine-tuning. Empirical evaluations of BPO across MuJoCo, Atari, and complex IsaacLab environments (e.g., Humanoid locomotion), and of GBPO for LLM fine-tuning tasks, demonstrate that BPO and GBPO generally match or outperform PPO and GRPO in stability and final performance.

</details>


### [331] [Sessa: Selective State Space Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18580)
*Liubomyr Horbatko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sessa introduces a new decoder architecture that combines attention with feedback paths to achieve power-law memory tails and selective retrieval capabilities, outperforming Transformers and Mamba on long-context tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing sequence models have limitations: Transformers dilute token influence with O(1/ℓ) scaling, while structured state-space models like Mamba suffer exponential decay when freeze time isn't sustained. Both architectures either retrieve in a single read or use a single feedback chain, limiting their long-range sensitivity and selective retrieval capabilities.

Method: Sessa places attention inside a feedback path, enabling recurrent many-path aggregation within a single layer. This architecture allows for power-law memory decay (O(ℓ^{-β})) rather than the faster 1/ℓ decay of Transformers or exponential decay of state-space models.

Result: Under matched architectures and training budgets, Sessa achieves the strongest performance on long-context benchmarks while remaining competitive with Transformers and Mamba on short-context language modeling. It enables flexible selective retrieval including non-decaying profiles that other models can't achieve.

Conclusion: Sessa's novel architecture combining attention with feedback paths provides superior long-range memory capabilities with power-law decay, addressing key limitations of both Transformer and state-space approaches while maintaining competitive short-context performance.

Abstract: Modern sequence models are dominated by Transformers, where self-attention mixes information from the visible context in an input-dependent way. However, when retrieval is not sharp and attention remains diffuse over an effective support $S_{\mathrm{eff}}(t)$, the influence of any individual token is diluted, typically scaling as $O(1/S_{\mathrm{eff}}(t))$ and reaching $O(1/\ell)$ for old tokens in full-prefix settings. Structured state-space models process sequences recurrently through an explicit feedback path; selective variants such as Mamba make this feedback input-dependent, yet when freeze time cannot be sustained over long intervals, their long-range sensitivity decays exponentially with lag. Existing architectures therefore either retrieve from the past in a single read or propagate information through a single feedback chain. We introduce Sessa, a decoder that places attention inside a feedback path, enabling recurrent many-path aggregation within a layer. Under stated assumptions, Sessa admits regimes with a power-law memory tail in lag $\ell$ of order $O(\ell^{-β})$ for $0<β<1$, which is asymptotically slower than $1/\ell$; moreover, this rate is tight in an explicit diffuse uniform-routing setting where the influence is $Θ(\ell^{-β})$. Under the same conditions, only Sessa among the compared model classes realizes flexible selective retrieval, including non-decaying profiles. Empirically, under matched architectures and training budgets, Sessa achieves the strongest performance on our long-context benchmarks while remaining competitive with Transformer and Mamba style baselines on short-context language modeling.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [332] [Conjunctive Prompt Attacks in Multi-Agent LLM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16543)
*Nokimul Hasan Arif,Qian Lou,Mengxin Zheng*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Conjunctive prompt attacks exploit LLM agent systems where benign triggers and templates become harmful when routing brings them together, bypassing existing defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM safety research focuses on single-agent models, but real applications use multiple interacting agents. The routing between agents creates attack surfaces that single-agent evaluations miss.

Method: The paper studies conjunctive prompt attacks where a trigger key in user query and a hidden adversarial template in a compromised remote agent appear benign alone but activate harmful behavior when combined via routing. The attacker controls only trigger placement and template insertion without changing model weights or client agent. The study examines star, chain, and DAG topologies with routing-aware optimization to increase attack success while keeping false activations low.

Result: Routing-aware optimization substantially increases attack success over non-optimized baselines across different topologies while maintaining low false activations. Existing defenses like PromptGuard, Llama-Guard variants, and system-level controls such as tool restrictions do not reliably stop the attack because no single component appears malicious in isolation.

Conclusion: The results expose a structural vulnerability in agentic LLM pipelines and motivate the need for defenses that reason over routing and cross-agent composition, rather than just individual components.

Abstract: Most LLM safety work studies single-agent models, but many real applications rely on multiple interacting agents. In these systems, prompt segmentation and inter-agent routing create attack surfaces that single-agent evaluations miss. We study \emph{conjunctive prompt attacks}, where a trigger key in the user query and a hidden adversarial template in one compromised remote agent each appear benign alone but activate harmful behavior when routing brings them together. We consider an attacker who changes neither model weights nor the client agent and instead controls only trigger placement and template insertion. Across star, chain, and DAG topologies, routing-aware optimization substantially increases attack success over non-optimized baselines while keeping false activations low.
