Existing defenses, including PromptGuard, Llama-Guard variants, and system-level controls such as tool restrictions, do not reliably stop the attack because no single component appears malicious in isolation. These results expose a structural vulnerability in agentic LLM pipelines and motivate defenses that reason over routing and cross-agent composition. Code is available at https://github.com/UCF-ML-Research/ConjunctiveAgents.

</details>


### [333] [Agentic AI for Education: A Unified Multi-Agent Framework for Personalized Learning and Institutional Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16566)
*Arya Mary K J,Deepthy K Bhaskar,Sinu T S,Binu V P*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Agentic AI system for education integrates multi-level support across students, educators, and institutions using LLMs, reinforcement learning, and analytics to improve recommendations, grading, and dropout prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI-based educational systems are fragmented and lack integration across multiple stakeholder levels (students, educators, institutions), preventing comprehensive, proactive educational support.

Method: Proposes Agentic Unified Student Support System (AUSS) - a multi-agent architecture with LLMs, reinforcement learning, predictive analytics, and rule-based reasoning. Integrates student-level personalization, educator-level automation, and institutional-level intelligence.

Result: Experimental results show improvements: recommendation accuracy (92.4%), grading efficiency (94.1%), and dropout prediction (F1-score: 89.5%). Demonstrates scalable, adaptive educational ecosystem capabilities.

Conclusion: The Agentic AI framework enables comprehensive, intelligent educational ecosystems by unifying multi-level support across stakeholders through proactive, autonomous decision-making systems.

Abstract: Agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI) represents a paradigm shift from reactive systems to proactive, autonomous decision making frameworks. Existing AI-based educational systems remain fragmented and lack multi-level integration across stakeholders. This paper proposes the Agentic Unified Student Support System (AUSS), a novel multi-agent architecture integrating student-level personalization, educator-level automation, and institutional-level intelligence. The framework leverages Large Language Models (LLMs), reinforcement learning, predictive analytics, and rule-based reasoning. Experimental results demonstrate improvements in recommendation accuracy (92.4%), grading efficiency (94.1%), and dropout prediction (F1-score: 89.5%). The proposed system enables scalable, adaptive, and intelligent educational ecosystems.

</details>


### [334] [CogGen: A Cognitively Inspired Recursive Framework for Deep Research Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17072)
*Kuo Tian,Pengfei Sun,Zhen Wu,Junran Ding,Xinyu Dai*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: CogGen is a cognitively-inspired recursive framework for generating deep research reports using LLMs, featuring hierarchical architecture for flexible planning and abstract visual representations for multimodal fusion, achieving state-of-the-art results comparable to professional analysts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches for autonomous research report synthesis using LLMs rely on rigid linear workflows that cause error accumulation, prevent global restructuring from new insights, and limit multimodal fusion and overall report quality.

Method: Proposes CogGen: 1) Hierarchical Recursive Architecture simulating cognitive writing for flexible planning and global restructuring, 2) Abstract Visual Representation (AVR) - concise intent-driven language for refining visual-text layouts without pixel regeneration, and 3) Cognitive Load Evaluation Framework (CLEF) with a benchmark from Our World in Data.

Result: CogGen achieves state-of-the-art results among open-source systems, generating reports comparable to professional analysts' outputs and surpassing Gemini Deep Research. Code and dataset are publicly available.

Conclusion: CogGen successfully addresses limitations of linear workflows by introducing cognitive-inspired recursion for both textual and multimodal content, enabling high-quality deep research report generation that outperforms existing methods.

Abstract: The autonomous synthesis of deep research reports represents a critical frontier for Large Language Models (LLMs), demanding sophisticated information orchestration and non-linear narrative logic. Current approaches rely on rigid predefined linear workflows, which cause error accumulation, preclude global restructuring from subsequent insights, and ultimately limit in-depth multimodal fusion and report quality. We propose CogGen, a Cognitively inspired recursive framework for deep research report Generation. Leveraging a Hierarchical Recursive Architecture to simulate cognitive writing, CogGen enables flexible planning and global restructuring. To extend this recursivity to multimodal content, we introduce Abstract Visual Representation (AVR): a concise intent-driven language that iteratively refines visual-text layouts without pixel-level regeneration overhead. We further present CLEF, a Cognitive Load Evaluation Framework, and curate a new benchmark from Our World in Data (OWID). Extensive experiments show CogGen achieves state-of-the-art results among open-source systems, generating reports comparable to professional analysts' outputs and surpassing Gemini Deep Research. Our code and dataset are available at https://github.com/NJUNLP/CogGen.

</details>


### [335] [Logic-Based Verification of Task Allocation for LLM-Enabled Multi-Agent Manufacturing Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17142)
*Jonghan Lim,Mostafa Tavakkoli Anbarani,Rômulo Meira-Góes,Ilya Kovalenko*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: A safety-preserving control architecture that combines large language models' flexibility with formal verification using temporal logic and discrete event systems to enable safe task planning in manufacturing systems with high product variability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manufacturing industries face challenges with increasing product variability and demand for personalization. Frequent system reconfigurations for new products can lead to unintended hazardous behaviors. Multi-agent architectures improve flexibility but are limited by predefined task models, while LLMs enhance adaptability but raise reliability concerns for safety-critical manufacturing environments.

Method: Proposes a control architecture that leverages LLMs for flexible task planning, combined with formal verification using temporal logic and discrete event systems. The framework verifies LLM-enabled task allocations before execution to ensure safety on the manufacturing shop floor.

Result: Demonstrated effectiveness through a multi-robot assembly case study, showing that unsafe tasks can be allocated safely before task execution by using the proposed verification framework.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses the reliability challenges of LLMs in manufacturing by combining their flexibility with formal safety verification, enabling adaptive task planning while preserving safety in environments with high product variability.

Abstract: Manufacturing industries are facing increasing product variability due to the growing demand for personalized products. Under these conditions, ensuring safety becomes challenging as frequent reconfigurations can lead to unintended hazardous behaviors. Multi-agent control architectures have been proposed to improve flexibility through decentralized decision-making and coordination. However, these architectures are based on predefined task models, which limit their ability to adapt task planning to new product requirements while preserving safety. Recently, large language models have been introduced into manufacturing systems to enhance adaptability, but reliability remains a key challenge. To address this issue, we propose a control architecture that leverages the flexibility of large language models while preserving safety on the manufacturing shop floor. Specifically, the proposed framework verifies large language model-enabled task allocations by using temporal logic and discrete event systems. The effectiveness of the proposed framework is demonstrated through a case study that involves a multi-robot assembly scenario, showing that unsafe tasks can be allocated safely before task execution.

</details>


### [336] [Dynamics of Cognitive Heterogeneity: Investigating Behavioral Biases in Multi-Stage Supply Chains with LLM-Based Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17220)
*Jiuyun Jiang,Yuecheng Hong,Bo Yang,Jin Yang,Guangxin Jiang,Xiaomeng Guo,Guang Xiao*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: LLMs simulate multi-stage supply chain dynamics to analyze cognitive heterogeneity's impact on agent coordination, revealing myopic behaviors exacerbate inefficiencies but information sharing mitigates them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional behavioral experiments for studying cognitive biases in supply chain inefficiencies face scalability and control limitations, creating a need for new approaches to model coordination among generative agents in complex multi-round decision-making.

Method: Introduce scalable experimental paradigm using LLMs (DeepSeek and GPT agents) grounded in Hierarchical Reasoning Framework to simulate multi-stage supply chain dynamics with systematic variation of reasoning sophistication across supply chain tiers. Use rigorously replicated and statistically validated simulations.

Result: Agents exhibit myopic and self-interested behaviors that exacerbate systemic inefficiencies. However, information sharing effectively mitigates these adverse effects. Cognitive diversity influences collective outcomes in supply chain interactions.

Conclusion: Findings extend traditional behavioral methods and offer insights into AI-enabled organization dynamics. Work underscores both potential and limitations of LLM-based agents as proxies for human decision-making in complex operational environments, providing new approach to studying coordination in multi-stage supply chains.

Abstract: Modeling coordination among generative agents in complex multi-round decision-making presents a core challenge for AI and operations management. Although behavioral experiments have revealed cognitive biases behind supply chain inefficiencies, traditional methods face scalability and control limitations. We introduce a scalable experimental paradigm using Large Language Models (LLMs) to simulate multi-stage supply chain dynamics. Grounded in a Hierarchical Reasoning Framework, this study specifically analyzes the impact of cognitive heterogeneity on agent interactions. Unlike prior homogeneous settings, we employ DeepSeek and GPT agents to systematically vary reasoning sophistication across supply chain tiers. Through rigorously replicated and statistically validated simulations, we investigate how this cognitive diversity influences collective outcomes. Results indicate that agents exhibit myopic and self-interested behaviors that exacerbate systemic inefficiencies. However, we demonstrate that information sharing effectively mitigates these adverse effects. Our findings extend traditional behavioral methods and offer new insights into the dynamics of AI-enabled organizations. This work underscores both the potential and limitations of LLM-based agents as proxies for human decision-making in complex operational environments.

</details>


### [337] [ARMove: Learning to Predict Human Mobility through Agentic Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17419)
*Chuyue Wang,Jie Feng,Yuxi Wu,Shenglin Yi,Hang Zhang*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: ARMove is a transferable framework for human mobility prediction using agentic reasoning that addresses LLM limitations in interpretability, iterative learning, and transferability through feature management, user customization, and large-small model synergy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human mobility prediction is complex and variable across populations and regions. Existing LLM-based methods suffer from limited interpretability (black-box reasoning), lack of iterative learning from new data, and poor transferability across different contexts.

Method: ARMove employs agentic reasoning with standardized feature management (four major feature pools), user profiles for segmentation, and automated generation integrating LLM knowledge. It uses iterative optimization, user-specific customization, and agentic decision-making that adjusts feature weights. The framework also implements large-small model synergy to distill strategies from large LLMs (72B) to smaller ones (7B).

Result: ARMove outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on 6 out of 12 metrics with gains of 0.78% to 10.47% across four global datasets. Transferability tests confirm robustness across regions, user groups, and model scales. The other 4 metrics achieved suboptimal results. The framework demonstrates interpretability in decision-making.

Conclusion: ARMove successfully addresses key limitations of existing LLM-based mobility prediction methods by providing a transferable, interpretable framework with iterative learning capabilities and cost-effective large-small model synergy, showing robust performance across diverse real-world scenarios.

Abstract: Human mobility prediction is a critical task but remains challenging due to its complexity and variability across populations and regions. Recently, large language models (LLMs) have made progress in zero-shot prediction, but existing methods suffer from limited interpretability (due to black-box reasoning), lack of iterative learning from new data, and poor transferability. In this paper, we introduce \textbf{ARMove}, a fully transferable framework for predicting human mobility through agentic reasoning. To address these limitations, ARMove employs standardized feature management with iterative optimization and user-specific customization: four major feature pools for foundational knowledge, user profiles for segmentation, and an automated generation mechanism integrating LLM knowledge. Robust generalization is achieved via agentic decision-making that adjusts feature weights to maximize accuracy while providing interpretable decision paths. Finally, large-small model synergy distills strategies from large LLMs (e.g., 72B) to smaller ones (e.g., 7B), reducing costs and enhancing performance ceilings. Extensive experiments on four global datasets show ARMove outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on 6 out of 12 metrics (gains of 0.78\% to 10.47\%), with transferability tests confirming robustness across regions, users, and scales. The other 4 items also achieved suboptimal results. Transferability tests confirm its 19 robustness across regions, user groups, and model scales, while interpretability 20 analysis highlights its transparency in decision-making. Our codes are available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ARMove-F847.

</details>


### [338] [Towards Self-Improving Error Diagnosis in Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17658)
*Jiazheng Li,Emine Yilmaz,Bei Chen,Dieu-Thu Le*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: ErrorProbe is a self-improving framework for semantic failure attribution in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems that identifies responsible agents and error steps through a three-stage pipeline with verified episodic memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems enable complex problem-solving but introduce significant debugging challenges including long interaction traces, inter-agent dependencies, and delayed error manifestation. Existing diagnostic approaches often rely on expensive expert annotation or 'LLM-as-a-judge' paradigms that struggle to pinpoint decisive error steps within extended contexts.

Method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) operationalizing the MAS failure taxonomy to detect local anomalies, (2) performing symptom-driven backward tracing to prune irrelevant context, and (3) employing a specialized multi-agent team (Strategist, Investigator, Arbiter) to validate error hypotheses through tool-grounded execution. The framework maintains a verified episodic memory that updates only when error patterns are confirmed by executable evidence.

Result: Experiments across the TracerTraj and Who&When benchmarks demonstrate that ErrorProbe significantly outperforms baselines, particularly in step-level localization. The verified episodic memory enables robust cross-domain transfer without retraining.

Conclusion: ErrorProbe provides an effective self-improving framework for semantic failure attribution in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems that addresses the limitations of existing diagnostic approaches by combining failure taxonomy, context pruning, and tool-grounded validation with verified memory.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) enable complex problem-solving but introduce significant debugging challenges, characterized by long interaction traces, inter-agent dependencies, and delayed error manifestation. Existing diagnostic approaches often rely on expensive expert annotation or ''LLM-as-a-judge'' paradigms, which struggle to pinpoint decisive error steps within extended contexts. In this paper, we introduce ErrorProbe, a self-improving framework for semantic failure attribution that identifies responsible agents and the originating error step. The framework operates via a three-stage pipeline: (1) operationalizing the MAS failure taxonomy to detect local anomalies, (2) performing symptom-driven backward tracing to prune irrelevant context, and (3) employing a specialized multi-agent team (Strategist, Investigator, Arbiter) to validate error hypotheses through tool-grounded execution. Crucially, ErrorProbe maintains a verified episodic memory that updates only when error patterns are confirmed by executable evidence, without the need for annotation. Experiments across the TracerTraj and Who&When benchmarks demonstrate that ErrorProbe significantly outperforms baselines, particularly in step-level localization, while the verified memory enables robust cross-domain transfer without retraining.

</details>


### [339] [Multi-UAV Path Following using Vector-Field Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17995)
*Gautam Kumar,Amit Shivam,Ashwini Ratnoo*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: A decentralized framework for multiple UAVs that provides collision-free path following with uniform spacing using vector field guidance, rotational repulsion, and velocity control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for safe, coordinated operation of multiple UAVs where they must follow paths while avoiding collisions and maintaining uniform spacing, which is crucial for formation flying and efficient coverage applications.

Method: Combines three components: 1) vector field-based guidance law for path following, 2) rotational repulsion mechanism using relative distance and bearing for collision avoidance, and 3) inter-UAV spacing error-based velocity control for uniform separation.

Result: Analytical guarantees established for collision avoidance and convergence of inter-UAV spacing errors to zero, ensuring uniform separation. Numerical simulations demonstrate the method's effectiveness in achieving both collision-free operation and uniform spacing along paths.

Conclusion: The proposed decentralized framework successfully achieves collision-free path following with uniform spacing for multiple UAVs, with formal guarantees of safety and spacing performance validated through simulation.

Abstract: This paper presents a decentralized, collision-free framework for path following guidance of multiple uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs), while maintaining uniform spacing along a reference path. A vector field-based guidance law is employed to drive each UAV toward the reference path. A rotational repulsion mechanism, utilizing relative distance and bearing between UAVs, is proposed to avoid collisions during convergence to the path, and an inter-UAV spacing error-based velocity control law is presented to achieve uniform separation along the path. Analytical guarantees are established for collision avoidance and convergence of the inter-UAV spacing errors to zero, ensuring uniform separation along the path. Numerical simulations demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed method.

</details>


### [340] [Diversity Collapse in Multi-Agent LLM Systems: Structural Coupling and Collective Failure in Open-Ended Idea Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18005)
*Nuo Chen,Yicheng Tong,Yuzhe Yang,Yufei He,Xueyi Zhang,Zou Qingyun,Qian Wang,Bingsheng He*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: A systematic study reveals that multi-agent systems (MAS) for idea generation often experience diversity collapse due to structural coupling in interactions, where stronger models, authority dynamics, and dense topologies actually reduce exploration diversity despite higher individual quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent systems are increasingly used for open-ended idea generation with the expectation that collective interaction broadens exploration diversity, but it remains unclear when and why such collaboration truly expands the solution space.

Method: The authors conduct a systematic empirical study of diversity in MAS-based ideation across three bottom-up levels: model intelligence, agent cognition, and system dynamics, analyzing how different structural elements affect exploration diversity.

Result: At the model level, stronger, highly aligned models yield diminishing marginal diversity despite higher per-sample quality (compute efficiency paradox). At the cognition level, authority-driven dynamics suppress semantic diversity compared to junior-dominated groups. At the system level, group-size scaling yields diminishing returns and dense communication topologies accelerate premature convergence. Diversity collapse arises from structural coupling where interaction contracts agent exploration.

Conclusion: The diversity collapse in MAS-based ideation arises primarily from interaction structure rather than inherent model insufficiency, highlighting the importance of preserving independence and disagreement when designing multi-agent systems for creative tasks. The key insight is that structural coupling inadvertently contracts agent exploration and triggers diversity collapse.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems (MAS) are increasingly used for open-ended idea generation, driven by the expectation that collective interaction will broaden the exploration diversity. However, when and why such collaboration truly expands the solution space remains unclear. We present a systematic empirical study of diversity in MAS-based ideation across three bottom-up levels: model intelligence, agent cognition, and system dynamics. At the model level, we identify a compute efficiency paradox, where stronger, highly aligned models yield diminishing marginal diversity despite higher per-sample quality. At the cognition level, authority-driven dynamics suppress semantic diversity compared to junior-dominated groups. At the system level, group-size scaling yields diminishing returns and dense communication topologies accelerate premature convergence. We characterize these outcomes as collective failures emerging from structural coupling, a process where interaction inadvertently contracts agent exploration and triggers diversity collapse. Our analysis shows that this collapse arises primarily from the interaction structure rather than inherent model insufficiency, highlighting the importance of preserving independence and disagreement when designing MAS for creative tasks. Our code is available at https://github.com/Xtra-Computing/MAS_Diversity.

</details>


### [341] [ConventionPlay: Capability-Limited Training for Robust Ad-Hoc Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18123)
*Abhishek Sriraman,Eleni Vasilaki,Robert Loftin*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: ConventionPlay is an RL-based approach that enables agents to actively steer ad-hoc teams toward effective joint strategies by probing partner capabilities and leading when possible, following when necessary.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In ad-hoc collaboration where partners can follow multiple conventions, agents need to do more than just adapt - they must actively steer the team toward the most effective joint strategy. The challenge is to coordinate when conventions have differentiated payoffs and partners have varied capabilities.

Method: ConventionPlay uses reinforcement learning to extend cognitive hierarchies with a diverse population of adaptive followers. The agent trains against partners with varied capability limits, learning to probe its partner's repertoire and dynamically decide when to lead the team versus when to follow.

Result: Results in canonical coordination tasks demonstrate that ConventionPlay achieves superior coordination efficiency, particularly in settings where conventions have differentiated payoffs, outperforming approaches that simply adapt rather than actively steer collaboration.

Conclusion: Actively steering collaboration through strategic probing of partner capabilities and flexible leadership/following roles leads to more effective coordination than simple adaptation, especially in complex environments with multiple conventions of varying value.

Abstract: Ad-hoc collaboration often relies on identifying and adhering to shared conventions. However, when partners can follow multiple conventions, agents must do more than simply adapt; they must actively steer the team toward the most effective joint strategy. We present ConventionPlay, a reinforcement learning-based approach that extends cognitive hierarchies to include a diverse population of adaptive followers. By training against partners with varied capability limits, our agent learns to probe its partner's repertoire, leading the team when possible and following when necessary. Our results in canonical coordination tasks show that ConventionPlay achieves superior coordination efficiency, particularly in settings where conventions have differentiated payoffs.

</details>


### [342] [Aether: Network Validation Using Agentic AI and Digital Twin](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18233)
*Jordan Auge,Sam Betts,Giovanna Carofiglio,Giulio Grassi,Martin Gysi,John Kenneth d'Souza*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Aether uses Generative Agentic AI with Network Digital Twins to automate network change validation, replacing manual processes with collaborative AI agents for faster, more accurate verification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Network change validation is currently manual, time-consuming, and error-prone. Existing verification tools are typically offline and don't handle continuous changes well, leading to partial coverage and post-deployment errors.

Method: Aether employs a multi-agent architecture with five specialized Network Operations AI agents that collaboratively handle the validation lifecycle. These agents operate on a unified Network Digital Twin integrating modeling, simulation, and emulation to maintain an accurate network view.

Result: Aether demonstrated 100% error detection, 92-96% diagnostic coverage, and validation completion in 6-7 minutes across synthetic scenarios and real ISP incidents, significantly outperforming traditional methods.

Conclusion: Aether successfully automates network change validation workflows using agentic AI and digital twins, reducing manual effort, minimizing errors, and improving operational agility while showing promising results in real-world evaluations.

Abstract: Network change validation remains a critical yet predominantly manual, time-consuming, and error-prone process in modern network operations. While formal network verification has made substantial progress in proving correctness properties, it is typically applied in offline, pre-deployment settings and faces challenges in accommodating continuous changes and validating live production behavior. Current operational approaches typically involve scattered testing tools, resulting in partial coverage and errors that surface only after deployment. In this paper, we present Aether, a novel approach that integrates Generative Agentic AI with a multi-functional Network Digital Twin to automate and streamline network change validation workflows. It features an agentic architecture with five specialized Network Operations AI agents that collaboratively handle the change validation lifecycle from intent analysis to network verification and testing. Aether agents use a unified Network Digital Twin integrating modeling, simulation, and emulation to maintain a consistent, up-to-date network view for verification and testing. By orchestrating agent collaboration atop this digital twin, Aether enables automated, rapid network change validation while reducing manual effort, minimizing errors, and improving operational agility and cost-effectiveness. We evaluate Aether over synthetic network change scenarios covering main classes of network changes and on past incidents from a major ISP operational network, demonstrating promising results in error detection (100%), diagnostic coverage (92-96%), and speed (6-7 minutes) over traditional methods.

</details>


### [343] [QRAFTI: An Agentic Framework for Empirical Research in Quantitative Finance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18500)
*Terence Lim,Kumar Muthuraman,Michael Sury*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: QRAFTI is a multi-agent AI framework that emulates quantitative research teams for equity factor research on large financial panel datasets, using tool integration and reflection-based planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There's a need for automated quantitative research systems that can efficiently work with large financial panel datasets, replicate established factors, and support the development of new trading signals with better performance and explainability than current methods.

Method: QRAFTI integrates a research toolkit for panel data with MCP servers that expose data access, factor construction, and custom coding operations as callable tools. It uses a multi-agent framework with chained tool calls and reflection-based planning approaches.

Result: The framework can help replicate established factors, formulate and test new signals, and generate standardized research reports with narrative analysis and computational traces. Reflection-based planning with chained tool calls offers better performance and explainability than dynamic code generation alone.

Conclusion: Multi-agent systems with tool integration and reflection-based planning provide an effective approach for quantitative equity factor research, offering advantages in performance and explainability over traditional dynamic code generation methods.

Abstract: We introduce a multi-agent framework intended to emulate parts of a quantitative research team and support equity factor research on large financial panel datasets. QRAFTI integrates a research toolkit for panel data with MCP servers that expose data access, factor construction, and custom coding operations as callable tools. It can help replicate established factors, formulate and test new signals, and generate standardized research reports accompanied by narrative analysis and computational traces. On multi-step empirical tasks, using chained tool calls and reflection-based planning may offer better performance and explainability than dynamic code generation alone.

</details>
